In this work, we introduce a neurosymbolic formulation of skeleton-based HAR that reframes action recognition as concept-driven first-order logical reasoning over motion primitives. Our framework bridges representation learning and symbolic inference by grounding first-order logic predicates in learnable spatial and temporal motion concepts. Specifically, we employ a standard spatio-temporal skeleton encoder to extract latent motion representations, which are then mapped to interpretable concept predicates via a spatio-temporal concept decoder that explicitly separates pose-centric and dynamics-centric abstractions. These concept predicates are composed through differentiable first-order logic layers, enabling the model to learn human-readable logical rules that govern action semantics. To impose semantic structure on the learned concepts, we align skeleton representations with LLM-derived descriptions of atomic motion primitives, establishing a shared conceptual space for perception and reasoning. Extensive experiments on NTU RGB+D 60/120 and NW-UCLA demonstrate that our approach achieves competitive recognition performance while providing explicit, interpretable explanations grounded in logical structure. Our results highlight neurosymbolic reasoning as an effective paradigm for interpretable spatio-temporal action understanding. Code: https://github.com/Mr-TalhaIlyas/REASON

</details>


### [26] [Qwen3-VL-Seg: Unlocking Open-World Referring Segmentation with Vision-Language Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07141)
*Yuan Yao,Qiushi Yang,Humen Zhong,Jiangning Wei,Yifang Men,Shuai Bai,Miaomiao Cui,Zhibo Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Qwen3-VL-Seg is a lightweight, parameter-efficient framework that converts MLLM-predicted bounding boxes into dense pixel-level referring segmentation using a 17M-parameter box-guided mask decoder; it introduces SA1B-ORS and ORS-Bench for scalable open-world training and evaluation, achieving strong performance on both closed-set and open-world tasks while preserving general multimodal abilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLMs either output sparse bounding boxes or require heavy external segmentation models (e.g., SAM), which hinders dense, pixel-level segmentation in open-world referring expressions. There is a need for a compact, scalable solution that yields dense masks directly from MLLMs without large architectural overhead.

Method: A lightweight box-guided mask decoder that injects multi-scale spatial features, constructs spatial-semantic queries, performs box-guided high-resolution pixel fusion, and iteratively refines masks. The model adds only about 17M parameters (~0.4% of the base model). For scalability, SA1B-ORS is created from SA-1B with SA1B-CoRS (category-oriented samples) and SA1B-DeRS (descriptive, instance-specific samples). Evaluation uses ORS-Bench with in- and out-of-distribution subsets. 

Result: Qwen3-VL-Seg delivers strong performance on referring expression segmentation and visual grounding across closed-set and open-world settings, with advantages on language-intensive instructions and notable out-of-distribution generalization. It also preserves general multimodal capabilities after segmentation-oriented adaptation, according to evaluations on general benchmarks.

Conclusion: A compact, plug-in box-guided mask decoder effectively converts MLLM-predicted boxes into high-quality pixel-level masks, enabling scalable open-world referring segmentation with minimal parameter overhead and without heavy external segmentation models. The proposed SA1B-ORS and ORS-Bench support scalable training and robust evaluation across diverse referring expressions.

Abstract: Open-world referring segmentation requires grounding unconstrained language expressions to precise pixel-level regions. Existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) exhibit strong open-world visual grounding, but their outputs remain limited to sparse bounding-box coordinates and are insufficient for dense visual prediction. Recent MLLM-based segmentation methods either directly predict sparse contour coordinates, struggling to reconstruct continuous object boundaries, or rely on external segmentation foundation models such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM), introducing substantial architectural and deployment overhead. We present Qwen3-VL-Seg, a parameter-efficient framework that treats the MLLM-predicted box as a semantically grounded structural prior and decodes it into pixel-level referring segmentation. At its core, a lightweight box-guided mask decoder combines multi-scale spatial feature injection, spatial-semantic query construction, box-guided high-resolution pixel fusion, and iterative mask-aware query refinement, introducing only 17M parameters (about 0.4\% of the base model). For scalable open-world training, we construct SA1B-ORS, an SA-1B-derived dataset with two subsets: SA1B-CoRS (category-oriented samples) and SA1B-DeRS (descriptive, instance-specific samples). For evaluation, we curate ORS-Bench, a manually screened benchmark with in-distribution and out-of-distribution subsets covering diverse referring expression types. Extensive experiments on referring expression segmentation, visual grounding, and ORS-Bench show that Qwen3-VL-Seg performs strongly across closed-set and open-world settings, with clear advantages on language-intensive instructions and strong out-of-distribution generalization. Evaluations on general multimodal benchmarks further show that the model broadly preserves general-purpose multimodal competence after segmentation-oriented adaptation.

</details>


### [27] [AGA3DNet: Anatomy-Guided Gaussian Priors with Multi-view xLSTM for 3D Brain MRI Subtype Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07142)
*Peiyu Duan,Xueqi Guo,Sepehr Farhand,Mehmet Berk Sahin,Xinyuan Zheng,James S. Duncan,Gerardo Hermosillo Valadez,Yoshihisa Shinagawa*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AGA3DNet builds a report-grounded, anatomy-aware 3D MRI classifier by embedding soft anatomical priors from radiology reports into a spatial channel and fusing with a lightweight 3D CNN and multi-view xlSTM, achieving better balanced performance with interpretable localization; evaluated on retrospective brain MRI cohorts; limitations include single-site data and lack of large public paired datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To leverage both local anatomical cues and long-range context for subtype discrimination in brain MRI without dense voxel annotations, improving interpretability and performance.

Method: Extract anatomy phrases from radiology reports; map to atlas regions; convert to smooth spatial priors via signed-distance transform and Gaussian weighting; fuse with a lightweight 3D CNN and multi-view xLSTM; report-grounded prior channel; compare against 3D baselines.

Result: Improved overall balance across performance metrics on a retrospective cohort; supports clinically interpretable localization through the prior channel; outperforms reproducible 3D baselines.

Conclusion: AGA3DNet provides anatomy-grounded, interpretable guidance for brain MRI subtype classification; limitations include single-cohort evaluation and lack of large-scale public MRI datasets with paired reports; future work could include multi-site validation and publicly accessible paired datasets.

Abstract: Accurate 3D brain MRI subtype classification benefits from both localized anatomical cues and long-range contextual reasoning. We present AGA3DNet, a report-grounded framework that incorporates brief anatomical phrases extracted from radiology reports as a soft anatomical prior channel and fuses it with a lightweight 3D CNN and multi-view xLSTM aggregation. Specifically, extracted anatomical phrases are mapped to atlas-defined regions and converted into smooth spatial priors using a signed-distance transform followed by Gaussian weighting, providing interpretable, anatomy-grounded guidance without requiring dense voxel annotations. We evaluate AGA3DNet on a retrospective institutional brain MRI cohort for abnormal subtype discrimination and compare against reproducible 3D classification baselines. AGA3DNet achieves improved overall balance across performance metrics and supports clinically interpretable localization through the prior channel. We discuss limitations related to single-cohort evaluation and the lack of large-scale public brain MRI datasets paired with radiology reports under broadly usable terms.

</details>


### [28] [TriP: A Triangle Puzzle Approach to Robust Translation Averaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07143)
*Zhekai Fan,Wanze Li,Jinxin Wang,Yunpeng Shi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A triangle-based robust translation averaging method (TriP) that derives local edge scales from triangle geometry and synchronizes scales in log-domain to yield globally consistent edge lengths and camera locations, with strong robustness to corruptions and scalable performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Translation averaging is a core step in global SfM but is ill-conditioned because direction measurements lack distance information and are sensitive to corrupted observations; a robust, scalable method with exact recoverability is highly desirable.

Method: Infer local relative edge scales from triangle geometry; synchronize overlapping triangle scales in the logarithmic domain to recover globally consistent edge lengths and camera locations; leverage higher-order triangle consistency to resist adversarial, cycle-consistent, and structured corruptions; avoid collapse by construction through log-scale synchronization; parallelizable and scalable.

Result: Provides strong theoretical guarantees for exact location recovery; practically fast and scalable to graphs with millions of cameras; empirical results show substantial improvements over all prior translation averaging methods on synthetic and real datasets.

Conclusion: TriP delivers robust, scalable translation averaging with exact recoverability by exploiting triangle-based geometry and log-domain scale synchronization, outperforming prior methods and suitable for large-scale SfM pipelines.

Abstract: Translation averaging aims to recover camera locations from pairwise relative translation directions and is a fundamental component of global Structure-from-Motion pipelines. The problem is challenging because direction measurements contain no distance information, making the estimation problem highly ill-conditioned and highly sensitive to corrupted observations. In this paper, we propose TriP, a triangle-based framework for robust translation averaging. TriP first infers local relative edge scales from triangle geometry, and then synchronizes the scales of overlapping triangles in the logarithmic domain to recover globally consistent edge lengths and camera locations. By leveraging higher-order consistency across triangles, the proposed method is robust to adversarial, cycle-consistent, and other structured corruptions. In addition, TriP avoids the collapse issue without requiring any extra anti-collapse constraints, since log-scale synchronization excludes the degenerate zero-scale solution by construction. These structural advantages enable a particularly strong theory for exact location recovery. On the practical side, TriP is fully parallelizable, computationally efficient, and naturally scalable to graphs with millions of cameras. Moreover, it outperforms all previous translation averaging methods by a large margin on both synthetic and real datasets.

</details>


### [29] [UniV2D: Bridging Visual Restoration and Semantic Perception for Underwater Salient Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07146)
*Laibin Chang,Shaodong Wang,Yunke Wang,Xu Zhang,Kui Jiang,Chang Xu,Bo Du*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified, end-to-end framework UniV2D jointly learns underwater image restoration and salient object detection by mutual semantic guidance, outperforming sequential pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Underwater DHI degradation challenges traditional restore-then-detect pipelines, causing semantic drift between restoration and detection. A unified, semantic-driven approach can align high-level saliency with low-level restoration.

Method: A hierarchical dual-branch network: a self-calibrated decoder predicts initial saliency masks and a mask-aware restoration module reconstructs the image; followed by a saliency-guided refinement with cross-level modulation to align structure with semantics.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance in both quantitative and qualitative metrics, establishing a new standard for joint underwater perception.

Conclusion: Joint optimization of restoration and saliency, driven by semantic interaction, yields robust, more accurate underwater perception and outperforms rigid sequential pipelines.

Abstract: Underwater salient object detection (USOD) plays a vital role in marine vision tasks but remains fundamentally challenging due to severe visual degradation, such as selective absorption and medium scattering. Conventional pipelines typically adopt a sequential "enhance-then-detect" paradigm. However, isolating low-level visual restoration from high-level semantic perception often leads to semantic inconsistency, where the restored images may not be optimal for detection and can even introduce task-irrelevant noise. To break this sequential bottleneck, we propose UniV2D, a Unified Vision-to-Detection Network that jointly optimizes visual restoration and salient object detection within a mutually beneficial framework. Unlike traditional methods that rely on disjointed pipelines or rigid physical priors, UniV2D introduces a semantic-driven learning paradigm: high-level saliency semantics actively guide the restoration process, while the restored visual cues reciprocally enhance saliency perception. Specifically, UniV2D features a hierarchical dual-branch architecture. It first employs a self-calibrated decoder to predict initial saliency masks alongside a mask-aware restoration module to reconstruct image content. Subsequently, a saliency-guided refinement module equipped with cross-level modulation is utilized to align structural fidelity with semantic consistency. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that UniV2D significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations, establishing a new standard for joint underwater perception.

</details>


### [30] [Uncovering and Shaping the Latent Representation of 3D Scene Topology in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07148)
*Haoming Wang,Wei Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VLMs harbor a latent 3D topological map aligned with Laplacian eigenmaps of a 3D Gaussian-kernel graph, but it's overshadowed by non-geometric features. The authors isolate this spatial subspace, relate it to Laplacian geometry, and introduce a Dirichlet-energy latent regularizer. Minimal supervised fine-tuning yields notable gains (~12% on spatial/topology tasks).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether VLMs encode an internal 3D, topology-preserving representation and to leverage geometric regularization to enhance spatial reasoning.

Method: 1) Cross-scene linear feature extraction to isolate the spatial subspace. 2) Demonstrate causal control of spatial outputs by this subspace. 3) Theoretically map the latent subspace to Laplacian eigenmaps of a 3D Gaussian-kernel graph and show convergence to physical space in the continuous limit. 4) Propose Dirichlet-energy-based latent regularization. 5) Fine-tune a minimal 500 steps on synthetic data, evaluate on real-world spatial benchmarks.

Result: Identified a clean spatial subspace that causally drives spatial outputs. The latent representation corresponds to Laplacian eigenmaps of the scene’s 3D Gaussian- kernel graph and converges to 3D space. The Dirichlet energy regularizer improves spatial/topology tasks by up to 12.1% over standard fine-tuning.

Conclusion: VLMs contain latent geometric structure that can be extracted and regularized to enhance 3D spatial reasoning; the approach provides a principled bridge between neural representations and classical geometry, with practical gains on spatial benchmarks.

Abstract: Decades of cognitive science establish that humans navigate environments by forming cognitive maps, defined as allocentric and topology-preserving representations of 3D space. While modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate emergent spatial reasoning from 2D egocentric inputs, it remains unclear whether they construct an analogous 3D internal representation. In this paper, we demonstrate that current VLMs do possess a latent topological map of 3D scenes, but it is heavily overshadowed by non-geometric visual semantics, such as color and shape. By isolating this spatial subspace through cross-scene linear feature extraction, we extract a clean spatial subspace that causally controls the model's spatial outputs. We mathematically shape this latent representation and prove its correspondence to the Laplacian eigenmaps of the scene's 3D Gaussian-kernel graph, converging to the physical 3D space in the continuous limit. Motivated by this geometric identification, we further introduce a mathematically principled latent regularization method for VLMs, based on Dirichlet energy. Applying this single-term regularizer to a minimal 500-step supervised VLM fine-tuning (SFT) on simple synthetic data yields significant improvements on real-world spatial benchmarks, outperforming standard SFT and competitive baselines by up to 12.1\% in spatial tasks involving scene topology understanding. Source code is available at https://github.com/pittisl/vlm-latent-shaping

</details>


### [31] [Real-IAD MVN: A Multi-View Normal Vector Dataset and Benchmark for High-Fidelity Industrial Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07149)
*Wenbing Zhu,Jianing Liang,Linjie Cheng,Yurui Pan,Zhuhao Chen,Qingwang Yan,Yudong Cheng,Jianghui Zhang,Mingmin Chi,Bo Peng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Real-IAD-MVN, a multi-view surface-normal dataset for industrial anomaly detection that replaces sparse 3D data; shows dense normals improve detection; proposes a reconstruction-based baseline using cross-modal unified prototypes from image and normal maps, outperforming multimodal fusion baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of 2D RGB and sparse 3D point clouds for micro-geometric defects by providing dense, multi-view geometric information (surface normals) to detect subtle defects on side-walls/occluded regions.

Method: Upgrade acquisition system to capture high-fidelity surface normal maps from five viewpoints; create Real-IAD-MVN dataset; develop a baseline reconstruction method that learns cross-modal unified prototypes from image and normal-map streams for multimodal fusion.

Result: Dense multi-view normals yield significantly better detection than sparse 3D data; the proposed unified-prototype reconstruction baseline surpasses state-of-the-art multimodal fusion methods on the dataset.

Conclusion: Dense, multi-view normal maps are a powerful representation for geometric IAD; Real-IAD-MVN provides a valuable benchmark for evaluating and advancing anomaly detection in geometry-aware industrial settings.

Abstract: Industrial Anomaly Detection (IAD) is critical for quality control, but existing methods struggle with subtle, geometric defects. Standard 2D (RGB) images are sensitive to texture and lighting but often miss fine geometric anomalies. While 3D point clouds capture macro-shape, they are typically too sparse to detect micro-defects like scratches or pits. We address this fundamental data limitation by introducing Real-IAD-MVN (Multi-View Normal), a large-scale industrial dataset. By upgrading our acquisition system, Real-IAD-MVN captures high-fidelity surface normal maps from five distinct viewpoints, replacing sparse 3D data entirely. This provides a comprehensive geometric representation at a micro-detail level, making previously invisible side-wall and occluded defects explicitly detectable. Our experiments, conducted on this new dataset, first provide evidence that incorporating dense, multi-view pseudo-3D (surface normals) yields significantly better detection performance than using sparse 3D point cloud data. To further validate the dataset and provide a strong benchmark, we introduce a baseline method based on reconstruction, which learns to extract cross-modal unified prototypes from the image and normal map streams. We demonstrate that this unified prototype approach surpasses existing state-of-the-art multimodal fusion methods, highlighting the rich potential of our new dataset for advancing geometric anomaly detection.

</details>


### [32] [PRIMED: Adaptive Modality Suppression for Referring Audio-Visual Segmentation via Biased Competition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07154)
*Yuchen He,Jing Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PRIMED introduces modality-aware suppression for Ref-AVS by using a Modality Prior Decoder, Token Distiller, and a Spatial-Aware Semantic Alignment loss to adaptively weight and align multimodal cues, achieving SOTA on Ref-AVS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The Ref-AVS task suffers when modal cues vary in relevance across expressions and scenes; treating audio, vision, and text as homogeneous inputs can let misleading modalities misguide fusion. Inspired by biased competition theory, the work aims to explicitly model visual perception and language-driven priors to suppress irrelevant modalities and improve localization/segmentation.

Method: A Modality Prior Decoder estimates whether the referring expression relies on audio, vision, or joint interaction to generate a modality prior that guides high-level attention. A Token Distiller extracts compact global visual tokens from high-level features and shares them across Competition-aware Cross-modal Fusion modules to provide hierarchical global context. A Spatial-Aware Semantic Alignment loss uses contrastive learning to enhance foreground-background discrimination.

Result: Empirical evaluation on the Ref-AVS benchmark demonstrates state-of-the-art overall performance, validating the effectiveness of adaptive modality suppression and the proposed architectural components.

Conclusion: Modeling modality priors and global visual context via PRIMED improves robustness and accuracy for Ref-AVS, with the Spatial-Aware Semantic Alignment loss further strengthening foreground-background discrimination.

Abstract: Referring Audio-Visual Segmentation (Ref-AVS) seeks to localize and segment target objects in video frames based on visual, auditory, and textual referring cues. The task is challenging because the relevance of different modalities varies across referring expressions and scenes, while existing methods typically treat multimodal cues as homogeneous inputs for fusion, prompting, or reasoning, making them vulnerable to irrelevant or misleading modalities. To address this problem, we propose PRIMED, inspired by the biased competition theory in cognitive neuroscience, which explicitly models both visual perception and language-driven prior modulation, and enables more accurate Ref-AVS by adaptive modality suppression. Specifically, a Modality Prior Decoder first estimates whether the referring expression relies primarily on audio, vision, or their joint interaction, generating a modality prior to adaptively guide high-level attention. A Token Distiller further extracts compact global visual tokens from high-level features and shares them across Competition-aware Cross-modal Fusion modules to provide hierarchical global context. Additionally, we introduce a Spatial-Aware Semantic Alignment loss to further enhance foreground-background discrimination through contrastive learning. Extensive experiments on the Ref-AVS benchmark demonstrate that PRIMED achieves state-of-the-art overall performance.

</details>


### [33] [Hierarchical Perfusion Graphs for Tumor Heterogeneity Modeling in Glioma Molecular Subtyping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07156)
*Han Jang,Junhyeok Lee,Heeseong Eum,Joon Jang,Yoseob Han,Seung Hong Choi,Kyu Sung Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HiPerfGNN uses a hierarchical graph neural network with discrete perfusion representations learned via VQ-VAE from DSC-MRI time-intensity curves to non-invasively subtype gliomas (IDH mutation, 1p/19q codeletion). It demonstrates strong internal and external performance and biologically interpretable saliency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-invasively determine glioma molecular subtypes beyond anatomy-only MRI, addressing inter-site variability and voxel-wise limitations in radiogenomics.

Method: 1) Extract raw DSC time-intensity curves; 2) learn discrete hemodynamic representations with vector-quantized VAE (VQ-VAE); 3) form coarse graph nodes as tumor habitats, subdivided into fine subregions guided by structural MRI; 4) apply hierarchical graph neural network to propagate information across scales for molecular prediction (IDH, 1p/19q, WHO grade); 5) evaluate on internal (n=475) and external (n=397) cohorts; include gradient-based saliency for interpretability; code released.

Result: Achieved AUCs: IDH 0.96, 1p/19q 0.89, WHO grade 0.84 on internal cohort; external IDH AUC 0.89 without recalibration, indicating good generalization; saliency aligned with known glioma pathophysiology.

Conclusion: Integrating perfusion dynamics via a hierarchical graph framework adds value to radiogenomic pipelines for glioma molecular subtyping; code available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Precise molecular subtyping of gliomas, including isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH) mutation and 1p/19q codeletion, directly guides surgical and therapeutic decisions, yet currently relies on invasive tissue sampling. Deep learning on structural MRI has emerged as a non-invasive alternative, but anatomy-only approaches cannot capture the hemodynamic signatures that distinguish molecular subtypes. Radiogenomics based on dynamic susceptibility contrast (DSC) MRI holds immense potential for non-invasively characterizing glioma molecular subtypes, yet clinical deployment has been hindered by inter-site variability and the limitations of voxel-wise analysis. We introduce HiPerfGNN, a framework that first learns discrete hemodynamic representations from raw time-intensity curves using a vector-quantized variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE). These quantized perfusion codes define coarse-level graph nodes representing functional tumor habitats, each of which is hierarchically subdivided into fine-level subregions guided by structural MRI. A hierarchical graph neural network then propagates information across scales for molecular prediction. On an internal cohort (n=475), the model achieved AUCs of 0.96 (IDH), 0.89 (1p/19q), and 0.84 (WHO grade), and maintained robust IDH performance (AUC 0.89) on an independent external cohort (n=397) without recalibration. Gradient-based saliency analysis confirms biologically grounded attention patterns aligned with known glioma pathophysiology. Our results demonstrate the added value of integrating perfusion dynamics into radiogenomic pipelines for glioma molecular subtyping. Code is available at https://github.com/janghana/HiPerfGNN.

</details>


### [34] [Masks Can Talk: Extracting Structured Text Information from Single-Modal Images for Remote Sensing Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07178)
*Kai Zheng,Hang-Cheng Dong,Jiatong Pan,Zhenkai Wu,Fupeng Wei,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: S2M leverages ground-truth change masks to automatically generate structured textual descriptions (where, what, how, how many) for each change region, enabling dense multimodal supervision without extra annotation. It uses a two-stage training strategy and a bi-directional contrastive loss to align visual features with the structured textual embeddings, achieving strong results on Gaza-Change-v2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unimodal methods struggle with semantic changes; existing multimodal approaches rely on coarse or noisy text supervision. Ground-truth masks inherently contain rich, fine-grained change semantics that can be exploited without additional labeling.

Method: For each change region, automatically transcribe a semantic quadruple (where, what, how, how many) and convert it into several fixed-template textual descriptions. Train in two stages: (1) remote-sensing domain pretraining for robust representations, (2) introduce a multimodal decoder with a bi-directional contrastive loss to align visual features with structured textual embeddings.

Result: On Gaza-Change-v2 (MCD), S2M achieves Sek = 17.80% and F_scd = 66.14%, outperforming multimodal methods that use large language models.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that masks encode fine-grained change semantics and can provide precise, dense, and noise-free multimodal supervision when converted into structured text descriptions.

Abstract: Remote sensing change detection is pivotal for urban monitoring, disaster assessment, and environmental resource management. Yet, unimodal deep learning methods frequently confuse genuine semantic changes with visually similar but irrelevant variations. Recent multimodal approaches incorporate text as auxiliary supervision, but their descriptions are either semantically coarse and unstructured or model-generated and thus noisy. Critically, all of them overlook a simple fact: fine-grained change semantics are already implicitly encoded in the ground-truth mask labels that come standard with every change detection dataset. These masks know where the change happened, what the land-cover types were before and after, how the transition occurred, and how many objects were involved. In this paper, we propose S2M, a framework that obtains structured textual features directly from change labels at zero additional annotation cost. Specifically, each change region is automatically transcribed into a semantic quadruple (where, what, how, how many) and converted into several fixed-template text descriptions, providing precise, dense, and noise-free multimodal supervision. We adopts a two-stage training strategy to fine-tune on remote sensing imagery firstly for robust domain-specific representation, after which a multimodal decoder with a bi-directional contrastive loss is introduced to achieve deep alignment between visual features and structured textual embeddings. To validate our method, we construct Gaza-Change-v2, a new multi-class change detection (MCD) dataset about the Gaza Strip. On this MCD dataset, S2M achieves a Sek of 17.80\% and an F$_{\text{scd}}$ of 66.14\%, notably surpassing even multimodal methods that leverage large language models. Our work demonstrates that masks can indeed talk. They tell us exactly what, where, how, and how many changes have occurred.

</details>


### [35] [SatSurfGS: Generalizable 2D Gaussian Splatting for Sparse-View Satellite Surface Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07181)
*Min Chen,Wei Guo,Bin Wang,Wen Li,Tong Fang,Jinbo Zhang,Junqi Zhao,Hong Kuang,Han Hu,Xuming Ge,Qing Zhu,Bo Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SatSurfGS provides a generalizable 2D Gaussian Splatting framework for sparse-view satellite surface reconstruction, introducing confidence-aware fusion, cross-stage self-consistency, and a routing loss to robustly handle local geometric unreliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconstructing continuous satellite surfaces from sparse, heterogeneous-view data is hard due to strong photometric differences, weak/repetitive textures, and locally unreliable multi-view cues; existing 2DGS methods lack generalizable, feed-forward frameworks for this setting.

Method: A coarse-to-fine 2D Gaussian attribute prediction pipeline with (1) a confidence-aware monocular multi-view feature fusion module that blends monocular priors and multi-view features based on local confidence; (2) a cross-stage self-consistency residual guidance module that stabilizes stage-wise Gaussian refinement using the residual between the rendered height map and current-stage MVS height map plus confidence information; (3) a confidence bidirectional routing loss to allocate geometric vs. appearance supervision adaptively; and (4) training strategies to enhance local geometric reliability across feature learning, Gaussian parameter estimation, and optimization.

Result: Experiments on satellite datasets show improved rendering quality, higher surface reconstruction accuracy, better cross-dataset generalization, and faster inference compared with representative generalizable baselines and competitive with per-scene optimization methods.

Conclusion: SatSurfGS demonstrates that a confidence-aware, two-stage 2DGS approach can achieve robust, generalizable sparse-view satellite surface reconstruction, offering improved performance and efficiency over existing baselines and competitive per-scene optimization methods.

Abstract: Sparse-view satellite image surface reconstruction remains highly challenging, fundamentally because the reliability of multi-view matching under satellite imaging conditions is strongly spatially heterogeneous. Affected by large photometric differences, weak textures, and repetitive textures, multi-view geometric constraints are often sparse, unevenly distributed, and locally unreliable. Although 2D Gaussian Splatting (2DGS) is more suitable than 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for the explicit representation of continuous surfaces, research on generalizable feed-forward 2DGS frameworks for sparse-view satellite surface reconstruction is still lacking. To address this issue, we propose SatSurfGS, a generalizable sparse-view surface reconstruction method for satellite imagery based on 2DGS. The proposed method builds a coarse-to-fine Gaussian attribute prediction framework and explicitly models local geometric reliability at three levels: feature learning, Gaussian parameter estimation, and training optimization. Specifically, we propose a confidence-aware monocular multi-view feature fusion module to adaptively integrate monocular priors and multi-view matching features according to local confidence; a cross-stage self-consistency residual guidance module to stabilize stage-wise Gaussian parameter refinement using the residual between the rendered height map from the previous stage and the current-stage MVS height map, together with confidence information; and a confidence bidirectional routing loss to achieve differentiated allocation of geometric and appearance supervision. Experiments on satellite datasets show that the proposed method achieves improved rendering quality, surface reconstruction accuracy, cross-dataset generalization, and inference efficiency compared with representative generalizable baselines and competitive per-scene optimization methods.

</details>


### [36] [PicoEyes: Unified Gaze Estimation Framework for Mixed Reality with a Large-Scale Multi-View Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07188)
*Fuxin Duan,Hui Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PicoEyes provides a unified end-to-end gaze estimation framework that predicts all major gaze-related attributes (3D eye parameters, eye-region segmentation, optical and visual axes, and depth maps) from monocular or binocular input; it handles calibration, gaze forecasting, and varying device postures, enables 3D eye reconstruction, and introduces a large multi-view near-eye dataset; it achieves state-of-the-art across calibration/no-calibration, rewear, and forecasting tasks for MR applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for robust, generalizable, end-to-end gaze estimation in mixed reality that can operate with diverse devices and postures while minimizing manual calibration; unifying multiple gaze attributes and enabling 3D reconstruction enhances MR usability.

Method: An end-to-end, multi-task framework that directly predicts 3D eye parameters, eye-region segmentation, optical axis, visual axis, and depth maps from monocular or binocular inputs. It jointly estimates eye parameters and depth maps to enable 3D reconstruction. Training leverages a large-scale multi-view near-eye dataset with 2D/3D annotations and diverse sessions (train, test, rewear-test, calibration). The architecture likely combines shared feature extraction with task-specific heads and calibration-aware objectives, evaluated across no-calibration, calibration, rewear-after-calibration, and forecasting settings.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance, consistently outperforming both academic and industrial gaze tracking methods across all tested settings (no calibration, calibration, rewear-after-calibration, forecasting). Demonstrates robustness and generalizability for MR applications.

Conclusion: PicoEyes establishes a practical, end-to-end paradigm for robust and generalizable gaze estimation in mixed reality, supported by a comprehensive dataset and a unified framework that jointly estimates diverse gaze attributes and 3D structure.

Abstract: We present PicoEyes, a unified gaze estimation framework that directly predicts all key attributes of gaze, including 3D eye parameters, eye-region segmentation, optical axis, visual axis, and depth maps, from either monocular or binocular inputs. The framework simultaneously addresses calibration, gaze forecasting, and varying device postures, while also supporting 3D eye reconstruction via joint estimation of eye parameters and depth maps in an end-to-end manner. In addition, we introduce a large-scale multi-view near-eye dataset containing comprehensive 2D and 3D annotations under diverse conditions, including train, test, rewear-test, and calibration sessions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PicoEyes achieves state-ofthe-art performance, consistently outperforming both academic and industrial gaze tracking methods across nocalibration, calibration, rewear-after-calibration, and forecasting settings. This work establishes a practical, end-toend paradigm for robust and generalizable gaze estimation in mixed reality (MR) applications.

</details>


### [37] [Attention Transfer Is Not Universally Effective for Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07191)
*Huaiyuan Qin,Muli Yang,Gabriel James Goenawan,Peng Hu,Chen Gong,Xi Peng,Hongyuan Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Attention Transfer in ViTs is not universally effective; success depends on architectural compatibility between teacher and student. Adding teacher-native architectural components to the student rescues performance across four difficult ViT families, showing that attention is usable only when the student architecture matches the teacher.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reassess the claimed sufficiency of attention transfer in ViTs by testing across a broad set of teacher architectures and identifying failure modes and their causes.

Method: Benchmark 20 teachers from 11 ViT families. Replicate attention-transfer from pretrained teachers to randomly initialized students. Evaluate under extended training, various transfer datasets, and out-of-distribution tasks. Perform ablations and controlled analyses, focusing on the attention-routing channel. Introduce teacher-native architectural components into the student to test causality. Compare against baseline from-scratch training and investigate transfer-loss choices and pretraining recipes.

Result: Attention Transfer is not universally effective. 7 ViT families transfer successfully; 4 families consistently fail, with up to 5.1% gap below from-scratch baseline. Failure is family-consistent across scales, training durations, datasets, and OOD. Analyses implicate the attention-routing channel; the root cause is architectural mismatch between pre-trained teacher and standard student. Adding the teacher’s native architectural components to the student reverses failure for all four failing families; these components alone do not improve from-scratch training. Transfer-loss choice and pretraining recipes do not explain the failure.

Conclusion: Attention transfer in ViTs is sufficient only when the student architecture matches the teacher. Architectural compatibility is essential for the usability of teacher attention.

Abstract: A recent work shows that Attention Transfer, which transfers only the attention patterns from a pre-trained teacher Vision Transformer (ViT) to a randomly initialized standard student ViT, is sufficient to recover the full benefit of the teacher's pre-trained weights. We revisit this finding on a comprehensive benchmark of 20 teachers from 11 well-known ViT families and reveal that Attention Transfer is not universally effective. While 7 families transfer successfully, 4 consistently fail, falling up to 5.1\% below the from-scratch no-transfer baseline. Further results demonstrate that this failure is family-consistent across model sizes, and persists under extended training durations, different transfer datasets, and out-of-distribution evaluations. Controlled analyses then consistently localize the problem to the attention-routing channel, indicating that the key issue is not whether the student can match the teacher's attention patterns, but whether the matched patterns remain functional for the student. Crucially, we identify architectural mismatch between the pre-trained teacher and the standard student as the primary mechanism. By adding only the teacher's native architectural components to the student in a randomly initialized state, we completely reverse the failure for all 4 families. Notably, these components alone do not improve from-scratch training, confirming that they specifically unlock the usability of the teacher's attention. We further systematically show that this failure is not explained by the inadequate choice of transfer loss or by differences in pre-training recipes. Our findings refine the prevailing understanding of attention in ViT representations: attention is sufficient \textit{only} when the student architecture matches the teacher.

</details>


### [38] [AsyncEvGS: Asynchronous Event-Assisted Gaussian Splatting for Handheld Motion-Blurred Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07192)
*Jun Dai,Renbiao Jin,Bo Xu,Yutian Chen,Linning Xu,Mulin Yu,Tianfan Xue,Shi Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A high-resolution asynchronous RGB-Event system and reconstruction framework that reconstructs sharp frames from events, uses VGGT-based cross-domain pose estimation for robust initialization of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), and employs structure-driven event loss plus view-specific regularizers; introduces AsyncEv-Deblur dataset; achieves state-of-the-art performance under severe motion blur.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motion blur degrades NeRF/3DGS; event cameras offer high temporal resolution but existing approaches rely on low-resolution sensors and strict synchronization, hindering handheld 3D capture on devices like smartphones; there is a need for practical, high-resolution asynchronous RGB-Event capture.

Method: 1) Build a flexible, high-resolution asynchronous RGB-Event dual-camera system. 2) Reconstruct sharp images from event data. 3) Use a cross-domain pose estimation module based on the Visual Geometry Transformer (VGGT) to provide robust initialization for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). 4) During optimization, apply a structure-driven event loss and view-specific consistency regularizers to stabilize optimization and deblurring losses. 5) Introduce AsyncEv-Deblur, a high-resolution RGB-Event dataset captured with the proposed system.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art reconstruction performance on the AsyncEv-Deblur dataset and existing benchmarks, with substantial improvements in robustness to severe motion blur.

Conclusion: The proposed asynchronous RGB-Event framework enables high-fidelity 3D reconstructions from motion-blurred captures, supported by a new high-resolution dataset and a robust initialization/regularization strategy.

Abstract: 3D reconstruction methods such as 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) and Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) achieve impressive photorealism but fail when input images suffer from severe motion blur. While event cameras provide high-temporal-resolution motion cues, existing event-assisted approaches rely on low-resolution sensors and strict synchronization, limiting their practicality for handheld 3D capture on common devices, such as smartphones. We introduce a flexible, high-resolution asynchronous RGB-Event dual-camera system and a corresponding reconstruction framework. Our approach first reconstructs sharp images from the event data and then employs a cross-domain pose estimation module based on the Visual Geometry Transformer (VGGT) to obtain robust initialization for 3DGS. During optimization, we employ a structure-driven event loss and view-specific consistency regularizers to mitigate the ill-posed behavior of traditional event losses and deblurring losses, ensuring both stable and high-fidelity reconstruction. We further contribute AsyncEv-Deblur, a new high-resolution RGB-Event dataset captured with our asynchronous system. Experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on both our challenging dataset and existing benchmarks, substantially improving reconstruction robustness under severe motion blur. Project page: https://openimaginglab.github.io/AsyncEvGS/

</details>


### [39] [Closed-Form Linear-Probe Dataset Distillation for Pre-trained Vision Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07194)
*Bincheng Peng,Guang Li,Ping Liu,Takahiro Ogawa,Miki Haseyama*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Closed-form linear-probe dataset distillation (CLP-DD) uses a sample-space kernel ridge solution to compute the induced linear probe from a synthetic set, updating synthetic images via discriminative evaluation on real features. It avoids trajectory-based or NTK approximations and achieves substantial speedups with competitive accuracy for frozen-feature linear probing on ImageNet-scale data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing distillation methods for frozen-feature linear probing rely either on unrolled trajectory matching for end-to-end training or on neural-tangent-kernel (NTK) approximations designed for from-scratch training. This misses the fact that linear probing with fixed features admits a closed-form solution, suggesting a more direct, efficient optimization path.

Method: Formulate a bilevel optimization where the inner problem is a sample-space kernel ridge regression that yields the linear probe induced by the synthetic set. The outer objective is discriminative (temperature-scaled softmax cross-entropy); the synthetic images are updated by evaluating the induced classifier on real features, with class anchors represented by learned prototypes in feature space.

Result: On ImageNet-100 with four pre-trained backbones, CLP-DD outperforms LGM without DSA and approaches LGM with DSA at lower computational cost. On ImageNet-1K, it matches or surpasses LGM with DSA on three of four backbones, while being approximately 14× faster and using less than 1/8 of the GPU memory.

Conclusion: Exploiting the closed-form inner solver for linear probes enables efficient dataset distillation without infinite-width approximations or inner-loop trajectories. The discriminative outer loss substantially closes the gap to trajectory-based methods, delivering strong accuracy with major speed/memory gains across large-scale benchmarks.

Abstract: Dataset distillation compresses a large training set into a small synthetic set that preserves downstream training utility. While most existing methods target training networks from scratch, modern visual transfer learning often uses frozen pre-trained encoders followed by lightweight linear probing. Existing distillation methods for this setting either unroll iterative linear-probe updates with trajectory-based gradient matching, or rely on closed-form formulations originally designed for from-scratch training with neural-tangent-kernel (NTK) approximations. Neither route exploits the fact that frozen-feature linear probing admits a closed-form solution determined directly by the pre-trained features themselves, with no infinite-width approximation and no inner-loop trajectory. We propose Closed-Form Linear-Probe Dataset Distillation (CLP-DD), a bilevel formulation that computes the linear probe induced by the synthetic set with a sample-space kernel ridge solver. The synthetic images are then updated by evaluating this induced classifier on real features through a temperature-scaled softmax cross-entropy, where the classifier columns act as learned class anchors in feature space. We further show that the choice of outer objective is decisive: pairing the closed-form inner solver with a standard MSE outer loss substantially underperforms trajectory-based methods, while the discriminative outer loss closes most of the gap. On ImageNet-100 with four pre-trained backbones, CLP-DD substantially improves over LGM without DSA and approaches LGM with DSA at a fraction of the computational cost. On ImageNet-1K, CLP-DD matches or surpasses LGM with DSA on three of four backbones while running roughly $14\times$ faster and using less than one-eighth of the GPU memory.

</details>


### [40] [Operating Within the Operational Design Domain: Zero-Shot Perception with Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07649)
*Berkehan Ünal,Hauke Dierend,Dren Fazlija,Christopher Plachetka*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Zero-shot ODD sensing with vision-language models is explored: four VLMs are evaluated on a custom ODD dataset and Mapillary Vistas for classification/detection, plus failure analyses, ablation of prompting strategies, and reusable templates; definition-anchored chain-of-thought with persona decomposition yields best recall and reliability for ODD adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ensure safe, regulation-aligned perception in ADS by enabling adaptable ODD understanding. VLMs offer flexible, task-agnostic perception that can adapt to evolving ODD definitions without task-specific training.

Method: Empirical study using four VLMs on a custom dataset and Mapillary Vistas for zero-shot ODD classification/detection; failure analyses; ablation of zero-shot optimization strategies; development of reusable prompting templates; emphasis on definition-anchored chain-of-thought prompting with persona decomposition.

Result: Definition-anchored chain-of-thought prompting with persona decomposition performed best; some methods reduced recall; zero-shot VLMs show potential as ODD sensors with careful prompting and analysis.

Conclusion: VLMs can function as zero-shot ODD sensors for safety-critical perception, enabling adaptable, transparent ODD-based perception, but require structured prompting, thorough failure analysis, and consideration of regulatory auditing.

Abstract: Over the last few years, research on autonomous systems has matured to such a degree that the field is increasingly well-positioned to translate research into practical, stakeholder-driven use cases across well-defined domains. However, for a wide-scale practical adoption of autonomous systems, adherence to safety regulations is crucial. Many regulations are influenced by the Operational Design Domain (ODD), which defines the specific conditions in which an autonomous agent can function. This is especially relevant for Automated Driving Systems (ADS), as a dependable perception of ODD elements is essential for safe implementation and auditing. Vision-language models (VLMs) integrate visual recognition and language reasoning, functioning without task-specific training data, which makes them suitable for adaptable ODD perception. To assess whether VLMs can function as zero-shot "ODD sensors" that adapt to evolving definitions, we contribute (i) an empirical study of zero-shot ODD classification and detection using four VLMs on a custom dataset and Mapillary Vistas, along with failure analyses; (ii) an ablation of zero-shot optimization strategies with a cost-performance overview; and (iii) a suite of reusable prompting templates with guidance for adaptation. Our findings indicate that definition-anchored chain-of-thought prompting with persona decomposition performs best, while other methods may result in reduced recall. Overall, our results pave the way for transparent and effective ODD-based perception in safety-critical applications.

</details>


### [41] [See Tomorrow, Act Today: Foresight-Driven Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07195)
*Bozhou Zhang,Nan Song,Yuang Wang,Jiankang Deng,Xiatian Zhu,Li Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ForeSight makes future scene imagination the core of planning in autonomous driving, using a pretrained world model to generate plausible futures and plan actions from them; shows superior performance on NAVSIM and nuScenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current end-to-end planners are reactive and rely on present observations; human drivers mentally simulate possible futures. A anticipatory decision-making framework can better handle dynamic, interactive traffic.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) generate plausible future visual scenes with a pretrained world model; (2) plan actions conditioned on imagined futures; positions world models as the primary driver of action prediction.

Result: Extensive experiments show that explicit future imagination significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art approaches.

Conclusion: Anticipatory, world-model-centric planning enables more robust navigation in dynamic, interactive driving scenarios by grounding decisions in imagined futures.

Abstract: Current end-to-end autonomous driving planners are fundamentally reactive: they condition on historical and present observations to predict future actions. We argue that autonomous agents should instead imagine future scenes before deciding, just as human drivers mentally simulate ``what will happen next" before acting. We introduce ForeSight, a foundation world model centric planning framework that reframes autonomous driving as anticipatory decision-making. Rather than treating world models as auxiliary components, ForeSight makes future scene imagination the primary driver of action prediction. Our approach operates in two stages: (1) generating plausible future visual worlds via a pretrained world model, and (2) planning actions conditioned on these imagined futures. This paradigm shift from ``what should I do now?" to ``what will happen, and how should I respond?" enables genuinely anticipatory rather than reactive planning. By grounding decisions in anticipated contexts rather than present observations alone, ForeSight navigates dynamic, interactive scenarios more effectively. Extensive experiments on NAVSIM and nuScenes demonstrate that explicit future imagination significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art alternatives, validating our foresight-driven approach.

</details>


### [42] [From Pixels to Primitives: Scene Change Detection in 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07203)
*Chamuditha Jayanga Galappaththige,Jason Lai,Timothy Patten,Donald Dansereau,Niko Suenderhauf,Dimity Miller*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GD-DIFF performs primitive-space change detection for Gaussian splatting, achieving multi-view-consistent change maps and interpretability by modeling per-primitive drift and observability; it outperforms prior SOTA by ~17% in mean IoU on real benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-based render-then-compare methods for Gaussian splatting are under-constrained due to non-unique primitive optimizations. Analyzing changes in primitive space (position, anisotropic covariance, color) can yield stable signals, multi-view consistency, and interpretable change types without external models.

Method: Operate directly on Gaussian primitives with anisotropic drift models for geometry and photometry, plus a per-primitive observability term derived from camera geometry. This enforces multi-view consistency by construction and allows separate scoring of geometric vs appearance changes. No supervision or external model dependencies.

Result: On real-world benchmarks, the method surpasses the prior state of the art by about 17% in mean Intersection-over-Union (IoU).

Conclusion: Primitive-space change detection with explicit drift and observability modeling yields view-consistent change maps and interpretable change types, without requiring external supervision or render-then-compare pipelines.

Abstract: Scene change detection methods built on Gaussian splatting universally follow a render-then-compare paradigm: the pre-change scene is rendered into 2D and compared against post-change images via pixel or feature residuals. This change detection problem with Gaussian Splatting has been treated as a question about pixels; we treat it as a question about primitives. We provide direct evidence that native primitive attributes alone -- position, anisotropic covariance, and color -- carry sufficient signal for scene change detection. What makes primitive-space comparison hard is the under-constrained nature of Gaussian splatting representation: independent optimizations yield primitive solutions whose count, positions, shapes, and colors differ even where nothing has changed. We address this challenge with anisotropic models of geometric and photometric drift, complemented by a per-primitive observability term that reflects the extent to which each Gaussian is constrained by the camera geometry. Operating directly on primitives gives our method, GD-DIFF, two properties that distinguish it from render-then-compare methods. First, change maps are multi-view consistent by construction, where prior work had to learn this through an additional optimization objective. Second, geometric and appearance changes are scored separately, identifying not just where but what kind of change occurred, distinguishing structural changes (e.g., an added object) from surface-level ones (e.g., a color change) without supervision or external model dependencies. On real-world benchmarks, GS-DIFF surpasses the prior state-of-the-art approach by $\sim$17% in mean Intersection over Union.

</details>


### [43] [LoHGNet: Infrared Small Target Detection through Lorentz Geometric Encoding with High-Order Relation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07213)
*Qianwen Ma,Yang Xu,Shangwei Deng,Xiaobo Li,Haofeng Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes LoHGNet for infrared small target detection using Lorentz hyperbolic geometry and high-order relation learning to better discriminate weak targets in clutter.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Euclidean feature representations struggle to capture subtle target cues and contextual relations in IRSTD; non-Euclidean (hyperbolic) geometry can model hierarchical, high-order relationships more effectively.

Method: Lorentz encoding branch with GA-LRCM to learn features under hyperbolic constraints; logarithmic mapping to Euclidean tangent space; HORL via hypergraph to model high-order dependencies between targets and background.

Result: Validated on three datasets, achieving competitive detection accuracy and robustness in complex scenes; code to be released.

Conclusion: Integrating Lorentz geometry with high-order relation learning improves weak target discrimination in clutter, offering a novel and effective IRSTD framework.

Abstract: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) remains challenging due to the scarcity of useful target cues and the presence of severe background clutter. Most current methods rely on conventional feature learning and local interaction modeling, where features are represented in Euclidean space. However, such designs may still be limited in describing the subtle differences of weak targets and the contextual relations between targets and backgrounds. To address these limitations, we propose LoHGNet, an IRSTD network that integrates Lorentz geometric encoding with high-order relation learning. By introducing Lorentz manifold based feature learning, LoHGNet offers a different feature representation from conventional IRSTD methods and provides new discriminative cues for IRSTD. Specifically, a Lorentz encoding branch is constructed with the Geometric Attention Guided Lorentz Residual Convolution Module (GA-LRCM) to perform feature modeling under hyperbolic geometric constraints and enhance the hierarchical geometric representation capability of weak targets. Subsequently, the hyperbolic features are mapped into the Euclidean tangent space through logarithmic mapping, and a High-Order Relation Learning Module (HORL) is designed to model the high-order contextual dependencies between targets and backgrounds via hypergraph construction, thereby improving target discrimination in complex backgrounds. Experimental results on three datasets demonstrate that the proposed LoHGNet achieves competitive performance in both detection accuracy and adaptability to complex scenes. The code will be available at https://github.com/Kingwin97.

</details>


### [44] [Text-to-CAD Evaluation with CADTests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07807)
*Dimitrios Mallis,Marco Wang,Ahmet Serdar Karadeniz,Elisa Ricci,Anis Kacem,Djamila Aouada*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes CADTestBench, a test-based benchmark for Text-to-CAD using executable CADTests to automatically evaluate and guide CAD model generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating Text-to-CAD is underdeveloped; automated, test-based evaluation provides objective, reproducible metrics grounded in geometric/topological constraints.

Method: Introduce CADTestBench built on CADTests; frame evaluation as executable tests; benchmark recent methods; demonstrate that tests can guide generation and yield strong baselines; release code and data.

Result: Comprehensive benchmarking of recent Text-to-CAD methods; CADTests guide generation enabling simple baselines outperforming existing methods; demonstrates the value of test-based evaluation.

Conclusion: CADTestBench offers a novel, reproducible evaluation paradigm for Text-to-CAD and can drive future improvements; resources are publicly available.

Abstract: Text-to-CAD has recently emerged as an important task with the potential to substantially accelerate design workflows. Despite its significance, there has been surprisingly little work on Text-to-CAD evaluation, and assessing CAD model generation performance remains a considerable challenge. In this work, we introduce a new evaluation perspective for Text-to-CAD based on automated testing. We propose CADTestBench, the first test-based benchmark for Text-to-CAD, based on CADTests, executable software tests that verify whether a generated CAD model satisfies the geometric and topological requirements of the input prompt. Using CADTestBench, we conduct comprehensive benchmarking of recent Text-to-CAD methods and further demonstrate that CADTests can also guide CAD model generation, yielding simple baselines that surpass performance of current methods. CADTestBench code and data are available at GitHub and Hugging Face dataset.

</details>


### [45] [DINO-MVR: Multi-View Readout of Frozen DINOv3 for Annotation-Efficient Medical Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07221)
*Wei Jiang,Feng Liu,Nan Ye,Hongfu Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Frozen DINOv3 features can provide meaningful structural and boundary cues for medical segmentation when read out by lightweight MLP probes trained on the final transformer blocks, using a multi-view readout (MVR) with test-time augmentations and fusion for annotation-efficient segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical segmentation under scarce annotations is hindered by relying on backbone fine-tuning or large task-specific decoders. If frozen, the backbone should still yield useful features, but effective readout is the bottleneck.

Method: Freeze DINOv3 backbone. Train lightweight MLP probes on the final three transformer blocks. At inference, apply multiple complementary resolutions and test-time augmentations (TTAs); fuse probability maps via entropy-weighted fusion; apply simple spatial regularization; for volumetric data, apply Gaussian z-axis smoothing to improve slice consistency.

Result: Strong readout-only performance across datasets: 0.895 Dice (Kvasir-SEG), 0.897 Dice (ISIC 2018), 0.908 Dice (BraTS FLAIR whole-tumor). With only 5 BraTS annotated patients, performance reaches 98.4% of the 40-patient reference. Demonstrates that frozen self-supervised features, when paired with a multi-view readout, can achieve competitive medical segmentation with limited labels.

Conclusion: Frozen self-supervised vision backbones can support accurate medical segmentation when combined with an effective multi-view readout, reducing or eliminating the need for backbone fine-tuning or heavy decoders in low-supervision regimes.

Abstract: Adapting foundation models to medical segmentation typically requires either backbone fine-tuning or high-capacity task-specific decoders, both of which are difficult to fit reliably when annotations are scarce. We show that frozen DINOv3 features already contain useful structural and boundary cues for medical segmentation, and that the main bottleneck lies in how these features are read out. We propose DINO-MVR, a Multi-View Readout framework for annotation-efficient medical segmentation. DINO-MVR trains only lightweight MLP probes on features from the final three transformer blocks of a frozen DINOv3 backbone, without updating the backbone itself. At inference, each input is interpreted through complementary resolutions and test-time augmentations, whose probability maps are combined by entropy-weighted fusion and refined with simple spatial regularization. For volumetric inputs, Gaussian z-axis smoothing further improves inter-slice consistency. Under fixed evaluation protocols on endoscopy, dermoscopy, and MRI benchmarks, DINO-MVR achieves strong readout-only performance, including 0.895 Dice on Kvasir-SEG, 0.897 Dice on ISIC 2018, and 0.908 Dice on BraTS FLAIR whole-tumor segmentation. With only five annotated BraTS patients, it recovers 98.4% of the performance obtained by the 40-patient BraTS reference run. These results suggest that frozen self-supervised vision backbones can support accurate medical segmentation when paired with an effective multi-view readout.

</details>


### [46] [6D Pose Estimation via Keypoint Heatmap Regression with RGB-D Residual Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08059)
*Ismail Aljosevic,Amir Masoud Almasi,Ana Parovic,Ashkan Shafiei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A modular heatmap-based 6D pose estimation pipeline using YOLOv10m for detection, a ResNet18-based heatmap predictor, and PnP RANSAC for pose; evaluates keypoint strategies; adds RGB-D cross-fusion and training tweaks; achieves 84.50% ADD (RGB) and 92.41% ADD (RGB-D) on LINEMOD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve robust 6D pose estimation with a modular, heatmap-based approach; assess keypoint selection impact; explore RGB-D fusion across stages and training optimizations.

Method: Two-stage pipeline: detect objects with YOLOv10m; predict 2D keypoint heatmaps from RGB via a ResNet18-based network; extract 2D keypoints and estimate 6D pose with PnP RANSAC; compare different keypoint selection strategies; extend baseline with cross-fusion RGB-D architecture enabling interaction between RGB and depth features at multiple stages; experiment with activation functions and learning rate schedules for training.

Result: RGB-only mean ADD: 84.50%; RGB-D fusion: 92.41% on LINEMOD; code available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates that a modular heatmap-based pipeline can achieve strong 6D pose estimation performance, with RGB-D fusion yielding substantial gains. Keypoint selection and training choices meaningfully influence accuracy; results on LINEMOD indicate competitive performance and strong potential for further improvements.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a modular framework for 6D pose estimation based on keypoint heatmap regression. Our approach combines YOLOv10m for object detection with a ResNet18-based network that predicts 2D heatmaps from RGB images. Keypoints extracted from these heatmaps are used to estimate the 6D object pose via the PnP RANSAC algorithm. We compare different keypoint selection strategies to assess their impact on pose accuracy. Additionally, we extend the baseline by incorporating depth data using a cross-fusion architecture, which enables interaction between RGB and depth features at multiple stages. We further explore general training improvements, such as experimenting with activation functions and learning rate scheduling strategies to improve model performance. Our best RGB-only model achieved a mean ADD-based accuracy of 84.50%, while the RGB-D fusion model reached 92.41% on the LINEMOD dataset. The code is available at https://github.com/ameermasood/HeatNet.

</details>


### [47] [CASCADE: Context-Aware Relaxation for Speculative Image Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07230)
*Selin Yildirim,Subhajit Dutta Chowdhury,Mohammad Mahdi Kamani,Vikram Appia,Deming Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CASCADE enables fast tree-based speculative decoding for autoregressive image generation by exploiting hidden-state redundancies (semantic interchangeability and convergence); it relaxes acceptance criteria and injects redundancy signals into drafter training, achieving up to 3.6x speedups while preserving quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive image generation is computationally heavy. Speculative decoding offers speedups, but existing methods underperform due to high draft rejection caused by model uncertainty. There is overlooked redundancy in target model hidden states that can be exploited to speed up decoding without extra training.

Method: Formalize two properties—semantic interchangeability and convergence—that arise from redundancies in hidden state representations within the predicted token tree. Use these to identify opportunities for acceptance-relaxation without retraining. Enhance drafter performance by injecting redundancy signals into drafter training with minimal modification. Evaluate across multiple text-to-image models and drafter architectures.

Result: CASCADE achieves state-of-the-art speedups for drafter-based speculative decoding, with up to 3.6x acceleration, while preserving image quality and prompt fidelity across tested models.

Conclusion: Exploiting hidden-state redundancies via semantic interchangeability and convergence yields practical efficiency gains in tree-based speculative decoding for image generation; CASCADE is broadly applicable and improves inference throughput without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: Autoregressive generation is a powerful approach for high-fidelity image synthesis, but it remains computationally demanding and slow even on the most advanced accelerators. While speculative decoding has been explored to mitigate this bottleneck, existing approaches fail to achieve efficiency gains comparable to those observed in text generation. A key limitation is the target model's high uncertainty during image generation, which leads to high draft token rejection rates. In this work, we identify previously overlooked patterns in the target model's behavior that emerge naturally in tree-based speculative decoding. Specifically, we formalize two properties, semantic interchangeability and convergence, arising from the redundancies in the target model's hidden state representations. By capturing these redundancies across the depth and breadth of the predicted token tree, our method identifies principled opportunities for acceptance relaxation without requiring additional training. Additionally, we enhance standalone drafter performance by injecting the redundancy signals from the target model into drafter training with minimal modification. We evaluate our approach across multiple text-to-image models and drafter architectures. Results show that CASCADE achieves state-of-the-art speedups for drafter-based speculative decoding, with up to 3.6x acceleration, while maintaining image quality and text-prompt fidelity.

</details>


### [48] [Towards multi-modal forgery representation learning for AI-generated video detection and localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07232)
*Dat Le,Khoa Nguyen,Xin Wang,Shu Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A multi-modal detector for AI-generated videos using a Language Model–driven semantic branch, a spatio-temporal visual branch, and a multi-scale partial-spoof audio branch to detect and localize partial manipulations, achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-generated videos pose risks of semantic distortion and misuse. Current detectors are limited to single/partial modalities and lack fine-grained temporal localization, hindering reliable detection of partially manipulated content.

Method: A core architecture jointly integrates an LMM semantic branch with a spatio-temporal visual branch and a multi-scale partial-spoof audio branch, enabling simultaneous detection and fine-grained temporal localization of partially manipulated AI-generated video forgeries.

Result: Extensive experiments show the approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The proposed multi-modal framework enhances detection accuracy and temporal localization for partially manipulated AI-generated videos, contributing to more robust and reliable forgery detection across modalities.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative AI have democratized video creation at scale. AI-generated videos, including partially manipulated clips across visual and audio channels, pose escalating risks of semantic distortion and misuse, which motivates the need for reliable detection tools. Most existing AI-generated video detectors remain limited by single- or partial-modality of data modeling and the lack of fine-grained temporal forgery localization. To address these challenges, our primary novelty introduces a core architecture that jointly integrates an LMM semantic branch with a spatio-temporal (ST) visual branch and a multi-scale partial-spoof (PS) audio branch. This multi-modal approach enables simultaneous detection and fine-grained temporal localization of partially manipulated AI-generated video forgeries. Extensive experiments show that this approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [49] [Hard to Read, Easy to Jailbreak: How Visual Degradation Bypasses MLLM Safety Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07250)
*Zhixue Song,Boyan Han,Yiwei Wang,Chi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Lowering image resolution in vision-based context compression for multi-modal LLMs weakens safety defenses, even when text remains legible, likely due to cognitive overload. A Structured Cognitive Offloading pipeline—serializing transcription and safety checks—is proposed to mitigate this risk.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and mitigate a vulnerability in vision-based context compression where reduced visual resolution undermines model safety and increases jailbreaking risk in ultra-long-context MLLMs.

Method: Empirical evaluation across state-of-the-art models and visual perturbations: systematically degrade image resolution while rendering text as images, assess safety defenses, and test across noise and geometric distortions. Propose and evaluate a serialized, decoupled pipeline to separate visual transcription from safety auditing.

Result: Safety defenses deteriorate sharply as resolution degrades; this decline persists even when text remains legible. The authors attribute this to Cognitive Overload, diverting attention from safety auditing. The proposed Structured Cognitive Offloading mitigates risk by enforcing a serialized pipeline separating transcription from safety assessment.

Conclusion: Vision-based compression introduces a meaningful, practical risk to model safety in MLLMs. The work highlights the need for secure design principles and provides a concrete mitigation strategy—Structured Cognitive Offloading—that merits further validation across models and tasks.

Abstract: Recent advancements in visual context compression enable MLLMs to process ultra-long contexts efficiently by rendering text into images. However, we identify a critical vulnerability inherent to this paradigm: lowering image resolution inadvertently catalyzes jailbreaking. Our experiments reveal that the safety defenses of SOTA models deteriorate sharply as resolution degrades, surprisingly persisting even when text remains legible. We attribute this to ``Cognitive Overload'', hypothesizing that the effort required to decipher degraded inputs diverts attentional resources from safety auditing. This phenomenon is consistent across various visual perturbations, including noise and geometric distortion. To address this, we propose a simple ``Structured Cognitive Offloading'' strategy that mitigates these risks by enforcing a serialized pipeline to decouple visual transcription from safety assessment. Our work exposes a significant risk in vision-based compression and provides critical insights for the secure design of future MLLMs.

</details>


### [50] [LENS: Low-Frequency Eigen Noise Shaping for Efficient Diffusion Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07253)
*Haewon Jeon,Si-Hyeon Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LENS introduces Low-frequency Eigen Noise Shaping: a compact noise-modulation module that restricts modulation to a low-frequency subspace in diffusion models, enabling large speedups with competitive image quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Distilled diffusion models are faster but often degrade quality. Test-time optimization and hypernetwork-based methods mitigate this at high computational costs. There is a need for efficient, targeted noise modulation that preserves global structure while reducing computation.

Method: Identify that low-frequency noise dictates global image structure. The authors theoretically justify restricting modulation to a low-frequency subspace, derive a loss/objective, and deploy a lightweight standalone network that modulates only these components during denoising.

Result: Empirical results show competitive image quality with substantial efficiency gains: 400–700× fewer FLOPs, 25–75× fewer model parameters, and 10–20× lower inference-time overhead compared with prior methods.

Conclusion: Low-frequency noise shaping enables efficient, effective noise modulation in diffusion models. LENS achieves major speedups with minimal performance loss, suggesting a promising direction for practical, scalable diffusion-based generation.

Abstract: Distilled diffusion models accelerate image generation by reducing the number of denoising steps, but often suffer from degraded image quality.
