To mitigate this trade-off, test-time optimization methods improve quality, yet their iterative nature incurs substantial computational overhead and leads to slow inference, limiting practical usability. Recent hypernetwork-based approaches amortize this process during training, but still require costly noise modulation in high-dimensional latent spaces. In this work, we propose LENS (Low-frequency Eigen Noise Shaping), an efficient noise modulation framework that operates in a low-dimensional subspace. Our approach is motivated by the observation that low-frequency components of the noise largely determine the global structure and visual fidelity of generated images. Based on this observation, we provide a theoretical justification for restricting modulation to the low-frequency subspace and derive a principled training objective. Building on this, LENS employs a lightweight, standalone network to selectively modulate these components, enabling efficient and targeted noise modulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LENS achieves competitive image quality while reducing FLOPs by 400-700$\times$, model parameters by 25-75$\times$, and inference-time overhead by 10-20$\times$ compared to prior methods.

</details>


### [51] [High-Fidelity Surface Splatting-Based 3D Reconstruction from Multi-View Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07254)
*Nandhana Sunil,Abhirami R Iyer,Avirup Mandal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Polynomial IMLS with local-support kernel and Laplacian regularization for multi-view reconstruction; enables end-to-end geometry/texture fields with improved high-frequency detail and state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the difficulty of recovering high-frequency geometry from sparse multi-view observations and the limitations of exponential kernels in IMLS; seeks end-to-end optimization without post-processing.

Method: Proposes a compact polynomial kernel with local support for IMLS to flexibly control frequency content; introduces stochastic regularization with Laplacian filtering; integrates into an end-to-end reconstruction and rendering pipeline from multi-view data.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in surface reconstruction and rendering, producing more accurate geometry and sharper visuals from multi-view data.

Conclusion: The combination of a local-support polynomial kernel and Laplacian-based regularization enhances high-frequency preservation and stabilizes optimization for end-to-end multi-view mesh reconstruction and rendering.

Abstract: Multi-view mesh reconstruction remains a core challenge in computer graphics and vision, especially for recovering high-frequency geometry from sparse observations. Recent methods such as 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) and Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) rely on post-processing for mesh extraction, thereby limiting joint optimization of geometry and appearance. Implicit Moving Least Squares (IMLS) instead enables direct conversion of point clouds into signed distance and texture fields, supporting end-to-end reconstruction and rendering. However, existing IMLS formulations use exponential kernels that struggle with high-frequency detail. We introduce a compact polynomial kernel with local support and greater flexibility, allowing better control over frequency content and improved geometric fidelity. To further enhance fine details, we incorporate stochastic regularization with Laplacian filtering. Together, these improve the preservation of high-frequency structure while maintaining stable optimization. Experiments show state-of-the-art performance in both surface reconstruction and rendering, yielding more accurate geometry and sharper visuals from multi-view data.

</details>


### [52] [TAS-LoRA: Transformer Architecture Search with Mixture-of-LoRA Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07256)
*Jeimin Jeon,Hyunju Lee,Bumsub Ham*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TAS-LoRA mitigates feature collapse in Transformer Architecture Search by adding parameter-efficient LoRA adapters and a Mixture-of-LoRAExperts with a group-wise router initialization; achieves state-of-the-art results on ImageNet and transfer benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In supernet-based TAS, shared weights cause subnet-specific features to be underlearned, hindering individual subnet performance. There is a need for a lightweight mechanism to enable subnet-specific feature learning without heavy cost.

Method: Introduce LoRA adapters into ViT subnets within a TAS supernet. Use a Mixture-of-LoRAExperts (MoLE) router to dynamically select LoRA experts conditioned on subnet architecture; apply group-wise router initialization to diversify early training. Maintain computational efficiency by keeping adapters parameter-efficient.

Result: Empirical results on ImageNet and transfer tasks (CIFAR-10/100, Flowers, CARS, INAT-19) show TAS-LoRA effectively reduces feature collapse and outperforms existing TAS methods.

Conclusion: TAS-LoRA provides a practical, efficient solution to feature collapse in TAS, enabling more robust subnet-specific learning and improved transfer performance; demonstrates potential for broader NAS for ViTs with LoRA-based adaptation.

Abstract: Transformer architecture search (TAS) discovers optimal vision transformer (ViT) architectures automatically, reducing human effort to manually design ViTs. However, existing TAS methods suffer from the feature collapse problem, where subnets within a supernet fail to learn subnet-specific features, mainly due to the shared weights in a supernet, limiting the performance of individual subnets. To address this, we propose TAS-LoRA, a novel method that introduces parameter-efficient low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to enable subnet-specific feature learning, while maintaining computational efficiency. TAS-LoRA incorporates a Mixture-of-LoRAExperts (MoLE) strategy, where a lightweight router dynamically assigns LoRA experts based on subnet architectures, and introduces a group-wise router initialization technique to encourage diverse feature learning across experts early in training. Extensive experiments on ImageNet and several transfer learning benchmarks, including CIFAR-10/100, Flowers, CARS, and INAT-19, demonstrate that TAS-LoRA mitigates feature collapse effectively, improving performance over state-of-the-art TAS methods significantly.

</details>


### [53] [Adaptive Subspace Projection for Generative Personalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07257)
*Van-Anh Nguyen,Anh Tuan Bui,Tamas Abraham,Junae Kim,Amardeep Kaur,Rollin Omari,Thuy-Trang Vu,Dinh Phung*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper identifies a semantic collapsing problem (SCP) in generative personalization, showing that semantic drift is concentrated in a low-dimensional subspace and that personalization destabilizes the base embedding. It proposes AdaptSP, a training-free method that anchors to the stable base embedding and projects drift onto the identified subspace for precise adjustment, reducing SCP while preserving identity. Experiments indicate improved prompt fidelity and contextual alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SCP degrades prompt fidelity and contextual control in personalized generative models. Existing solutions may require retraining or do not address the underlying drift of the embedding. A training-free, anchor-based, subspace-aware method could provide robust, efficient mitigation without altering the base model.

Method: Test-time Embedding Adjustment with Adaptive Subspace Projection (AdaptSP). It uses the stable pre-trained embedding as an anchor, identifies the drift subspace, projects the drift onto this subspace, and makes a targeted adjustment to mitigate SCP. The approach is training-free and aims to preserve the subject identity while improving contextual fidelity.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows that AdaptSP substantially improves prompt fidelity and contextual alignment compared to baselines, validating that isolating semantic drift in a low-dimensional subspace and anchoring to the base embedding is effective for mitigating SCP.

Conclusion: AdaptSP offers a practical, training-free solution to semantic collapsing in personalized generation by anchoring to the stable base embedding and orthogonally projecting semantic drift into a learned subspace, leading to better prompt fidelity and contextual alignment while preserving identity.

Abstract: Generative personalization often suffers from the semantic collapsing problem (SCP), where a learned personalized concept overpowers the rest of the text prompt, causing the model to ignore important contextual details. To address this, we first analyze the underlying cause, revealing that the semantic drift responsible for SCP is not random but is concentrated within a specific low-dimensional subspace. We also discover that the personalization process perturbs the embedding of the original base concept, making it an unstable reference point. Based on these insights, we introduce Test-time Embedding Adjustment with Adaptive Subspace Projection (AdaptSP), a training-free method that uses the stable, pre-trained embedding as an anchor. AdaptSP isolates the semantic drift and projects it onto the identified subspace, performing a precise adjustment that mitigates SCP while maintaining the subject identity. Our experiments show that this targeted approach significantly improves prompt fidelity and contextual alignment.

</details>


### [54] [From Clouds to Hallucinations: Atmospheric Retrieval Hijacking in Remote Sensing Vision-Language RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07273)
*Jiaju Han,Chao Li,Chengyin Hu,Qike Zhang,Xuemeng Sun,Xin Wang,Fengyu Zhang,Xiang Chen,Yiwei Wei,Jiahuan Long,Jiujiang Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CloudWeb, an input-space attack that overlays cloud/haze-like patterns on remote sensing images to hijack vision-language retrievers in multimodal RAG, without altering models or knowledge bases; achieves large degradation in retrieval and downstream generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess vulnerability of retrieval-stage evidence in remote sensing multimodal RAG to input-space perturbations; existing studies focus on corpus/memory or end-task attacks, leaving atmospheric-style retrieval hijacking underexplored.

Method: Optimize parameterized cloud- and haze-like overlays on remote sensing images using a retrieval-oriented objective. The loss pulls adversarial image embeddings toward target atmospheric evidence, suppresses source-scene evidence, enforces rank separation, and includes regularization for naturalness and coverage. Evaluated on seven remote sensing RAG datasets with five CLIP-style retrievers and downstream generators.

Result: CloudWeb consistently improves over clean retrieval, atmospheric baselines, random perturbations, and fixed variants in injecting weather-related evidence into top-ranked results. For GeoRSCLIP ViT-B/32, Weather@5 rises from 0.71% to 43.29%. Downstream generation shows weather-related hallucinations and semantic shifts, indicating propagation of retrieval hijacking to final RAG output.

Conclusion: Retrieval-stage atmospheric hijacking is a practical failure mode for remote sensing multimodal RAG. Natural-looking atmospheric changes can compromise retrieval before generation, highlighting the need for robust retrieval defenses and detection to mitigate such attacks.

Abstract: Multimodal RAG systems increasingly rely on vision-language retrievers to ground visual queries in external textual evidence. Existing adversarial studies on RAG mainly manipulate the retrieval corpus or memory, while attacks on vision-language and remote sensing models typically target end-task predictions. Input-space threats to the evidence retrieval stage of remote sensing multimodal RAG remain underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce CloudWeb, an atmospheric retrieval hijacking attack that modifies only the input image while keeping the retriever, generator, and knowledge base fixed at deployment. CloudWeb overlays parameterized cloud- and haze-like patterns on remote sensing images and optimizes them with a retrieval-oriented objective that pulls adversarial image embeddings toward target atmospheric evidence, suppresses source-scene evidence, enforces rank separation, and regularizes naturalness and coverage. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study of retrieval-stage atmospheric evidence hijacking in remote sensing multimodal RAG. We evaluate CloudWeb on a seven-dataset remote sensing RAG benchmark with five CLIP-style retrievers, including GeoRSCLIP, RemoteCLIP, OpenAI CLIP, and OpenCLIP, together with downstream vision-language generators. Across retrievers, CloudWeb consistently outperforms clean retrieval, handcrafted atmospheric baselines, random cloud perturbations, and fixed variants in injecting weather-related evidence into top-ranked results. On GeoRSCLIP ViT-B/32, Weather@5 increases from 0.71\% to 43.29\%. Downstream generation further shows measurable weather hallucination and semantic shift, indicating that retrieval-stage hijacking can propagate to the final RAG response. These findings reveal a practical failure mode: natural-looking atmospheric changes can compromise evidence retrieval before generation begins.

</details>


### [55] [SplatWeaver: Learning to Allocate Gaussian Primitives for Generalizable Novel View Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07287)
*Yecong Wan,Fan Li,Mingwen Shao,Wangmeng Zuo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SplatWeaver introduces region-aware allocation of Gaussian primitives for generalizable novel view synthesis by using cardinality experts and a pixel-level routing scheme, enabling adaptive primitive counts per location. It uses a high-frequency prior and routing regularization to focus detail where needed, claiming state-of-the-art performance with fewer primitives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform primitive budgets per pixel/voxel waste capacity in real scenes; need expressive yet compact 3D representations that adapt to local complexity without per-scene optimization.

Method: A set of cardinality Gaussian experts where each expert corresponds to a specific primitive count (0..M). A pixel-level routing mechanism selects and combines experts to allocate a variable number of Gaussians per location. A high-frequency prior with a guidance module stabilizes expert selection and encourages complexity-aware allocation; routing regularization promotes sparse, region-specific usage. This builds on 3D Gaussian splatting for feed-forward, generalizable view synthesis.

Result: Empirical evaluations across diverse scenarios show SplatWeaver achieves more faithful novel-view renderings using fewer Gaussian primitives than state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Region-aware, dynamic primitive allocation via specialized experts and routing yields improved rendering quality with compact representations, advancing generalizable novel view synthesis without per-scene optimization.

Abstract: Generalizable novel view synthesis aims to render unseen views from uncalibrated input images without requiring per-scene optimization. Recent feed-forward approaches based on 3D Gaussian Splatting have achieved promising efficiency and rendering quality. However, most of them assign a fixed number of Gaussians to each pixel or voxel, ignoring the spatially varying complexity of real-world scenes. Such uniform allocation often wastes Gaussian primitives in smooth regions while providing insufficient capacity for fine structures, complex geometry, and high-frequency details. This motivates us to predict region-dependent primitive cardinalities rather than impose a fixed primitive budget everywhere, enabling a more expressive yet compact 3D scene representation. Therefore, we propose SplatWeaver, a generalizable novel view synthesis framework that is able to dynamically allocate Gaussian primitives over different regions in a feed-forward manner. Specifically, SplatWeaver introduces cardinality Gaussian experts and a pixel-level routing scheme, wherein each expert specializes in producing a specific number of primitives from 0 to M, and the routing scheme coordinates these experts to adaptively determine how many Gaussian primitives should be allocated to each spatial location. Moreover, SplatWeaver incorporates a high-frequency prior with attendant guidance module and routing regularization to stabilize expert selection and promote complexity-aware allocation. By leveraging high-frequency structural cues, the routing process is encouraged to assign more Gaussian primitives to fine structures, complex geometry, and textured regions, while suppressing redundant primitives in smooth areas. Extensive experiments across diverse scenarios show that SplatWeaver consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, delivering more faithful novel-view renderings with fewer Gaussian primitives.

</details>


### [56] [Sword: Style-Robust World Models as Simulators via Dynamic Latent Bootstrapping for VLA Policy Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07288)
*Jiaxuan Gao,Yongjian Guo,Zhong Guan,Wen Huang,Wanlun Ma,Xi Xiao,Junwu Xiong,Sheng Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sword introduces a robust World Model (WM) framework for Vision-Language-Action models by disentangling visual textures from task dynamics and enforcing training-inference consistency, achieving strong generalization and improved RL performance on LIBERO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World Models used as generative simulators in VLA systems suffer severe generalization gaps, sensitivity to initial-state perturbations (color, illumination, textures) causing hallucinations, and long-horizon error accumulation during closed-loop rollouts, undermining reliability.

Method: Structure-Guided Style Augmentation to separate interactive environment textures from dynamics; Dynamic Latent Bootstrapping to maintain training-inference coherence with low memory usage.

Result: On the LIBERO benchmark, Sword significantly outperforms the baseline WoVR across generalization, generation quality, robustness, fidelity, and RL post-training success.

Conclusion: Sword provides a robust approach to mitigate perceptual and temporal degradation in world models for VLA systems, improving the reliability of imagination-based policy optimization.

Abstract: The integration of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models with World Models has gained increasing attention. One representative approach treats learned World Models as generative simulators, enabling policy optimization entirely within "imagination." However, when deployed as simulators for specific environments such as the LIBERO benchmark, existing World Models often suffer from poor generalization and long-horizon error accumulation. During closed-loop rollouts, these models are highly sensitive to initial-state perturbations; minor changes in color, illumination, and other visual factors can trigger cascading hallucinations, leading to severe blurriness or overexposure. Moreover, long-horizon error accumulation further degrades the quality and fidelity of predicted future states. These issues limit the reliability of World Models as simulators. To mitigate these problems, we propose Sword, a robust World Model framework. Our method introduces Structure-Guided Style Augmentation to disentangle the visual textures of interactive environments from task-relevant dynamics, thereby improving generalization. We further propose Dynamic Latent Bootstrapping, which maintains consistency between training and inference while keeping memory consumption low. Extensive experiments on the LIBERO benchmark show that our method significantly outperforms the baseline WoVR in terms of generalization, generation quality, robustness, fidelity, and the success rate of reinforcement-learning post-training for VLA models.

</details>


### [57] [EgoPro-Bench: Benchmarking Personalized Proactive Interaction in Egocentric Video Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07299)
*Dongchuan Ran,Linyu Ou,Xueheng Li,Wenwen Tong,Chenxu Guo,Hewei Guo,Kaibing Wang,Lewei Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EgoPro-Bench introduces a proactive interaction benchmark for MLLMs using streaming egocentric videos, with simulated user profiles, 2.4k eval videos and 12k training videos, plus a low-latency, token-budgeted interaction protocol and the principle of short thinking to improve intention understanding and timely HMI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLMs are mostly reactive, lacking continuous perception and personalized proactive assistance. Existing benchmarks focus on alerts and neglect timing and individualized context, hindering evaluation of proactive HMI.

Method: Develop EgoPro-Bench consisting of 2,400 evaluation videos and 12,000+ training videos derived from streaming egocentric footage; generate diverse user intents via simulated profiles across 12 domains; establish an evaluation protocol and metrics for proactive interaction; train models for efficient reasoning and low-latency streaming processing; introduce the 'short thinking, better interaction' principle with a bounded token budget before intent recognition.

Result: Experiments show EgoPro-Bench improves intention understanding in MLLMs and enables accurate timing for HMI, enabling better, user-centric proactive agents.

Conclusion: EgoPro-Bench provides a solid foundation for next-generation proactive interactive agents by offering a comprehensive benchmark, evaluation protocol, and an interaction principle that balances reasoning with latency in streaming scenarios.

Abstract: Existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) remain primarily reactive, failing to continuously perceive environments or proactively assist users. While emerging benchmarks address proactivity, they are largely confined to alert scenarios, neglect personalized context, and fail to evaluate the precise timing of human-machine interactions (HMI).In this paper, we introduce EgoPro-Bench, a novel benchmark for training and evaluating proactive interaction capabilities based on streaming egocentric videos; it comprises 2,400 videos in the evaluation set and over 12,000 videos in the training set.Unlike previous works, EgoPro-Bench leverages simulated user profiles to generate diverse user intentions and to construct high-fidelity HMI data across 12 distinct domains.Subsequently, we propose a specialized evaluation protocol and metrics, train proactive interaction models designed for efficient reasoning and low-latency interaction on streaming video data, and conduct comprehensive evaluations.Furthermore, we introduce an interaction principle termed "short thinking, better interaction", which allocates a limited token budget prior to intent recognition, thereby enhancing interaction performance.The experiments demonstrate that EgoPro-Bench substantially enhances the intention understanding capabilities of MLLMs and enables accurate identification of appropriate timings for HMI, thereby laying a solid foundation for next-generation user-centric proactive interactive agents.

</details>


### [58] [Amortized-Precision Quantization for Early-Exit Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07317)
*Rui Fang,Hsi-Wen Chen,Ming-Syan Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Amortized-Precision Quantization (APQ) and its extension MAQEE enable stable, efficient Vision Transformers with low-precision early exiting by accounting for layer-wise quantization exposure and optimizing exit decisions and bit-widths; achieves up to 95% BOP reduction and up to 20% accuracy gains across tasks, establishing a superior Pareto frontier.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantization noise destabilizes dynamic early-exit pathways in ViTs, while existing methods assume static full-depth execution; need a formulation that models depth-precision trade-offs and exposure to noise to improve inference stability.

Method: APQ: a utilization-aware, layer-wise stochastic exposure model to quantify quantization noise and depth-precision trade-offs. MAQEE: a bi-level optimization framework that jointly learns exit thresholds and per-layer bit-widths under explicit risk control to balance accuracy and efficiency.

Result: MAQEE achieves a superior Pareto frontier in accuracy-efficiency; reduces BOPs by up to 95% while maintaining accuracy and outperforming baselines by up to 20% across classification, detection, and segmentation tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed APQ and MAQEE provide a stable, efficient deployment path for ViTs with early exits, demonstrating that utilization-aware quantization and joint exit-bitwidth optimization yield significant gains in robustness and resource efficiency; they set a new direction for dynamic, quantized inference in vision transformers.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) achieve strong performance across vision tasks, yet their deployment with low-precision early exiting remains fragile. Existing quantization methods assume static full-depth execution, making them unstable when exit decisions are perturbed by quantization noise, which can amplify errors along dynamic inference paths. In this paper, we introduce Amortized-Precision Quantization (APQ), a utilization-aware formulation that accounts for layer-wise stochastic exposure to quantization noise and reveals depth-precision trade-offs. Building on APQ, we propose Mutual Adaptive Quantization with Early Exiting (MAQEE), a bi-level framework that jointly optimizes exit thresholds and bit-widths under explicit risk control to improve inference stability. MAQEE establishes a superior Pareto frontier in the accuracy-efficiency trade-off, reducing BOPs by up to 95% while maintaining accuracy and outperforming strong baselines by up to 20\% across classification, detection, and segmentation tasks.

</details>


### [59] [GEM: Generating LiDAR World Model via Deformable Mamba](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07326)
*Yang Wu,Zhaojiang Liu,Qiang Meng,Youquan Liu,Renliang Weng,Jianjun Qian,Jian Yang,Jin Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GEM is a LiDAR-based world model that uses a tri-path deformable Mamba with LiDAR-specific tokenization and an unsupervised dynamic-static separator to achieve state-of-the-art predictive and imaginative capabilities, with optional planning and BEV-based rollout for “what-if” scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LiDAR data are inherently unordered and sparse, making it hard to build accurate, dynamic scene models. Existing LiDAR world models lag behind camera/occupancy-based approaches in fidelity and forward imagination.

Method: Introduce a LiDAR scene tokenizer to compactly represent scans, apply an unsupervised dynamic-static separator to disentangle features, and employ a tri-path deformable Mamba for selective scanning and adaptive fusion of features. Optionally incorporate a planner and a BEV layout controller for autonomous rollout and scenario generation.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks and evaluation settings, showing improved fidelity and imaginative capability in LiDAR-based world modeling.

Conclusion: GEM advances LiDAR world modeling by effectively handling disorder and dynamic/static separation, enabling higher-fidelity predictions and controllable imagination with optional planning tools.

Abstract: World models, which simulate environmental dynamics and generate sensor observations, are gaining increasing attention in autonomous driving. However, progress in LiDAR-based world models has lagged behind those built on camera videos or occupancy data, primarily due to two core challenges: the inherent disorder of LiDAR point clouds and the difficulty of distinguishing dynamic objects from static structures. To address these issues, we propose GEM: a Generative LiDAR world model that leverages deformable mamba architecture, significantly improving fidelity and imaginative capability. Specifically, leveraging the structural similarity between sequential laser scanning and Mamba's processing mechanism, we first tokenize LiDAR sweeps into compact representations via a custom LiDAR scene tokenizer. After unsupervised disentanglement of tokenized features via a dynamic-static separator, a tri-path deformable Mamba is introduced to perform selective scanning and adaptive gating fusion over the disentangled features, leading to enhanced spatial-temporal understanding of the world evolution. Optionally, a planner and a BEV layout controller can be integrated to explore the model's capability for autonomous rollout and its potential to generate ``what-if" scenarios. Extensive experiments show that GEM achieves state-of-the-art performances across diverse benchmarks and evaluation settings, demonstrating its superiority and effectiveness. Project page: https://github.com/wuyang98/GEM.

</details>


### [60] [Teacher-Feature Drifting: One-Step Diffusion Distillation with Pretrained Diffusion Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07327)
*Yuan Zhang,Chenyi Li,Guoqing Ma,Jiajun Zha,Yuanming Yang,Bo Wang,Wei Tang,Wenbo Li,Haoyang Huang,Nan Duan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A one-step distillation method for diffusion/flow models using the teacher's own intermediate states as features within a drifting loss, plus a lightweight mode coverage term, yielding competitive one-shot generation with simpler training and strong FID on ImageNet-64x64 and SDXL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce costly multi-pass sampling and avoid heavy auxiliary networks by leveraging the pretrained teacher's representation geometry for distillation.

Method: Replace/augment drifting objective to use intermediate hidden states of the pretrained teacher as feature representation; no extra feature extractor; add mode coverage loss to encourage diversity and mitigate mode collapse; achieve one-step distillation.

Result: Empirical results on ImageNet and SDXL show efficient one-step generation with competitive FID scores: 1.58 on ImageNet-64x64 and 18.4 on SDXL, indicating strong image quality and diversity with a simplified distillation pipeline.

Conclusion: The proposed approach simplifies distillation considerably by using the teacher's own representations and a lightweight mode coverage loss, achieving competitive one-step generation without extra networks or complex training stages.

Abstract: Sampling from pretrained diffusion and flow-matching models typically requires many forward passes to generate diverse and high-fidelity images. Existing distillation methods often rely on multiple auxiliary networks, carefully designed training stages, or complex optimization pipelines. In this work, we revisit the recently proposed Drifting Model objective and show that a single drifting loss can be directly used to simplify one step distillation. A key observation is that the pretrained diffusion teacher itself already provides a strong representation space. Unlike the original Drifting Model, which relies on an additional pretrained feature extractor, we use intermediate hidden states of the pretrained teacher model as the feature representation. This removes the need for training or introducing an extra representation network while preserving a semantically meaningful feature geometry for drifting. Furthermore, we introduce a lightweight mode coverage loss to mitigate mode collapse during distillation and encourage the student generator to cover diverse teacher-supported regions. Extensive experiments on ImageNet and SDXL demonstrate that our method achieves efficient one step generation with competitive image quality and diversity, achieving FID scores of 1.58 on ImageNet-64$\times$64 and 18.4 on SDXL, while substantially simplifying the overall distillation framework.

</details>


### [61] [GC-ART: Global Learnable Second-Order Rational Tone Curves for Illumination Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07329)
*Wei Huang,Joyce Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GC-ART learns endpoint-pinned rational tone curves from per-channel soft histograms to pre-process images, achieving robust CIFAR-10 classification with low FLOPs and competitive corruption robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Develop a lightweight, differentiable pre-processing module that enhances robustness of image classifiers to common corruptions without heavy computation or changes to the classifier architecture.

Method: A 643-parameter MLP predicts an endpoint-pinned rational tone curve per color channel from per-channel soft histograms. The curve is applied pointwise before the classifier. Training uses cross-entropy loss and a soft monotonicity penalty, end-to-end.

Result: On CIFAR-10 with a CIFAR-style ResNet-18, GC-ART matches clean baseline accuracy, improves over baseline under multiplicative darkening, and achieves the best learned-method result on contrast corruption (48.45% vs baseline 46.27% and Zero-DCE++ 47.13%). It uses far fewer FLOPs at 32x32.

Conclusion: Histogram-conditioned rational curves can learn useful global tone corrections, including contrast expansion, while preserving edges via pointwise mapping. The approach is computationally efficient, though the authors note untuned hyperparameters leave room for systematic improvement.

Abstract: We introduce GC-ART (Global Curve Adaptive Rational Tone-mapping), a lightweight differentiable pre-processing module for robust image classification. GC-ART predicts an endpoint-pinned rational tone curve from per-channel soft histograms using a 643-parameter MLP, then applies the curve pointwise before the classifier. The module is trained end-to-end with cross-entropy and a soft monotonicity penalty. On CIFAR-10 with a CIFAR-style ResNet-18, GC-ART matches clean accuracy with the unenhanced baseline and other learned enhancers, improves over the baseline on multiplicative darkening, and achieves the best learned-method result on contrast corruption (48.45% vs. 46.27% for the baseline and 47.13% for Zero-DCE++). These results suggest that histogram-conditioned rational curves can learn useful global tone corrections, including contrast-expanding behavior, while preserving edge locations by construction through pointwise mapping. GC-ART also uses substantially fewer FLOPs than convolutional learned enhancers at 32 x 32. The current hyperparameters are untuned, leaving room for systematic improvement.

</details>


### [62] [RCoT-Seg: Reinforced Chain-of-Thought for Video Reasoning and Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07334)
*Junwei Wen,Deshui Miao,Guangming Lu,Xin Li,Wenjie Pei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RCoT-Seg decouples temporal reasoning and keyframe perception for video reasoning segmentation by introducing an agentic keyframe selection module (TVR) refined with CoT and GRPO, and a high-resolution segmentation (KTP) stage using SAM2-based propagation, achieving improved temporal localization and spatial precision over state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLM-based VRS methods rely on simple frame sampling and auxiliary selectors with limited supervision, leading to narrow keyframes and weak temporal understanding in complex scenes. A decoupled framework with explicit temporal reasoning and robust keyframe perception aims to improve holistic temporal reasoning and segmentation quality.

Method: TVR stage: agentic keyframe selection module initialized from a curated CoT-start corpus and refined by GRPO under task-aligned rewards, enabling self-evaluation and reselecting keyframes. KTP stage: high-resolution segmentation on the selected frame, followed by SAM2-based mask propagation across the sequence, replacing heuristic sampling and external selectors.

Result: Extensive experiments show favorable performance against state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: The proposed RCoT-Seg demonstrates that factorizing VRS into temporal video reasoning and keyframe target perception, with self-evaluated keyframe selection and SAM2-based propagation, yields improved temporal localization and spatial precision; code and models will be released.

Abstract: Video Reasoning Segmentation (VRS) aims to segment target objects in videos based on implicit instructions that convey human intent and temporal logic. Existing MLLM-based methods predict masks with a [SEG] token after selecting frames via simple sampling or an auxiliary MLLM, where limited supervision and frame-language similarity rules often yield narrow-scope keyframe choices that weaken holistic temporal understanding and lead to brittle localization in complex multi-object scenes. To address these issues, we introduce RCoT-Seg, a video-of-thought framework that factorizes VRS into temporal video reasoning (TVR) and keyframe target perception (KTP), explicitly separating temporal reasoning from spatial perception. Specifically, in the TVR stage, an agentic keyframe selection module, initialized with a curated CoT-start corpus and refined by GRPO under task-aligned rewards, is proposed to generate and reselect the keyframe through self-evaluation, strengthening moment localization and temporal reasoning. In the KTP stage, RCoT-Seg performs high-resolution segmentation on the selected frame and propagates masks with SAM2-based methods across the sequence, replacing heuristic sampling and external selectors while improving spatial precision and inter-frame consistency. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed RCoT-Seg achieves favorable performance against the state-of-the-art methods. The code and models will be publicly released at https://github.com/Victor-wjw/RCoT-Seg.

</details>


### [63] [SoLAR: Error-Resilient Streamable Long-Horizon Free-Viewpoint Video Reconstruction with Anchor Activation and Latent Recalibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07346)
*Haotian Zhang,Xu Mo,Yixin Yu,Guanhua Zhu,Jian Xue,Tongda Xu,Yan Wang,Jiaqi Zhang,Siwei Ma,Wen Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SoLAR proposes an error-resilient, streamable long-horizon Free-Viewpoint Video framework based on dynamic anchors and latent recalibration to maintain stable quality with minimal storage, without GOP partitioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon FVV suffers from error accumulation and inefficiency; there is a need for a rate-distortion-guided, scalable, and robust streaming solution that does not rely on GOPs.

Method: Introduces SoLAR with Anchor Activation Dynamics (AAD) to dynamically select informative anchors for non-rigid scene representations, and Latent Discrepancy Aware Recalibration (LaDAR) to identify and correct latent representation discrepancies to curb error propagation; frames are streamed, aiming for real-time performance and compact storage.

Result: SoLAR reportedly achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality on long sequences with minimal additional storage and without GOP partitioning; extensive experiments and demo videos in supplementary material.

Conclusion: SoLAR offers a novel, practical approach to LFVV that addresses error resilience and bitrate/storage concerns, enabling better real-time deployment of immersive systems.

Abstract: Free-Viewpoint Video (FVV) has emerged as a cornerstone of next-generation immersive media systems and attracted widespread attention. Previous methods primarily focus on short video sequences and suffer from significant performance degradation when processing long-horizon free-viewpoint video (LFVV). Motivated by bit allocation theory, we analyze dynamic-anchor-based volumetric video representation within a rate-distortion optimization framework and propose \textbf{SoLAR}, which is the first error-resilient streamable FVV framework that maintains stable reconstruction quality on long sequences without requiring group-of-pictures partitioning. We propose the Anchor Activation Dynamics (AAD), which enables dynamic anchors to model non-rigid transformations by dynamically activating informative anchors and suppressing redundant ones. Furthermore, we introduce Latent Discrepancy Aware Recalibration (LaDAR), which is a mechanism to identify discrepancies between latent representations and recalibrate the correspondences encoded in the network, effectively mitigating error propagation in LFVV without compromising real-time performance or storage compactness. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \textbf{SoLAR} achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction performance while maintaining minimum storage overhead, which provides a new direction for LFVV reconstruction and advances the practical deployment of immersive systems. Demo free-viewpoint videos are provided in the supplementary material.

</details>


### [64] [Disambiguating 2D-3D Correspondences in Gaussian Splatting-based Feature Fields for Visual Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07351)
*Miso Lee,Sangeek Hyun,Yerim Jeon,Jae-Pil Heo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SplitGS-Loc uses mixture-of-Gaussians splitting and cross-view weighting to derive one-to-one 2D-3D correspondences from Gaussian Feature Fields, enabling robust PnP localization without scene-specific training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Photometrically optimized GSFFs yield many-to-one pixel-to-point mappings and weak multi-view consistency, destabilizing PnP pose estimation for localization.

Method: Decompose each Gaussian into smaller Gaussians (Mixture-of-Gaussians-based splitting) to obtain precise one-to-one correspondences; leverage GS rasterization composition weights to select Gaussians that consistently contribute across views; strengthen pixel-Gaussian associations to enforce multi-view consistency and aggregate discriminative features.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art localization performance on benchmarks with stable PnP convergence and without per-scene training or iterative pose refinement; experimental validation confirms improved 2D-3D matching reliability and efficiency.

Conclusion: SplitGS-Loc extends photometric GSFFs to accurate and efficient localization by exploiting Gaussian attributes to produce reliable 2D-3D correspondences and multi-view-consistent features.

Abstract: While Gaussian Splatting-based Feature Fields (GSFFs) have shown promise for visual localization, this paper highlights that photometrically optimized GSFFs are inherently ill-suited for 2D-3D matching. The volumetric extent of each Gaussian induces many-to-one pixel-to-point mappings that destabilize PnP-based pose estimation, while photometric optimization gives rise to superfluous Gaussians devoid of multi-view consistency. To address these issues, we propose SplitGS-Loc, a localization-specialized GSFFs construction framework that disambiguates 2D-3D correspondences by exploiting Gaussian attributes. Our key design, Mixture-of-Gaussians-based splitting, decomposes each Gaussian into smaller Gaussians, replacing ambiguous many-to-one with precise one-to-one correspondences. In parallel, we exploit composition weights from GS rasterization to select Gaussians that significantly and consistently contribute across multiple views and aggregate discriminative features through strong pixel-Gaussian associations, enforcing multi-view consistency. The resulting compact yet discriminative feature fields enable stable PnP convergence, achieving state-of-the-art performance on localization benchmarks. Extensive experiments validate that SplitGS-Loc extends the utility of photometric GSFFs to accurate and efficient localization by exploiting Gaussian attributes, without per-scene training or iterative pose refinement.

</details>


### [65] [TTF: Temporal Token Fusion for Efficient Video-Language Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07355)
*Simin Huo,Ning LI*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Temporal Token Fusion (TTF) is a training-free, plug-and-play token compression approach for Video-Language Models (VLMs) that leverages temporal redundancy by selecting an anchor frame and performing local window similarity-based token fusion across subsequent frames, maintaining positional alignment. It reduces visual tokens significantly with minimal accuracy loss and low computing overhead, enabling more efficient video understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video-language models incur high inference costs as visual tokens scale with video length; existing compression methods rely on global similarity or attention-guided reduction and incur trade-offs. A training-free, temporally aware compression could substantially reduce cost without retraining.

Method: Training-free framework. Automatically select an anchor frame; for each subsequent frame, perform a local window similarity search (e.g., 3x3) and fuse tokens that exceed a similarity-threshold. Maintain temporal and spatial positional consistency via coordinate realignment so the compressed sequence works with both prefill and decoding. Threshold t controls fusion aggressiveness.

Result: On Qwen3-VL-8B with threshold t=0.70, about 67% of visual tokens are removed while preserving ~99.5% of baseline accuracy, with only ~0.16 GFLOPs of matching overhead. The approach is plug-and-play with existing VLM pipelines.

Conclusion: TTF provides a practical, efficient, training-free solution for reducing token counts in video inputs for VLMs, achieving substantial compression with minimal accuracy loss and easy integration. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Video-language models (VLMs) face rapid inference costs as visual token counts scale with video length. For example, 32 frames at $448{\times}448$ resolution already yield >8,000 visual tokens in Qwen3-VL, making LLM prefill the dominant throughput bottleneck. Existing methods often rely on global similarity or attention-guided compression, incurring offsets to their gains. We propose \textbf{Temporal Token Fusion (TTF)}, a training-free, plug-and-play pre-LLM token compression framework that exploits structured temporal redundancy in video. TTF automatically selects an anchor frame, then for each subsequent frame, performs a local window similarity search (e.g.,$3\times 3$), fusing tokens that exceed a threshold. The compressed sequence maintains positional consistency across both prefill and decoding through coordinate realignment, enabling seamless integration with existing VLM pipelines. On Qwen3-VL-8B with threshold t=0.70, TTF removes about 67\% of visual tokens while retaining 99.5\% of the baseline accuracy and introducing only ${\approx}0.16$\,GFLOPs of matching overhead. Overall, TTF offers a practical, efficient solution for video understanding. The code is available at \href{https://github.com/Cominder/ttf}{https://github.com/Cominder/ttf}

</details>


### [66] [UniD-Shift: Towards Unified Semantic Segmentation via Interpretable Share-Private Multimodal Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07356)
*Shuai Zhang,Zhecheng Shi,Zhuxiao Li,Jing Ou,Tengxi Wang,Yuan Liu,Wufan Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified 2D-3D semantic segmentation framework that decomposes cross-modal features into shared (semantic) and private (modality-specific) subspaces, with SAM-based 2D encoder and SPTNet-based 3D geometric encoder; an attention-based fusion module combines shared representations; regularized training enforces semantic alignment and subspace independence, yielding improved performance and generalization on SemanticKITTI, nuScenes, and cross-domain USA-Singapore.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-modal alignment between sparse LiDAR 3D point clouds and dense 2D images is challenging due to sampling sparsity and view-dependent distortions. The goal is to exploit common semantics across modalities while preserving modality-specific information to improve robust, scalable segmentation.

Method: Two-branch architecture: a SAM-based vision encoder for 2D images and a SPTNet-based geometric encoder for 3D data. Features are decomposed into shared and private subspaces. A lightweight attention-based fusion module aggregates the shared components into a cross-modal representation. A regularized training objective enforces semantic alignment between modalities and independence of the subspaces to prevent information leakage.

Result: Experiments on SemanticKITTI and nuScenes show consistent accuracy gains over representative multimodal baselines with competitive efficiency. Cross-domain evaluation on nuScenes USA-Singapore demonstrates robust generalization under distribution shifts. Code is publicly available.

Conclusion: The proposed unified 2D-3D segmentation framework using shared/private subspace decomposition and cross-modal fusion improves segmentation accuracy and generalization in large-scale 3D perception, validating the value of explicit cross-modal factorization and alignment.

Abstract: Semantic segmentation of large-scale 3D point clouds is crucial for applications such as autonomous driving and urban digital twins. However, the sparse sampling pattern of LiDAR and the view-dependent geometric distortion in image observations complicate cross-modal alignment and hinder stable fusion. Inspired by the fact that 2D images captured by cameras are representations of the 3D world, we recognize that the features learned from 2D and 3D segmentation share some common semantics, while other aspects remain modality-specific. This insight motivates a unified multimodal framework for joint 2D-3D semantic segmentation. We combine a SAM-based vision encoder with a SPTNet-based geometric encoder to extract complementary semantic and geometric representations. The resulting features from both modalities are explicitly decomposed into shared and private subspaces, where the shared components summarize semantic factors common to both domains, and the private components preserve properties that are unique to each modality. A lightweight attention-based fusion module aggregates the shared features into a consistent cross-modal representation, and a regularized training objective ensures both semantic alignment and subspace independence. Experiments on the SemanticKITTI and nuScenes benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in segmentation accuracy over representative multimodal baselines, accompanied by competitive computational efficiency. Cross-domain evaluation on nuScenes USA-Singapore shows stable performance under distribution shifts, demonstrating strong generalization. The implementation code is publicly available at: https://github.com/shuaizhang69/UniD-Shift.

</details>


### [67] [UniISP: A Unified ISP Framework for Both Human and Machine Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07359)
*Hanxi Li,Yao Cheng,Bo Zhang,Li Zeng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniISP jointly optimizes ISP for human perception and CV tasks using HAM and a Feature Adapter, achieving state-of-the-art results across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Raw sensor data contains richer information than RGB; conventional ISP pipelines may discard or compress information, and many CV-friendly pipelines use minimal ISP that yields poor visualization; there is a need for an ISP that serves both human viewing and machine perception.

Method: Introduce Hybrid Attention Module (HAM) within the ISP pipeline to balance perceptual quality and information preservation, supervise learning to align with human aesthetics, and add a Feature Adapter to transfer ISP-stage features to downstream networks for improved task performance; end-to-end training.

Result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art results across various scenarios and datasets, demonstrating strong generalization and effectiveness in both perceptual quality and downstream CV performance.

Conclusion: A unified ISP design like UniISP, with HAM and Feature Adapter, can satisfy dual objectives of human visual preferences and machine-based recognition, offering robust generalization across datasets and conditions.

Abstract: Compared to RGB images, raw sensor data provides a richer representation of information, which is crucial for accurate recognition, particularly under challenging conditions such as low-light environments. The traditional Image Signal Processing (ISP) pipeline generates visually pleasing RGB images for human perception through a series of steps, but some of these operations may adversely impact the information integrity by introducing compression and loss. Furthermore, in computer vision tasks that directly utilize raw camera data, most existing methods integrate minimal ISP processing with downstream networks, yet the resulting images are often difficult to visualize or do not align with human aesthetic preferences. This paper proposes UniISP, a novel ISP framework designed to simultaneously meet the requirements of both human visual perception and computer vision applications. By incorporating a carefully designed Hybrid Attention Module (HAM) and employing supervised learning, the proposed method ensures that the generated images are visually appealing. Additionally, a Feature Adapter module is introduced to effectively propagate informative features from the ISP stage to subsequent downstream networks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across various scenarios and multiple datasets, proving its generalizability and effectiveness.

</details>


### [68] [RELO: Reinforcement Learning to Localize for Visual Object Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07379)
*Xin Chen,Chuanyu Sun,Jiao Xu,Houwen Peng,Dong Wang,Huchuan Lu,Kede Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RELO reframes visual object tracking as reinforcement-learning-based localization, replacing handcrafted spatial priors with a learned policy. It uses a reward that combines frame-level IoU and sequence-level AUC, and introduces layer-aligned temporal token propagation to maintain semantic consistency with minimal overhead. It reports 57.5% AUC on LaSOText without template updates, suggesting reward-driven localization can outperform prior-driven localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional trackers rely on handcrafted priors (e.g., heatmaps) that do not align well with tracking objectives like IoU and AUC, potentially limiting optimization and evaluation compatibility.

Method: Formulate localization as a Markov decision process; learn a spatial localization policy via reinforcement learning; design rewards that blend frame IoU with sequence AUC; implement layer-aligned temporal token propagation to enhance cross-frame semantic consistency with low overhead.

Result: Achieves superior performance with a notable 57.5% AUC on LaSOText without template updates, indicating effective reward-driven localization.

Conclusion: Reward-driven localization via reinforcement learning is a viable and potentially superior alternative to handcrafted priors for visual object tracking.

Abstract: Conventional visual object trackers localize targets using handcrafted spatial priors, often in the form of heatmaps. Such priors provide only surrogate supervision and are poorly aligned with tracking optimization and evaluation metrics, such as intersection over union (IoU) and area under the success curve (AUC). Here, we introduce RELO, a REinforcement-learning-to-LOcalize method for visual object tracking that formulates target localization as a Markov decision process. Specifically, RELO replaces handcrafted spatial priors with a localization policy learned over spatial positions via reinforcement learning, with rewards combining frame-level IoU and sequence-level AUC. We additionally introduce layer-aligned temporal token propagation to improve semantic consistency across frames, with negligible computational overhead. Across multiple benchmarks, RELO achieves superior results, attaining 57.5% AUC on LaSOText without template updates. This confirms that reward-driven localization provides an effective alternative to prior-driven localization for visual object tracking.

</details>


### [69] [A Marine Debris Detection Framework for Ocean Robots via Self-Attention Enhancement and Feature Interaction Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07388)
*Yuyang Li,Jiashu Han,Yinyi Lai,Wenbin Kang,Zenghui Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: YOLO-MD: an enhanced YOLO-based marine debris detector featuring DB-CASA self-attention, a shift-based efficiency module, and SFG-Loss, achieving strong metrics on UODM and validated on edge robotics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detecting marine debris in ocean robotics is hampered by low-quality, blurred images, cluttered backgrounds, and small targets, requiring accurate, efficient detectors suitable for real-time edge deployment.

Method: Introduce YOLO-MD with: (1) Dual-Branch Convolutional Enhanced Self-Attention (DB-CASA) to strengthen spatial-channel feature interactions; (2) lightweight shift-based operation for improved fine-grained features across scales with low parameter cost; (3) SFG-Loss that dynamically reweights samples to mitigate class imbalance and optimization instability.

Result: On the UODM dataset, YOLO-MD attains precision 0.875, F1-score 0.822, and mAP50 0.849, outperforming recent state-of-the-art methods; real-world robotic edge deployments confirm practical effectiveness.

Conclusion: The DB-CASA, shift-based feature extraction, and SFG-Loss collectively enhance degraded-image detection and enable robust, real-time marine debris detection on edge-enabled robotic systems.

Abstract: Marine debris detection for ocean robot is crucial for ecological protection, yet performance is often degraded by low-quality images with blur, complex backgrounds, and small targets. To address these challenges, we propose YOLO-MD, an enhanced YOLO-based detection framework. A Dual-Branch Convolutional Enhanced Self-Attention (DB-CASA) module is designed to strengthen spatial-channel interactions, improving feature representation in degraded images. Additionally, a lightweight shift-based operation is introduced to enhance fine-grained feature extraction for objects of varying scales while maintaining parameter efficiency. We further propose SFG-Loss to mitigate class imbalance and optimization instability via dynamic sample reweighting. Experiments on the UODM dataset demonstrate that YOLO-MD achieves 0.875 precision, 0.822 F1-score, and 0.849 mAP50, outperforming the latest state-of-the-art methods. The effectiveness of this method has also been verified through real-world robotic edge deployment experiments.

</details>


### [70] [ST-Gen4D: Embedding 4D Spatiotemporal Cognition into World Model for 4D Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07390)
*Haonan Wang,Hanyu Zhou,Tao Gu,Luxin Yan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ST-Gen4D introduces a 4D spatiotemporal cognition framework for 4D generation, integrating a 4D cognition graph via multi-modal representations, global/local dynamic graphs, and a world-model-guided diffusion generator, plus a ST-4D dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 4D generative models emphasize global appearance coherence but neglect local dynamic topology; a 4D cognition approach can capture both global structure and local dynamics for physically plausible 4D generation.

Method: Four-pronged design: (1) encode multiple modalities into diverse spatiotemporal representations; (2) build and fuse a global appearance graph and a local dynamic graph into a 4D cognition graph via semantic-bridged fusion; (3) use a world model to reason future states from 4D cognition; (4) condition latent diffusion on the cognition for 4D Gaussian generation; also assemble ST-4D datasets from public and self-built sources.

Result: Experiments show ST-Gen4D achieves superior performance on both 3D and 4D generation tasks, with enhanced structural rationality and topological consistency due to integrated 4D cognition and generative priors.

Conclusion: Integrating 4D intrinsic cognition with generative priors enables more coherent and topologically consistent 4D generation; the ST-4D dataset enables broader evaluation across 4D tasks.

Abstract: Generative models have achieved success in producing apparently coherent 2D videos, but remain challenging in the physical world due to lack of 4D spatiotemporal scale. Typically, existing 4D generative models directly embed macro scale constraints to enhance overall spatiotemporal consistency. However, these methods only ensure global appearance coherence and fail to reveal the local dynamics of the physical world. Our insight is that global appearance structure and local dynamic topology empower 4D spatiotemporal cognition, thereby enabling 4D generation with spatiotemporal regularities. In this work, we propose ST-Gen4D, a 4D generation framework with 4D spatiotemporal cognition-based world model. Our model is guided by four key designs: 1) Spatiotemporal representation. We encode various modalities into multiple representations as a feature basis. 2) Spatiotemporal cognition. We sculpture these representations into global appearance graph and local dynamic graph, and fuse them via semantic-bridged spatiotemporal fusion to obtain a 4D cognition graph. 3) Spatiotemporal reasoning. We utilize a world model to derive future state based on the 4D cognition. 4) Spatiotemporal generation. We leverage the derived cognition as condition to guide latent diffusion for 4D Gaussian generation. By deeply integrating 4D intrinsic cognition with generative priors, our model guarantees the structural rationality and topological consistency of 4D generation. Moreover, we propose ST-4D datasets by aggregating public 4D datasets and self-built subset. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of our ST-Gen4D across 3D and 4D generation tasks.

</details>


### [71] [BalCapRL: A Balanced Framework for RL-Based MLLM Image Captioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07394)
*Shaokai Ye,Vasileios Saveris,Yihao Qian,Jiaming Hu,Elmira Amirloo,Peter Grasch*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A balanced reinforcement-learning framework for image captioning that optimizes utility, reference coverage, and linguistic quality via GDPO-style normalization and length-aware masking, yielding consistent improvements across multiple base models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing captioning RL methods optimize narrow quality signals, causing trade-offs (e.g., more useful but noisier or fluent but generic). A joint objective is needed to balance correctness, coverage, and fluency.

Method: Train models with a continuous multi-objective reward combining utility, coverage, and language quality; apply GDPO-style reward-decoupled normalization to continuous rewards; introduce length-conditional reward masking; evaluate on LLaVA-1.5-7B and Qwen2.5-VL 3B/7B.

Result: Shows consistent improvements in captioning metrics; peak gains reported as +13.6 DCScore, +9.0 CaptionQA, and +29.0 CapArena across different base models.

Conclusion: A balanced RL framework with decoupled normalization and length-aware penalties improves caption quality beyond prior RL approaches and generalizes across diverse base models.

Abstract: Image captioning is one of the most fundamental tasks in computer vision. Owing to its open-ended nature, it has received significant attention in the era of multimodal large language models (MLLMs). In pursuit of ever more detailed and accurate captions, recent work has increasingly turned to reinforcement learning (RL). However, existing captioning-RL methods and evaluation metrics often emphasize a narrow notion of caption quality, inducing trade-offs across core dimensions of captioning. For example, utility-oriented objectives can encourage noisy, hallucinated, or overlong captions that improve downstream question answering while harming fluency, whereas arena-style objectives can favor fluent but generic descriptions with limited usefulness. To address this, we propose a more balanced RL framework that jointly optimizes utility-aware correctness, reference coverage, and linguistic quality. In order to effectively optimize the resulting continuous multi-objective reward formulation, we apply GDPO-style reward-decoupled normalization to continuous-valued captioning rewards and show that it improves performance over vanilla GRPO. Additionally, we introduce length-conditional reward masking, yielding a more suitable length penalty for captioning. Across LLaVA-1.5-7B and Qwen2.5-VL 3B and 7B base models, our method consistently improves caption quality, with peak gains of +13.6 DCScore, +9.0 CaptionQA, and +29.0 CapArena across different models.

</details>


### [72] [Exposing and Mitigating Temporal Attack in Deepfake Video Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07398)
*Zheyuan Gu,Minghao Shao,Zhen Wang,Yusong Wang,Mingkun Xu,Shijie Zhang,Hao Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes SpInShield to decouple semantic motion from spectral artifacts, using a learnable spectral adversary and shortcut suppression to defend spatiotemporal deepfake detectors against spectral-evasion attacks, achieving a large AUC boost under amplitude spectral attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spatiotemporal deepfake detectors overfit to fragile temporal spectral cues and are vulnerable to spectral-deformation evasion. A robust defense should enforce semantic causality and reduce reliance on unstable spectral statistics.

Method: Introduce SpInShield, a temporal spectral-invariant defense framework. It includes a learnable spectral adversary that generates severe spectral deformations and a shortcut suppression optimization that forces the encoder to rely on reliable forensic cues while purging unstable spectral statistics from the latent space.

Result: SpInShield achieves competitive accuracy on standard datasets and outperforms the strongest baseline by 21.30 percentage points in AUC under simulated amplitude spectral attacks.

Conclusion: Temporal spectral invariance enhances robustness of deepfake detectors by decoupling semantic motion from manipulatable spectral cues, offering a practical defense against spectral-attack evasions.

Abstract: While spatiotemporal deepfake detectors achieve high AUC, our experiments reveal their susceptibility to evasion attacks. These models tend to overfit on fragile temporal spectrum cues, rather than learning robust semantic causality. To mitigate this vulnerability, we propose SpInShield, a temporal spectral-invariant defense framework explicitly designed to decouple semantic motion from manipulatable spectral artifacts. We propose a learnable spectral adversary that dynamically synthesizes severe spectral deformations, simulating extreme attack scenarios. By employing a shortcut suppression optimization strategy, SpInShield compels the encoder to extract reliable forensic cues while purging unstable spectral statistics from the latent space. Experiments show that SpInShield obtains competitive performance on widely used datasets and outperforms the strongest baseline by 21.30 percentage points in AUC under simulated amplitude spectral attacks.

</details>


### [73] [GPO-V: Jailbreak Diffusion Vision Language Model by Global Probability Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07399)
*Yu Pan,Andi Zhang,Yi Wang,Sibei Yang,Wenjie Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This work uncovers hidden jailbreak vulnerabilities in diffusion vision-language models (dVLMs), introducing Global Probability Optimization (GPO) and GPO-V for visual modalities. It demonstrates stealthy, cross-model transferable attacks, challenging current defenses and urging safety-alignment reevaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the safety of diffusion-based multimodal models and the limitations of fixed-prefix optimization (FPO) defenses, revealing latent attack surfaces in the denoising trajectory of diffusion models.

Method: Empirical analysis of refusal patterns in dVLMs (Immediate Refusal and Progressive Refusal); formulation of Global Probability Optimization (GPO) as a general jailbreak paradigm targeting the denoising process; extension to visual modality with GPO-V; evaluation of perturbation stealthiness and cross-model transferability across diffusion-based architectures.

Result: GPO-V generates stealthy perturbations with strong cross-model transferability, exposing a security gap in non-sequential generative architectures; results underscore the need for safety alignment in dVLMs; code is released.

Conclusion: There is an urgent need to rethink defense strategies for diffusion-based generation; safety alignment should account for the unique vulnerabilities of diffusion language and vision-language systems.

Abstract: Diffusion Vision-Language Models (dVLMs), built upon the non-causal foundations of Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs), have demonstrated remarkable efficacy in multimodal tasks by departing from the traditional autoregressive generation paradigm. While dVLMs appear inherently robust against conventional jailbreak tactics, which we categorize as Fixed Prefix Optimization (FPO) (e.g., anchoring responses with "Sure, here is"), this perceived resilience is deceptive. Our investigation into the safety landscape of dVLMs reveals a unique refusal pattern: Immediate Refusal and Progressive Refusal. We find that while FPO-based attacks often fail by triggering the latter, the progressive refinement process itself uncovers a novel, latent attack surface. To exploit this vulnerability, we propose Global Probability Optimization (GPO), a general jailbreak paradigm designed specifically for the denoising trajectory of masked diffusion models. Unlike prefix-based methods, GPO manipulates the global generative dynamics to bypass guardrails in diffusion language models. Building on this, we introduce GPO-V, the first visual-modality jailbreak framework tailored for dVLMs. Empirical results demonstrate that GPO-V produces stealthy perturbations with exceptional cross-model transferability, revealing a critical security gap in non-sequential generative architectures. Our findings underscore the critical urgency of addressing safety alignment in dVLMs. These results necessitate an immediate and fundamental re-evaluation of current defense paradigms to mitigate the unique risks of diffusion-based generation. Our code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/GPO-V-0250.

</details>


### [74] [InsHuman: Towards Natural and Identity-Preserving Human Insertion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07402)
*Jie Li,Shulian Zhang,Yangyang Gao,Wenbo Li,Yulun Zhang,Yong Guo,Jian Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel framework InsHuman enables natural, identity-preserving human insertion into target backgrounds, with HBAF for pose/count/adaptation, FFIP for identity consistency, and BDP for a realistic dataset; experiments show improved plausibility and identity preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current image editing models often produce incongruent poses, erroneous person counts, or altered facial identity when inserting humans into new backgrounds, and public datasets lack full-body portraits and realistic human-background interactions.

Method: Three core components: (1) Human-Background Adaptive Fusion (HBAF) detects foreground humans, computes a binary mask, and applies region-aware weighting to align predicted latents with ground-truth, ensuring coherent pose, count, and appearance adaptation to the background; (2) Face-to-Face ID-Preserving (FFIP) detects and matches faces between generated and source images using face recognition features to enforce identity consistency per face; (3) Bidirectional Data Pairing (BDP) constructs the BDP-InsHuman dataset with realistic human-background interactions.

Result: Experiments show significant improvements in generating plausible images and maintaining human identity during insertion.

Conclusion: InsHuman demonstrates that integrating adaptive foreground-background fusion, identity-preserving facial matching, and a realistic paired dataset enables natural and identity-consistent human insertion, addressing key failure modes in prior work.

Abstract: Human insertion aims to naturally place specific individuals into a target background. Although existing image editing models may have such ability, they often produce failure cases, including inappropriate human pose in new background, inconsistent number of people, and modified facial identity. Moreover, publicly available human datasets often lack full-body portraits and realistic physical interaction between humans and their background. To address these challenges, we propose InsHuman for natural and identity-preserving human insertion. Specifically, we propose Human-Background Adaptive Fusion (HBAF), which detects foreground humans to obtain a binary mask and applies region-aware weighting to align the human regions between predicted and ground-truth latents, ensuring the person's pose, count, and overall appearance are coherently adapted to the target background.We further propose Face-to-Face ID-Preserving (FFIP), which detects and matches faces between the generated image and the source image in terms of face recognition features to enforce identity consistency for each face.In addition, we propose Bidirectional Data Pairing (BDP) strategy to construct BDP-InsHuman, a high-quality dataset with realistic human-background interactions. Experiments demonstrate that InsHuman achieves significant improvements in generating plausible images while keeping human identity unchanged.

</details>


### [75] [ChartREG++: Towards Benchmarking and Improving Chart Referring Expression Grounding under Diverse referring clues and Multi-Target Referring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07415)
*Tianhao Niu,Ziyu Han,Qingfu Zhu,Wanxiang Che*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a chart referring expression grounding benchmark with multi-target localization, multiple localization forms, and diverse chart types; introduces a code-driven synthesis pipeline to generate pixel-accurate masks, trains an instance segmentation model, and integrates into a multimodal grounding framework, achieving improved performance and generalization over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current chart grounding benchmarks are limited by coarse localization (boxes), single/multi-target references, reliance on textual cues, and narrow chart type coverage. There is a need for fine-grained, multi-target, and modality-robust evaluation to advance spatial grounding in charts.

Method: Develop a code-driven synthesis pipeline that maps plotting programs to rendered chart primitives to derive pixel-perfect masks; train an instance segmentation model on these synthesized masks; integrate the segmentation model into a general-purpose multimodal grounding framework; evaluate on a comprehensive chart grounding benchmark and a ChartQA-derived real-chart grounding dataset.

Result: The integrated system consistently outperforms baselines on the proposed benchmark and generalizes to real-chart grounding tasks, revealing a sizable performance gap for current multimodal large models.

Conclusion: A systematic benchmark and a synthesis-driven segmentation-augmented grounding framework enable fine-grained, multi-target grounding across diverse chart types, advancing chart grounding research and practical QA tasks.

Abstract: Referring expression grounding is a core problem in visual grounding and is widely used as a diagnostic of spatial grounding and reasoning in vision and language models, yet most prior work focuses on natural images. In contrast, existing chart referring expression grounding-related benchmarks remain limited: (1) they largely adopt bounding boxes, constraining localization precision for fine chart elements (2) they mostly assume a single and two referred target instances, failing to handle multi-instance target references; (3) the language expressions over-rely on textual cues or data-rank clues (4) they cover only a narrow range of chart types. To address these issues, we introduce a chart referring expression grounding benchmark that systematically supports multiple localization forms, multiple referred targets, diverse grounding cues and diverse chart types. Results across representative multimodal large models reveal a significant performance gap. We further introduce a code-driven synthesis pipeline that exploits the inherent alignment between plotting programs and rendered chart primitives to derive pixel accurate instance masks across chart element types and granularities. We train an instance segmentation model with the synthesized masks and integrate it into a general-purpose multimodal grounding framework. The resulting system consistently outperforms baselines on our benchmark and generalizes well to a ChartQA-derived real-chart grounding benchmark.

</details>


### [76] [Learning Image-Adaptive Scale Fields for Metric Depth Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07418)
*Yuanyan Li,Matthias Althoff*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A method to recover metric depth from monocular estimates by modeling an image-adaptive scale field as a low-dimensional linear combination of basis maps, with weights solved from sparse anchors via least-squares; improves accuracy, robustness, and interpretability across models and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monocular depth estimates are ambiguous up to scale/shift; practical metric depth requires anchors. The goal is robust, interpretable metric depth recovery even with very sparse anchors by leveraging semantic and geometric cues embedded in MDE outputs.

Method: Formulate metric-depth correction as a linear combination of image-adaptive basis maps derived from semantic and geometric cues present in MDE estimates and intermediate representations. Determine the combination weights by solving a sparse linear least-squares problem using sparse metric anchors.

Result: Achieves improved metric depth accuracy and strong robustness under extreme anchor sparsity. Provides an interpretable decomposition of spatial scale variations and demonstrates general applicability across multiple datasets and representative MDE models.

Conclusion: The proposed image-adaptive scale-field framework offers a general, robust, and interpretable solution for recovering metric depth from monocular cues with sparse anchors, broadly applicable to diverse MDE backbones and data regimes.

Abstract: Monocular depth estimation (MDE) typically produces depth estimations that are defined up to an unknown scale or shift. When only sparse metric anchors are available, recovering accurate metric depth becomes challenging yet necessary for practical applications. We address this problem by formulating metric depth recovery as image-adaptive scale field modeling. Instead of directly correcting the depth, we reformulate the correction as a low-dimensional linear combination of image-adaptive basis maps. These maps are derived from semantic and geometric cues encoded in the MDE estimations and intermediate representations. The weights of basis maps are efficiently determined from sparse metric anchors via a least-squares problem. This formulation yields improved metric depth accuracy, strong robustness under extreme anchor sparsity, and an interpretable decomposition of spatial scale variations.
