Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and representative MDE models demonstrate the effectiveness and general applicability of our approach.

</details>


### [77] [Towards Photorealistic and Efficient Bokeh Rendering via Diffusion Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07429)
*Linxiao Shi,Siming Zheng,Zerong Wang,Hao Zhang,Jinwei Chen,Bo Li,Shifeng Chen,Peng-Tao Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces MagicBokeh, a diffusion-based unified framework that jointly performs bokeh rendering and super-resolution with a degradation-aware depth module for mobile images, delivering photorealistic results efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile cameras suffer from small apertures and high digital zoom, leading to unrealistic bokeh and loss of fine details. A two-stage SR+render pipeline is inefficient and error-prone; a unified approach is desirable.

Method: A diffusion-based framework with an alternative training strategy and a focus-aware masked attention mechanism that jointly optimizes bokeh rendering and super-resolution, plus a degradation-aware depth module to improve depth estimation from low-quality inputs.

Result: Experiments show photorealistic bokeh effects and improved controllability and fidelity on real-world low-resolution images, with efficient performance. Code and models are released at the provided repo.

Conclusion: MagicBokeh demonstrates the viability of unified SR and bokeh rendering with depth awareness for mobile devices, paving the way for future high-quality, efficient bokeh synthesis; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Existing mobile devices are constrained by compact optical designs, such as small apertures, which make it difficult to produce natural, optically realistic bokeh effects. Although recent learning-based methods have shown promising results, they still struggle with photos captured under high digital zoom levels, which often suffer from reduced resolution and loss of fine details. A naive solution is to enhance image quality before applying bokeh rendering, yet this two-stage pipeline reduces efficiency and introduces unnecessary error accumulation. To overcome these limitations, we propose MagicBokeh, a unified diffusion-based framework designed for high-quality and efficient bokeh rendering. Through an alternative training strategy and a focus-aware masked attention mechanism, our method jointly optimizes bokeh rendering and super-resolution, substantially improving both controllability and visual fidelity. Furthermore, we introduce degradation-aware depth module to enable more accurate depth estimation from low-quality inputs. Experimental results demonstrate that MagicBokeh efficiently produces photorealistic bokeh effects, particularly on real-world low-resolution images, paving the way for future advancements in bokeh rendering. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/vivoCameraResearch/MagicBokeh.

</details>


### [78] [Sparse Autoencoders as Plug-and-Play Firewalls for Adversarial Attack Detection in VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07447)
*Hao Wang,Yiqun Sun,Pengfei Wei,Lawrence B. Hsieh,Daisuke Kawahara*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Plug-and-play sparse autoencoder (SAE) module inserted into pretrained vision-language models to detect adversarial image perturbations with reconstruction-based latent features; shows strong in-domain, cross-domain, and cross-attack detection, with no adversarial training and low overhead; multi-layer signals improve robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs are increasingly used in real-world settings but remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks. Existing defenses often require heavy training or are not generalizable. A lightweight, plug-in detector could enhance safety without significant overhead.

Method: Insert a lightweight SAE module into a pretrained VLM and train it with standard reconstruction objectives. Use the sparse latent features to classify whether an input image is adversarial. Improve robustness by fusing signals from multiple layers.

Result: SAEigis achieves strong detection performance across in-domain, cross-domain, and cross-attack scenarios, with notable gains in cross-domain generalization over baselines. No adversarial training is required, overhead is minimal, and multiple-layer signals further boost robustness.

Conclusion: SAE-based plug-and-play detection provides a practical and effective safety enhancement for real-world VLM systems, marking a first step towards robust, low-overhead adversarial detection in VLMs.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have advanced rapidly and are increasingly deployed in real-world applications, especially with the rise of agent-based systems. However, their safety has received relatively limited attention. Even the latest proprietary and open-weight VLMs remain highly vulnerable to adversarial attacks, leaving downstream applications exposed to significant risks. In this work, we propose a novel and lightweight adversarial attack detection framework based on sparse autoencoders (SAEs), termed SAEgis. By inserting an SAE module into a pretrained VLM and training it with standard reconstruction objectives, we find that the learned sparse latent features naturally capture attack-relevant signals. These features enable reliable classification of whether an input image has been adversarially perturbed, even for previously unseen samples. Extensive experiments show that SAEgis achieves strong performance across in-domain, cross-domain, and cross-attack settings, with particularly large improvements in cross-domain generalization compared to existing baselines. In addition, combining signals from multiple layers further improves robustness and stability. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to explore SAE as a plug-and-play mechanism for adversarial attack detection in VLMs. Our method requires no additional adversarial training, introduces minimal overhead, and provides a practical approach for improving the safety of real-world VLM systems.

</details>


### [79] [EditTransfer++: Toward Faithful and Efficient Visual-Prompt-Guided Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07455)
*Lan Chen,Qi Mao,Yiren Song,Yuchao Gu,Siwei Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EditTransfer++ improves visual-prompt-guided image edits by decoupling textual conditioning, applying progressive structured training, a best-worst contrastive refinement, and efficient condition compression to enable high-resolution editing with faster, more faithful results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address misalignment between diffusion transformer backbones and the visual-prompt editing task, caused by: (1) pretrained textual conditioning bias, (2) stochastic sampling instability, and (3) high computational cost for 1024-pixel edits. The goal is faithful visual prompt adherence and efficient inference.

Method: 1) Text-decoupled fine-tuning to force learning from visual evidence while allowing optional text guidance at inference. 2) Best-worst contrastive refinement to reshape denoising trajectories, reducing unfaithful generations and improving seed-consistency. 3) Condition compression and reuse to lower token redundancy and enable 1024-pixel edge editing, achieving faster generation.

Result: EditTransfer++ achieves state-of-the-art visual prompt faithfulness and substantially faster inference compared to prior diffusion-transformer methods, demonstrated on existing benchmarks and a new EditTransfer-Bench.

Conclusion: Progressively structured training with an efficient conditioning scheme yields scalable, faithful visual prompt-guided editing and highlights a viable path for broader visual in-context learning.

Abstract: Visual-prompt-guided edit transfer aims to learn image transformations directly from example pairs, offering more precise and controllable editing than purely text-driven approaches. However, existing diffusion transformer-based methods often fail to faithfully reproduce the demonstrated edits due to structural mismatches between the task and the backbone, including a pretrained bias toward textual conditioning and inherent stochastic instability during sampling. To bridge this gap, we present EditTransfer++, a framework that combines progressively structured training with an efficient conditioning scheme to improve both visual prompt faithfulness and inference efficiency. We first mitigate textual dominance with a text-decoupled training strategy that removes text conditioning during fine-tuning, compelling the model to infer transformations solely from visual evidence while still supporting optional text guidance at inference. On top of this visually grounded model, a best-worst contrastive refinement mechanism reshapes the denoising trajectories to suppress unfaithful generations and improve consistency across random seeds. To alleviate the computational bottleneck of high-resolution in-context editing, we further introduce a condition compression and reuse strategy that reduces token redundancy and enables efficient generation of images with a 1024-pixel long edge. Extensive experiments on existing benchmarks and the proposed EditTransfer-Bench show that EditTransfer++ achieves state-of-the-art visual prompt faithfulness with substantially faster inference than prior methods, suggesting a promising direction for scalable prompt-guided image editing and broader visual in-context learning.

</details>


### [80] [EditRefiner: A Human-Aligned Agentic Framework for Image Editing Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07457)
*Zitong Xu,Huiyu Duan,Yifei Nie,Mingda Du,Sijing Wu,Xiongkuo Min,Tianyi Zheng,Jian Zhang,Shusong Xu,Jinwei Chen,Bo Li,Guangtao Zhai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces EditFHF-15K dataset and EditRefiner framework to self-correct text-guided image edits with a perception-reasoning-action-evaluation loop, achieving improved localization, diagnosis, and human-aligned refinements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address persistent fine-grained artifacts and misalignment in text-guided image editing due to costly regeneration and weak spatial grounding of vision-language models, and to provide a dataset for fine-grained feedback to enable reliable post-edit corrections.

Method: Construct EditFHF-15K: 15k edited images from 12 TIE models across 43 editing tasks; 60k artifact regions and 80k editing-failure regions with textual reasoning; 45k MOS assessments. Propose EditRefiner with a hierarchical perception–reasoning–action–evaluation loop: perception agent detects artifact/failure saliency; reasoning agent performs diagnostic inference; action agent plans/localized re-editing; evaluation agent assesses and guides further refinements.

Result: EditRefiner outperforms state-of-the-art methods in distortion localization, diagnostic accuracy, and alignment with human perception; demonstrates a self-corrective and perceptually reliable editing paradigm; code released at GitHub.

Conclusion: Presents a new human-aligned, interpretable agentic framework for robust post-edit correction in TIE, establishing a paradigm that combines fine-grained feedback with structured correction to enhance perceptual reliability.

Abstract: Recent text-guided image editing (TIE) models have made remarkable progress, yet edited images still frequently suffer from fine-grained issues such as unnatural objects, lighting mismatch, and unexpected changes. Existing refinement approaches either rely on costly iterative regeneration or employ vision-language models (VLMs) with weak spatial grounding, often resulting in semantic drift and unreliable local corrections. To address these limitations, we first construct EditFHF-15K, a dataset of fine-grained human feedback for edited images, comprising (1) 15K images from 12 TIE models spanning 43 editing tasks, (2) 60K annotated artifact regions and 80K editing failure regions, each accompanied by textual reasoning, and (3) 45K mean opinion scores (MOSs) assessing perceptual quality, instruction following, and visual consistency. Based on EditFHF-15K, we propose EditRefiner, a hierarchical, interpretable, and human-aligned agentic framework that reformulates post-editing correction as a human-like perception-reasoning-action-evaluation loop. Specifically, we introduce: (1) a perception agent that detects contextual saliency maps of artifacts and editing failures, (2) a reasoning agent that interprets these perceptual cues to perform human-aligned diagnostic inference, (3) an action agent that uses the reasoning output to plan and execute localized re-editing, and (4) an evaluation agent that assesses the re-edited image and guides the action agent on whether further refinements are required. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EditRefiner consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in distortion localization, diagnose accuracy and human perception alignment, establishing a new paradigm for self-corrective and perceptually reliable image editing. The code is available at https://github.com/IntMeGroup/EditRefiner.

</details>


### [81] [A Unified Framework for the Detection and Classification of Fatty Pancreas in Ultrasound Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07466)
*Ioan-Tudor-Alexandru Anghel,Ciprian-Mihai Ceausescu,Elena Dana Nedelcu,Elena Raluca Stirban,Camelia Croitoru,Despina Ungureanu,Ana Maria Palan,Gabriela Pop*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An end-to-end ultrasound-based fatty pancreas classifier using segmentation-guided texture analysis, leveraging TransUNet-based pancreas and splenic vein segmentation with anatomically-guided patch extraction and SVM classification; achieves ~89.7% cross-validated accuracy on a 214-image dataset, with 107 labeled cases, and claims novelty in segmentation-guided texture-based fat assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NAFPD is underdiagnosed and currently relies on subjective ultrasound interpretation. An objective, automated method could improve detection, standardization, and explainability by mimicking clinical reasoning.

Method: Use TransUNet (ResNet encoder with transformer bottleneck) to segment pancreas and splenic vein. Extract anatomically-guided patches and perform patient-level classification via pairwise texture comparison between peri-venous fat echogenicity and pancreatic parenchyma. Features derived to resemble clinical reasoning; segmentation models initialized by transfer learning from liver segmentation. Evaluate on 214 ultrasound images (107 labeled cases) with 5-fold cross-validation. Classify with SVM (RBF); unsupervised K-Means as baseline.

Result: Mean cross-validated accuracy: 89.7% ± 1.8%; F1 score: 0.898 ± 0.019. K-Means baseline accuracy: 87.8%.

Conclusion: Proposes the first end-to-end automated framework for fatty pancreas classification from ultrasound using segmentation-guided texture analysis; the proposed features capture the relevant clinical signal and perform well even with limited labeled data.

Abstract: Non-alcoholic fatty pancreas disease (NAFPD) is an underdiagnosed condition associated with metabolic syndrome, insulin resistance, and increased risk of pancreatic cancer. Diagnosis typically relies on subjective visual assessment of ultrasound images by clinicians. We propose an end-to-end framework for automatically classifying normal versus fatty pancreas from abdominal ultrasound images. Our method employs a TransUNet-based segmentation architecture with a ResNet encoder and transformer bottleneck to delineate the pancreas and the splenic vein, followed by anatomically-guided patch extraction and patient-level classification through pairwise texture comparison. The feature engineering mimics clinical reasoning by comparing the echogenicity of peri-venous fat to the pancreatic parenchyma, providing an interpretable signal for classification. The segmentation models are initialized via domain-specific transfer learning from a liver segmentation task. We validate the full pipeline on a clinical dataset of 214 abdominal ultrasound images with 107 expert-labeled cases using 5-fold cross-validation. SVM with RBF kernel achieves a mean cross-validated accuracy of 89.7\%\,$\pm$\,1.8\% and F1 of 0.898\,$\pm$\,0.019, while the unsupervised K-Means baseline reaches 87.8\% accuracy, demonstrating that the proposed features capture the relevant clinical signal even without labeled training data. To our knowledge, this is the first end-to-end automated framework for fatty pancreas classification from ultrasound using segmentation-guided texture analysis.

</details>


### [82] [Consensus Entropy: Harnessing Multi-VLM Agreement for Self-Verifying and Self-Improving OCR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.11101)
*Yulong Zhang,Tianyi Liang,Xinyue Huang,Erfei Cui,Guoqing Wang,Xu Guo,Chenhui Li,Gongshen Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-freeconsensus-entropy metric (CE) estimates OCR output reliability via inter-model agreement entropy, enabling a model-agnostic CE-OCR ensemble that verifi es outputs, selects best results, and routes adaptively; yields significant F1 gains over single-model or self-consistency baselines with no training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of unsupervised quality control and sample-level error detection in OCR for VLMs. Develop a training-free, model-agnostic metric to quantify output reliability via ensemble disagreement for reliable output verification.

Method: Define Consensus Entropy (CE) as the entropy of inter-model agreement across multiple models. Build CE-OCR: a lightweight, training-free multi-model framework that uses ensemble agreement to verify outputs, selects top outputs, and employs adaptive routing to reduce computation.

Result: CE is robust for quality verification; CE-OCR outperforms single-model baselines and self-consistency at the same cost, achieving OCR gains with improved F1 scores (notably +42.1% over VLM-as-Judge). CE requires no training or supervision and can be plugged into existing pipelines (code provided).

Conclusion: CE provides a practical, plug-and-play quality control metric for OCR in VLM systems. CE-OCR demonstrates consistent OCR gains and more efficient verification via ensemble agreement, suggesting broader applicability to unsupervised quality control in vision-language tasks.

Abstract: Optical Character Recognition (OCR) is fundamental to Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and high-quality data generation for LLM training. Yet, despite progress in average OCR accuracy, state-of-the-art VLMs still struggle with detecting sample-level errors and lack effective unsupervised quality control. We introduce Consensus Entropy (CE), a training-free, model-agnostic metric that estimates output reliability by measuring inter-model agreement entropy. The core insight is that correct predictions converge in output space, while errors diverge. Based on CE, we develop CE-OCR, a lightweight multi-model framework that verifies outputs by ensemble agreement, selects the best outputs, and further improves efficiency through adaptive routing. Experiments demonstrate that CE is robust for quality verification, improving F1 scores by 42.1% over VLM-as-Judge. CE-OCR achieves consistent OCR gains, outperforming self-consistency and single-model baselines at the same cost. Notably, CE requires no training or supervision, enabling plug-and-play integration. Code: https://github.com/Aslan-yulong/consensus-entropy.

</details>


### [83] [ForgeVLA: Federated Vision-Language-Action Learning without Language Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07474)
*Yuhao Zhou,Yunpeng Zhu,Yang Zhou,Jindi Lyu,Jian Lan,Zhangyuan Wang,Dan Si,Thomas Seidl,Qing Ye,Jiancheng Lyu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ForgeVLA is a federated vision-language-action training framework that learns from distributed vision-action pairs without centralizing data, reconstructs language via an on-device instruction classifier to form vision-language-action triplets, mitigates vision-language feature collapse with a client-side contrastive planning loss and a server-side adaptive aggregation, and demonstrates strong performance improvements over baselines with ablation validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models promise broad robotic intelligence but require large annotated datasets. Centralized data collection is expensive and often infeasible due to privacy, bandwidth, and heterogeneity across robots. There is abundant, heterogeneous vision-action data on robots that remains untapped due to fragmentation and lack of language annotations. A federated approach can harness this data without sharing raw sensor data or requiring manual labeling.

Method: Each client runs an embodied instruction classifier that converts vision-action pairs into a predefined instruction set, effectively reconstructing the missing language modality and producing complete vision-language-action triplets. The framework addresses vision-language feature collapse via (1) a client-side contrastive planning loss and (2) a server-side adaptive aggregation strategy to learn discriminative, task-aligned representations across heterogeneous clients. Federated training is performed across distributed robots without centralizing raw data or language annotations.

Result: Empirical evaluation across multiple benchmarks shows ForgeVLA significantly outperforms strong baselines. Ablation studies corroborate the importance of each component (instruction-based triplet construction, client-side contrastive loss, and server-side adaptive aggregation) for performance gains.

Conclusion: ForgeVLA provides an effective solution for scaling VLA models through federated learning, addressing data heterogeneity and the absence of centralized annotations while delivering improved task-discriminative representations and strong empirical gains.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models hold great promise for general-purpose robotic intelligence, yet scaling up such models is severely bottlenecked by the high cost of acquiring annotated training data. Fortunately, vision-equipped robots deployed across various domains already produce abundant vision-action pairs that can be leveraged to scale up VLA training more efficiently. However, these raw data cannot be centrally aggregated due to various constraints and also exhibit severe heterogeneity. To address these challenges, in this paper, we propose ForgeVLA, a federated VLA training framework that learns VLA models from distributed vision-action pairs without centralizing raw data or requiring manual annotations. Specifically, each client in ForgeVLA is equipped with an embodied instruction classifier that maps vision-action pairs to a predefined instruction set, recovering the missing language modality and forming complete vision-language-action triplets. Beyond triplet construction, we also identify vision-language feature collapse as a critical challenge that has been largely overlooked in prior federated VLA research. To mitigate this issue, ForgeVLA combines a client-side contrastive planning loss with a server-side adaptive aggregation strategy to learn task-discriminative representations efficiently. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks show that ForgeVLA significantly outperforms other baselines, and ablation studies further validate the contribution of each component.

</details>


### [84] [ReasonEdit: Towards Interpretable Image Editing Evaluation via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07477)
*Honghua Chen,Zitong Xu,Huiyu Duan,Xinyun Zhang,Xiongkuo Min,Guangtao Zhai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReasonEdit proposes a comprehensive interpretable evaluation framework for text-guided image editing, centered on ReasonEdit-22K (22K edited images with 113K Chain-of-Thought samples and 1.3M human judgments) and RE-Reward (an MLLM-based reward model). ReasonEdit is trained via GRPO to produce an interpretable evaluation model that aligns with human preferences and can generate interpretable evaluation text, showing strong generalization on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current TIE evaluation relies on scalar metrics with limited interpretability and lacks high-quality interpretable data and reward models to train evaluators. There is a need for datasets and models that can assess interpretable reasoning in editing.

Method: Construct ReasonEdit-22K dataset (22K edited images + 113K CoT samples + 1.3M judgments on logicality/accuracy/usefulness). Develop RE-Reward, an MLLM-based reward model for human-aligned feedback on interpretable reasoning. Train ReasonEdit using GRPO with rewards from RE-Reward to learn an interpretable evaluation model, capable of generating interpretable evaluation text.

Result: ReasonEdit achieves superior alignment with human preferences and strong generalization across public benchmarks; it can produce high-quality interpretable evaluation text. Code is released at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: The framework provides transparent, trustworthy evaluation for TIE by combining a large-scale interpretable data resource with a reinforcement-learning-based evaluator, enabling better interpretability and reliability in image-editing assessment.

Abstract: Recent text-guided image editing (TIE) models have achieved remarkable progress, however, many edited results still suffer from artifacts, unintended modifications, and suboptimal aesthetics. Although several benchmarks and evaluation methods have been proposed, most existing approaches rely on scalar scores and lack interpretability. This limitation largely stems from the absence of high-quality interpretation datasets for TIE and effective reward models to train interpretable evaluators. To address these challenges, we introduce ReasonEdit-22K, the first dataset that combines 22K edited images with 113K Chain-of-Thought (CoT) samples, along with 1.3M human judgments assessing these interpretations in terms of logicality, accuracy, and usefulness. Building upon this dataset, we propose RE-Reward, a multimodal large language model (MLLM)-based reward model designed to provide human-aligned feedback for evaluating interpretable reasoning in image editing. Furthermore, we develop ReasonEdit, which is trained using reward signals derived from RE-Reward and the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm to learn an interpretable evaluation model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ReasonEdit achieves superior alignment with human preferences and exhibits strong generalization across public benchmarks. In addition, it is capable of generating high-quality interpretable evaluation text, enabling more transparent and trustworthy assessment for image editing. The code is available at https://github.com/IntMeGroup/ReasonEdit.

</details>


### [85] [AudioFace: Language-Assisted Speech-Driven Facial Animation with Multimodal Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07478)
*Kai Zheng,Zejian Kang,Rui Mao,Hongyuan Zou,Yuanchen Fei,Xuanyang Xu,Xiangru Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AudioFace uses language-assisted priors to drive speech-to-blendshape facial animation, outperforming audio-only mappings by incorporating transcript and phoneme cues guided by multimodal LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Direct audio-to-facial mapping neglects linguistic/phonetic structure; a structured generation approach can leverage articulatory information to improve mouth movements accuracy.

Method: Introduce AudioFace; integrate transcript- and phoneme-level cues; leverage multimodal large language models as priors to bridge speech signals with mouth-related facial coefficients; treat facial motion as structured generation conditioned on linguistic information.

Result: Experimental results show superior performance across multiple evaluation metrics, validating the language-assisted and multimodal-prior-guided approach.

Conclusion: Language-assisted, multimodal-prior-guided speech-driven facial animation is effective and enhances interpretability by aligning facial actions with linguistic structure.

Abstract: Speech-driven facial animation requires accurate correspondence between acoustic signals and facial motion, especially for articulation-related mouth movements. However, directly mapping speech audio to facial coefficients often overlooks the linguistic and phonetic structure underlying speech production. In this paper, we propose AudioFace, a language-assisted framework for speech-driven blendshape generation that treats mouth-related facial coefficient prediction as a structured generation problem guided by linguistic and articulatory information. Instead of relying solely on acoustic features, our method leverages the prior knowledge of multimodal large language models and introduces transcript- and phoneme-level cues to bridge speech signals with interpretable facial actions. Extensive experiments show that AudioFace achieves superior performance across multiple evaluation metrics, validating the effectiveness of language-assisted and multimodal-prior-guided speech-driven facial animation.

</details>


### [86] [Implicit Multi-Camera System Calibration Using Gaussian Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07491)
*Ivan De Boi,Bart Ribbens,Veronika Golanova,Ursula Kapov,Simon Verspeek*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A Gaussian Process (GP) based implicit calibration framework for multi-camera systems that learns a 2D-UV to 3D mapping across all cameras, bypassing explicit intrinsics/extrinsics, with Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) and Active Learning (AL) to enable data-efficient, uncertainty-aware calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explicit calibration methods struggle with complex, non-linear distortions from unconventional optics and NN-based implicit methods require大量 data and lack reliable uncertainty estimates. A probabilistic, data-efficient, uncertainty-aware approach is needed for practical, complex multi-camera calibration.

Method: Train a GP to map concatenated 2D image coordinates from all cameras (uv) to a 3D world coordinate, avoiding explicit intrinsic/extrinsic parameter estimation. Use GP uncertainty as a natural uncertainty quantification. Integrate Active Learning to sample data where the GP's predictive uncertainty is highest, thus improving data efficiency and calibration reliability.

Result: The approach provides predictive uncertainty for 3D points, with higher uncertainty in regions where uv-space data is sparse (near camera projections). AL enhances data efficiency; experiments demonstrate robust 3D predictions with quantifiable confidence bounds, especially under limited calibration data.

Conclusion: A data-efficient, uncertainty-aware calibration framework for complex multi-camera systems that bypasses explicit parameter estimation, leveraging GP-based mapping and Active Learning to achieve reliable 3D measurements with credible uncertainty estimates.

Abstract: This paper proposes a novel framework for implicit multi-camera system calibration utilizing Gaussian Process (GP) regression. Conventional explicit calibration methods are constrained by rigid mathematical models and struggle with complex, non-linear distortions from unconventional optics, while existing neural network-based implicit approaches are typically data-hungry and lack inherent uncertainty quantification (UQ). Our GP-based model directly learns the complex, non-linear mapping from 2D image coordinates across all cameras to a 3D world coordinate, completely bypassing time-consuming estimation of explicit intrinsic and extrinsic parameters. Moreover, the inherent UQ is critical for transforming a simple 3D point prediction into a verifiable 3D measurement, complete with statistically-sound confidence bounds. To further enhance data efficiency and practical deployment, we integrate Active Learning (AL), which intelligently leverages the GP's predictive uncertainty to strategically guide the acquisition of new calibration data. This approach results in a robust, data-efficient, and reliable calibration solution, proving particularly effective in practical scenarios where collecting extensive calibration data is a dominant constraint. Our experiments show that the uncertainty for the 3D predictions is higher closer to the cameras. The data points in $uv$-coordinate space are more sparse in that region, even though they are not in 3D space. This work is relevant for anyone who is tasked with the calibration of complex multi-camera systems.

</details>


### [87] [How Far Is Document Parsing from Solved? PureDocBench: A Source-TraceableBenchmark across Clean, Degraded, and Real-World Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07492)
*Zhiheng Li,Zongyang Ma,Jiaxian Chen,Jianing Zhang,Zhaolong Su,Yutong Zhang,Zhiyin Yu,Ruiqi Liu,Xiaolei Lv,Bo Li,Jun Gao,Ziqi Zhang,Chunfeng Yuan,Bing Li,Weiming Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PureDocBench is a programmatically generated, source-traceable benchmark for document parsing that addresses OmniDocBench issues; evaluation across 40 models shows specialization matters, and degradation effects alter rankings, with best models around 74/100.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OmniDocBench’s annotation quality and contamination risk undermine fair benchmarking; a traceable, diverse benchmark across domains and degradation conditions is needed.

Method: Create a source-generated dataset by rendering HTML/CSS documents into images across 10 domains, 66 subcategories, and 1,475 pages, yielding 4,425 images in three versions (clean, digitally degraded, real-degraded); provide verifiable annotations from the same source; assess 40 models spanning pipeline specialists, end-to-end specialists, and general VLMs; release all data, code, and artifacts publicly.

Result: Top model ~74/100; 44.6-point gap between best and worst; specialist parsers with <=4B parameters rival larger VLMs; formula recognition remains a bottleneck with no model exceeding 67% across tracks; general VLMs suffer small degradation (0.99/8.52 points digitally/real) versus pipeline specialists (4.90/14.21); ranking reversals imply clean-only evaluation is misleading; 12.08% of blocks flagged as errors in OmniDocBench raise concerns about annotation quality.

Conclusion: PureDocBench offers a traceable, diverse benchmark to better assess document parsing, reveals ongoing bottlenecks like formula recognition, and demonstrates the importance of degradation-aware evaluation for deployment; all resources are publicly available.

Abstract: The past year has seen over 20 open-source document parsing models, yet thefield still benchmarks almost exclusively on OmniDocBench, a 1,355-pagemanually annotated dataset whose top scores have saturated above 90%. Athree-stage audit pipeline we run on OmniDocBench screens its 21,353evaluator-scored blocks and confirms 2,580 errors (12.08%); combined with overa year of public availability, both annotation quality and contamination riskcall its rankings into question. To address these issues, we presentPureDocBench, a programmatically generated, source-traceable benchmark thatrenders document images from HTML/CSS and produces verifiable annotations fromthe same source, covering 10 domains, 66 subcategories, and 1,475 pages, eachin three versions: clean, digitally degraded, and real-degraded (4,425 imagestotal). Evaluating 40 models spanning pipeline specialists, end-to-endspecialists, and general-purpose VLMs, we find: (i) document parsing is farfrom solved: the best model scores only ~74 out of 100, with a 44.6-point gapbetween the strongest and weakest models; (ii) specialist parsers with <=4Bparameters rival or surpass general VLMs that are 5-100x larger, yet formularecognition remains a shared bottleneck where no model exceeds 67% whenaveraging the formula metric across all three tracks; (iii) general VLMs loseonly 0.99/8.52 Overall points under digital/real degradation versus 4.90/14.21for pipeline specialists, producing ranking reversals that make clean-onlyevaluation misleading for deployment. All data, code, and artifacts arepublicly released.

</details>


### [88] [DIMoE-Adapters: Dynamic Expert Evolution for Continual Learning in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07494)
*Mengxin Qin,Xiang Zhang,Xi Wang,Kun Wei,Xu Yang,Cheng Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dynamic incremental mixture-of-experts adapters (DIMoE-Adapters) for continual learning in multi-domain vision-language tasks; introduces Self-Calibrated Expert Evolution (SCEE) and Prototype-Guided Expert Selection (PGES) to dynamically evolve and select sparse expert pools, achieving state-of-the-art performance and balanced stability-plasticity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multi-domain task-incremental learning with large domain shifts, fixed architectures struggle to adapt and suffer severe forgetting due to the stability-plasticity dilemma. Dynamic, scalable mechanisms are needed to grow and selectively utilize capacity as tasks evolve.

Method: DIMoE-Adapters implement a dynamic expert evolution paradigm with two components: (1) Self-Calibrated Expert Evolution (SCEE) constructs and evolves a sparse pool of experts via optimization dynamics to enhance plasticity while avoiding redundant capacity; (2) Prototype-Guided Expert Selection (PGES) governs expert usage based on the SCEE-shaped pool, promoting stability across previously seen and unseen tasks.

Result: Empirical results show DIMoE-Adapters outperform prior state-of-the-art methods across multiple settings in continual learning with large domain shifts.

Conclusion: A dynamic, jointly optimized pool of lightweight experts with prototype-guided selection can effectively balance stability and plasticity in multi-domain continual vision-language models, mitigating catastrophic forgetting.

Abstract: Continual learning enables vision-language models to accumulate knowledge and adapt to evolving tasks without retraining from scratch. However, in multi-domain task-incremental learning, large domain shifts intensify the stability-plasticity dilemma. Most existing methods rely on fixed architectures with statically allocated parameters, which limits adaptation to new domains and aggravates catastrophic forgetting. To address these challenges, we propose DIMoE-Adapters, a Dynamic Incremental Mixture-of-Experts Adapters framework that introduces a dynamic expert evolution paradigm to balance stability and plasticity. This paradigm is implemented through two collaborative components: Self-Calibrated Expert Evolution (SCEE) and Prototype-Guided Expert Selection (PGES). SCEE constructs and evolves a sparse expert pool through expert optimization dynamics, improving plasticity while reducing redundant capacity. PGES controls expert utilization based on the pool shaped by SCEE, improving stability across both previously encountered and unseen tasks. Extensive experiments show that DIMoE-Adapters outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods across various settings.

</details>


### [89] [Lightweight Unpaired Smartphone ISP Transfer with Semantic Pseudo-Pairing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07495)
*Yujin Cho,Flavien Armangeon,Yanhao Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A semantically guided unpaired smartphone ISP method using patch-based context, DINOv2 embeddings, and FGW optimal transport to create pseudo pairs, trained with a tiny CNN for robust color rendering with competitive metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unpaired RAW→RGB mappings lack scene/color alignment; adversarial approaches are unstable; a stable, lightweight, unpaired learning framework is desired for smartphone ISP.

Method: 1) Upsample training patches to recover global context; 2) extract semantic embeddings with DINOv2; 3) use fused Gromov-Wasserstein (FGW) OT to establish pseudo pairs at image and patch levels; 4) train a compact 7k-parameter CNN focused on color transformation.

Result: Achieves 22.569 PSNR, 0.675 SSIM, and 8.067 ΔE on the final hidden test; ranks 3rd in SSIM and ΔE among challenge entries; code released at github.com/nuniniyujin/Unpaired-ISP.

Conclusion: Semantic-driven pseudo pairing reduces the unpaired data gap and enables stable, artifact-resistant color rendering with a tiny model, suitable for real-time smartphone ISP pipelines.

Abstract: Unpaired smartphone ISP is a challenging problem due to the lack of scene and color alignment between RAW and target RGB images. Many existing methods either require paired data or rely heavily on adversarial training, which can become unstable in the unpaired setting. In this work, we present a simple and effective approach developed for the NTIRE 2026 Learned Smartphone ISP Challenge with Unpaired Data. Our method first reconstructs larger images from training patches to recover global context. Then, we extract semantic embeddings with DINOv2, and use fused Gromov-Wasserstein (FGW) optimal transport to build pseudo pairs between RAW and RGB images at both image and patch levels. This semantic matching allows us to partially alleviate the unpairedness of the data and build these pseudo input-target pairs. Based on these pseudo pairs, we train a lightweight CNN with only 7K parameters for color rendering. The network is designed to be compact and focus on color transformation rather than structural change, which helps reduce artifacts and improve training stability. Our challenge submission achieves 22.569 PSNR, 0.675 SSIM, and 8.067 $ΔE$ on the final hidden test set, significantly improving over the baseline and achieving the 3rd best SSIM and $ΔE$ among all challenge entries. Our code is available at github.com/nuniniyujin/Unpaired-ISP .

</details>


### [90] [Cloud-top infrared observations reveal the four-dimensional precipitation structure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07499)
*Tianchi Xu,Ziqiang Ma,Andrea Marinoni,Yuanpeng He,Xiaoqing Li,Chuanfeng Zhao,Kang He,Jintao Xu,Bohan Zhou,Wenbo Zhao,Haoshuang Chen,Tun Wang,Dongdong Wang,Yang Hong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Cloud-top infrared data can encode sub-cloud precipitation; a physically constrained deep learning model (4DPrecipNet) recovers the 4D structure of precipitation by forcing latent space to predict precipitable water vapour, enabling vertical-temporal precipitation reconstruction from geostationary IR radiances and radar data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the global gap in 4D precipitation observations by leveraging information hidden in cloud-top IR measurements, and enforce thermodynamic consistency to link radiances to precipitable water and precipitation structure.

Method: Propose 4DPrecipNet, a physically constrained deep learning framework with a moisture-first constraint that requires the latent representation to recover precipitable water vapour. Combines multi-channel infrared radiances with this constraint and radar-derived precipitation profiles to reconstruct the vertical and temporal evolution of precipitation systems from geostationary orbit.

Result: The framework captures deep convective structures and their evolution with robust performance across large samples and shows agreement with independent radar measurements, demonstrating that sub-cloud precipitation is encoded in cloud-top IR observations.

Conclusion: Sub-cloud precipitation is physically encoded in cloud-top infrared observations, enabling a new pathway for continuous global monitoring of precipitation structure via GOES-era radiances and similar datasets.

Abstract: Accurate four-dimensional (4D) precipitation information is essential for understanding the Earth's energy and water cycles, yet remains observationally unresolved at global scales. Conventional theory holds that geostationary infrared observations primarily sense cloud-top properties, with limited sensitivity to sub-cloud precipitation. Here we show that cloud-top infrared measurements nevertheless encode sufficient information to recover the four-dimensional structure of precipitation, revealing a previously unexploited observability of sub-cloud processes. We introduce a physically constrained deep learning framework, 4DPrecipNet, in which a moisture-first constraint requires the latent representation to recover precipitable water vapour, anchoring the model in thermodynamic consistency. By integrating multi-channel infrared radiances with these constraints and radar-derived precipitation profiles, we reconstruct the vertical and temporal evolution of precipitation systems from geostationary orbit. The framework captures deep convective structures and their evolution, with robust performance across large samples and independent radar comparisons. These results demonstrate that sub-cloud precipitation is physically encoded in cloud-top infrared observations, establishing a new pathway for continuous global monitoring of precipitation structure.

</details>


### [91] [Diffusion-APO: Trajectory-Aware Direct Preference Alignment for Video Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07503)
*Jingyuan Zhu,Biaolong Chen,Le Zhang,Aixi Zhang,Hao Jiang,Pipei Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-APO introduces trajectory-aware alignment for video diffusion models and a modular RLHF framework to unify online/offline preference learning, achieving improved visual quality and instruction following while maintaining fidelity under acceleration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the misalignment between training noise distributions and practical inference denoising trajectories. Critiques Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for relying on bias-prone reward models or suboptimal sampling; aims for scalable, trajectory-aware alignment for video diffusion.

Method: Propose Diffusion-APO to synchronize training noise with inference-time denoising paths to maximize gradient signal efficacy. Develop a unified RLHF framework with online ranking, half-online anchoring, offline refinement, and distillation-aware drift correction; supports multi-stage preference alignment across diverse data and compute constraints without scalar rewards.

Result: Empirically, Diffusion-APO outperforms standard baselines in visual quality and instruction following and preserves generative fidelity during model acceleration, demonstrating an end-to-end scalable pathway for video diffusion alignment.

Conclusion: Diffusion-APO provides a scalable, trajectory-aware alignment method and a modular RLHF framework that enables robust multi-stage preference alignment without scalar reward gradients, suitable for large-scale video diffusion models.

Abstract: Efficiently aligning large-scale video diffusion models with human intent requires a scalable and trajectory-aware pathway that bridges the inherent discrepancy between training noise distributions and practical inference trajectories. While existing paradigms such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) attempt to address this, they are often hindered by either reliance on bias-prone, complex reward models or suboptimal timestep sampling. In this paper, we propose Diffusion-APO (Aligned Preference Optimization), a trajectory-aware algorithm that resolves this misalignment by synchronizing training noise with inference-time denoising paths to maximize gradient signal efficacy. To translate this algorithmic innovation into a practical solution, we introduce a unified and modular RLHF framework that integrates online ranking, half-online anchoring, offline refinement, and distillation-aware drift correction. This framework enables flexible, multi-stage preference alignment across diverse data and computational constraints without relying on scalar-reward-based policy gradients. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that Diffusion-APO consistently outperforms standard baselines in visual quality and instruction following, while effectively preserving generative fidelity during model acceleration, providing a robust, end-to-end pathway for scalable video diffusion alignment.

</details>


### [92] [InterLV-Search: Benchmarking Interleaved Multimodal Agentic Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07510)
*Bohan Hou,Jiuning Gu,Jiayan Guo,Ronghao Dang,Sicong Leng,Xin Li,Xuemeng Song,Jianfei Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces InterLV-Search, a benchmark and toolset for interleaved language-vision agentic search with three levels of multimodal evidence use; shows current models lag with <50% accuracy, highlighting gaps in visual evidence seeking, search control, and multimodal integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks lock visual evidence to the input or treat it as a final answer, not as part of an interleaved search trajectory. This work motivates evaluating end-to-end capability to use textual and visual cues iteratively to guide search and reasoning.

Method: Constructs 2,061 examples across three levels: Level 1 (active visual evidence seeking), Level 2 (controlled offline interleaved multimodal search), Level 3 (open-web interleaved multimodal search). Levels 1-2 built with automated pipelines; Level 3 via machine-led, human-supervised open-web pipeline. Provides InterLV-Agent to standardize tool use, log trajectories, and enable evaluation. Includes multimodal multi-branch samples comparing multiple entities. Evaluates both proprietary and open-source multimodal agents.

Result: Current systems perform far from solving interleaved multimodal search; the best model achieves below 50% overall accuracy, underscoring difficulties in obtaining visual evidence, controlling the search process, and integrating multimodal cues.

Conclusion: The benchmark and accompanying agent framework are released to spur progress in interleaved multimodal search and tool use, with demonstrated gaps indicating substantial room for improvement in visual evidence seeking, search control, and multimodal evidence integration.

Abstract: Existing benchmarks for multimodal agentic search evaluate multimodal search and visual browsing, but visual evidence is either confined to the input or treated as an answer endpoint rather than part of an interleaved search trajectory. We introduce \textbf{InterLV-Search}, a benchmark for Interleaved Language-Vision Agentic Search, in which textual and visual evidence is repeatedly used to condition later search. It contains 2,061 examples across three levels: active visual evidence seeking, controlled offline interleaved multimodal search, and open-web interleaved multimodal search. Beyond existing benchmarks, it also includes multimodal multi-branch samples that involve comparison between multiple entities during the evidence search. We construct Level 1 and Level 2 with automated pipelines and Level 3 with a machine-led, human-supervised open-web pipeline. We further provide InterLV-Agent for standardized tool use, trajectory logging, and evaluation. Experiments on proprietary and open-source multimodal agents show that current systems remain far from solving interleaved multimodal search, with the best model below 50% overall accuracy, highlighting challenges in visual evidence seeking, search control, and multimodal evidence integration. We release the benchmark data and evaluation code at https://github.com/hbhalpha/InterLV-Search-Bench

</details>


### [93] [Hierarchical Dual-Subspace Decoupling for Continual Learning in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07512)
*Mengxin Qin,Xiang Zhang,Kun Wei,Xu Yang,Cheng Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HDSD: a hierarchical dual-subspace decoupling framework for continual learning in vision-language models that decomposes parameter updates into general and task-specific subspaces via a lightweight Feature Modulation Module, with a General Fusion Module for stable knowledge and a Hierarchical Learning Module using SVD to constrain updates across subspace scales; claims state-of-the-art performance with reduced subspace interference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning, addressing subspace interference caused by updates lying in overlapping low-rank subspaces in high-dimensional parameter spaces.

Method: Introduce a lightweight Feature Modulation Module (FMM) to split parameter space into general vs task-specific subspaces. Develop a General Fusion Module (GFM) to evaluate relative parameter changes across tasks and apply an adaptive threshold to identify stable/transferable knowledge. Develop a Hierarchical Learning Module (HLM) that performs structured parameter decomposition via Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) and uses a scaling mechanism to constrain updates within distinct subspace scales.

Result: Extensive experiments on conventional benchmarks show that HDSD achieves state-of-the-art results in continual learning for vision-language models.

Conclusion: The approach reduces subspace interference and parameter drift, enabling improved continual learning performance in vision-language models.

Abstract: Class-incremental learning aims to continuously acquire new knowledge while preserving previously learned information, thereby mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Existing methods primarily restrict parameter updates but often overlook their structural properties in high-dimensional spaces. From a subspace perspective, updates induced by different tasks tend to lie in multiple overlapping low-rank subspaces, leading to cross-task subspace interference and severe forgetting. To address this issue, we propose HDSD, a Hierarchical Dual-Subspace Decoupling framework for continual learning in vision-language models. Specifically, we introduce a lightweight Feature Modulation Module (FMM) that explicitly decomposes the parameter space into general and task-specific subspaces. Building on this design, we develop two complementary components. First, a General Fusion Module (GFM) evaluates relative parameter changes across tasks and uses an adaptive threshold to capture stable and transferable knowledge. Second, a Hierarchical Learning Module (HLM) performs structured parameter decomposition via Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) and uses a scaling mechanism to constrain updates within distinct subspace scales. Together, these designs reduce subspace interference and parameter drift. Extensive experiments on conventional benchmarks show that HDSD achieves state-of-the-art results.

</details>


### [94] [Implicit Preference Alignment for Human Image Animation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07545)
*Yuanzhi Wang,Xuhua Ren,Jiaxiang Cheng,Bing Ma,Kai Yu,Tianxiang Zheng,Qinglin Lu,Zhen Cui*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An implicit preference alignment (IPA) framework for post-training hand animation that eliminates explicit preference data by maximizing the likelihood of self-generated high-quality samples while staying close to the pretrained prior, with a Hand-Aware Local Optimization to focus on hand regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-fidelity hand motion in human image animation is difficult due to high degrees of freedom and motion complexity. Collecting strict, frame-consistent preference data for RLHF is expensive and impractical, hindering data-efficient optimization.

Method: Introduce Implicit Preference Alignment (IPA) that optimizes a model by maximizing the likelihood of its own high-quality samples and penalizing deviations from the pretrained prior (implicit reward maximization). Add a Hand-Aware Local Optimization module to explicitly steer alignment toward hand regions.

Result: Experiments show IPA achieves effective preference optimization for improved hand generation quality with substantially reduced need for paired preference data; code is released.

Conclusion: IPA provides a data-efficient post-training approach to preference alignment that reduces data collection burdens while improving hand-region generation quality, with a practical hand-aware optimization component.

Abstract: Human image animation has witnessed significant advancements, yet generating high-fidelity hand motions remains a persistent challenge due to their high degrees of freedom and motion complexity. While reinforcement learning from human feedback, particularly direct preference optimization, offers a potential solution, it necessitates the construction of strict preference pairs. However, curating such pairs for dynamic hand regions is prohibitively expensive and often impractical due to frame-wise inconsistencies. In this paper, we propose Implicit Preference Alignment (IPA), a data-efficient post-training framework that eliminates the need for paired preference data. Theoretically grounded in implicit reward maximization, IPA aligns the model by maximizing the likelihood of self-generated high-quality samples while penalizing deviations from the pretrained prior. Furthermore, we introduce a Hand-Aware Local Optimization mechanism to explicitly steer the alignment process toward hand regions. Experiments demonstrate that our method achieves effective preference optimization to enhance hand generation quality, while significantly lowering the barrier for constructing preference data. Codes are released at https://github.com/mdswyz/IPA

</details>


### [95] [Probabilistic Object Detection with Conformal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07549)
*Christopher Ries,Moussa Kassem Sbeyti,Nicolas Bianco,Nadja Klein*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Scaled, coordinate-wise conformal prediction with uncertainty-based scaling improves prediction-interval sharpness in multi-output object detection while preserving coverage, across datasets and distribution shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Object detection requires multiple outputs; standard CP yields fixed-width intervals and may be inefficient; need systematic comparison of unscaled vs scaled CP in multi-class detection and integration with complementary UQ methods.

Method: Apply CP coordinate-wise to bounding box corners with Bonferroni for box-level coverage; scale CP intervals using per-prediction aleatoric uncertainty from a probabilistic detector with loss attenuation, test in uncalibrated and two calibrated variants; add a two-step pipeline where class sets are constructed with RAPS and bounding boxes conditioned on the class set.

Result: Scaled CP shows improved interval sharpness over unscaled CP across KITTI, BDD, CODA, including cross-domain shift; up to 19% higher IoU and 39% lower interval scores, with preserved coverage; class-wise calibration further improves coverage with negligible impact on sharpness.

Conclusion: Scaled CP with calibrated uncertainty integration yields more actionable, reliable uncertainty estimates for real-time object detection; demonstrates benefit of combining CP with aleatoric scaling and RAPS in multi-output settings.

Abstract: Conformal Prediction (CP) is a distribution-free method for constructing prediction sets with marginal finite-sample coverage guarantees, making it a suitable framework for reliable uncertainty quantification in safety-critical object detection. However, object detection introduces structured multi-output predictions, complicating the application of classical CP theory developed for single outputs. In addition, standard, unscaled CP produces fixed-width prediction intervals across inputs, leading to unnecessary width for low-uncertainty predictions. While scaled CP addresses this by adapting the interval width to an input-dependent uncertainty estimate, prior work has neither systematically compared unscaled and scaled CP for multi-class object detection, nor integrated CP with a complementary uncertainty quantification method in this setting. We fill this gap by: (i) applying CP coordinate-wise to bounding box corners with a Bonferroni correction for box-level guarantees; (ii) scaling the resulting intervals using per-prediction aleatoric uncertainty estimates derived from a probabilistic object detector trained with loss attenuation, evaluated in uncalibrated and two calibrated variants; (iii) extending to a two-step pipeline that constructs prediction sets for the class using RAPS and conditions the conformalized bounding boxes on the predicted class set. Across three autonomous driving datasets (KITTI, BDD, CODA), including a cross-domain setting under distribution shift, scaled CP consistently improves interval sharpness over unscaled CP, achieving up to 19% higher IoU and 39% lower interval scores, without sacrificing coverage. Class-wise calibration further improves coverage for both variants with a negligible effect on sharpness. Together, these improvements yield more actionable uncertainty estimates for real-time, real-world object detection.

</details>


### [96] [Mind the Gap: Geometrically Accurate Generative Reconstruction from Disjoint Views](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07550)
*Grzegorz Wilczynski,Mikołaj Zielinski,Bartosz Świrta,Dominik Belter,Przemysław Spurek*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GLADOS is a modular framework for zero-overlap 3D reconstruction that uses generative bridging, robust coarse reconstruction, and iterative consistency optimization to connect disjoint views; it includes a new dataset and metrics, benchmarks against state-of-the-art, and a public implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconstruction methods depend on geometric or appearance overlap, which is impractical in distributed data collection scenarios (e.g., swarm robotics, crowd-sourced data). There is a need for zero-overlap reconstruction that can connect disjoint inputs via generative synthesis.

Method: Three-stage architecture: (1) Generative Bridging—foundation models synthesize intermediate perspectives to connect disjoint inputs; (2) Robust Coarse 3D Reconstruction—global alignment to create a coarse geometric scaffold that tolerates local generative contradictions; (3) Iterative Context Expansion and Consistency Optimization—fill missing regions and unify reconstruction; architecture-agnostic, modular for integration with future generation, reconstruction, and inpainting methods. Dataset and evaluation metrics for zero-overlap scenarios are provided, and source code is released.

Result: Benchmarks indicate existing state-of-the-art methods fail catastrophically on zero-overlap tasks, yielding disconnected geometries or semantically incoherent reconstructions; GLADOS achieves more coherent and connected reconstructions, and demonstrates robustness to disjoint inputs; a dedicated dataset and metrics support zero-overlap evaluation.

Conclusion: GLADOS presents a general, modular paradigm for connecting disjoint views in 3D reconstruction via generative synthesis and robust optimization, enabling future advances and broader applicability in real-world zero-overlap scenarios.

Abstract: 3D vision systems are fundamentally constrained by their reliance on visual overlap: reconstruction methods require it for geometric alignment, while generative models use it to enforce multi-view consistency. This limitation is particularly acute in real-world scenarios such as distributed swarm robotics or crowd-sourced data collection, where capturing overlapping perspectives, both in terms of spatial and appearance overlap, is often impossible. We introduce Generative Reconstruction from Disjoint Views as a new paradigm, establish a comprehensive dataset, and propose specialized evaluation metrics for zero-overlap scenarios. Our benchmarking demonstrates that existing state-of-the-art methods fail catastrophically on this task, producing disconnected geometries or semantically incoherent reconstructions. To address these limitations, we propose GLADOS, a general, modular framework that operates through three stages: (1) Generative Bridging, where foundation models synthesize intermediate perspectives to connect disjoint inputs; (2) Robust Coarse 3D Reconstruction, that establish coarse geometric scaffold via global alignment which absorbs local contradictions from generative process; and (3) Iterative Context Expansion and Consistency Optimization to fill missing regions and unify the reconstruction. As an architectureagnostic framework, GLADOS enables seamless integration of future advances in generation, reconstruction, and inpainting. The source code is available at: https://github.com/gwilczynski95/GLADOS.

</details>


### [97] [VIMCAN: Visual-Inertial 3D Human Pose Estimation with Hybrid Mamba-Cross-Attention Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07552)
*Zepeng Yang,Junxuan Bai,Hao Li,Ju Dai,Junjun Pan,Yongfeng Yin,Bin Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VIMCAN is a hybrid architecture for multimodal 3D human pose estimation that combines Mamba-based efficient temporal modeling with Cross-Attention for spatial reasoning to fuse RGB keypoints and wearable IMU data. It achieves real-time inference (>60 FPS) with improved accuracy, reporting MPJPE of 17.2 mm on TotalCapture and 45.3 mm on 3DPW, outperforming prior Transformer-based and SOTA methods. Source code is available on GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformer-based HPE pipelines have quadratic complexity, hindering real-time processing on long sequences. There is a need to improve spatial reasoning in multimodal settings (RGB + IMU) while maintaining efficiency. The proposed approach aims to combine efficient sequence modeling with strong spatial dependencies to enable robust visual–inertial fusion and accurate 3D HPE.

Method: Propose VIMCAN, a hybrid architecture that pairs Mamba’s dynamic parameterization for temporal modeling with Cross-Attention to extract spatial dependencies. The model performs robust visual–inertial fusion by integrating RGB-derived keypoints with wearable IMU data, enabling accurate multimodal 3D HPE while maintaining real-time performance on consumer hardware (over 60 FPS). The approach leverages Mamba for temporal processing efficiency and Attention mechanisms for spatial reasoning.

Result: VIMCAN achieves MPJPEs of 17.2 mm on TotalCapture and 45.3 mm on 3DPW, outperforming prior Transformer-based and other SOTA approaches. It also achieves real-time inference at over 60 FPS on consumer-grade hardware.

Conclusion: VIMCAN effectively combines efficient temporal modeling with spatial attention to deliver accurate, real-time multimodal 3D HPE for RGB keypoints and IMU data, surpassing existing SOTA methods. The work highlights the benefits of hybrid temporal-spatial design and is accompanied by publicly available source code.

Abstract: The rapid advances in deep learning have significantly enhanced the accuracy of multimodal 3D human pose estimation (HPE). However, the state-of-the-art (SOTA) HPE pipelines still rely on Transformers, whose quadratic complexity makes real-time processing for long sequences impractical. Mamba addresses this issue through selective state-space modeling, enabling efficient sequence processing without sacrificing representational power. Nevertheless, it struggles to capture complex spatial dependencies in multimodal settings. To bridge this gap, we propose VIMCAN, a hybrid architecture that combines the efficient sequence modeling of Mamba with the spatial reasoning of Cross-Attention, and performs robust visual-inertial fusion and human pose estimation between RGB keypoints and wearable IMU data. By leveraging Mamba's dynamic parameterization for temporal modeling and Attention for spatial dependency extraction, VIMCAN achieves superior accuracy, with mean per-joint position errors (MPJPE) of 17.2 mm on TotalCapture and 45.3 mm on 3DPW. VIMCAN outperforms prior Transformer-based and other SOTA approaches while supporting real-time inference at over 60 frames per second on consumer-grade hardware. The source code is available on GitHub.

</details>


### [98] [Dynamic Mode Decomposition along Depth in Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07556)
*Nishant Suresh Aswani,Saif Eddin Jabari*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViT depth can be locally approximated by a single recurrent linear operator K over short spans, but this linearization does not generalize downstream; early spans are low-rank and CLS-token–biased, and an identity baseline becomes competitive at the final state.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether contiguous ViT blocks implement autonomous linear dynamics that can be captured by a single operator K applied recurrently across a span, clarifying the relationship between depth, linearization, and downstream usefulness.

Method: Apply Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD) to hidden-state sequences from four pretrained DINO ViTs. Fit a single operator K from consecutive hidden-state pairs for short spans (p ≤ 4) and predict p-step evolution via K^p. Analyze regularization, rank, and calibration data requirements; assess reconstruction of intermediate activations, token-wise linearization (noting CLS token), and downstream transfer, including the final state where an identity baseline is competitive.

Result: For p ≤ 4, K^p matches the unconstrained endpoint map within ~0.02 cosine similarity on DINOv3-H/16+ and recovers intermediate activations across skipped blocks. Early cut starts yield compressed dynamics with rank much less than the hidden size d and require minimal calibration data; CLS token shows strongest linearization. These properties decay monotonically with depth. However, this local fidelity does not translate to downstream performance; at the final hidden state after propagating through remaining blocks, an identity baseline becomes competitive.

Conclusion: Local linear dynamics exist in ViT depth for short spans, but a single recurrent operator cannot robustly capture downstream function across full depth. The linearization is depth- and token-dependent (CLS most amenable) and loses predictive power downstream; identity baselines near end suggest nontrivial nonlinear processing persists.

Abstract: Recent work has shown that contiguous vision transformer (ViT) blocks (a) can be replaced by a linear map and (b) organize into recurrent phases of computation. We ask whether these observations coincide: does ViT depth implement approximately \textit{autonomous linear} dynamics, admitting a single operator $K$ applied recurrently across a contiguous span? We test this using Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD), which fits $K$ from selected, consecutive hidden-state pairs and predicts $p$ steps ahead via $K^p$. On four pretrained DINO ViTs, we study the regularization, rank, and calibration budget required for stable fitting. For short spans ($p \leq 4$), $K^p$ tracks an unconstrained endpoint map to within $0.02$ cosine similarity on DINOv3-H/16+, while also recovering intermediate activations at each skipped block. At early cut starts, the fitted operators compress to rank $\ll d$ with minimal calibration data, and across tokens, \texttt{cls} is most amenable to linearization; both properties decay monotonically with depth. Yet this local fidelity does not transfer downstream. At the final hidden state, after propagating through the remaining blocks, an identity baseline becomes competitive.

</details>


### [99] [Multimodal Stepwise Clinically-Guided Attention Learning for Pathological Complete Response Prediction in Breast Cancer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07561)
*Alice Natalina Caragliano,Valerio Guarrasi,Michela Gravina,Carlo Sansone,Paolo Soda*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A clinically-guided, multimodal, stepwise attention model for predicting pathological complete response (pCR) from breast MRI, with external validation and interpretable, tumor-grounded attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: pCR is a key prognostic factor in breast cancer, but pre-treatment pCR prediction is hindered by severe class imbalance and poor generalizability across diverse clinical settings; robust, interpretable, multimodal models are needed.

Method: A stepwise training framework: (1) learn global discriminative imaging patterns from MRI, (2) introduce attention constrained to anatomically defined tumor regions to focus the model, (3) integrate clinical variables to refine decisions; multimodal architecture with clinically guided attention and external validation across heterogeneous MRI cohorts.

Result: The approach yields higher sensitivity than non-guided single-stage baselines while maintaining competitive specificity, and produces anatomically coherent attention maps, with improved cross-institution generalization in external validation.

Conclusion: Clinically-guided multimodal attention learning shows promise for robust, generalizable pCR prediction in breast cancer, offering interpretable attention and potential for better clinical deployment across diverse MRI settings.

Abstract: Pathological complete response (pCR) is a key prognostic factor in breast cancer patients undergoing neoadjuvant therapy, strongly associated with long-term survival and treatment personalization. However, accurate pre-treatment pCR prediction remains challenging due to severe class imbalance and limited generalizability across diverse clinical settings. In this work, we propose a multimodal stepwise clinically-guided attention learning framework for pCR prediction from breast magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), designed to address these limitations through medically grounded spatial guidance and multimodal integration. The approach follows a stepwise training strategy inspired by physician reasoning: the model first learns global discriminative imaging patterns, then attention mechanisms are introduced to constrain the network toward tumor regions, and finally clinical variables are integrated to refine decision-making. This guidance strategy encourages prioritization of task-relevant features, improving identification of responders despite their limited representation in the dataset. Moreover, grounding attention in anatomically consistent tumor regions reduces reliance on dataset-specific patterns, thereby enhancing cross-institutional generalization. The framework is evaluated through external validation across heterogeneous MRI cohorts. Compared to non-guided single-stage baselines, the proposed approach improves sensitivity while maintaining competitive specificity, and produces anatomically coherent attention maps that support interpretation of the model's predictions. These findings highlight the potential of clinically-guided multimodal attention learning for robust and generalizable pCR prediction in breast cancer.

</details>


### [100] [Beyond GSD-as-Token: Continuous Scale Conditioning for Remote Sensing VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07562)
*Song Zhang,Yanlong Chen,Yilin Li,Yining Chen,Zili Yi,Xiaowei Zhang,Yawei Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ScaleEarth introduces a parameter-efficient RS-VLM fine-tuning by conditioning computation on ground sampling distance (GSD) with a continuous scale-aware path (CS-HLoRA), plus GSD-aware uncertainty prediction (SSE-U) and a scale-conditioned RS-VQA dataset (GeoScale-VQA). Achieves SOTA on RS benchmarks with QLoRA on an 8B backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RS-VLMs face a fundamental scale mismatch: objects look different at varying ground sampling distances. Treating GSD as discrete text or ignoring it forces a single parameter set to cover the entire scale spectrum, hurting performance and generalization.

Method: 1) CS-HLoRA modulates the LoRA subspace via a GSD-driven gate to route computation according to physical scale. 2) SSE-U predicts GSD and its uncertainty from visual features to remove sensor metadata at deployment. 3) GeoScale-VQA: a 1.5M-sample scale-aware RS-VQA dataset with questions conditioned on the same scale variable to close the method-data loop. 4) Training with QLoRA on an 8B-scale backbone (Qwen3-VL-based) for parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on remote-sensing benchmarks, including XLRS-Bench and OmniEarth-Bench, across diverse Earth-system tasks.

Conclusion: ScaleEarth demonstrates that continuous, scale-conditioned computation plus data-driven GSD estimation can effectively cover multi-GSD inputs with parameter efficiency, reducing reliance on explicit sensor metadata while enabling near-SOTA performance on RS tasks.

Abstract: Remote sensing vision-language models (RS-VLMs) face a fundamental mismatch with natural-image counterparts: the same geographic object exhibits radically different visual evidence across ground sampling distances (GSDs) spanning multiple orders of magnitude. Yet existing RS-VLMs often discard GSD or inject it as a discrete text token, forcing a single static parameter set to absorb the entire scale spectrum. We introduce ScaleEarth, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework built on Qwen3-VL that treats GSD as a continuous conditioning variable governing the model's computation path. At its core, CS-HLoRA (Continuous Scale-Conditioned Hyper-LoRA) modulates the LoRA low-rank subspace through a GSD-driven gate, enabling the model to dynamically route computation by physical scale. To remove reliance on sensor metadata at deployment, we pair CS-HLoRA with SSE-U, a lightweight heteroscedastic sub-head that predicts GSD and its uncertainty from visual features. To provide matching supervision, we construct GeoScale-VQA, a 1.5M-sample scale-layered RS-VQA corpus whose question-answer generation is conditioned on the same physical scalar that drives CS-HLoRA, forming a closed method-data loop. Trained with QLoRA on an 8B backbone, ScaleEarth achieves state-of-the-art results on remote-sensing benchmarks covering diverse Earth-system tasks, including XLRS-Bench and OmniEarth-Bench.

</details>


### [101] [Tracing the Arrow of Time: Diagnosing Temporal Information Flow in Video-LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07568)
*Peitao Han,Fei Cheng,Lis K. Pereira,Qianying Liu,Shigeru Kitazawa*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Temporal reasoning in Video-LLMs hinges on both how video information is encoded and how it is transferred to the LLM; bottlenecks arise in projection and information flow, not just the encoder.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify where temporal information bottlenecks arise in Video-LLMs (encoder, projector, or LLM) by isolating components and tracing temporal signals.

Method: Isolate vision encoder from Video-LLM; compare video-centric vs frame-centric encoders; evaluate Q-Former vs time-preserved MLP projections; perform layer-wise temporal analysis; train a temporal-aware video-centric encoder with a time-preserved projector and AoT supervision; evaluate on AoT_PP_B and related tasks.

Result: Video-centric encoders with explicit temporal modeling encode strong temporal signals; frame-centric encoders do not. In standard Video-LLM pipelines, temporal information transfer collapses; Q-Former disrupts temporal signals whereas a time-preserved MLP projection enhances access to temporal information. A temporal-aware design achieves 98.1% on AoT_PP_B (surpassing humans) and yields gains on VITATECS-Direction and TVBench.

Conclusion: Effective temporal reasoning in Video-LLMs requires both robust temporal encoding and reliable preservation of temporal information as it travels through the projection stage into the LLM; architectural choices in the projector and supervision critically shape performance.
