Abstract: The Arrow-of-Time (AoT) task, determining whether a video plays forward or backward by recognizing temporal irreversibility, is one humans solve with near-perfect accuracy, yet frontier Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) perform only modestly above chance. This gap raises a key question: do visual backbones fail to encode temporal information, or does information bottleneck lie elsewhere in the Video-LLM architecture? We address this question by isolating the vision encoder from the Video-LLM and tracing temporal information across the encoder, projector, and LLM. We find that video-centric encoders with explicit temporal modeling encode strong temporal signals, whereas frame-centric encoders do not. However, when video-centric representations are passed through a standard Video-LLM architecture, performance often collapses, revealing a bottleneck of temporal information flow. We identify projector design as a key factor: Q-Former disrupts temporal information, while a time-preserved MLP projection substantially improves the LLM's access to such information. Our layer-wise analysis further shows temporal representation dynamics across encoder layers. Guided by these findings, we build a Video-LLM with temporal-aware video-centric encoder, time-preserved projector, and AoT supervision, surpassing human performance on AoT$_{PPB}$ with 98.1\% accuracy, and improving broader temporal reasoning tasks by up to 6.0 points on VITATECS-Direction and 1.3 points on TVBench. Our results show that temporal reasoning in Video-LLMs requires both effective temporal encoding and reliable transfer of this information to the LLM.

</details>


### [102] [PolarVLM: Bridging the Semantic-Physical Gap in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07574)
*Yuliang Li,Chu Zhou,Heng Guo,Boxin Shi,Imari Sato,Zhanyu Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PolarVLM introduces polarimetric inputs to vision-language models via a dual-stream architecture and two-stage training, plus PolarVQA benchmark (75k pairs) for physics-grounded, polarization-aware VQA; shows large gains over RGB baselines, especially in reflection recognition and glass counting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome severe optical ambiguities in RGB-only VLMs (e.g., reflections, transparency) by leveraging polarimetric physical parameters, enabling physics-aware and open-ended reasoning.

Method: A dual-stream VLM that fuses RGB with polarimetric data, trained in progressive two-stage strategy; physics-grounded instruction tuning; creation of PolarVQA dataset with 75k pairs targeting reflective/transparent scenes.

Result: Outperforms RGB baselines by 25.4% overall across five tasks; +26.6% in reflection recognition; +34.0% in glass counting.

Conclusion: Integrating polarimetric signals enables physics-aware semantics in VLMs while preserving general visual abilities; introduces a new polarization-aware VQA benchmark.

Abstract: Mainstream vision-language models (VLMs) fundamentally struggle with severe optical ambiguities, such as reflections and transparent objects, due to the inherent limitations of standard RGB inputs. While polarization imaging captures polarimetric physical parameters that resolve these ambiguities, existing methods are constrained by fixed-format outputs and remain isolated from open-ended reasoning. To bridge this semantic-physical gap, we introduce PolarVLM, the first multimodal framework integrating polarimetric physical parameters into VLMs. By employing a dual-stream architecture and a progressive two-stage training strategy, PolarVLM effectively prevents physical misinterpretations while preserving general visual abilities. Complementing our architecture, we construct PolarVQA, the first benchmark for polarization-aware VQA, featuring 75K physics-grounded instruction-tuning pairs targeting reflective and transparent scenes. Experiments show that PolarVLM surpasses the RGB baseline by 25.4% overall across five evaluation tasks, with remarkable gains of 26.6% in reflection recognition and 34.0% in glass counting, successfully unlocking physics-aware semantic understanding.

</details>


### [103] [Response-G1: Explicit Scene Graph Modeling for Proactive Streaming Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07575)
*Ke Ma,Jiaqi Tang,Bin Guo,Xueting Han,Ruonan Xu,Qingfeng He,Ziheng Wang,Xu Wang,Qifeng Chen,Zhiwen Yu,Yunhao Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proactive streaming video understanding with Response-G1: a three-stage, fine-tuning-free framework that aligns accumulated video evidence with query response conditions via shared scene-graph representations, using online SG generation, memory-based retrieval, and retrieval-augmented prompting for per-frame silence/response decisions; shows improved proactive/reactive performance on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of query-agnostic, implicit visual evidence modeling in video-LLMs, which hampers timely and accurate responses during streaming when decisions must be made on-the-fly.

Method: Three-stage, fine-tuning-free pipeline: (1) online query-guided scene graph generation from streaming clips; (2) memory-based retrieval of semantically relevant historical scene graphs; (3) retrieval-augmented trigger prompting for per-frame silence/response decisions. Both evidence and response conditions are grounded in a shared scene graph representation.

Result: Experimental results on established benchmarks show that Response-G1 outperforms baselines in both proactive and reactive tasks, validating the efficacy of explicit scene-graph modeling and retrieval in streaming video understanding.

Conclusion: Explicit scene graph grounding and retrieval enhance interpretability and accuracy of response timing in streaming video understanding, with demonstrated gains in proactive/reactive tasks; the approach emphasizes structured alignment over implicit, query-agnostic modeling.

Abstract: Proactive streaming video understanding requires Video-LLMs to decide when to respond as a video unfolds, a task where existing methods often fall short due to their implicit, query-agnostic modeling of visual evidence. We introduce Response-G1, a novel framework that establishes explicit, structured alignment between the accumulated video evidence and the query's expected response conditions via scene graphs. The framework operates in three fine-tuning-free stages: (1) online query-guided scene graph generation from streaming clips; (2) memory-based retrieval of the most semantically relevant historical scene graphs; and (3) retrieval-augmented trigger prompting for per-frame "silence/response" decisions.By grounding both evidence and conditions in a shared graph representation, Response-G1 achieves more interpretable and accurate response timing decisions. Experimental results on established benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of our method in both proactive and reactive tasks, validating the advantage of explicit scene graph modeling and retrieval in streaming video understanding.

</details>


### [104] [Beyond Defenses: Manifold-Aligned Regularization for Intrinsic 3D Point Cloud Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07590)
*Pedro Alonso,Chongshou Li,Tianrui Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAPR introduces a manifold-aligned regularization for 3D point clouds that improves robustness by aligning latent geometry with intrinsic surface geometry, via intrinsic features and a consistency loss, without adversarial training, achieving notable gains on ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robustness in 3D networks is limited by a latent–intrinsic geometry misalignment. Small geometry-preserving perturbations can cause large feature distortions, indicating a geometric root cause not addressed by augmentation or defense strategies.

Method: MAPR augments each point with intrinsic features (local curvature, diffusion structure) and applies a consistency loss that enforces invariance to intrinsic, geometry-preserving perturbations, thereby regularizing latent geometry without requiring adversarial training or extra data.

Result: Empirical robustness gains under multiple adversarial attacks on ModelNet40 (+20.02% avg) and ScanObjectNN (+8.58%), outperforming baselines.

Conclusion: Aligning latent geometry with intrinsic surface geometry is a key driver of robustness in 3D point networks; MAPR provides a practical, data-efficient approach that generalizes across datasets and attacks.

Abstract: Despite extensive progress in point cloud robustness, existing methods primarily improve performance through augmentation or defense mechanisms, while overlooking the geometric root cause of adversarial fragility. We hypothesize that adversarial vulnerability in 3D networks arises from a manifold misalignment between the latent geometry learned by the model and the intrinsic geometry of the underlying surface. Small, geometry-preserving perturbations along the input manifold often induce disproportionate distortions in feature space, revealing a misalignment between latent and intrinsic geometries. We formalize this phenomenon by developing a geometric interpretation of 3D robustness that links classical adversarial theory to the intrinsic structure of point clouds. Motivated by this analysis, we introduce Manifold-Aligned Point Recognition (MAPR), a framework that regularizes the latent geometry by aligning predictions across intrinsic perturbations. MAPR augments each point cloud with intrinsic features capturing local curvature and diffusion structure, and applies a consistency loss that preserves invariance to intrinsic, geometry-preserving perturbations. Without relying on adversarial training or additional data, MAPR consistently improves robustness across multiple adversarial attacks on both the ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN datasets, achieving average robustness gains of +20.02% and +8.58% on ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN, respectively.

</details>


### [105] [TraceAV-Bench: Benchmarking Multi-Hop Trajectory Reasoning over Long Audio-Visual Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07593)
*Hengyi Feng,Hao Liang,Mingrui Chen,Bohan Zeng,Meiyi Qiang,Zhengyang Zhao,Zimo Meng,Zeang Sheng,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TraceAV-Bench is a long-form audio-visual multi-hop reasoning benchmark with 2,200 MC questions over 578 long videos (339.5h), testing 15 sub-tasks and multimodal hallucination robustness; current OmniLLMs struggle, with best closed-source 68.29% and best open-source 51.70%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap in real-world, long-form AV understanding where evidence is sparse and dispersed across modalities, by evaluating multi-hop reasoning over extended timelines and robustness to hallucination.

Method: Constructed 578 long videos with 2,200 MC questions; 4 evaluation dimensions across 15 sub-tasks; each question linked to an explicit 3.68-hop reasoning chain over 15.1 minutes; dataset built via a three-step semi-automated pipeline with strict QA; evaluated several OmniLLMs.

Result: Benchmark is challenging for current models; strongest closed-source Gemini 3.1 Pro achieves 68.29% on general tasks; best open-source Ming-Flash-Omni-2.0 achieves 51.70%; reveals partial decoupling between multimodal hallucination robustness and general multimodal reasoning.

Conclusion: TraceAV-Bench will spur development of OmniLLMs capable of coherent, faithful reasoning over long-form AV content and addressing hallucination robustly.

Abstract: Real-world audio-visual understanding requires chaining evidence that is sparse, temporally dispersed, and split across the visual and auditory streams, whereas existing benchmarks largely fail to evaluate this capability. They restrict videos to short clips, isolate modalities, or reduce questions to one-hop perception. We introduce TraceAV-Bench, the first benchmark to jointly evaluate multi-hop reasoning over long audio-visual trajectories and multimodal hallucination robustness. TraceAV-Bench comprises 2,200 rigorously validated multiple-choice questions over 578 long videos, totaling 339.5 hours, spanning 4 evaluation dimensions and 15 sub-tasks. Each question is grounded in an explicit reasoning chain that averages 3.68 hops across a 15.1-minute temporal span. The dataset is built by a three-step semi-automated pipeline followed by a strict quality assurance process. Evaluation of multiple representative OmniLLMs on TraceAV-Bench reveals that the benchmark poses a persistent challenge across all models, with the strongest closed-source model (Gemini 3.1 Pro) reaching only 68.29% on general tasks, and the best open-source model (Ming-Flash-Omni-2.0) reaching 51.70%, leaving substantial headroom. Moreover, we find that robustness to multimodal hallucination is largely decoupled from general multimodal reasoning performance. We anticipate that TraceAV-Bench will stimulate further research toward OmniLLMs that can reason coherently and faithfully over long-form audio-visual content.

</details>


### [106] [LithoBench: Benchmarking Large Multimodal Models for Remote-Sensing Lithology Interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07640)
*Jun Wang,Fengpeng Li,Hang Dong,Tianjin Huang,Wei Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LithoBench is a large, expert-annotated, multi-level benchmark for remote sensing lithology interpretation, enabling evaluation of geological semantic understanding in vision-language models, with 10k tasks across 12 lithologies and five cognitive levels; an expert-in-the-loop pipeline supports data construction; initial experiments show current models struggle with high-level reasoning and applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Geological lithology interpretation is knowledge-intensive and lacks benchmarks that capture lithological semantics and expert assessment, hindering reliable evaluation of knowledge-grounded multimodal models.

Method: Semi-automated, expert-in-the-loop pipeline to construct a 10k-task benchmark (4k multiple-choice, 6k open-ended) across five cognitive levels, using structured geological image descriptions and multi-subprocess validation to ensure geological validity and annotation reliability.

Result: Experiments with large vision-language models reveal substantial limitations in geological semantic understanding, especially for higher-order explanation, application, and reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: LithoBench enables systematic benchmarking of geological semantic understanding in remote sensing lithology interpretation and highlights gaps in current models, underscoring the need for improved knowledge-grounded multimodal reasoning and robust evaluation protocols.

Abstract: Remote sensing lithology interpretation is fundamental to geological surveys, mineral exploration, and regional geological mapping. Unlike general land-cover recognition, lithology interpretation is a knowledge-intensive task that requires experts to infer rock types from various features, e.g., subtle visual, spectral, textural, geomorphological, and contextual cues, making reliable automated interpretation highly challenging. Geological knowledge-guided large multimodal models offer new opportunities, yet their evaluation remains constrained by the lack of benchmarks that capture lithological annotations, multi-level geological semantics, and expert-informed assessment. Here, we propose LithoBench, a multi-level benchmark for evaluating geological semantic understanding in remote sensing lithology interpretation. LithoBench contains 10,000 expert-annotated interpretation instances across 12 representative lithological categories, including 4,000 multiple-choice and 6,000 open-ended tasks organized into five cognitive levels: Identification and Description, Comparative Analysis, Mechanism Explanation, Practical Application, and Comprehensive Reasoning. We further develop an expert-in-the-loop, knowledge-grounded semi-automated construction pipeline, coupling multi sub-processes, e.g., structured geological image descriptions, to enhance geological validity and evaluation reliability. Experiments with multiple large vision-language models eveal substantial limitations in geological semantic understanding, particularly on higher-order explanation, application, and reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [107] [EggHand: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Egocentric Hand Pose Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07642)
*Jaeyoung Choi,Hyeondong Kim,Yujin Kim,Daehee Park*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EggHand is a foundation-model-based framework for egocentric hand pose forecasting that fuses a Vision-Language-Action action decoder with an egocentric video-text encoder to model motion, context, and intent, achieving state-of-the-art results on EgoExo4D and enabling language-driven controllable predictions without external tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecasting 3D hand pose sequences from egocentric video is hard due to ego-motion, dexterous articulations, and viewpoint shifts. A robust method should integrate motion dynamics, contextual understanding, and high-level intent without relying on body pose tracking.

Method: EggHand combines a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model's action decoder to capture structured temporal dynamics with an egocentric video-text encoder to provide viewpoint-aware context. This multimodal, foundation-model-based framework avoids dependency on external tracking and supports joint reasoning over motion, context, and intent, with language-based task prompts for controllable predictions.

Result: On EgoExo4D, EggHand achieves state-of-the-art forecasting accuracy, remains robust under severe ego-motion, and enables controllable prediction via language prompts. Project page provided.

Conclusion: EggHand demonstrates that unifying multimodal semantic reasoning with dynamic motion modeling via a foundation-model framework yields accurate, robust, and controllable egocentric hand-pose forecasting without external tracking, highlighting the potential of VLA and video-text encoders for embodied perception tasks.

Abstract: Forecasting future 3D hand pose sequences from egocentric video is essential for understanding human intention and enabling embodied applications such as AR/VR assistance and human-robot interaction. However, this task remains a highly challenging problem because egocentric hand motion is driven by complex human intent, exhibits highly dexterous articulations, and is observed under drastic viewpoint shifts induced by ego-motion. In this work, we introduce EggHand, a foundation-model-based framework for egocentric hand pose forecasting that unifies multimodal semantic reasoning with dynamic motion modeling. Our approach couples an action decoder from a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model, which captures the structured temporal dynamics of hand motion, with an egocentric video-text encoder that provides viewpoint-aware contextual information learned from large-scale first-person video. Together, these components overcome the brittleness of generic visual encoders under ego-motion and enable joint reasoning over motion, context, and high-level intent-without relying on body pose or external tracking. Experiments on the EgoExo4D dataset show that EggHand sets a new state of the art in forecasting accuracy, remains robust under severe ego-motion, and further enables controllable prediction via language-based task prompts. Project page: https://jyoun9.github.io/EggHand

</details>


### [108] [Aquatic Neuromorphic Optical Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07653)
*Pei Zhang,Yunkai Liang,Kaiqiang Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Self-supervised optical-flow estimation from underwater event streams using spiking neural networks, achieving competitive accuracy with higher efficiency, addressing data scarcity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Underwater imaging faces severe quality and resource constraints, and underwater data scarcity hinders traditional supervised learning. Event cameras and neuromorphic vision offer low-latency, energy-efficient sensing suitable for edge platforms, motivating a self-supervised approach in aquatic environments.

Method: A spiking neural network-based framework trained in a self-supervised manner to estimate per-pixel optical flow from asynchronous event streams, bypassing the need for large underwater labeled datasets.

Result: Extensive evaluations show competitive visual and quantitative performance compared with leading methods, while offering superior computational efficiency, enabling real-time operation on resource-limited underwater edge devices.

Conclusion: Bridges neuromorphic sensing with aquatic intelligence, enabling lightweight, real-time, low-cost perception for underwater edge platforms and paving new opportunities for agile underwater navigation and sensing.

Abstract: Underwater environments impose severe constraints on conventional imaging systems and demand solutions that balance high-quality sensing with strict resource efficiency. While emerging event cameras offer a promising alternative, their potential in aquatic scenarios remains largely unexplored. Through the lens of neuromorphic vision, this work pioneers the investigation of motion fields that serve as key media for agile underwater perception. Built upon spiking neural networks, we introduce a self-supervised framework to estimate per-pixel optical flow from asynchronous event streams, elegantly bypassing the long-standing bottleneck of underwater data scarcity. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our method achieves competitive visual and quantitative results against leading techniques while operating with superior computational efficiency. By bridging neuromorphic sensing and aquatic intelligence, this work opens new frontiers for lightweight, real-time, and low-cost perception on resource-constrained underwater edge platforms.

</details>


### [109] [Towards Billion-scale Multi-modal Biometric Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07655)
*Arka Koner,Chetan S. Naik,Lokesh Kurre,Vivek Raghavan,Barada P. Sabut,Tanusree Deb Barma,Anoop M. Namboodiri,Anil K. Jain*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: First large-scale analysis of Bharat ABIS, a multimodal (fingerprint, face, iris) biometric search system for 1:N country-level identity, with 220M-demographically stratified gallery from Aadhaar and 40M additional gallery tests. Reported FNIR 0.3% at FPIR 0.5% (adult probes), 13.5 KB per-person template, and 100 searches/sec on a single server with 8x H100 GPUs and 2 TB RAM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the scalability, accuracy, and robustness challenges of country-level, multimodal biometric search, including handling missing modalities and presentation attack detection, to enable fast 1:N search (de-duplication) over extremely large galleries using open-source architectures.

Method: End-to-end pipeline with modality-specific preprocessing (segmentation), quality assessment, presentation attack detection, and embedding learning for fingerprint, face, and iris; concatenation into a 13.5 KB template; multimodal fusion via concatenation; evaluation on a demographically stratified gallery of 220M identities drawn from 1.55B Aadhaar records, plus comparisons to three COTS systems on a 20M gallery; hardware-assisted deployment (8×NVIDIA H100 GPUs, 2 TB RAM) achieving 100 searches/s on 40M gallery.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art or competitive performance for large-scale multimodal 1:N search with low FNIR at modest FPIR thresholds (0.3% FNIR @ 0.5% FPIR for adults); achieves high throughput (100 searches/s on 40M gallery) and compact templates (13.5 KB); provides empirical insights into integration of fingerprint, face, and iris modalities at scale; benchmarked against COTS baselines on a large gallery.

Conclusion: Bharat ABIS shows the feasibility and practicality of large-scale, open-source, multimodal biometrics for country-level identity systems, delivering strong accuracy and throughput. It highlights the viability of end-to-end pipelines combining modality-specific preprocessing, PAD, and embedding-based templates, while underscoring considerations around data governance, privacy, and deployment at scale.

Abstract: Searching a multi-biometric database of a billion records for a country-level identity system requires pushing the limits of all aspects of a biometric system, including acquisition, preprocessing, feature extraction, accuracy, matching speed, presentation attack detection, and handling of special cases (e.g., missing finger digits). This is the first paper that gives insights into such a large-scale multimodal biometric search system, called Bharat ABIS, based on open-source architectures. The end-to-end pipeline of Bharat ABIS processes fingerprint, face and iris modalities through modality-specific stages of preprocessing (segmentation), quality assessment, presentation attack detection, and learning an embedding (feature extraction), producing a concatenated template of 13.5KB per person. We present a detailed analysis of the modalities and how they are integrated to create an efficient and effective solution for 1:N search (de-duplication). Evaluations on a demographically stratified gallery of 220 million identities, randomly sampled from 1.55 billion records in India's Aadhaar database, yield an FNIR of 0.3% at an FPIR of 0.5%, for adult probes (over 18 years). We also compare the performance of Bharat ABIS against three state-of-the-art COTS systems on a 20M gallery. Our system achieves a throughput of 100 searches per second on a gallery of 40M on a single server (8xNvidia H100 GPUs, 2TB RAM).

</details>


### [110] [OphEdit: Training-Free Text-Guided Editing of Ophthalmic Surgical Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07695)
*Ritul Jangir,Arkya Jyoti Bagchi,Aiman Farooq,Mangalton Okram,Saurabh Seetaram Korgaonkar,Deepak Mishra*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free, text-guided editing of ophthalmic surgical videos using a second-order ODE inversion with stored Attention Value tensors, preserving anatomy and timeline while enabling semantic edits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for precise, linguistically guided edits without fine-tuning, to support clinical training and AI development; maintain anatomical fidelity and temporal consistency in surgical videos.

Method: Deterministic second-order ODE inversion to extract Attention Value tensors from the original video; inject selectively into the conditional CFG during denoising to map text edits while preserving eye geometry; training-free framework.

Result: Clinical evaluations show robust handling of instrument swaps and procedural variations with superior structural fidelity and temporal consistency compared to natural-domain editors; scalable for annotated dataset generation.

Conclusion: First training-free video editing approach for ophthalmic surgery; enables diverse annotated data without extensive recording or fine-tuning; code available at GitHub.

Abstract: High-fidelity surgical video generation can greatly improve medical training and the development of AI, adapting these generative models for precise video editing remains a formidable challenge. Modifying surgical attributes, such as instrument tissue interactions or procedural phases is challenging due to the strict anatomical and temporal constraints. In this paper, we propose OphEdit, a novel training-free framework for the text-guided editing of ophthalmic surgical videos. Our approach leverages a deterministic second-order ODE inversion pipeline to capture Attention Value (V) tensors from the original video. By selectively injecting these stored tensors into the conditional Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) branch during the denoising phase, OphEdit rigorously preserves the intricate anatomical geometry of the eye while seamlessly mapping text-driven semantic modifications onto the video stream. Clinical evaluations demonstrates that OphEdit effectively handles complex surgical transformations, such as instrument swaps and procedural variations, with superior structural fidelity and temporal consistency compared to natural-domain video editors. Our work represents the first application of training-free video editing in the ophthalmic surgical domain, offering a scalable solution for generating diverse, annotated medical datasets without the need for exhaustive manual recording or costly model fine-tuning. The code and prompts can be accessed at https://github.com/ophedit/OphEdit

</details>


### [111] [Benchmarking Foundation Models for Renal Lesion Stratification in CT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07749)
*Hartmut Häntze,Sarah de Boer,Myrthe Buser,Alessa Hering,Bram van Ginneken,Mathias Prokop,Jawed Nawabi,Sebastian Ziegelmayer,Lisa Adams,Keno Bressem*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Medical foundation models (FMs) underperform compared to radiomics for CT renal lesion classification in a data-scarce setting; FMs match from-scratch CNN performance but offer much lower compute, yet radiomics remains the top performer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate transferability of open-source medical foundation model embeddings to a clinically relevant, data-scarce CT-based renal lesion classification task and compare with traditional radiomics and a 3D CNN trained from scratch.

Method: Frozen feature-probing using three medical FMs; compare embeddings to handcrafted radiomics and a 3D ResNet-50 trained from scratch. Dataset: 2,854 lesions for training/validation; external test set of 234 lesions from The Cancer Imaging Archive (TCIA). Six-class classification including cysts, clear cell RCC, and rare subtypes.

Result: FM embeddings achieved AUC 0.70–0.77, comparable to the from-scratch 3D ResNet-50 (AUC 0.72) but with far lower hardware demands (CPU-only feature extraction). The radiomics baseline achieved AUC 0.88 (significantly better, p ≤ 0.002), outperforming all deep learning approaches.

Conclusion: Generalist medical FMs, in their current form, do not capture the fine-grained texture/shape cues needed for renal lesion subtyping; radiomics remains the state-of-the-art in this data-scarce setting, though FMs offer computational efficiency benefits.

Abstract: The rapid proliferation of open-source medical foundation models (FMs) raises a practical question: how well do their pre-trained representations transfer to clinically relevant but data-scarce classification tasks? Particularly in CT-based renal lesion classification, a push toward greater generalizability would be meaningful, as the field is constrained by inherently limited training data. We addressed this through a benchmark of three medical FMs on this specific task. This six-class problem spans common entities like cysts and clear cell renal cell carcinoma, alongside rare subtypes. Using a frozen feature-probing protocol, we compared FM embeddings against a handcrafted radiomics classifier and a 3D ResNet-50 trained from scratch. Models were trained on a composite dataset of 2,854 lesions and evaluated on an external test set of 234 lesions from The Cancer Imaging Archive. Our results reveal two key findings. First, FM performance (AUC 0.70-0.77) matched the from-scratch ResNet (AUC 0.72) while drastically reducing hardware demand, requiring only seconds on a CPU after feature extraction. However, the conventional radiomics baseline significantly outperformed all deep learning approaches, achieving an AUC of 0.88 (all p $\leq$ 0.002). This suggests that current generalist FM embeddings do not yet capture the fine-grained texture and shape heterogeneity driving histological subtype discrimination. Despite their potential in data-scarce settings, medical FMs did not surpass established models for renal lesion stratification, leaving radiomics as the current state-of-the-art.

</details>


### [112] [Head Similarity: Modeling Structured Whole-Head Appearance Beyond Face Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07766)
*Yingfeng Wang,Yuxuan Xiao,Shengcai Liao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Head Similarity to model intra-identity appearance variation and hierarchical similarity across identity and appearance states, enabling comparisons under occlusion/ rear views; creates a large-scale weakly-supervised benchmark; proposes a simple identity-aware distillation framework; shows that traditional face models miss appearance-dependent similarity and that structured whole-head similarity modeling is feasible.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Face recognition models pursue intra-identity invariance, but many applications require comparing appearances of the same person across varying hairstyles, occlusions, and views. The paper argues this limits usefulness in appearance-sensitive scenarios and seeks an explicit, structured representation of head-level appearance.

Method: Propose Head Similarity: extend identity-centric recognition to structured whole-head similarity with hierarchical supervision. Jointly model identity discrimination and appearance-sensitive similarity using identity-aware distillation, trained on a large-scale, weakly-supervised benchmark derived from long-form videos that cover diverse poses, occlusions, and temporal changes.

Result: Conventional face recognition models fail to capture appearance-dependent similarity; the proposed approach demonstrates feasibility of structured whole-head similarity modeling and improves robustness to non-frontal views and occlusion in their benchmark.

Conclusion: Head Similarity provides a principled extension from identity recognition to structured head-level similarity, enabling meaningful comparisons under challenging conditions. The work also supplies a large-scale benchmark and a simple, effective training framework as a first step, suggesting further exploration of hierarchical appearance states and distillation for richer representations.

Abstract: Many vision applications require identity consistency beyond strict biometric recognition, especially under non-frontal views or when facial cues are missing. However, conventional face recognition models enforce intra-identity invariance, collapsing appearance variations such as hairstyle or styling changes into a single representation, limiting their use in appearance-sensitive scenarios. To address this limitation, we introduce Head Similarity, a new formulation that extends identity-centric recognition to structured whole-head similarity modeling. Our approach explicitly captures intra-identity appearance variation and enforces hierarchical similarity ordering across identity and appearance states, enabling meaningful comparison even under occlusion or rear-view conditions. We construct a large-scale benchmark from long-form videos with weakly-supervised appearance states, covering diverse poses, occlusions, and temporal changes. As a first step, we develop a simple yet effective framework that jointly models identity discrimination and appearance-sensitive similarity through hierarchical supervision and identity-aware distillation. Experiments show that conventional face recognition models fail to capture appearance-dependent similarity, while our approach demonstrates the feasibility of structured whole-head similarity modeling.

</details>


### [113] [SIMI: Self-information Mining Network for Low-light Image Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07767)
*Xuanshuo Fu,Lei Kang,Javier Vazquez-Corral*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Unsupervised Self-Information Mining (SIMI) uses bit-plane decomposition to extract intrinsic information from low-light images, enabling fast convergence and state-of-the-art enhancement without external data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Poor lighting degrades image quality and existing methods often rely on complex models and external data; exploiting intrinsic information in low-light images can improve efficiency and performance in an unsupervised way.

Method: Introduce SIMI network that performs bit-plane decomposition to decompose low-light images into multiple components, mining intrinsic information without external data.

Result: Experiments on standard benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance and improved efficiency (faster convergence, lower computational overhead).

Conclusion: Unsupervised bit-plane based information mining is effective for low-light image enhancement and broad real-world applicability.

Abstract: Poor lighting conditions significantly impact image quality, posing substantial challenges for image editing and visualization. Many existing enhancement methods aim at proposing complex models while neglecting the intrinsic information contained within low-light images. In this work, we propose the Self-Information Mining (SIMI) network, an innovative unsupervised framework that decomposes low-light images into multiple components based on bit-plane decomposition. Our approach allows mining intrinsic information without relying on external data. This not only accelerates model convergence but also improves performance and reduces computational overhead. The unsupervised nature of our method facilitates real-world applicability. Experiments conducted on standard benchmarks demonstrate that SIMI achieves state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [114] [Differentiable Ray Tracing with Gaussians for Unified Radio Propagation Simulation and View Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07781)
*Niklas Vaara,Lam Huynh,Pekka Sangi,Miguel Bordallo López,Janne Heikkilä*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes differentiable RF propagation on visually reconstructed neural scenes by embedding Gaussian primitives in a ray-tracing backbone, enabling deterministic multi-bounce RF paths and extracting channel impulse responses to unify EM propagation with photorealistic view synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between neural scene representations used for high-fidelity rendering and the deterministic geometry-driven requirements of RF propagation in digital twins.

Method: Embed Gaussian primitives into a hardware-accelerated ray tracing structure as the spatial representation; enable differentiable RF propagation simulation; compute point-to-point RF paths in the neural scene; extract physically meaningful channel impulse responses from the visual reconstruction.

Result: Framework that yields differentiable RF propagation within neural scenes and demonstrates that neural reconstructions can provide a unified spatial representation for both EM propagation and photorealistic rendering, via accessible channel impulse responses.

Conclusion: Neural reconstructions can serve as a single spatial representation supporting both electromagnetic propagation simulation and high-quality visual synthesis.

Abstract: Explicit neural representations such as 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) enable high-fidelity and real-time novel view synthesis, yet optimize for alpha-composited optical appearance rather than ray-intersectable geometry. In contrast, radio-frequency (RF) digital twins require deterministic multi-bounce paths, where the geometry dictates trajectories and their associated attenuation and delay. We introduce a framework enabling differentiable RF propagation simulation directly within visually reconstructed neural scenes, allowing point-to-point path computation between arbitrary 3D locations while preserving high-quality visual rendering. Unlike conventional RF simulation pipelines that rely on manually constructed meshes, we embed Gaussian primitives into a hardware-accelerated ray tracing structure as the underlying spatial representation. By extracting physically meaningful channel impulse responses from visual-only reconstructions, we provide cross-modal evidence that neural reconstructions can serve as unified spatial representations for both electromagnetic propagation simulation and photorealistic view synthesis.

</details>


### [115] [Radiologist-Guided Causal Concept Bottleneck Models for Chest X-Ray Interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07785)
*Amy Rafferty,Rishi Ramaesh,Ajitha Rajan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: XpertCausal is a radiologist-guided causal concept-bottleneck model (CBM) for chest X-ray interpretation that uses a probabilistic noisy-OR generative model to link pathologies to radiographic concepts. It inverts this model via Bayesian inference to estimate pathology probabilities from predicted concepts, with radiologist-curated concept–pathology associations constraining the reasoning. Evaluated on MIMIC-CXR, it shows improved AUROC, calibration, explanation quality, and alignment with expert pathways over a non-causal CBM and an unconstrained causal ablation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard CBMs treat concepts as discriminative predictors of pathology without modeling the underlying clinical generative process; there is a need for clinically plausible causal structure and better interpretability/calibration.

Method: A causal CBM with a probabilistic noisy-OR pathway from pathologies to concepts is proposed. Radiologist-curated concept–pathology associations constrain the model structure. Inference is performed via Bayesian inversion to obtain pathology probabilities from predicted concepts. The approach is evaluated on MIMIC-CXR for pathology classification, calibration, explanation quality, and alignment with radiologist reasoning.

Result: XpertCausal achieves improved AUROC, calibration, and clinically relevant explanation quality compared to a non-causal CBM baseline and a causal ablation with unconstrained learned associations, and learns concept–pathology relationships that align more closely with expert knowledge.

Conclusion: Incorporating clinically motivated causal structure and domain knowledge into CBMs yields more accurate, interpretable, and clinically aligned models for chest X-ray interpretation.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) in medical imaging aim to improve model interpretability by predicting intermediate clinical concepts before final diagnoses. However, most existing CBMs treat concepts as discriminative predictors of pathology labels, without explicitly modelling the underlying clinical generative process where diseases produce observable radiographic findings. We propose XpertCausal, a radiologist-guided causal CBM for chest X-ray interpretation which models pathology-to-concept relationships using a probabilistic noisy-OR framework. This generative model is then inverted via Bayesian inference to estimate pathology probabilities from predicted concepts. Radiologist-curated concept-pathology associations are used to constrain model structure to radiologist-defined clinically plausible reasoning pathways. We evaluate XpertCausal on MIMIC-CXR across pathology classification performance, calibration, explanation quality, and alignment with radiologist-defined reasoning pathways. Compared with both a non-causal CBM baseline and a causal ablation with unconstrained learned associations, XpertCausal achieves improved AUROC, calibration, and clinically relevant explanation quality, while learning concept-pathology relationships that more closely align with expert knowledge. These results demonstrate that incorporating clinically motivated causal structure and expert domain knowledge into CBMs can lead to more accurate, interpretable, and clinically aligned models for CXR interpretation.

</details>


### [116] [APEX: Assumption-free Projection-based Embedding eXamination Metric for Image Quality Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07786)
*Caterina Gallegati,Monica Bianchini,Franco Scarselli,Vittorio Murino,Barbara Toniella Corradini*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: APEX introduces a projection-based evaluation using the Sliced Wasserstein Distance to replace traditional feature-distribution metrics like FID. It is embedding-agnostic, uses CLIP and DINOv2 as features, scalable to high dimensions, and demonstrates robust, stable performance across degradations and cross-domain datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional metrics (e.g., FID) are limited by closed-vocabulary feature bottlenecks and rigid parametric assumptions. There is a need for an assumption-free, scalable, open-vocabulary evaluation framework for image generation quality.

Method: APEX employs the Sliced Wasserstein Distance as a similarity measure and projection-based embedding examination. It remains embedding-agnostic and uses two open-vocabulary foundation models (CLIP and DINOv2) as feature extractors. The approach emphasizes high-dimensional scalability and factor-robustness through projection-based SWD estimates.

Result: Empirical and theoretical results show that APEX is robust to visual degradations, maintains intra- and cross-dataset stability, and provides stable evaluations on out-of-domain datasets, outperforming established baselines.

Conclusion: APEX offers a principled, assumption-free, and scalable evaluation framework for image synthesis quality that is embedding-agnostic and resistant to common evaluation pitfalls of traditional metrics.

Abstract: As generative models achieve unprecedented visual quality, the gold standard for image evaluation remains traditional feature-distribution metrics (e.g., FID). However, these metrics are provably hindered by the closed-vocabulary bottleneck of outdated features and the assumptive bias of rigid parametric formulations. Recent alternatives exploit modern backbones to solve the feature bottleneck, yet continue to suffer from parametric limitations. To close this gap, we introduce APEX (Assumption-free Projection-based Embedding eXamination), a novel evaluation framework leveraging the Sliced Wasserstein Distance as a mathematically grounded, assumption-free similarity measure. APEX inherits effective scalability to high-dimensional spaces, as we prove with theoretical and empirical evidences. Moreover, APEX is embedding-agnostic and uses two open-vocabulary foundation models, CLIP and DINOv2, as feature extractors. Benchmarking APEX against established baselines reveals superior robustness to visual degradations. Additionally, we show that APEX metrics exhibit intra- and cross-dataset stability, ensuring highly stable evaluations on out-of-domain datasets.

</details>


### [117] [SARA: Semantically Adaptive Relational Alignment for Video Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07800)
*Jiesong Lian,Zixiang Zhou,Ruizhe Zhong,Yuan Zhou,Qinglin Lu,Rui Wang,Long Hu,Yixue Hao,Baoru Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SARA uses text-conditioned saliency to adaptively route token-pair supervision in token-relation distillation, improving semantic alignment and motion quality in video diffusion models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video diffusion models suffer from mis-binding and weak interactions due to fixed, non-adaptive supervision budgets in pairwise token-relations. Existing methods rely on cues not aligned with prompt relevance, limiting fine-grained alignment.

Method: Maintain token-relation distillation (TRD) on a frozen visual foundation model. Introduce a text-conditioned saliency module and a lightweight Stage 1 aligner trained with per-entity SAM 3.1 masks and an InfoNCE regulariser. Fuse continuous saliency into TRD via a pair-routing operator that weights token pairs when either endpoint is salient, biasing supervision toward subject-subject and subject-background pairs and away from background-background pairs.

Result: SARA improves text alignment and motion quality over SFT, VideoREPA, and MoAlign in Wan2.2 continual-training, achieving better scores on a 13-dimension VLM rubric, on public VBench benchmarks, and in blind user studies.

Conclusion: Semantically adaptive relational alignment effectively allocates supervision where it matters most for prompt-driven semantics, enabling stronger alignment and motion fidelity in video diffusion models.

Abstract: Recent video diffusion models (VDMs) synthesize visually convincing clips, yet still drop entities, mis-bind attributes, and weaken the interactions specified in the prompt. Representation-alignment objectives such as VideoREPA and MoAlign improve fine-grained text following by distilling spatio-temporal token relations from a frozen visual foundation model, but their pairwise supervision budget is allocated by visual or motion cues rather than by how relevant each pair is to the prompt. We present SARA, Semantically Adaptive Relational Alignment, which keeps token-relation distillation (TRD) on a frozen VFM target and adds a text-conditioned saliency that decides which token pairs carry supervision. A lightweight Stage 1 aligner is trained with per-entity SAM 3.1 mask supervision and an InfoNCE regulariser, and its continuous saliency is fused into TRD through a pair-routing operator that assigns each token pair a weight whenever either of its two endpoints is salient, thereby routing supervision toward subject-subject and subject-background pairs and away from background-background ones. In the Wan2.2 continual-training setting, SARA improves both text alignment and motion quality over SFT, VideoREPA, and MoAlign on a 13-dimension VLM rubric, on the public VBench benchmarks, and in a blind user study.

</details>


### [118] [ICDAR 2026 Competition on Writer Identification and Pen Classification from Hand-Drawn Circles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07816)
*Thomas Gorges,Janne van der Loop,Lukas Hüttner,Linda-Sophie Schneider,Fei Wu,Mathias Seuret,Vincent Christlein*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CircleID is a large-scale IC DAR 2026 competition on writer identification and pen classification from scanned hand-drawn circles, exploring minimal-trace features under open-set and cross-writer conditions; includes a 46k-image dataset and ResNet baselines, achieving strong but differing performance across tasks and highlighting OOD generalization and feature disentanglement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify how biometric writer traits and physical pen features entangle in minimal static traces and to push open-set writer identification and cross-writer pen classification, while examining generalization to unseen writers.

Method: Two tasks: open-set writer identification and cross-writer pen classification. Dataset of 46,155 tightly cropped circle images from 50 known and 16 unknown writers across eight pens. Kaggle two tracks (public/private) with a provided ResNet baseline. Large-scale participation and evaluation via Top-1 accuracy.

Result: Best private leaderboard: writer identification Top-1 64.801% and pen classification Top-1 92.726%. 389 teams (436 participants) made 3,185 submissions for pen classification; 113 teams (141 participants) made 1,737 submissions for writer identification. Dataset: 46,155 images, 50 known, 16 unknown writers, 8 pens. Analyses address out-of-distribution writers and feature disentanglement.

Conclusion: CircleID establishes a new baseline for minimal-trace analysis in writer identification and pen classification, providing a large-scale dataset, baseline methods, and insights on generalization and feature disentanglement for future research.

Abstract: This paper presents CircleID, a large-scale ICDAR 2026 competition on writer identification and pen classification from scanned hand-drawn circles. The primary objective is to investigate how biometric writer characteristics and physical pen features naturally entangle within minimal, static traces. CircleID comprises two distinct tasks: (1) open-set writer identification, requiring models to recognize known writers while explicitly rejecting unknown ones, and (2) cross-writer pen classification, evaluated across both seen and unseen writers. Participants were provided with a new, controlled dataset of 46,155 tightly cropped circle images, digitized at 400 DPI and annotated for writer identity and pen type. The dataset comprises samples from 50 known and 16 unknown writers using eight different pens. Hosted on Kaggle as two separate tracks with public and private leaderboards, the competition provided participants with a ResNet baseline. In total, 389 teams (436 participants) made 3,185 submissions for the pen classification task, and 113 teams (141 participants) made 1,737 submissions for the writer identification track. The best-performing private leaderboard submissions achieved a Top-1 accuracy of 64.801% for writer identification and 92.726% for pen classification. This paper details the dataset, evaluates the winning methodologies, and analyzes the impact of out-of-distribution writers on model generalization and feature disentanglement. In this large-scale competition, CircleID establishes a new baseline for minimal-trace analysis.

</details>


### [119] [GazeVLM: Active Vision via Internal Attention Control for Multimodal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07817)
*Brown Ebouky,Gabriele Carrino,Niccolo Avogaro,Christoph Studer,Andrea Bartezzaghi,Mattia Rigotti*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GazeVLM introduces internal metacognitive gaze control via <LOOK> tokens to steer attention, enabling dynamic local-global reasoning and superior high-resolution multimodal performance within a 4B-param model using GRPO, without external cropping or extra patches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Vision-Language Models process visuals passively with massive token contexts that dilute spatial reasoning and invite linguistic hallucinations. A brain-like metacognitive mechanism is proposed to actively allocate attention resources and maintain peripheral scene awareness.

Method: A 4B-parameter multimodal architecture (GazeVLM) that generates autonomous gaze tokens (<LOOK>) to control its causal attention mask, implementing a continuous suppression bias for selective local reasoning (foveal-like) and restoring global view after local reasoning. Trained with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) that rewards valid grounding, avoiding external cropping tools and additional patches.

Result: Achieves strong high-resolution multimodal reasoning performance, surpassing state-of-the-art VLMs in its parameter class by ~4% and agentic multimodal pipelines by >5% on HRBench-4k and HRBench-8k.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a paradigm shift toward metacognitive, agency-like control within VLMs, enabling fluid transitions between global and local reasoning without external inputs and improving grounding and reasoning in high-resolution tasks.

Abstract: Human visual reasoning is governed by active vision, a process where metacognitive control drives top-down goal-directed attention, dynamically routing foveal focus toward task-relevant details while maintaining peripheral awareness of the global scene. In contrast, modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) process visual information passively, relying on the static accumulation of massive token contexts that dilute spatial reasoning and induce linguistic hallucinations. Here we propose the following paradigm shift: GazeVLM, a multimodal architecture that internalizes this metacognitive oversight over its deployment of attention resources directly into the reasoning loop. By empowering the VLM to autonomously generate gaze tokens ($\texttt{<LOOK>}$), GazeVLM establishes a top-down control mechanism over its own causal attention mask. The model dynamically dictates its focal intent, triggering a continuous suppression bias that dampens irrelevant visual features, implementing spatial selective attention and simulating foveal fixation. Once local reasoning concludes, the bias lifts, seamlessly restoring the global view. This architecture enables the model to fluidly transition between global spatial awareness and localized focal reasoning without relying on external agentic contraptions like cropping tools, or inflating the context window with additional visual tokens derived from localized visual patches. Trained with a bespoke Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) procedure that rewards valid grounding, our 4B-parameter GazeVLM delivers strong high-resolution multimodal reasoning performance, surpassing state-of-the-art VLMs in its parameter class by nearly 4% and agentic multimodal pipelines built around thinking with images by more than 5% on HRBench-4k and HRBench-8k.

</details>


### [120] [Divide and Conquer: Object Co-occurrence Helps Mitigate Simplicity Bias in OOD Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07821)
*Boyang Dai,Chaoqi Chen,Yizhou Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Object-Centric OOD detection (OCO) that leverages object co-occurrence in images by predicting disentangled representations and adaptively dividing patterns into three ID-based scenarios, enabling divide-and-conquer OOD detection for improved near-OOD and semantic/covariate shift handling; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current OOD methods rely on entangled features and often ignore rich scene context. Humans use object co-occurrence for understanding scenes, suggesting that modeling co-occurrence can improve OOD detection, especially for near-OOD where learning discriminative features is hard due to simplicity bias.

Method: Predict disentangled representations for a test sample, then adaptively partition observed object-cooccurrence patterns into three scenarios based on their prevalence in ID training data, and perform OOD detection in a divide-and-conquer fashion conditioned on the scenario.

Result: Demonstrates competitive performance across challenging and full-spectrum OOD benchmarks, with evidence that incorporating semantic contextual relationships helps address both semantic and covariate shifts.

Conclusion: Introduces a new object-centric paradigm for OOD detection (OCO) that leverages co-occurrence patterns to improve near-OOD discrimination and robustness to shifts; released code for reproducibility.

Abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is crucial for ensuring the reliability of deep learning models. Existing methods mostly focus on regular entangled representations to discriminate in-distribution (ID) and OOD data, neglecting the rich contextual information within images. This issue is particularly challenging for detecting near-OOD, as models with simplicity bias struggle to learn discriminative features in disentangled representations. The human visual system can use the co-occurrence of objects in the natural environment to facilitate scene understanding. Inspired by this, we propose an Object-Centric OOD detection framework that learns to capture Object CO-occurrence (OCO) patterns within images. The proposed method introduces a new OOD detection paradigm that understands object co-occurrence within an image by predicting disentangled representations for the test sample, then adaptively divides patterns into three scenarios based on object co-occurrence patterns observed in ID training data, and finally performs OOD detection in a divide-and-conquer manner. By doing so, OCO can distinguish near-OOD by considering the semantic contextual relationships present in their images, avoiding the tendency to focus solely on simple, easily learnable regions. We evaluate OCO through experiments across challenging and full-spectrum OOD settings, demonstrating competitive results and confirming its ability to address both semantic and covariate shifts. Code is released at https://github.com/Michael-McQueen/OCO.

</details>


### [121] [Explainable Part-Based Vehicle Classifier with Spatial Awareness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07831)
*Andreas Caduff,Klaus Zahn,Jonas Hofstetter,Martin Rechsteiner,Patrick Flaig*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A part-based, spatial-probabilistic vehicle classifier that extends a CNN detector with spatial maps and softmax-based final decision, achieving interpretability and robustness with comparable accuracy to end-to-end CNNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve interpretability and extensibility of vehicle classification in ITS while maintaining competitive accuracy. By moving from a fully black-box CNN to a modular, part-based approach with spatial context, the method enables adding new vehicle categories without retraining the entire model and reduces false positives from part detections.

Method: 1) Use a CNN-based detector to locate semantically meaningful vehicle parts. 2) Build spatial probability maps for each part conditioned on the vehicle category, replacing binary presence with probabilistic, spatially-aware features. 3) Perform final classification via softmax regression on the constructed features. Compared to a state-of-the-art end-to-end CNN, the approach emphasizes interpretability and robustness while maintaining comparable accuracy.

Result: The spatially aware, part-based method shows improved robustness against false part detections and achieves accuracy comparable to a leading end-to-end CNN. It demonstrates that interpretability can be achieved without sacrificing performance, supporting easy extension to new vehicle categories.

Conclusion: Incorporating spatial probability maps into a part-based CNN framework yields competitive accuracy while enhancing interpretability and extensibility, making it a practical basis for ITS vehicle classification systems that balance performance with explainability.

Abstract: In the area of Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS), fine-grained vehicle classification systems play an essential role. Recently, the authors have presented a novel vision-based classification approach in which standard end-to-end Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have been decomposed into 1) a CNN-based detector for semantically strong vehicle parts, followed by 2) feature construction and 3) final classification by a decision tree. In contrast to conventional CNNs, this allows both easy extensibility to new vehicle categories - without the need to fully retrain the part detector - and an important step towards the interpretability of the model, removing partially the black-box nature inherent to CNNs. Here we present an important extension of this approach that now incorporates spatial awareness of the vehicle parts: while the feature construction 2) of the previous approach used a binary decision for each feature (present vs. absent), now a full spatial probability map is constructed to condition the presence of each individual part with respect to a given vehicle category. The classification is performed using a softmax regression approach for the overall vehicle probabilities. This method shows a considerably improved robustness against false (part-)detections, a point that is crucial for practical application. Comparative analyses with a state-of-the-art end-to-end CNN indicate that our part-based methods achieve comparable accuracy, effectively challenging the presumed trade-off between accuracy and explainability. This research represents a significant advance in vehicle classification for ITS and forms the basis for systems that combine high accuracy with intuitive interpretability.

</details>


### [122] [BRIDGE: Background Routing and Isolated Discrete Gating for Coarse-Mask Local Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07846)
*Peilin Xiong,Honghui Yuan,Junwen Chen,Keiji Yanai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BRIDGE decouples mask guidance from the image-editing backbone to mitigate mask-shape bias in coarse-mask local edits, using a two-path BridgePath (Main Path for background, Subject Path for edits) and a learnable Discrete Geometric Gate to route token positional embeddings, achieving stronger local editing while preserving context with a compact ~13.3M parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rough masks often act as unintended shape priors, pulling edited objects toward mask boundaries. A Two-Zone Constraint is proposed to keep the background stable while the editable region follows user instruction without inheriting mask contours.

Method: BRIDGE keeps masks outside the backbone for support construction and blending, avoiding internal mask injection. It introduces BridgePath generation with a Main Path (background context) and a Subject Path (editable content from independent noise). A Learnable Discrete Geometric Gate routes token-level positional embeddings, deciding whether subject tokens reuse background coordinates near fusion zones or maintain subject-centric coordinates for geometric freedom. Evaluation is performed on BRIDGE-Bench, MagicBrush, and ICE-Bench, with a compact parameter count (13.31M).

Result: On BRIDGE-Bench, Local SigLIP2-T improves from 0.262 (FLUX.1-Fill) and 0.390 (ACE++) to 0.503, with parallel gains in local DINO and DreamSim. Zero-shot results on MagicBrush and ICE-Bench show competitive alignment and source preservation beyond the benchmark. The routing module remains compact relative to ControlNet-style copied branches, at ~13.31M parameters.

Conclusion: BRIDGE effectively mitigates mask-shape bias by separating mask guidance from the backbone, enabling background stability and flexible subject edits with a lightweight routing module, and demonstrates robust performance gains across both curated and zero-shot evaluations.

Abstract: Coarse-mask local image editing asks a model to modify a user-indicated region while preserving the surrounding scene. In practice, however, rough masks often become unintended shape priors: instead of serving as flexible edit support, the mask can pull the generated object toward its accidental boundary. We study this failure as mask-shape bias and frame the task through a Two-Zone Constraint, where the background should remain stable while the editable region should follow the instruction without being forced to inherit the mask contour. BRIDGE addresses this setting by keeping masks outside the DiT backbone for support construction and blending, avoiding DiT-internal mask injection and copied control branches. It uses BridgePath generation, where a Main Path preserves background context and a Subject Path generates editable content from independent noise. Motivated by a diagnostic Qwen-Image experiment showing that positional embeddings and attention connectivity regulate which image context visual tokens reuse, BRIDGE introduces a learnable Discrete Geometric Gate for token-level positional-embedding routing. This gate lets subject tokens borrow background-anchored coordinates near fusion regions or keep subject-centric coordinates for geometric freedom. We evaluate BRIDGE on BRIDGE-Bench, MagicBrush, and ICE-Bench. On BRIDGE-Bench, BRIDGE improves Local SigLIP2-T from 0.262 with FLUX.1-Fill and 0.390 with ACE++ to 0.503, with parallel gains in local DINO and DreamSim. Zero-shot results on MagicBrush and ICE-Bench further indicate competitive alignment and source preservation beyond the curated benchmark, while the added routing module remains compact at 13.31M parameters compared with ControlNet-style copied branches.

</details>


### [123] [EyeCue: Driver Cognitive Distraction Detection via Gaze-Empowered Egocentric Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07859)
*Lang Zhang,JinYi Yoon,Matthew Corbett,Abhijit Sarkar,Bo Ji*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EyeCue is a gaze-empowered framework that detects driver cognitive distraction by modeling the interaction between eye gaze and egocentric visual context; the authors introduce CogDrive, a multi-scenario dataset augmenting four driving datasets with cognitive distraction annotations, achieving 74.38% accuracy and strong cross-scenario generalization; code and data are released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cognitive distraction is a subtle, non-visual form of driver distraction that is difficult to detect with appearance cues alone, yet it critically impacts driving safety. There is a lack of large, diverse datasets to study gaze-context interactions in driving. The work aims to leverage gaze-context interactions and provide a scalable dataset to improve detection of cognitive distraction.

Method: Propose EyeCue, which fuses eye gaze with egocentric video to model temporal gaze-context interactions for driver attention; introduce CogDrive to augment four existing driving datasets with cognitive distraction annotations; evaluate across multiple driving scenarios and baselines to demonstrate robustness and generalizability.

Result: EyeCue achieves 74.38% accuracy, outperforming 11 baselines from 6 model families by over 7%; maintains over 70% accuracy across different road types, times of day, and weather conditions, showing strong generalizability.

Conclusion: Modeling gaze-context interactions is crucial for cognitive distraction detection in driving; EyeCue demonstrates effective cross-modal interaction modeling and benefits from the CogDrive dataset, with publicly released code and data enabling further research.

Abstract: Driver cognitive distraction is a major cause of road collisions and remains difficult to detect. Unlike manual or visual distraction, cognitive distraction is diverted by thoughts unrelated to driving, even when the driver appears visually attentive and exhibits no explicit physical movements. In this work, we propose EyeCue, a gaze-empowered egocentric video understanding framework, to detect driver cognitive distraction. A key insight is that cognitive distraction manifests in the interaction between eye gaze and visual context. To capture this interaction, EyeCue integrates eye gaze with egocentric video to enable context-aware modeling of the driver's attention over time. Furthermore, to tackle the limited scale and diversity of existing datasets, we introduce CogDrive, a comprehensive multi-scenario dataset that augments four existing driving datasets with cognitive distraction annotations. Through extensive evaluations on CogDrive, we show that EyeCue achieves the highest accuracy of 74.38%, outperforming 11 baselines from 6 model families by over 7%. Notably, EyeCue can achieve an accuracy of over 70% across various driving scenarios (different road types, times of day, and weather conditions) with strong generalizability. These results highlight the importance of modeling gaze-context interactions and the effectiveness of cross-modal interaction modeling for multimodal cognitive distraction detection. Our codes and CogDrive dataset resources are available at https://github.com/langzhang2000/EyeCue.

</details>


### [124] [From Synthetic to Real: Toward Identity-Consistent Makeup Transfer with Synthetic and Real Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07861)
*Yue Yu,Jiayu Wang,Jiajia Shi,Jingjing Chen,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A data-curation pipeline ConsistentBeauty and a synthetic-to-real post-training framework RealBeauty improve makeup transfer by aligning synthetic data with identity fidelity and bridging the synthetic-real domain gap, plus a new diverse benchmark; achieves SOTA with strong identity preservation in real-world scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two core issues: (1) synthetic supervision often distorts identity; (2) domain gap limits generalization to real-world makeup transfers.

Method: 1) ConsistentBeauty data curation for makeup fidelity and strict identity consistency within synthesized data. 2) RealBeauty framework for synthetic-to-real adaptation, including reinforcement learning with task-specific verifiable rewards to exploit real makeup patterns beyond synthetic supervision. 3) New diverse benchmark spanning skin tones, ages, genders, poses, makeup styles.

Result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks, with improved identity preservation and enhanced handling of complex real-world cases.

Conclusion: The approach mitigates limitations of purely supervised synthetic data and domain gap, enabling better generalization and robust makeup transfer; the new benchmark facilitates comprehensive evaluation.

Abstract: Makeup transfer aims to apply the makeup style of a reference portrait to a source portrait while preserving identity and background. Early methods formulate this task as unsupervised image-to-image translation, relying on surrogate objectives and often yielding limited performance. Recent diffusion- and flow-based approaches instead exploit synthetic data for supervised training, leading to significant improvements. However, these methods still face two critical challenges: synthetic supervision frequently fails to faithfully preserve identity, and the domain gap between synthetic and real data limits generalization, resulting in degraded performance in complex real-world scenarios. To address these issues, this paper first proposes ConsistentBeauty, a novel data curation pipeline that ensures makeup fidelity and strict identity consistency within the synthesized data. Second, we propose RealBeauty, a synthetic-to-real post-training framework. Beyond supervised learning on curated synthetic data, we further adapt the model to real-world scenarios through reinforcement learning and design novel verifiable rewards tailored to the makeup transfer task. It allows the model to further benefit from real makeup patterns beyond synthetic supervision. In addition, we establish a new diverse benchmark for makeup transfer, covering a wide range of skin tones, ages, genders, poses, and makeup styles, thereby enabling a more comprehensive evaluation of model performance under diverse real-world conditions. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks and demonstrates clear advantages in identity preservation and performance on complex real-world cases.

</details>


### [125] [Video Understanding Reward Modeling: A Robust Benchmark and Performant Reward Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07872)
*Yuancheng Wei,Linli Yao,Lei Li,Haojie Zhang,Hao Zhou,Fandong Meng,Xu Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Video Understanding Reward Benchmark (VURB) and Video Understanding Preference Dataset (VUP-35K); trains discriminative VideoDRM and generative VideoGRM reward models; achieves state-of-the-art results and shows data improves reasoning and scaling performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in robust evaluation benchmarks and high-quality preference data for video reward modeling; provide a unified framework for benchmark design, data construction, and reward model training; enable better evaluation of video understanding and reasoning.

Method: Proposes VURB with 2,100 preference pairs including long chain-of-thought traces (avg ~1,143 tokens) and majority voting evaluation across general, long, and reasoning-oriented tasks. Builds VUP-35K via an automated pipeline for large-scale supervision. Trains two reward models: VideoDRM (discriminative) and VideoGRM (generative). Evaluates on VURB and VideoRewardBench, with best-of-N test-time scaling.

Result: Both models achieve state-of-the-art performance on VURB and VideoRewardBench. VUP-35K improves reward performance and model reasoning capability. Significant gains observed under best-of-N test-time scaling.

Conclusion: A unified framework for video reward modeling (benchmark, data, and models) is effective; automated data construction yields high-quality supervision; both discriminative and generative rewards are valuable, particularly under scalable evaluation.

Abstract: Multimodal reward models have advanced substantially in text and image domains, yet progress in video understanding reward modeling remains severely limited by the lack of robust evaluation benchmarks and high-quality preference data. To address this, we propose a unified framework spanning benchmark design, data construction, and reward model training. We introduce Video Understanding Reward Bench (VURB), a benchmark featuring 2,100 preference pairs with long chain-of-thought reasoning traces (averaging 1,143 tokens) and majority voting evaluation across general, long, and reasoning-oriented video tasks. We further construct Video Understanding Preference Dataset (VUP-35K) via a fully automated pipeline, providing large-scale high-quality supervision for video reward training. Building on the data, we train VideoDRM and VideoGRM, a discriminative and a generative reward model, both achieving state-of-the-art performance on VURB and VideoRewardBench. Further analysis confirms that VUP-35K enhances both reward performance and model reasoning capability, while VideoDRM and VideoGRM yield significant gains under best-of-$N$ test-time scaling.

</details>


### [126] [Semantic-Aware Adaptive Visual Memory for Streaming Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07897)
*Hang Wu,Sherin Mary Mathews,Yujun Cai,Ming-Hsuan Yang,Yiwei Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free, dual-stage framework SAVEMem introduces semantic-aware memory and query-aware retrieval for streaming video understanding, achieving higher task scores and reduced memory usage without model training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Streaming video understanding requires managing unbounded inputs under fixed memory while answering queries with variable timing. Existing compression relies on visual similarity or post-hoc KV-cache retrieval and lacks semantic guidance or tight stage coordination.

Method: Stage 1 builds a fixed-budget, three-tier streaming memory with a light semantic prior via a pseudo-question bank to bias retention by semantic salience. Stage 2 uses query-aware retrieval with an anchor-conditioned recency gate to adapt the retrieval scope from short-term to long-term, followed by late interaction between query and memory tokens to select candidate frames.

Result: When applied to Qwen2.5-VL without training, SAVEMem raises the OVO-Bench score from 52.27 to 62.69 and yields consistent gains on StreamingBench and ODV-Bench, while reducing peak GPU memory by 48% at 128 frames compared with the backbone.

Conclusion: SAVEMem provides a training-free solution that injects semantic awareness into memory and adapts the retrieval scope per query, improving performance and memory efficiency across multiple benchmarks; its effectiveness depends on the quality of the semantic prior and the adaptability of the anchor-conditioned gate.

Abstract: Online streaming video understanding requires models to process continuous visual inputs and respond to user queries in real time, where the unbounded stream and unpredictable query timing turn memory management into a central challenge. Existing methods typically compress visual tokens via visual similarity heuristics, or augment compression with KV-cache-level retrieval. However, compression decisions rarely incorporate semantic signals, and retrieval is often added after compression is finalized, making the two stages hard to coordinate. We present SAVEMem, a training-free dual-stage framework that brings semantic awareness into memory generation and lets the retrieval scope adapt per query. In Stage~1, SAVEMem builds a three-tier streaming memory online under a constant memory budget. A fixed pseudo-question bank provides a lightweight semantic prior, so that long-term retention is shaped by semantic salience rather than visual similarity alone. In Stage~2, SAVEMem performs query-aware retrieval over this memory.
