An anchor-conditioned recency gate adapts the retrieval scope from short-term to mid- and long-term memory based on whether the query targets the present or the distant past. Within this scope, late interaction between query and memory tokens selects candidate frames for answering. Applied to Qwen2.5-VL without training, SAVEMem improves the OVO-Bench overall score from 52.27 to 62.69 and yields consistent gains on StreamingBench and ODV-Bench, while reducing peak GPU memory by 48\% at 128 frames over the backbone.

</details>


### [127] [One World, Dual Timeline: Decoupled Spatio-Temporal Gaussian Scene Graph for 4D Cooperative Driving Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07910)
*Yulong Chen,Xiaoyun Dong,Haoyu Zhang,Zongxian Yang,Lewei Xie,Xinke Li,Yifan Zhang,Kai Wang,Jianping Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Dust decouples per-source poses in Gaussian Scene Graphs for 4D cooperative driving, addressing asynchrony; achieves state-of-the-art on V2X-Seq.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Temporal asynchrony between independent vehicle and infrastructure cameras causes cross-source interference in single-timeline GS models, manifesting as ghosting on dynamic agents.

Method: Introduce Dust: a canonical Gaussian appearance per agent; decouple pose trajectories per source aligned to each source capture timestamp; prove gradient kernel becomes block-diagonal; static anchor-based pose correction; pose-regularized joint optimization.

Result: On 26 V2X-Seq sequences, dynamic-area PSNR improves by 3.2 dB over the best baseline; Fréchet Video Distance reduces by 37.7%; robust to larger temporal asynchrony.

Conclusion: Decoupled spatio-temporal modeling in Gaussian Scene Graphs enables effective 4D reconstruction in VICAD, with theoretical guarantees and practical corrections yielding strong empirical gains.

Abstract: Reconstructing dynamic scenes from Vehicle-to-Infrastructure Cooperative Autonomous Driving (VICAD) data is fundamentally complicated by temporal asynchrony: vehicle and infrastructure cameras operate on independent clocks, capturing the same dynamic agent such as cars and pedestrians at different physical times. Existing Gaussian Scene Graph methods implicitly assume synchronized observations and assign a single pose per agent per frame, which is an assumption that breaks in cooperative settings, where the resulting gradient conflicts cause severe ghosting on dynamic agents. We identify this as a representation-level failure, not an optimization artifact: we prove that any single-timeline formulation incurs an irreducible photometric loss scaling quadratically with agent velocity and cross-source time offset. To resolve this, we propose Dust (DecoUpled Spatio-Temporal) Gaussian Scene Graph for 4D Cooperative Driving Reconstruction. DUST Gaussian Scene Graph shares a canonical Gaussian set per agent for appearance consistency, while maintaining decouple pose trajectories aligned to each source's true capture timestamps. We prove that this decoupling enables the pose-gradient kernel block-diagonal, eliminating cross-source interference entirely. To make Dust practical, we further introduce a static anchor-based pose correction pipeline that corrects spatio misalignment between vehicle and infrastructure annotations, and a pose-regularized joint optimization scheme that prevents trajectory jitter and drift during early training. On 26 sequences from V2X-Seq, DUST achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving dynamic-area PSNR by 3.2 dB over the strongest baseline and reducing Fréchet Video Distance by 37.7%, with keeping robustness under larger temporal asynchrony.

</details>


### [128] [What Matters for Diffusion-Friendly Latent Manifold? Prior-Aligned Autoencoders for Latent Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07915)
*Zhengrong Yue,Taihang Hu,Mengting Chen,Haiyu Zhang,Zihao Pan,Tao Liu,Zikang Wang,Jinsong Lan,Xiaoyong Zhu,Bo Zheng,Yali Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Explicitly shaping the latent manifold of tokenizers for latent diffusion models via Prior-Aligned AutoEncoder (PAE) yields faster training and superior generation, achieving state-of-the-art gFID on ImageNet 256x256.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current tokenizers either optimize reconstruction fidelity or inherit pretrained representations, but do not ensure a diffusion-friendly latent space. The paper investigates which latent-space properties correlate with generation quality and aims to design a tokenizer that enforces these properties.

Method: Construct controlled tokenizer variants to study latent-manifold properties. Identify three diffusion-friendly properties—coherent spatial structure, local manifold continuity, and global manifold semantics—and show their stronger correlation with generation quality than reconstruction fidelity. Propose Prior-Aligned AutoEncoder (PAE) that uses refined priors from Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) and perturbation-based regularization to explicitly train for spatial structure, locality, and semantics.

Result: PAE improves training efficiency and generation quality over existing tokenizers on ImageNet 256x256, achieving performance comparable to Rectangle AutoEncoder (RAE) with up to 13x faster convergence under the same training setup and a new state-of-the-art gFID of 1.03.

Conclusion: Organizing the latent manifold is crucial for diffusion-based generation. Explicitly shaping the latent space with priors and regularization (PAE) yields diffusion-friendly manifolds and substantial gains, suggesting broader applicability to latent diffusion models.

Abstract: Tokenizers are a crucial component of latent diffusion models, as they define the latent space in which diffusion models operate. However, existing tokenizers are primarily designed to improve reconstruction fidelity or inherit pretrained representations, leaving unclear what kind of latent space is truly friendly for generative modeling. In this paper, we study this question from the perspective of latent manifold organization. By constructing controlled tokenizer variants, we identify three key properties of a diffusion-friendly latent manifold: coherent spatial structure, local manifold continuity, and global manifold semantics. We find that these properties are more consistent with downstream generation quality than reconstruction fidelity. Motivated by this finding, we propose the Prior-Aligned AutoEncoder (PAE), which explicitly shapes the latent manifold instead of leaving diffusion-friendly manifold to emerge indirectly from reconstruction or inheritance. Specifically, PAE leverages refined priors derived from VFMs and perturbation-based regularization to turn spatial structure, local continuity, and global semantics into explicit training objectives. On ImageNet 256x256, PAE improves both training efficiency and generation quality over existing tokenizers, reaching performance comparable to RAE with up to 13x faster convergence under the same training setup and achieving a new state-of-the-art gFID of 1.03. These results highlight the importance of organizing the latent manifold for latent diffusion models.

</details>


### [129] [MedVIGIL: Evaluating Trustworthy Medical VLMs Under Broken Visual Evidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07919)
*Hanqi Jiang,Junhao Chen,Yi Pan,Lifeng Chen,Weihang You,Haozhen Gong,Ruiyu Yan,Jinglei Lv,Lin Zhao,Hui Ren,Quanzheng Li,Tianming Liu,Xiang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: medvigil is a radiologist-annotated benchmark (300 cases; 2,556 MCQ probes; 240 counterfactual triplets; ROI and risk annotations) to detect silent failures in medical VQA under perturbed evidence, evaluating 16 vision models and 2 text baselines, with a composite MCS score.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: to ensure trustworthy clinical deployment of medical vision–language models by testing their ability to recognize when evidential support is faulty or absent, addressing the gap of evaluations that rely on intact premises.

Method: A four-radiologist team constructed medvigil with end-to-end supervision, parallel dual annotations, a senior consolidation, and an independent radiologist human baseline. The dataset includes gold answers, refusal options, candidate-answer sets, paraphrases, false-premise traps, ROI boxes, and clinical risk tiers. It comprises 2,556 MCQ probes, 240 counterfactual triplets, risk/admissibility annotations, ROI annotations, and an open-ended variant. Seven correctness-conditioned audit metrics are aggregated into the medvigil Composite Score (MCS).

Result: Independent radiologist assigns MCS 83.3 with a silent-failure rate of 5.8%; strongest model (Claude Opus 4.7) achieves 69.2 MCS, leaving 14.1-point headroom. The benchmark and harness are publicly released, and evaluated across 16 vision-capable models plus 2 text baselines.

Conclusion: medvigil provides a rigorous, publicly available evaluation framework for assessing the trustworthiness of medical VLMs under evidence perturbations, revealing persistent silent-failure risks and offering a standardized benchmark for progress.

Abstract: Medical vision--language models (VLMs) are usually evaluated on intact image--question pairs, but trustworthy clinical use requires a stronger property: a model must recognise when the evidential basis for an answer has failed. We study this through silent failures under perturbed evidence, where a vision-required medical question is paired with a false premise, wording perturbation, knowledge-only rewrite, or ROI-corrupted image, yet the model returns a fluent non-refusal answer. We introduce medvigil, a 300-case evaluation suite drawn from four public medical VQA sources, supervised end to end by four board-certified radiologists: every gold answer, refusal option, candidate-answer set, paraphrase, false-premise trap, ROI box, and clinical risk tier is clinician-authored. Two attending radiologists annotate every case in parallel, a senior radiologist consolidates the released manifest, and a separate fourth radiologist independent of construction answers every probe to provide the human reference baseline. The release contains 2{,}556 MCQ probes, 240 counterfactual triplets, physician-adjudicated risk-tier and answerability flags, ROI boxes, and a paired open-ended variant. We report seven correctness-conditioned audit metrics that summarise into the medvigil Composite Score (MCS), and audit 16 vision-capable models plus two text-only baselines. The independent radiologist scores MCS 83.3 at silent-failure rate 5.8%, leaving a 14.1-point composite headroom above the strongest audited model (Claude Opus 4.7 at 69.2). The benchmark and evaluation harness are publicly released.

</details>


### [130] [One Token Per Frame: Reconsidering Visual Bandwidth in World Models for VLA Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07931)
*Zuojin Tang,Shengchao Yuan,Xiaoxin Bai,Zhiyuan Jin,De Ma,Gang Pan,Bin Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OneWM-VLA shows that compressing each frame to a single semantic token and training a unified flow-matching objective can improve long-horizon VLA performance while using a frozen backbone and small LoRA adapters, achieving substantial gains on several long-horizon benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address how to parameterize world-model modules on top of pretrained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) systems under limited adaptation budget, ensuring efficient use of visual information and coherent action planning over long horizons.

Method: Introduce Adaptive Attention Pooling to reduce per-frame visual input to one semantic token, forming a latent stream. Train a world-model-augmented VLA with a single flow-matching objective that jointly produces the latent stream and action trajectory, without a separate decoder. Use a π0 (2B) backbone with 14.71M LoRA parameters.

Result: Significant performance gains across tasks: MetaWorld MT50 from 47.9% to 61.3% average success; LIBERO-Long from 85.2% to 95.6%; Fold Cloth (real Piper arm) from 20.0% to 60.0% on long-horizon deformable task.

Conclusion: Reducing per-frame visual bandwidth to a single token is viable for long-horizon VLA with a frozen backbone and small adapters, and a unified flow-matching objective suffices to align latent representations with action trajectories, yielding strong empirical gains.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models increasingly rely on auxiliary world modules to plan over long horizons, yet how such modules should be parameterized on top of a pretrained VLA remains an open design question. Existing world-model-augmented VLAs typically pass the per-frame visual stream into the world module at high visual bandwidth and treat its rollout as a side product of action prediction; under a constrained adaptation budget on a frozen backbone, this leaves both the per-frame representation and the latent action coupling under-examined. We introduce OneWM-VLA, which compresses each view into a single semantic token per frame through an Adaptive Attention Pooling, and produces the resulting latent stream and the action trajectory under a single flow-matching objective rather than connecting them through a separate decoder. Empirically, we find that per-frame visual bandwidth can be reduced to a single token without compromising long-horizon performance under our setup. Trained with 14.71M LoRA parameters on a $π_0$ (2B) backbone, OneWM-VLA improves the average success rate from 47.9% to 61.3% on MetaWorld~MT50, reaches 95.6% on LIBERO-Long (vs.85.2% for $π_0$), and reaches 60.0% on the long-horizon deformable task Fold Cloth on a real Piper arm (vs.20.0% for $π_0$).

</details>


### [131] [Delta-Adapter: Scalable Exemplar-Based Image Editing with Single-Pair Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07940)
*Jiacheng Chen,Songze Li,Han Fu,Baoquan Zhao,Wei Liu,Yanyan Liang,Li Qing,Xudong Mao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Delta-Adapter enables single-pair supervised exemplar-based image editing by extracting a semantic delta via a pre-trained vision encoder and injecting it into a pre-trained editing model through a Perceiver-based adapter, plus a semantic delta consistency loss; achieves improved editing accuracy and content fidelity and generalizes to unseen edits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pair-of-pairs supervision constrains training data collection and hampers generalization; a scalable, single-pair supervision approach without textual guidance is desirable to broaden editing capabilities.

Method: Extract a semantic delta from a source-target pair using a pre-trained vision encoder. Inject this delta into a pre-trained editing model via a Perceiver-based adapter. Train under single-pair supervision using the target as the supervision signal, while never directly exposing the target image to the model. Introduce a semantic delta consistency loss to align the generation’s semantic change with the exemplar delta.

Result: Empirically, Delta-Adapter surpasses four strong baselines on seen edits in terms of editing accuracy and content fidelity, and shows stronger generalization to unseen editing tasks.

Conclusion: The approach offers scalable transferable image editing under single-pair supervision by leveraging semantic deltas and a versatile adapter, enabling effective reuse of existing editing datasets and improving cross-task generalization; code will be released.

Abstract: Exemplar-based image editing applies a transformation defined by a source-target image pair to a new query image. Existing methods rely on a pair-of-pairs supervision paradigm, requiring two image pairs sharing the same edit semantics to learn the target transformation. This constraint makes training data difficult to curate at scale and limits generalization across diverse edit types. We propose Delta-Adapter, a method that learns transferable editing semantics under single-pair supervision, requiring no textual guidance. Rather than directly exposing the exemplar pair to the model, we leverage a pre-trained vision encoder to extract a semantic delta that encodes the visual transformation between the two images. This semantic delta is injected into a pre-trained image editing model via a Perceiver-based adapter. Since the target image is never directly visible to the model, it can serve as the prediction target, enabling single-pair supervision without requiring additional exemplar pairs. This formulation allows us to leverage existing large-scale editing datasets for training. To further promote faithful transformation transfer, we introduce a semantic delta consistency loss that aligns the semantic change of the generated output with the ground-truth semantic delta extracted from the exemplar pair. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Delta-Adapter consistently improves both editing accuracy and content consistency over four strong baselines on seen editing tasks, while also generalizing more effectively to unseen editing tasks. Code will be available at https://delta-adapter.github.io.

</details>


### [132] [Rebalancing gradient to improve self-supervised co-training of depth, odometry and optical flow predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07945)
*Marwane Hariat,Antoine Manzanera,David Filliat*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoopNet dynamically balances gradient flow among co-trained networks to improve self-supervised learning for depth, odometry, and optical flow by using a disagreement-based hybrid loss that filters moving-object pixels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve cooperative learning among co-trained networks and robustly train on self-supervised signals by addressing when moving objects corrupt reconstruction-based losses; ensure equitable progress across tasks.

Method: Introduce dynamic gradient apportionment and a hybrid loss built on a distribution model of photometric reconstruction errors from depth+odometry vs. optical flow networks; moving-object pixels are those with large disagreement between reconstructions; provide theoretical justification and experimental validation.

Result: On KITTI and CityScapes, CoopNet achieves improvements or parity with state-of-the-art in depth, odometry, and optical flow predictions.

Conclusion: Dynamic gradient apportionment and disagreement-driven masking enable effective joint training across related tasks in self-supervised settings, offering competitive performance on standard benchmarks.

Abstract: We present CoopNet, an approach that improves the cooperation of co-trained networks by dynamically adapting the apportionment of gradient, to ensure equitable learning progress. It is applied to motion-aware self-supervised prediction of depth maps, by introducing a new hybrid loss, based on a distribution model of photo-metric reconstruction errors made by, on the one hand the depth + odometry paired networks, and on the other hand the optical flow network. This model essentially assumes that the pixels from moving objects (that must be discarded for training depth and odometry), correspond to those where the two reconstructions strongly disagree. We justify this model by theoretical considerations and experimental evidences. A comparative evaluation on KITTI and CityScapes datasets shows that CoopNet improves or is comparable to the state-of-the-art in depth, odometry and optical flow predictions.

</details>


### [133] [TimeLesSeg: Unified Contrast-Agnostic Cross-Sectional and Longitudinal MS Lesion Segmentation via a Stochastic Generative Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07955)
*Vicent Caselles-Ballester,Eloy Martínez-Heras,Giuseppe Pontillo,Zoe Mendelsohn,Elena M. Marrón,Juan Luis García Fernández,Laia Subirats,Jon Stutters,Jeremy Chataway,Frederik Barkhof,Sara Llufriu,Ferran Prados*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A unified, contrast-agnostic CNN (TimeLesSeg) segments MS lesions across cross-sectional and longitudinal inputs by using lesion masks as priors, simulating lesion evolution, and Gaussian mixture model-based domain randomization; it outperforms contrast-agnostic SOTA on single-modality data and improves longitudinal lesion load dynamics over SAMSEG and LST-AI, with open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MS lesion segmentation exhibits high heterogeneity and distribution shifts (scanner changes) and bifurcation between cross-sectional and longitudinal approaches. A single network that handles presence/absence of temporal information and varying contrast is highly desirable.

Method: TimeLesSeg is a single CNN that ingests the current scan plus lesion priors. Cross-sectional capability is achieved by training with empty priors. Longitudinal priors are generated by stochastically deforming lesions with morphological operations to simulate evolution. Contrast-agnosticism is attained via Gaussian mixture model-based domain randomization. The model leverages priors processed with the current scan.

Result: On three public and two in-house datasets, TimeLesSeg outperforms the contrast-agnostic SOTA on single-modality inputs across overlap- and distance-based metrics. In longitudinal settings, it outperforms SAMSEG and better captures lesion load dynamics than SAMSEG and LST-AI.

Conclusion: TimeLesSeg provides a unified, robust framework for MS lesion segmentation across modalities and time, combining prior-based learning, synthetic longitudinal priors, and data-driven contrast normalization, with open-source code.

Abstract: Multiple sclerosis (MS) expresses substantial clinical and radiological heterogeneity, which poses significant challenges for automatic lesion segmentation. The current deep learning-based SOTA is highly susceptible to changes in both distribution, e.g., changes in scanner; as well as the structure of inputs, evident in the current divide between cross-sectional and longitudinal approaches. We introduce TimeLesSeg, a unified contrast-agnostic framework designed to segment MS lesions regardless of the presence of a temporal dimension in its inputs, with a single convolutional neural network. Our approach models pathological priors through lesion masks, which are processed together with the current scan. Cross-sectional processing is enabled by exposing the model to training cases where no prior information is available, which are modeled with an empty mask, allowing it to operate seamlessly in both scenarios. To overcome the scarcity and inconsistency of longitudinal datasets, we propose a novel generative pipeline in which patterns of lesion evolution are simulated by stochastically deforming each individual lesion with morphological operations, producing realistic prior timepoints. In parallel, we achieve contrast agnosticism through Gaussian mixture model-based domain randomization, enabling the network to experience a wide spectrum of intensity profiles. Results on three publicly available and two in-house datasets show that TimeLesSeg outperforms the contrast-agnostic state of the art on single-modality inputs across overlap- and distance-based metrics. In longitudinal processing, our method outperforms SAMSEG, and captures lesion load dynamics more accurately than both the former and LST-AI. All source code related to the development of TimeLesSeg is available at https://github.com/NeuroADaS-Lab/TimeLesSeg.

</details>


### [134] [DVD: Discrete Voxel Diffusion for 3D Generation and Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07971)
*Zhengrui Xiang,Jiaqi Wu,Fupeng Sun,Heliang Zheng,Yingzhen Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Discrete Voxel Diffusion (DVD) introduces a discrete, categorical diffusion process for sparse voxel generation, uncertainty estimation, and editing in 3D pipelines, enabling efficient inpainting in a single pass.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations of continuous diffusion for sparse voxel scaffolds by modeling voxel occupancy as native discrete variables, providing interpretable dynamics, robust uncertainty, and lightweight editing capabilities.

Method: Develop a discrete diffusion framework where voxel occupancy is treated as categorical variables; leverage entropy as an uncertainty metric; apply block-structured perturbations for lightweight fine-tuning; integrate as a first-stage prior in SLat-based 3D generation, enabling inpainting and editing within a single sampling round.

Result: DVD yields quality gains and provides interpretable generation dynamics via explicit categoricals; predictive entropy identifies ambiguous voxel regions for data filtering and quality assessment; a lightweight fine-tuning strategy enables inpaint/edit with negligible extra computation and no extra model evaluations.

Conclusion: Discrete diffusion is a viable, interpretable, and efficient prior for sparse voxel generation and editing in 3D generative pipelines, with practical benefits in uncertainty estimation and one-shot editing.

Abstract: We introduce Discrete Voxel Diffusion (DVD), a discrete diffusion framework to generate, assess, and edit sparse voxels for SLat (Structured LATent) based 3D generative pipelines. Although discrete diffusion has not generally displaced continuous diffusion in image-like generation, we show that it can be an effective first-stage prior for sparse voxel scaffolds. By treating voxel occupancy as a native discrete variable, DVD avoids continuous-to-discrete thresholding and provides a simple framework for voxel generation, uncertainty estimation, and editing. Beyond quality gains, DVD provides more interpretable generation dynamics through explicit categorical modeling. Furthermore, we leverage the predictive entropy as a robust uncertainty metric to identify ambiguous voxel regions and complicated samples, facilitating tasks such as data filtering and quality assessment. Finally, we propose a lightweight fine-tuning strategy using block-structured perturbation patterns. This approach empowers the model to inpaint and edit voxels within a single sampling round, requiring negligible auxiliary computation and no additional model evaluations.

</details>


### [135] [HEART: Hyperspherical Embedding Alignment via Kent-Representation Traversal in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07973)
*Arani Roy,Shristi Das Biswas,Kaushik Roy*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HEART reframes text-to-image editing on a hypersphere: it uses Kent-distribution–based, geodesic edits on the embedding sphere to enable training-free, geometry-consistent object replacements and attribute tweaks, preserving the scene across diffusion-model architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-based edits in Euclidean embedding space suffer from unintended side effects due to non-linear, anisotropic semantic structure. The authors hypothesize that concept embeddings lie on a hypersphere and are better captured by Kent distributions, motivating geometry-aware edits.

Method: Propose HEART, a training-free framework that performs Kent-aware geodesic transformations directly on the embedding hypersphere. It avoids finetuning, inversion, or optimization and is model-agnostic, operating on the existing text embeddings to effect edits via spherical geometry adjustments.

Result: Demonstrates intuitive edits such as consistent subject replacement and fine-grained attribute control while preserving the original scene, with fast edits and generalization across diffusion architectures; emphasizes that shifting perspective from linear Euclidean to spherical geometry enables improved controllability.

Conclusion: Embedding geometry of text encoders is hyperspherical and anisotropic; Kent-based geodesic edits provide a simple, effective, training-free edit mechanism for diffusion models, enabling precise, robust control without altering background content.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models can generate visually stunning images, yet, controlling what appears and how it appears, remains surprisingly difficult, especially when operating solely within the constraints of the text-conditioning space. For example, changing a subject or adjusting an attribute often leads to unintended side effects, such as altered backgrounds or distorted details. This is because most existing text-based control methods treat the embedding space as Euclidean and apply simple linear transformations, which do not reflect how semantic concepts are actually organized. In this work, we take a step back and ask: what is the true geometry of these embeddings? We find that text encoder representations lie on a hypersphere, where concepts are not linear directions but structured, anisotropic distributions better captured by Kent distributions. Building on this insight, we propose HEART, a training-free framework that performs Kent-aware geodesic transformations directly on the hypersphere. By respecting the underlying geometry, HEART enables intuitive and precise edits, such as consistent subject replacement and fine-grained attribute control, while preserving the original scene. Importantly, HEART requires no finetuning, inversion, or optimization, and generalizes across diffusion model architectures. Our results show that a simple shift in perspective, from linear to spherical, can unlock fast, and controllable image generation.

</details>


### [136] [Seeing Across Skies and Streets: Feedforward 3D Reconstruction from Satellite, Drone, and Ground Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07978)
*Qiwei Wang,Zhongyao Tuo,Xianghui Ze,Yujiao Shi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Cross3R uses a single UAV image as an intermediate viewpoint to recover a cross-view 3D point cloud and 6-DoF poses, enabling precise cross-view localization from satellite imagery. It introduces CrossGeo, a 278K-image tri-view dataset, and achieves state-of-the-art results on CrossGeo and KITTI without KITTI-specific training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nadir satellite imagery lacks roll, pitch, and altitude cues, forcing 3-DoF localization that fails on uneven terrain and tilted cameras. An intermediate UAV view reveals 3D structure and 6-DoF information, enabling robust cross-view localization.

Method: Cross3R is a flexible feed-forward model that ingests a satellite tile with a UAV image, a ground image, or both, and outputs in a single forward pass a cross-view 3D point cloud, 6-DoF poses for all inputs, and the on-tile x,y and yaw for each perspective camera. Training/evaluation uses CrossGeo, a 278K-image tri-view dataset spanning 85 scenes. The model supports any combination of inputs and emphasizes intra- and inter-view geometry.

Result: Cross3R outperforms feed-forward 3D baselines for point-cloud reconstruction, 6-DoF pose estimation, and cross-view localization on CrossGeo. It also outperforms dedicated cross-view methods trained on KITTI despite no KITTI training, indicating strong generalization.

Conclusion: Using a UAV intermediate viewpoint resolves tilt/roll/altitude ambiguity in satellite-only cross-view localization, enabling accurate 3D reconstruction and 6-DoF pose estimation. The CrossGeo dataset supports evaluation of tri-view localization, and Cross3R demonstrates strong cross-dataset generalization.

Abstract: Cross-view localization classically asks: where does this ground image lie on the satellite tile? Existing methods are typically limited to 3-DoF estimates -- an $(x,y)$ position and a yaw angle -- because nadir satellite imagery provides no direct cues for roll, pitch, or altitude, forcing a reliance on planar-motion and zero-tilt assumptions. These assumptions break on real terrain with slopes, ramps, and tilted camera mounts. To overcome this, we introduce a single UAV image as an intermediate viewpoint: it reveals the 3D structure invisible from nadir, supplies the cues for roll, pitch, and altitude that the satellite alone cannot provide, and needs only spatial overlap with the ground camera -- no known relative pose is required. Building on this insight, we propose **Cross3R**, a flexible feed-forward model that ingests a satellite tile together with a UAV image, a ground image, or both, and, in a single forward pass, recovers a cross-view 3D point cloud, the 6-DoF poses of every input camera, and the on-tile $(x,y)$ position and yaw of each perspective camera. For training and evaluation, we also construct **CrossGeo**, a 278K-image tri-view dataset spanning 85 scenes across every continent except Antarctica. On CrossGeo, Cross3R consistently outperforms feed-forward 3D baselines in point-cloud reconstruction, 6-DoF camera-pose estimation, and cross-view localization. On KITTI, it outperforms dedicated cross-view methods trained on KITTI on most metrics, despite having no KITTI training itself.

</details>


### [137] [Rethinking Dense Optical Flow without Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08000)
*Praroop Chanda,Suryansh Kumar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Single-pass dense optical flow using foundation priors (frozen DINO-v2 + monocular depth cues) to avoid test-time refinement, achieving strong generalization and competitive accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on test-time iterative refinement in dense optical flow, which is computationally expensive, by leveraging pretrained foundation model priors.

Method: Fuse semantic features from a frozen DINO-v2 backbone with geometric cues from a monocular depth foundation model into a unified representation and apply a global matching formulation to estimate dense correspondences in a single forward pass without iterative updates.

Result: Achieves 2.81 EPE on Sintel Final without refinement, outperforming SEA-RAFT under similar training, RAFT, GMFlow (without refinement), and FlowSeek in the same setting, and demonstrates strong cross-dataset generalization.

Conclusion: Foundation priors can substitute for test-time scaling/refinement, offering a computationally efficient alternative to refinement-heavy dense optical flow pipelines.

Abstract: Recent progress in dense optical flow has been driven by increasingly complex architectures and multi-step refinement for test-time scaling. While these approaches achieve strong benchmark performance, they also require substantial computation during inference. This raises a fundamental question: Is scaling test-time computation the only way to improve dense optical flow accuracy? We argue that it is not. Instead, powerful visual semantic and geometric priors encoded in modern foundation models can reduce, if not overcome, the need for computationally expensive iterative refinement at test-time. In this paper, we present a framework that estimates dense optical flow in a single forward pass, leveraging pretrained foundation representations, while avoiding iterative refinement and additional inference-time computation, thus offering an alternative to test-time scaling. Our method extracts visual semantic features from a frozen DINO-v2 backbone and combines them with geometric cues from a monocular depth foundation model. We fuse these complementary priors into a unified representation and apply a global matching formulation to estimate dense correspondences without recurrent updates or test-time optimization. Despite avoiding iterative refinement, our approach achieves strong cross-dataset generalization across challenging benchmarks. On Sintel Final, we obtain 2.81 EPE without refinement, significantly improving over state-of-the-art (SOTA) SEA-RAFT under comparable training conditions and outperforming RAFT, GMFlow (without refinement), and recent FlowSeek in the same setting. These results suggest that strong foundation priors can substitute for test-time scaling, offering a computationally efficient alternative to refinement-heavy pipelines.

</details>


### [138] [SphereVAD: Training-Free Video Anomaly Detection via Geodesic Inference on the Unit Hypersphere](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08003)
*Chao Huang,Penfei Wei,Wei Wang,Jie Wen,Zhihua Wang,Li Shen,Wenqi Ren,Xiaochun Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SphereVAD is a training-free, zero-shot video anomaly detection framework that uses von Mises-Fisher (vMF) geodesic inference on the unit hypersphere to discriminate anomalies, leveraging intermediate features from pre-trained multimodal LLMs. It centers distributions with Frechet mean, uses Holistic Scene Attention with cross-video priors, and applies vMF-guided Spherical Geodesic Pulling, requiring only minimal synthetic calibration data. It achieves state-of-the-art results among training-free methods and competes with supervised methods on three benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Anomaly detection in untrimmed videos typically requires large-scale annotations and task-specific training, hindering deployment to new scenes. The authors observe that intermediate features from pre-trained multimodal LLMs already contain rich anomaly semantics, but existing methods underutilize the geometric discriminability of these features by focusing on language outputs. A training-free, geometry-based approach could unlock rapid deployment across scenes without heavy annotation.

Method: 1) Frechet mean centering to unfold feature distributions and reduce domain bias. 2) Holistic Scene Attention (HSA) to enforce feature consistency across videos using cross-video priors. 3) von Mises-Fisher (vMF) likelihood-ratio geodesic inference on the unit hypersphere, with Spherical Geodesic Pulling (SGP) to align ambiguous segments with directional prototypes on the sphere. 4) Minimal synthetic images for calibration. The pipeline is fully training-free, relying on geometric reasoning rather than learning new representations.

Result: SphereVAD reports new state-of-the-art results among training-free approaches on three major VAD benchmarks and remains competitive with fully supervised baselines.

Conclusion: A training-free VAD framework that leverages the geometric discriminability of intermediate mult i-modal model features on a hypersphere, using Frechet mean centering, Holistic Scene Attention, and vMF-guided SGP, can achieve strong anomaly detection performance without training, and shows competitive results against supervised methods.

Abstract: Video anomaly detection (VAD) aims to automatically identify events that deviate from normal patterns in untrimmed surveillance videos. Existing methods universally depend on large-scale annotations or task-specific training procedures, severely limiting their rapid deployment to novel scenes. We observe that intermediate-layer features of pre-trained multimodal large language models (MLLMs) already encode rich anomaly semantics, yet existing approaches rely on the language output pathway and fail to exploit the geometric discriminability latent in these representations. Based on this finding, we propose SphereVAD, a fully training-free, zero-shot VAD framework that recasts anomaly discrimination as von Mises-Fisher (vMF) likelihood-ratio geodesic inference on the unit hypersphere, unleashing latent discriminability through principled geometric reasoning rather than learning new representations. Specifically, SphereVAD first applies Frechet mean centering to unfold feature distributions and eliminate domain biases, then employs Holistic Scene Attention (HSA) to reinforce feature consistency using cross-video priors, and finally performs vMF-guided Spherical Geodesic Pulling (SGP) to align ambiguous segments with directional prototypes on the spherical manifold. This training-free pipeline requires only minimal synthetic images for calibration. SphereVAD establishes new state-of-the-art results among training-free approaches on three major benchmarks and remains competitive with fully supervised baselines. Code will be available upon acceptance.

</details>


### [139] [TRAS: An Interactive Software for Tracing Tree Ring Cross Sections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08025)
*Henry Marichal,Diego Passarella,Gregory Randall*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Open-source Tree Ring Analyzer Suite (TRAS) integrates classical detection (CS-TRD) with two deep-learning detectors (DeepCS-TRD, INBD) in a GUI for automatic delineation and manual correction of tree rings. Evaluated on 18 Pinus taeda cross-sections; DeepCS-TRD yielded best automatic detection (F1 0.81, precision 0.864); automation reduces manual boundary editing to ~20%; ring-width correlation with CooRecorder r>0.99; cross-platform; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dendrochronology and dendrometry rely on manual tree-ring delineation, which is time-consuming, subjective, and hard to scale to large image datasets. An open-source, integrated tool combining multiple detection strategies and an interactive correction interface can improve throughput, reproducibility, and accessibility.

Method: An open-source GUI (TRAS) that integrates three detection algorithms for ring delineation: CS-TRD (classical image processing), DeepCS-TRD (deep-learning), and INBD (another DL-based detector). The interface supports automatic detections, post-processing to remove false positives, and manual annotation to add missing rings. It computes dendrochronological metrics such as earlywood/latewood areas, ring perimeter, equivalent ring width, and path-based ring-width measurements. Evaluation on 18 expertly annotated Pinus taeda cross-sections shows DeepCS-TRD as the best automatic detector (F1=0.81, precision=0.864). Automatic detection reduces manual correction to ~20% of ring boundaries; correlation of one-dimensional ring-width measurements with CooRecorder is r>0.99.

Result: DeepCS-TRD achieved the highest automatic detection performance with F1=0.81 and precision=0.864. Automatic detection lowers manual edit workload to ~20% of ring boundaries. 1D ring-width measurements showed excellent agreement with CooRecorder (r>0.99). The tool supports multi-platform use (Windows/macOS/Linux) and provides open-source code at the given URL.

Conclusion: TRAS provides a flexible, reproducible, open-source solution for tree-ring analysis that combines multiple detection strategies within an interactive interface, enabling efficient processing of large image datasets. It offers robust one-dimensional ring-width validation and cross-platform availability, with potential for broader validation across species and imaging conditions.

Abstract: Tree ring marking remains a key step in dendrometry and dendrochronology, but it is often performed manually, making the process time-consuming, subjective, and difficult to scale to large image datasets.
  We present the Tree Ring Analyzer Suite (TRAS), an open-source graphical software for automatic delineation, manual correction, and measurement of tree rings in wood cross-sectional images. TRAS integrates three complementary detection algorithms: the classical image-processing method CS-TRD and two deep-learning approaches, DeepCS-TRD and INBD. The interface allows users to refine automatic detections, remove false positives, and manually add missing rings. It also computes dendrochronological metrics such as earlywood and latewood areas, ring perimeter, equivalent ring width, and custom path-based ring-width measurements.
  TRAS was evaluated on 18 expertly annotated Pinus taeda L. cross-section images. DeepCS-TRD achieved the best automatic detection performance, with an F-score of 81.0% and precision of 86.4%. Automatic detection reduced the required manual correction effort to approximately 20% of ring boundaries. For one-dimensional ring-width measurements, TRAS showed excellent agreement with CooRecorder ($r > 0.99$). Common detection errors, such as jump propagation or false positives near knots, were easily corrected through the postprocessing interface.
  TRAS provides a flexible and reproducible solution for tree-ring analysis on Windows, macOS, and Linux. Code is available at the https://hmarichal93.github.io/tras.

</details>


### [140] [STARFlow2: Bridging Language Models and Normalizing Flows for Unified Multimodal Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08029)
*Ying Shen,Tianrong Chen,Yuan Gao,Yizhe Zhang,Yuyang Wang,Miguel Ángel Bautista,Shuangfei Zhai,Joshua M. Susskind,Jiatao Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STARFlow2: a unified autoregressive-flow model that interleaves a pretrained VLM stream with a TarFlow stream using a shared causal mask, enabling cache-friendly, left-to-right generation of text and images within a single autoregressive framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal systems often couple autoregressive text models with diffusion-based image generators, which creates a structural mismatch between causal text generation and iterative visual denoising. A unified autoregressive approach promises alignment with LLMs, shared KV-cache semantics, and efficient interleaved generation for text and images.

Method: Builds on Pretzel architecture to vertically interleave a pretrained VLM stream with a TarFlow stream via residual skip connections, both operating under the same causal mask. Employs a deep-shallow flow design and a unified FAE latent space to enable cache-friendly interleaved generation where both text and visual outputs feed directly into the KV-cache without re-encoding.

Result: Experiments show strong performance on image generation and multimodal understanding benchmarks, indicating that autoregressive flows can serve as a viable foundation for unified multimodal modeling.

Conclusion: Autoregressive flows are a viable foundation for unified multimodal modeling, and STARFlow2 demonstrates effective, cache-friendly interleaved generation by leveraging shared causal structure and a unified latent space.

Abstract: Deep generative models have advanced rapidly across text and vision, motivating unified multimodal systems that can understand, reason over, and generate interleaved text-image sequences. Most existing approaches combine autoregressive language modeling with diffusion-based image generators, inheriting a structural mismatch between causal text generation and iterative visual denoising. We observe that autoregressive normalizing flows are autoregressive Transformers--sharing the same causal mask, KV-cache mechanism, and left-to-right structure as LLMs--making them the most natural paradigm for true unified multimodal generation. We present STARFlow2, built on the Pretzel architecture that vertically interleaves a pretrained VLM stream with a TarFlow stream via residual skip connections, both operating under the same causal mask. Combined with a deep-shallow flow design and a unified FAE latent space, STARFlow2 enables cache-friendly interleaved generation where both text and visual outputs directly enter the KV-cache without re-encoding. Experiments demonstrate strong performance across image generation and multimodal understanding benchmarks, validating autoregressive flows as a viable foundation for unified multimodal modeling.

</details>


### [141] [PET-Adapter: Test-Time Domain Adaptation for Full and Limited-Angle PET Image Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08030)
*Rüveyda Yilmaz,Yuli Wu,Johannes Stegmaier,Volkmar Schulz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PET-Adapter enables test-time domain adaptation for PET recon models trained on phantom data to clinical data, using layer-wise low-rank anatomical conditioning and OSEM-based warm-start, reducing diffusion steps from 50 to 2 while improving 3D reconstruction in full- and limited-angle settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DL-based PET reconstruction struggles to generalize from phantom to real clinical data and under limited-angle acquisitions due to distribution shifts and physics degradations.

Method: Introduce layer-wise low-rank anatomical conditioning during test-time adaptation and use Ordered Subset Expectation Maximization (OSEM)–based warm-start to initialize diffusion from physics-informed reconstructions. The method adapts generative PET reconstruction models at test time without paired ground-truth, evaluated across multiple clinical datasets in full-angle and limited-angle scenarios.

Result: Achieves superior 3D reconstruction performance on clinical datasets in both full- and limited-angle settings. Demonstrates clinical feasibility and significant computational efficiency by reducing diffusion steps from 50 to 2 without compromising quality.

Conclusion: PET-Adapter provides robust, data-efficient test-time domain adaptation for PET reconstruction, enabling phantom-trained models to perform well on diverse clinical data with reduced computational cost.

Abstract: Positron Emission Tomography (PET) image reconstruction is inherently challenged by Poisson noise and physical degradation factors, which are further exacerbated in limited-angle acquisitions. While deep learning methods demonstrate promising performance, their generalization to unseen clinical data distributions remains limited without extensive retraining. We propose PET-Adapter, a test-time domain adaptation framework for generative PET reconstruction models pretrained solely on phantom data. Our method enables adaptation to clinical datasets with varying anatomies, tracers, and scanner configurations without requiring paired ground truth. PET-Adapter introduces layer-wise low-rank anatomical conditioning during adaptation and Ordered Subset Expectation Maximization-based warm-starting that initializes the generation from physics-informed reconstructions, reducing diffusion steps from 50 to 2 without compromising quality. Experiments across multiple clinical datasets demonstrate superior 3D reconstruction performance in both full-angle and limited-angle settings, highlighting the clinical feasibility and computational efficiency of the proposed approach.

</details>


### [142] [Object Hallucination-Free Reinforcement Unlearning for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08031)
*Kaidi Jia,Yujie Lin,Chengyi Yang,Jiayao Ma,Jinsong Su*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HFRU is a two-stage reinforcement unlearning method that targets the vision encoder to erase sensitive knowledge in vision-language models, achieving high forgetting with minimal hallucinations and retaining performance; code available at GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-language models raise privacy, copyright, and bias concerns. Existing methods mainly fine-tune the language decoder, which yields superficial forgetting and persistent visual representations that can still cause object hallucination. A deep semantic removal in the visual encoder is needed to robustly erase sensitive knowledge.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) alignment disruption to weaken the link between visual representations and sensitive concepts; (2) GRPO-based optimization using a composite reward that includes an abstraction reward to encourage semantically valid substitutions and mitigate hallucinations.

Result: On object recognition and face identity tasks, HFRU achieves >98% forgetting and retention, with negligible object hallucination and strong performance gains over prior methods.

Conclusion: HFRU provides effective deep semantic unlearning by operating on the vision encoder, addressing privacy/copyright/bias concerns in VLMs with robust forgetting and low hallucination. Code and details are available at the provided GitHub URL.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) raise growing concerns about privacy, copyright, and bias, motivating machine unlearning to remove sensitive knowledge. However, existing methods primarily fine-tune the language decoder, leading to superficial forgetting that fails to erase underlying visual representations and often introduces object hallucination. We propose HFRU, a reinforcement unlearning framework that operates on the vision encoder for deep semantic removal. Our two-stage approach combines alignment disruption with GRPO-based optimization using a composite reward, including an abstraction reward that encourages semantically valid substitutions and mitigates hallucinations. Experiments on object recognition and face identity tasks show that HFRU achieves over 98% forgetting and retention performance, while introducing negligible object hallucination, significantly outperforming prior methods.Our code and implementation details are available at https://github.com/XMUDeepLIT/HFRU.

</details>


### [143] [SCOPE: Structured Decomposition and Conditional Skill Orchestration for Complex Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08043)
*Tianfei Ren,Zhipeng Yan,Yiming Zhao,Zhen Fang,Yu Zeng,Guohui Zhang,Hang Xu,Xiaoxiao Ma,Shiting Huang,Ke Xu,Wenxuan Huang,Lionel Z. Wang,Lin Chen,Zehui Chen,Jie Huang,Feng Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes SCOPE, a specification-guided framework that preserves semantic commitments across generation, with Gen-Arena/EGIP benchmarks showing improved alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the Conceptual Rift: discontinuities in tracking semantic commitments across grounding, generation, and verification in text-to-image systems.

Method: Maintain an evolving structured specification of commitments; conditionally invoke retrieval, reasoning, and repair skills around unresolved or violated commitments; evaluate with Gen-Arena (EGIP), plus WISE-V and MindBench.

Result: Significant gains: EGIP 0.60 on Gen-Arena; 0.907 on WISE-V; 0.61 on MindBench; outperforms baselines.

Conclusion: Persistent commitment tracking improves faithful, complex image generation and demonstrates the utility of a specification-guided control loop.

Abstract: While text-to-image models have made strong progress in visual fidelity, faithfully realizing complex visual intents remains challenging because many requirements must be tracked across grounding, generation, and verification. We refer to these requirements as semantic commitments and formalize their lifecycle discontinuity as the Conceptual Rift, where commitments may be locally resolved or checked but fail to remain identifiable as the same operational units throughout the generation lifecycle. To address this, we propose SCOPE, a specification-guided skill orchestration framework that maintains semantic commitments in an evolving structured specification and conditionally invokes retrieval, reasoning, and repair skills around unresolved or violated commitments. To evaluate commitment-level intent realization, we introduce Gen-Arena, a human-annotated benchmark with entity- and constraint-level specifications, together with Entity-Gated Intent Pass Rate (EGIP), a strict entity-first pass criterion. SCOPE substantially outperforms all evaluated baselines on Gen-Arena, achieving 0.60 EGIP, and further achieves strong results on WISE-V (0.907) and MindBench (0.61), demonstrating the effectiveness of persistent commitment tracking for complex image generation.

</details>


### [144] [MoCoTalk: Multi-Conditional Diffusion with Adaptive Router for Controllable Talking Head Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08050)
*Xinyan Ye,Jiankang Deng,Abbas Edalat*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MoCoTalk unifies four control signals (reference image, keypoints, shading mesh, and speech) in a diffusion framework using an Adaptive Multi-Condition Router to mitigate interference, supported by a Mouth-Augmented Shading Mesh and a lip-consistency loss for improved audio-visual alignment; it achieves state-of-the-art results and attribute-level controllability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current talking-head methods typically model only a subset of identity, pose, expression, mouth dynamics, or rely on fixed, heuristic fusion when combining conditions. There is a need for a unified, controllable framework that can fuse heterogeneous signals without destructive interference while preserving temporal consistency and audio-visual alignment.

Method: Introduce MoCoTalk, a multi-conditional video diffusion model with four control streams (reference image, facial keypoints, 3DMM-based shading meshes, and speech). An Adaptive Multi-Condition Router provides channel-wise, timestep-aware gating to fuse conditions adaptively by feature subspace and noise level. Develop Mouth-Augmented Shading Mesh (3DMM-based) to decouple head motion, mouth motion, expression, and lighting, offering a stable geometric prior. Add a lip consistency loss to tighten audio-visual alignment. Train and evaluate on standard benchmarks to demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and attribute-level controllability.

Result: Extensive experiments show MoCoTalk achieves state-of-the-art performance on most structural, motion, and perceptual metrics. It also offers attribute-level controllability that single-condition methods lack, enabling flexible recombination of attributes at inference.

Conclusion: A unified, controllable diffusion-based framework successfully integrates multiple complementary controls for talking-head video synthesis, resolving interference among heterogeneous conditions and improving lip-audio synchronization, with broad improvements across structural and perceptual metrics.

Abstract: Talking-head generation requires joint modeling of identity, head pose, facial expression, and mouth dynamics. Existing methods typically address only a subset of these factors, and rely on fixed-weight or heuristic fusion when multiple conditions are involved. We present MoCoTalk, a multi-conditional video diffusion framework that unifies four complementary control signals: a reference image, facial keypoints, 3DMM-rendered shading meshes, and the corresponding speech audio. To resolve destructive interference among heterogeneous conditions, we introduce an Adaptive Multi-Condition Router that computes channel-wise, timestep-aware gating over the four condition streams, allowing the fusion strategy to vary with both feature subspace and noise level. To better capture speech-related facial dynamics, we design a Mouth-Augmented Shading Mesh, a 3DMM-based representation that decouples head motion, mouth motion, expression, and lighting. This design provides a temporally consistent geometric prior and allows flexible recombination of these attributes at inference. We further introduce a lip consistency loss to tighten audio-visual alignment. Extensive experiments show that MoCoTalk achieves state-of-the-art performance on the majority of structural, motion, and perceptual metrics, while offering attribute-level controllability that single-condition methods do not provide.

</details>


### [145] [Towards Highly-Constrained Human Motion Generation with Retrieval-Guided Diffusion Noise Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08054)
*Hanchao Liu,Fang-Lue Zhang,Shining Zhang,Tai-Jiang Mu,Shi-Min Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A retrieval-guided, training-free diffusion-based method for constrained motion generation using relational task parsing and LLM-driven retrieval to satisfy hard spatiotemporal constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable reliable generation of human motion under very tight spatiotemporal constraints (obstacles, step counts) without requiring task-specific training.

Method: Search large motion datasets for guidance; use relational task parsing to identify difficult constraints; initialize diffusion noise via a reward-guided mask blending random and retrieved noise; optimize diffusion noise; employ LLMs to automate retrieval decisions.

Result: Demonstrates success on highly constrained generation tasks that are challenging for prior methods by leveraging retrieval-guided initialization and training-free diffusion optimization.

Conclusion: A training-free, retrieval-guided diffusion framework with relational parsing and LLM-driven retrieval improves controllable motion generation under severe constraints.

Abstract: Generating human motion that satisfies customized zero-shot goal functions, enabling applications such as controllable character animation and behavior synthesis for virtual agents, is a critical capability. While current approaches handle many unseen constraints, they fail on tasks with very challenging spatiotemporal restrictions, such as severe spatial obstacles or specified numbers of walking steps. To equip motion generators for these highly constrained tasks, we present a retrieval-guided method built on the training-free diffusion noise optimization framework. The key idea is to search within large motion datasets for guidance that can potentially satisfy difficult constraints. We introduce relational task parsing to group target constraints and identify the difficult ones to be handled by retrieved reference. A better initialization for diffusion noise is then obtained via a reward-guided mask that combines random noise with retrieved noise. By optimizing diffusion noise from this improved initialization, we successfully solve highly constrained generation tasks. By leveraging LLM for relational task parsing, the whole framework is further enabled to automatically reason for what to retrieve, improving the intelligence of moving agents under a training-free optimization scheme.

</details>


### [146] [Flow-OPD: On-Policy Distillation for Flow Matching Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08063)
*Zhen Fang,Wenxuan Huang,Yu Zeng,Yiming Zhao,Shuang Chen,Kaituo Feng,Yunlong Lin,Lin Chen,Zehui Chen,Shaosheng Cao,Feng Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Flow-OPD unifies on-policy distillation with Flow Matching for text-to-image models to address reward sparsity and gradient interference. It uses a two-stage alignment with domain-specialized teachers, a Flow-based Cold-Start, and a three-step on-policy consolidation, plus Manifold Anchor Regularization. It improves GenEval and OCR substantially on Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium, showing emergent teacher-surpassing and scalability for generalist T2I models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Flow Matching (FM) models suffer from reward sparsity due to scalar-valued rewards and gradient interference from jointly optimizing heterogeneous objectives, causing a seesaw effect and reward hacking. A unified post-training alignment framework is needed to stabilize and generalize performance.

Method: Two-stage alignment: (1) train domain-specialized teachers via single-reward GRPO fine-tuning to maximize individual objectives; (2) Flow-based Cold-Start to establish a solid initial policy; (3) consolidate into a single student through on-policy sampling, task-routing labeling, and dense trajectory-level supervision. Introduce Manifold Anchor Regularization (MAR) using a task-agnostic teacher to provide full-data supervision that anchors generation to a high-quality manifold.

Result: Quantitative gains: GenEval rises 63→92; OCR accuracy rises 59→94, yielding ~10-point improvement over vanilla GRPO. Maintains image fidelity and human-preference alignment; exhibits emergent teacher-surpassing effects.

Conclusion: Flow-OPD offers a scalable, unified alignment paradigm for building generalist text-to-image models by integrating on-policy distillation with Flow Matching and MAR.

Abstract: Existing Flow Matching (FM) text-to-image models suffer from two critical bottlenecks under multi-task alignment: the reward sparsity induced by scalar-valued rewards, and the gradient interference arising from jointly optimizing heterogeneous objectives, which together give rise to a 'seesaw effect' of competing metrics and pervasive reward hacking. Inspired by the success of On-Policy Distillation (OPD) in the large language model community, we propose Flow-OPD, the first unified post-training framework that integrates on-policy distillation into Flow Matching models. Flow-OPD adopts a two-stage alignment strategy: it first cultivates domain-specialized teacher models via single-reward GRPO fine-tuning, allowing each expert to reach its performance ceiling in isolation; it then establishes a robust initial policy through a Flow-based Cold-Start scheme and seamlessly consolidates heterogeneous expertise into a single student via a three-step orchestration of on-policy sampling, task-routing labeling, and dense trajectory-level supervision. We further introduce Manifold Anchor Regularization (MAR), which leverages a task-agnostic teacher to provide full-data supervision that anchors generation to a high-quality manifold, effectively mitigating the aesthetic degradation commonly observed in purely RL-driven alignment. Built upon Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium, Flow-OPD raises the GenEval score from 63 to 92 and the OCR accuracy from 59 to 94, yielding an overall improvement of roughly 10 points over vanilla GRPO, while preserving image fidelity and human-preference alignment and exhibiting an emergent 'teacher-surpassing' effect. These results establish Flow-OPD as a scalable alignment paradigm for building generalist text-to-image models.

</details>


### [147] [Proxy3D: Efficient 3D Representations for Vision-Language Models via Semantic Clustering and Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08064)
*Jerry Jiang,Haowen Sun,Denis Gudovskiy,Yohei Nakata,Tomoyuki Okuno,Kurt Keutzer,Wenzhao Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proxy3D introduces compact 3D proxies for vision-language models by semantic-geometric encoding of video frames, clustering to form 3D proxies, and multi-stage training with a curated SpaceSpan dataset, achieving competitive to state-of-the-art results on 3D VQA, visual grounding, and spatial intelligence with short video sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLMs rely on 2D pixel-aligned representations or full 3D priors, leading to either spatial inconsistency in correspondence-based approaches or inefficiencies in representation-based methods, especially for short vision sequences.

Method: Extract semantic and geometric scene features from video frames using dedicated encoders; perform semantic-aware clustering to produce a set of 3D proxies; curate the SpaceSpan dataset for representation alignment; apply multi-stage training to align these 3D proxies with the vision-language model.

Result: Achieves competitive or state-of-the-art performance on 3D visual tasks including 3D visual question answering and visual grounding, particularly when using shorter video sequences.

Conclusion: 3D proxy representations can provide compact yet comprehensive spatial understanding for VLMs, improving efficiency and performance in 3D reasoning tasks; the approach demonstrates effectiveness with shorter input sequences and a dedicated 3D proxy dataset (SpaceSpan).

Abstract: Spatial intelligence in vision-language models (VLMs) attracts research interest with the practical demand to reason in the 3D world.Despite promising results, most existing methods follow the conventional 2D pipeline in VLMs and use pixel-aligned representations for the vision modality. However, correspondence-based models with implicit 3D scene understanding often fail to achieve spatial consistency, and representation-based models with 3D geometric priors lack efficiency in vision sequence serialization. To address this, we propose a Proxy3D method with compact yet comprehensive 3D proxy representations for the vision modality. Given only video frames as input, we employ semantic and geometric encoders to extract scene features and then perform their semantic-aware clustering to obtain a set of proxies in the 3D space. For representation alignment, we further curate the SpaceSpan dataset and apply multi-stage training to adopt the proposed 3D proxy representations with the VLM. When using shorter sequences for vision information, our method achieves competitive or state-of-the-art performance in 3D visual question answering, visual grounding and general spatial intelligence benchmarks.

</details>


### [148] [Normalizing Trajectory Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08078)
*Jiatao Gu,Tianrong Chen,Ying Shen,David Berthelot,Shuangfei Zhai,Josh Susskind*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NTM proposes modeling each reverse diffusion step as a conditional normalizing flow, enabling exact trajectory likelihood training and four-step sampling that matches or surpasses strong baselines while preserving probabilistic consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models typically require many small denoising steps; existing few-step methods rely on distillation, consistency training, or adversarial objectives but often abandon the likelihood framework. A likelihood-preserving, few-step alternative is desired for sample efficiency and principled probability estimates.

Method: Per-step conditional normalizing flows implemented via shallow invertible blocks, combined with a deep parallel predictor that captures the entire trajectory. End-to-end trainable from scratch or initialized from pretrained flow-matching models. Exact trajectory likelihood enables self-distillation: a lightweight denoiser trained on the model’s own score to produce high-quality four-step samples.

Result: On text-to-image benchmarks, NTM matches or outperforms strong baselines with only four sampling steps while uniquely retaining exact likelihood over the generative trajectory.

Conclusion: NTM provides a likelihood-preserving, data-efficient framework for few-step diffusion, compatible with pretrained flow models and enabling self-distillation, achieving competitive sample quality with reduced steps and preserved probabilistic rigor.

Abstract: Diffusion-based models decompose sampling into many small Gaussian denoising steps -- an assumption that breaks down when generation is compressed to a few coarse transitions. Existing few-step methods address this through distillation, consistency training, or adversarial objectives, but sacrifice the likelihood framework in the process. We introduce Normalizing Trajectory Models (NTM), which models each reverse step as an expressive conditional normalizing flow with exact likelihood training. Architecturally, NTM combines shallow invertible blocks within each step with a deep parallel predictor across the trajectory, forming an end-to-end network trainable from scratch or initializable from pretrained flow-matching models. Its exact trajectory likelihood further enables self-distillation: a lightweight denoiser trained on the model's own score produces high-quality samples in four steps. On text-to-image benchmarks, NTM matches or outperforms strong image generation baselines in just four sampling steps while uniquely retaining exact likelihood over the generative trajectory.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [149] [RateQuant: Optimal Mixed-Precision KV Cache Quantization via Rate-Distortion Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06675)
*Fei Zuo,Zikang Zhou,Hao Cong,Xiaoyan Xi,Ho Fai Leung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RateQuant resolves distortion-model mismatch in mixed-precision KV cache quantization for LLMs by per-quantizer distortion modeling and rate-distortion optimization; calibrates models on a small set, then uses reverse waterfilling to allocate bits. Achieves large perplexity reductions with minimal calibration and zero inference overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV cache memory is a bottleneck; fixed bit-width per head is suboptimal due to varying head importance. Mixed-precision naive allocation worsens due to distortion-model mismatch across quantizers.

Method: Calibrate per-quantizer distortion model from small calibration set; solve bit allocation with reverse waterfilling per rate-distortion theory; implement at quantization time with negligible inference overhead.

Result: On Qwen3-8B at ~2.5 average bits, RateQuant reduces KIVI perplexity from 49.3 to 14.9; QuaRot improves by 6.6 PPL; calibration 1.6 s on one GPU; zero overhead at inference.

Conclusion: RateQuant successfully mitigates distortion-model mismatch and enables effective per-head mixed-precision quantization with practical calibration cost and no inference-time overhead.

Abstract: Large language models cache all previously computed key-value (KV) pairs during generation, and this KV cache grows linearly with sequence length, making it a primary memory bottleneck for serving. Quantizing the KV cache to fewer bits reduces this cost, yet all current quantizers assign the same bit-width to every attention head, ignoring the large variation in head importance. A natural idea is to allocate more bits to important heads and fewer to the rest. We show, however, that such mixed-precision allocation has a hidden pitfall: each quantizer follows a different distortion curve D(b)=alpha*beta^{-b}, and the decay rate beta varies from 3.6 to 5.3 across quantizer designs. Applying one quantizer's distortion model to another inverts the allocation order and makes performance worse than uniform quantization. We call this failure mode distortion model mismatch and propose RateQuant to resolve it. RateQuant fits a per-quantizer distortion model from a small calibration set, then solves the resulting bit-allocation problem in closed form via reverse waterfilling from rate-distortion theory. On Qwen3-8B at 2.5 average bits, calibrated RateQuant reduces KIVI's perplexity from 49.3 to 14.9 (70% reduction) and improves QuaRot by 6.6 PPL. The entire calibration takes 1.6 s on a single GPU and adds zero overhead at inference time.

</details>


### [150] [LKV: End-to-End Learning of Head-wise Budgets and Token Selection for LLM KV Cache Eviction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06676)
*Enshuai Zhou,Yifan Hao,Chao Wang,Rui Zhang,Di Huang,Jiaming Guo,Xing Hu,Zidong Du,Qi Guo,Yunji Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Learned KV Eviction (LKV) reframes KV cache compression as differentiable optimization, learning budgets and KV importance to maximize task fidelity. It achieves state-of-the-art results at high compression, e.g., ~15% KV retention with near-lossless performance on LongBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context inference in LLMs is bottlenecked by linear KV cache growth. Heuristic KV cache compression misallocates resources because it relies on priors or static attentional biases rather than aligning with the downstream task objective.

Method: Formulate KV compression as an end-to-end differentiable optimization. LKV-H learns task-optimized global budgets for KV usage; LKV-T derives intrinsic KV importance without materializing attention matrices, avoiding heuristic proxies and per-query dependencies.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on LongBench and RULER benchmarks at high compression rates; on LongBench, near-lossless performance with only 15% KV cache retention. Analysis indicates learned budgeting is the dominant driver of fidelity.

Conclusion: Data-driven budgeting and task-aligned KV importance are essential to surpass hand-crafted heuristics. The approach bypasses traditional attention-based proxies, directly optimizing for task objectives.

Abstract: Long-context inference in Large Language Models (LLMs) is bottlenecked by the linear growth of Key-Value (KV) cache memory. Existing KV cache compression paradigms are fundamentally limited by heuristics: heuristic budgeting relies on statistical priors rather than task objectives, causing resource misallocation, while heuristic selection relies on coupled query-key interactions or static inductive biases (e.g., attention sinks). To address this limitation, we introduce LKV (Learned KV Eviction), which formulates KV compression as an end-to-end differentiable optimization problem. LKV integrates LKV-H to learn task-optimized global budgets, and LKV-T to derive intrinsic KV importance without materializing attention matrices. This design bypasses heuristic proxies, strictly aligning compression with task objectives. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that LKV achieves state-of-the-art performance on both LongBench and RULER benchmarks at high compression rates. In particular, on LongBench, LKV achieves near-lossless performance with only 15\% KV cache retention. Crucially, our analysis identifies learned budgeting as the dominant driver of fidelity, demonstrating that data-driven allocation is essential to overcome the limitations of hand-crafted heuristics.

</details>


### [151] [A Wasserstein GAN-based climate scenario generator for risk management and insurance: the case of soil subsidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06678)
*Antoine Heranval,Olivier Lopez,Didier Ngatcha,Daniel Nkameni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SwiGAN uses a Conditional GAN to generate future spatio-temporal SWI drought trajectories for France up to 2050, enabling scenario-based drought analysis to inform adaptive insurance and risk management; the approach is generalizable to other climate hazards and actuarial tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rising costs from natural disasters and the insufficiency of short-horizon prudential regulation (e.g., Solvency II) create a need for long-horizon, data-driven drought risk modeling to support insurance strategy and risk management.

Method: A Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (Conditional GAN) framework that produces plausible spatio-temporal sequences of Soil Wetness Index (SWI) maps for a drought-prone region in France, projected up to 2050, enabling exploration of drought propagation under climate-change scenarios.

Result: The paper proposes SwiGAN and demonstrates its ability to generate realistic SWI sequences, offering insights into drought dynamics under climate change and supporting adaptive risk management and insurance design; the method is adaptable to other climate-related perils and actuarial applications such as economic scenario generation.

Conclusion: SwiGAN presents a generalizable AI framework for long-horizon drought risk modeling with potential applications in insurance and risk management; future work likely includes validation, region expansion, multi-hazard integration, and robust scenario evaluation.

Abstract: According to the United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (2025), the average annual cost of natural catastrophes increased from 70--80 billion USD between 1970 and 2000 to 180--200 billion USD between 2001 and 2020. Reports from organizations such as the IFOA and the WWF highlight the need for the insurance sector to adapt to this rapidly evolving context by developing medium- to long-term strategies that go beyond the one-year horizon of prudential regulations such as Solvency II. This paper introduces an artificial intelligence framework based on Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks (Conditional GANs) to generate future spatio-temporal trajectories of climatic indices. The approach focuses on the Soil Wetness Index (SWI), a key indicator used in France to assess drought severity. Drought accounts for approximately 30% of the indemnities paid under the French natural catastrophe insurance scheme. The proposed model, SwiGAN, simulates plausible drought propagation patterns up to 2050 for a region of France particularly exposed to this hazard. By generating realistic sequences of SWI maps, SwiGAN provides insights into drought dynamics under climate change scenarios and supports the design of adaptive risk management and insurance strategies. The methodology is also generalizable to other climate-related perils and actuarial applications such as economic scenario generation.

</details>


### [152] [Breaking the Illusion: When Positive Meets Negative in Multimodal Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06679)
