*Yubo Jiang,Yitong An,Xin Yang,Abudukelimu Wuerkaixi,Xuxin Cheng,Fengying Xie,Zhiguo Jiang,Cao Liu,Ke Zeng,Haopeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PND is a decoding-time, training-free framework that reduces object hallucination in Vision-Language Models by using dual decoding paths—positive (enhancing visual evidence) and negative (counterfactuals) to ground outputs to the image, achieving state-of-the-art without retraining on POPE, MME, CHAIR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs often rely on linguistic priors, causing object hallucination that contradicts visual reality. This is linked to an attention imbalance where visual features are under-weighted, motivating a decoding-time intervention to enforce visual fidelity without retraining.

Method: A training-free inference framework with a dual-path decoding strategy: a positive path that amplifies visual evidence and a negative path that constructs counterfactuals to penalize prior-dominant generation. Outputs from both paths are contrasted during decoding to steer toward visually grounded results.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on POPE, MME, and CHAIR datasets without retraining of the model.

Conclusion: PND provides a practical, training-free approach to mitigate object hallucination in VLMs by rebalancing attention during decoding via dual-path contrast, enabling more visually grounded outputs across benchmarks.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are frequently undermined by object hallucination, generating content that contradicts visual reality, due to an over-reliance on linguistic priors. We introduce Positive-and-Negative Decoding (PND), a training-free inference framework that intervenes directly in the decoding process to enforce visual fidelity. PND is motivated by our finding of an attention imbalance in VLMs, where visual features are under-weighted. Our framework introduces a dual-path contrast: a positive path that amplifies visual evidence and a negative path that constructs counterfactuals to penalize prior-dominant generation. By contrasting outputs from both paths during decoding, PND steers generation toward visually grounded results. Experiments on POPE, MME, and CHAIR demonstrate state-of-the-art performance without retraining.

</details>


### [153] [On the Role of Strain and Vorticity in Numerical Integration Error for Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06680)
*Chenxi Tao,Seung-Kyum Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decomposing velocity Jacobian into strain and vorticity reveals distinct impacts on integration error in flow-based models, with strain driving exponential error growth via the logarithmic norm and vorticity contributing only linearly to local truncation error. The optimal transport velocity field is irrotational with zero material derivative, enabling second-order Euler accuracy; regularization weights on strain and vorticity improve performance. Experiments validate theory with 2D data and preliminary CIFAR-10 gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how intrinsic properties of the learned velocity field (strain vs. vorticity) govern numerical integration error in flow-based generative models and to guide regularization and design choices.

Method: The analysis decomposes the velocity field Jacobian into its symmetric part S (strain rate) and antisymmetric part Omega (vorticity). It derives how S affects error via the logarithmic norm, and shows Omega contributes linearly to local truncation error. It argues that the optimal transport velocity field is irrotational and has zero material derivative, implying second-order Euler accuracy, and that exact displacement interpolation yields Lagrangian particles integrated exactly by Euler. It introduces weighted Jacobian regularization with strain weight alpha and vorticity weight beta, and validates predictions with 2D experiments and preliminary CIFAR-10 results.

Result: Strain dominates exponential error amplification through the logarithmic norm; vorticity affects error only linearly at the local truncation level. The optimal transport velocity field is irrotational with zero material derivative, yielding second-order Euler accuracy. Weighted regularization (alpha for strain, beta for vorticity) improves integration accuracy and downstream metrics (up to 2.7x lower error at NFE=5; CIFAR-10 trends show 14% FID improvement at NFE=10).

Conclusion: The research clarifies how to tailor velocity field properties to control integration error in flow-based models. Prioritizing strain reduction (while controlling vorticity) and enforcing irrotational, zero-derivative transport can enhance accuracy and efficiency; regularization strategies guided by S and Omega weights can yield practical gains in synthetic and real-data tasks.

Abstract: Flow matching generates data by integrating a learned velocity field, where the number of integration steps (NFE) directly determines inference cost. We analyze which properties of the velocity field govern integration error by decomposing the velocity Jacobian into its symmetric part S (strain rate) and antisymmetric part Omega (vorticity). We prove that strain and vorticity play different roles: strain controls exponential error amplification through the logarithmic norm, while vorticity contributes only linearly to the local truncation error. We further show that the optimal transport velocity field is irrotational and has zero material derivative, implying second-order Euler accuracy; for exact displacement interpolation, the associated Lagrangian particle dynamics are integrated exactly by Euler. Motivated by this analysis, we study weighted Jacobian regularization with strain weight alpha and vorticity weight beta. Experiments on 2D synthetic data confirm the main theoretical predictions, showing up to 2.7x lower integration error at NFE=5. Preliminary CIFAR-10 experiments show consistent trends, with a lightweight fine-tuning procedure improving FID by 14 percent at NFE=10 while preserving high-NFE quality.

</details>


### [154] [A Hierarchical Ensemble Pipeline for Anomaly Detection in ESA Satellite Telemetry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06681)
*Lorenzo Riccardo Allegrini,Geremia Pompei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hierarchical ensemble pipeline for anomaly detection in ESA telemetry combining shapelet-based and statistical features with per-channel modeling, intra-channel stacking, and cross-channel aggregation, validated with time-series cross-validation and masking to prevent information leakage, achieving strong generalization on the ESA-ADB benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detect subtle anomalies in multivariate satellite telemetry and prevent information leakage in evaluation; leverage hierarchical modeling to exploit per-channel and cross-channel structure for robust generalization.

Method: Train a pipeline that (1) extracts shapelet-based and statistical features per channel, (2) builds per-channel models, (3) performs intra-channel stacking of predictions, (4) aggregates across channels via a final cross-channel model, (5) uses time-series cross-validation with two-level masking to avoid leakage.

Result: On the ESA-ADB benchmark, the approach shows strong generalization and effective detection of subtle anomalies in realistic satellite telemetry.

Conclusion: A hierarchical ensemble approach that combines diverse feature types and channel-wise modeling with cross-channel aggregation yields robust anomaly detection in multivariate satellite telemetry, validating the utility of hierarchical architectures for this domain; future work could explore ablations, scalability, and real-time deployment.

Abstract: A hierarchical ensemble pipeline is introduced to address anomaly detection in multivariate telemetry data provided by European Space Agency (ESA). The method integrates shapelet-based and statistical feature extraction, per-channel modeling, intra-channel stacking, and a final cross-channel aggregation. The pipeline is trained and validated using time-series cross-validation and two-level masking strategies to prevent information leakage. Results on the European Space Agency Anomaly Detection Benchmark (ESA-ADB) challenge demonstrate strong generalization, highlighting the effectiveness of hierarchical modeling in detecting subtle anomalies in realistic satellite telemetry.

</details>


### [155] [Toeplitz MLP Mixers are Low Complexity, Information-Rich Sequence Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06683)
*Benjamin L. Badger,Ethan Roland*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces the Toeplitz MLP Mixer (TMM), a transformer-like model that replaces attention with triangular-masked Toeplitz matrix multiplication, achieving O(d n log n) training time and O(d n) memory, with O(d n) inference prefill. Claims improved information retention, copying, information retrieval, and in-context learning, and provides an operator-index-theory analysis suggesting trained Toeplitz layers in causal non-invertible models are often invertible or nearly invertible.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the quadratic time/space complexity of self-attention while preserving information retention and empirical performance. Explore a structured, less biased alternative to attention that can still support in-context learning.

Method: Replace attention with triangular-masked Toeplitz matrix multiplication along the sequence dimension, enabling a transform-like mixing operation with favorable asymptotics. Training uses Toeplitz-based linear operators with O(d n log n) time and O(d n) space; inference prefill is O(d n) in time and space. The approach is evaluated against comparable architectures on information retention and in-context learning benchmarks and analyzed via operator index theory.

Result: TMM achieves greater training efficiency (loss per compute and device memory) and better information retention, translating to improved copying, information retrieval, and in-context learning benchmarks relative to comparable architectures. The operator-index analysis indicates trained Toeplitz layers in causal non-invertible models are more likely to be invertible or nearly invertible than models that are invertible by construction.

Conclusion: Architectural biases in attention-intensive models may limit information retention; structured Toeplitz operations provide a competitive, more information-retentive alternative with favorable compute/space complexity. Theoretical analysis on operator index theory adds a complementary perspective on the invertibility properties of these layers.

Abstract: Transformer-based large language models are in some respects limited by the quadratic time and space computational complexity of attention. We introduce the Toeplitz MLP Mixer (TMM), a transformer-like architecture that swaps attention for triangular-masked Toeplitz matrix multiplication over the sequence dimension resulting in $\mathcal{O} (dn \log n)$ time and $\mathcal O(dn)$ space complexity during training and $\mathcal O(dn)$ time and space at inference prefill. Despite the lack of sophisticated input modulation or state maintenance present in other sub-quadratic architectures, TMMs yield greater training efficiency in terms of loss achieved per compute and device memory. We demonstrate that TMMs are capable of retaining more input information resulting in improved copying ability, which we argue results from a lack of architectural biases. Consistent with higher input information retention, TMMs exhibit superior information retrieval and in-context learning benchmark accuracy compared to comparable architectures. We conclude with an analysis from the perspective of operator index theory and show that, counterintuitively, trained Toeplitz layers of causal non-invertible models are more likely to be invertible or nearly so than models that are actually invertible over their inputs.

</details>


### [156] [From Canopy to Collision: A Hybrid Predictive Framework for Identifying Risk Factors in Tree-Involved Traffic Crashes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06684)
*Abdul Azim,Ahmed Hossain,Soumyadip Maitra,Panick Kalambay*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-method framework using CRSS 2020–2023 data identifies key risk factors for injury severity in tree-involved run-off-road crashes, with restraint non-use, vehicle age, speeding, and driver impairment as top drivers and notable interactions among lighting, vehicle age, speeding, and road surface.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand factors driving crash severity in tree-involved ROR crashes and provide evidence-based targets for interventions to reduce fatalities and severe injuries.

Method: Step 1: CatBoost binary classification to identify factors predicting fatal/incapitating vs non-incapacitating/possible injury. Step 2: SHAP to quantify marginal effects of top factors. Step 3: Binary logistic regression to estimate effects and validate SHAP importance. Step 4: SHAP interaction plots to explore combined factor effects.

Result: Restraint non-use is the strongest predictor; unrestrained occupants nearly triple risk of severe outcomes due to ejection. Vehicle age, speeding, and driver impairment also exert substantial effects. Key interactions include lighting vs vehicle age, speeding vs lighting, restraint use vs vehicle age, and road surface vs speeding, indicating additive risk effects under reduced visibility and other conditions.

Conclusion: Findings support targeted safety interventions—enhanced seat belt enforcement, speed management in low-visibility conditions, and fleet modernization—and demonstrate a robust analytics framework for disentangling factors and interactions in crash severity.

Abstract: Tree-involved crashes represent a critical subset of run-off-road (ROR) collisions, often resulting in fatal or severe injuries due to high-energy impacts. This study develops a comprehensive analytical framework to identify and quantify risk factors contributing to crash severity in tree-involved collisions using the Crash Report Sampling System (CRSS) database spanning 2020-2023. The modeling framework follows a multi-step process. First, a machine learning based classification model (CatBoost) identifies key factors associated with binary crash injury severity (KA: fatal or incapacitating injury versus BC: non-incapacitating or possible injury). Second, SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) tool is used to quantify and visualize the marginal effects of top influential factors on crash severity. Third, a binary logistic regression model estimates factor effects and validates SHAP-derived importance measures. Finally, SHAP interaction plots examine the combined effects of key contributing factors. Results reveal restraint non-use as the most influential predictor, with unrestrained occupants nearly three times more likely to experience severe outcomes due to ejection risk. Vehicle age, speeding violations, and driver impairment demonstrate substantial effects, reflecting reduced crashworthiness, increased impact forces, and reduced control capabilities. Critical interactions emerge between lighting conditions and vehicle age, speeding and lighting conditions, restraint use and vehicle age, and road surface and speeding, demonstrating additive risk effects with specific interactions. These findings provide critical insights for targeted safe system-based interventions, including enhanced seat belt enforcement, speed management in reduced visibility conditions, and vehicle fleet modernization.

</details>


### [157] [Robustness of Refugee-Matching Gains to Off-Policy Evaluation Choices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06686)
*Kirk Bansak,Elisabeth Paulson,Dominik Rothenhäusler,Jeremy Ferwerda,Jens Hainmueller,Michael Hotard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Counterfactual impact estimates for refugee matching are robust across multiple off-policy evaluation methods (IPW, AIPW variants) and modeling/assignment variations, with results aligning with the original findings of Bansak et al. (2018).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess the stability and robustness of counterfactual impact evaluation results for refugee matching in the United States and to validate consistency with prior work.

Method: Apply several off-policy evaluation methods (inverse probability weighting and multiple augmented inverse probability weighting variants) and test various modeling architectures and assignment procedures to estimate counterfactual impact.

Result: Impact estimates maintain consistent magnitude across scenarios and are statistically significant in most cases; findings are consistent with Bansak et al. (2018).

Conclusion: The study provides evidence for the robustness and credibility of refugee matching effects, supporting the reliability of prior results under diverse evaluation methods and modeling choices.

Abstract: Previous research has investigated the potential of refugee matching for boosting refugee outcomes, first considered by Bansak et al. (2018). This paper demonstrates the stability of counterfactual impact evaluation results in the context of refugee matching in the United States using a range of off-policy evaluation methods. In order to estimate counterfactual impact and test the robustness of our results, we employ several evaluation methods, including inverse probability weighting (IPW) and multiple variants of augmented inverse probability weighting (AIPW). We also consider various modifications, including alternative modeling architectures and different assignment procedures. The impact estimates remain consistent in magnitude in all scenarios as well as statistically significant in most cases. Furthermore, the estimates are also consistent with the results originally presented in Bansak et al. (2018).

</details>


### [158] [Conditional generation of antibody sequences with classifier-guided germline-absorbing discrete diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06720)
*Justin Sanders,Luca Giancardo,Lan Guo,Yue Zhao,Kemal Sonmez,Nina Cheng,Melih Yilmaz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Discrete diffusion fine-tuning and germline absorbing diffusion improve antibody sequence design with protein language models, enabling flexible classifier-guided conditional generation and reducing germline bias; results include substantial gains in non-germline residue prediction (26%→46%) and improved class-adherence vs. sample quality for hydrophobicity and binding affinity tasks, outperforming EvoProtGrad.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome two key limitations in antibody sequence design with protein language models: (1) reliance on memorized germline sequences rather than biologically meaningful somatic variation; (2) limited support for flexible, classifier-guided conditional generation.

Method: Introduce discrete diffusion fine-tuning to enable conditioned generation using arbitrary classifiers, and germline absorbing diffusion where the germline sequence is the absorbing state in the diffusion process; demonstrate improvements on non-germline residue prediction and conditional generation tasks (hydrophobicity and binding affinity).

Result: Non-germline residue prediction accuracy increases from 26% to 46%. Conditional generation shows better tradeoffs between classifier adherence and sample quality, outperforming EvoProtGrad on sampled antibody design tasks.

Conclusion: Germline diffusion reduces germline bias by constraining diffusion trajectories to germline→observed-sequence paths; combined with discrete diffusion fine-tuning, this approach improves both the quality of generated antibody sequences and their controllability via off-the-shelf classifiers, advancing state-of-the-art in antibody design with pLMs.

Abstract: Antibody therapeutics are among the most successful modern medicines, yet computationally designing antibodies with desirable binding and developability properties remains challenging. While protein language models (pLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for antibody sequence design, existing approaches largely suffer from two key limitations: they predominantly memorize germline sequences rather than modeling biologically meaningful somatic variation, and they offer limited support for flexible classifier-guided conditional generation. We address these challenges through two primary contributions. First, we demonstrate that discrete diffusion fine-tuning achieves strong language modeling performance on antibody sequences while allowing for generation conditioned on any off-the-shelf classifier. Second, we introduce germline absorbing diffusion, a novel modification of the discrete diffusion noise process in which the germline sequence - rather than a masked sequence - serves as the absorbing state. This biologically motivated inductive bias restricts the model to learning the trajectory from germline to observed sequence, effectively excluding genetic variation and V(D)J recombination statistics from the learned distribution and dramatically mitigating germline bias. We show that germline diffusion improves non-germline residue prediction accuracy from 26 percent to 46 percent, approaching the theoretical upper bound set by true biological variability. We then demonstrate the utility of our germline diffusion model on the conditional generation tasks of sampling antibodies with improved hydrophobicity and predicted binding affinity. On both tasks our model shows an improved tradeoff between class adherence and sample quality, significantly outperforming EvoProtGrad, a popular strategy to sample from pLMs with gradient-based discrete Markov Chain Monte Carlo.

</details>


### [159] [Enabling Unsupervised Training of Deep EEG Denoisers With Intelligent Partitioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06724)
*Qiyu Rao,Haozhe Tian,Homayoun Hamedmoghadam,Danilo Mandic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-supervised EEG denoising via iPSD that partitions a single noisy EEG segment into independent realizations to enable zero-shot denoising without clean references; achieves state-of-the-art performance, especially at very low SNR and with EMG artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wearable EEG is contaminated by time-varying, spectrally overlapping noise; traditional fixed-rule methods fail; acquiring clean EEG is impractical; needs self-supervised approach.

Method: Intelligent Partitioning for Self-supervised Denoising (iPSD): partition input into independent noisy realizations preserving the underlying signal; train denoisers without clean references; feasible in zero-shot with a single segment; validated on in-ear wearables.

Result: State-of-the-art performance, robust at SNR as low as -10 dB, handling challenging artifacts like EMG; spectral fidelity substantially higher than baselines.

Conclusion: iPSD enables practical, self-supervised EEG denoising for wearables, eliminating the need for clean references and enabling robust performance in adverse noise conditions.

Abstract: Denoising wearable electroencephalogram (EEG) is inherently challenging since neural activity is not only subtle but also inseparable from spectrally overlapping noise artifacts. Classical signal processing methods, relying on fixed or heuristic rules, cannot handle the time-varying pervasive artifacts in wearable EEGs. Deep learning methods, on the other hand, show promise in decomposition-free EEG denoising using highly expressive neural networks, but the training requires artifact-free EEG, which is inherently unobtainable. To address this, we propose Intelligent Partitioning for Self-supervised Denoising (iPSD). Our method eliminates the need for clean references by learning to partition an input EEG segment into independent noisy realizations with the same underlying signal. This enables self-supervision of deep learning denoisers, even in zero-shot settings where only a single EEG segment to be denoised is available. We validate iPSD through extensive experiments, including validations on wearable EEG from in-ear sensors. The results show that iPSD achieves state-of-the-art performance, most notably under extremely low signal-to-noise ratios (down to -10 dB) and challenging artifacts (e.g., EMG), with spectral fidelity orders of magnitude higher than competitive baselines.

</details>


### [160] [Transformer-Based Wildlife Species Classification from Daily Movement Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06726)
*Obed Irakoze,Prasenjit Mitra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformers outperform other sequence models for inferring wildlife species from GPS trajectories; 1-hour resolution with feature augmentation improves performance, especially for underrepresented species; finer 30-minute sampling offers limited additional gains due to data gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle species identification from large-scale wildlife movement data; compare model architectures, feature representations, and temporal resolution under data-limited conditions.

Method: Train Transformer, LSTM, CNN, and Temporal Convolutional Networks on 7-species Movebank GPS trajectories; hold out entire telemetry studies or regions; evaluate with balanced accuracy and AUC; analyze basic displacement vs augmented features (speed, direction, turning); compare 1-hour vs 30-minute resolutions.

Result: Transformers achieve higher balanced accuracy than baselines by ~8–22 percentage points depending on species/setting. In an elephant binary task (1-hour data), Transformer yields balanced accuracy 0.83 and AUC 0.92. Feature augmentation improves performance, especially for underrepresented species such as large carnivores, lions, and zebras. A unified 1-hour resolution provides robust performance across studies by reducing missing data; 30-minute resolution can capture short-term patterns for some species but offers no consistent overall gains.

Conclusion: Transformer-based sequence modeling is robust for species identification from movement data. Feature-rich representations help underrepresented species, and a 1-hour temporal resolution enhances cross-study generalization by mitigating missing data, though finer sampling may benefit certain species.

Abstract: Inferring the identity of wildlife species from daily movement data alone is a challenging task. We train sequence models on large-scale, 7-species GPS trajectories from the Movebank platform. Trajectories models are evaluated using a protocol in which entire telemetry studies or regions are heldout during testing. We compare Transformer-based sequence models to LSTM, CNN, and Temporal Convolutional Networks, and find that Transformers consistently achieve higher balanced accuracy with gains of approximately 8 to 22 percentage points, depending on the species and experimental setting. In an elephant binary classification task with 1-hour resolution, the Transformer achieves a balanced accuracy of 0.83 and an AUC of 0.92, substantially outperforming all baseline models. We examine, under data-limited conditions, feature representations by analyzing the differences between a basic displacement-based encoding and an expanded range of movement descriptors that include speed, direction, and turning behavior. With feature augmentation, we see clear performance gains, especially for underrepresented and sparsely represented species, such as large carnivores, lions, and Zebras. Finally, experiments comparing 1-hour and 30-minutetemporal resolutions show that while finer sampling can capture short-term movement patterns for some species, a unified 1-hour resolution yields more promising performance across studies by reducing missing data and ensuring consistent temporal coverage.

</details>


### [161] [Medical Imaging Classification with Cold-Atom Reservoir Computing using Auto-Encoders and Surrogate-Driven Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06727)
*Nuno Batista,Ana Morgado,Oscar Ferraz,Sagar Silva Pratapsi,Jorge Lobo,Gabriel Falcao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A guided, differentiable quantum-classical pipeline using neutral-atom reservoir computing and a guided auto-encoder for polyp-detection in medical images; uses a differentiable surrogate for the quantum layer to enable end-to-end training; latent space encoded as detunings in a Rydberg Hamiltonian; simulations show improvements over PCA/unguided autoencoders; robust for NISQ-era deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle high dimensionality in medical image classification while addressing the non-differentiable gradient barrier of quantum measurements; propose a trainable quantum-classical system that yields compact, discriminative representations suited for quantum embedding and end-to-end optimization in practical, near-term quantum hardware.

Method: A hybrid pipeline: (1) a guided auto-encoder learns compact, discriminative representations; (2) neutral-atom reservoir computing provides quantum embeddings; (3) a differentiable surrogate model emulates the quantum layer to enable backpropagation; (4) joint optimization for classification accuracy and image reconstruction; (5) latent codes encoded as pulse detuning parameters within a Rydberg Hamiltonian; (6) quantum embeddings obtained from expectation values and fed into a linear classifier; (7) ablation studies to assess the impact of quantum parameters and training settings.

Result: In simulations, the approach outperforms traditional baselines such as PCA-based methods or unguided autoencoders; ablation confirms robustness and flexibility of the pipeline, indicating viability for real-world medical imaging tasks in the current NISQ era.

Conclusion: The proposed guided, end-to-end trainable quantum-classical pipeline demonstrates a feasible and effective route for quantum-assisted medical image classification, combining state-of-the-art representation learning with quantum embeddings and addressing the gradient barrier through a differentiable surrogate, with demonstrated robustness and potential real-world applicability.

Abstract: We introduce a hybrid quantum-classical pipeline, based on neutral-atom reservoir computing, for medical image classification, focusing on the binary classification task of polyp detection. To deal effectively with the high dimensionality, we integrate a guided auto-encoder. This pipeline learns compact and discriminative representations of image data that are also well-suited for quantum reservoir computing. A key challenge in such systems is the non-differentiable nature of quantum measurements, which creates a 'gradient barrier' for standard training. We overcome this barrier by incorporating a differentiable surrogate model that emulates the quantum layer, enabling end-to-end backpropagation through the entire system. This guided training process is jointly optimized for classification accuracy and for faithful image recovery from the auto-encoder. The learned latent representations are encoded as pulse detuning parameters within a Rydberg Hamiltonian, and quantum embeddings are subsequently obtained through expectation values. These embeddings are then passed to a linear classifier. Our simulations show that this method outperforms some traditional approaches that use PCA or unguided autoencoders. We also conduct ablation studies to assess the impact of various quantum and training parameters, demonstrating the robustness and flexibility of our proposed pipeline for real-world medical imaging applications, even in the current NISQ era.

</details>


### [162] [The E$Δ$-MHC-Geo Transformer: Adaptive Geodesic Operations with Guaranteed Orthogonality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06729)
*Arash Shahmansoori*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces the EΔ-MHC-Geo Transformer, unifying manifold-constrained hyper-connections, deep delta learning, and Cayley transforms to produce input-adaptive, orthogonal residuals. A data-dependent Cayley rotation preserves orthogonality for all β and inputs. To handle negation, a hybrid with a learned gate combines Cayley rotation and Householder reflection, with a midpoint-collapse regularizer to push gate decisions toward crisp orthogonal components. Demonstrates strong stability, near-π rotation accuracy, and norm preservation with fewer layers, outperforming baselines including JPmHC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve input-adaptive, exactly orthogonal residual connections that work for all inputs and β, overcoming the limitations of Deep Delta Learning (orthogonality only at β in {0,2}) and addressing negation and reflection in orthogonal transformations.

Method: Define a data-dependent Cayley rotation Q(x)=(I+(β/2)A(x))^{-1}(I-(β/2)A(x)) and integrate it into an EΔ-MHC-Geo Hybrid: X' = γ(X)Q(X)X + (1-γ(X))H2(X)X. Use a learned gate γ(X) and a midpoint-collapse regularizer 4γ(1-γ) to encourage crisp boundary decisions; compare against baselines with matched parameters.

Result: Outperforms four baselines (including JPmHC and GPT) on long-horizon stability (1.9x over JPmHC, 3.8x over GPT), near-π rotation loss (4.5x over JPmHC), maintains strong norm preservation (0.001 mean deviation), and achieves 0.96 negation cosine alignment in a diagnostic probe, using ~1.79M parameters per model with ~33% fewer layers across three seeds.

Conclusion: The hybrid Cayley-Householder approach enables access to both connected components of O(n), overcoming JPmHC’s exclusion of the λ=−1 operator and absence of a reflection branch, yielding improved stability and rotation performance with fewer layers.

Abstract: We present the E$Δ$-MHC-Geo Transformer, a novel architecture that unifies Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections (mHC), Deep Delta Learning (DDL), and the Cayley transform to obtain input-adaptive, unconditionally orthogonal residual connections. Unlike DDL, whose Householder operator is orthogonal only at $β\in \{0,2\}$, our Data-Dependent Cayley rotation $Q(x)=(I+(β/2)A(x))^{-1}(I-(β/2)A(x))$ preserves orthogonality for all $β$ and all inputs. To handle negation, an eigenvalue $-1$ case that Cayley provably excludes, we introduce the E$Δ$-MHC-Geo Hybrid, which combines Cayley rotation with Householder reflection via a learned operator-selection gate $X'=γ(X)Q(X)X+(1-γ(X))H_2(X)X$. A midpoint-collapse regularizer, $4γ(1-γ)$, encourages boundary gate decisions, where each selected component is orthogonal. In matched-parameter comparisons, with approximately 1.79M parameters per model and mean +/- standard deviation over 3 seeds, against four baselines including the concurrent JPmHC, E$Δ$-MHC-Geo achieves the best long-horizon stability, 1.9x over JPmHC and 3.8x over GPT; the best near-$π$ rotation loss, 4.5x over JPmHC on single-plane; strong norm preservation, with 0.001 mean deviation; and 0.96 negation cosine alignment in a diagnostic reflection probe, all with 33% fewer layers. While JPmHC's wider representation excels on pure rotation, its finite Cayley residual mixer excludes an exact $λ=-1$ operator and has no reflection branch, motivating our hybrid approach for accessing both connected components of $O(n)$.

</details>


### [163] [Semantic State Abstraction Interfaces for LLM-Augmented Portfolio Decisions: Multi-Axis News Decomposition and RL Diagnostics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06730)
*Likhita Yerra,Remi Uttejitha Allam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Semantic State Abstraction Interfaces (SSAI) introduces a template to map sparse unstructured text into K auditable coordinates to separate representation hypotheses from optimization variance in sequential decision systems, instantiated with K=4 axes and evaluated on a US-equity panel; results show potential gains but robustness concerns, positioning SSAI as an interpretability-performance diagnostic protocol rather than a winner-takes-all approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a transparent, auditable representation framework for sparse-text decision systems by decoupling representation from optimization noise, enabling diagnostic evaluation across models and baselines.

Method: Define SSAI as a general template; instantiate with K=4 axes (sentiment, risk, confidence, volatility forecast); apply to a US-equity panel (30 NASDAQ-100 names, FNSPID news, 2019–2023); evaluate across direct factor portfolios, supervised ridge forecasters, and RL agents (DP-PPO, SAC) with a fixed φ; compare against baselines and report a structured evaluation protocol.

Result: A four-factor portfolio achieves 307.2% cumulative return and Sharpe 1.067, but apparent gains versus buy-and-hold (243.6%) fail coverage-stratified controls; gains reverse at costs ≥0.2%; results are fragile versus a sentiment-only baseline; a PC1 composite and FinBERT baseline offer stronger ranking signals; ridge and RL blocks help diagnose representation versus optimizer effects.

Conclusion: SSAI is positioned as an interpretability-performance diagnostic framework and reusable protocol for sparse-text decision systems.

Abstract: We introduce Semantic State Abstraction Interfaces (SSAI): a methodological template for mapping sparse unstructured text into $K$ auditable, named coordinates with neutral defaults on no-news days, designed to separate representation hypotheses from optimisation variance in sequential decision systems. Our contribution is the framework and its evaluation protocol, not a claim that SSAI outperforms denser alternatives.
  We instantiate SSAI with $K=4$ axes (sentiment, risk, confidence, volatility forecast) on a US-equity panel (30 NASDAQ-100 names, FNSPID news, 2019--2023 test), and evaluate it across direct factor portfolios, supervised ridge forecasters, and RL agents (DP-PPO, SAC) that share the same fixed $φ$. The four-factor factor portfolio reaches 307.2% cumulative return and Sharpe 1.067, but apparent gains versus buy-and-hold (243.6%) fail coverage-stratified controls, reverse at $\geq 0.2$% costs, and are statistically fragile versus a sentiment-only baseline; a PC1 composite and a FinBERT portfolio baseline are stronger ranking signals in this setting. Ridge and RL blocks diagnose representation versus optimiser effects. We position SSAI as an interpretability-performance diagnostic and reusable protocol for sparse-text decision systems.

</details>


### [164] [On Training in Imagination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06732)
*Nadav Timor,Ravid Shwartz-Ziv,Micah Goldblum,Yann LeCun,David Harel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In model-based RL with learned dynamics and reward models, the paper derives the optimal allocation of samples between dynamics and reward data to minimize return error, connects Lipschitz constants to tighter bounds, and analyzes REINFORCE with noisy rewards showing a zero-mean noise leaves the gradient unbiased and introduces a one-dimensional budget optimization between cheaper noisy rollouts and expensive accurate rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify error propagation from learned models to policy optimization in imagined rollouts; extend existing theory to learned reward models; provide practical budgeting guidance for model-based RL.

Method: Extend Asadi et al. (2018) to MDPs with learned reward models; derive optimal sample allocation under power-law scaling; identify lower Lipschitz constants as a design desideratum; connect to temporal-straightening objective; analyze REINFORCE with noisy rewards and show unbiased gradient with a variance term decreasing with rollouts; reduce budgeting to a 1D optimization problem.

Result: Derived a bound-based optimal ratio of dynamics-to-reward samples under assumed scaling; highlighted that lower Lipschitz constants tighten the bound; established that zero-mean reward noise does not bias the gradient and only adds variance diminishing with more rollouts; proposed a one-dimensional optimization to choose rollout budget for given cost constraints.

Conclusion: Provides principled guidelines for allocating data collection between dynamics and reward estimation in model-based RL with learned rewards, and clarifies the trade-off between rollout quantity and reward quality; supports practical budgeting decisions via a simple one-dimensional optimization.

Abstract: State-of-the-art model-based reinforcement learning methods train policies on imagined rollouts. These rollouts are trajectories generated by a learned dynamics model and are scored by a learned reward model, but without querying the true environment during policy updates. We study this training paradigm by quantifying how errors in learned dynamics and reward models affect returns and policy optimization. First, we extend the analysis of Asadi et al. (2018) to MDPs with learned reward models, and derive the optimal sample allocation--the ratio of dynamics samples to reward samples that minimizes a bound on return error under power-law scaling assumptions. We identify lower Lipschitz constants of the learned dynamics, reward, and policy as a representation desideratum that tightens this bound, and we connect this perspective to the temporal-straightening objective of Wang et al. (2026). Second, we examine how policy optimization with REINFORCE tolerates noisy rewards, which are often cheaper to obtain. We show that zero-mean reward noise leaves the gradient estimator unbiased and adds at most a variance term that decreases with the number of rollouts. This introduces a practical tradeoff: given a fixed budget, should one buy more rollouts with cheaper but noisier rewards, or fewer rollouts with more expensive but less noisy rewards? We reduce this choice to a one-dimensional optimization problem and characterize the optimum.

</details>


### [165] [Beyond Factor Aggregation: Gauge-Aware Low-Rank Server Representations for Federated LoRA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06733)
*Jinqian Chen,Chang Liu,Jihua Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GLoRA enables semantically meaningful aggregation in federated LoRA by using a gauge-aware server representation, improving robustness to heterogeneity and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Directly averaging LoRA factors is representation-dependent due to gauge freedom, causing a semantic mismatch in federated aggregation.

Method: Compute a consensus update subspace from client projectors; aggregate updates in shared reference coordinates; maintain low-rank form; provide rank-compatible readout to instantiate adapters of varying ranks from the same server state without dense reconstruction.

Result: On GLUE and SuperNI, GLoRA outperforms federated LoRA baselines under data/resource/task heterogeneity, supports heterogeneous client ranks and sparse participation, scales to larger backbones, and shows favorable efficiency–performance trade-offs.

Conclusion: Semantic update aggregation via a gauge-aware server representation is essential for effective federated LoRA; GLoRA provides a principled framework and empirical gains.

Abstract: Federated LoRA enables parameter-efficient adaptation of large language models under decentralized data and limited client resources.However, directly averaging LoRA factors is representation-dependent: the same intrinsic update admits infinitely many gauge-equivalent factorizations, so factor-level aggregation can change under arbitrary coordinate choices while the underlying update remains unchanged. This reveals a semantic mismatch in existing federated LoRA aggregation rules. We propose \textbf{GLoRA}, a gauge-aware server representation for federated LoRA.Instead of aggregating raw factors, GLoRA estimates a consensus update subspace from client projectors and aggregates client updates in shared reference coordinates, thereby representing semantic update aggregation entirely in low-rank form. To support heterogeneous client capacities, GLoRA further provides a rank-compatible readout that instantiates adapters of different ranks from the same server state without dense update reconstruction. Experiments on GLUE and SuperNI show that GLoRA consistently outperforms federated LoRA baselines under data, resource, and task heterogeneity, including heterogeneous client ranks, sparse participation, larger backbones, and unseen-task evaluation. GLoRA also achieves a favorable efficiency--performance trade-off, suggesting that effective federated LoRA requires not merely averaging low-rank factors, but defining a semantically meaningful server-side representation for aggregation.

</details>


### [166] [Gated QKAN-FWP: Scalable Quantum-inspired Sequence Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06734)
*Kuo-Chung Peng,Samuel Yen-Chi Chen,Jiun-Cheng Jiang,Chen-Yu Liu,En-Jui Kuo,Yun-Yuan Wang,Prayag Tiwari,Andrea Ceschini,Chi-Sheng Chen,Yu-Chao Hsu,Chun-Hua Lin,Tai-Yue Li,Antonello Rosato,Massimo Panella,Simon See,Saif Al-Kuwari,Kuan-Cheng Chen,Nan-Yow Chen,Hsi-Sheng Goan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gated QKAN-FWP combines fast-weight updates with quantum-inspired Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks using single-qubit data re-uploading activations (DARUAN) to deliver scalable, parameter-efficient, NISQ-compatible sequence modeling. It introduces a scalar-gated update rule, with theoretical guarantees on memory and gradient properties; achieves strong long-horizon forecasting and is validated on quantum hardware with minimal gap to noiseless simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scalability and training stability limitations of existing Quantum Fast Weight Programmers (QFWPs) that rely on multi-qubit circuits and heavy classical simulation. Propose a single-qubit, quantum-inspired architecture (DARUAN) combined with a fast-weight update mechanism (QKAN-FWP) to enable scalable, memory-stable sequence modeling with practical applicability (long-horizon forecasting) and theoretical guarantees.

Method: Integrate fast-weight programming with a Quantum-inspired Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (QKAN) using single-qubit data re-uploading activations (DARUAN). Introduce a scalar-gated fast-weight update rule to stabilize parameter evolution. Provide theoretical analysis of an adaptive memory kernel, geometric boundedness, and parallelizable gradient paths. Evaluate on time-series benchmarks, MiniGrid RL, and solar-cycle forecasting; validate NISQ compatibility by deploying on IonQ and IBM Quantum hardware. 

Result: In long-horizon experiments (528-month input, 132-month forecast), a 12.5k-parameter model achieves lower scaled MSE, peak amplitude error, and peak timing error than a suite of classical recurrent baselines with up to 13x more parameters (LSTM, WaveNet-LSTM, Vanilla RNN, ESN). NISQ hardware experiments show forecasting accuracy within 0.1% relative MSE of noiseless simulator at 1024 shots on IonQ/IBM quantum processors.

Conclusion: Gated QKAN-FWP presents a scalable, parameter-efficient, and NISQ-compatible approach to quantum-inspired sequence modeling, with strong practical performance in long-horizon forecasting and reinforcement-learning-related tasks.

Abstract: Fast Weight Programmers (FWPs) encode temporal dependencies through dynamically updated parameters rather than recurrent hidden states. Quantum FWPs (QFWPs) extend this idea with variational quantum circuits (VQCs), but existing implementations rely on multi-qubit architectures that are difficult to scale on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices and expensive to simulate classically. We propose gated QKAN-FWP, a fast-weight framework that integrates FWP with Quantum-inspired Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (QKAN) using single-qubit data re-uploading circuits as learnable nonlinear activation, known as DatA Re-Uploading ActivatioN (DARUAN). We further introduce a scalar-gated fast-weight update rule that stabilizes parameter evolution, supported by a theoretical analysis of its adaptive memory kernel, geometric boundedness, and parallelizable gradient paths. We evaluate the framework across time-series benchmarks, MiniGrid reinforcement learning, and highlight real-world solar cycle forecasting as our main practical result. In the long-horizon setting with 528-month input window and 132-month forecast horizon, our 12.5k-parameter model achieves lower scaled Mean Square Error (MSE), peak amplitude error, and peak timing error than a suite of classical recurrent baselines with up to 13x more parameters, including Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks (25.9k-89.1k parameters), WaveNet-LSTM (167k), Vanilla recurrent neural network (11.5k), and a Modified Echo State Network (132k). To validate NISQ compatibility, we further deploy the trained fast programmer on IonQ and IBM Quantum processors, recovering forecasting accuracy within 0.1% relative MSE of the noiseless simulator at 1024 shots. These results position gated QKAN-FWP as a scalable, parameter-efficient, and NISQ-compatible approach to quantum-inspired sequence modeling.

</details>


### [167] [STDA-Net: Spectrogram-Based Domain Adaptation for cross-dataset Sleep Stage Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06736)
*Unaza Tallal,Shruti Kshirsagar,Ankita Shukla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STDA-Net proposes spectrogram-based 2D inputs with CNN, BiLSTM, and DANN for unsupervised domain adaptation to achieve robust cross-dataset sleep staging, outperforming 1D EEG baselines (Sleep-EDF, SHHS-1, SHHS-2; avg accuracy 89.03%, macro F1 87.64%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-dataset sleep staging variability due to EEG montage, sampling rate, environment, and population hinders generalization. 2D spectrogram representations combined with temporal modeling and unsupervised domain adaptation aim to improve cross-dataset robustness.

Method: STDA-Net integrates (1) CNN-based spectrogram feature extractor, (2) BiLSTM for temporal sleep dynamics, and (3) DANN for source-to-target feature alignment without labeled target data. Evaluated on Sleep-EDF, SHHS-1, SHHS-2 with six cross-dataset transfer settings.

Result: Avg accuracy 89.03% and macro F1 87.64%, outperforming existing 1D EEG baselines with lower variance across five runs, indicating improved stability and reproducibility across cross-dataset folds.

Conclusion: 2D spectrogram-based representations, temporal modeling, and adversarial domain adaptation provide a robust and competitive alternative to 1D EEG inputs for cross-dataset sleep staging.

Abstract: Accurate sleep stage classification across datasets remains challenging due to variability in EEG channel montages, sampling rates, recording environments, and subject populations. Although deep learning has shown considerable promise for automated sleep staging, most existing cross-dataset methods rely on one-dimensional EEG signal representations, whereas the use of two-dimensional spectrogram-based inputs within an unsupervised domain adaptation framework has remained largely unexplored. Here, we propose STDA-Net (Spectrogram-based Temporal Domain Adaptation Network), a framework that combines a convolutional neural network (CNN) for spectrogram-based feature extraction, a bidirectional long short-term memory (BiLSTM) module for temporal modeling of sleep dynamics, and a domain-adversarial neural network (DANN) for source-to-target feature alignment without requiring any labeled target-domain data during training. Experiments are conducted on three publicly available datasets Sleep-EDF, SHHS-1, and SHHS-2 under six cross-dataset transfer settings. Results show that the proposed framework achieves an average accuracy of 89.03% and an average macro F1-score of 87.64%, consistently outperforming existing 1D baseline methods in terms of balanced classification performance, with substantially lower variance across five independent runs, indicating improved stability and reproducibility. Overall, these findings demonstrate that 2D spectrogram-based representations, combined with temporal modeling and adversarial domain adaptation, provide a robust and competitive alternative to conventional 1D EEG inputs for cross-dataset sleep staging.

</details>


### [168] [Geometric Kolmogorov--Arnold Network (GeoKAN)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06740)
*Abhijit Sen,Bikram Keshari Parida,Giridas Maiti,Mahima Arya,Denys I. Bondar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GeoKANs are geometry-aware KAN-type models that learn a diagonal Riemannian metric to warp inputs before basis expansion, reallocating representation capacity to regions with rapid variation for improved performance on nonuniform, stiff PDE-like problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard KANs operate in fixed Euclidean coordinates; introducing a learned metric provides geometric inductive bias through local length scaling and volume distortion, and, in physics-informed settings, affects the differential structure the model sees.

Method: Introduce a diagonal metric that warps inputs; perform basis expansion and feature mixing in the warped coordinates; develop three main variants (GeoKAN-NNMetric, GeoKAN-γ, LM-KAN) and three basis-specific LM-KANs (RBF, Wavelet, Fourier); aim to study geometry-aware KANs as general function approximators and physics-informed surrogates.

Result: The geometry-aware warping reallocates representational resolution by stretching regions with rapid variation and compressing smoother regions, enabling better handling of sharp, stiff, localized, and non-uniform regimes; suitable for scientific ML and PDE problems.

Conclusion: GeoKAN extends KANs to geometry-aware coordinates with multiple variants, offering a flexible framework that couples geometry-aware representation with physics-informed learning for challenging regimes in differential-equation problems.

Abstract: We introduce Geometric Kolmogorov--Arnold Networks (GeoKANs), a family of geometry-aware KAN-type models in which approximation is carried out in learned, geometry-adapted coordinates rather than in fixed Euclidean input coordinates. GeoKAN achieves this by learning a diagonal Riemannian metric that warps the input before basis expansion and feature mixing. The learned metric provides a geometric inductive bias through local length scaling and volume distortion, and in physics-informed settings it also affects the differential structure seen by the model. Within this framework, we develop three main variants, namely GeoKAN-NNMetric, GeoKAN-$γ$, and LM-KAN. For LM-KAN, we further consider three basis-specific versions, LM-KAN-RBF, LM-KAN-Wav, and LM-KAN-Fourier. These variants allow us to study geometry-aware KAN models both as general function approximators and as surrogates in physics-informed learning. By stretching regions with rapid variation and compressing smoother regions, GeoKAN reallocates representational resolution in a task-dependent manner, allowing the model to place capacity where it is most needed. As a result, GeoKAN is well suited to sharp, stiff, localized, and strongly non-uniform regimes arising in scientific machine learning and differential-equation problems.

</details>


### [169] [A Closed-Form Upper Bound for Admissible Learning-Rate Steps in Belief-Space Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06741)
*Zixi Li,Youzhen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Local belief-space update on the probability simplex yields a computable upper bound for admissible learning-rate steps, ensuring KL/Bregman contractivity; the bound is a formula, not a heuristic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Move learning-rate selection from heuristic tuning to principled, geometry-driven bounds that guarantee convergence through contraction in KL/Bregman geometry.

Method: Model the update as a projected forward step on the probability simplex. Define admissibility via contractivity in KL/Bregman geometry, and derive an explicit upper bound formula for the admissible step size.

Result: Admissible steps are governed by a derived upper bound that is a concrete formula, ensuring contractivity in KL/Bregman geometry rather than relying on tuning heuristics.

Conclusion: Provides a principled, geometry-based bound for learning-rate steps in belief-space updates, enabling stable convergence and eliminating reliance on tuning slogans.

Abstract: Learning-rate steps are usually treated as hyperparameters. This paper isolates a local beliefspace calculation: when an update is modeled as a projected forward step on the probability simplex, admissibility means contractivity in the natural KL/Bregman geometry. Under this model, the upper bound of an admissible step is not a tuning slogan but a formula.

</details>


### [170] [Gradient Extrapolation-Based Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06755)
*Ismam Nur Swapnil,Aranya Saha,Tanvir Ahmed Khan,Mohammad Ariful Haque,Ser-Nam Lim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GXPO provides a gradient-extrapolation-based policy update for GRPO-style reasoning RL that achieves near multi-step lookahead with only three backward passes, improving accuracy and speed while maintaining fixed cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the computational burden of full multi-step lookahead in GRPO-style reasoning RL while retaining the benefits of longer horizon updates.

Method: Two fast optimizer steps on the active batch; estimate gradient changes; predict a virtual K-step lookahead; move policy partway toward that extrapolated point; apply a corrective update with the true gradient at the new position; reuse the same rollout batch; automatic switching back to GRPO when extrapolation unstable; plain-gradient-descent surrogate analysis.

Result: On Qwen2.5 and Llama math-reasoning, GXPO improves average sampled pass@1 by 1.65–5.00 points over GRPO and 0.14–1.28 over SFPO; up to 4x step speedup, 2.33x wall-clock speedup, 1.33x backward-pass speedup to reach GRPO peak.

Conclusion: GXPO provides a practical gradient-extrapolation technique that delivers better performance at a constant backward-pass budget, with auto-switching and a theoretical justification for when extrapolation is exact and where errors arise.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning is widely used to improve the reasoning ability of large language models, especially when answers can be automatically checked. Standard GRPO-style training updates the model using only the current step, while full multi-step lookahead can give a better update direction but is too expensive because it needs many backward passes. We propose Gradient Extrapolation-Based Policy Optimization (GXPO), a plug-compatible policy-update rule for GRPO-style reasoning RL. GXPO approximates a longer local lookahead using only three backward passes during an active phase. It reuses the same batch of rollouts, rewards, advantages, and GRPO loss, so it does not require new rollouts or reward computation at the lookahead points. GXPO takes two fast optimizer steps, measures how the gradients change, predicts a virtual K-step lookahead point, moves the policy partway toward that point, and then applies a corrective update using the true gradient at the new position. When the lookahead signal becomes unstable, GXPO automatically switches back to standard single-pass GRPO. We also give a plain-gradient-descent surrogate analysis that explains when the extrapolation is exact and where its local errors come from. Across Qwen2.5 and Llama math-reasoning experiments, GXPO improves the average sampled pass@1 by +1.65 to +5.00 points over GRPO and by +0.14 to +1.28 points over the strongest SFPO setting, while keeping the active-phase cost fixed at three backward passes. It also achieves up to 4.00x step speedup, 2.33x wall-clock speedup, and 1.33x backward-pass speedup in reaching GRPO's peak accuracy.

</details>


### [171] [Physics-based Digital Twins for Integrated Thermal Energy Systems Using Active Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06756)
*Umme Mahbuba Nabila,Paul Seurin,Linyu Lin,Majdi I. Radaideh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An active-learning framework couples Modelica-based system simulations with four surrogates (SINDyC, MvG-SINDyC, FNN, GRU) to create accurate, interpretable, and uncertainty-aware digital twins for a glycol heat exchanger in a thermal-energy distribution system, achieving similar accuracy with ~20% of simulations; GRU delivers best fidelity, SINDyC the best efficiency/interpretability, and MvG-SINDyC enables uncertainty quantification with large AL gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable real-time supervisory control of thermal energy distribution systems, there is a need for digital twins that are accurate, interpretable, and uncertainty-aware while being computationally efficient. High-fidelity simulations are costly, and purely data-driven surrogates may lack robustness, motivating a physics-informed, data-efficient framework.

Method: Couple system-level Modelica simulations with four surrogate models: deterministic SINDyC, probabilistic multivariate-Gaussian SINDyC (MvG-SINDyC), feedforward neural network (FNN), and gated recurrent unit (GRU). Apply AL query strategies tailored to each surrogate (Mahalanobis-distance sampling in coefficient space for MvG-SINDyC; error-based sampling in prediction space for SINDyC, FNN, GRU) to prioritize dynamically informative trajectories. Demonstrated on the glycol heat exchanger (GHX) subsystem of the Thermal Energy Distribution System (TEDS) at INL, focusing on outputs: bypass mass flow rate dot{m}_GHX and heat transfer rate Q_GHX.

Result: Across key GHX outputs, the AL framework achieves comparable predictive accuracy using as few as one-fifth of the simulation trajectories required by random sampling. GRU yields the highest predictive fidelity, SINDyC offers the best computational efficiency and interpretability, and MvG-SINDyC provides uncertainty quantification and shows the largest computational gains under AL.

Conclusion: The proposed AL framework enables accurate, interpretable, and uncertainty-aware digital twins with substantial data efficiency. The learning process benefits from targeted AL strategies, with GRU delivering top performance, SINDyC offering interpretability and efficiency, and MvG-SINDyC delivering uncertainty quantification and strong AL-driven savings.

Abstract: Real-time supervisory control of thermal energy distribution systems requires digital twins that are accurate, interpretable, and uncertainty-aware, yet remain data and computationally efficient. High-fidelity simulations alone are costly, while purely data-driven surrogates often lack robustness. To address these challenges, this work proposes an active learning (AL) framework that couples system-level Modelica simulations with four simpler physics-informed and data-driven surrogate modeling approaches: deterministic Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics with Control (SINDyC), its probabilistic multivariate-Gaussian extension (MvG-SINDyC), feedforward neural network (FNN), and gated recurrent unit (GRU) network. Tailored to each surrogate, model-specific AL query strategies are employed, including Mahalanobis-distance sampling in coefficient space for MvG-SINDyC and error-based sampling in prediction space for SINDyC, FNN, and GRU, allowing the learning process to prioritize dynamically informative trajectories.
  The proposed approach is demonstrated on the glycol heat exchanger (GHX) subsystem of the Thermal Energy Distribution System (TEDS) at Idaho National Laboratory. Across key GHX outputs--the bypass mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{\mathrm{GHX}}$ and heat transfer rate $Q_{\mathrm{GHX}}$-the AL framework achieves comparable predictive accuracy using as few as one-fifth of the simulation trajectories required by random sampling. Among the evaluated surrogates, the GRU achieves the highest predictive fidelity, while SINDyC remains the most computationally efficient and interpretable. The probabilistic MvG-SINDyC surrogate further enables uncertainty quantification and exhibits the largest computational gains under AL.

</details>


### [172] [Sparse Attention as a Range Searching Problem: Towards an Inference-Efficient Index for KV Cache](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06763)
*Mohsen Dehghankar,Abolfazl Asudeh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Louver, a KV cache index for sparse attention that guarantees zero false negatives above a threshold, reformulating sparse attention as halfspace range searching to enable dynamic recall across decoding steps while achieving practical speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse attention trades accuracy for efficiency, but missing critical key-value entries can cause large errors, especially in long reasoning. There is a need for guaranteed recall of relevant keys across decoding steps to maintain reliability.

Method: Reformulates sparse attention as a halfspace range searching problem and designs Louver, a lightweight, hardware-aware KV-cache index that guarantees zero false negatives with respect to a threshold. It integrates into existing LLM pipelines and optimizes for CPU/GPU.

Result: Empirical evaluations show Louver outperforms prior sparse-attention methods in accuracy and runtime and even surpasses optimized dense attentions like FlashAttention, due to its recall guarantees and efficient indexing.

Conclusion: Recall guarantees for sparse attention are crucial. Louver demonstrates that theoretically grounded, efficient KV-cache indices can achieve both correctness guarantees and practical speed, suggesting a new direction for memory-friendly, reliable LLM inference.

Abstract: Sparse attention improves LLM inference efficiency by selecting a subset of key-value entries, but at the cost of potential accuracy degradation. In particular, omitting critical KV entries can induce substantial errors in model outputs. Existing methods typically operate under fixed or adaptive token budgets and provide empirical robustness or partial theoretical guarantees, yet they do not ensure zero false negatives in decoding steps, particularly since the set of relevant tokens is both query- and step-dependent. Our empirical observations confirm that missing even one critical key can lead to sharp error spikes, especially in long reasoning tasks where the set of important tokens varies throughout decoding. This observation motivates the need for indexing methods that dynamically adapt to these variations across decoding steps while guaranteeing a full recall of the relevant keys above a certain threshold. We address this challenge by reformulating sparse attention as the halfspace range searching problem. However, existing range searching indices are not suitable for modern LLM inference due to their computational and implementation overheads. To overcome this, we introduce Louver, a novel index structure tailored for efficient KV cache retrieval. Louver (i) guarantees zero false negatives with respect to a specified threshold in both theory and practice, (ii) is lightweight to integrate into existing LLM pipelines, and (iii) incorporates hardware-aware optimizations for both CPU and GPU executions. Our experiments demonstrate that Louver outperforms prior sparse attention methods in both accuracy and runtime, and is faster than highly optimized dense attentions such as FlashAttention. These results highlight that recall guarantees are a critical and overlooked dimension of sparse attention, and open a new direction for building theoretically grounded, efficient KV cache indices.

</details>


### [173] [Revisiting Adam for Streaming Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06764)
*Florin Gogianu,Adrian Catalin Lutu,Razvan Pascanu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues online RL with bounded objective derivatives and variance-adjusted weight updates is robust; it re-evaluates standard updates (DQN/C51) in an online setting, finds C51 competitive with StreamQ on Atari, and introduces Adaptive Q(λ) with eligibility traces that substantially improves performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Revisit online reinforcement learning without replay buffers to identify update properties and optimizer interactions that yield robust learning, and to compare traditional off-policy updates with newer online methods.

Method: Empirical study of online updates (DQN, C51, StreamQ) in an online setting, analysis of optimizer interactions (notably Adam), identification of two key properties (bounded objective derivative and variance-adjusted updates), and development and evaluation of a new variance-adjusted eligibility-trace algorithm, Adaptive Q(λ), on 55 Atari games.

Result: C51 exhibits the desired properties and is competitive with StreamQ on a subset of 55 Atari games. Adaptive Q(λ), leveraging eligibility traces and variance-adjusted updates, approaches double the human baseline on the same subset and surpasses existing methods by all performance metrics.

Conclusion: Online RL benefits from updates whose objective derivatives are bounded and whose weight updates are variance-adjusted. Eligibility-trace-based variance adjustment yields strong performance (Adaptive Q(λ)), suggesting a viable path toward simpler, more robust online algorithms without replay buffers.

Abstract: Learning from a sequence of interactions, as soon as observations are perceived and acted upon, without explicitly storing them, holds the promise of simpler, more efficient and adaptive algorithms. For over a decade, however, deep reinforcement learning walked the contrary path, augmenting agents with replay buffers or parallel sampling routines, in an effort to tame learning instability. Recently, this topic has been revisited by Elsayed et al. (2024), focusing on update computation through eligibility traces and modifications to the optimisation routine, resulting in the StreamQ algorithm. In this work we take a step back, investigating the efficacy of established updates, such as those implemented by DQN and C51 within this online setting. Not only do we find that they perform well, but through analysing how the optimisation algorithm generally, and Adam in particular, interacts with these updates, we contend that two properties are essential for robust performance: i) the derivative of the objective is to be bounded and ii) weight updates are variance-adjusted. Rigorous and exhaustive experimentation demonstrates that C51, which exhibits both characteristics, is competitive with StreamQ across a subset of 55 Atari games. Using these insights, we derive a variance-adjusted algorithm based on eligibility traces, termed Adaptive Q$(λ)$, which approaches double the human baseline on the same subset, surpassing existing methods by all performance metrics.

</details>


### [174] [Distributional Process Reward Models: Calibrated Prediction of Future Rewards via Conditional Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06785)
*Rachel Ma,Dylan Hadfield-Menell,Kristjan Greenewald*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First application of conditional OT to calibrate Process Reward Models (PRMs) for inference-time scaling; yields monotone conditional quantiles and uncertainty bounds; integrated with IAS; improves calibration on MATH-500 and AIME, outperforming uncalibrated PRMs and quantile regression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PRMs often provide poorly calibrated success probabilities, leading to overconfident/inaccurate decisions. Existing calibration methods struggle to enforce monotone, structure-preserving quantiles and to provide flexible, arbitrary-level confidence bounds. Conditional OT offers monotone conditional quantile estimation with structural guarantees, enabling robust uncertainty estimation.

Method: Adapt CondOT map learning to estimate a monotone conditional quantile function of PRM success probabilities conditioned on PRM hidden states. Derive quantile estimates and corresponding confidence bounds, then integrate these into the instance-adaptive scaling (IAS) framework. Evaluate on MATH-500 and AIME benchmarks and compare against uncalibrated PRMs and quantile regression.

Result:  Calibrated PRMs show substantial improvement over uncalibrated PRMs and quantile regression when ranking signals are reliable. In downstream Best-of-N IAS tasks, the method generally improves performance compared to uncalibrated PRMs. CondOT provides structural guarantees and flexible uncertainty estimation.

Conclusion: Conditional OT is a principled, practical approach to PRM calibration, expanding the toolkit for inference-time scaling by delivering monotone conditional quantiles, robust uncertainty bounds, and improved IAS performance.

Abstract: Inference-time scaling methods rely on Process Reward Models (PRMs), which are often poorly calibrated and overestimate success probabilities. We propose, to our knowledge, the first use of conditional optimal transport for calibrating PRMs, modifying conditional OT (CondOT) map learning \cite{bunne2022supervised} to estimate a monotonic conditional quantile function over success probabilities estimated by the PRM, conditioned on PRM hidden states. This yields structurally valid quantile estimates and enables efficient extraction of confidence bounds at arbitrary levels, which we integrate into the instance-adaptive scaling (IAS) framework of \cite{park2025know}. We evaluate on mathematical reasoning benchmarks spanning moderate-difficulty problems (MATH-500) and harder out-of-distribution problems (AIME). For PRMs with reliable ranking signals, our method substantially improves calibration over both uncalibrated PRMs and quantile regression. On downstream Best-of-N IAS performance, our method generally improves over uncalibrated PRMs. These results establish conditional optimal transport as another principled and practical approach to PRM calibration, offering structural guarantees and flexible uncertainty estimation.

</details>


### [175] [Conformal Agent Error Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06788)
*Naihe Feng,Yi Sui,Shiyi Hou,Ga Wu,Jesse C. Cresswell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A conformal-prediction-based framework for error attribution in multi-agent systems (MAS) that provides finite-sample, distribution-free uncertainty sets in the form of contiguous trajectory sequences to identify and rollback errors efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MAS exhibit long interaction traces, making error attribution difficult; reliable recovery requires principled uncertainty estimates and efficient rollback mechanisms, particularly for language-model–driven MAS.

Method: Develop filtration-based conformal prediction algorithms tailored for sequential data (agent trajectories) that output contiguous prediction sets (sequence intervals) rather than single points; the framework is model-agnostic and provides finite-sample guarantees.

Result: Theoretical guarantees are established across varied agents/datasets; errors can be precisely isolated using the prediction sets; the sets enable rollback of MAS to correct errors; code is released for reproducibility and adoption.

Conclusion: Provides a principled uncertainty layer for MAS error attribution and supports automated recovery by identifying and rolling back erroneous trajectories; the approach is model-agnostic and practically applicable.

Abstract: When multi-agent systems (MAS) fail, identifying where the decisive error occurred is the first step for automated recovery to an earlier state. Error attribution remains a fundamental challenge due to the long interaction traces that large language model-based MAS generate. This paper presents a framework for error attribution based on conformal prediction (CP) which provides finite-sample, distribution-free coverage guarantees. We introduce new algorithms for filtration-based CP designed for sequential data such as agent trajectories. Unlike existing CP algorithms, our approach predicts sets that are contiguous sequences to enable efficient recovery and debugging. We verify our theoretical guarantees on a variety of agents and datasets, show that errors can be precisely isolated, then use prediction sets to rollback MAS to correct their own errors. Our overall approach is model-agnostic, and offers a principled uncertainty layer for MAS error attribution. We release code at https://github.com/layer6ai-labs/conformal-agent-error-attribution.

</details>


### [176] [MIND: Monge Inception Distance for Generative Models Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06797)
*Quentin Berthet,Yu-Han Wu,Clement Crepy,Romuald Elie,Klaus Greff,Michael Eli Sander*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MIND uses sliced Wasserstein distance to create a discriminative, robust, and sample-efficient alternative to FID for evaluating generative models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address FID's limitations: high sample complexity, covariance estimation issues, and vulnerability to adversarial/moment-matching attacks.

Method: Compute the sliced Wasserstein distance by projecting high-dimensional features onto multiple 1D axes, sorting per projection, and averaging the resulting 1D OT distances to form MIND; avoids estimating high-dimensional means/covariances.

Result: Empirically, MIND is ~10x more sample-efficient, ~100x faster, and more robust to adversarial/moment-matching attacks than FID; 5k MIND samples match 50k FID performance with high correlation and superior discrimination; 1k–2k samples remain informative for rapid iteration.

Conclusion: MIND is a practical, faster, and more robust evaluation metric for generative models, suitable as an alternative or complement to FID, especially for rapid development and smaller datasets.

Abstract: We propose the Monge Inception Distance (MIND), a metric for evaluating generative models that addresses key limitations of the widely adopted Fréchet Inception Distance (FID). The MIND metric leverages the sliced Wasserstein distance to compare distributions by averaging one-dimensional optimal transport distances, efficiently computed via sorting. This approach circumvents the estimation of high-dimensional means and covariance matrices, which underlie FID's poor sample complexity and vulnerability to adversarial attacks. We empirically demonstrate three primary advantages: (i) it is more sample-efficient by one order of magnitude, (ii) it is faster to compute by two orders of magnitude, (iii) it is more robust to adversarial attacks such as moment-matching. We show that MIND with 5k samples can replace the evaluation performance of FID with 50k samples, providing high correlation with this standard benchmark and superior discriminative performance. We further demonstrate that even smaller sample sizes (e.g., 1k or 2k) remain highly informative for rapid model iteration.

</details>


### [177] [From Model to Data (M2D): Shifting Complexity from GNNs to Graphs for Transparent Graph Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06814)
*Debolina Halder Lina,Arlei Silva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Model-to-Data distillation (M2D) transfers model complexity into the data space by augmenting graphs so a simple student can match a teacher GNN's performance, revealing architectural advantages directly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the opacity of GNN architectures and the gap in explainability between simple and complex models; existing explainability methods focus on predictions, not architectural transparency or performance gaps.
