Method: Train teacher GNN; construct an augmented graph with enriched features/structure encoding the teacher's behavior; train a lightweight student on this augmented data to match the teacher's performance; allow inspection of how data encodes model mechanisms (e.g., fairness objectives, attention-based aggregation).

Result: A simple student attains teacher-level performance on the same task; the augmented data exposes architectural advantages, offering interpretable insights into mechanisms behind GNN performance and enabling transparency without sacrificing accuracy.

Conclusion: M2D provides architectural transparency for GNNs by materializing model behavior in data, enabling human inspection of why complex models outperform simple ones and highlighting mechanisms like fairness and attention in an interpretable manner.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) achieve high performance but can be opaque to humans, making it difficult to understand and compare the many proposed architectures. While existing explainability methods attribute individual predictions to nodes, edges, or features, they do not provide architectural transparency or explain the fundamental performance gap between simple and more complex models. To address this limitation, we introduce Model-to-Data (M2D) distillation, a new framework that increases transparency by transferring model complexity into the data space. M2D distills the teacher model into an augmented graph with enriched features and structure, enabling a simple student to match the teacher's performance. By materializing model behavior in the data, our approach allows humans to inspect architectural advantages directly. We show that M2D reveals underlying mechanisms such as fairness objectives and attention-based aggregation in an interpretable way, enhancing GNN transparency while preserving performance.

</details>


### [178] [A Theory of Online Learning with Autoregressive Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06819)
*Ilan Doron-Arad,Idan Mehalel,Elchanan Mossel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Online learnability of autoregressive generation: taxonomy of mistake bounds as a function of horizon M; End-to-End feedback yields rates from constant to logarithmic with a logarithmic ceiling; Chain-of-Thought feedback eliminates dependence on M; extensions to autoregressive linear threshold classes and new lower bounds; resolves questions from Joshi et al. 2025 and parallels statistical results by Hanneke et al. 2026.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the learnability of input-output maps induced by autoregressive next-token generation in an online setting, and how the horizon M and feedback granularity affect the optimal mistake bounds.

Method: Develop an online analogue of the PAC framework for autoregressive generation. Analyze two feedback models (End-to-End and Chain-of-Thought). Establish upper and lower bounds on the mistake rate as a function of M. Extend analysis to linear threshold classes and provide new statistical lower bounds. Compare with existing statistical results to identify qualitative similarities and differences.

Result: End-to-End: a taxonomy of possible mistake-bound growth rates in M, ranging essentially from constant to logarithmic; the logarithmic ceiling is unavoidable. Chain-of-Thought: access to the full M-step trajectory eliminates dependence on M. Autoregressive linear threshold classes: optimal mistake bounds established and a new lower bound in the statistical setting. The results also resolve open questions from Joshi et al. 2025.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates a qualitative correspondence between online and statistical learnability of autoregressive processes, highlights the crucial role of feedback granularity in mitigating horizon dependence, and extends the theory to linear threshold models with tight bounds and new lower bounds, thereby clarifying the impact of horizon M on learnability.

Abstract: Autoregressive generation lies at the heart of the mechanism of large language models. It can be viewed as the repeated application of a next-token generator: starting from an input string (prompt), the generator is applied for $M$ steps, and the last generated token is taken as the final output. [Joshi et al., 2025] proposed a PAC model for studying the learnability of the input-output maps arising from this process. We develop an online analogue of this framework, focusing on the mistake bound of learning the final output induced by an unknown next-token generator.
  We distinguish between two forms of feedback. In the End-to-End model, after each round the learner observes only the final token produced after $M$ autoregressive steps. In the Chain-of-Thought model, the learner is additionally shown the entire $M$-step trajectory. Our goal is to understand how the optimal mistake bound depends on the generation horizon $M$, and to what extent observing intermediate tokens can reduce this dependence.
  Our main results show that the online theory of autoregressive learning exhibits a qualitative picture analogous to the statistical one found by [Hanneke et al., 2026], but with a different scale of dependence on the generation horizon. In the End-to-End model, we prove a taxonomy of possible mistake-bound growth rates in the generation horizon $M$: essentially any rate between constant and logarithmic can arise. We further show that this logarithmic ceiling is unavoidable. In the Chain-of-Thought model, we show that access to the full generated trajectory eliminates the dependence on $M$ altogether.
  We also analyze autoregressive linear threshold classes, and prove optimal mistake bounds, as well as a new lower bound for the statistical setting. Along the way, our results resolve several questions left open by [Joshi et al., 2025].

</details>


### [179] [A Rod Flow Model for Adam at the Edge of Stability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06821)
*Eric Regis,Sinho Chewi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Rod flow generalizes edge-of-stability modeling from gradient descent to momentum-based optimizers (Adam, RMSProp variants, Nesterov, heavy-ball) by formulating a joint phase-space flow in (w, m) with ν treated as a smooth auxiliary variable; empirically, it tracks discrete iterates more accurately than stable-flow across eight optimizers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend continuous-time edge-of-stability modeling to momentum-based optimizers to better capture their dynamics near stability limits, enabling unified analysis and intuition beyond plain gradient descent.

Method: Construct rod-flow dynamics in the joint space (w, m) for Adam, treating ν as a smooth auxiliary; develop analogous rod flows for heavy-ball, Nesterov, and scalar/per-component RMSProp, Adam, and NAdam; perform empirical evaluation on representative ML architectures to compare rod-flow tracks against actual discrete iterates.

Result: Rod-flow models track the discrete optimizer iterates in edge-of-stability regimes significantly more accurately than the corresponding stable-flow across all eight optimizers.

Conclusion: Rod flows provide a versatile and accurate continuous-time framework for edge-of-stability dynamics for momentum-based optimizers, extending the original gradient-descent rod flow to a broader class of algorithms.

Abstract: Cohen et al. (arXiv:2207.14484) observed that adaptive gradient methods such as Adam operate at the edge of stability. While there has been significant work on continuous-time modeling of gradient descent at the edge of stability, extending these models to momentum methods remains underdeveloped. In the gradient descent setting, Regis et al. (arXiv:2602.01480) introduced rod flow, which models consecutive iterates as an extended one-dimensional object -- a "rod." Here we extend rod flow to Adam by working in the joint phase space of parameters and first moment $(w, m)$ and treating the second moment $ν$ as a smooth auxiliary variable. We also develop rod flows for heavy ball momentum, Nesterov momentum, and scalar and per-component versions of RMSProp, Adam, and NAdam. For all eight optimizers, we empirically evaluate rod flow on representative machine learning architectures, where it tracks the discrete iterates through the edge-of-stability regime significantly more accurately than the corresponding stable flow.

</details>


### [180] [SHARP: A Self-Evolving Human-Auditable Rubric Policy for Financial Trading Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06822)
*Xiwen Chen,Wenhui Zhu,Songzhu Zheng,Kashif Rasul,Yueyue Deng,Huayu Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SHARP is a neuro-symbolic, rubric-based policy framework that replaces unbounded prompt optimization with structured, auditable condition-action rules for LLM-driven trading, using cross-sample attribution to identify rule failures and walk-forward validation to ensure robustness, yielding improved performance and transparency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the credit-assignment problem in noisy, delayed P&L rewards by avoiding unstructured text mutations; provide interpretable, auditable decision logic suitable for institutional finance.

Method: A bounded, human-readable rubric of explicit condition-action rules governs the agent. When trades underperform, an attribution agent performs cross-sample reasoning to pinpoint failing rules, enabling targeted, atomic edits. Edits are regularized via strict walk-forward validation.

Result: Across three equity sectors and four LLM backbones, SHARP improves initial heuristics into robust strategies, boosting average performance by 10–20 percentage points (e.g., with GPT-4o-mini). Demonstrates dynamic adaptation with enhanced transparency suitable for finance.

Conclusion: LLMs can achieve dynamic, efficient adaptation under structured, auditable policy frameworks; SHARP improves robustness and auditability, addressing core needs in institutional trading.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed for autonomous financial trading, a domain requiring continuous adaptation to noisy, non-stationary markets. Existing self-improving agents typically address this through unbounded free-form prompt optimization. However, in low signal-to-noise environments with delayed scalar rewards (P\&L), this unstructured approach exacerbates the fundamental credit assignment problem: optimizers cannot reliably distinguish systematic logic flaws from stochastic market variance, inevitably leading to policy drift. To overcome this bottleneck, we introduce the Self-Evolving Human-Auditable Rubric Policy (SHARP), a neuro-symbolic framework that replaces unconstrained text mutation with structured, symbolic policy optimization. SHARP confines the agent's reasoning to a bounded, human-readable rubric of explicit condition-action rules. When sub-optimal trades occur, an attribution agent employs cross-sample reasoning across multiple samples to isolate specific rule failures. This enables targeted, atomic policy edits that are subsequently regularized through strict walk-forward validation. Evaluated across three diverse equity sectors and four LLM backbones, SHARP consistently transforms generic initial heuristics into highly robust strategies, lifting the empirical performance of compact models by 10 to 20 percentage points on average (e.g., GPT-4o-mini). Ultimately, SHARP demonstrates that LLMs can achieve dynamic and efficient adaptation while significantly enhancing the structural transparency and auditability demanded by institutional finance.

</details>


### [181] [A Unified Measure-Theoretic View of Diffusion, Score-Based, and Flow Matching Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06829)
*Aditya Ranganath,Mukesh Singhal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unifies diffusion, score-based, and flow-matching generative models through a time-dependent vector field framework, deriving reverse-time dynamics, linking probability-flow ODEs to likelihood-based flows, and clarifying when flow matching aligns with score-based training; discusses Schrodinger bridges, entropic OT, and practical tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Methods like diffusion, score-based, and flow matching have converging ideas but use disparate notation and derivations. A unified framework clarifies their connections, enabling better sampling efficiency, stability, and scalability.

Method: Introduce a unified framework where a time-dependent vector field induces a family of marginals solving the continuity and Fokker-Planck equations. Derive reverse-time sampling for diffusion and score-based models as controlled stochastic dynamics. Show that the probability flow ODE yields identical marginals and links diffusion to likelihood-based normalizing flows. Interpret flow matching as velocity-field regression under a chosen interpolation, clarifying when it matches or diverges from score-based training.

Result: Establishes formal connections among diffusion, score-based, and flow-matching methods within a single framework. Provides unified notation for objectives, sampling schemes, and discretization errors; relates to Schrödinger bridges and entropic optimal transport; outlines theoretical guarantees and identifies open problems in approximation, stability, and scalability.

Conclusion: A unified theory clarifies the relationships and tradeoffs among continuous-time generative models, enabling cross-pollination and addressing practical challenges in sampling, stability, and computation; highlights directions for future study in approximation, stability, and scalability.

Abstract: We survey continuous-time generative modeling methods based on transporting a simple reference distribution to a data distribution via stochastic or deterministic dynamics. We present a unified framework in which diffusion models, score-based generative models, and flow matching are instances of learning a time-dependent vector field that induces a family of marginals $(ρ_t)_{t \in [0,1]}$ governed by continuity and Fokker-Planck equations. Such a unified theory is timely because these methods are converging methodologically, yet fragmented notation and competing derivations continue to obscure their shared structure and the practical tradeoffs governing sampling, stability, and computation. Within this framework, we (i) derive reverse-time sampling for diffusion and score-based models as controlled stochastic dynamics, (ii) show that the probability flow ODE yields identical marginals and connects diffusion to likelihood-based normalizing flows, and (iii) interpret flow matching as direct regression of the velocity field under a chosen interpolation, clarifying when it coincides with or differs from score-based training. We compare objectives, sampling schemes, and discretization errors under unified notation, discuss connections to Schrodinger bridges and entropic optimal transport, and summarize theoretical guarantees and open problems on approximation, stability, and scalability.

</details>


### [182] [Why DDIM Hallucinates More than DDPM: A Theoretical Analysis of Reverse Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06831)
*Muhammad H. Ashiq,Samanyu Arora,Abhinav N. Harish,Ishaan Kharbanda,Hung Yun Tseng,Grigorios G. Chrysos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In diffusion models, stochastic DDPM reduces hallucinations compared to deterministic DDIM. For Gaussian mixtures, DDIM can get stuck on the segment between nearest modes after a critical time, while DDPM's stochasticity helps it escape, reducing hallucination. Extra stochastic steps can mitigate DDIM hallucinations; results support design of improved samplers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand and mitigate hallucinations in generative diffusion samplers by comparing stochastic (DDPM) and deterministic (DDIM) dynamics, focusing on mode connectivity in Gaussian mixtures.

Method: Theoretical analysis of the reverse dynamics: SDE for DDPM and ODE for DDIM on a Gaussian mixture target, proving sticking behavior after a critical time; empirical validation showing relative hallucination rates; exploration of adding stochastic steps to DDIM.

Result: DDIM may become stuck on the segment between two nearest modes after a critical time; DDPM's stochasticity helps escape this region, reducing hallucinations; empirical data show lower hallucination rates for DDPM; adding stochastic steps to DDIM can alleviate hallucinations.

Conclusion: Stochasticity in diffusion samplers helps avoid hallucinations by avoiding persistent stagnation in mode-connectivity regions; design guidance: incorporate stochastic steps or maintain stochastic dynamics to improve sample fidelity and reduce hallucinations.

Abstract: We theoretically study the hallucination phenomena in two canonical diffusion samplers: the stochastic Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) and the deterministic Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM). We analyze the reverse ODE (DDIM) and SDE (DDPM) for a Gaussian mixture target, proving that after a critical time $τ$, (a) DDIM can become stuck on the segment connecting the two nearest modes and (b) DDPM *stochasticity* helps it become unstuck from this region, thus avoiding hallucination. Our empirical validation verifies that DDPM has a significantly lower hallucination rate than DDIM when this region is entered. Building on our observations, we exhibit how using additional stochastic steps can help DDIM avoid hallucinations and offer new insights on how to design improved samplers.

</details>


### [183] [Attribution-Based Neuron Utility for Plasticity Restoration in Deep Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06834)
*Patrick Elisii,Lucas Beauchemin,Dawer Jamshed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes gradient times difference from reference (GXD) as a utility for guiding adaptive resets to maintain plasticity in continual learning; frames reset as an intervention cost estimation problem and shows alignment with functional cost improves reliability when traditional reset criteria fail.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address loss of plasticity in continual learning and the inadequacy of existing reset proxies, which can misalign with the true cost of removing/replacing network units.

Method: Introduce GXD, a reference-based gradient attribution measure that estimates the first-order functional cost of replacing a unit; use it to select low-utility parameters for reset during training.

Result: Empirical results indicate GXD-guided resets yield more reliable restoration of trainability, outperforming traditional proxy-based reset criteria in scenarios where those criteria degrade.

Conclusion: Reframes adaptive resetting as an intervention cost estimation problem and provides a practical approach for robust continual learning by aligning reset decisions with the actual functional cost.

Abstract: Continual learning research attempts to conserve two fundamental capabilities: new knowledge acquisition and the preservation of previously acquired knowledge. While knowledge in this case can be measured through performance over an implicit or explicit task space, model plasticity generally concerns adaptability as data distributions evolve. Though much of the literature has focused on catastrophic forgetting, deep networks can also suffer from loss of plasticity, becoming progressively harder to update under continued training. Recent research has identified multiple mechanisms underlying this phenomenon, including neuron saturation, parameter norm growth, and loss of useful curvature directions. Adaptive reset-based interventions, which selectively reinitialize low-utility network parameters, have emerged as practical solutions to restore trainability. Existing utility measures used to guide resets, such as activation magnitude, contribution utility, or gradient-based activity, rely on proxy signals that can become misaligned with the intervention they are meant to guide. In this paper, we introduce gradient times difference from reference (GXD), a theoretically motivated utility measure based on reference-based gradient attribution that estimates the first-order functional cost of replacing a unit. Our results show that utility measures aligned with the functional cost of the reset can make interventions more reliable in settings where existing reset criteria degrade. GXD reframes adaptive resetting as an intervention cost estimation problem, providing a practical path toward more robust continual learning systems.

</details>


### [184] [On Privacy Leakage in Tabular Diffusion Models: Influential Factors, Attacker Knowledge, and Metrics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06835)
*Masoumeh Shafieinejad,D. B. Emerson,Behnoosh Zamanlooy,Elaheh Bassak,Fatemeh Tavakoli,Sara Kodeiri,Marcelo Lotif,Xi He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tabular diffusion models (TDMs) enable high-quality synthetic tabular data but pose privacy risks. This work uses state-of-the-art membership inference attacks in black- and white-box settings to quantify leakage, showing that attacker knowledge and synthesis choices affect leakage and that practical attacks can succeed without perfect knowledge or massive compute. It also flags the unreliability of simple privacy metrics like distance-to-closest-record.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With growing use of synthetic tabular data to mitigate privacy risk, it is crucial to quantify real privacy leakage under realistic attacker capabilities and across training/synthesis settings.

Method: Apply state-of-the-art membership inference attacks to TDMs in black- and white-box settings. Systematically vary training setup, synthesis choices, and attacker knowledge. Compare empirical leakage with heuristic privacy metrics such as distance-to-closest-record.

Result: Adversaries can construct successful leaks without perfect knowledge or massive compute. Some training/synthesis configurations amplify leakage. Heuristic metrics like distance-to-closest-record can be misleading indicators of actual privacy risk.

Conclusion: Privacy leakage in TDMs is non-trivial under realistic attacker assumptions. Relying on heuristic metrics is insufficient. More robust evaluation frameworks and defenses are needed to quantify and mitigate privacy risk in tabular synthetic data.

Abstract: Tabular data plays an important role in many fields and industries, including those with elevated privacy considerations and risks. As such, there is a rising interest in generating high-quality synthetic proxies for real tabular data as a means of reducing privacy risk and proprietary data exposure. With tabular diffusion models (TDMs) demonstrating leading performance in synthesizing such data, understanding and measuring the privacy risks associated with these models is imperative. Leveraging state-of-the-art membership inference attacks for TDMs in both black- and white-box settings, this work quantifies the impact of training setup, synthesis choices, and attacker knowledge on privacy leakage. Moreover, the results demonstrate that adversaries need not have perfect knowledge of the training setup, identical data distributions, or massive compute resources to construct successful attacks. Finally, the pitfalls associated with applying heuristic privacy metrics, such as distance-to-closest record, are revealed.

</details>


### [185] [Learning Material-Aware Hamiltonian Risk Fields for Safe Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07038)
*Aditya Sai Ellendula,Yi Wang,Chandrajit Bajaj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a context-energy augmented port-Hamiltonian navigation policy that yields a falsifiable selectivity signature: activates evasive forces along lower-risk directions when feasible, and suppresses lateral forces when no safe alternative exists. Uses a route-aware CVaR objective to focus on rare, high-consequence risk transitions, validated across four settings with improved safety and maintained efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve risk-aware navigation that only exposes degrees of freedom when a safer alternative exists, avoiding hallucinated unsafe maneuvers and reducing premature evasive actions.

Method: Augments a port-Hamiltonian navigation policy with a context-energy term to create a learned force channel. Derives a route-aware gate that suppresses lateral force when no lower-risk path is available. Optimizes a CVaR tail-risk objective to bias gradient updates toward rare but consequential risk transitions. Evaluates on four benchmarks: primary delayed-required-escape, REllis-3D off-road, DFC2018 static maps, and highway traffic.

Result: Route-aware CVaR reduces premature force activation (0.950→0.180 vs DWA) and increases success without replans (0.480→0.810). Real terrain shows activation rate 0.837 and false activation 0.114 vs 0.378/0.752 for scalar risk gradients. Static maps reduce catastrophic failure from 0.60 to 0.10 and greatly reduce oscillation (92-91%? specifics: 90.7%). Highway: collisions drop from 100% to 0% when escape feasible; when not, lateral maneuver is suppressed. Demonstrates selectivity via gradient structure rather than tuning.

Conclusion: Selectivity in risk-aware navigation arises from the gradient structure of the context energy, not from tuning. The framework yields safer, more efficient navigation across diverse settings by enabling evasive behavior only when a lower-risk route exists and suppressing spuriously unsafe actions otherwise.

Abstract: Risk-aware navigation should be selective: a policy should expose evasive degrees of freedom only when the local scene admits a lower-risk feasible maneuver, and suppress them when no safer alternative exists. We show that adding one context-energy term to a port-Hamiltonian navigation policy produces a learned force channel with exactly this falsifiable signature. When the local risk field contains a feasible lower-risk direction, the induced context force activates toward it; when the apparent escape is blocked or not yet available, a route-aware gate suppresses lateral force rather than hallucinating an unsafe maneuver. A CVaR tail-risk objective focuses gradient updates on rare but consequential risk transitions. We validate the selectivity signature across four settings. In the primary delayed-required-escape benchmark, route-aware CVaR reduces premature force activation from 0.950 to 0.180 versus DWA while raising success from 0.480 to 0.810 with zero replans. On real off-road terrain (RELLIS-3D), route-aware enrichment achieves correct activation rate 0.837 and false activation rate 0.114, compared to 0.378/0.752 for scalar risk gradients. On static semantic maps (DFC2018), enrichment reduces catastrophic failure from 0.60 to 0.10 and oscillation by 90.7% while preserving path efficiency. In highway traffic, collisions drop from 100% to 0% when a lane escape is feasible; when no escape exists, the policy suppresses the lateral maneuver. The selectivity property follows from the gradient structure of the context energy rather than from training-time tuning.

</details>


### [186] [How to Compress KV Cache in RL Post-Training? Shadow Mask Distillation for Memory-Efficient Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06850)
*Rui Zhu,Weiheng Bai,Qiushi Wu,Yang Ren,Haixu Tang,Yuchu Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KV cache compression during online RL rollouts for long-context LLMs reduces memory usage but induces an off-policy bias that is amplified by RL optimization; standard reweighting methods suffer from high gradient variance and sample inefficiency, challenging bias correction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand the memory bottlenecks and bias implications of using KV cache compression in online RL for long-context LLMs (e.g., RLHF/RLAIF) and why current corrections fail.

Method: The work analyzes the interaction between sparse-context rollouts (with compressed KV caches) and dense-context parameter updates, highlighting the resulting off-policy bias. It also discusses the (in)effectiveness of importance reweighting under high gradient variance.

Result: KV cache compression introduces a non-negligible off-policy bias that is exacerbated by RL training dynamics. Even near-lossless compression can significantly distort updates. Importance reweighting struggles due to high gradient variance and sample inefficiency, failing to adequately correct the bias.

Conclusion: Current statistical corrections are insufficient to address the amplified bias arising from KV compression in online RL with LLMs. There is a need for novel bias mitigation strategies that account for the RL optimization dynamics and the mismatch between rollout context and update context.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a crucial paradigm for unlocking the advanced reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), encompassing frameworks like RLHF and RLAIF. Regardless of the specific optimization algorithm (e.g., PPO, GRPO, or Online DPO), online RL inherently requires an exploratory trajectory generation (rollout) phase. However, for long-context reasoning tasks, this rollout phase imposes a severe ``memory wall'' due to the exorbitant Key-Value (KV) cache footprint. While applying KV cache compression during rollouts mitigates this memory overhead, it induces a critical off-policy bias. Although modern KV compression is often nearly lossless during standard inference, even minuscule approximation errors are drastically amplified by the inherent instability of RL optimization. Specifically, the sampler generates responses under a sparse context, whereas the learner updates parameters using the full, dense context. Existing statistical solutions, such as importance reweighting, struggle to correct this magnified bias, suffering from high gradient variance and severe sample inefficiency.

</details>


### [187] [Benchmarked Yet Not Measured -- Generative AI Should be Evaluated Against Real-World Utility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06856)
*Ishani Mondal,Shweta Bhardwaj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Advocates utility-centered evaluation for generative AI; introduces SCU-GenEval and deployment-oriented instruments to measure real-world human outcomes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: A gap exists between strong benchmark performance and real-world utility, shown across 28 deployment cases in education, healthcare, software engineering, and law. The authors identify three evaluation failures—proxy displacement, temporal collapse, and distributional concealment—and argue for utility-centric evaluation grounded in human outcome trajectories.

Method: Proposes SCU-GenEval, a four-stage framework: (1) stakeholder-goal mapping, (2) construct-indicator specification, (3) mechanism modeling, (4) longitudinal utility measurement. To operationalize, introduces three instruments: structured deployment protocols, context-conditioned user simulators, and persona- and goal-conditioned proxy metrics.

Result: Conceptual framework with actionable instruments for evaluating real-world impact; emphasizes longitudinal, context-aware utility over static benchmark metrics—no empirical results are reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: Immediate shift in generative AI evaluation toward measuring measurable improvements in human outcomes within deployment contexts, rather than relying on benchmark performance alone.

Abstract: Generative AI systems achieve impressive performance on standard benchmarks yet fail to deliver real-world utility, a disconnect we identify across 28 deployment cases spanning education, healthcare, software engineering, and law. We argue that this benchmark utility gap arises from three recurring failures in evaluation practice: proxy displacement, temporal collapse, and distributional concealment. Motivated by these observations, we argue that generative AI evaluation requires a paradigm shift from static benchmark-centered transparency toward stakeholder, goal, and context-conditioned utility transparency grounded in human outcome trajectories. Existing evaluations primarily characterize properties of model outputs, while deployment success depends on whether interaction with AI improves stakeholders' ability to achieve their goals over time. The missing construct is therefore utility: the change in a stakeholder's capability induced through sustained interaction with an AI system within a deployment context. To operationalize this perspective, we propose SCU-GenEval, a four-stage evaluation framework consisting of stakeholder-goal mapping, construct-indicator specification, mechanism modeling, and longitudinal utility measurement. To make these stages practically deployable, we introduce three supporting instruments: structured deployment protocols, context-conditioned user simulators, and persona- and goal-conditioned proxy metrics. We conclude with domain-specific calls to action, arguing that progress in generative AI must be evaluated through measurable improvements in human outcomes rather than benchmark performance alone.

</details>


### [188] [Christoffel-DPS: Optimal sensor placement in diffusion posterior sampling for arbitrary distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06861)
*James Rowbottom,Nick Huang,Carola-Bibiane Schönlieb,Ben Adcock*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a distribution-free sensor placement framework using the Christoffel function for posterior sampling in generative-model–based state reconstruction, deriving non-asymptotic sensor-count bounds and presenting offline/online Christoffel-DPS variants that outperform Gaussian and other generative-model baselines on non-Gaussian benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical OSP relies on Gaussian assumptions and fails to capture complex real-world distributions, while modern generative-recovery models require sensor-placement tools that align with non-Gaussian priors and provide principled guarantees.

Method: Develop Christoffel sampling via the Christoffel function to formulate optimal sensing for arbitrary sensors and signal distributions; derive non-asymptotic guarantees on the number of sensors; instantiate with offline and online Christoffel-DPS variants; test across unconditional DPS and flow-matching models.

Result: Christoffel-DPS achieves superior performance to Gaussian OSP baselines and existing generative-model placement methods, particularly in low-sensor-budget regimes, on structurally non-Gaussian benchmarks; framework is model-agnostic and applicable to a range of generative reconstruction models.

Conclusion: Distribution-free sensing with Christoffel functions provides principled, practically effective OSP compatible with modern posterior sampling methods, enabling reliable state reconstruction under sparse sensor regimes.

Abstract: State estimation is a critical task in scientific, engineering and control applications. Since the reliability of reconstructions depends on the number and position of sensors, optimal sensor placement (OSP) is essential in scenarios where measurements are sparse and expensive. Classical OSP approaches rely on Gaussian assumptions and are consequently unable to account for the complex distributions encountered in many real-world systems. Generative-model-based reconstruction using sensor guided diffusion posterior sampling (DPS) has emerged as a promising technique for reconstructing states from highly complex distributions. However, existing sensor-selection methods either require unrealistically many sensors or emulate classical OSP, creating a mismatch between modern recovery models with classical OSP tools motivating the need for fundamentally new ideas towards OSP that match the recent advances made in powerful recovery models. We introduce a distribution-free sensor placement framework based on the Christoffel function: a mathematical formulation of optimal sampling and recovery guarantees for posterior sampling with arbitrary sensors and signal distributions, from which we derive a new OSP strategy with non-asymptotic bounds on the number of sensors needed for recovery. We develop Christoffel-DPS, with offline and online variants, instantiating Christoffel sampling for generative models. Christoffel-DPS outperforms Gaussian OSP baselines and existing generative-model placement methods, validating that distribution-free sensing is both theoretically principled and practically superior. The framework is model-agnostic; we demonstrate its application to a range of unconditional DPS and flow-matching models on structurally non-Gaussian benchmarks, showing the efficacy of Christoffel-DPS in low sensor budget regimes.

</details>


### [189] [Multi-Objective Multi-Agent Bandits: From Learning Efficiency to Fairness Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06864)
*John Wang,Mengfan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Pareto UCB1 Gossip and Simulated NSW UCB Gossip for MO-MA-MAB on time-varying graphs. Achieves strong Pareto-regret bounds and NSW-based fairness, showing a trade-off between efficiency and fairness due to consensus constraints; empirical gains are substantial.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address efficient and fair learning in multi-objective, multi-agent bandit settings with heterogeneous rewards and dynamic communication networks; quantify Pareto regret for efficiency and social welfare for fairness.

Method: Develop two gossip-based UCB algorithms: Pareto UCB1 Gossip (targeting Pareto regret) and Simulated NSW UCB Gossip (targeting Nash Social Welfare). A novel exploration radius decouples statistical uncertainty from consensus error; incorporate preference-based reward simulation and gossip-based utility estimation; apply UCB-style exploration.

Result: Theoretical guarantees: Pareto UCB1 Gossip achieves O(log T) regret and instance-independent O(sqrt(T)); Simulated NSW UCB Gossip achieves instance-independent O(T^{3/4}) regret. Separation shows fairness constraints slow information aggregation. Empirical results show performance gains of ~100% in efficiency and ~50% in fairness over baselines.

Conclusion: Imposing fairness reduces aggregation efficiency but can be managed with tailored gossip-based UCB schemes. The work provides rigorous regret guarantees for MO-MA-MAB with both Pareto efficiency and NSW fairness and demonstrates practical improvements via experiments.

Abstract: We study multi-objective multi-agent multi-armed bandits (MO-MA-MAB) under stochastic rewards, where agents observe heterogeneous reward vectors and communicate over time-varying graphs. We formulate this emerging problem setting to address \emph{efficient learning}, measured by Pareto regret, and incorporate \emph{fair learning} as an additional goal, captured via social welfare. To measure efficiency, we formulate Pareto regret and develop \textsc{Pareto UCB1 Gossip}, whose novel exploration radius explicitly separates statistical uncertainty in Pareto-based inference from consensus error. To express the fairness constraint, we formulate a Nash Social Welfare objective over preference-scalarized rewards and propose \textsc{Simulated NSW UCB Gossip}, which integrates preference-based reward simulation, gossip-based utility estimation, and UCB-style exploration. We prove that \textsc{Pareto UCB1 Gossip} achieves \(\mathcal{O}(\log T)\) regret and an instance-independent rate of \(\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{T})\), while \textsc{Simulated NSW UCB Gossip} achieves an instance-independent regret bound of \(\mathcal{O}(T^{3/4})\). This separation reveals the cost of imposing the fairness constraint to our efficiency objective: fairness limits information aggregation and slows convergence. Experiments show that our methods consistently outperform baselines, improving performance by approximately \(100\%\) and \(50\%\) in the efficiency and fairness settings, respectively.

</details>


### [190] [Dataset Watermarking for Closed LLMs with Provable Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06865)
*Pengrun Huang,Kamalika Chaudhuri,Yu-Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a dataset watermarking method for closed LLMs by boosting co-occurrence of word pairs via rephrasing; enables provable detection of watermark in outputs, even when watermark is ~1% of data; preserves utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the risk that LLMs are trained on proprietary or benchmark datasets; requires verifiable evidence that a model was trained on a watermarked dataset; extend watermarking to closed models with provable detection.

Method: Embed a dataset-level watermark by increasing the co-occurrence frequency of randomly selected word pairs through rephrasing; during fine-tuning, watermark tokens; detection via a statistical test on co-occurrence patterns in generated outputs; robust in data mixture settings; open to multiple base models and datasets.

Result: Watermark reliably detected (p<0.01) in the fine-tuning stage; remains effective when watermarked data is ~1% of total fine-tuning tokens; maintains semantic integrity and benchmark utility.

Conclusion: First dataset watermarking approach for closed LLMs with provable detection; demonstrates practicality and robustness; suggests utility for dataset attribution and compliance auditing.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are pre-trained and post-trained on vast amounts of loosely curated data, raising the possibility that these models may have been trained on proprietary datasets or the same benchmarks used for evaluation. This motivates the need for dataset watermarking: designing datasets such that training on them leaves detectable signatures in the resulting model. Prior work has explored this problem for open models. We introduce the first dataset watermarking method for closed LLMs with provable detection. In particular, we embed a dataset-level watermark signal by increasing the co-occurrence frequency of randomly selected word pairs through rephrasing, and detect it using a statistical test on co-occurrence patterns in model-generated outputs. We evaluate our method with multiple base models and benchmark datasets and show that it reliably detects the watermark ($p <0.01$) in the fine-tuning stage. Notably, our method remains effective in a data mixture setting where the watermarked dataset constitutes only approximately $1\%$ of the total fine-tuning tokens. Furthermore, we show that our method preserves the utility and semantic integrity of the benchmark.

</details>


### [191] [Drifting Field Policy: A One-Step Generative Policy via Wasserstein Gradient Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07727)
*Juil Koo,Mingue Park,Jiwon Choi,Yunhong Min,Minhyuk Sung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Drifting Field Policy (DFP): a non-ODE, one-step generative policy updating via reverse-KL Wasserstein-2 flow toward a soft target, combining ascent to high-Q regions with anchor-policy score matching, plus a tractable BC-like surrogate; achieves state-of-the-art one-step results on Robomimic/OGBench vs ODE-based policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address inefficiencies of ODE-based generative policies by introducing a non-ODE, one-step update that blends probability-space gradient flow with a stable trust-region mechanism; aim for tractable optimization and improved manipulation performance.

Method: Frame policy update as a reverse-KL Wasserstein-2 gradient flow toward a soft target policy; decompose the gradient into ascent toward higher action-value regions and score matching to the anchor policy as a trust region; derive a tractable surrogate loss akin to behavior cloning on top-K critic-selected actions; leverage a drifting backbone with non-ODE parameterization; enable one-step inference.

Result: Empirically achieves state-of-the-art performance on manipulation tasks across Robomimic and OGBench with one-step inference, outperforming ODE-based policies.

Conclusion: DFP's non-ODE, one-step approach with a tractable surrogate and probability-space gradient flow yields strong manipulation performance, highlighting the effectiveness of drifting-backbone generative policies over traditional ODE-based methods.

Abstract: We propose Drifting Field Policy (DFP), a non-ODE one-step generative policy built on the drifting model paradigm. We frame the policy update as a reverse-KL Wasserstein-2 gradient flow toward a soft target policy, so that each DFP update corresponds to a gradient step in probability space. By construction, this gradient is decomposed into an ascent toward higher action-value regions and a score matching with the anchor policy as a trust region. We further derive a simple, tractable surrogate of the otherwise intractable update loss, akin to behavior cloning on top-K critic-selected actions. We find empirically that this mechanism uniquely benefits the drifting backbone owing to its non-ODE parameterization. With one-step inference, DFP achieves state-of-the-art performance on several manipulation tasks across Robomimic and OGBench, outperforming ODE-based policies.

</details>


### [192] [A Finite-Iteration Theory for Asynchronous Categorical Distributional Temporal-Difference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06866)
*Ege C. Kaya,Abolfazl Hashemi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Asynchronous single-state distributional TD methods in Cramér and MMD geometries with finite-iteration guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address gap between finite-iteration theory for distributional policy evaluation and practical asynchronous single-state categorical TD under online and Markovian data.

Method: Apply isometric embeddings to derive asynchronous single-state stochastic-approximation recursions that contract in a statewise supremum norm for two methods: scalar categorical TD in Cramér geometry and multivariate signed-categorical TD in MMD geometry.

Result: Finite-iteration convergence guarantees in discounted problems under i.i.d. and Markovian sampling, and in undiscounted fixed-horizon problems under i.i.d. episodic sampling.

Conclusion: Provides bridging theory linking practical distributional TD implementations with convergence guarantees for non-synchronous, single-state updates.

Abstract: Recent non-asymptotic analyses have substantially advanced the theory of distributional policy evaluation, but they largely concern synchronous full-state updates under a generative model, model-based estimators, accelerated variants, or different approximation architectures. Standard categorical temporal-difference learning is typically used in a different regime. It asynchronously performs a single-state update at each iteration and, in online settings, is driven by a Markovian trajectory. This leaves an important gap between existing finite-iteration theory and the categorical recursions most closely aligned with practical distributional temporal-difference implementations. We bridge this gap for two categorical policy-evaluation methods: scalar categorical temporal-difference learning in the Cramér geometry and multivariate signed-categorical temporal-difference learning in the maximum mean discrepancy geometry. After suitable isometric embeddings, both algorithms take the form of asynchronous single-state stochastic-approximation recursions that contract in a statewise supremum norm. This permits finite-iteration guarantees in discounted problems under both i.i.d. and Markovian state sampling, and in undiscounted fixed-horizon problems under i.i.d. episodic sampling.

</details>


### [193] [When Descent Is Too Stable: Event-Triggered Hamiltonian Learning to Optimize](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06868)
*Yi Wang,Chandrajit Bajaj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SHAPE: a structured adaptive port-Hamiltonian optimizer for fixed-budget nonconvex optimization that uses an augmented phase space (q, p) and event-triggered stages to balance descent, exploration, and budget via energy shaping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In fixed-budget nonconvex optimization, progress stalls at informative minima due to excessive stability of descent; there is a need for an adaptive method to switch between descent, exploitation, and exploration within a limited evaluation budget.

Method: Lift gradient descent to an augmented phase space; the cotangent variable p carries directional sensitivity; a learnable Hamiltonian vector field drives stage-local descent; an event clock triggers updates to ports and memory; stage horizons generalized; the design preserves passivity and supports clean, stochastic, or estimated gradients.

Result: Empirical results show SHAPE improves best-so-far performance compared with fixed-policy optimizers on fixed-budget tasks; demonstrates effective balance of descent, exploration, and budget allocation.

Conclusion: Adaptive energy-shaping via a port-Hamiltonian framework provides a principled mechanism to balance descent and exploration under budget constraints in difficult nonconvex optimization landscapes.

Abstract: Fixed-budget nonconvex optimization can fail not because local descent is unstable, but because it is too stable: after reaching a nearby stationary point, an optimizer may spend the remaining evaluations refining an uninformative local minimum. We formulate this failure mode as a control problem over optimizer dynamics, where the learner must decide when to descend, when to exploit a promising basin, and when stagnation should trigger movement elsewhere. We introduce SHAPE, a structured adaptive port-Hamiltonian task-family optimizer for event-triggered minima hunting under local information. Starting from gradient-descent dynamics, SHAPE lifts optimization to an augmented phase space $(q, p)$, where the primal state $q$ represents the candidate solution, the cotangent variable $p$ carries directional sensitivity, and a controller $u$ provides processed information from current gradient oracle. Within each stage, a learned Hamiltonian vector field induces structured local descent; across stages, a fixed event clock in the implementation updates ports and memory when local equilibria are detected, with stage-dependent horizons treated in the analysis as a direct generalization. This design preserves a passivity-compatible structure while allowing the same trained policy to use clean, stochastic, or estimated gradient inputs. Experiments on fixed-budget nonconvex optimization tasks show that SHAPE improves best-so-far performance compared with fixed-policy optimizers. These results suggest that adaptive Hamiltonian energy shaping provides a principled mechanism for balancing descent, exploration, and budget allocation in difficult optimization landscapes.

</details>


### [194] [Continuous First, Discrete Later: VQ-VAEs Without Dimensional Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06870)
*Xinyu Zhao,Nikita Karagodin,Hamed Hassani,Sinan Hersek,Paul Pu Liang,Yury Polyanskiy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dimensional collapse in VQ-VAE imposes a hard loss floor; an unquantized autoencoder warm-up before VQ training mitigates collapse, restores latent dimensionality, and yields substantial improvements in reconstruction and perceptual metrics across codebook sizes in image (VQGAN) and audio (WavTokenizer) tasks, with theory guiding warm-up length.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In VQ-VAEs, training often pushes representations into a very low-rank subspace, suppressing lower-variance directions and creating a hard loss lower bound that existing codebook improvements cannot surpass.

Method: Develop a theoretical analysis extending Saxe et al. sequential learning with rate-distortion theory. Propose AE Warm-Up: train as an unquantized autoencoder before introducing vector quantization. Validate on synthetic data and large-scale models (image: VQGAN; audio: WavTokenizer). Evaluate across codebook sizes K in {2^10, 2^14, 2^16} (image) and K in {2^13, 2^14} (audio). Measure latent dimensionality, rFID, PESQ, and final loss; study warm-up duration and derive an adaptive switching criterion.

Result: AE Warm-Up increases the codebook effective dimension from ~3–5 to ~17–19; reduces rFID by 17–35% in VQGAN; improves PESQ by 11–14% in WavTokenizer; achieves lower reconstruction and perceptual loss at the same training budget; theory predicts downstream performance as a function of warm-up length.

Conclusion: A simple, effective remedy for latent dimension collapse in VQ-VAEs. The proposed warm-up aligns with theory, enables adaptive switching between autoencoder warm-up and VQ-VAE training, and yields consistent improvements across multimodal tasks and varying codebook sizes.

Abstract: While many approaches to improve VQ-VAE performance focus on codebook size and utilization, the effect of dimensional collapse, where trained VQ-VAE representations live in an extremely low-dimensional subspace (1-2% of full rank), remains unaddressed. We show theoretically and empirically that dimension collapse causes a hard loss lower bound that various codebook improvement techniques fail to surpass. Our analytic framework extends the sequential learning effect of Saxe et al. [2014] by introducing ideas from rate-distortion theory and explains how the latent collapse is caused by the VQ suppressing lower-variance directions. Our theory justifies a simple solution: a "warm-up phase" that trains the model as an (unquantized) autoencoder before introducing VQ. On both synthetic experiments and large-scale image (VQGAN) and audio (WavTokenizer) VQ-VAEs, we show that AE Warm-Up successfully restores representation dimension, leading to lower reconstruction and perceptual loss at the same training budget. Across codebook sizes $K \in$ {$2^{10}, 2^{14}, 2^{16}$}, AE warm-up raises VQGAN codebook effective dimension from 3-5 to 17-19 and reduces rFID by 17-35%; on WavTokenizer at $K \in$ {$2^{13}, 2^{14}$}, it raises codebook dimension from 4 to 17-19 and improves PESQ by 11-14%. We empirically characterize how warm-up duration governs the achievable final loss. In agreement with experiment, our theoretical analysis predicts downstream performance as a function of warm-up length, enabling an adaptive criterion for switching from AE Warm-up to VQ-VAE training.

</details>


### [195] [On the Divergence of Differential Temporal Difference Learning without Local Clocks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06874)
*David Antrobius,Shangtong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In discounted RL, convergence with local and global clocks coincides; in average-reward RL, this equivalence breaks: a counterexample shows differential TD converges under a local clock but diverges under a global clock, resolving open questions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify how learning-rate schedules (global vs local clocks) affect convergence in average-reward RL and contrast with the known results for discounted RL.

Method: Construct a theoretical counterexample demonstrating divergence under a global clock for differential TD learning in average-reward RL while preserving convergence under a local clock.

Result: Demonstrates breakdown of clock-based convergence equivalence in average-reward RL; local-clock differential TD can converge while global-clock version diverges, solving open problems from Wan et al. (2021) and Blaser et al. (2026).

Conclusion: The equivalence between local and global learning-rate schedules does not hold in average-reward RL, unlike discounted RL; this has implications for algorithm design and theoretical guarantees in average-reward settings.

Abstract: Learning rate is a critical component of reinforcement learning (RL). This work uses global and local clocks to distinguish two types of learning rates. The former is of the standard form $α_t$ that depends only on the time step $t$ (i.e., a global clock). The latter is of the form $α_{ν(S_t, t)}$, where $ν(s, t)$ counts the number of visits to state $s$ until time $t$ (i.e., a local clock). In discounted RL, an RL algorithm that is convergent with a local clock is always also convergent with a global clock, and vice versa. We are not aware of any counterexample. The key contribution of this work is to show that this nice correspondence breaks down in average-reward RL. Specifically, we construct a counterexample showing that although differential temporal difference learning is convergent with a local clock, it can diverge with a global clock. This counterexample closes the open problem in Wan et al. [2021], Blaser et al. [2026].

</details>


### [196] [Temporal Attention for Adaptive Control of Euler-Lagrange Systems with Unobservable Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06877)
*Giansalvo Cirrincione,Adriano Fagiolini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Attention-based meta-control for computed-torque control in non-Markovian EL systems with hidden friction; head-count chosen via rank-tracking surrogate on short history, fixed during RL; robust in short memory but fragile in long memory; suggests online head adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle adaptive control when friction is governed by a finite-horizon, unobservable internal state, making joint measurements non-Markovian and breaking certainty-equivalence guarantees.

Method: Propose a meta-control architecture where gains of a computed-torque controller are produced by a self-attention block over a short motion-history window. The number of attention heads is selected a priori using a surrogate based on autocovariance of memory-state gradient via a temporal adaptation of an incremental rank-tracking framework. The chosen head count is fixed and used as a hyperparameter in a subsequent RL stage with a shielded admissibility constraint.

Result: Empirical evaluation on a 2-DOF manipulator with nonlinear friction and variable payload shows: in short/matched memory regimes, an attention-only meta-controller outperforms a deeper Transformer baseline, with tracking-error reductions of 12 and 19 percentage points and large effect sizes (d≈−1.1 and −2.1; p<0.05). In the long-memory regime, the advantage vanishes; 4 of 10 trials diverge or yield payload-invariant policy collapse, indicating the static Phase-1 head-count is weak.

Conclusion: Static pretraining of attention-head count can be insufficient for non-Markovian control; integrating rank-tracking into the RL loop to prune or grow heads online (instead of fixing before training) is a promising direction to maintain stability and performance across memory regimes.

Abstract: Adaptive control of Euler-Lagrange systems is challenging when friction is governed by a finite-horizon internal state that is not directly observable from joint measurements. In this setting, the measured closed-loop state is no longer Markovian, and standard certainty-equivalence adaptive laws may lose their convergence guarantees.
  The paper proposes a meta-control architecture in which the gains of a computed-torque controller are generated by a self-attention block processing a short window of recent motion history. The number of attention heads is selected before policy training through a surrogate analysis of the autocovariance of the memory-state gradient along the temporal window. This surrogate is based on a temporal adaptation of an incremental rank-tracking framework previously developed by the authors. The selected head count is then fixed and used as an architectural hyperparameter in a reinforcement-learning stage, where the policy is trained under a shielded admissibility constraint.
  The approach is tested on a 2-DOF manipulator with nonlinear friction and variable payload. In the short and matched memory regimes, the single-layer attention-only meta-controller outperforms a deeper Transformer baseline, with tracking-error reductions of 12 and 19 percentage points, respectively. The reported effect sizes are large, with d approximately -1.1 and -2.1, and Mann-Whitney p < 0.05 in both cases.
  In the long memory regime, however, the advantage disappears. Four out of ten training runs show either divergence or payload-invariant policy collapse, revealing a weakness in the static Phase-1 head-count prescription. This motivates moving rank-tracking inside the reinforcement-learning loop, allowing attention heads to be pruned or grown at runtime instead of fixed before training.

</details>


### [197] [Better Protein Function Prediction by Modeling Survivorship Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06879)
*Zhongmou Chao,Poompol Buathong,Ekaterina Selivanovitch,Susan Daniel,Peter I. Frazier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evo-PU is a positive-unlabeled learning framework that accounts for survivorship bias in protein sequence data by incorporating mutation-based observability models, improving predictions on single-organism surveillance tasks and offering insights for multi-organism data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting protein functionality from sequences faces survivorship bias and missing data; standard PU methods ignore evolutionary observability, leading to biased learning.

Method: Proposes Evo-PU: integrate nucleotide mutation process into PU learning to model survivorship bias; use single-organism uniform-coverage surveillance data for three tasks (influenza, RSV mutagenesis held-out predictions, SARS-CoV-2 variants); compare to PU, OCC, PLMs; analyze generalization to multi-organism ProteinGym.

Result: Evo-PU outperforms standard PU, OCC, and PLMs on single-organism tasks; potential generalization to multi-organism requires further opportunities.

Conclusion: Modeling evolutionary observability reduces survivorship bias, improving functional prediction from sequence data; extensible framework with generalization challenges across surveillance heterogeneity.

Abstract: Protein sequence data from nature exhibits survivorship bias: we only observe data from those organisms that survive and reproduce, while non-functional protein mutations are eliminated by natural selection. Thus, predicting whether a protein sequence is functional often requires learning from positive examples alone. While positive-unlabeled (PU) learning frameworks offer a generic solution to this problem, existing PU methods ignore the evolutionary processes that shape sequence observability and cause survivorship bias. Consider a sequence that is one mutation away from a commonly-observed protein variant in a well-surveilled organism. If the sequence were functional, it would likely be observed. If it is not observed, this suggests non-functionality. In contrast, sequences that are unlikely to arise through mutation may be missing simply because they never arose. Thus, these two kinds of missing sequences should be treated differently when training models. In this work, we propose Evo-PU, a PU learning framework that uses a scientific understanding of nucleotide mutation to model survivorship bias for well-surveilled single-organism sequence data. On three prediction tasks using single-organism uniform-coverage surveillance data -- predicting results from held-out influenza and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) mutagenesis studies, and predicting future SARS-CoV-2 variants -- Evo-PU outperforms standard PU learning, one-class classification (OCC), and protein language models (PLMs). On prediction tasks from multi-organism ProteinGym datasets with more heterogeneous surveillance coverage, we identify opportunities to generalize our approach.

</details>


### [198] [Don't Retrain, Align: Adapting Autoregressive LMs to Diffusion LMs via Representation Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06885)
*Fred Zhangzhi Peng,Alexis Fox,Anru R. Zhang,Alexander Tong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Represents a simple AR-to-DLM transfer via representation alignment: REPR-ALIGN aligns diffusion language model hidden states with a frozen autoregressive model to preserve semantic representations, yielding faster training and strong performance in low-data settings without adapters or architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether the semantic and representational geometry learned by next-token prediction can transfer across generation orders, treating diffusion model training as relearning the decoding path rather than language representations.

Method: Introduce REPR-ALIGN: align DLM hidden states to the frozen AR model at every layer using cosine similarity while optimizing the standard masked denoising objective. No adapters or architecture changes beyond the attention mask.

Result: Up to 4x training acceleration in the authors' setting, with strong effectiveness in low-data regimes; suggests linguistic representations transfer across generation orders; simple and effective training technique with available code.

Conclusion: Representation alignment can preserve and leverage pretrained AR representations during AR-to-DLM conversion, enabling faster diffusion model training and reinforcing the view that diffusion models can exploit AR-derived linguistic structure without additional adapters or architectural changes.

Abstract: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have recently demonstrated capabilities that complement standard autoregressive (AR) models, particularly in non-sequential generation and bidirectional editing. Although recent work has shown that pretrained autoregressive checkpoints can be converted into diffusion language models, existing recipes primarily transfer parameters through continued denoising training with objective- and attention-level modifications. We instead ask whether the internal representation geometry learned by next-token prediction can be explicitly preserved during AR-to-DLM conversion. We hypothesize that much of the semantic structure learned by AR pretraining can transfer across generation orders, and thus DLM training should be viewed as relearning the decoding path rather than relearning language representations. To investigate this, we introduce REPR-ALIGN, a representation alignment objective that adapts a bidirectional masked diffusion model to reuse representations from a pretrained AR model of identical architecture. Concretely, we align the hidden states of the DLM to the frozen AR model at every layer using cosine similarity, while optimizing the standard masked denoising objective. This simple alignment, with no adapters and no architectural changes beyond the attention mask, yields up to 4x training acceleration in our setting and is particularly effective in low-data regimes. Our results suggest that linguistic representations can transfer across generation order, and that representation alignment provides a simple and effective technique for training diffusion language models. Code is available at https://github.com/pengzhangzhi/Open-dLLM.

</details>


### [199] [Streaming Adversarial Robustness in Fuzzy ARTMAP: Mechanism-Aligned Evaluation, Progressive Training, and Interpretable Diagnostics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06902)
*Shane Cairns,Leonardo Enzo Brito da Silva,Sasha Petrenko,Donald C. Wunsch,Jian Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WB-Softmax yields high attack success on vanilla Fuzzy ARTMAP and reveals protocol-dependent robustness in streaming learners; progressive selective training offers strongest replay-free robustness; interpretable diagnostics arise from ARTMAP geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore adversarial robustness for strict single-pass streaming learners (ART-based) where offline defenses may not transfer; establish a protocol-aware robustness evaluation framework for streaming prototype-based models.

Method: Introduce WB-Softmax, a differentiable white-box attack surrogate aligned with ARTMAP's category-competition and map-field prediction; formalize a streaming evaluation principle requiring robustness to be evaluated on the final deployed model; validate on four image benchmarks, comparing attack and defense protocols.

Result: WB-Softmax achieves 89-100% attack success on vanilla Fuzzy ARTMAP models; defense rankings can reverse across protocols (offline adversarial training may appear strong under transfer but collapse under adaptive white-box evaluation); progressive two-stage selective training provides the strongest overall replay-free robustness; ART's category geometry enables interpretable diagnosis of separation collapse and match-score inversion.

Conclusion: Presents a mechanism-aligned, protocol-aware framework for adversarial robustness in streaming prototype-based learners.

Abstract: Adversarial robustness has been studied extensively for offline deep networks, but less is known about strict single-pass streaming neural learners. This paper studies adversarial robustness in Fuzzy ARTMAP, an Adaptive Resonance Theory architecture based on category competition, complement coding, match tracking, and replay-free prototype updates. We introduce WB-Softmax, a differentiable white-box attack surrogate aligned with ARTMAP's category-competition and map-field prediction mechanism, and formalize a streaming evaluation principle requiring robustness to be assessed on the final deployed model. Across four image benchmarks, WB-Softmax achieves 89-100% attack success on vanilla Fuzzy ARTMAP models. We show that defense rankings can reverse across protocols: offline adversarial training may appear strong under transfer attacks yet collapse under adaptive white-box evaluation, whereas progressive two-stage selective training provides the strongest overall replay-free robustness. We further show that ART's explicit category geometry enables interpretable diagnosis of separation collapse and match-score inversion. These results provide a mechanism-aligned, protocol-aware framework for adversarial robustness in streaming prototype-based learners.

</details>


### [200] [Conservative Flows: A New Paradigm of Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06905)
*Eshed Gal,Md Shahriar Rahim Siddiqui,Moshe Eliasof,Eldad Haber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A data-invariant discrete stochastic sampling framework for pretrained flow models that starts from data-supported states and uses Metropolis-corrected Langevin dynamics or a predictor-corrector flow to sample, improving generation on Swiss-roll, ImageNet-256, and Flowers-102.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address reliance on noise-based generation by diffusion; enable probability-preserving sampling that preserves the data distribution and leverages existing checkpoints.

Method: Two sampling schemes: (i) corrected Langevin dynamics with a Metropolis adjustment; (ii) predictor-corrector flow. Both operate directly on pretrained flow checkpoints, initialized from data-supported states, ensuring the data distribution remains invariant.

Result: Empirical validation on synthetic and real datasets shows consistent improvements over original generation procedures.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that discrete stochastic dynamics preserving the data distribution can effectively augment or replace standard noise-driven generation when using pretrained flow models; broad applicability and potential gains in fidelity and efficiency.

Abstract: Modern generative modeling is dominated by transport from a noise prior to data. We propose an alternative paradigm in which generation is performed by a discrete stochastic dynamics that leaves the data distribution invariant, initialized from data-supported states rather than from noise. The framework can utilize any pretrained flow model. We develop two probability-preserving sampling mechanisms, a corrected Langevin dynamics with a Metropolis adjustment and a predictor-corrector flow, that operate directly on existing checkpoints. We validate the framework on a synthetic Swiss-roll target, ImageNet-256 and Oxford Flowers-102, where our samplers consistently improve over the original generation procedures.

</details>


### [201] [TraXion: Rethinking Pre-training Frameworks for Mobility and Beyond](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06906)
*Shang-Ling Hsu,Mark Tenzer,Cyrus Shahabi,Khurram Shafique*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TraXion offers a unified pretraining framework for multi-entity spatiotemporal event streams (MESES). It defines three axioms and shows a single TraXion model per dataset outperforms task-specific baselines across six mobility tasks and transfers to enterprise security and ICU mortality domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mobility pretraining repurposes language-modeling objectives that treat trajectories as sentences and visits as tokens, failing to honor the three structural properties of MESES (joint location-time-activity semantics, persistent user signatures, and cross-user co-location as signal). A general MESES framework is needed to model multi-entity, shared infrastructure domains such as mobility, security, and healthcare.

Method: Define three axioms capturing MESES properties; design TraXion with objectives and architecture aligned to these axioms; train a single TraXion checkpoint per dataset; evaluate on mobility tasks (anomaly detection, next-POI, next-visit, social-link prediction) and transfer to enterprise authentication logs and ICU mortality prediction.

Result: TraXion achieves state-of-the-art performance on six mobility datasets across multiple tasks, beating task-specific baselines; the same approach matches or exceeds prior work on enterprise authentication logs and ICU mortality, demonstrating cross-domain applicability of a unified MESES framework.

Conclusion: A principled, axioms-driven MESES framework (TraXion) can model diverse, non-independent event streams with cross-entity interactions, yielding strong results within mobility and generalizing to security and healthcare domains.

Abstract: Human mobility differs from text and from generic time series in three structural ways: visits are tuple-valued events whose meaning depends on the joint distribution over location, time, and activity; users carry persistent signatures across trajectories; and visits are not independent across users, since co-location at shared places is a primary signal. Existing pre-training recipes for mobility import objectives from language modeling, treating trajectories as sentences and visits as tokens, an analogy that fails against each of the three properties above. These properties define a broader class, multi-entity spatiotemporal event streams (MESES), spanning enterprise authentication logs, electronic health records, and other event-stream domains where entities share infrastructure, schedules, or contexts. We make the properties precise as three axioms that any pre-training framework for MESES should satisfy, and introduce TraXion, whose objectives and architecture are jointly designed to meet them. A single TraXion checkpoint per dataset beats task-specific baselines on every task across six public mobility datasets covering anomaly detection, next-POI recommendation, next-visit prediction, and social-link prediction. The same recipe, applied unchanged to enterprise authentication logs and ICU mortality prediction, matches or exceeds prior work on both, showing that event streams from domains as different as mobility, security, and healthcare can be modeled under a single framework.

</details>


### [202] [Same Signal, Opposite Meaning: Direction-Informed Adaptive Learning for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06908)
*Ziming Li,Jiatan Huang,Xiaoguang Guo,Guilin Wang,Chuxu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DIAL learns the direction of compute benefit with a direction-informed adaptive gate, trained via counterfactual exploration, to improve test-time compute allocation for LLM agents beyond fixed-direction gates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gating signals (confidence/uncertainty/difficulty) assumed a fixed direction from signal to compute needs. This alignment is unstable across environments/backbones; wrong-direction gates can harm performance by selecting harmful states.

Method: Introduce DIAL (Direction-Informed Adaptive Learning), a sparse gate trained in a signal-agnostic counterfactual exploration framework to infer the utility direction of state features for each environment and backbone.

Result: DIAL achieves a stronger overall success-cost trade-off than fixed-direction baselines across six environments and three backbones.

Conclusion: Effective gating requires modeling compute need and compute suitability as two sources; a learned direction-aware gate via counterfactual exploration improves robustness and performance across heterogeneous settings.

Abstract: Adaptive test-time compute for LLM agents aims to invoke extra computation only when it improves performance. Existing methods typically use confidence-, uncertainty-, or difficulty-based gates, assuming a fixed direction from the gating signal through compute need to the value of computation. This makes gating a utility-calibration problem: gating signals should align with whether extra computation improves the final outcome over the base policy. We show that this alignment is unstable: the same signal predicts rollout benefit in one setting and rollout harm in another, with reversals across environments and backbones even when the task is fixed. Wrong-direction gates can therefore worsen performance by precisely selecting harmful states. This reversal reflects a deeper distinction between compute need and compute suitability: a high uncertainty signal may indicate decision-difficult states where rollouts help compare alternatives, or intervention-unsuitable states where the current context does not support useful rollout-based improvement. Under this two-source model, fixed-direction gates are unreliable across heterogeneous settings. To address this, we propose DIAL (Direction-Informed Adaptive Learning), a sparse gate trained from signal-agnostic counterfactual exploration to learn the utility direction of state features per (environment, backbone). Across six environments and three backbones, DIAL yields a stronger overall success-cost trade-off than fixed-direction baselines.

</details>


### [203] [Dual-Scale Temporal Fusion Reveals Structured Predictability in Subseasonal-to-Seasonal Temperature Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06911)
*Elnaz Bashir,Jiali Wang,Lin Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dual-scale, spatially adaptive framework for subseasonal-to-seasonal (S2S) temperature forecasts that separates calendar-aligned climate context from lead-time matched recent weather, fuses them via spatially varying weights, and enforces topology-aware coherence to yield stable 30–90 day forecasts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecast skill for S2S temperatures varies across seasons and regions and cannot be explained by lead-time decay alone. A structured, multi-scale view of predictability is needed to improve interpretability and practical use in agriculture, energy, and risk management.

Method: Develop a dual-scale learning framework that (1) extracts calendar-aligned historical climate context (interannual/seasonal patterns) and (2) captures lead-time matched recent weather evolution. These components are fused with spatially adaptive weights. Topology-aware structural constraints are applied to enhance spatial coherence, especially over complex terrain.

Result: The framework yields stable 30–90 day forecasts.
