<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL with continuous or partially observed environments often violate the Markov property; causally informed representations can in principle recover Markovian states from observed variables, but a clear construction of provably Markovian, minimal state representations is lacking. The work addresses how to derive such states from a longitudinal causal graph and why minimal sufficiency alone is insufficient for deep RL.

Method: 1) Propose a procedure to construct provably minimal state representations from a longitudinal causal graph over observed variables. 2) Introduce MOSE (Multi-Order State Exposure): feed multiple order historical state constructions (e.g., varying time windows) into the same Q-network. 3) Empirically compare against minimal-state baselines and single-window policies across standard RL benchmarks and synthetic datasets; test the benefit of including the minimal representation alongside MOSE.

Result: MOSE consistently outperforms both the minimal-state construction and single-window policies across benchmarks and synthetic datasets. The minimal representation alone does not improve performance in deep RL, indicating neural networks cannot exploit minimal Markovian sufficiency directly. Including the minimal state with MOSE yields further gains.

Conclusion: Minimal sufficiency is not enough for enabling causal state information in deep RL; controlled redundancy via multi-order state exposure is essential to unlock benefits from causal state information. MOSE provides a practical method to construct and leverage richer state representations, suggesting guidelines for designing state representations in causal deep RL and potentially improving generalization and sample efficiency.

Abstract: Online reinforcement learning (RL) relies on the Markov property for guaranteed performance, but real-world applications often lack well-defined states given raw observed variables. While causal RL has attracted growing interest, existing work typically assumes Markovian states are provided and focuses on using causality to accelerate learning, leaving a fundamental gap: \emph{given a longitudinal causal graph over observed variables, how does one construct MDP states that provably satisfy the Markov property?} We address this by providing a procedure that constructs a provably minimal state representation. In deep RL, we observe that the minimal representation alone empirically fails to improve performance, indicating that neural networks cannot directly exploit Markovian minimality. To address this, we propose \textbf{MOSE} (Multi-Order State Exposure), which feeds multi-order historical state constructions into the same $Q$-function. MOSE consistently outperforms both the minimal state construction and single-window policies on common benchmarks and synthetic datasets. Including the minimal representation alongside MOSE can further improve performance. Our results establish a core principle for causal deep RL: minimal sufficiency is not enough, and \emph{controlled redundancy} is necessary to unlock the benefit of causal state information.

</details>


### [230] [Dr. Post-Training: A Data Regularization Perspective on LLM Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07063)
*Pingbang Hu,Xueshen Liu,Z. Morley Mao,Jiaqi W. Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Dr. Post-Training, a data-regularized post-training framework that treats general training data as a regularizer to prevent overfitting to scarce target data, via projecting target-update directions onto a feasible set derived from general data; yields richer bias–variance tradeoffs and improved efficiency for LLM post-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data selection for scarce, high-fidelity target data is suboptimal; a data-induced regularizer can better regulate learning than filtering data, unifying and extending existing data-selection methods along a bias–variance spectrum.

Method: At each training step, build a feasible set of update directions from general data and project the scarce target-data update onto this set. Introduce a family of data-induced regularizers; reinterpret standard training and data-selection techniques as special cases; optimize for large-scale LLMs with system-level improvements.

Result: Consistent improvements over state-of-the-art data-selection baselines across supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), and RL with value-based objectives (RLVR); demonstrates efficiency with minimal overhead.

Conclusion: Reframes data usage in post-training as a configurable regularization problem, opening a richer design space for bias–variance control and practical deployment in large-scale models.

Abstract: Data selection methods address a critical challenge in LLM post-training: effectively leveraging scarce, high-fidelity target data alongside abundant but imperfectly aligned general training data. In this work, we move beyond the data-selection framing and introduce Dr. Post-Training (Data-Regularized Post-Training), a novel framework that reconceptualizes general training data as a data-induced regularizer that prevents overfitting to the scarce target objective, rather than serving as a pool for selection. Specifically, our framework proposes that at each training step, construct a feasible set of model update directions using the general training data, and project the model update direction specified by the scarce target data onto that feasible set. Standard training and existing data selection methods arise as special cases with different choices of the data-induced regularizer, and these methods correspond to different points on a bias--variance spectrum with different regularization strength. Building on this view, we propose a family of methods offering a richer design space and more flexible bias--variance tradeoffs. For practical LLM-scale use, we introduce careful system optimizations that realize these methods with minimal overhead. Extensive experiments across SFT, RLHF, and RLVR show that our methods consistently outperform state-of-the-art data selection baselines, and system benchmarks confirm their efficiency.

</details>


### [231] [PolarAdamW: Disentangling Spectral Control and Schur Gauge-Equivariance in Matrix Optimisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07067)
*Haozhou Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PolarAdamW is a hybrid optimizer that separates Muon’s spectral-control polar map from AdamW’s preconditioning, achieving different performance depending on multiplicity-basis freedom; demonstrates a double dissociation between spectral control and Schur gauge-equivariance across DeiT-Tiny and SO(3) tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To decouple spectral control from gauge-equivariance in matrix-level updates to leverage AdamW preconditioning while preserving or controlling spectral norm; to understand how multiplicity-basis freedom influences optimization in standard transformers versus equivariant networks.

Method: Introduce PolarAdamW by applying Muon’s Newton–Schulz polar map to the AdamW preconditioned direction on a per-iteration basis. Empirically evaluate on DeiT-Tiny (from-scratch, four 100-class ImageNet-1k subsets) and on SO(3)-equivariant 3D point-cloud regression. Compare against Muon and AdamW, analyze Schur gauge-equivariance vs coordinatewise preconditioning, and discuss the role of multiplicity-basis freedom.

Result: On DeiT-Tiny subsets, PolarAdamW outperforms Muon by +1.93 percentage points (pp) in test accuracy on average and AdamW by +9.5 pp. Under a 300-epoch DeiT-like recipe, PolarAdamW remains ahead of Muon by +1.37 pp and ahead of AdamW by +5.80 pp on average. On SO(3)-equivariant 3D regression, where multiplicity-basis freedom is non-trivial, Muon outperforms PolarAdamW at all audited capacities, with the gap widening with capacity; both matrix-polar optimisers outperform AdamW.

Conclusion: The results reveal a double dissociation between spectral control and Schur gauge-equivariance. Spectral control via Muon’s polar map composes well with AdamW preconditioning on standard transformers, while gauge-equivariance becomes consequential when multiplicity-basis freedom is structurally non-trivial (as in SO(3) equivariant networks). This suggests task- and structure-dependent optimizer choices: PolarAdamW for spectral-control-friendly, standard architectures; Muon for multiplicity-basis-rich, equivariant settings; both outperforming AdamW.

Abstract: Muon's matrix-level update couples two distinct effects: spectral control via a polar map, and equivariance under orthogonal changes of multiplicity-space basis (Schur gauge-equivariance). We separate them with PolarAdamW, a controlled hybrid that preserves Muon's polar spectral-norm control but breaks the gauge-equivariance, since AdamW's coordinatewise preconditioner is basis-dependent. Algorithmically, PolarAdamW applies Muon's Newton-Schulz polar map to AdamW's preconditioned direction rather than to raw momentum, at per-iteration wall-time comparable to Muon. We prove that Muon's polar step is Schur gauge-equivariant on multiplicity matrices while AdamW's coordinatewise step is not.
  On DeiT-Tiny trained from scratch on four independently sampled 100-class subsets of ImageNet-1k, where multiplicity-basis freedom is trivial, PolarAdamW outperforms Muon by +1.93 pp in test accuracy on average and AdamW by +9.5 pp; under the 300-epoch DeiT-style recipe, it remains ahead of Muon by +1.37 pp and AdamW by +5.80 pp on average. On SO(3)-equivariant 3D point-cloud regression, where multiplicity-basis freedom is non-trivial, the ordering reverses: Muon outperforms PolarAdamW at every audited capacity, and the gap widens with capacity. Both matrix-polar optimisers continue to outperform AdamW. This double dissociation separates spectral control from Schur gauge-equivariance: the first composes well with AdamW preconditioning on standard transformers, while the second becomes consequential when multiplicity-basis freedom is structurally non-trivial.

</details>


### [232] [Less Random, More Private: What is the Optimal Subsampling Scheme for DP-SGD?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07072)
*Andy Dong,Ayfer Özgür*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Balanced Iteration Subsampling (BIS) fixes per-sample participation to a fixed count, yielding stronger DP-SGD privacy amplification than Poisson subsampling and is asymptotically optimal at both low and high noise; a near-exact Monte Carlo accountant enables finite-noise guarantees; empirical results show up to 9.6% noise reduction in low-noise regimes; practical code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Poisson subsampling introduces large participation variance, which limits privacy amplification despite its randomness; reducing participation variance while keeping uniform marginal participation could improve the privacy-utility tradeoff in DP-SGD.

Method: Introduce BIS, a structured subsampling scheme where each data point participates in exactly a fixed number of iterations. Develop theoretical analysis showing stronger amplification and asymptotic optimality at σ→0 and σ→∞. Provide a near-exact Monte Carlo accountant for BIS to obtain finite-noise guarantees, outperforming RDP/PLD approaches. Empirically evaluate across 60+ DP-SGD configurations comparing BIS to Poisson subsampling.

Result: BIS achieves stronger privacy amplification than Poisson subsampling; reduces the required noise multiplier by up to 9.6% in low-noise regimes; demonstrates that eliminating participation variance can be more beneficial than maximizing sampling randomness; provides practical Monte Carlo accountant and release-ready implementation.

Conclusion: Eliminating participation variance while maintaining uniform marginal participation improves the privacy-noise tradeoff in DP-SGD. BIS is near-optimally private in finite-noise regimes, offering practical advantages and enabling more private, high-utility training. An implementation is provided via the linked GitHub repository.

Abstract: Poisson subsampling is the default sampling scheme in differentially private machine learning, largely because its unstructured randomness yields tractable privacy amplification analyses. Yet this same randomness introduces substantial participation variance: each sample appears in very different numbers of training iterations. In this work, we show that this variance is not merely a practical artifact to be tolerated, but a fundamental source of suboptimal privacy amplification. We prove that Balanced Iteration Subsampling (BIS), a structured scheme in which each sample participates in exactly a fixed number of iterations, achieves stronger privacy amplification than Poisson subsampling and is optimal at both extremes of the noise spectrum ($σ\to 0$ and $σ\to \infty$). Our analysis reveals that the privacy-noise tradeoff is governed not by maximizing randomness, but by eliminating participation variance while preserving uniform marginal participation across iterations. To translate this asymptotic theory into finite-noise guarantees, we introduce a practical near-exact Monte Carlo accountant for BIS, which removes the analytical slack of existing RDP and composition-based PLD analyses. Evaluations across more than 60 practical DP-SGD configurations show that BIS consistently outperforms Poisson subsampling in the low-noise regimes most relevant for high-utility private training, reducing the required noise multiplier by up to $9.6\%$. These results overturn the common intuition that more sampling randomness necessarily yields stronger privacy amplification: in DP-SGD, structured participation can be both more practical and more private. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/dong-xin-ao-andy/bis-mc-accountant.

</details>


### [233] [ModelLens: Finding the Best for Your Task from Myriads of Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07075)
*Rui Cai,Weijie Jacky Mo,Xiaofei Wen,Qiyao Ma,Wenhui Zhu,Xiwen Chen,Muhao Chen,Zhe Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ModelLens learns a performance-aware latent space from leaderboard data to rank unseen models on unseen datasets without costly evaluations, outperforming baselines and boosting routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The open-source model ecosystem has exploded; selecting the right model for a dataset is infeasible; existing methods cover only fragments.

Method: Construct a latent space over (model, dataset, metric) tuples using public leaderboard interactions; train to predict performance; rank unseen models for new datasets; provide Top-K candidate pools to enhance routing methods.

Result: On a large benchmark (1.62M records, 47K models, 9.6K datasets), ModelLens outperforms metadata-only or per-model-forward-pass baselines; Top-K pools improve routing methods by up to 81% across QA benchmarks; generalizes to text and vision-language tasks.

Conclusion: Demonstrates scalable, generalizable model recommendation in-the-wild via noisy leaderboard signals, enabling effective model selection without runtime evaluation on the target dataset.

Abstract: The open-source model ecosystem now contains hundreds of thousands of pretrained models, yet picking the best model for a new dataset is increasingly infeasible: new models and unbenchmarked datasets emerge continuously, leaving practitioners with no prior records on either side. Existing approaches handle only fragments of this in-the-wild setting: AutoML and transferability estimation select models from small predefined pools or require expensive per-model forward passes on the target dataset, while model routing presupposes a given candidate pool. We introduce ModelLens, a unified framework for model recommendation in the wild. Our key insight is that public leaderboard interactions, though scattered and noisy, collectively trace out an implicit atlas of model capabilities across heterogeneous evaluation settings, a signal rich enough to learn from directly. By learning a performance-aware latent space over model--dataset--metric tuples, ModelLens ranks unseen models on unseen datasets without running candidates on the target dataset. On a new benchmark of 1.62M evaluation records spanning 47K models and 9.6K datasets, ModelLens surpasses baselines that either rely on metadata alone or require running each candidate on the target dataset. Its recommended Top-K pools further improve multiple representative routing methods by up to 81% across diverse QA benchmarks. Case studies on recently released benchmarks further confirm generalization to both text and vision-language tasks.

</details>


### [234] [Test-Time Compositional Generalization in Diffusion Models via Concept Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07078)
*Zekun Wang,Anant Gupta,Tianyi Zhu,Christopher J. MacLellan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pretrained diffusion models can discover query-specific concepts from time-indexed scores and compose them at test time via a Product-of-Experts teacher, enabling compositional generation without a predefined concept library and improving performance on held-out benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve compositional generalization in diffusion-based generation without relying on a fixed set of concepts or conditioning signals, by leveraging the intrinsic geometry of time-indexed scores p_t(x_t).

Method: Identify local density modes by gradient ascent on s_theta(x_t,t) ≈ ∇ log p_t(x_t) across multiple timesteps. Map modes to clean Gaussian prototypes, select a subset via a submodular likelihood objective, and fuse them into a PoE teacher with an analytic score. Sample via classifier-free guidance or train a new class embedding and low-rank adapter using the teacher.

Result: On ColorMNIST/CelebA-based held-out compositions, the PoE-based sampler and the low-rank adapted model outperform query-only and nearest-trained-class baselines, demonstrating effective test-time concept discovery and composition.

Conclusion: The time-indexed score geometry of diffusion models encodes reusable density-mode concepts, enabling test-time compositional generation without a predefined concept library.

Abstract: Compositional generalization requires models to produce novel configurations from familiar parts. In diffusion models, prior compositional generation methods typically assume that the relevant concepts or conditioning signals are already available. We instead ask whether a pretrained diffusion model can discover query-specific concepts from the time-indexed scores it learns for the noisy marginals $p_t(x_t)$ and compose them at test time. Given a single out-of-distribution query, our method performs gradient ascent on $s_θ(x_t,t) \approx \nabla_{x_t}\log p_t(x_t)$ at multiple noising timesteps to recover local density modes, maps these modes into clean-space Gaussians, greedily selects relevant prototypes with a submodular likelihood objective, and combines them into a product-of-experts (PoE) teacher model with an analytic score. This teacher model can be sampled directly through classifier-free guidance or used to generate a sample pool for training a new class embedding and low-rank adapter. On held-out composition benchmarks built from ColorMNIST and CelebA, both the analytic PoE sampler and the low-rank adapted model outperform query-only and nearest trained-class baselines. These results suggest that the time-indexed score geometry of the diffusion model contains reusable density-mode concepts that support test-time compositional generation without a predefined concept library.

</details>


### [235] [Actor-Critic with Active Importance Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07094)
*Majid Molaei,Gabor Paczolay,Matteo Papini,Alberto Maria Metelli,Marcello Restelli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AISAC reduces variance in policy gradient estimates by optimizing a Gaussian behavior policy via importance sampling, improving data efficiency and stability in continuous-control Actor-Critic, with theoretical guarantees of unbiasedness and variance reduction; demonstrates faster learning in Inverted Pendulum and Half Cheetah; future work includes integrating with SAC and TD3.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Policy gradient methods suffer from high variance, leading to sample-inefficient and unstable learning. Reducing gradient variance while maintaining unbiased estimates can improve data efficiency and stability by steering data collection toward distributions aligned with the target policy gradients.

Method: Extend Actor-Critic by introducing a learnable behavior policy optimized to minimize gradient variance under the constraint of unbiased gradient estimates. Use importance sampling to align data collection with target gradients. For continuous actions, model the behavior policy as a Gaussian and optimize its parameters via cross-entropy minimization. Provide theoretical analysis for variance reduction and unbiasedness. Empirically evaluate on Inverted Pendulum and Half Cheetah, comparing with standard Actor-Critic baselines.

Result: The paper demonstrates variance reduction and unbiasedness guarantees for AISAC. Empirically, AISAC achieves faster learning, improved sample efficiency, and greater training stability compared with standard Actor-Critic methods on continuous control tasks (Inverted Pendulum and Half Cheetah). The results also indicate enhanced critic estimation accuracy and more robust performance across hyperparameters.

Conclusion: AISAC effectively accelerates convergence and stabilizes RL training by optimizing the behavior policy for variance reduction in policy gradient estimation. It is a promising approach for real-world applications and can be integrated with more advanced algorithms (e.g., Soft Actor-Critic, TD3) for more complex environments.

Abstract: This paper introduces the Active-Importance-Sampling Actor-Critic (AISAC) algorithm, an extension of the Actor-Critic framework for reducing variance in policy gradient estimation. AISAC optimizes the behavior policy to minimize gradient variance while preserving unbiased gradient estimates. Using importance sampling principles, the algorithm adapts the behavior policy toward efficient data collection distributions aligned with target policy gradients.
  For continuous action spaces, AISAC employs Gaussian behavior policies optimized through cross-entropy minimization. We provide theoretical analysis demonstrating variance reduction and unbiasedness. Experiments on Inverted Pendulum and Half Cheetah tasks show improved learning speed, sample efficiency, and training stability compared to standard Actor-Critic methods.
  Results indicate that optimizing the behavior policy improves both target policy updates and critic estimation accuracy across different hyperparameter settings. AISAC accelerates convergence and stabilizes reinforcement learning training, making it promising for real-world applications. Future work includes integration with advanced algorithms such as Soft Actor-Critic and TD3 for more complex environments.

</details>


### [236] [Query-efficient model evaluation using cached responses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07096)
*Hayden Helm,Ben Johnson,Carey Priebe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Data Kernel Perspective Space (DKPS) to predict benchmark performance using cached model outputs, enabling query-efficient evaluation with an offline query-selection strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating new models on existing benchmarks is costly; caching previous model responses offers an opportunity to reduce the number of queries while maintaining accuracy.

Method: Proposes DKPS to quantify relationships between models in black-box settings and predicts benchmark performance from cached responses; provides theoretical guarantees of query-efficiency under certain conditions; introduces an offline query-selection method that maximizes goodness-of-fit on reference models.

Result: DKPS-based predictions achieve the same mean absolute error as baselines with substantially fewer queries; empirical evaluation supports efficiency gains; offline query selection improves prediction accuracy over random sampling.

Conclusion: DKPS-based evaluation is effective for cost-efficient benchmark assessment; the offline query-selection strategy further enhances predictive accuracy; offers practical guidance for deploying evaluation in resource-constrained settings.

Abstract: Evaluating a new model on an existing benchmark is often necessary to understand its behavior before deployment. For modern evaluation frameworks, generating and evaluating a response for all queries can be prohibitively expensive. In practice, responses from previously-evaluated models are often cached -- creating a potential opportunity to use this additional information to decrease the number of queries required to accurately evaluate a new model. In this paper, we introduce an approach for predicting benchmark performance that leverages cached model responses based on the Data Kernel Perspective Space (DKPS), a method for quantifying the relationship between models in the black-box setting. Theoretically, we show that DKPS-based methods are query-efficient under certain conditions. Empirically, we demonstrate that DKPS-based methods achieve the same mean absolute error as baselines with a substantially decreased query budget. We conclude by proposing an offline method for selecting a set of queries that maximizes the goodness-of-fit on reference models, improving prediction accuracy over random query selection.

</details>


### [237] [CarCrashNet: A Large-Scale Dataset and Hierarchical Neural Solver for Data-Driven Structural Crash Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07098)
*Mohamed Elrefaie,Dule Shu,Matthew Klenk,Faez Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Public, high-fidelity, open-source CarCrashNet benchmark for data-driven structural crash simulation, with extensive multi-model FE datasets and CrashSolver ML predictor, validated against experiments and commercial solvers; enables reproducible AI-driven crash testing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Crash modeling is nonlinear, high-dimensional, and resource-intensive; there is a need for large, open, reproducible benchmarks and data-driven tools to accelerate design and virtual testing.

Method: Assembles multi-modal FE datasets: 14k bumper-beam pole-impact simulations and 825 full-vehicle crashes across three vehicle models; validates the OpenRadioss workflow against experimental data and LS-DYNA; introduces CrashSolver, an ML model for full-vehicle crash prediction from high-resolution FE data; benchmarks CrashSolver against geometric DL and transformer-based solvers.

Result: Released CarCrashNet datasets and validated FE workflow; demonstrated CrashSolver performance and provided extensive benchmarks, establishing a standard for reproducible crash-simulation research.

Conclusion: CarCrashNet provides a foundational, open benchmark for reproducible research in structural crash simulation and AI-driven virtual testing, enabling broader community evaluation and development.

Abstract: Crash simulation is a cornerstone of modern vehicle development because it reduces the need for costly physical prototypes, accelerates safety-driven design iteration, and increasingly supports virtual testing workflows. At the same time, modeling structural crash mechanics remains exceptionally challenging: the response is governed by nonlinear contact, large deformation, material plasticity, failure, and complex multi-body interactions evolving over space and time on high-resolution finite-element meshes. In this work, we introduce \textsc{CarCrashNet}, a public high-fidelity open-source benchmark for data-driven structural crash simulation. \textsc{CarCrashNet} combines component-scale and full-vehicle simulations in a multi-modal format, including more than 14{,}000 bumper-beam pole-impact simulations with varying geometry, materials, and boundary conditions, together with 825 full-vehicle crash simulations built from three industry-standard vehicle models of increasing structural complexity: Dodge Neon, Toyota Yaris, and Chevrolet Silverado. To establish the reliability of the benchmark, we validate our open-source finite-element workflow based on OpenRadioss against both experimental crash data and the commercial solver Ansys LS-DYNA. We also introduce \textsc{CrashSolver}, a machine-learning model designed for full-vehicle crash prediction from high-resolution finite-element crash data. We further perform extensive benchmarking across the released datasets and evaluate \textsc{CrashSolver} against state-of-the-art geometric deep learning and transformer-based neural solvers. Our results position \textsc{CarCrashNet} as a foundation for reproducible research in structural simulation, crashworthiness modeling, and AI-driven virtual crash testing. The dataset is available at https://github.com/Mohamedelrefaie/CarCrashNet.

</details>


### [238] [Almost Sure Convergence Rates of Stochastic Approximation and Reinforcement Learning via a Poisson-Moreau Drift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07104)
*Xinyu Liu,Zixuan Xie,Shangtong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Establishes almost sure convergence rates for stochastic approximation with Markovian noise under contractive updates, achieving near-optimal rates for both power-law and harmonic stepsizes in RL contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide rigorous convergence-rate guarantees for stochastic approximation algorithms used in reinforcement learning (e.g., Q-learning, TD learning) under Markovian noise.

Method: Lyapunov drift analysis with a Poisson-equation based correction for Markovian noise, integrated with Moreau-envelope smoothing for contractive maps.

Result: For learning rate η in (1/2,1): almost sure rate arbitrarily close to o(n^{1-2η}). For η=1 (harmonic): rate arbitrarily close to o(n^{-1}); approaching the iid-law-of-iterated-log superior bound of O(n^{-1} log log n) in that special case.

Conclusion: Provides near-optimal convergence guarantees for a broad class of stochastic approximation algorithms under Markovian noise, with direct relevance to reinforcement learning algorithms such as Q-learning and TD learning.

Abstract: Establishing almost sure convergence rates for stochastic approximation and reinforcement learning under Markovian noise is a fundamental theoretical challenge. We make progress towards this challenge for a class of stochastic approximation algorithms whose expected updates are contractive, a setting that arises in many reinforcement learning algorithms such as $Q$-learning and linear temporal difference learning. Specifically, for a power-law learning rate $O(n^{-η})$ with $η\in (1/2, 1)$, we obtain an almost sure convergence rate arbitrarily close to $o(n^{1 - 2η})$. For a harmonic learning rate $O(n^{-1})$, we obtain an almost sure convergence rate arbitrarily close to $o(n^{-1})$, which we argue is a strong result because it is close to the optimal rate $O(n^{-1}\log\log n)$ given by the law of the iterated logarithm (for a special case of i.i.d. noise). Key to our analysis is a novel Lyapunov drift construction that applies a Poisson-equation based correction for Markovian noise to the well-established Moreau-envelope smoothing for the contractive mapping.

</details>


### [239] [Theoretical Limits of Language Model Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07105)
*Lucas Monteiro Paes,Natalie Mackraz,Barry-John Theobald,Federico Danieli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KL-regularized alignment has a fundamental information-theoretic limit on reward gains under a KL budget; optimal gain is tied to Jeffreys divergence, not sqrt(KL); best-of-N nearly reaches the limit; reward hacking grows with proxy error and lower KL factor; reward ensembling mitigates hacking; empirical KL-reward frontier shows best-of-N close to limit while PPO/GRPO lag; implications for alignment algorithm design and costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify the ultimate limits of reward improvement under KL constraints in LM alignment, explain empirical patterns, and guide development of better-aligned models.

Method: Derive a closed-form expression for the maximal expected reward gain under a fixed KL budget; show the optimum is governed by Jeffreys divergence; reformulate as a base-model covariance to enable practical estimation from samples; extend to proxy rewards; analyze reward hacking and the mitigating effect of reward ensembling; empirically compute the KL-reward Pareto frontier for safety and summarization tasks and compare with PPO/GRPO.

Result: The optimal reward gain under a KL budget is determined by a Jeffreys-divergence-based expression; a practical covariance-based estimator from base-model samples is proposed; proxy rewards increase reward hacking with larger reward error and smaller KL factor; reward ensembling mitigates hacking; best-of-N closely approaches the theoretical limit, PPO/GRPO are suboptimal; results align with observed empirical phenomena and indicate room for algorithmic improvement with lower inference costs.

Conclusion: The theory clarifies why existing alignment methods behave as observed, supports best-of-N usage, and points to the need for new algorithms that approach the limit with lower costs; suggests future work on near-optimal, cost-efficient alignment methods and further study of proxy-reward and ensembling strategies.

Abstract: Language model (LM) alignment improves model outputs to reflect human preferences while preserving the capabilities of the base model. The most common alignment approaches are (i) reinforcement learning, which maximizes the expected reward under a KL-divergence constraint, and (ii) best-of-$N$ alignment, which selects the highest-reward output among $N$ independent samples. Despite their widespread use, the fundamental limits of reward improvement under a KL budget remain poorly understood. We characterize the information-theoretic limits of KL-regularized alignment by deriving the maximum achievable expected reward gain for a fixed KL-divergence budget. Our first result provides a closed-form expression for the optimal reward improvement, governed by a Jeffreys divergence term rather than the $\sqrt{\texttt{KL}}$ used in prior analyses. We further reformulate this expression as a covariance under the base model, yielding a practical estimator that predicts achievable alignment gains from base model samples alone. We extend our analysis to the proxy reward setting, showing that the gap between ideal and proxy alignment (reward hacking) grows with the magnitude of reward error and when the KL penalty factor decreases. We then prove that reward ensembling mitigates reward hacking, providing a theoretical justification for this technique used in practice. Empirically, we compute the KL-reward Pareto frontier for two tasks for LMs, safety and summarization, and show that best-of-$N$ closely approaches the theoretical limit, while PPO and GRPO remain substantially suboptimal. Our theoretical results shed light on several empirically observed phenomena in the alignment literature and suggest that algorithmic improvements are needed to achieve optimal alignment without high inference costs.

</details>


### [240] [Solving Max-Cut to Global Optimality via Feasibility-Preserving Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07113)
*Hao Chen,Chendi Qian,Christopher Morris,Andrea Lodi,Can Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Max-Cut–specific graph neural network acts as a fast, validity-preserving surrogate for semidefinite programming relaxations within branch-and-bound, delivering primal and dual SDP solutions for bounding. It is trained self-supervised, avoids solving full SDPs, and achieves substantial speedups (up to 10.6x) over Mosek while enabling feasible Goemans–Williamson cuts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exact Max-Cut solving via branch-and-bound relies on strong SDP relaxations to bound subproblems. Repeatedly solving these SDPs is the bottleneck, motivating a lightweight, problem-specific surrogate that preserves feasibility and accelerates bounding.

Method: A Max-Cut–tailored graph neural network with update complexity O(n^2 + n e) that outputs primal and dual feasible SDP solutions. The primal solution can be rounded to a feasible Max-Cut via the Goemans–Williamson algorithm. Training is self-supervised and does not require solved SDP relaxations as labels.

Result: Empirically, the approach reduces the bounding cost in exact Max-Cut solving by up to 10.6x compared with Mosek, demonstrating that learned surrogates can substantially speed up exact optimization over structured convex relaxations.

Conclusion: Validity-preserving neural surrogates for convex relaxations can accelerate exact optimization in structured problems like Max-Cut, justifying broader exploration of learned surrogates in B&B and related frameworks.

Abstract: Exact solution of hard combinatorial optimization problems often relies on strong convex relaxations, but solving these relaxations repeatedly inside a branch-and-bound algorithm can be prohibitively expensive. Hence, we consider this challenge for Max-Cut, where branch and bound commonly uses semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxations to bound subproblems. We propose a Max-Cut-specific graph neural network that serves as a principled, lightweight neural proxy for these SDP solvers and can be plugged directly into an exact branch-and-bound framework. The proposed architecture has update steps of complexity $\mathcal{O}(n^2 + ne)$, and predicts both primal- and dual-feasible SDP solutions. The primal SDP solutions yield feasible Max-Cut solutions via the Goemans--Williamson algorithm. In addition, it is trained in a self-supervised fashion without requiring solved SDP relaxations as labels. Empirically, we show that our architecture can substantially reduce the cost of bounding in exact Max-Cut solving by up to $10.6 \times$ compared with using the state-of-the-art SDP solver Mosek. Our work highlights the potential of learned, validity-preserving surrogates for accelerating exact optimization over structured convex relaxations.

</details>


### [241] [Where to Spend Rollouts: Hit-Utility Optimal Rollout Allocation for Group-Based RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07114)
*Tao Wang,Shuo Li,Yan Sun,Dongsheng Ding,Edgar Dobriban*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduction of HORA, a learning-free rollout allocation policy for RLVR that maximizes posterior hit utility. It preserves Pass@1 and improves Pass@K over GRPO across benchmarks, with RLOO compatibility; ablations show a uniform prior performs well.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniform rollout allocation in group-based policy optimization (e.g., GRPO) is inefficient, leading to wasted compute on saturated prompts and under-exploration of prompts that could yield correct trajectories. An adaptive, principled allocation strategy can improve efficiency and performance.

Method: Define hit utility as the posterior probability that at least one additional rollout for a prompt yields a correct trajectory. Allocate rollouts within each batch to maximize total posterior hit utility (HORA), a learning-free policy that adaptively reallocates budgets while keeping reward evaluation and group-based estimators unchanged. HORA is drop-in compatible with estimators like RLOO; ablation includes a uniform prior vs prompt-conditioned learned priors.

Result: Empirical evaluation on four mathematical reasoning benchmarks across three model scales shows HORA preserves Pass@1 and improves Pass@K over compute-matched GRPO in 10 of 12 model–benchmark configurations (1 tie, 1 saturated exception). HORA is compatible with other group-based estimators such as RLOO. Ablations indicate the uniform prior used by HORA is competitive with five prompt-conditioned learned-prior alternatives.

Conclusion: HORA provides an effective, computation-efficient rollout allocation strategy for RLVR that yields stronger or comparable performance across varied settings, while remaining easy to integrate with existing group-based estimation frameworks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a central paradigm for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models. Group-based policy optimization methods, such as GRPO, typically allocate a fixed number of rollouts to every prompt. This uniform allocation can be inefficient: it over-allocates compute to prompts whose sampled groups are already saturated while under-exploring prompts for which additional samples may reveal useful correct trajectories. To address this limitation, we introduce hit utility, the posterior probability that at least one rollout in a proposed additional allocation for a prompt will be correct. Building on this notion, we propose Hit-Utility Optimal Rollout Allocation (HORA), a learning-free rollout allocation policy that maximizes total posterior hit utility within each allocation batch. HORA adaptively reallocates rollout budgets while leaving the downstream reward evaluation and group-based advantage estimator unchanged. Across four mathematical reasoning benchmarks and three model scales, HORA preserves comparable Pass@1 and improves Pass@K over compute-matched GRPO in ten of twelve model--benchmark configurations, with one tie and one saturated exception. It is also drop-in compatible with other group-based estimators such as RLOO. Ablation studies indicate that the uniform prior used by HORA is competitive with five prompt-conditioned learned-prior alternatives.

</details>


### [242] [Conformal-Style Quantile Analyses for Stochastic Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07115)
*Chengyu Du,Mengfan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ACP-UCB1 is a conformal-style, upper-tail aware bandit policy that achieves logarithmic upper-quantile regret by estimating the arm-wise upper endpoint with adaptive conformal quantiles and combining this with a UCB-style optimism bonus.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mean-reward optimization can misrank arms for risk-sensitive objectives. To favor arms with strong upper-tail performance, the natural target is the upper endpoint F_j^{-1}(1-α/2). The policy must estimate this endpoint accurately under evolving data and adaptively chosen levels.

Method: Integrate an adaptive conformal estimate of the upper endpoint with a UCB-type optimism bonus. Conformity scores are recomputed from evolving empirical quantile estimates and evaluated at an adaptive level. Control of the endpoint uses reward-quantile concentration, a perturbation argument for recomputed score quantiles, and deterministic localization of the adaptive level.

Result: The authors establish logarithmic upper-quantile regret with per-arm contribution O(log n / Δ_j^{ACP}). They provide metric-specific regret decompositions contrasting ACP-UCB1 with UCB1 and back their claims with numerical experiments.

Conclusion: ACP-UCB1 demonstrates that conformal-style estimation can be effectively integrated with bandit optimism to target upper-tail performance, delivering both theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements; it opens avenues for distributional-risk-aware bandit algorithms.

Abstract: Stochastic bandit algorithms are usually analyzed under a mean-reward criterion, yet many problems favor arms with strong upper-tail performance, which we study herein. For a fixed miscoverage level \(α\), the natural upper-tail target of arm \(j\) is the upper endpoint \(F_j^{-1}(1-α/2)\) of a central prediction interval. This target can rank arms differently from their means, creating a central mismatch with the classical bandit objective. To this end, we propose ACP-UCB1, a conformal-style policy that combines an adaptive conformal estimate of the upper endpoint with a UCB-type optimism bonus. The technical challenge is that the conformity scores used by ACP-UCB1 are recomputed from evolving empirical quantile estimates and evaluated at an adaptive level. We control this endpoint through reward-quantile concentration, a perturbation argument for recomputed score quantiles, and deterministic localization of the adaptive level. ACP-UCB1 achieves logarithmic upper-quantile regret with per-arm contribution \(O(\nicefrac{\log n}{Δ_j^{\mathrm{ACP}}})\). We also provide metric-specific regret decompositions comparing ACP-UCB1 with UCB1 and use numerical experiments to validate performance and improvement.

</details>


### [243] [Stabilized neural Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman solvers: Error analysis and applications in model-based reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07116)
*Minseok Kim,Yeongjong Kim,Namkyeong Cho,Yeoneung Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid physics-informed neural solver for continuous-time HJB-based RL is analyzed theoretically and empirically. It uses neural value functions evaluated with finite-difference-like shift operators, yielding a stable error framework that separates residual, policy-mismatch, and learned-dynamics errors, plus finite-sample certificates; demonstrated on high-dimensional control benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge between grid-based methods and continuous-PDE PINNs in Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman RL, providing rigorous error control for a regime where value is NN-based and policy evaluation uses shifted-point queries with learned dynamics.

Method: Model the finite-difference policy-evaluation as shift operators on neural networks; derive population L2 stability for one policy-evaluation step with learned dynamics; decompose error into residual error, initial/exterior-collar mismatch, policy mismatch, and model-identification error; derive gradient amplification factor for learned dynamics; establish finite-sample collocation certificates and conditional multi-step propagation through greedy policy improvement; conduct experiments on compact-control LQR (up to 64D), Allen–Cahn control, pendulum, Hopper, and 3D quadrotor.

Result: Theoretically, a stable error bound is established without inverse-viscosity blow-up and with an explicit error decomposition. Finite-sample certificates and multi-step propagation results are provided. Empirically, the method shows residual, policy-mismatch, and learned-model error trends consistent with the theory and competitive performance on high-dimensional control tasks against model-based and model-free baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid regime preserves stabilized finite-difference policy evaluation while avoiding grid-based value unknowns, with provable error control and practical effectiveness across diverse continuous-control tasks.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural solvers offer a promising route to model-based reinforcement learning in continuous time, where optimal feedback synthesis is governed by Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman (HJB) equations. Practical implementations often occupy a regime that is neither a classical grid method nor a continuous-PDE PINN: the value function is represented by a neural network, finite-difference HJB policy-evaluation operators are evaluated by network queries at shifted points, and residuals are minimized by random continuous collocation. This regime preserves the stabilized finite-difference policy-evaluation structure while avoiding grid-based value unknowns. We develop an error theory for this hybrid regime. Interpreting finite differences as shift operators acting on neural networks, we prove a population $L^2$ stability estimate for one policy-evaluation step with learned dynamics. The bound separates residual error, initial and exterior-collar mismatch, policy mismatch, and model-identification error, with an explicit gradient amplification factor for learned dynamics, while the underlying linear evaluation stability remains free of hidden inverse-viscosity blow-up. We further give a finite-sample collocation certificate and a conditional multi-step propagation result through greedy policy improvement. Experiments on compact-control LQR upto 64 dimensions, Allen--Cahn control, pendulum, Hopper, and 3D quadrotor benchmarks compare against representative model-based and model-free RL baselines, demonstrating the predicted residual, policy-mismatch, and learned-model error trends.

</details>


### [244] [When Symbol Names Should Not Matter: A Logistic Theory of Fresh-Symbol Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07120)
*Wenjie Guan,Jelena Bradic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decomposes the fixed-label template-task classifier into an ideal template-level rule plus finite-sample perturbations from token overlaps, using a colored collision graph to prove margin-transfer guarantees for fresh-symbol classification; shows when prompting improves abstraction generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain whether transformers can reason with abstract symbols independent of vocabulary; extend template analyses to logistic classification in the transformer-kernel regime; establish margin-based conditions predicting when abstraction/prompts improve fresh-symbol generalization.

Method: Regularized kernel logistic regression in the transformer-kernel regime; decompose learned predictor into an ideal template-level classifier and perturbations from accidental token overlaps; model overlaps with a colored collision graph; derive high-probability margin-transfer bounds; analyze how vocabulary size and collision geometry affect margin preservation; generalize to abstraction-augmented inputs and prompting strategies.

Result: Proves high-probability margin-transfer guarantees for fresh-symbol classification; shows vocabulary size controls average collision rate while collision geometry governs whether the ideal margin is preserved; presents a margin-versus-collision criterion for prompting strategies; synthetic-template experiments corroborate the theory, highlighting roles of regularization, sample size, and transformer-kernel structure.

Conclusion: Provides a perturbation-based framework for template-based abstraction in logistic classification, clarifying when prompting strategies improve fresh-symbol generalization and offering design guidance on vocabulary diversity, regularization, and kernel architecture in transformer models.

Abstract: Template tasks have emerged as a clean testbed for asking whether transformers reason with abstract symbols rather than concrete token names. We study the fixed-label classification version of this problem, where train and test examples share latent templates but may use disjoint vocabularies. Unlike next-token prediction, the model need not emit unseen symbols; it must learn a decision rule invariant to symbol renaming. We analyze regularized kernel logistic classification in the transformer-kernel regime. Our main result decomposes the learned predictor into an ideal template-level classifier and a finite-sample perturbation caused by accidental token overlaps in the training data. We encode these overlaps by a colored collision graph and prove high-probability margin-transfer guarantees for fresh-symbol classification. This perspective extends template-based analyses to logistic classification and refines scalar diversity conditions: vocabulary size controls the average rate of collisions, but collision geometry controls whether the ideal classification margin is preserved. More broadly, the same perturbation framework applies to abstraction-augmented inputs, yielding a general margin-versus-collision criterion for identifying when prompting strategies improve fresh-symbol generalization. Synthetic template experiments illustrate the predicted roles of regularization, sample size, and transformer-kernel structure.

</details>


### [245] [Convergence and Emergence of In-Context Reinforcement Learning with Chain of Thought](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07123)
*Zixuan Xie,Xinyu Liu,Rohan Chandra,Shangtong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This work analyzes how Chain-of-Thought (CoT) generation interacts with In-context Reinforcement Learning (ICRL) in a linear Transformer, showing CoT can replicate TD updates, with error decaying geometrically in CoT length and saturating at a context-dependent floor; optimal Transformer parameters emerge as global minimizers of pretraining loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the empirical observation that CoT boosts ICRL by providing a theoretical mechanism, clarifying when and how CoT aids policy evaluation.

Method: Theoretical analysis in a policy evaluation setting using a linear Transformer. The authors prove that, for certain Transformer parameters, CoT generation is equivalent to repeatedly applying temporal-difference (TD) updates. They derive finite-sample convergence results showing policy evaluation error decays geometrically with CoT length and saturates at a floor determined by context length. They also show that the parameters enabling this behavior are global minimizers of the pretraining loss.

Result: Policy evaluation error decreases geometrically with CoT length and plateaus due to a statistical floor tied to context length. The desired Transformer parameters align with global minimizers of the pretraining objective, offering a theoretical explanation for their empirical emergence.

Conclusion: The paper provides a theoretical foundation linking CoT, ICRL, and Transformer pretraining, identifies parameter regimes that enable effective CoT-driven TD-like updates, and explains why such parameters may arise during pretraining.

Abstract: In-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) refers to the ability of RL agents to adapt to new tasks at inference time without parameter updates by conditioning on additional context. Recent empirical studies further demonstrate that Chain-of-Thought (CoT) generation can amplify this ICRL capability. This paper is the first to provide a theoretical understanding on how CoT interacts with ICRL. We conduct our analysis in a policy evaluation setup with linear Transformer. We prove that with specific Transformer parameters, the CoT generation process is equivalent to repeatedly executing temporal difference learning updates. Additionally, we provide finite sample convergence analysis showing that the policy evaluation error decreases geometrically with CoT length and eventually saturates at a statistical floor determined by the context length. We also prove that the desired Transformer parameters are a global minimizer of the pretraining loss, providing a theoretical understanding on the empirical emergence of those parameters.

</details>


### [246] [The Position Curse: LLMs Struggle to Locate the Last Few Items in a List](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07127)
*Zhanqi Zhang,Hua-Dong Xiong,Robert C. Wilson,Mikio Aoi,Marcelo G. Mattar,Li Ji-An*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLMs suffer a Position Curse in sequence retrieval: backward retrieval lags forward; PosBench mitigates with LoRA but saturation is not reached; position-aware retrieval is crucial for coding tasks and future training/objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Precise indexing in long sequences (e.g., code) is essential for understanding and editing. While LLMs can locate a single relevant fact, they struggle to retrieve items at specific positions, especially near the end of a list.

Method: Define two retrieval tasks: (1) given a position, retrieve the corresponding item; (2) given an item, retrieve its position. Positions are specified as forward/backward offsets from an anchor (start, end, or another item). Evaluate multiple models (open-source and frontier closed-source). Create PosBench, a position-focused training dataset. Apply LoRA fine-tuning to improve retrieval and test generalization on PyIndex (a held-out code-understanding benchmark).

Result: Across models, backward retrieval significantly lags forward retrieval. LoRA fine-tuning improves both forward and backward retrieval and generalizes to PyIndex, but absolute performance remains far from saturated.

Conclusion: Position-based retrieval is a key capability for code understanding and editing. It should guide future pretraining objectives and model design, particularly for coding agents dealing with large codebases.

Abstract: Modern large language models (LLMs) can find a needle in a haystack (locating a single relevant fact buried among hundreds of thousands of irrelevant tokens) with near-saturated accuracy, yet fail to retrieve the last few items in a short list. We call this failure the Position Curse. For instance, even in a two-line code snippet, Claude Opus 4.6 misidentifies the second-to-last line most of the time. To characterize this failure, we evaluated two complementary queries: given a position in a sequence (of letters or words), retrieve the corresponding item; and given an item, return its position. Each position is specified as a forward or backward offset from an anchor, either an endpoint of the list (its start or end) or another item in the list. Across both open-source and frontier closed-source models, backward retrieval substantially lags forward retrieval. To test whether this capability can be rescued by post-training, we constructed PosBench, a position-focused training dataset. LoRA fine-tuning improves both forward and backward retrieval and generalizes to a held-out code-understanding benchmark (PyIndex), yet absolute performance remains far from saturated. As LLM coding agents increasingly operate over large codebases where precise indexing becomes essential for code understanding and editing, position-based retrieval emerges as a key capability for future pretraining objectives and model design.

</details>


### [247] [Simple KNN-Based Outlier Detection Achieves Robust Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07130)
*Tianle Jiang,Yufa Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A simple KNN-distance outlier removal heuristic achieves constant-factor approximation to robust k-Means under mild assumptions, without adding centers or discarding additional outliers, with competitive empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether classic outlier-detection heuristics can be effective for robust clustering and to bridge outlier detection and clustering methods.

Method: Remove z points with large K-Nearest-Neighbor distances; under a practical assumption on optimal cluster sizes, prove a constant-factor reduction from robust k-Means to standard k-Means; no extra centers or extra discarded outliers required.

Result: Theoretical guarantee: a constant-factor approximation; empirical results on real datasets show the approach matches or outperforms more sophisticated algorithms in clustering cost and runtime.

Conclusion: Simple KNN-based outlier heuristics can be surprisingly effective for robust clustering, suggesting fruitful cross-pollination between outlier detection and clustering.

Abstract: Being robust to the presence of outliers is crucial for applying clustering algorithms in practice. In the $\textit{robust $k$-Means}$ problem (i.e., $k$-Means with outliers), the goal is to remove $z$ outliers and minimize the $k$-Means cost on the remaining points. Despite the close connection between robust $k$-Means and outlier detection, both theoretical and empirical understanding of the effectiveness of $\textit{classic outlier detection heuristics}$ for robust $k$-Means remains limited. In this paper, we prove that under a practical assumption on the optimal cluster sizes, simply removing points with large $K$-Nearest-Neighbor distances achieves performance comparable to prior work in terms of approximation guarantees: it yields a constant-factor reduction from robust $k$-Means to standard $k$-Means, without introducing additional centers or discarding extra outliers, as is commonly required by existing approaches. Empirically, experiments on real-world datasets show that our method outperforms or matches several more sophisticated algorithms in terms of clustering cost and runtime. These results demonstrate that simple KNN-based heuristics can be surprisingly effective for robust clustering, highlighting new opportunities to bridge techniques from outlier detection and clustering.

</details>


### [248] [GAD in the Wild: Benchmarking Graph Anomaly Detection under Realistic Deployment Challenges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07133)
*Jingjing Zhou,Shiyu Huang,Qing Qing,Zuquan Yuan,Huafei Huang,Ziqi Xu,Mingliang Hou,Xikun Zhang,Renqiang Luo,Ivan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-dimensional benchmark for Graph Anomaly Detection (GAD) evaluates models on million-scale graphs, extremely rare anomalies, and missing attributes, revealing scalability, robustness, and imputation-sensitivity issues across nine models; code and benchmark released to drive robust, scalable GAD in practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridging the gap between small-scale, balanced, curated GAD benchmarks and real-world deployment where graphs are huge, anomalies are extremely scarce, and attributes can be incomplete.

Method: Construct a family of controlled benchmark variants from five diverse graphs (including two industrial-scale datasets >3.7M nodes), and evaluate nine representative GAD models across deployment-relevant challenges (scalability, extreme anomaly scarcity, missing node attributes); analyze memory, recall at low anomaly ratios, and sensitivity to attribute imputation.

Result: GNN-based methods struggle to scale to million-node graphs due to memory; detection performance collapses at realistic anomaly ratios (e.g., 0.1%); reconstruction-based models are highly sensitive to how attributes are imputed.

Conclusion: The benchmark reveals practical robustness gaps in current GAD models and provides a diagnostic testbed and released code to foster development of robust, scalable GAD for large, imperfect graphs.

Abstract: Graph Anomaly Detection (GAD) is a critical task in graph machine learning with vital applications in financial fraud detection and social platform governance. However, existing GAD benchmarks are often restricted to small-scale, curated graphs with relatively balanced anomaly ratios, leaving a substantial gap between academic evaluation and real-world deployment. To bridge this gap, we present a multi-dimensional benchmark that systematically evaluates GAD models under three deployment-relevant challenges: million-scale graphs, extreme anomaly scarcity, and missing node attributes. We derive a family of controlled benchmark variants from five diverse graphs, including two native industrial-scale datasets with over 3.7 million nodes. Our extensive evaluation of nine representative GAD models reveals three major limitations: (1) most GNN-based methods fail to scale to million-node graphs due to prohibitive memory requirements; (2) detection performance drops sharply under realistic anomaly ratios (e.g., 0.1\%), often resulting in zero recall; and (3) reconstruction-based models are highly sensitive to attribute imputation strategies. Our findings suggest that strong performance in laboratory settings does not guarantee robustness in production environments. We release this benchmark and empirical evaluation as a diagnostic testbed to promote the development of robust and scalable GAD systems for large-scale, imperfect graphs encountered in practice. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Benchmark_GAD-E7A3.

</details>


### [249] [Adaptive Negative Reinforcement for LLM Reasoning:Dynamically Balancing Correction and Diversity in RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07137)
*Yash Ingle,Jaival Chauhan,Ankit Yadav,Sudhakar Mishra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two extensions to negative sample reinforcement (Adaptive NSR and Confidence-Weighted NSR) address fixed-penalty NSR drawbacks by introducing time-scheduled updates and confidence-based penalty weights, improving reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards for LLM reasoning on math benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NSR with a fixed penalty treats all mistakes equally and does not adapt to training dynamics, risking unstable learning and overfitting. Dynamic, uncertainty-aware penalties can stabilize training, leverage prior probability redistribution, and improve reasoning accuracy.

Method: Introduce Adaptive NSR (A-NSR) with time-dependent scheduling that emphasizes error correction early and shifts to subtler updates later; introduce Confidence-Weighted NSR (CW-NSR) that weights penalties by the model's normalized sequence likelihood, penalizing highly confident wrong paths more and uncertain errors less. Provide token-level formal analysis of update dynamics and validate on difficult math datasets using the Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B model.

Result: Formal analysis outlines how the proposed mechanisms govern token-level updates and prevent overfitting; empirical evaluation on math reasoning benchmarks (MATH, AIME 2025, AMC23) demonstrates improved performance over fixed-penalty NSR and standard RL methods.

Conclusion: A-NSR and CW-NSR offer robust, dynamic, uncertainty-aware extensions to NSR, yielding better training dynamics and improved reasoning performance in RLVR for LLMs on challenging math datasets; results motivate broader application and further tuning across domains.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a highly effective method for improving the reasoning abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). Recent research shows that Negative Sample Reinforcement (NSR) -- which focuses on penalizing incorrect steps rather than simply rewarding correct ones -- can match or even exceed the performance of more complex frameworks like PPO and GRPO across the entire Pass@k spectrum. However, current NSR techniques usually apply a fixed penalty throughout the training process and treat every incorrect response with the same weight.
  To address these limitations, we propose two extensions to the NSR framework: Adaptive Negative Sample Reinforcement. Rather than using a fixed update rule, A-NSR uses time-dependent scheduling functions. In the initial training phases, the system focuses heavily on correcting errors to stabilize the model. As training continues, it shifts toward more subtle and controlled updates. We also introduce Confidence-Weighted Negative Reinforcement, which operates on the principle that different mistakes carry different levels of importance. CW-NSR assigns specific penalty weights based on the model's normalized sequence likelihood. If the model is highly confident in a wrong path, it receives a larger penalty and for uncertain errors -- where the model is effectively exploring -- are penalized less strictly. Our formal analysis shows how these mechanisms govern token-level updates, allowing the model to leverage prior-guided probability redistribution while providing a natural defense against overfitting. We evaluated these methods on difficult reasoning datasets, including MATH, AIME 2025, and AMC23, using the Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B architecture.

</details>


### [250] [Regret-Oracle Complexity Tradeoffs in Agnostic Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07155)
*Idan Attias,Steve Hanneke,Arvind Ramaswami*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic pruning with a weak-consistency oracle yields near-optimal agnostic online learning with polynomial query complexity in T, namely O(T^{d_VC+1}), and memory benefits, by an adaptive agnostic-to-realizable reduction that bounds active paths via VC dimension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agnostic online learning typically reduces to realizable learning via Littlestone's SOA, but SOA is intractable. Oracle-efficient approaches using ERM suffer doubly-exponential oracle complexity in the Littlestone dimension. There is a need for a weaker oracle and more efficient reductions.

Method: Introduce an adaptive, dynamic agnostic-to-realizable reduction that uses a weak-consistency oracle (decides realizability of labeled datasets). The algorithm actively prunes non-realizable label sequences. It bounds the number of dynamically maintained active paths using the VC dimension d_VC, achieving O(T^{d_VC+1}) total queries. The design also yields memory savings and a regret guarantee near the optimum, with a formal regret–oracle-Complexity tradeoff and lower bounds.

Result: Oracle complexity reduced from doubly-exponential to O(T^{d_VC+1}) queries. Memory usage is reduced compared to standard reductions. Provides upper bounds interpolating between query budgets and regret; lower bound: any learner with Q = o(sqrt(T)) queries incurs regret Ω(T/Q).

Conclusion: The work substantially improves the practicality of agnostic online learning by using a weak-consistency oracle and dynamic pruning to achieve near-optimal regret with polynomial query complexity, plus a quantified regret–oracle-care tradeoff and matching lower bounds.

Abstract: Agnostic online learning is classically solved via a reduction to the realizable setting, utilizing Littlestone's Standard Optimal Algorithm (SOA) as a base learner. However, the SOA is computationally intractable to execute even for a single round. To overcome this barrier, recent work in oracle-efficient online learning replaces the SOA with a realizable base learner that accesses the concept class exclusively through an offline empirical risk minimization (ERM) oracle. While such agnostic learners achieve near-optimal expected regret, they suffer from a doubly-exponential oracle complexity of $O\big(T^{2^{O(d_\mathrm{LD})}}\big)$, where $d_\mathrm{LD}$ is the Littlestone dimension and $T$ is the number of rounds. In this work, we significantly improve this oracle complexity while relying on an even weaker primitive: a weak-consistency oracle, which merely decides whether a given labeled dataset is realizable. At the core of our approach is an adaptive and dynamic agnostic-to-realizable reduction that actively prunes non-realizable label sequences on the fly. By using the VC dimension ($d_\mathrm{VC}$) to bound the number of dynamically maintained active paths, our algorithm reduces the total query complexity down to $O(T^{d_\mathrm{VC}+1})$ while perfectly preserving near-optimal expected regret. Crucially, this dynamic pruning also yields a memory reduction over the standard reduction. Furthermore, we formally quantify the regret--oracle complexity tradeoff, providing upper bounds that smoothly interpolate between restricted query budgets and attainable expected regret. We complement these with lower bounds proving that any learner restricted to $Q = o(\sqrt{T})$ queries must suffer an expected regret of $Ω(T/Q)$.

</details>


### [251] [Learned Lagrangian Models of PDEs via Euler-Lagrange Residual Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07157)
*Lyra Zhornyak,Eric Forgoston,M. Ani Hsieh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces an optimization-based, learned continuous Lagrangian approach for PDE dynamics with near-symplectic, mesh-free integrator; achieves stable long-range predictions and linear-scaling Jacobi iterations; generalizes without retraining and matches symplectic methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To exploit the conservative structure of PDEs encoded by a learned Lagrangian, enabling stable long-range forecasts and decoupling model error from integration error, overcoming limitations of fixed discretizations in learned models.

Method: Develops an optimization-based integrator that minimizes the squared Euler–Lagrange residual on local spacetime patches using a mesh-free near-symplectic construction; scales linearly via Jacobi iteration; decouples errors; does not require network-specific structure; compatible with physics-guided ML.

Result: Validated on a learned double pendulum, 1D wave equation, and 2D wave equation; achieves error comparable to classical symplectic methods and generalizes to spatially varying dynamics and arbitrary boundary conditions without retraining.

Conclusion: The approach provides stable, scalable, and flexible long-range predictions for learned PDE dynamics, enabling integration of learned Lagrangians with existing ML physics methods and expanding applicability to varied boundary conditions without retraining.

Abstract: We present the first method to directly use a learned continuous Lagrangian to forecast the dynamics of systems governed by partial differential equations, exploiting the inherent conservative structure to achieve stable long-range predictions. We develop an optimization-based integrator that minimizes the squared Euler--Lagrange residual via a mesh-free near-symplectic construction on local space-time patches. Different from integrators for analytical models, integrators for learned models should decouple model error (phase error) from integration error (conservation error). By relying on optimization rather than time-stepping, we bypass the global coupling inherent to fixed discretizations, which slows time- and space-stepping and complicates learning. Our method scales linearly with domain size via Jacobi iteration, and places no structural requirements on the learned network, allowing it to be coupled with existing physics-guided machine learning (ML) methods. We validate our approach on a learned representation of a double pendulum, a one-dimensional wave equation, and a two-dimensional wave equation. Our method achieves error comparable to classical symplectic methods while generalizing to spatially varying dynamics and arbitrary boundary conditions without retraining.

</details>


### [252] [Neurosymbolic Imitation Learning with Human Guidance: A Privileged Information Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07166)
*Nikhilesh Prabhakar,Varun Balaji,Athresh Karanam,Kristian Kersting,Sriraam Natarajan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A neurosymbolic imitation learning framework combines high-dimensional data handling with strong generalization by leveraging training-time privileged information (gaze data).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural methods require大量 samples and risk overfitting; pure symbolic methods generalize but struggle with high-dimensional data; a neurosymbolic approach can unify the strengths of both and exploit privileged information available only during training to improve efficiency and generalization.

Method: A neurosymbolic framework that integrates neural representations with symbolic reasoning and utilizes privileged training data (gaze) to guide learning, improving sample efficiency and generalization in imitation learning.

Result: Empirical evaluations show the approach is effective, efficient, and generalizes well, demonstrating the ability to exploit privileged information (gaze) to achieve better performance than purely neural or purely symbolic baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed neurosymbolic method with training-time privileged information provides the best of both worlds for imitation learning, with strong generalization and efficiency. Further work could explore additional privileged data sources and scalability.

Abstract: Imitation learning is widely used for learning to act in complex environments. While pure neural-based methods handle high dimensional data effectively, they suffer from the requirement of large number of samples and are prone to overfitting. Pure symbolic approaches, while generalize well, do not handle high-dimensional data effectively. We propose a neurosymbolic approach that achieves the best of both worlds, i.e, handling high-dimensional data while achieving generalization. The key advantage of our approach is that it can effectively exploit additional privileged information that is available only during training (in our case, gaze data). Our empirical evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness, efficiency and the generalization capability of our proposed approach.

</details>


### [253] [Cost-Ordered Feasibility for Multi-Armed Bandits with Cost Subsidy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07171)
*Ishank Juneja,Carlee Joe-Wong,Osman Yağan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Cost-Ordered Feasibility (COF) for multi-armed bandits with cost subsidies (MAB-CS). Proves instance-dependent lower bounds on sub-optimal samples, presents COF with data-efficient sampling to assess feasibility of cheap arms, and provides instance-dependent upper bounds on cumulative cost and quality regret; validated empirically on MovieLens, Goodreads, and synthetic data with superior performance versus baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In settings where the objective is to minimize cost subject to a reward constraint under uncertainty, with known arm costs and an unknown best reward, there is a need for tighter, instance-dependent guarantees and practical algorithms.

Method: Derive instance-dependent lower bounds on required sub-optimal samples. Propose COF that combines samples across arms to gauge the feasibility of a cheap arm. Analyze COF to obtain instance-dependent upper bounds on cumulative cost and quality regret (relative to the cheapest feasible arm). Validate via extensive simulations on MovieLens, Goodreads, and synthetic instances, comparing to baselines.

Result: COF achieves improved theoretical regret bounds and demonstrates improved empirical performance over baselines in both synthetic and real-data simulations.

Conclusion: The COF framework advances MAB-CS by delivering tighter instance-dependent guarantees and a practical, data-efficient policy, with strong empirical support on real-world datasets and synthetic instances.

Abstract: The classic multi-armed bandit (MAB) problem tackles the challenge of accruing maximum reward while making decisions under uncertainty. However, in applications, often the goal is to minimize cost subject to a constraint on the minimum permissible reward, an objective captured by multi-armed bandits with cost-subsidy (MAB-CS). Of interest to this paper is the setting where the quality (reward) constraint is specified relative to the unknown best reward and the cost of each arm is known. We characterize the expected sub-optimal samples required by any policy by proving instance-dependent lower bounds that offer new insight into the problem and are a strict generalization of prior bounds. Then, we propose an algorithm called Cost-Ordered Feasibility (COF) that leverages our insight and intelligently combine samples from all arms to gauge the feasibility of a cheap arm. Thereafter, we analyze COF to establish instance-dependent upper bounds on its expected cumulative cost and quality regret, i.e., relative to the cheapest feasible arm. Finally, we empirically validate the merits of COF, comparing it to baselines from the literature through extensive simulation experiments on the MovieLens and Goodreads datasets as well as representative synthetic instances. Not only does our paper develop qualitatively better theoretical regret upper bounds, but COF also convincingly demonstrates improved empirical performance.

</details>


### [254] [Learning Multi-Relational Graph Representations for DNA Methylation-Based Biological Age Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07175)
*Qing Qing,Xikun Zhang,Zhongyuan Zhang,Jiarui Liu,Xingtong Yu,Xiaotao Shen,Ziqi Xu,Qixin Zhang,Zhe Wang,Renqiang Luo*

Main category: cs.LG
