TL;DR: RelAge-GNN uses a multi-relational GNN with three CpG relationship graphs to predict biological age from DNA methylation; it achieves competitive accuracy and improved age-acceleration detection, with interpretable contributions across relations and CpG sites.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CpG sites in DNA methylation data are not independent; their biological relationships (co-methylation, genomic co-localization, gene-level associations) are rich and heterogeneous. Existing models largely treat CpGs as independent features, potentially limiting predictive power and interpretability. A multi-relational graph approach can better leverage these relationships for aging clocks.

Method: Construct three complementary graphs over CpG sites capturing (1) co-methylation patterns, (2) genomic co-localization, and (3) gene-level associations. Each graph is processed by a dedicated GNN branch; a learnable gating mechanism fuses the branch representations. Post hoc interpretability analyses quantify contributions of relational structures and CpGs.

Result: On large-scale datasets, RelAge-GNN achieves competitive predictive accuracy and stronger correlation with chronological age compared to state-of-the-art methods. It also shows improved sensitivity in detecting age acceleration across diverse disease cohorts.

Conclusion: RelAge-GNN offers biologically meaningful insights into aging from DNA methylation data, highlighting the value of multi-relational modeling and providing directions for aging-related research; code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Aging clocks aim to estimate biological age, a measure of physiological state distinct from chronological age, from observable biomarkers, and are widely used for health assessment and disease analysis. DNA methylation is a particularly informative biomarker due to its stability and strong association with aging, and recent learning-based approaches have improved predictive performance. However, most existing methods treat CpG sites as independent features, overlooking the complex and heterogeneous biological relationships among them. We propose RelAge-GNN, a multi-relational graph neural network framework for DNA methylation-based age prediction. Our method constructs three complementary graphs capturing co-methylation patterns, genomic co-localization, and gene-level associations among CpG sites. Each graph is modeled by an independent GNN branch, and a learnable gating mechanism adaptively fuses the resulting representations. Experiments on large-scale datasets show that RelAge-GNN achieves competitive accuracy and stronger correlation with chronological age compared to state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, the model exhibits improved sensitivity in detecting age acceleration across diverse disease cohorts, highlighting its potential utility for disease characterization. Finally, through post hoc interpretability analyses, we quantify the contributions of different relational structures and CpG sites, providing biologically meaningful insights and suggesting potential directions for aging-related research. Our code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/RelAge-GNN-F1E3/.

</details>


### [255] [HyperEyes: Dual-Grained Efficiency-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Parallel Multimodal Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07177)
*Guankai Li,Jiabin Chen,Yi Xu,Xichen Zhang,Yuan Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HyperEyes introduces a parallel, efficiency-optimized multimodal search agent that performs concurrent multi-entity queries and treats inference efficiency as a first-class objective; it achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with substantially fewer tool calls via a two-stage training pipeline and a two-level RL framework, plus a new efficiency benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal search agents process entities sequentially, causing redundant rounds and inefficiency. The paper argues for wider, concurrent search to improve inference efficiency.

Method: HyperEyes fuses visual grounding and retrieval into a single atomic action enabling concurrent search across multiple entities. Training comprises two stages: (1) data synthesis with Parallel-Amenable Pipeline and Progressive Rejection Sampling to generate efficient trajectories; (2) a Dual-Grained Efficiency-Aware Reinforcement Learning framework with (a) TRACE trajectory-level reward that tightens over time to reduce unnecessary tool use, and (b) On-Policy Distillation providing dense token-level feedback from an external teacher for failed rollouts. They also introduce IMEB, a human-curated benchmark for efficiency-aware evaluation.

Result: Across six benchmarks, HyperEyes-30B achieves 9.9% higher accuracy than the strongest comparable open-source agent while using 5.3x fewer tool-call rounds on average.

Conclusion: Efficient, concurrent multimodal search is achievable with the proposed training paradigm and benchmark. The combination of a parallel grounding action, a monotone-reducing reward, and dense token-level signals yields substantial gains in accuracy and search efficiency.

Abstract: Existing multimodal search agents process target entities sequentially, issuing one tool call per entity and accumulating redundant interaction rounds whenever a query decomposes into independent sub-retrievals. We argue that effective multimodal agents should search wider rather than longer: dispatching multiple grounded queries concurrently within a round. To this end, we present HyperEyes, a parallel multimodal search agent that fuses visual grounding and retrieval into a single atomic action, enabling concurrent search across multiple entities while treating inference efficiency as a first-class training objective. HyperEyes is trained in two stages. For cold-start supervision, we develop a Parallel-Amenable Data Synthesis Pipeline covering visual multi-entity and textual multi-constraint queries, curating efficiency-oriented trajectories via Progressive Rejection Sampling. Building on this, our central contribution, a Dual-Grained Efficiency-Aware Reinforcement Learning framework, operates at two levels. At the macro level, we propose TRACE (Tool-use Reference-Adaptive Cost Efficiency), a trajectory-level reward whose reference is monotonically tightened during training to suppress superfluous tool calls without restricting genuine multi-hop search. At the micro level, we adapt On-Policy Distillation to inject dense token-level corrective signals from an external teacher on failed rollouts, mitigating the credit-assignment deficiency of sparse outcome rewards. Since existing benchmarks evaluate accuracy as the sole metric, omitting inference cost, we introduce IMEB, a human-curated benchmark of 300 instances that jointly evaluates search capability and efficiency. Across six benchmarks, HyperEyes-30B surpasses the strongest comparable open-source agent by 9.9% in accuracy with 5.3x fewer tool-call rounds on average.

</details>


### [256] [Coupling Models for One-Step Discrete Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07193)
*Fred Zhangzhi Peng,Avishek Joey Bose,Anru R. Zhang,Alexander Tong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: One-step discrete generative model (Coupling Models) learns a direct coupling between discrete data and Gaussian latents, enabling single-step sample generation without flow-based transforms or distillation. It outperforms strong one-step baselines across text, biology, and vision tasks and suggests that the data-noise coupling design is key for effective one-step discrete generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond autoregressive or multi-step refinement for discrete generation and avoid costly distillation or continuous normalizing flows on the simplex, enabling true one-shot generation.

Method: Introduce Coupling Models: a learned coupling between discrete sequences and Gaussian latents, with a purpose-built decoder that inverts this coupling to generate samples in one step. The approach avoids complex continuous flows and hand-designed data-to-noise couplings, presumably learning the forward coupling and a decoder to invert it.

Result: Empirically improves strongest one-step baselines: LM1B perplexity down 33% at the best point, Fly Brain enhancer-design FBD down 18%, MNIST-Binary FID down 46%. Code available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Single-step discrete generation can be highly effective when the data-noise coupling is designed appropriately; Coupling Models offer a simple, competitive alternative to distillation or flow-based methods for one-step generation across text, biology, and vision domains.

Abstract: Generative modeling over discrete structures underpins applications across deep learning, from biological sequence design and code generation to large language models, yet generation often remains sequential, relying on autoregressive decoding or iterative refinement. In this work, we introduce Coupling Models(Coupling Models), a one-step discrete generative model that learns a direct coupling between discrete sequences and Gaussian latents. Unlike recent distillation methods that compress a pretrained multi-step sampler into a few steps, Coupling Model trains a purpose-built decoder to invert this coupling and generate samples in a single step. The model also avoids complex continuous flows over the simplex and hand-specified data-to-noise couplings. Empirically,Coupling Model improves the strongest one-step baselines in each domain: it reduces LM1B text-generation perplexity by 33% at its lowest-perplexity operating point, Fly Brain enhancer-design FBD by 18%, and MNIST-Binary FID by 46%. These results suggest that effective one-step discrete generation depends strongly on how data and noise are coupled before decoding. Code is available at https://github.com/pengzhangzhi/Coupling-Models.

</details>


### [257] [Arrow: A Foundation Model for Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07204)
*Ryan Thompson,He Zhao,Daniel M. Steinberg,Edwin V. Bonilla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Arrow is a transformer-based foundation model for zero-shot causal discovery on observational tabular data. It factorizes a DAG into a skeleton and a topological order, enabling acyclicity by construction, and is pretrained on synthetic data to generalize to new datasets with fast inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal discovery from observational data faces combinatorial search, identifiability, and computational costs. There is a need for reusable, scalable methods that can generalize across datasets and yield fast, zero-shot results.

Method: Represent DAG with skeleton (undirected edges) and node order. Use transformer to contextualize variables and predict skeleton edge probabilities and node order scores. Train end-to-end with differentiable directed edge composite likelihood derived from skeleton-order factorization. Train on diverse synthetic graphs with ground-truth edges and orders; inference on new data yields graph by combining skeleton and order.

Result: Arrow matches or outperforms existing causal discovery methods on in- and out-of-distribution synthetic, semi-synthetic, and real datasets, while offering substantially lower inference cost than competitive alternatives.

Conclusion: Large-scale pretraining on diverse synthetic data enables zero-shot causal discovery that is fast, accurate, and reusable for new datasets.

Abstract: We introduce Arrow, a foundation model for zero-shot causal discovery on observational tabular data. Arrow factorizes a directed acyclic graph into an undirected skeleton and a topological order, guaranteeing acyclicity by construction. Given a new dataset, it uses a transformer-based architecture to contextualize variables within and across observations, then predicts skeleton edge probabilities and node order scores that together define a graph. Arrow is trained in a supervised fashion on synthetic datasets with ground-truth graphs, using an end-to-end differentiable directed edge composite likelihood induced by the skeleton-order factorization. The training distribution spans diverse graph families, functional forms, noise models, and dataset shapes. Across in- and out-of-distribution synthetic, semi-synthetic, and real datasets, Arrow matches or outperforms existing causal discovery methods at substantially lower inference cost than competitive alternatives. Our results demonstrate that large-scale pretraining on diverse synthetic data can yield zero-shot causal discovery models that are fast, accurate, and reusable on new datasets.

</details>


### [258] [FAME: Forecasting Academic Impact via Continuous-Time Manifold Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07208)
*Jianrong Ding,Jianyuan Zhong,Zhengyan Shi,Qiang Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FAME is a trajectory-aware framework for forecasting scholarly impact by learning a continuous-time dynamic latent space from text and knowledge-flow graphs; it outperforms LLM evaluators and enhances LLM forecasting when integrated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assessing the future impact of research ideas is difficult; static text judgments are insufficient and there is a need for verifiable, prospective benchmarks for scientific evaluation.

Method: Proposes FAME, a spatiotemporal framework that projects papers into a dynamic latent space guided by textual features and a verified knowledge-flow graph, enforcing geometric constraints so impactful papers align with forward momentum; uses continuous-time manifold evolution and experiments on 3,200 arXiv papers across three fast-evolving subfields.

Result: FAME consistently and substantially outperforms state-of-the-art LLM evaluators in prospective multidimensional impact forecasting; integrating FAME's dynamic geometric signals into LLMs further improves forecasting performance.

Conclusion: Manuscript impact forecasting is a useful proxy benchmark; FAME provides a trajectory-aware foundation for automated scientific evaluation and can enhance LLM-based judging.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to brainstorm and evaluate research ideas, yet assessing such judgments is fundamentally difficult because the true impact of a new idea may take years to emerge. We address this challenge by using the impact forecasting of human-authored manuscripts as a verifiable proxy task. In a prospective forecasting study, we find that frontier LLMs fail to reliably distinguish high-impact papers from ordinary publications, suggesting that static text-based judging is insufficient for scientific evaluation. To address this limitation, we propose $\textbf{FAME}$ ($\underline{\text{F}}$orecasting $\underline{\text{A}}$cademic Impact via Continuous-Time $\underline{\text{M}}$anifold $\underline{\text{E}}$volution), a spatiotemporal framework for modeling the dynamic trajectories of scientific topics. FAME projects papers into a dynamic latent space informed by textual features and a verified knowledge-flow graph, learning geometric constraints that align impactful manuscripts with the forward momentum of their fields. Experiments on 3,200 arXiv papers across three fast-evolving subfields show that FAME consistently and substantially outperforms state-of-the-art LLM evaluators in prospective multidimensional impact forecasting. Furthermore, integrating FAME's dynamic geometric signals into LLMs significantly improves their forecasting performance. These results support manuscript impact forecasting as a useful, measurable proxy benchmark and position FAME as a strong, trajectory-aware foundation for automated scientific evaluation.

</details>


### [259] [HARMONY: Bridging the Personalization-Generalization Gap by Mitigating Representation Skew in Heterogeneous Split Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07211)
*Jiseok Youn,You Rim Choi,Goodsol Lee,Sangtae Ha,Hyung-Sin Kim,Saewoong Bahk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HARMONY enables heterogeneous client architectures in hybrid split federated learning by meta-learning diverse extractors and aligning server-side features through contrastive learning, achieving substantial accuracy gains with acceptable latency and preserved personalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile devices have resource constraints and non-IID data; existing hybrid SFL suffers from representation skew under heterogeneity, degrading OOD server predictions.

Method: Meta-learning to simulate diverse extractors across models to enable personalization; server-side contrastive learning aligns features without sharing raw labels or compromising personalization.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art across datasets/model families, with up to 43.0% accuracy improvement without OOD and 28.3% with OOD; latency remains acceptable.

Conclusion: HARMONY is the first framework to support heterogeneous client architectures in hybrid SFL, mitigating representation skew while preserving personalization and efficiency.

Abstract: Mobile devices face diverse resource constraints and non-IID data class distributions, requiring fast on-device inference for local in-distribution (ID) classes and on-demand remote support for client-specific out-of-distribution (OOD) classes. Hybrid split federated learning (Hybrid SFL) couples personalized client-side front ends (supporting early exit) with a generalized server-side backend for fallback inference, balancing accuracy and cost. However, under client architectural heterogeneity, the existing hybrid SFL suffers from representation skew, where features from customized extractors fail to align in the shared space, leading to a sharp degradation in the server model responsible for OOD prediction. We propose HARMONY, the first hybrid SFL framework to support heterogeneous client architectures. HARMONY modifies meta-learning to simulate diverse extractors across parameters and architectures, and to learn to personalize. To mitigate representation skew, HARMONY conducts server-side contrastive learning to align extracted features, neither sacrificing clients' personalization nor sharing raw labels. Compared to the state of the art across multiple datasets and model families, HARMONY improves test accuracy by up to 43.0%/28.3% without/with OOD, respectively, while maintaining acceptable latency.

</details>


### [260] [Same Brain, Different Prediction: How Preprocessing Choices Undermine EEG Decoding Reliability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07212)
*Dengzhe Hou,Zihao Wu,Lingyu Jiang,Zirui Li,Fangzhou Lin,Kazunori D. Yamada*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EEG deep learning predictions are highly unstable to preprocessing choices, with up to 42% of trial-level predictions flipping when varying the preprocessing pipeline; the work introduces a Walsh-Hadamard decomposition to analyze sensitivity, Preprocessing Uncertainty (PU) as a per-trial diagnostic, and Normalized Adaptive PGI (NA-PGI) as a graph-regularized mitigation exploiting pipeline structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preprocessing acts as a counterfactual intervention space in EEG analysis, yet standard uncertainty quantifies only fixed pipelines. This undermines reliability, reproducibility, and interpretability of DL EEG models.

Method: Formalize preprocessing as a 2^7 binary intervention space; evaluate across six datasets and four paradigms; apply Walsh-Hadamard decomposition to assess sensitivity; define Preprocessing Uncertainty (PU) per trial; propose Normalized Adaptive PGI (NA-PGI) as a graph-structured regularizer to mitigate instability with clear scope conditions.

Result:  found that up to 42% of trials flip across preprocessing changes; Walsh-Hadamard decomposition reveals near-additive sensitivity in practice; PU provides a complementary diagnostic to model-based confidence; NA-PGI reduces instability under specified scope conditions.

Conclusion: Preprocessing choices materially affect EEG model predictions; the proposed framework (measurement via PU, decomposition via Walsh-Hadamard, and mitigation via NA-PGI) enables more robust, interpretable, and reproducible EEG DL modeling and reporting.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a cornerstone of brain-computer interfaces and clinical neuroscience, yet deep learning models are typically trained and evaluated under a single, unreported preprocessing pipeline. We formalize preprocessing choices as a counterfactual intervention space and show that EEG predictions are surprisingly unstable under this space: across six datasets spanning four paradigms, up to 42% of trial-level predictions flip when only the preprocessing changes, a variability that standard uncertainty methods do not explicitly quantify because they condition on a fixed preprocessing pipeline. We provide three tools to make this instability measurable, decomposable, and reducible. First, a Walsh-Hadamard decomposition of the 2^7 pipeline space reveals that sensitivity is near-additive in practice under the binary intervention design, enabling efficient step-by-step optimization. Second, we introduce Preprocessing Uncertainty (PU), a per-trial diagnostic that captures a dimension of instability complementary to model-based confidence. Third, we study Normalized Adaptive PGI (NA-PGI), a graph-structured regularizer that exploits the compositional structure of preprocessing interventions as one mitigation strategy with clear scope conditions.

</details>


### [261] [Improved Model-based Reinforcement Learning with Smooth Kernels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07218)
*Kun Long,Yuqiang Li,Xianyi Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Kernel-smoothing model-based online RL for finite-horizon Lipschitz MDPs using Bernstein-style bonuses; achieves improved horizon-dependent regret; introduces a Bernstein-type martingale concentration inequality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of restrictive structural assumptions (e.g., low-rank MDPs) in continuous state-action RL by leveraging smoothness and non-parametric kernel estimates to achieve better sample efficiency in online finite-horizon settings.

Method: A kernel-smoothing, model-based online RL algorithm under Lipschitz continuity; incorporates a Bernstein-style exploration bonus within the kernel framework; finite-horizon analysis with careful handling of kernel bias/variance and exploration terms.

Result: Regret bound with improved horizon dependence compared to state-of-the-art; demonstrates a favorable interaction between Bernstein bonuses and kernel smoothing; introduces a new tight Bernstein-type concentration inequality for martingales that may have independent interest.

Conclusion: The proposed kernel-smoothing with Bernstein bonus advances online RL under smooth MDPs, providing improved theoretical guarantees; the novel martingale inequality could be broadly useful beyond this work.

Abstract: For continuous state-action space scenarios, classical reinforcement learning (RL) theory predominantly focuses on low-rank Markov decision processes (MDPs), which provide sample-efficient guarantees at the expense of restrictive structural assumptions. Kernel smoothing model-based approaches offer a promising alternative paradigm that instead leverages the smoothness of the MDP and employs non-parametric kernel smoothing estimates of transition dynamics. This paper proposes a new kernel-smoothing model-based approach for online reinforcement learning in finite-horizon settings under Lipschitz continuity assumptions on the MDP. By incorporating a Bernstein-style exploration bonus into the kernel smoothing framework, our method achieves a regret bound which improves upon the state-of-the-art regret bound in its dependence on the horizon. The theoretical advancement relies on a delicate analysis of the synergy between Bernstein-style bonuses and kernel smoothing, where a new tight Bernstein-type concentration inequality for martingales may be of independent interest.

</details>


### [262] [On the Robustness of Distribution Support under Diffusion Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07220)
*Ruijia Cao,Yuchen Wu,Nisha Chadramoorthy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion guidance is theoretically justified: with exact score access, guided diffusion stays near the target support across DDIM, DDPM, and exponential-integrator discretizations, providing a rigorous basis for the plausibility of generated samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of theoretical understanding of why diffusion guidance yields consistently high-quality, structurally plausible samples; establish a property (robustness of support) that explains the empirical success.

Method: Theoretical analysis proving a robustness-of-support property for guided diffusion with exact score functions; the analysis covers both DDIM and DDPM and a broad class of discretization schemes induced by exponential integrators.

Result: Guided diffusion, under exact score access, almost surely generates samples that remain close to the target support; off-support samples are structurally implausible and unlikely; results hold across the analyzed models and discretizations.

Conclusion: Provides a rigorous foundation for understanding why diffusion guidance yields physically meaningful and structurally plausible samples, clarifying the conditions under which high-quality results are achieved.

Abstract: Diffusion guidance is a powerful technique that enables controllable and high-fidelity sample generation with diffusion models. At a high level, it modifies the score function by incorporating a guidance term that steers the generative process toward a desired condition. Despite its empirical success, the theoretical properties of diffusion guidance remain largely unexplored, and it is not well understood why it consistently produces high-quality samples.
  In this work, we explain the effectiveness of diffusion guidance by establishing a \emph{robustness of support} property. Specifically, we show that, given exact access to the score functions, guided diffusion processes almost always generate samples that remain close to the target support. This property is particularly desirable, as samples that lie off the support are often structurally implausible and may adversely affect downstream tasks. Our analysis covers both Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) and Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM), and applies to a wide range of discretization schemes induced by exponential integrators. Our results provide a rigorous foundation for understanding why diffusion guidance produces physically meaningful and structurally plausible samples.

</details>


### [263] [Don't Learn the Shape: Forecasting Periodic Time Series by Rank-1 Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07222)
*Takato Honda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FLAIR achieves competitive forecasting of periodic time series with very few parameters using a fixed rank-1 structure per period, yielding a fast, tuning-free, closed-form method that avoids overfitting in high-rank regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate parameter efficiency for forecasting periodic signals and test whether smoothing/low-rank methods offer real gains over a simple baseline.

Method: Compare 8 alternatives (e.g., Fourier, EWMA, James-Stein, rank-r SVD) across 97 GIFT-Eval configurations against a frozen baseline (last 2 cycles), reshape hourly data into 24xN. Develop FLAIR as a per-period SVD-based estimator with GCV-averaged Ridge, closed-form, no GPU, no pre-training, and no per-task tuning.

Result: Across all configurations, none of the 8 alternatives significantly beat the frozen baseline after Holm correction; two perform worse. FLAIR matches PatchTST on aggregate GIFT-Eval (relMASE 0.838 vs 0.849), is compact (28 scalars hourly, 57 weekly), fast (22 minutes on one CPU core), closed-form, and hands-off.

Conclusion: In high-rank-1, many-cycle regimes, added flexibility mainly adds estimation noise; a simple, per-period, low-parameter approach like FLAIR achieves effective, compact, fast forecasting without tuning.

Abstract: How few parameters do we really need to forecast a periodic time series? An hourly electricity series, reshaped as a 24-row matrix with one column per day, is approximately rank-1: a daily shape modulated by a daily level (median centered rank-1 energy 0.82 on GIFT-Eval). Should we learn the shape? Smoothing, shrinkage, and low-rank fits all seem like obvious upgrades over the simple average of the last K=2 cycles. On all 97 GIFT-Eval configurations, we tested 8 such alternatives (e.g., Fourier, EWMA, James-Stein, rank-r SVD): none significantly beats the frozen baseline under Holm correction; two are significantly worse. The resulting method, FLAIR, is (a) Effective: matches PatchTST on aggregate GIFT-Eval (relMASE 0.838 vs 0.849); (b) Compact: 28 scalars for hourly, 57 for weekly; (c) Fast: 22 minutes on one CPU core of a MacBook Pro; (d) Closed-form & Hands-Off: one SVD per period candidate, GCV-averaged Ridge, no GPU, no pre-training, no per-task tuning. In the high-rank-1, many-cycle regime, extra flexibility is estimation noise.

</details>


### [264] [Modulated learning for private and distributed regression with just a single sample per client device](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07233)
*Praneeth Vepakomma,Amirhossein Reisizadeh,Samuel Horváth,Munther Dahleh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A single-sample-per-client private FL method that perturbs each data point with calibrated noise to produce a privatized representation, which the server aggregates to form an unbiased gradient update that matches the non-private centralized gradient in expectation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many devices contribute only one data sample, causing ineffective local updates and compounded privacy noise in standard federated learning; there is a need for a privacy-preserving collaboration where extremely small datasets can still learn a global model.

Method: Each client applies a carefully calibrated noisy perturbation to its single data sample to produce a transformed representation, which is sent to the server after post-processing. The server aggregates these representations to compute an unbiased gradient update whose expectation equals the centralized non-private gradient, enabling private learning. This approach relies on data-transformation instead of sharing model coefficients, unlike traditional DP-FL.

Result: The method yields an unbiased gradient in expectation that approximates the centralized gradient while preserving data privacy, enabling devices with very limited data to collaboratively learn a global model without exposing raw data or requiring large local datasets.

Conclusion: This approach extends private federated learning to one-shot clients by using privatized data representations and unbiased gradient aggregation, offering a privacy-preserving alternative to model-parameter sharing and enabling accurate learning with minimal local data.

Abstract: This work focuses on the question of learning from a large number of devices with each device holding only a single sample of data. Several real-world applications exist to this one sample per client setup up including learning from fitness trackers, data/app usage aggregators, body-worn sensing devices, and daily event monitors to name a few. When a client has only one sample, the standard federated learning paradigm breaks down as a local update based on that single point is far from being useful, especially in the earlier rounds for estimation of the model coefficients. This utility is further weakened by the privacy-inducing noise applied at every round. This work caters to this problem to enable such clients to collaboratively contribute to effectively learn a global model without leaking the privacy of their data. The proposed approach injects a single, carefully calibrated noisy perturbation to transform the sample at each client, followed by a post-processed representation which is shared with the server. These representations aggregated at the server are processed to obtain an unbiased gradient update that in expectation matches the non-private centralized gradient while preserving data privacy. This approach is different than traditional private federated learning, where the communication payloads involve model coefficients as opposed to privately transformed data samples. This method enables devices with extremely limited data to collaborate and learn accurate, privacy-preserving models without requiring large local datasets or sacrificing individual privacy.

</details>


### [265] [Sample Complexity of Stochastic Optimization with Integer Variables](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07239)
*Hongyu Cheng,Yinghao Zheng,Marco Molinaro,Amitabh Basu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This work studies how moving from continuous to integer variables in stochastic optimization affects the number of samples needed to reach ε-accuracy, revealing equal, smaller, or larger sample complexities depending on geometry and convexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand data/sample efficiency implications of discretizing decisions; compare discrete stochastic optimization to its continuous counterpart; clarify when discretization helps or hurts.

Method: Derives tight sample complexity bounds for stochastic optimization with integer variables under specific geometric settings: Lipschitz objectives over subsets of the l_infty ball, Lipschitz objectives over subsets of the l2 ball, and strongly convex smooth objectives. Compares to continuous analogs and establishes tight bounds (Ω(1/ε^2) vs O(1/ε) in different regimes).

Result: 1) For Lipschitz objectives on l_infty subsets, integer complexity equals that of stochastic linear with bound constraints. 2) For Lipschitz on l2 subsets, integer can require strictly fewer samples in some regime. 3) For strongly convex smooth objectives, integer optimization needs Ω(1/ε^2) samples, versus O(1/ε) in continuous.

Conclusion: Sample complexity gap between integer and continuous stochastic optimization is regime- and geometry-dependent; the paper provides tight results and shows integer optimization can be more or less sample-efficient; suggests nuanced guidance for decision discretization in stochastic optimization.

Abstract: We establish sample complexity results for stochastic optimization over the integers, especially with a view to understand the complexity with respect to the corresponding continuous optimization problem. We show that integer optimization can sometimes require strictly more samples and sometimes strictly smaller number of samples, depending on the structure of the objective and constraints. 1. For Lipschitz objectives over subsets of the $\ell_\infty$ ball, the statistical complexity of general stochastic mixed-integer, nonlinear, nonconvex optimization is exactly the same as stochastic linear optimization with just bound constraints. 2. For Lipschitz objectives over subsets of the $\ell_2$ ball, we show that integer optimization can require strictly *smaller* sample size compared to the continuous setting in a certain regime. To get to this result, we also establish tight sample complexity results for nonconvex continuous stochastic optimization which, to the best of our knowledge, do not appear in prior work. 3. For strongly convex, smooth objectives, integer optimization has high statistical complexity compared to the continuous setting. In particular, we show that integer optimization requires $Ω(1/ε^2)$ samples to report an $ε$-approximate solution, compared to the well-known $O(1/ε)$ sample complexity from the continuous optimization literature.

</details>


### [266] [Experience Sharing in Mutual Reinforcement Learning for Heterogeneous Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07244)
*Xiaoze Liu,Dhananjay Ram,Yuting Zhang,Zhaoyang Zhang,Wei Xia,Stefano Soatto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Mutual Reinforcement Learning (MRL) for concurrent RL post-training among heterogeneous LLM policies, enabling typed experience exchange while preserving separate parameters, objectives, and tokenizers. It combines Shared Experience Exchange (SEE), Multi-Worker Resource Allocation (MWRA), and Tokenizer Heterogeneity Layer (THL); instantiates PRP, XGRPO, and SGT on GRPO; analyzes stability–support trade-offs via contextual-bandit; outcomes-level sharing (SGT) is most favorable in evaluated regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable cross-model experience sharing post-training across diverse policy architectures and vocabularies without altering individual models’ objectives or tokenizers, thereby leveraging peer knowledge while preserving autonomy.

Method: Proposes an integrated substrate (SEE, MWRA, THL) and implements three probes on GRPO: PRP (data-level rollout sharing), XGRPO (value-level advantage sharing), and SGT (outcome-level transfer). Applies contextual-bandit analysis to map stability–support trade-offs.

Result: Trade-off analysis shows PRP incurs density-ratio variance and THL residual costs; XGRPO preserves on-policy actor support while shifting scalar baselines; SGT provides a rescue-set direction toward verified peer successes, with outcome-sharing occupying the favorable stability–support point in the evaluated regime.

Conclusion: MRL with THL-enabled cross-model experience exchange offers a practical framework for post-training collaboration across model families, with outcome-level sharing delivering the most favorable balance; results motivate further refinement of THL and exploration of additional transfer modalities.

Abstract: We introduce Mutual Reinforcement Learning, a framework for concurrent RL post-training in which heterogeneous LLM policies exchange typed experience while keeping separate parameters, objectives, and tokenizers. The framework combines a Shared Experience Exchange (SEE), Multi-Worker Resource Allocation (MWRA), and a Tokenizer Heterogeneity Layer (THL) that retokenizes text and aligns token-level traces across incompatible vocabularies. This substrate makes the experience-sharing design question operational across model families. We instantiate three controlled probes on top of GRPO: data-level rollout sharing via Peer Rollout Pooling (PRP), value-level advantage sharing via Cross-Policy GRPO Advantage Sharing (XGRPO), and outcome-level success transfer via Success-Gated Transfer (SGT). A contextual-bandit analysis characterizes their structural positions on a stability-support trade-off: PRP pays density-ratio variance and THL residual costs, XGRPO preserves on-policy actor support while changing scalar baselines, and SGT supplies a rescue-set score direction toward verified peer successes. In the evaluated regime, outcome-level sharing occupies the favorable point of this trade-off.

</details>


### [267] [When Are Experts Misrouted? Counterfactual Routing Analysis in Mixture-of-Experts Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07260)
*Youngsik Yoon,Siwei Wang,Wei Chen,Jungseul Ok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Suboptimal MoE routing for fragile tokens; evaluating equal-compute alternatives reveals standard top-k routes are not always utility-aligned; a minimal final-layer router update can improve downstream tasks, indicating routing misallocation rather than capacity limits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Directly assess token-level routing quality in MoE models beyond standard training signals to understand why routers underperform on hard reasoning tokens and whether routing decisions can be improved.

Method: Fix model; compare standard top-k router vs equal-compute alternative routes for the same token. Score routes by the next-token probability of the realized token along a verified reasoning trajectory. Analyze across multiple models (Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-OSS-20B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite, OLMoE-1B-7B). Note that standard loss evaluates only the executed route and load balancing uses aggregate statistics. Apply a minimal router-only update to the final-layer router and measure shifts in pass@K on AIME/HMMT.

Result: Standard router aligns with route utility on confident tokens but is uninformative on fragile, hard-reasoning tokens. Lower-loss equal-compute routes exist inside the frozen model but are not selected. The pattern persists across models; routing decisions are not fully captured by token loss or load-balancing statistics.

Conclusion: Partially due to router-reachable misallocation rather than pure capacity limits. A small router update to the final-layer router can shift task performance, indicating that improving routing efficiency is a viable path to better MoE models.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models route each token to a small subset of experts, but whether the routes selected by a trained top-$k$ router are good ones is rarely evaluated directly. Holding the model fixed, we compare each standard route against sampled equal-compute alternatives for the same token and score each by the next-token probability it assigns to the realized token in a verified reasoning trajectory. The result is sharply token-conditional: the standard router is well-aligned with route utility on confident tokens but uninformative on the fragile tokens that drive hard reasoning, where lower-loss equal-compute routes consistently exist inside the frozen model but are not selected. The same pattern holds across Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-OSS-20B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite, and OLMoE-1B-7B, and follows structurally from how standard top-$k$ training evaluates routing decisions: the language modeling loss scores only the executed route, and load balancing depends only on aggregate routing statistics. A minimal router-only update to the final-layer router, leaving every expert and every other router frozen, is sufficient to shift pass@K on AIME 2024+2025 and HMMT 2025 for both Qwen3-30B-A3B and GPT-OSS-20B, suggesting that at least part of the failure reflects router-reachable misallocation rather than expert capacity alone.

</details>


### [268] [PerCaM-Health: Personalized Dynamic Causal Graphs for Healthcare Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07267)
*Elahe Khatibi,Ziyu Wang,Saba A. Farahani,Di Huang,Hung Cao,Ramesh Jain,Amir M. Rahmani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PerCaM-Health learns personalized dynamic causal graphs for health data by starting from a knowledge-guided population temporal graph and adaptively updating it with patient-specific temporal evidence and rolling windows, enabling patient-level counterfactual queries and improved decision support.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a fundamental gap between population-level causal models (stable but non-personalized) and the need for patient-specific, time-varying mechanisms for intervention reasoning in healthcare; existing methods either lack personalization or struggle with short, noisy, irregular trajectories.

Method: Construct a knowledge-guided population temporal graph; conservatively adapt and evolve it using patient-specific temporal evidence and rolling-window updates; couple the graphs with temporal structural equations to enable counterfactual queries; provide interpretable and auditable graph sequences.

Result: On a semi-synthetic dynamic health benchmark, PerCaM-Health improves graph recovery, dynamic edge tracking, and intervention direction accuracy compared to cohort-level, per-patient, and non-personalized baselines.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling personalization and temporal evolution yields more reliable causal structure and intervention reasoning for patient-specific healthcare decision making.

Abstract: Personalized healthcare decisions require reasoning about how physiological and behavioral variables influence an individual patient over time. Existing temporal causal discovery methods are poorly matched to this setting: cohort-level models provide stable but non-personalized structures, while per-patient discovery is unreliable because individual trajectories are short, noisy, irregular, and non-stationary. This creates a fundamental gap between population-level causal modeling and the patient-specific, time-varying mechanisms needed for intervention reasoning. We introduce PerCaM-Health, a framework for learning personalized dynamic causal graphs from longitudinal health data. The framework learns a knowledge-guided population temporal graph, then conservatively adapts and evolves it using patient-specific temporal evidence and rolling-window updates, producing interpretable and auditable graph sequences. By coupling these graphs with temporal structural equations, the framework enables patient-level counterfactual queries, such as estimating short-horizon outcome changes under hypothetical behavioral interventions. Experiments on a semi-synthetic dynamic health benchmark show that PerCaM-Health improves graph recovery, dynamic edge tracking, and intervention direction accuracy compared to cohort-level, per-patient, and non-personalized temporal baselines. These results demonstrate that jointly modeling personalization and temporal evolution yields more reliable causal structure and intervention reasoning.

</details>


### [269] [bispectrum: Selective $G$-Bispectra Made Practical](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07270)
*Johan Mathe,Adele Myers,Simon Mataigne,Nina Miolane*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a PyTorch library, bispectrum, implementing selective G-bispectra for seven groups as differentiable modules, achieving faster, near-exact G-invariance and improved pooling in DL, with strong low-data gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many ML tasks are invariant to group actions; the G-bispectrum is a complete invariant but costly and patchy in implementations. There is need for a scalable, open-source, differentiable library to enable G-invariant representations in standard ML pipelines.

Method: Develop selective G-bispectra for seven groups, with cost reductions: O(|G|) for finite groups (vs O(|G|^2)); use disk bispectrum for planar rotations; use augmented selective bispectrum at band-limit L for spherical 3D rotations to achieve O(L^2) from O(L^3); implement as differentiable PyTorch modules; optimize compute and profile; evaluate as pooling layers in DL models.

Result: Library achieves near-exact G-invariance with selective bispectra on GPU, delivering sub-millisecond runtimes for common bandlimits; outperforms norm pooling, gated pooling, Fourier-ELU pooling, max pooling, and non-equivariant baselines on three classical benchmarks, especially in low-data, moderate-capacity regimes.

Conclusion: Selective G-bispectra provide practical, scalable, complete invariants for group actions; the bispectrum library makes G-invariant representations readily usable in modern DL pipelines, enabling improved performance with limited data across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Many machine learning tasks are invariant under the action of a group $G$ of transformations: signal classification can be invariant under translations, image classification under 2D rotations, and spherical-image classification under 3D rotations. The $G$-bispectrum is a principled complete invariant of a signal (retaining all all signal's information up to the group action) with proven benefits in machine learning and as a pooling layer in deep networks. However, its deployment has been hampered by high computational cost and a patchwork of group-specific implementations. We present bispectrum, an open-source, fully unit-tested PyTorch library that implements selective $G$-bispectra for seven different group actions, as differentiable modules that can be directly incorporated into machine learning pipelines and deep learning architectures. For finite groups $G$, selectivity reduces the computational cost from $O(|G|^2)$ to $O(|G|)$. For planar rotations, we leverage the disk bispectrum. For spherical 3D rotations, we introduce an augmented selective bispectrum at band-limit $L$ which reduces the cost from $O(L^3)$ to $Θ(L^2)$ coefficients. We profile the entire library (for which we implemented various compute optimizations), showing that it delivers near-exact $G$-invariance with its selective $G$-bispectra computed in sub-millisecond time on GPU (up to commonly used bandlimits). We evaluate the benefits of incorporating $G$-bispectra as pooling layers into deep learning architectures on three classical benchmark datasets --comparing against norm pooling, gated pooling, Fourier-ELU pooling, max pooling, and (non-equivariant) data-augmented convolutional baselines. Results show that $G$-bispectra consistently outperform alternatives in the low-data, moderate-capacity regime.

</details>


### [270] [Bifurcation Models: Learning Set-Valued Solution Maps with Weight-Tied Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07277)
*Caleb Jore,Jialin Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A weight-tied dynamical system (bifurcation model) captures multiple valid solutions as attractors, showing that set-valued maps can be represented by regular equilibria; diversity of solutions can be promoted explicitly but with an accuracy-diversity trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many scientific and combinatorial problems have multiple correct solutions. Standard supervision selects a single label, which is arbitrary and hard to learn. A framework that represents a landscape of attractors (multiple equilibria) can model the full solution set without forcing a single target.

Method: Introduce bifurcation models with shared weights that yield different stable equilibria from different initializations. Prove that broad set-valued maps with locally Lipschitz branches can be represented by regular equilibrium dynamics; show induced selectors are almost everywhere regular, while manual selectors can be arbitrarily irregular. Evaluate on frustrated Ising models to demonstrate discovery of multiple equilibria without branch labels and on Allen–Cahn flows to study explicit diversity control and the associated accuracy-diversity trade-off.

Result: They establish a representation of set-valued maps via regular equilibrium dynamics and prove almost-everywhere regularity of the induced selectors; manual (ad-hoc) selectors can be irregular. Experiments show the dynamics find multiple valid equilibria without branch labels and outperform single-branch supervision; diversity can be encouraged in Allen–Cahn but with an accuracy–diversity trade-off.

Conclusion: Bifurcation-based, weight-tied dynamics provide a principled framework for learning and representing multi-solution problems, enabling discovery of multiple valid equilibria and explicit control of diversity, albeit with trade-offs in accuracy for diversity.

Abstract: Many scientific and combinatorial problems admit multiple correct solutions, not a single label. Standard supervised learning resolves this ambiguity by choosing one solution as the target, but this hidden selector can be arbitrary, discontinuous, and harder to learn than the underlying solution set. We study bifurcation models, a weight-tied dynamical view in which different initializations can converge to different stable equilibria, so the model represents an attractor landscape rather than one chosen branch. We prove that broad set-valued maps with locally Lipschitz branches can be represented by regular equilibrium dynamics and that the induced selectors are almost everywhere regular, while manual selectors can be arbitrarily irregular. Experiments on frustrated Ising models show that such dynamics can discover multiple valid equilibria without branch labels and outperform single-branch supervision. Allen--Cahn experiments further show that diversity is not automatic: it can be encouraged explicitly, but with an accuracy--diversity tradeoff.

</details>


### [271] [Predictive but Not Plannable: RC-aux for Latent World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07278)
*Wenyuan Li,Guang Li,Keisuke Maeda,Takahiro Ogawa,Miki Haseyama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RC-aux provides a lightweight, planning-aligned correction for latent world models by introducing (i) multi-horizon open-loop predictions and (ii) budget-aware reachability supervision with negative samples, improving planning under limited action budgets without changing the backbone model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent world models often yield accurate short-horizon predictions but produce latent spaces misaligned with planning due to spatiotemporal mismatch. Euclidean distances in latent space may not align with reachability under a finite action budget, hindering long-horizon goal-directed search.

Method: RC-aux adds two planning-aligned supervision signals without altering the world-model backbone: (a) time axis: multi-horizon open-loop prediction beyond one-step to enforce long-horizon consistency; (b) space axis: budget-conditioned reachability supervision plus temporal hard negatives to shape latent space so that states reachable within the budget are distinguished from those requiring more steps; at test time, a reachability signal can guide planning.

Result: Applied to LeWorldModel in continuation-training and matched-from-scratch setups; on goal-conditioned pixel-control tasks and LIBERO-Goal extension, RC-aux improves planning performance with modest computational cost, suggesting latent representations need to encode temporal/geometric structure for effective search.

Conclusion: Planning with latent world models benefits from representations that encode temporal horizons and reachable-state geometry. RC-aux provides a practical augmentation to existing models, improving planning without major backbone changes; code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: A latent world model may achieve accurate short-horizon prediction while still inducing a latent space that is poorly aligned with planning. A key issue is spatiotemporal mismatch: these models are often trained with local predictive supervision, but deployed for long-horizon goal-directed search in latent spaces where Euclidean distance may not reflect what is reachable within a finite action budget. We present the Reachability-Correction auxiliary objective (RC-aux), a lightweight correction for this mismatch in reconstruction-free latent world models. RC-aux keeps the world-model backbone unchanged and adds planning-aligned supervision along two axes. Along the time axis, multi-horizon open-loop prediction trains the model beyond one-step consistency. Along the space axis, budget-conditioned reachability supervision, together with temporal hard negatives, encourages the latent space to distinguish states that are eventually reachable from those reachable within the current planning horizon. At test time, the learned reachability signal can also be used by a reachability-aware planner to favor trajectories that are both goal-directed and attainable under the available budget. We instantiate RC-aux on LeWorldModel and evaluate it under both continuation-training and matched-from-scratch settings. Across goal-conditioned pixel-control tasks and a LIBERO-Goal extension, RC-aux improves LeWM-style planning with modest additional cost. These results suggest that planning with latent world models depends not only on predictive accuracy, but also on whether the learned representation encodes the temporal and geometric structure required by downstream search. The code is available at https://github.com/Guang000/RC-aux.

</details>


### [272] [Mask2Cause: Causal Discovery via Adjacency Constrained Causal Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07280)
*Omar Muhammad,Pasupuleti Dhruv Shivkant,Deepak N. Subramani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Mask2Cause offers end-to-end causal discovery for time-series forecasting by integrating inverted variable embedding and adjacency-constrained masked attention, achieving state-of-the-art discovery and substantial parameter reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Causal discovery in time series is hampered by component-wise models and post-hoc graph extraction, which fail to capture shared system dynamics and can overfit to spurious correlations.

Method: An end-to-end framework that recovers the causal graph during the forecasting forward pass, introducing inverted variable embedding and adjacency-constrained masked attention, trained with homoscedastic or heteroscedastic objectives to model causal effects in mean and variance.

Result: Empirical results on synthetic chaotic dynamics and realistic biological simulations show state-of-the-art causal discovery with significantly reduced parameter complexity; inferred graphs enable >70% reduction in forecasting model parameters without sacrificing accuracy.

Conclusion: Mask2Cause demonstrates effective integrated causal discovery in time-series forecasting, delivering improved efficiency and generality across domains.

Abstract: Leveraging deep learning for causal discovery in time series remains challenging because existing neural methods predominantly rely on component-wise architectures that fail to capture shared system dynamics or employ decoupled post-hoc graph extraction that risks overfitting to spurious correlations. We propose $\textbf{Mask2Cause}$, an end-to-end framework that recovers the underlying causal graph directly during the forecasting forward pass. Our approach introduces an Inverted Variable Embedding and an Adjacency-Constrained Masked Attention mechanism, trained with homoscedastic or heteroscedastic objectives to capture causal influences in both mean and variance. Empirical results on diverse benchmarks, from synthetic chaotic dynamics to realistic biological simulations, demonstrate state-of-the-art causal discovery with significantly reduced parameter complexity compared to standard baselines. We further show that inferred causal structures can be used to reduce parameter count of forecasting models by more than 70% on average while maintaining predictive accuracy.

</details>


### [273] [The Convergence Gap: Instruction-Tuned Language Models Stabilize Later in the Forward Pass](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07282)
*Yifan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A diagnostic study of convergence dynamics in instruction-tuned vs pretrained language models, identifying that instruction-tuned models settle later in the network and that late-stage MLP computations are the strongest lever on that delay, validated across multiple readouts and ablations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how instruction-following models evolve during decoding, quantify when and where final outputs diverge from intermediate layer distributions, and identify robust intervention points that modulate predictive delay.

Method: Decode each layer's next-token distribution, compute its KL divergence to the model's final distribution. Compare six paired pretrained and instruction-tuned checkpoints in native prompting regimes. Test robustness across endpoint-matched and endpoint-free readouts, same-history checks, and template replay. Use matched-prefix interventions to swap late MLP windows (late IT grafts into PT hosts and PT-late swaps into IT hosts) and compare against matched random late perturbations. Include a preselected case study (Gemma) for plausibility of late swap effects.

Result: Instruction-tuned checkpoints remain farther from their final predictions deeper in the stack. Late MLP windows emerge as the strongest leverage point: late IT grafts into PT hosts increase late KL by +0.34 nats; PT-late swaps into IT hosts decrease it by -0.51 nats. Matched random late perturbations yield +0.003 versus +0.327 for the true late graft. Results are robust across endpoint-matched/raw vs tuned readouts, endpoint-free checks, and template replay. Gemma case study supports behavior-facing plausibility but is not a benchmark claim.

Conclusion: Post-training dynamics show instruction-following checkpoints tend to settle later, and late MLP computation is the strongest bidirectional handle on that delay under matched histories.

Abstract: Final outputs hide when a checkpoint commits to its next-token prediction. We introduce the convergence gap, a model-diffing diagnostic that decodes each layer's next-token distribution and measures its distance to the model's own final distribution. Across six paired pretrained and instruction-tuned checkpoints in native prompting regimes, instruction-tuned checkpoints remain farther from their final predictions later into the stack. The effect persists under endpoint-matched raw and tuned readouts, endpoint-free same-history checks, and fixed-history template replay. Matched-prefix interventions identify late MLP windows as the largest tested leverage point: late IT grafts into PT hosts increase late KL by +0.34 nats, while PT-late swaps into IT hosts reduce it by -0.51 nats; matched random late perturbations give only +0.003 versus +0.327 for the true late graft. A preselected Gemma case study provides behavior-facing plausibility for the same late swap, without serving as a benchmark claim. These results identify a robust predictiondynamics signature of post-training: released instruction-following checkpoints tend to settle later, and late MLP computation is the strongest tested bidirectional handle on that delay under matched histories.

</details>


### [274] [Instruction Tuning Changes How Upstream State Conditions Late Readout: A Cross-Patching Diagnostic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07284)
*Yifan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First-divergence cross-patching reveals late-stack effects on next-token margins depend on upstream (earlier-layer) state; IT late stack shows larger impact when reading its own upstream state; sparse, final-layer features mediate; results urge cross-upstream-state tests to attribute late-layer effects correctly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how earlier computation (upstream) and late-stack processing cooperate to shape post-trained model behavior, and to distinguish genuine late-layer influence from readout artifacts across training recipes.

Method: Perform first-divergence cross-patching: at the first token where pretrained base (PT) and instruction-tuned (IT) checkpoints diverge, cross each model's earlier-layer state with each model's late stack (4 cross-combinations). Evaluate logits and forced-token scores across five dense families (4B–32B). Compare OpenMath2 domain SFT and CPT controls to assess domain learning transfer.

Result: IT late stack contributions: +0.76 logits from PT upstream and +2.44 logits from IT upstream, yielding a +1.68 interaction (positive across all families). Late-stack effects rely on the model’s own upstream state, while OpenMath2 shows substantial portability from base earlier layers. Sparse final-layer features mediate the effect and are driven by upstream patches. Forced-token scoring indicates local token choices can alter later answer accuracy.

Conclusion: Late-stack effects are real but interdependent with upstream state; to attribute effects to the late stack, test whether the effect survives under the other checkpoint’s upstream state. This approach reveals a handoff from earlier representations to final-layer activations and clarifies how training recipes influence interpretability findings.

Abstract: Recent interpretability work has identified model-internal handles on post-trained behavior, including refusal directions, assistant/persona axes, and sparse chat-tuning features. These results localize where behaviors can be read out or controlled, often in middle-to-late layers. We ask how earlier computation and the late stack cooperate to turn those differences into next-token margins. To test this, we introduce first-divergence cross-patching: at the first token where pretrained base (PT) and instruction-tuned (IT) checkpoints disagree, we cross each model's earlier-layer state with each model's late stack. The diagnostic separates training recipes: same-base instruction-following descendants show late effects that depend on their own earlier-layer state, while OpenMath2 math-domain SFT and controlled code/biomed CPT controls with verified domain learning do not; for OpenMath2, the late effect is already largely portable from base earlier-layer state. Across five dense families (4B-32B), the IT late stack adds +0.76 logits from PT upstream and +2.44 from IT upstream, giving a +1.68 interaction that is positive in every family. Thus the late stack has a real PT-upstream effect, but its larger effect in the IT checkpoint appears only when it reads its own post-trained upstream state. Sparse features in final MLP layers partially mediate the effect and are driven by upstream patches, supporting a handoff from earlier state to final-layer feature activation to IT-token margin. Forced-token scoring shows that the local token choice can change later exact-answer success. Operationally, paired-checkpoint studies that localize a difference to late layers should test whether it survives under the other checkpoint's upstream state before treating the late stack as self-contained.

</details>


### [275] [Pretraining Induces a Reusable Spectral Basis for Downstream Task Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07302)
*Junjie Yu,Yue Wang,Zihan Deng,Yan Zhu,Wenxiao Ma,Quanying Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pretraining builds a reusable spectral basis in weight matrices; finetuning explores a small subspace, with top singular vectors remaining stable and task-invariant; freezing those vectors and tuning leading spectral coefficients yields competitive results with ~0.2% trainable parameters on GLUE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why certain finetuning directions are never utilized and whether these stable directions encode transferable knowledge, thus illuminating how pretrained representations transfer across tasks.

Method: Systematic spectral analysis (SVD) of pretrained weight matrices across vision and language models; assess stability of top singular vectors under finetuning and distribution shift; compare across model scales; develop a parameter-efficient finetuning method by freezing pretrained singular vectors and optimizing leading spectral coefficients; evaluate on GLUE.

Result: Leading singular vectors are highly stable under finetuning and are shared across unrelated downstream tasks. Larger pretraining data yields greater spectral stability under distribution shift or task change. The proposed method of freezing singular vectors and training only leading spectral coefficients achieves competitive GLUE performance with about 0.2% trainable parameters.

Conclusion: Pretraining establishes a reusable spectral coordinate system; the stable directions encode transferable structure rather than noise. Increased pretraining scale improves geometric transferability, enabling effective, parameter-efficient transfer by operating in the spectral basis.

Abstract: Finetuning pretrained models occurs in a low-dimensional subspace of the full parameter space. Prior work has focused on characterizing this optimization subspace, but largely ignored the complementary question: why do certain directions remain unexplored during finetuning? Are these stable directions irrelevant to downstream tasks, or do they already encode task-relevant structure that requires no further adjustment? Answering this question is central to understanding how pretrained knowledge transfers. Through systematic spectral analysis across vision and language models, we show that the leading singular vectors of pretrained weight matrices remain highly stable under finetuning and are shared across unrelated downstream tasks, revealing that pretraining establishes a reusable spectral coordinate system. Models pretrained on larger datasets exhibit greater spectral stability under distribution shift or task change, directly linking pretraining scale to geometric transferability. Motivated by these findings, we propose a parameter-efficient method that freezes pretrained singular vectors and optimizes only leading spectral coefficients, achieving competitive performance on GLUE with 0.2% trainable parameters. Our results reveal that the stable directions encode transferable structure rather than irrelevant noise: successful pretraining discovers spectral bases that downstream tasks inherit and operate within.

</details>


### [276] [Latent Order Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07304)
*Emil Carlsson,Newton Mwai,Fredrik D. Johansson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent Order Bandits (LOB) relax latent bandits by using only a partial order of action preferences across latent states, allowing same-state instances to differ in reward distributions. It provides UCB and posterior-sampling algorithms with regret guarantees and demonstrates competitive performance, especially when reward scales vary across instances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce sample complexity in cross-instance personalization by leveraging shared action-preference structure without requiring fully specified latent-state reward models; accommodate varying reward scales across instances.

Method: Formalize LOB with total and partial latent orders; derive an upper-confidence bound (UCB) algorithm for LOB and a regret bound; propose a posterior-sampling algorithm; conduct empirical evaluations comparing to full-prior latent bandits across problem instances with shared or scaled rewards.

Result: Both UCB and posterior sampling are competitive with full-prior latent bandits when same-state reward parameters are shared; they outperform them when reward scales differ across instances with the same latent state; overall, LOB improves exploration efficiency under partial-order knowledge.

Conclusion: Partial orders suffice to exploit cross-instance structure in bandits, broadening latent-bandit applicability to heterogeneous reward scales and improving sample efficiency in personalization tasks.

Abstract: Bandit algorithms solve diverse sequential decision-making problems, but are often too sample-inefficient for from-scratch personalization. To substantially reduce exploration times, latent bandit algorithms exploit cross-instance structure implied by discrete latent states, provided that the posterior distribution of rewards and latent states is known and accurate. However, obtaining an accurate model of this structure is difficult, and a small number of latent states may be insufficient to characterize the reward distributions in all problem instances. We propose latent order bandits (LOB), relaxing the assumptions of latent bandits to require only prior knowledge of a partial order of action preferences in each state. This allows instances of the same state to vary in reward distributions, as long as the partial order of actions is shared. For example, groups of users on a streaming service may agree on which movie genres are the best but rate experiences on different scales. We give an upper-confidence bound procedure for the LOB problem, applicable to both total and partial latent orders, and give an upper bound on its regret. To improve empirical performance, we propose a posterior-sampling algorithm and show, in a suite of experiments, that both are competitive with full-prior latent bandits when same-state instances share reward parameters, and preferable to them when reward scales differ between instances with the same latent state.

</details>


### [277] [Generative Modeling with Flux Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07319)
*Peter Pao-Huang,Xiaojie Qiu,Stefano Ermon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flux Matching generalizes score-based models to non-conservative vector fields; it uses a weaker objective that yields infinitely many vector fields sharing the data as stationary distribution, enabling flexible dynamics, faster sampling, and interpretable/mechanistic models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to move beyond score-based models that require learning a field equal to the data score, by allowing non-conservative vector fields and injecting inductive biases, priors, and dynamical properties directly into the model. This expands the design space for generative modeling and enables directed dependencies between variables.

Method: Introduce Flux Matching as a training objective that enforces the data distribution to be stationary under the learned vector field, without requiring the field to match the data score. This yields a flexible family of dynamics that can be regularized or constrained to encode priors, priors, and mechanistic structure while still generating the data; demonstrate on high-dimensional image data and show benefits such as faster sampling and interpretability.

Result: Demonstrates strong performance on high-dimensional image datasets; the additional freedom allows faster sampling, interpretable/mechanistic models, and dynamics with directed dependencies between variables; code released for public use.

Conclusion: Flux Matching reframes vector fields as a design choice in generative modeling, expanding beyond score matching and enabling priors and dynamics to be incorporated directly while preserving the data distribution as stationary. This broadens the methodological toolkit for generative modeling.

Abstract: We introduce Flux Matching, a new paradigm for generative modeling that generalizes existing score-based models to a broader family of vector fields that need not be conservative. Rather than requiring the model to equal the data score, the Flux Matching objective imposes a weaker condition that admits infinitely many vector fields whose stationary distribution is the data. This flexibility enables a class of generative models that cannot be learned under score matching, in which inductive biases, structural priors, and properties of the dynamics can be directly imposed or optimized. We show that Flux Matching performs strongly on high-dimensional image datasets and, more importantly, that our added freedom unlocks a range of applications including faster sampling, interpretable and mechanistic models, and dynamics that encode directed dependencies between variables. More broadly, Flux Matching opens a new dimension in generative modeling by turning the vector field itself into a design choice rather than a fixed target. Code is available at https://github.com/peterpaohuang/flux_matching.

</details>


### [278] [SparseRL-Sync: Lossless Weight Synchronization with ~100x Less Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07330)
*Lucas Hu,Ranchi Zhao,Isaac Zhu,Zach Zhang,Hscos Zhang,Hugh Yin,Jason Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SparseRL-Sync replaces full-weight transfers with a lossless sparse update payload (indices + values), achieving ~100x reduction in transmitted data at ~99% sparsity, while preserving fidelity and improving scalability in bandwidth-limited RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bandwidth and latency bottlenecks in decoupled Trainer-Rollout RL as model size grows, especially in bandwidth-constrained or asynchronous deployments; need to reduce per-update data without sacrificing accuracy.

Method: Identify highly sparse parameter updates; transmit only nonzero indices and corresponding values; reconstruct exact weights on the rollout side; employ bucketing to reduce overhead and launch/control-plane costs; maintain 100% fidelity by lossless reconstruction.

Result: Under a simplified cost model, per-update communication volume S reduces to about S/X; with 99% sparsity (X ~ 100), ~100x reduction in data; improved scalability and end-to-end efficiency in bandwidth-limited and asynchronous RL settings.

Conclusion: SparseRL-Sync offers a practical, lossless solution to weight-update communication bottlenecks in large-scale RL, enabling higher throughput and lower tail latency, particularly in constrained networks; effectiveness hinges on actual sparsity patterns and integration with bucketing and system design.

Abstract: In large-scale reinforcement learning (RL) systems with decoupled Trainer-Rollout execution, the Trainer must regularly synchronize policy weights to the Rollout side to limit policy staleness. When inter-node bandwidth is abundant, such synchronization is usually only a small fraction of end-to-end cost. As model size grows, however, the communication demand rises rapidly. In bandwidth-constrained or network-variable deployments -- for example, cross-datacenter or cross-cluster settings, heterogeneous resource pools, and online RL -- weight synchronization can become a dominant bottleneck for throughput and tail latency. We observe that, in mainstream large-model RL training, the locations where parameters actually change are highly sparse at the element level (often 99%+ sparsity). Building on this observation, we propose and implement SparseRL-Sync, which replaces full-weight transfers with a lossless sparse update payload (indices and values) that can be exactly reconstructed on the inference side, thereby preserving 100% fidelity. Under a simplified cost model, sparse synchronization reduces the per-update communication volume from S to approximately S/X; with 99% sparsity (X ~ 100), this yields about a 100x reduction in transmitted data. Combined with appropriate bucketing, SparseRL-Sync also reduces launch and control-plane overhead, significantly improving scalability and end-to-end efficiency in bandwidth-limited and highly asynchronous RL settings.

</details>


### [279] [Rethinking Importance Sampling in LLM Policy Optimization: A Cumulative Token Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07331)
*Yuheng Zhang,Chenlu Ye,Shuowei Jin,Changlong Yu,Wei Xiong,Saurabh Sahu,Nan Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CTPO introduces a cumulative token importance sampling ratio for off-policy policy-gradient in LLM post-training, achieving unbiased prefix corrections with lower variance than full-sequence IS. It combines this ratio with position-adaptive clipping in log-space (scaling ~ sqrt(t)) and demonstrates superior performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks versus GRPO and GSPO; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the fundamental bias-variance trade-off in off-policy IS for token-level RL in LLMs. Token-level IS (PPO) biases by ignoring prefix state distribution; full-sequence IS corrects trajectories but has high variance; GSPO stabilizes via length normalization at the cost of exact IS. A principled prefix-correcting ratio with lower variance is sought.

Method: Define the cumulative token IS ratio as the product of per-token IS ratios up to position t. Prove it yields an unbiased prefix-correction for each token-level gradient term under token-level policy-gradient. Show it has lower variance than the full-sequence ratio. Introduce CTPO by combining this ratio with position-adaptive clipping, using log-space clip bounds that grow with sqrt(t). Evaluate on tool-integrated reasoning benchmarks across model scales, comparing to GRPO and GSPO. Provide code at the referenced repository.

Result: CTPO achieves the best average performance across model scales among the baselines GRPO and GSPO on several challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
