Conclusion: The cumulative token IS ratio provides a principled, lower-variance unbiased prefix correction for token-level policy gradients in RLHF-like settings. CTPO offers stable regularization across token positions and outperforms existing baselines, with code released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning, including reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), has emerged as a powerful approach for LLM post-training. Central to these approaches is the design of the importance sampling (IS) ratio used in off-policy policy-gradient estimation. Existing methods face a fundamental bias-variance dilemma: token-level IS ratios, as adopted by PPO (Schulman et al., 2017) and GRPO (Shao et al., 2024), introduce bias by ignoring prefix state distribution mismatch; full sequence ratios provide exact trajectory-level correction but suffer from high variance due to the multiplicative accumulation of per-token ratios, while GSPO (Zheng et al., 2025) improves numerical stability via length normalization at the cost of deviating from the exact full-sequence IS correction. In this work, we identify the cumulative token IS ratio, the product of per-token ratios up to position $t$, as a theoretically principled solution to this dilemma. We prove that, under the token-level policy-gradient formulation, this ratio provides an unbiased prefix correction for each token-level gradient term and has strictly lower variance than the full sequence ratio. Building on this insight, we propose CTPO (Cumulative Token Policy Optimization), which combines the cumulative token IS ratio with position-adaptive clipping that scales log-space clip bounds according to the natural $\sqrt{t}$ growth of the cumulative log-ratio. This yields more consistent regularization across token positions. We implement and evaluate CTPO in the tool-integrated reasoning setting on several challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks, achieving the best average performance across both model scales compared with strong GRPO and GSPO baselines. Code will be available at https://github.com/horizon-llm/CTPO.

</details>


### [280] [Beyond Linear Attention: Softmax Transformers Implement In-Context Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07333)
*Zixuan Xie,Xinyu Liu,Claire Chen,Shuze Daniel Liu,Rohan Chandra,Shangtong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper provides a theoretical framework for in-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) using standard softmax attention, showing layerwise Transformer updates correspond to a weighted softmax TD learning algorithm in kernel space, with convergence under a contraction condition and a global minimizer property for pretraining loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend theoretical understanding of ICRL beyond linear attention to practical softmax attention, connecting Transformer dynamics to TD learning and kernel methods to explain how pretrained parameters enable context-based adaptation without updates.

Method: Demonstrate equivalence between the Transformer's layerwise forward pass with softmax attention and iterative updates of a weighted softmax TD algorithm; formalize weighted softmax TD as a RL algorithm encompassing linear and tabular TD as special cases; establish contraction condition yielding decay of policy evaluation error with depth; prove global minimizer property of pretraining parameters for a given loss.

Result: The results establish an equivalence between softmax-attention Transformer updates and weighted softmax TD learning in kernel space; under a contraction condition, policy evaluation error decays as the number of layers grows; the identified parameters globally minimize the pretraining loss, explaining their emergence in experiments.

Conclusion: This work delivers the first theoretical understanding of ICRL with standard softmax attention, linking Transformer dynamics to kernel-based TD learning, and explaining why pretraining yields parameters that enable effective in-context adaptation without explicit updates.

Abstract: In-context reinforcement learning (ICRL) studies agents that, after pretraining, adapt to new tasks by conditioning on additional context without parameter updates. Existing theoretical analyses of ICRL largely rely on linear attention, which replaces the softmax function in the standard attention with an identity mapping. This paper provides the first theoretical understanding of ICRL without making the unrealistic linear attention simplification. In particular, we consider the standard softmax attention used in practice. We show that, with certain parameters, the layerwise forward pass of a Transformer with such softmax attention is equivalent to iterative updates of a weighted softmax temporal difference (TD) learning algorithm. Here, weighted softmax TD is a new RL algorithm that performs policy evaluation in kernel space and adopts both linear TD and tabular TD as special cases. We also prove that under a certain contraction condition, the policy evaluation error decays as the number of layers grows, with the identified parameters above. Finally, we prove that those parameters are a global minimizer of a pretraining loss, explaining their emergence in our numerical experiments.

</details>


### [281] [CellScientist: Dual-Space Hierarchical Orchestration for Closed-Loop Refinement of Virtual Cell Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07335)
*Mengran Li,Bo Li,Jiaying Wang,Wenbin Xing,Yixuan Dong,Chengyang Zhang,Hongliang Zhang,Yuzhong Peng,Jinlin Wu,Bob Zhang,Bingo Wing-Kuen Ling,Fuji Yang,Zhen Lei,Jiebo Luo,Zelin Zang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dual-space hierarchical framework (CellScientist) enables structured feedback routing from executable predictions back to high-level hypotheses, improving executable models for virtual cell modeling with auditable refinement traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VMC predictions fail due to misaligned modeling assumptions, representation decisions, and implementations. Current LLM-assisted refinement lacks cross-level feedback, causing repairs in code without correcting the underlying assumption.

Method: Represent modeling decisions as structured states and realize them as admissible programs under task/interface constraints. Maintain a closed Hypothesis -> Implementation -> Hypothesis loop that routes execution discrepancies to targeted hypothesis or implementation updates, producing auditable refinement traces.

Result: Executable models selected by CellScientist outperform reference baselines under fixed split/evaluation protocols across morphology and transcriptomic benchmarks, with additional single-cell perturbation evaluations and auditable refinement traces.

Conclusion: CellScientist delivers a principled refinement-routing mechanism for VCM, yielding improved executable models and traceable, auditable workflow.

Abstract: Virtual Cell Modeling (VCM) requires models that not only predict perturbation responses, but also support targeted revision when predictions fail. Current LLM-assisted modeling workflows face a refinement-routing problem: prediction discrepancies are observed through executable implementations, but the relevant revision may involve the modeling assumption, representation design, implementation, or task constraint. Without structured feedback propagation across these levels, iterative refinement may repair code while failing to revise the assumption responsible for the discrepancy. We propose CellScientist, a dual-space hierarchical framework that couples a high-level hypothesis space with a low-level executable implementation space. CellScientist represents modeling decisions as structured states, realizes them as admissible programs under task and interface constraints, and routes execution discrepancies back to targeted hypothesis or implementation updates. This enables a closed Hypothesis -> Implementation -> Hypothesis loop where failures become structured signals for model refinement rather than debugging events. Across morphology and transcriptomic benchmarks, with additional single-cell perturbation evaluations, the final executable models selected by CellScientist improve over reference baselines under fixed split and evaluation protocols, while the workflow produces auditable refinement traces.

</details>


### [282] [Mage: Multi-Axis Evaluation of LLM-Generated Executable Game Scenes Beyond Compile-Pass Rate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07342)
*Hugh Xuechen Liu,Kıvanç Tatar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multi-axis evaluation reveals compile-pass rate is a poor proxy for functional correctness in LLM-generated domain-specific artifacts; IR-conditioned evaluation improves domain-faithful structure despite lower runtime success, highlighting the need for multi-axis benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compile-pass rate is the dominant but potentially misleading signal for LLM code/scene generation when artifacts have multiple components and behavior depends on structure and mechanism.

Method: Four-axis evaluation protocol (Mage): compile success, runtime success, structural fidelity, mechanism adherence. Tested 858 generation attempts across four open-weight LLMs (7B–30B), 26 Unity goal-pattern concepts, and two IR granularity levels. Compared NL-to-C# generation vs IR-conditioned pipelines. Used F1 for mechanism/structure, McNemar test for granularity comparison.

Result: NL-to-C# achieves highest runtime pass (~43% mean) but yields structurally vacuous scenes (mechanism F1 ≈ 0.12). IR conditioning halves runtime success but yields domain-faithful structure (F1 up to 1.00). Behavior-only vs full-scene granularity are statistically indistinguishable (McNemar p = 1.0), suggesting granularity saturation at input level.

Conclusion: Compile rate is anti-correlated with functional correctness in this domain; multi-axis evaluation is essential to detect divergence. The authors release the benchmark, replay logs, and per-record metrics for independent verification.

Abstract: Compile-pass rate is the dominant evaluation signal for LLM code generation, yet for multi-component domain-specific artifacts it can be actively misleading. We demonstrate this on executable game scene synthesis with a four-axis evaluation protocol (named `Mage') -- compile success, runtime success, structural fidelity, and mechanism adherence -- applied to 858 generation attempts across four open-weight LLMs (7B--30B), 26~hand-crafted Unity goal pattern playable concepts, and two automatically extracted IR granularity levels. Direct NL-to-C\# generation achieves the highest runtime-pass rate (43\% mean) yet produces structurally vacuous scenes (mechanism $F_1 \approx 0.12$). Structural IR conditioning halves the runtime rate but recovers domain-faithful structure ($F_1$ up to 1.00). Within IR conditioning, behavior-only and full-scene granularity are statistically indistinguishable (McNemar $p = 1.0$), indicating input-level granularity saturation. These results show that compile rate is anti-correlated with functional correctness in this domain and that multi-axis evaluation is necessary to detect the divergence. We release the benchmark, replay logs, and per-record metrics for independent verification.

</details>


### [283] [MISA: Mixture of Indexer Sparse Attention for Long-Context LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07363)
*Ruijie Zhou,Fanxu Meng,Yufei Xu,Tongxuan Liu,Guangming Lu,Muhan Zhang,Wenjie Pei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MISA is a mixture-of-indexer-experts approach that replaces DSA's multi-head token indexer with a lightweight router to activate only a few heads per query. This reduces per-query cost while preserving indexer diversity and accuracy. A hierarchical variant enlarges the candidate set and re-ranks with DSA to recover almost exact token selection. Empirically, eight active heads match DSA on LongBench across DeepSeek-V3.2 and GLM-5 with notable speedups (e.g., TileLang kernel ~3.8x faster).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DSA’s learned token-wise indexer uses many query heads that share the same selected token set; this makes the indexer the dominant cost for long contexts. There is a need to reduce per-query cost without sacrificing diversity and accuracy of token selection.

Method: Treat DSA indexer heads as a mixture-of-experts. A lightweight router uses cheap block-level statistics to select a small subset of active heads for a given query; only these heads perform heavy token-level scoring. A negligible router term computed on pooled keys accompanies the heavy scoring. A hierarchical variant keeps a larger candidate set via the routed pass and then re-ranks it with the original DSA indexer to recover final tokens almost exactly. No additional training is required. A TileLang kernel provides substantial hardware speedups.

Result: With eight active heads, MISA matches the dense DSA indexer on LongBench for DeepSeek-V3.2 and GLM-5, while using four to eight times fewer indexer heads. MISA outperforms HISA on average, preserves Needle-in-a-Haystack heatmaps up to 128K tokens, and recovers over 92% of DSA-selected tokens per layer. The TileLang kernel yields ~3.82x speedup over DSA’s original indexer on an NVIDIA H200 GPU.

Conclusion: MISA offers a practical, training-free drop-in replacement for DSA that substantially reduces per-query cost without sacrificing accuracy, thanks to a mixture-of-experts routing mechanism and an optional hierarchical re-ranking variant. It delivers meaningful speedups on specialized hardware while maintaining token-selection fidelity.

Abstract: DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA) sets the state of the art for fine-grained inference-time sparse attention by introducing a learned token-wise indexer that scores every prefix token and selects the most relevant ones for the main attention. To remain expressive, the indexer uses many query heads (for example, 64 on DeepSeek-V3.2) that share the same selected token set; this multi-head design is precisely what makes the indexer the dominant cost on long contexts. We propose MISA (Mixture of Indexer Sparse Attention), a drop-in replacement for the DSA indexer that treats its indexer heads as a pool of mixture-of-experts. A lightweight router uses cheap block-level statistics to pick a query-dependent subset of only a few active heads, and only those heads run the heavy token-level scoring. This preserves the diversity of the original indexer pool while reducing the per-query cost from scoring every prefix token with every head to scoring it with only a handful of routed heads, plus a negligible router term computed on a small set of pooled keys. We further introduce a hierarchical variant of MISA that uses the routed pass to keep an enlarged candidate set and then re-ranks it with the original DSA indexer to recover the final selected tokens almost exactly. With only eight active heads and no additional training, MISA matches the dense DSA indexer on LongBench across DeepSeek-V3.2 and GLM-5 while running with eight and four times fewer indexer heads respectively, and outperforms HISA on average. It also preserves fully green Needle-in-a-Haystack heatmaps up to a 128K-token context and recovers more than 92% of the tokens selected by the DSA indexer per layer. Our TileLang kernel delivers roughly a 3.82 times speedup over DSA's original indexer kernel on a single NVIDIA H200 GPU.

</details>


### [284] [QuadNorm: Resolution-Robust Normalization for Neural Operators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07375)
*Bum Jun Kim,Makoto Kawano,Yusuke Iwasawa,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces quadrature-based normalization (QuadNorm, BlendQuadNorm) for neural operators to achieve discretization-robust statistics, reducing cross-resolution transfer error. Demonstrates O(h^2) consistency of quadrature moments and transfer-error scaling; QuadNorm often outperforms standard normalization, especially on nonperiodic PDEs and nonspectral architectures; BlendQuadNorm provides a conservative default nearby LayerNorm, suitable for periodic FNO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Normalization in neural operators is typically computed by uniform grid averaging, making it discretization-dependent and a source of transfer error across meshes. This work seeks discretization-robust normalization to improve cross-resolution generalization.

Method: Replace uniform averaging in normalization layers with numerical quadrature to form QuadNorm and BlendQuadNorm. Analyze O(h^2)-consistency of quadrature moments on endpoint-inclusive uniform grids, derive a transfer-error bound linking mismatch to resolution gap and network depth, and validate via experiments on Darcy flow and real-data benchmarks across resolutions (e.g., 64^2 to 256^2). Compare with existing norms and architectures, including nonspectral and periodic FNO variants.

Result: Quadrature moments exhibit O(h^2) consistency across discretizations. Transfer-error bound predicts how normalization-induced mismatch scales with resolution gap and depth, corroborated by experiments. QuadNorm achieves the best cross-resolution performance for Darcy across tested targets; Transolver with QuadNorm attains near-resolution-invariant transfer on real data. Largest gains on nonperiodic PDEs and nonspectral architectures; BlendQuadNorm behaves like LayerNorm and serves as a safe default for periodic FNO.

Conclusion: Normalization is a previously overlooked source of resolution dependence in neural operators. Quadrature normalization (QuadNorm, BlendQuadNorm) provides discretization-robust transfer, with QuadNorm as the strong option and BlendQuadNorm as a conservative default for periodic settings.

Abstract: Normalization layers in neural operators usually compute statistics by uniformly averaging discrete grid values, making the normalization itself discretization-dependent and thereby a source of transfer error across different resolutions or meshes. To enable discretization robustness, we introduce a quadrature normalization family that replaces existing uniform averaging in normalization layers with numerical quadrature: QuadNorm and BlendQuadNorm. On endpoint-inclusive uniform grids, the proposed quadrature moments are $O(h^2)$-consistent across discretizations, meaning that their cross-resolution mismatch decays quadratically with grid spacing. A transfer-error bound then predicts how normalization-induced mismatch scales with both the resolution gap and network depth. The experiments show the same gap- and depth-scaling trends predicted by the transfer-error bound. On Darcy, QuadNorm delivers the best cross-resolution performance at every tested target resolution from $64^2$ to $256^2$; on real-data benchmarks, Transolver with QuadNorm achieves nearly resolution-invariant transfer. The largest gains appear on nonperiodic PDEs and nonspectral architectures, where native-resolution improvements also emerge. We also validate BlendQuadNorm, which stays close to LayerNorm behavior and serves as a conservative default for periodic FNO settings. These results identify normalization as a previously overlooked source of resolution dependence in neural operators.

</details>


### [285] [Zero-Shot Neural Network Evaluation with Sample-Wise Activation Patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07378)
*Yameng Peng,Andy Song,HaythamM. Fayek,Vic Ciesielski,Xiaojun Chang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SWAP introduces Sample-Wise Activation Patterns (SWAP) and SWAP-Score as training-free, zero-shot metrics that predict neural network performance across CNNs and Transformers, achieving strong correlations and enabling fast NAS (SWAP-NAS). Example correlations: ~0.93 with CIFAR-10 for DARTS and ~0.71 with GLUE for FlexiBERT; label-independent and applicable at pretraining. Code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing zero-shot metrics suffer from weak correlations and poor generalization across architectures and tasks, with many working only for CNNs or Transformers. A universal, training-free proxy for both architecture families and downstream tasks is highly desirable to accelerate NAS and pretraining evaluation.

Method: Define SWAP by measuring the expressivity of a network over a mini-batch via its activation patterns. Derive SWAP-Score as a scalar, training-free proxy that correlates with ground-truth performance. Applicable to both CNNs and Transformers; label-independent, enabling estimation at pretraining time. For NAS, instantiate SWAP-NAS to guide architecture search with minimal GPU time.

Result: SWAP-Score shows higher predictive power than existing zero-shot metrics across computer vision and NLP tasks for both CNNs and Transformers. Strong Spearman correlations reported (e.g., 0.93 on CIFAR-10 for DARTS CNNs; 0.71 on GLUE with FlexiBERT). SWAP-NAS achieves competitive performance using only about 6–9 minutes of GPU time on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. The authors provide publicly available code.

Conclusion: SWAP provides a broadly applicable, training-free proxy for neural network expressivity that generalizes across architecture families and task domains, enabling faster NAS and pretraining evaluation with strong predictive performance.

Abstract: Zero-shot proxies, also known as training-free metrics, are widely adopted to reduce the computational overhead in neural network evaluation for scenarios such as Neural Architecture Search (NAS), as they do not require any training. Existing zero-shot metrics have several limitations, including weak correlation with the true performance and poor generalisation across different networks or downstream tasks. For example, most of these metrics apply only to either convolutional neural networks (CNNs) or Transformers, but not both. To address these limitations, we propose Sample-Wise Activation Patterns (SWAP), and its derivative, SWAP-Score, a novel and highly effective zero-shot metric. SWAP-Score is broadly applicable across both architecture families and task domains, demonstrating strong predictive performance in the majority of tasks. This metric measures the expressivity of neural networks over a mini-batch of samples, showing a high correlation with the neural networks' ground-truth performance. For both CNNs and Transformers, the SWAP-Score outperforms existing zero-shot metrics across computer vision and natural language processing tasks. For instance, Spearman's correlation coefficient between the SWAP-Score and CIFAR-10 validation accuracy for DARTS CNNs is 0.93, and 0.71 for FlexiBERT Transformers on GLUE tasks. Moreover, SWAP-Score is label-independent, hence can be applied at the pre-training stage of language models to estimate their performance for downstream tasks. When applied to NAS, SWAP-empowered NAS, SWAP-NAS can achieve competitive performance using only approximately 6 and 9 minutes of GPU time, on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet respectively. Our code is available at: https://github.com/pym1024/SWAP_Universal

</details>


### [286] [StreamPhy: Streaming Inference of High-Dimensional Physical Dynamics via State Space Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07384)
*Panqi Chen,Yifan Sun,Shikai Fang,Xiao Fu,Lei Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StreamPhy enables efficient, real-time streaming inference of full-field, high-dimensional physical dynamics from irregular sparse measurements using a data-adaptive encoder, a memory-efficient state-space model, and an expressive FT-FiLM decoder; FT-FiLM is provably more expressive than the functional Tucker model; it outperforms baselines by ~48% in accuracy and by 20–100x in inference speed compared to diffusion-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of inferring evolving, multi-modal physical fields from irregular, sparse observations in real time. Existing diffusion-based and functional tensor methods are offline, require full temporal data, or are computationally expensive. A streaming, online-capable framework is needed.

Method: A three-component architecture: (1) a data-adaptive observation encoder robust to irregular observation patterns, (2) a structured state-space model enabling memory-efficient online updates across irregular time intervals, and (3) a Functional Tensor Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FT-FiLM) decoder for continuous-field generation. The paper also proves FT-FiLM is more expressive than the functional Tucker model, enabling richer dynamics. 

Result: Empirical evaluation on three representative physical systems under challenging sampling patterns shows StreamPhy consistently improves accuracy (≥48%) over state-of-the-art baselines and achieves substantial speedups (20–100×) over diffusion-based methods.

Conclusion: StreamPhy provides an end-to-end streaming solution for real-time inference of full-field dynamics from irregular sparse measurements, combining robust encoding, online memory-efficient state updates, and a highly expressive decoder. FT-FiLM’s higher expressivity expands the function class for modeling complex spatio-temporal dynamics; the method demonstrates robust, faster performance across challenging sampling patterns.

Abstract: Inferring the evolution of high-dimensional and multi-modal (e.g., spatio-temporal) physical fields from irregular sparse measurements in real time is a fundamental challenge in science and engineering. Existing approaches, including diffusion-based generative models and functional tensor methods, typically operate in offline settings, depend on full temporal observations, or incur substantial inference cost. We propose StreamPhy, an end-to-end framework that enables efficient and accurate streaming inference of full-field physical dynamics from incoming irregular sparse measurements. The framework integrates a data-adaptive observation encoder that is robust to arbitrary observation patterns, a structured state-space model that supports memory-efficient online updates across irregular time intervals, and an expressive Functional Tensor Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FT-FiLM) decoder for continuous-field generation. We prove that FT-FiLM is more expressive than the functional Tucker model, admitting a richer function class for handling complex dynamics. Experiments on three representative physical systems under challenging sampling patterns show that StreamPhy consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with at least 48\% improvement in accuracy and up to 20--100X faster inference than diffusion-based methods.

</details>


### [287] [Convex Optimization with Nested Evolving Feasible Sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07386)
*Karthick Krishna M.,Haricharan Balasundaram,Rahul Vaze*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Online convex optimization with nested evolving feasible sets (S1 ⊇ S2 ⊇ ... ⊇ ST) and a fixed objective f. Introduces a lazy algorithm achieving a convex-loss regret-movement trade-off and a Frugal algorithm achieving zero regret with logarithmic movement; establishes lower bounds proving the optimality of Frugal.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To study optimization where the feasible region shrinks over time, extending nested convex body chasing to an optimization setting with a fixed objective. The goal is to keep feasibility while balancing regret against the static hindsight optimum and total movement.

Method: For convex losses, propose a lazy online algorithm that achieves simultaneous regret O(T^{1-β}) and movement cost O(T^β) for any β∈(0,1]. For strongly convex or α-sharp losses, propose Frugal which attains zero regret and movement cost O(log T). Prove a lower bound: any algorithm with o(T) regret incurs Ω(log T) movement, showing Frugal’s optimality.

Result: Two regimes: (i) a parametric trade-off algorithm (lazy) with tunable balance between regret and movement via β; (ii) Frugal with zero regret and polylogarithmic movement for strongly convex/α-sharp losses. A matching Ω(log T) lower bound confirms optimality of Frugal within the stated assumptions.

Conclusion: CONES generalizes nested convex body chasing to optimization with a fixed objective, yielding practical online strategies that simultaneously bound regret and movement. The results include a tunable regret-movement trade-off and an optimal zero-regret, logarithmic-movement algorithm, backed by matching lower bounds.

Abstract: Convex Optimization with Nested Evolving Feasible Sets (CONES)} is considered where the objective function $f$ remains fixed but the feasible region evolves over time as a nested sequence $S_1 \supseteq S_2 \supseteq \cdots \supseteq S_T$. The goal of an online algorithm is to simultaneously minimize the regret with respect to hindsight static optimal benchmark and the total movement cost while ensuring feasibility at all times. CONES is an optimization-oriented generalization of the well-known nested convex body chasing problem. When the loss function is convex, we propose a lazy-algorithm and show that it achieves $O(T^{1-β}), O(T^β)$ simultaneous regret and movement cost for any $β\in (0,1]$, over a time horizon of $T$. When the loss function is strongly convex or $α$-sharp, we propose an algorithm Frugal that simultaneously achieves zero regret and a movement cost of $O(\log T)$. To complement this, we show that any online algorithm with $o(T)$ regret has a movement cost of $Ω(\log{T})$ for both cases, proving optimality of Frugal.

</details>


### [288] [Unsolvability Ceiling in Multi-LLM Routing: An Empirical Study of Evaluation Artifacts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07395)
*Saloni Garg,Amit Sagtani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Large-scale multi-LLM routing study shows that much reported unsolvability is due to evaluation artifacts (verbosity bias, truncation, format mismatches). Dual-judge validation and exact-match anchoring reduce apparent unsolvability; routers trained on biased signals collapse to majority class (~79% smallest-tier optima) with notable opportunity cost. Headroom estimates are inflated; robust evaluation and cost-sensitive routing are needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the validity of routing headroom in multi-LLM systems by isolating evaluation artifacts that inflate unsolvability and wasted routing opportunities across model pools.

Method: Empirical study with 206,000 query–model pairs across six benchmarks (MMLU, MedQA, HumanEval, MBPP, Alpaca, ShareGPT) using Gemma 4 and Llama 3.1. Evaluations deployed with LLM-as-judge and exact-match metrics; introduced a decomposition framework to attribute failures to artifacts; used random-feature and shuffled-label controls; assessed router training signals and the impact of evaluation choices; proposed recommendations.

Result: A large portion of unsolvability stems from artifacts (judge biases toward verbosity, budget-induced truncation, output-format mismatches). Dual-judge validation and exact-match anchoring reduce unsolvability; artifacts bias router training so standard routers default to a majority class (~79% smallest-tier optimum), incurring 13–17 percentage point opportunity costs. Findings indicate existing routing headroom is substantially inflated.

Conclusion: Reliable evaluation protocols are essential for multi-LLM routing. The study advocates dual-judge validation, exact-match anchoring, and cost-sensitive objectives to obtain trustworthy routing headroom estimates and improve cost-quality tradeoffs.

Abstract: Efficient routing across multiple LLMs enables cost-quality tradeoffs by directing queries to the cheapest capable model. Prior work attributes routing headroom to an "unsolvability ceiling", queries no model in the pool can solve. We present a large-scale study of multi-tier LLM routing with 206,000 query-model pairs across six benchmarks (MMLU, MedQA, HumanEval, MBPP, Alpaca, ShareGPT) using the Gemma 4 and Llama 3.1 families. Evaluating with both LLM-as-a-judge and exact-match metrics, we show that a substantial portion of reported unsolvability stems from evaluation artifacts: (i) systematic judge biases favoring verbosity over correctness, (ii) truncation under fixed generation budgets, and (iii) output format mismatches. Through dual-judge validation and exact-match grounding, we reduce measured unsolvability across tasks. We introduce a decomposition framework attributing failures to these artifacts, revealing consistent patterns across domains and model families. These artifacts also distort router training signals: standard routers collapse to majority-class prediction (~79% smallest-tier optimal), confirmed via random-feature and shuffled-label controls, incurring a 13-17 percentage point opportunity cost. We provide actionable recommendations including dual-judge validation, exact-match anchoring, and cost-sensitive objectives. Our findings suggest existing routing headroom estimates are substantially inflated, underscoring the need for reliable evaluation protocols in multi-LLM systems.

</details>


### [289] [Rubric-based On-policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07396)
*Junfeng Fang,Zhepei Hong,Mao Zheng,Mingyang Song,Gengsheng Li,Houcheng Jiang,Dan Zhang,Haiyun Guo,Xiang Wang,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ROPD: rubric-based on-policy distillation that derives prompt-specific rubrics from teacher–student contrasts to score student rollouts, enabling black-box OPD without logits and achieving up to 10x sample efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Teacher logits restrict on-policy distillation to white-box settings; there is a need for scalable, black-box-compatible alignment methods.

Method: Derive prompt-specific rubrics from teacher–student contrasts and use these rubrics to score student rollouts, guiding on-policy optimization without access to raw logits.

Result: ROPD outperforms advanced logit-based OPD methods in most scenarios, with up to 10× gains in sample efficiency.

Conclusion: Rubric-based OPD provides a flexible, black-box-compatible baseline for scalable distillation across LLMs; a simple, scalable alternative to logit-based OPD; code available.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) is a powerful paradigm for model alignment, yet its reliance on teacher logits restricts its application to white-box scenarios. We contend that structured semantic rubrics can serve as a scalable alternative to teacher logits, enabling OPD using only teacher-generated responses. To prove it, we introduce ROPD, a simple yet foundational framework for rubric-based OPD. Specifically, ROPD induces prompt-specific rubrics from teacher-student contrasts, and then utilizes these rubrics to score the student rollouts for on-policy optimization. Empirically, ROPD outperforms the advanced logit-based OPD methods across most scenarios, and achieving up to a 10x gain in sample efficiency. These results position rubric-based OPD as a flexible, black-box-compatible alternative to the prevailing logit-based OPD, offering a simple yet strong baseline for scalable distillation across proprietary and open-source LLMs. Code is available at https://github.com/Peregrine123/ROPD_official.

</details>


### [290] [Have Graph -- Will Lift? The Case for Higher-Order Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07397)
*Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Need for new benchmark datasets in geometric deep learning to complement existing graph datasets; call for community-driven dataset curation including higher-order information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite diverse models at the intersection of geometry/topology and ML, benchmark datasets are scarce. Researchers often lift graphs to higher-order structures to test ideas, signaling a gap between model diversity and data resources.

Method: Opinion/position paper arguing for proactive dataset sourcing and curation. It discusses the status quo and advocates community action; no empirical methodology or experiments are presented.

Result: Proposes shifting research emphasis toward developing and compiling new datasets that incorporate higher-order information to underpin theoretical and empirical advances.

Conclusion: Encourage the community to actively source and publish diverse datasets that capture higher-order structures, thereby strengthening the foundations of geometric and topological deep learning.

Abstract: After a somewhat rocky start, geometry and topology have established a foothold in machine learning. Message passing, either on graphs or higher-order complexes, is one of the main drivers of geometric deep learning, and paradigms that were once considered to be firmly in the realm of the abstract-like sheaves-have been "tamed" to serve as novel inductive biases for model architectures in topological deep learning. The veritable diversity of models, however, is in stark contrast to the scarcity of suitable benchmark datasets. As a result, researchers often resort to lifting existing graph datasets to include higher-order information. In this opinion paper, I want to encourage the community to also source new datasets, which may be used to prop up the foundations of our research field.

</details>


### [291] [Emergent Symbolic Structure in Health Foundation Models: Extraction, Alignment, and Cross-Modal Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07407)
*Gajendra Katuwal,Advait Koparkar,Salar Abbaspourazad,Anshuman Mishra,Sarvesh Kirthivasan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-training symbolic decomposition of frozen health embeddings enables cross-modal alignment and transfer without retraining; symbols capture health-relevant information across PPG and accelerometer data, with robust transfer from limited paired data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve interpretability of health foundation models (FMs) learned from wearables and enable cross-domain transfer without joint retraining.

Method: Decompose frozen embeddings into interpretable directions (symbols) and align embedding spaces across modalities without retraining. Evaluate on three FMs for PPG and accelerometer data pretrained on ~20M minutes from ~172K participants, analyzed on a held-out cohort of ~30K subjects.

Result: Extracted symbols associate selectively with health conditions and physiological attributes; partial sharing of associations across modalities and architectures. Cross-modal transfer via symbols retains >95% of in-domain performance, is nearly symmetric across domain directions, and saturates with limited paired data, suggesting alignment recovers a shared low-dimensional subspace rich in physiological information.

Conclusion: Health FM embeddings contain an interpretable symbolic organization shared across modalities that supports cross-domain transfer without joint training.

Abstract: Health foundation models (FMs) learn useful representations from wearable sensors, but interpreting what they encode and transferring that knowledge across modalities after training remains difficult. We present a post-training framework that decomposes frozen embeddings into interpretable directions, referred to as symbols, and use these symbols to align the embedding spaces without retraining. We evaluate the framework on three FMs for photoplethysmography (PPG) and accelerometer data, independently pretrained on ~20M minutes of unlabeled data from ~172K participants, and analyzed on a held-out cohort of 30K subjects. We find that extracted symbols associate selectively with health conditions and physiological attributes, and these associations are partially shared across modalities and architectures. Cross-modal transfer via symbols retains more than 95% of in-domain performance, is nearly symmetric across domain directions, and saturates with limited paired data, together indicating that alignment recovers a shared low-dimensional subspace rich in physiological information. Overall, these results suggest that health FM embeddings contain an interpretable symbolic organization that is shared across modalities and supports cross-domain transfer without joint training.

</details>


### [292] [Tracking Large-scale Shared Bikes with Inertial Motion Learning in GNSS Blocked Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07412)
*Feng Liu,Kejia Li,Zhiwei Yang,Chunwei Yang,Qun Li,Guobin Wu,Qiang Ni,Ruipeng Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An inertial bicycle tracking framework using a mixture-of-experts and pedal-to-wheel coupling to improve low-cost IMU-based localization with uncertainty-aware estimates; validated on DiDi shared bikes, achieving ≥12% improvement and wheel-speed error <0.5 m/s (95th percentile).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNSS struggles in urban canyons and during intermittent satellite visibility; low-cost inertial navigation suffers from drift and robustness issues; there is a need for scalable, sensor-efficient localization leveraging feasible priors and multi-task learning.

Method: Integrate bicycle mechanical constraints with a mixture-of-experts (MoE) model: multiple expert modules capture shared representations and are fused via a gating mechanism to yield uncertainty-aware trajectory estimates; exploit the mechanical transmission between pedal and rear wheel to relate pedalling behavior to wheel speed, enabling dynamic calibration of the IMU-based trajectory.

Result: Real-world experiments on DiDi shared bikes show improvements of at least 12% over baselines in localization accuracy, with wheel-speed estimation errors below 0.5 m/s at the 95th percentile.

Conclusion: The framework provides a scalable, robust inertial tracking approach for bicycles in challenging environments by combining mechanical priors with probabilistic MoE estimation, enabling more reliable trajectory tracking and wheel-speed calibration suitable for large-scale deployment.

Abstract: Although Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) provide a general solution for bike tracking outdoors, there still exist complex riding environments where only inertial navigation systems work, such as urban canyons. Despite decades of research, localization using only low-cost inertial sensors still faces challenges such as cumulative drifts and poor robustness caused by filtering methods. Furthermore, sensors such as visual and LiDAR could provide reliable measurements, but they are not suitable for large-scale deployment. In this paper, we propose an inertial tracking framework that integrates bicycle mechanical constraints with a mixture-of-experts model. Specifically, we leverage multiple expert modules to capture shared representations and weight them through the gating mechanism, thus improving multi-task learning performance and enabling uncertainty-aware trajectory estimation. Furthermore, based on the mechanical transmission between the pedal and the rear wheel of a bike, we explore the intrinsic relationship between the rider's periodic pedalling behaviors and acceleration variations, and convert such patterns into bike's wheel speed for dynamic calibration. Experiments with real-world riding data from shared bikes of the DiDi ride-hailing platform demonstrate that our system improves the accuracy of baselines by at least 12%, with wheel speed errors below 0.5 m/s at 95-percentile.

</details>


### [293] [Risk-Consistent Multiclass Learning from Random Label-Subset Membership Queries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07413)
*Jiaxu Su,Junpeng Li,Changchun Hua,Yana Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces multiclass learning from random label-subset queries (weak supervision) and provides unbiased risk estimation under ERM, with non-negative and absolute-value corrections to tackle negative empirical risk, plus generalization and bias results, validated experimentally under a matched query mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exact labels are costly, unreliable, or privacy-constrained; using label-subset queries provides a practical weak supervision signal. There is a lack of systematic theory for learning directly from such query-response observations.

Method: Model the data-generating process of query-response observations for random label-subset queries; derive an unbiased estimator of the target risk within the ERM framework; propose corrected risk estimators (non-negative and absolute-value) to mitigate negative empirical risk and overfitting; establish conditional generalization and excess-risk bounds for the unbiased estimator and bias/consistency results for the corrected estimators; validate the approach with experiments under a matched random-query mechanism.

Result: Existence of an unbiased risk estimator under the proposed query model; risk-correction schemes stabilize training and prevent negative empirical risk; theoretical bounds on generalization and bias/consistency; experimental results demonstrate feasibility of direct query-response learning and stabilization due to risk correction.

Conclusion: Direct query-response learning is feasible for multiclass problems under label-subset queries; risk correction enhances stability and provides theoretical guarantees; the framework offers a principled approach to weak supervision via query responses.

Abstract: Obtaining accurate class labels is often costly or unreliable, and may also be limited by privacy or other practical conditions. Compared with asking an annotator to provide the exact class, it is often easier to ask whether the true label belongs to a certain label subset. This query-response form defines a distinct weak-supervision mechanism: weak supervision information is generated through feedback on a label subset. Although weakly supervised learning has studied many learning frameworks, most existing work starts from established weak label objects. A systematic characterization is still lacking for weakly supervised learning generated directly by such query response observations. This paper proposes a multiclass learn ing framework under random label-subset queries. We model the data-generating distribution of query-response observations and derive an unbiased estimator of the target risk under the empirical risk minimization (ERM) framework. To address negative empirical risk and the associated overfitting problem, we introduce corrected risk estimators based on non-negative and absolute-value corrections. Theoretical analysis establishes a conditional generalization and excess-risk bound for the unbiased estimator, and a bias-and-consistency result for the corrected risk estimator. Experiments under the matched random-query mechanism demonstrate the feasibility of direct query-response learning and the stabilization effect of risk correction.

</details>


### [294] [SR$^2$-LoRA: Self-Rectifying Inter-layer Relations in Low-Rank Adaptation for Class-Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07420)
*Fengqiang Wan,Yipeng Lin,Kan Lv,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SR$^2$-LoRA: a simple, effective PEFT method for class-incremental learning that constrains inter-layer relation drift by aligning the singular values of relation matrices between previous and current models, improving catastrophic forgetting as task count grows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Catastrophic forgetting remains a core challenge in class-incremental learning (CIL) with parameter-efficient fine-tuning. The paper reframes forgetting as inter-layer relation drift—the disruption of relationships among layer-wise representations when learning new tasks—and argues that increased drift shrinks decision margins for earlier tasks.

Method: Introduce Self-Rectifying inter-layer Relation Low-Rank Adaptation (SR$^2$-LoRA). It constructs relation matrices induced by the previous and current models on current-task samples, then aligns their singular values (low-rank, robust to perturbations). The approach constrains how inter-layer representations can drift across tasks. The authors provide theoretical results showing greater robustness of singular-value alignment to estimation perturbations compared with entry-wise alignment.

Result: Empirical evaluation on standard CIL benchmarks demonstrates that SR$^2$-LoRA effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting. The benefits become more pronounced as the number of tasks increases. Code is publicly available.

Conclusion: SR$^2$-LoRA offers a simple yet effective mechanism to curb inter-layer relation drift in PEFT-based CIL, improving long-term retention of previous tasks. Theoretical and empirical results support robustness and scalability with task count.

Abstract: Pre-trained models with parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) have demonstrated promising potential for class-incremental learning (CIL), yet catastrophic forgetting still persists when adapting models to new tasks. In this paper, we present a novel perspective on catastrophic forgetting through the analysis of inter-layer relation drift, i.e., the progressive disruption of relationships among layer-wise representations during the learning of new tasks. We theoretically show that the increase of such drift reduces the classification margins of previously learned tasks, thereby degrading overall model performance. To address this issue, we propose \underline{S}elf-\underline{R}ectifying inter-layer \underline{R}elation Low-Rank Adaptation~(SR$^2$-LoRA), a simple yet effective method that mitigates catastrophic forgetting by constraining inter-layer relation drift. Specifically, SR$^2$-LoRA constructs the relation matrices induced by the previous and current models on current-task samples, and aligns the corresponding singular values. We further theoretically show that this alignment exhibits greater robustness to estimation perturbations than direct entry-wise alignment. Extensive experiments on standard CIL benchmarks demonstrate that SR$^2$-LoRA effectively mitigates catastrophic forgetting, with its advantages becoming more pronounced as the number of tasks increases. Code is available in the \href{https://github.com/FqWan24/SR-2-LoRA}{repository}.

</details>


### [295] [A Flexible Adaptive Stable Clustering Algorithm for Archive-Scale Online Mass Spectrometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07424)
*Shao Shi,Xin Yang,Huiran Feng,Jianhuai Ye,Tianlong Hu,Yaling Zeng,Tzung-May Fu,Lei Zhu,Huizhong Shen,Chen Wang,Shu Tao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable, deterministic clustering framework (FASC) that decouples similarity from optimization, enabling linear-time clustering of massive mass-spectrometry datasets with high accuracy and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mass spectrometry data from environmental studies are massive and yield multi-terabyte streams. Traditional clustering trades off scalability, metric flexibility, or stability, hindering actionable chemical insights. A decoupled, deterministic framework is needed to achieve scalable, accurate clustering without stochastic drift.

Method: Flexible Adaptive Stable Clustering (FASC) uses a dynamical-systems approach with a Density-Augmented Similarity Selection rule and geometric constraints to separate the similarity kernel from the optimization logic, ensuring deterministic, order-independent convergence.

Result: Validated on ML ground truths achieving >99.5% cluster purity and 0.99 Adjusted Rand Index. Demonstrated linear runtime scaling (O(N)) on 25 million atmospheric aerosol mass spectra, identifying aging pathways of secondary inorganic aerosols and isolating ultra-rare tracers (<0.2% abundance).

Conclusion: FASC provides a scalable, reproducible infrastructure for mining environmental big data, delivering accurate clustering with deterministic convergence and linear-time performance on very large datasets.

Abstract: Modern online mass spectrometry generates multi-terabyte data streams critical for understanding Earth's environmental systems. However, extracting actionable chemical insights from these repositories is impeded by a computational bottleneck: existing clustering methods force a compromise among scalability, metric flexibility, and algorithmic stability. Here, we introduce Flexible Adaptive Stable Clustering (FASC), a dynamical systems framework that resolves these constraints by architecturally decoupling the similarity kernel from rigorous optimization logic. Unlike legacy heuristics that suffer from stochastic drift and algorithmic blending, FASC employs a Density-Augmented Similarity Selection rule and geometric constraints to guarantee deterministic, order-independent convergence. After validating FASC on canonical machine-learning ground truths (achieving >99.5% cluster purity and 0.99 Adjusted Rand Index), we deployed the framework on 25 million mass spectra of atmospheric aerosols. Demonstrating strictly linear empirical runtime scaling (O(N)), FASC autonomously mapped atmospheric aging pathways of secondary inorganic aerosols while isolating ultra-rare industrial tracers (<0.2% abundance), providing a scalable infrastructure for mining environmental big data.

</details>


### [296] [GameGen-Verifier: Parallel Keypoint-Based Verification for LLM-Generated Games via Runtime State Injection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07442)
*Chaobo Jia,Ruipeng Wan,Ting Sun,Weihao Tan,Borui Wan,Yuxuan Tong,Guangming Sheng,Hong Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GameGen-Verifier decomposes LLM-generated games into verifiable keypoints and uses bounded verification units with a scalable harness to improve automated game verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address reliability gaps in verifying long-horizon game correctness in LLM-generated games, where traditional verification relies on open-ended gameplay.

Method: Decompose specification into verifiable keypoints, patch runtime to a target state, execute bounded interactions, and judge outcomes; implement GGV-Harness for concurrency, isolation, fault recovery.

Result: On VeriGame (100 games, 7 genres), achieves up to 92.2% accuracy vs human judgments (vs 58.8% for a baseline), and reduces wall-clock time by up to 16.6x.

Conclusion: The approach enables scalable, reliable automated verification of LLM-generated games via modular verification units and harnessed runtime, with strong empirical gains.

Abstract: LLM-based game generation promises to turn natural-language specifications into executable games, but progress is limited by the lack of reliable automated verification. Unlike conventional code generation, game correctness is defined over long-horizon interaction: a game may appear correct while violating core mechanics such as state updates, interaction rules, and phase transitions. Existing Agent-as-a-Verifier approaches collapse verification into open-ended gameplay, making verdicts reachability-bound, time-consuming, coverage-limited, and sensitive to the agent's gameplay ability.
  We present GameGen-Verifier, an automated verification paradigm for LLM-generated games that decomposes a specification into verifiable keypoints and grounds them into independent verification units. Each unit patches the game runtime into a concrete target state, executes a bounded interaction, and judges the outcome against the keypoint assertion. We implement GGV-Harness, a scalable agentic harness providing concurrency management, runtime isolation, and fault recovery.
  On VeriGame, our dataset of 100 games across seven genres, GameGen-Verifier achieves up to 92.2% accuracy against human judgments versus 58.8% for the coverage-enforced Agent-as-a-Verifier baseline, while reducing wall-clock time by up to 16.6x.

</details>


### [297] [Inference-Time Attribute Distribution Alignment for Unconditional Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07456)
*Hao Luan,See-Kiong Ng,Chun Kai Ling*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A plug-and-play method for inference-time distributional alignment in unconditional diffusion models via optimal-control perturbations to the reverse process, without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address real-world needs to generate samples from conditional-like distributions (e.g., demographic balance, semantic proportions) without fine-tuning the pretrained model.

Method: Represent reverse diffusion as a dynamical system; introduce time-dependent additive perturbations (controls) during inference; solve for perturbations by optimizing a differentiable distribution-matching objective with a penalty on control effort (to preserve fidelity) using an optimal-control framework.

Result: Empirical results show improved alignment of generated attribute distributions to flexible test-time targets compared with baselines, in image generation, with no retraining/fine-tuning of the diffusion model.

Conclusion: Inference-time, plug-and-play distributional alignment is feasible for unconditional diffusion models via optimal-control-guided perturbations, enabling flexible, targeted sample populations without altering the pretrained model.

Abstract: Inference-time controllable generation is essential for real-world applications of unconditional diffusion models. However, most existing techniques focus on individual samples, struggling in applications that require the sample population to follow specific attribute distributions (e.g., demographic balance or semantic proportions). We formalize this setting as the inference-time attribute distributional alignment problem for pretrained unconditional diffusion models. To address this, we cast inference-time attribute distributional alignment as an optimal control problem over the reverse diffusion process, viewing the process as the rollout of a dynamical system and augmenting it with additive, time-dependent perturbations as control. We solve for the perturbations using an optimal-control-based algorithm to optimize a differentiable distribution-matching objective while penalizing control effort to preserve data fidelity. Experiment results in image generation demonstrate that our proposed plug-and-play approach can better align attribute distributions to diverse and flexible test-time targets compared to baselines, without retraining or finetuning the pretrained diffusion model.

</details>


### [298] [Mean Mode Screaming: Mean--Variance Split Residuals for 1000-Layer Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06169)
*Pengqi Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Very deep Diffusion Transformers can enter a mean-dominated collapse due to Mean Mode Screaming (MMS); introduced MV-Split residuals to maintain stability and enable training at extreme depths (400- and 1000-layer DiTs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand and mitigate a stability collapse mechanism in extremely deep diffusion transformer architectures, caused by mean-dominated representations and degraded gradient paths.

Method: Mechanistic auditing of gradient dynamics; exact decomposition of gradients into mean-coherent and centered components; analysis of attention-logit gradient suppression via Softmax Jacobian nullspace; design of MV-Split Residuals combining centered residual updates with leaky trunk-mean replacement; empirical evaluation on deep DiT models (400-layer single-stream, 1000-layer scale validation).

Result: MV-Split prevents divergent collapse in a 400-layer DiT, maintaining performance close to the baseline pre-crash trajectory and outperforming token-isotropic gating methods like LayerScale; establishes stable trainability at the 1000-layer scale.

Conclusion: The collapse is a tractable phenomenon tied to mean-variance dynamics and Softmax Jacobian geometry; MV-Split provides an effective architectural fix enabling stable, scalable diffusion transformers at depths previously prone to collapse.

Abstract: Scaling Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) to hundreds of layers introduces a structural vulnerability: networks can enter a silent, mean-dominated collapse state that homogenizes token representations and suppresses centered variation. Through mechanistic auditing, we isolate the trigger event of this collapse as Mean Mode Screaming (MMS). MMS can occur even when training appears stable, with a mean-coherent backward shock on residual writers that opens deep residual branches and drives the network into a mean-dominated state. We show this behavior is driven by an exact decomposition of these gradients into mean-coherent and centered components, compounded by the structural suppression of attention-logit gradients through the null space of the Softmax Jacobian once values homogenize.
  To address this, we propose Mean-Variance Split (MV-Split) Residuals, which combine a separately gained centered residual update with a leaky trunk-mean replacement. On a 400-layer single-stream DiT, MV-Split prevents the divergent collapse that crashes the un-stabilized baseline; it tracks close to the baseline's pre-crash trajectory while remaining substantially better than token-isotropic gating methods such as LayerScale across the full schedule. Finally, we present a 1000-layer DiT as a scale-validation run at boundary scales, establishing that the architecture remains stably trainable at extreme depth.

</details>


### [299] [Estimation of Motor Unit Parameters from Surface Electromyograms using an Informed Autoencoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07458)
*Kaja Balzereit,Malte Mechtenberg,Axel Schneider*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Informed autoencoder estimates multiple subject-specific motor unit parameters from surface EMG; demonstrates plausible non-invasive estimation with synthetic data (innervation zone centre MAE ~2.60 mm; conduction velocity MAE ~0.170 m/s), enabling simultaneous parameter estimation with reduced manual modelling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-invasive, subject-specific estimation of motor unit parameters is challenging due to variability and contraction-dependence; existing white-box approaches require substantial manual modelling; a data-driven, physics-informed method could automate and improve accuracy.

Method: Develop an informed autoencoder that reconstructs surface EMG while learning motor unit parameters in its latent space and enforcing physical laws that link these parameters to the EMG signals; frames the problem as a nonlinear inverse problem.

Result: On synthetic data, innervation zone centers are estimated with MAE 2.5989 mm and conduction velocities with MAE 0.1697 m/s, demonstrating the feasibility of simultaneous, data-driven parameter estimation under physical constraints.

Conclusion: Physically informed machine learning can enable non-invasive, simultaneous estimation of multiple motor unit parameters, reducing manual modelling; future work should test on real EMG data, assess robustness across contraction levels, and extend to additional parameters.

Abstract: Motor unit parameters such as the innervation zone centre or the conduction velocity of the electrical potential harbour the potential to improve the fidelity of neuromechanical models used for movement and force prediction. Determining these parameters in a non-invasive way is challenging, as they are subject-specific and may vary with muscle contraction. Existing work on the estimation of motor unit parameters mainly relies on white-box modelling and therefore requires substantial manual modelling effort. This work targets the simultaneous estimation of multiple subject-specific motor unit parameters from electromyography (EMG) recordings measured non-invasively at the skin surface. This results in an inverse problem with a nonlinear loss function. To address this problem, an informed autoencoder is developed. This autoencoder reconstructs the surface EMG recordings while learning the parameters in its latent space and adhering to physical laws that relate the parameters to the EMG signals. In experiments on synthetic data, innervation zone centres are estimated with a mean absolute error of 2.5989 $\mathrm{mm}$, and conduction velocities of the electric potential are estimated with a mean absolute error of 0.1697 $\mathrm{m}\mathrm{s}^{-1}$. These results demonstrate the plausibility of this novel approach, which enables the simultaneous estimation of several motor unit parameters while reducing manual modelling effort through the integration of data-driven machine learning.

</details>


### [300] [Learning Minimal-Deviation Corrections for Multi-Dimensional Mismodelling in HEP Simulations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07460)
*Matthias Schott,Lucie Flek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural-network-based transformation of simulated events to match available 1D targets while preserving baseline correlations, addressing multidimensional mismodelling under limited data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-energy physics MC often mismodels in multidimensional space, yet only 1D distributions are available for tuning; full multidimensional corrections require large target data and may ignore correlations with 1D-only approaches.

Method: Train a neural network to transform simulated events so that their 1D target distributions are matched, while applying a minimal-deviation constraint to keep the transformed events close to the original simulation and preserve global correlations.

Result: Controlled studies with pseudo-data show improved agreement with the 1D targets and maintenance of a consistent multidimensional structure, indicating scalable improvement in high-dimensional MC modelling under data-limited conditions.

Conclusion: The method provides a scalable solution for correcting complex, high-dimensional MC mismodelling using limited information, preserving correlations and enabling targeted corrections of mismodeled features.

Abstract: Accurate Monte Carlo (MC) modelling in high-energy physics is challenging, particularly in complex scenarios where simulations fail to reproduce observed data. In practice, experimental information is often limited to one-dimensional (1D) distributions, while mismodelling arises in a multidimensional feature space. This restricts traditional correction methods, as one-dimensional reweighting ignores correlations and fully multidimensional approaches require large target datasets. We propose a neural network-based method that operates under these constraints by learning a transformation of simulated events that reproduces the available 1D target distributions while remaining close to the original simulation. This minimal-deviation principle preserves the global correlation structure of the baseline model while enabling targeted corrections of mismodelled features. Using controlled studies with simulated pseudo-data, we show that the method improves agreement with target distributions and maintains a consistent multidimensional structure. The approach is designed for complex, high-dimensional analyses where traditional techniques are insufficient, providing a scalable way to enhance MC modelling under limited information.

</details>


### [301] [Approximation Error Upper and Lower Bounds for Hölder Class with Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07463)
*Xin He,Yuling Jiao,Xiliang Lu,Jerry Zhijian Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer networks with Softmax, ReLU, and residuals can approximate Hölder functions with near-optimal rates; upper bound: O(ε^{-d0/α}) blocks; lower bound: Ω(ε^{-d0/(4α)}) blocks; extends to regression with excess risk bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify the expressive power of Transformers in function approximation and establish scaling laws with respect to smoothness α and input dimension d0; provide theoretical guidance for network depth and practical regression performance.

Method: Perform a constructive approximation analysis for the standard Transformer with Softmax, ReLU, and residuals to derive an explicit ε-approximation upper bound for Hölder functions; obtain a matching lower bound via VC-dimension arguments; extend the framework to regression to derive excess risk rates.

Result: Precise upper and lower bounds on the number of Transformer blocks needed to ε-approximate Hölder functions: upper bound scales as O(ε^{-d0/α}); lower bound scales as Ω(ε^{-d0/(4α)}); extended results provide excess risk rates for regression.

Conclusion: The work establishes that Transformers can achieve near-optimal approximation rates for Hölder-smooth targets, providing theoretical justification for their empirical success in regression, though a gap between upper and lower bounds remains.

Abstract: We explore the expressive power of Transformers by establishing precise approximation error upper and lower bounds for Hölder class. Specifically, a new approximation upper bound is derived for the standard Transformer architecture equipped with Softmax operators, ReLU activation functions, and residual connections. We prove that a Transformer network composed of at most $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon^{-{d_{0}}/α})$ blocks can approximate any bounded Hölder function with $d_{0}$-dimensional input and smoothness $α\in(0,1]$ under any accuracy $\varepsilon>0$. In the case of approximation lower bounds, leveraging the VC-dimension upper bound, we are the first to rigorously prove that Transformers demand for at least $Ω(\varepsilon^{-{d_{0}}/({4α})})$ blocks to achieve the $\varepsilon$ approximation accuracy. As a final step, we extend the derived results for standard Transformers to a general regression task and establish the corresponding excess risk rates demonstrating Transformers' empirical effectiveness in real-world settings.

</details>


### [302] [Physical Simulators as Do-Operators: Causal Discovery under Latent Confounders for AI-for-Science](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07467)
*Tsuyoshi Okita*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CFM-SD uses first-principles simulators as do-operators to perform causal discovery under latent confounding, achieving identifiability with O(d) single-variable interventions and strong intrinsic/extrinsic performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In AI-for-Science domains (e.g., molecular design, materials science), latent confounders are common and real interventions are expensive; existing methods relying on virtual interventions struggle with realism and data efficiency.

Method: CFM-SD (Causal Flow Matching with Simulation Data) integrates physics-based simulators into Pearl's do-calculus as intervention tools and employs flow-matching techniques to recover the causal structure under latent confounding, with a theoretical identifiability claim that a d-variable structure is identifiable with O(d) single-variable interventions under physical realizability constraints.

Result: Intrinsic evaluation on synthetic data (γ in [0.2, 0.8]) yields average F1 of 0.800, outperforming baselines (0.127–0.562). Extrinsic evaluation on real data reports 57–58% bias reduction in molecular toxicity prediction and battery electrolyte optimization.

Conclusion: CFM-SD shows practical value in real-world AI-for-Science settings by leveraging simulators for interventions and achieving strong identifiability and bias-reduction results, though full validation across domains and details on assumptions and scalability are needed.

Abstract: Existing interventional causal discovery methods -- IGSP, DCDI, ENCO -- assume causal sufficiency (no latent confounders) and rely on virtual interventions in synthetic simulators. In AI-for-Science settings such as molecular design and materials science, latent confounders are ubiquitous and real interventions (e.g., physics-based simulations) require hours to days per data point. We propose CFM-SD (Causal Flow Matching with Simulation Data), which uses first-principles physical simulators as do-operators in Pearl's interventional calculus to simultaneously handle latent confounders and real interventional data. Theoretically, $d$-variable causal structure is identifiable with $O(d)$ single-variable interventions -- the minimum under physical realizability constraints. In Intrinsic Evaluation on synthetic data ($γ=0.2$--$0.8$), CFM-SD achieves average F1$=0.800$ vs. F1$=0.127$--$0.562$ for all baselines. In Extrinsic Evaluation on real scientific data, CFM-SD achieves 57--58\% bias reduction in molecular toxicity prediction and battery electrolyte optimization, demonstrating practical value beyond synthetic benchmarks.

</details>


### [303] [Uncovering Hidden Systematics in Neural Network Models for High Energy Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07470)
*Lucie Flek,Philipp Alexander Jungs,Akbar Karimi,Timo Saala,Alexander Schmid,Matthias Schott,Philipp Soldin,Christopher Wiebusch,Ulrich Willemsen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NNs in HEP can be systematically fooled by perturbations within experimental uncertainties, revealing hidden sensitivity not captured by standard control-region or nominal-variation estimates; a quantitative framework is proposed to probe and quantify this risk and guide uncertainty control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Control of systematic uncertainties in NN-based analyses is challenging because standard variation strategies may underestimate true model uncertainty; subtle yet physically plausible input perturbations can disproportionately affect NN outputs.

Method: Adopt adversarial-like perturbations that lie within the experimental uncertainty envelopes of input observables, preserving 1D distributions and correlations. Evaluate their impact across representative HEP tasks (event classification, object identification) and various NN architectures, then formulate a quantitative framework to measure hidden sensitivity and guide uncertainty management.

Result: Demonstrated that networks can be systematically fooled at non-negligible rates within allowed uncertainty envelopes, with substantial output changes while input distributions remain nearly unchanged; framework enables assessment of hidden sensitivity across tasks.

Conclusion: To robustly quantify NN uncertainties in physics analyses, incorporate realistic perturbations within uncertainty envelopes; the proposed framework provides a practical method to measure, understand, and mitigate hidden NN sensitivity.

Abstract: Neural networks (NNs) are inherently multidimensional classifiers that learn complex, non-linear relationships among input observables. While their flexibility enables unprecedented performance in high-energy physics (HEP) analyses, it also makes them sensitive to small variations in their inputs. Consequently, the propagation and estimation of systematic uncertainties in NN-based models remain an open challenge. There are indications that uncertainties derived in control regions or from nominal variations of input features can underestimate the true model uncertainty, potentially leaving biases unaccounted for. Inspired by insights from adversarial-attack studies in machine learning, we explore how subtle perturbations, fully consistent with the experimental uncertainties on the input observables, can lead to substantial changes in NN outputs, while keeping the one-dimensional and correlated input distributions nearly unchanged. Using a set of representative HEP tasks, including event classification and object identification, and testing across a variety of network architectures, we demonstrate that networks can be systematically "fooled" at significant rates within the allowed uncertainty envelopes. Building on this observation, we introduce a quantitative framework to probe and measure the hidden sensitivity of neural networks to realistic experimental variations, providing a practical path to evaluate and control their systematic uncertainty in physics analyses.

</details>


### [304] [SHRED: Retain-Set-Free Unlearning via Self-Distillation with Logit Demotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07482)
*Zizhao Hu,Ameya Godbole,Johnny Tian-Zheng Wei,Mohammad Rostami,Jesse Thomason,Robin Jia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Intro: Proposes SHRED, a retain-set-free unlearning method for LLMs via token-wise information-based forgetting and self-distillation.
Motivation: Eliminate dependence on curated retain sets while efficiently forgetting memorized content; leverage heterogeneity of token informativeness.
Method: Two-stage process: (1) Selection of forget positions by lowest autoregressive probabilities (high Shannon information) within forget-set instances; (2) Training with modified KL targets to demote memorized token logits at forget positions while preserving benign tokens; uses a single top KL self-distillation objective.
Result: Demonstrates Pareto-optimal trade-offs between forget efficacy and model utility on four benchmarks; outperforms retain-set-dependent methods; robust to relearning and membership attacks; stable utility across sequential unlearning.
Conclusion: Retain-set-free unlearning via high-surprisal token targeting is effective and robust, offering practical deployment advantages and privacy protections.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the practical dependency on curated retain sets for unlearning in LLMs and the uneven distribution of memorized content across tokens; enable efficient, plug-in forget operations without full retraining.

Method: Forward pass on forget-set instances to compute per-token probabilities; select bottom (lowest probability) tokens as forget positions; construct modified KL targets to demote memorized tokens while keeping benign tokens; train with a single top KL self-distillation objective to forget while preserving utility.

Result: Empirical evaluation on four standard unlearning benchmarks shows SHRED achieves Pareto-optimal forget-utility trade-offs, surpassing retain-set-based methods; robust against relearning and membership inference; maintains utility after multiple sequential unlearning runs.

Conclusion: Token-level information-based forgetting combined with self-distillation enables retain-set-free unlearning that is both effective and robust, with practical deployment benefits.

Abstract: Machine unlearning for large language models (LLMs) aims to selectively remove memorized content such as private data, copyrighted text, or hazardous knowledge, without costly full retraining. Most existing methods require a retain set of curated examples to prevent catastrophic degradation of general model utility, creating an extra data dependency that complicates deployment. We propose SHRED (Self-distillation via High-surprisal-only Retain-set-free Entropy Demotion), a retain-set-free unlearning method built on a key insight: not all tokens within a forget set instance carry memorized information equally. High-information tokens concentrate the model's memorized knowledge, while low-information tokens reflect general language competence. SHRED operates in two stages. (1) Selection: We perform a forward pass on a forget set instance, collect per-token autoregressive probabilities, and select the bottom (lowest probability, highest Shannon information) as forget positions; the remaining positions are retained as benign anchors. (2) Training: We construct modified KL targets that demote the memorized token's logit at forget positions while preserving the original distribution at benign positions. The model is then trained via a single top KL self-distillation objective that simultaneously drives forgetting and utility preservation. We evaluate SHRED across four standard unlearning benchmarks and demonstrate that it establishes a new Pareto-optimal trade-off between forget efficacy and model utility, outperforming retain-set-dependent methods.
