Our analysis shows that SHRED is robust against relearning attacks and membership-inference attacks, and it maintains stable utility even after many sequential unlearning runs.

</details>


### [305] [Transfer Learning Across Fast- and Full-Simulation Domains in High-Energy Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07471)
*Matthias Schott,Lucie Flek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transfer learning across fast vs full simulation improves performance and data efficiency across three LHC tasks; pretrained representations transfer across detectors (ATLAS CMS) and domain shifts; potential to publish reusable models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Simulation is computationally expensive when fully simulating events; fast simulation provides large statistics at reduced realism. The study investigates whether representations learned on fast simulations can transfer to other fast-sim domains and to fully simulated data, enabling data-efficient, robust models for LHC analyses.

Method: Pretrain dense nets, graph neural networks, and transformers on ATLAS-like fast-sim data for three tasks: signal-background classification, quark-gluon jet tagging, and MET reconstruction. Domain adaptation to CMS-like fast-sim and to fully simulated ATLAS Open Data. Compare against independently trained baselines; evaluate data efficiency and cross-domain performance.

Result: Pretrained models consistently outperform independently trained baselines across all tasks. Target-domain data requirements are significantly reduced, typically by about a factor of two. The learned representations are robust and reusable across domains (ATLAS fast → CMS fast → ATLAS Open Data full sim).

Conclusion: Fast simulation can serve as a source of transferable, reusable representations for LHC analyses. Transfer learning reduces data requirements and supports publishing trained models as reusable scientific assets beyond large foundation models.

Abstract: Machine-learning models in high-energy physics are often trained on simulated data, where fully simulated samples are computationally expensive while fast simulation provides large statistics at reduced realism. In this work, we systematically study transfer learning between fast-simulated and fully simulated datasets in a realistic LHC environment. We consider three representative tasks, signal-background classification, quark-gluon jet tagging, and missing transverse energy reconstruction, using dense neural networks, graph neural networks, and transformer-based architectures. Models are pretrained on ATLAS-like fast simulation and adapted to CMS-like fast simulation and to fully simulated ATLAS Open Data. Across all tasks, pretrained models consistently outperform independently trained baselines and require significantly less target-domain training data, typically reducing the needed statistics by about a factor of two. These results demonstrate that fast simulation can be used to learn robust, reusable representations and motivate publishing trained models as reusable scientific assets beyond large foundation models.

</details>


### [306] [Does Your Neural Network Extrapolate? Feature Engineering as Identifiability Bias for OOD Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07483)
*Leonel Aguilar,Jan Nagler,Christoph Hoelscher,Nino Antulov-Fantulin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OOD generalization hinges on a structural commitment that decouples feature learning from DGP identifiability; with identifiable commitments (e.g., Fourier coordinates), OOD extrapolation becomes reliable, while ID criteria alone cannot guarantee it. Representation choices can cause large (up to ~520x) shifts in OOD performance; correct features plus a model class that can express the target and data covering the representation space are necessary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why deep nets struggle to learn OOD-relevant representations from ID training windows due to non-identifiability of the data-generating process; show how architectural commitments, pretraining, augmentation, inputs, and domain knowledge inject missing structure to enable reliable OOD generalization.

Method: Theoretical framework decoupling feature learning from DGP identifiability; define ε-observational equivalence on training data; introduce a structural commitment (feature map φ, label map ψ, model class M) that fixes the assumed DGP; analyze how this commitment governs OOD generalization; demonstrate via Fourier coordinates turning extrapolation into interpolation on S^1; validate across mass-action chemistry, Keplerian exoplanet prediction, cross-species DNA detection, and a 264-run study across Transformer, Mamba, and S4D; and include a controlled study on the necessity of correct features and representation-space coverage.

Result: With a correct and identifiable commitment, OOD error vanishes; changing the representation can produce up to ~520× differences in OOD performance while leaving in-distribution loss largely unchanged; Fourier coordinates illustrate the mechanism by turning extrapolation into interpolation; the framework predicts outcomes across diverse natural-science domains and large-scale model studies.

Conclusion: Correct, identifiable OOD-relevant commitments are essential for reliable out-of-distribution generalization. ID performance may be preserved, but without the right representation and model class, OOD extrapolation remains unreliable; beyond features, the transformed training data must cover the relevant representation space.

Abstract: Successful deep neural networks discover salient features of data. We show when and why they fail to learn out-of-distribution (OOD)-relevant representations from an in-distribution (ID) training window. This requires decoupling feature learning from data-generating-process (DGP) identifiability. From a single training window, OOD extrapolation is non-identifiable: infinitely many DGPs are $\varepsilon$-observationally equivalent on the training data but diverge arbitrarily outside it, and no in-distribution criterion alone reliably breaks the tie. A structural commitment, the feature map, label map, and model class $(\varphi, ψ, \mathcal{M})$, dictates the assumed DGP and governs OOD generalization while leaving ID performance essentially unchanged. When architecture, pretraining, augmentation, input formats, or domain knowledge implicitly inject the missing commitment, the model succeeds. When it cannot infer OOD-relevant structure from ID evidence, it fails. Changing only the representation can make the same architecture, at the same in-distribution loss, differ by ${\sim}520\times$ out of distribution. When the commitment is correct and identifiable, OOD error vanishes. For example, Fourier coordinates turn periodic extrapolation into interpolation on $\mathbb{S}^1$. The same mechanism predicts outcomes in three natural-science settings (mass-action chemistry; Kepler's-third-law exoplanet prediction, $n=2{,}362$; and cross-species coding-DNA detection) and in a 264-run positional-encoding study across Transformer, Mamba, and S4D. Finally, a controlled study shows: correct features are necessary but not sufficient. The model class must express the target, and the transformed training data must cover the relevant representation space.

</details>


### [307] [NPMixer: Hierarchical Neighboring Patch Mixing for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07476)
*Jung Min Choi,Vijaya Krishna Yalavarthi,Lars Schmidt-Thieme*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NPMixer combines a learnable wavelet-based decomposition with a patch-based MLP mixer to capture multi-scale local and global patterns in multivariate time series, plus channel mixing on high-frequency components; it achieves state-of-the-art MSE on seven datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multivariate forecasting is hindered by complex local dynamics and cross-variable dependencies; existing methods struggle to jointly model multi-scale temporal patterns efficiently.

Method: Introduce Learnable Stationary Wavelet Transform to adaptively decompose signals into trend and detail; Neighboring Patching Mixer Block with hierarchical MLP layers on non-overlapping patches to expand receptive field and capture multi-scale temporal dependencies; Channel-Mixing Encoder on high-frequency components to learn inter-channel correlations while preserving the global trend.

Result: Empirical evaluation on seven benchmark datasets shows consistent improvement over SOTA, achieving better MSE in 20 of 28 (71.4%) experimental setups.

Conclusion: The architecture effectively models local/global dependencies via adaptive wavelet decomposition and patch-based mixing, yielding robust performance across datasets; well-suited for multivariate forecasting with multi-scale structure.

Abstract: Multivariate time series forecasting remains a challenge due to the complexity of local temporal dynamics and global dependencies across multiple variables.
  In this paper, we propose \textbf{N}eighboring \textbf{P}atching \textbf{Mixer} (\textbf{NPMixer}), a hierarchical architecture featuring a Learnable Stationary Wavelet Transform that adaptively learns filter coefficients to decompose signals into trend and detail components in a data-dependent manner.
  Our framework introduces a Neighboring Mixer Block that captures local temporal dynamics through a series of hierarchical MLP layers operating on non-overlapping patches.
  Specifically, the mixer block utilizes MLPs to learn temporal patterns within and across these patches, expanding the receptive field to capture multi-scale dependencies.
  A Channel-Mixing Encoder is applied to high-frequency components to learn channel correlations while preserving the stability of the underlying global trend.
  Extensive experiments on seven benchmark datasets demonstrate that NPMixer consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models, achieving better performance in 20 out of 28 ($71.4\%$) evaluated experimental setups for MSE.

</details>


### [308] [Excluding the Target Domain Improves Extrapolation: Deconfounded Hierarchical Physics Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07485)
*Tsuyoshi Okita*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DHG deconfounds temperature confounding in hierarchical physics constraints of physics-constrained generative models; uses do-calculus and backdoor adjustment with coarse-to-fine constraints; reveals pretraining-withheld target-domain improves extrapolation; achieves RMSE 0.215 on Li-ion temperature extrapolation, 46% better than baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extrapolation under distribution shift is a fundamental challenge for physics-constrained deep generative models. Existing approaches apply static, uniform regularization and neglect hierarchical structure and confounding, limiting out-of-distribution generalization.

Method: Introduce Deconfounded Hierarchical Gate (DHG) that detects when and how strongly temperature confounding affects each constraint level, using counterfactual estimation (do-operator) combined with backdoor adjustment to remove confounding, followed by coarse-to-fine physical constraints.

Result: Key empirical findings include (i) pretraining without target-domain data yields 39% better extrapolation (RMSE 0.224 vs 0.324); (ii) on a lithium-ion battery temperature extrapolation task trained at 24°C and evaluated 4–43°C, DHG achieves RMSE 0.215, a 46% improvement over the unconstrained baseline (Pure CFM: 0.397).

Conclusion: Deconfounding hierarchical physical constraints via DHG improves out-of-distribution extrapolation in physics-constrained generative models; withholding target-domain data during pretraining can enable learning of domain-agnostic physical patterns that transfer better to new domains.

Abstract: Extrapolation to out-of-distribution conditions is a fundamental challenge for physics-constrained deep generative models. Existing methods apply physical constraints as a single static regularization term uniformly across the generation process, and address neither the hierarchical structure of physical laws and the confounding variable problem. We propose the Deconfounded Hierarchical Gate (DHG), which serves as a diagnostic and control mechanism: it identifies when and how strongly temperature confounding contaminates each constraint level, so that hierarchical gates reflect intrinsic physical inconsistency rather than spurious temperature effects. DHG combines counterfactual estimation via the do-operator with backdoor adjustment to remove confounding, then applies Coarse-to-Fine physical constraints progressively. We report a counter-intuitive finding in pretraining: excluding the target-domain data from pretraining outperforms including it by 39% in extrapolation performance (RMSE 0.224 vs. 0.324). This occurs because FNO learns domain-agnostic physical patterns that transfer more effectively when the target domain is withheld. On a lithium-ion battery temperature extrapolation benchmark (trained at 24 degrees Celsius, evaluated at 4.0--43.0 degrees Celsius), our method achieves RMSE = 0.215, a 46% improvement over the unconstrained baseline (Pure CFM: 0.397).

</details>


### [309] [Why Self-Inconsistency Arises in GNN Explanations and How to Exploit It](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07527)
*Wenxin Tai,Yaqian Liu,Ting Zhong,Fan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-Explanations in Self-Interpretable Graph Neural Networks (SI-GNNs) can be self-inconsistent when re-applied to their explanatory graphs. The paper identifies re-explanation-induced context perturbation as the direct cause of score variation, posits a latent signal assignment hypothesis to explain sensitivity of some edges, and studies conciseness regularization. It then proposes Self-Denoising (SD), a model-agnostic, training-free post-processing method that calibrates explanations with one extra forward pass. Empirical results across SI-GNNs, backbones, and datasets show SD improves explanation quality with only ~4–6% additional computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why self-inconsistency arises in SI-GNN explanations and to provide a practical, broadly applicable method to stabilize and calibrate explanations without heavy re-training.

Method: 1) Diagnose self-explanation inconsistency as caused by re-explanation-induced perturbations in the explanatory context. 2) Propose a latent signal assignment hypothesis to explain why only a subset of edges are sensitive to perturbation. 3) Analyze the impact of conciseness regularization on latent signal assignment. 4) Propose Self-Denoising (SD), a model-agnostic, training-free post-processing step that uses one extra forward pass to calibrate explanations.

Result: Empirical validation across representative SI-GNN frameworks, backbone architectures, and benchmark datasets demonstrates that SD consistently improves the quality of explanations, with only about 4–6% computational overhead.

Conclusion: Self-Denoising provides a simple, effective, and general post-processing solution to stabilize and improve SI-GNN explanations, supporting the proposed perturbation-based and latent-signal hypotheses while remaining model-agnostic and efficient.

Abstract: Recent work has observed that explanations produced by Self-Interpretable Graph Neural Networks (SI-GNNs) can be self-inconsistent: when the model is reapplied to its own explanatory graph subset, it may produce a different explanation. However, why self-inconsistency arises remains poorly understood. In this work, we first identify re-explanation-induced context perturbation as the direct cause of score variation. We then introduce a latent signal assignment hypothesis to explain why only some edges are sensitive to this perturbation, and analyze how conciseness regularization affects latent signal assignment. Given that self-inconsistent edges do not provide stable evidence for the model's prediction, we propose Self-Denoising (SD), a model-agnostic and training-free post-processing strategy that calibrates explanations with only one additional forward pass. Experiments across representative SI-GNN frameworks, backbone architectures, and benchmark datasets support our hypothesis and show that SD consistently improves explanation quality while adding only about 4--6\% computational overhead in practice.

</details>


### [310] [ExpThink: Experience-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Chain-of-Thought Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07501)
*Tingcheng Bian,Yuzhe Zhang,Jing Jin,Jinchang Luo,MingQuan Cheng,Haiwei Wang,Wenyuan Jiang,Miaohui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ExpThink introduces two RL-based mechanisms to compress chain-of-thought (CoT) in large reasoning models: experience-guided reward shaping and difficulty-adaptive advantage. These create a self-evolving curriculum and problem-aware gradient scaling, yielding substantial reductions in response length (up to 77%) while improving accuracy and achieving up to 3x higher accuracy–efficiency ratios compared with baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large reasoning models relying on extended CoT incur high token costs and latency. Existing RL approaches use static length penalties that do not adapt to model progress or problem difficulty, limiting compression without sacrificing accuracy.

Method: Two complementary mechanisms: (1) experience-guided reward shaping—track the shortest correct solution per problem and assign three-tier rewards (full credit for concise correct responses, discounted credit for verbose correct ones, zero for incorrect); the threshold tightens automatically as the model improves, forming a self-evolving curriculum. (2) difficulty-adaptive advantage—replace standard deviation normalization with correct-count normalization to produce monotonically difficulty-scaled gradients that amplify learning on hard problems and suppress gradients on easy problems, preserving accuracy while encouraging brevity.

Result: Empirical results on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show ExpThink reduces average response length by up to 77% and achieves up to 3× higher accuracy-efficiency ratio (accuracy per token) than the vanilla baseline, outperforming existing RL-based compression methods on both metrics.

Conclusion: The combination of an accuracy-first objective with a self-evolving curriculum and problem-aware gradient scaling yields effective CoT compression. ExpThink demonstrates strong improvements in both compression and accuracy, indicating potential for broader application to RL-based CoT optimization.

Abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) achieve strong performance via extended chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, yet suffer from excessive token consumption and high inference latency. Existing reinforcement learning (RL) approaches for CoT compression rely on uniform, static length penalties that neglect model capability dynamics and problem-level difficulty variation. We propose \textbf{ExpThink}\xspace, an RL framework that addresses both dimensions through two complementary mechanisms. First, \emph{experience-guided reward shaping} tracks the shortest correct solution found so far for each problem and applies a three-tier reward: full credit for concise correct responses, discounted credit for verbose correct ones, and zero for incorrect ones. The threshold tightens automatically with model improvement, forming a self-evolving curriculum that requires no manual scheduling. Second, \emph{difficulty-adaptive advantage} replaces standard deviation normalization with correct-count normalization, yielding monotonically difficulty-scaled gradients that amplify learning on hard problems to preserve accuracy while suppressing gradients on easy ones to encourage brevity. Together, these mechanisms enforce an accuracy-first, compression-second training objective. Experiments on multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that \textbf{ExpThink}\xspace reduces average response length by up to 77\% while simultaneously improving accuracy, achieving up to $3\times$ higher accuracy-efficiency ratio (accuracy divided by average token count) than the vanilla baseline and outperforming existing RL-based compression methods on both metrics.

</details>


### [311] [Ensemble Distributionally Robust Bayesian Optimisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07565)
*Tigran Ramazyan,Denis Derkach*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Ensemble Distributionally Robust Bayesian Optimization (EDRBO) for zeroth-order optimization with continuous context, delivering a tractable algorithm with sublinear regret and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust Bayesian optimization under context distributional uncertainty; ensemble surrogates reduce model misspecification and data noise.

Method: Develops a novel ensemble-based distributionally robust BO algorithm that remains computationally tractable and handles continuous context; combines multiple surrogate models in a distributionally robust framework.

Result: Theoretical sublinear regret bounds are established, improving existing state-of-the-art; experiments confirm that empirical performance matches the theory.

Conclusion: EDRBO provides robust, scalable zeroth-order optimization with strong theoretical guarantees and empirical support, effectively handling continuous context under distributional uncertainty.

Abstract: We study zeroth-order optimisation under context distributional uncertainty, a setting commonly tackled using Bayesian optimisation (BO). A prevailing strategy to make BO more robust to the complex and noisy nature of data is to employ an ensemble as the surrogate model, thereby mitigating the weaknesses of any single model. In this study, we propose a novel algorithm for Ensemble Distributionally Robust Bayesian Optimisation that remains computationally tractable while managing continuous context. We obtain theoretical sublinear regret bounds, improving current state-of-the-art results. We show that our method's empirical behaviour aligns with its theoretical guarantees.

</details>


### [312] [Stochastic Transition-Map Distillation for Fast Probabilistic Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07661)
*George Rapakoulias,Peter Garud,Lingjiong Zhu,Panagiotis Tsiotras*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes STMD, a teacher-free method to distill the full SDE transition map of diffusion models via a conditional Mean Flow model, enabling fast one- or few-step stochastic samplers while preserving stochasticity and transition structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce diffusion model inference cost and preserve probabilistic sample generation without relying on pretrained teachers, trajectory caching, or bi-level optimization.

Method: Train a conditional Mean Flow model to parameterize the diffusion SDE transitions (the full transition map). Use it to obtain one- or few-step stochastic samplers that retain the diffusion process's transition structure. The approach is teacher-free and avoids trajectory caching and bi-level optimization.

Result: STMD yields efficient, scalable training and allows stochastic inference compatible with diffusion posterior sampling and inverse problems. It provides convergence guarantees in Wasserstein distance and demonstrates competitive results on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CelebA.

Conclusion: STMD offers a theoretically grounded, practical alternative to distillation for diffusion models by distilling transition maps instead of score means, enabling fast stochastic samplers without pretrained teachers and with applicability to various stochastic inference tasks.

Abstract: Diffusion models achieve strong generation quality, diversity, and distribution coverage, but their performance often comes with expensive inference. In this work, we propose Stochastic Transition-Map Distillation (STMD), a teacher-free framework for accelerating diffusion model inference while preserving probabilistic sample generation. In contrast to score-based diffusion models, whose denoising parametrization models the mean of the posterior distribution, STMD distills the full transition map associated with the sampling stochastic differential equation (SDE). We parameterize these SDE transitions with a conditional Mean Flow model, yielding a one- or few-step stochastic sampler that retains the transition structure of the underlying diffusion process. This perspective is especially useful for downstream tasks that require stochastic inference, such as diffusion posterior sampling, inverse problems, and energy-based fine-tuning. Compared to recent distillation methods, STMD requires no pretrained teacher, bi-level optimization, or trajectory simulation and caching, enabling efficient and scalable training. We derive convergence bounds for our method in the Wasserstein distance, providing a strong theoretical foundation for our approach, and validate STMD on various image generation examples on the MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CelebA datasets.

</details>


### [313] [Tessellations of Semi-Discrete Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07513)
*Emile Pierret,Johannes Hertrich,Samuel Hurault,Julie Delon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: In semi-discrete Flow Matching, the terminal preimages (assignment regions) of target atoms are open and simply connected; under an extra assumption they are homeomorphic to the unit ball, yet they can differ sharply from Laguerre cells (non-convex, curved boundaries) as shown by a planar four-point example; the geometry is intrinsic to the exact objective, prior to neural approximation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Characterize the intrinsic geometry induced by the exact semi-discrete Flow Matching objective and contrast it with semi-discrete OT Laguerre cells, to understand foundational structure independent of optimization/approximation.

Method: Analytical study of the semi-discrete Flow Matching map with a Gaussian source and finite target; topological analysis of terminal flow preimages (assignment regions); construction of a planar four-point counterexample to illustrate possible shapes and adjacency patterns.

Result: Terminal assignment regions are open and simply connected; under an additional assumption, they are homeomorphic to the unit ball; these regions can be non-convex with curved boundaries, differing from Laguerre cells.

Conclusion: The exact semi-discrete Flow Matching objective induces geometry that can substantially differ from OT-based Laguerre partitions; these findings provide a baseline for the intrinsic geometry of Flow Matching prior to neural approximation, informing modeling and interpretation in generative settings.

Abstract: We study Flow Matching in a semi-discrete setting where a Gaussian source is transported toward a discrete target supported on finitely many points. This semi-discrete regime is the theoretical setting behind the use of Flow Matching for generative modeling, where the target distribution is represented by a finite dataset. In this semi-discrete regime, the exact Flow Matching velocity field is available in closed form, which makes it possible to analyze the geometry induced by the terminal flow map independently of optimization and approximation effects. We investigate the terminal assignment regions, namely the preimages of the target atoms under the terminal flow. We show that these regions are open, simply connected and, under an additional assumption, homeomorphic to the unit ball. At the same time, a planar four-point example shows that these cells can differ sharply from Laguerre cells arising in semi-discrete optimal transport: they may be non-convex, have curved boundaries, and exhibit different boundedness and adjacency patterns. These results clarify the geometry intrinsically induced by the exact semi-discrete Flow Matching objective before neural approximation enters the picture.

</details>


### [314] [Spectral Surgery: Class-Targeted Post-Hoc Rebalancing via Hessian Spike Perturbation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07790)
*Hugo Vigna,Samuel Bontemps*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-hoc spectral perturbation method (Spectral Surgery) perturbs weights along Hessian spike eigenvectors to rebalance per-class accuracy without retraining, using a spike-class sensitivity matrix, constrained optimization, and adaptive amplitude control; shows improvements in CIFAR-10 and ISIC-2019 for balanced accuracy and reduced variance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The Hessian spectrum of trained deep networks exhibits a bulk of near-zero eigenvalues with a small number of large spikes. The spike count equals the number of classes minus one, suggesting a class-structured curvature that could be exploited to adjust class performance without retraining.

Method: Identify spike eigenvectors of the Hessian and construct a spike-class sensitivity matrix that maps perturbations along each spike to changes in per-class accuracy. Solve a constrained optimization to determine perturbation coefficients that improve weak classes while preserving strong ones. Use adaptive amplitude control to adjust the perturbation budget based on iteration-level improvement signals. Apply post-hoc weight perturbations along spike directions.

Result: Encouraging improvements in balanced accuracy and reduction of standard deviation on CIFAR-10 and ISIC-2019, validating the practical utility of an RMT-informed post-hoc optimization approach.

Conclusion: Spectral Surgery demonstrates that exploiting the Hessian spike structure can yield operational gains in classification performance without retraining, highlighting a promising direction for randomized matrix theory–informed interventions in deep learning.

Abstract: The Hessian spectrum of trained deep networks exhibits a characteristic structure: a continuous bulk of near-zero eigenvalues and a small number of large outlier eigenvalues (spikes), confirming the relevance of Random Matrix Theory in deep learning. The spike count matches the number of classes minus one. While prior work has described this structure, no method has exploited it operationally to improve classification performance. We propose Spectral Surgery, a post-hoc optimization method that directly perturbs model weights along spike eigenvectors to rebalance per-class accuracy without retraining. We introduce (i) a spike-class sensitivity matrix that quantifies the directional derivative of each class's accuracy along each spike eigenvector, (ii) a constrained optimization of perturbation coefficients that targets weak classes while preserving strong ones, and (iii) an adaptive amplitude control that raises or lowers the perturbation budget based on iteration-level improvement signals. We obtain encouraging results on CIFAR-10 and ISIC-2019 on both balanced accuracy and standard deviation.

</details>


### [315] [Your Language Model is Its Own Critic: Reinforcement Learning with Value Estimation from Actor's Internal States](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07579)
*Yunho Choi,Jongwon Lim,Woojin Ahn,Minjae Oh,Jeonghoon Shim,Yohan Jo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: POISE: a lightweight, online value estimator that leverages a policy model's internal signals to estimate verifiable rewards, enabling single-rollout value estimation and reduced variance without large policy-model critics or multiple rollouts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL methods for verifiable rewards in large reasoning models suffer from high compute due to critics requiring large models (PPO) or unstable empirical means requiring multiple rollouts (GRPO). A low-cost, unbiased baseline derived from the policy's own internal states could improve stability and efficiency.

Method: Introduce Policy Optimization with Internal State Value Estimation (POISE). A lightweight probe predicts expected verifiable reward from hidden states of the prompt and generated trajectory, plus token-entropy statistics, trained online with the policy. To maintain gradient unbiasedness, employ a cross-rollout construction that predicts each rollout's value from an independent rollout's internal states. POISE uses a single rollout for value estimation, enabling higher prompt diversity for fixed compute and removing sampling costs for zero-advantage prompts.

Result: Empirical evaluation on Qwen3-4B and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B across math reasoning benchmarks shows POISE matches DAPO with less compute. The value estimator performs comparably to a separate LLM-scale value model and generalizes to various verifiable tasks.

Conclusion: POISE demonstrates that leveraging the model’s internal representations via a lightweight, online state-valued probe can provide unbiased, low-variance value estimates, enabling stable, efficient policy optimization with reduced compute and improved prompt diversity.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for Large Reasoning Models hinges on baseline estimation for variance reduction, but existing approaches pay a heavy price: PPO requires a policy-model scale critic, while GRPO needs multiple rollouts per prompt to keep its empirical group mean stable. We introduce Policy Optimization with Internal State Value Estimation), which obtains a baseline at negligible cost by using the policy model's internal signals already computed during the policy forward pass. A lightweight probe predicts the expected verifiable reward from the hidden states of the prompt and generated trajectory, as well as token-entropy statistics, and is trained online alongside the policy. To preserve gradient unbiasedness despite using trajectory-conditioned features, we introduce a cross-rollout construction that predicts each rollout's value from an independent rollout's internal states. Because POISE estimates prompt value using only a single rollout, it enables higher prompt diversity for a fixed compute budget during training. This reduces gradient variance for more stable learning and also eliminates the compute overhead of sampling costs for detecting zero-advantage prompts. On Qwen3-4B and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B across math reasoning benchmarks, POISE matches DAPO while requiring less compute. Moreover, its value estimator shows similar performance to a separate LLM-scale value model and generalizes to various verifiable tasks. By leveraging the model's own internal representations, POISE enables more stable and efficient policy optimization.

</details>


### [316] [Enhancing Federated Quadruplet Learning: Stochastic Client Selection and Embedding Stability Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07888)
*Ozgu Goksu,Nicolas Pugeault*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedQuad uses contrastive metric learning to align intra-class representations across federated clients, reducing aggregation-induced misalignment and improving generalization under non-IID data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning suffers from data heterogeneity and limited data per client, leading to degraded global generalization and representation misalignment after model aggregation (e.g., FedAvg). A method that explicitly aligns per-class representations across clients can mitigate this issue.

Method: FedQuad introduces a metric-learning objective that minimizes distances between positive (intra-class) pairs and maximizes distances between negative (inter-class) pairs. This intra-class alignment and inter-class separation is integrated into FL training to reduce representation collapse during aggregation. Evaluation is performed on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under diverse non-IID settings and varying numbers of clients.

Result: FedQuad demonstrates consistent improvements over baselines across the evaluated datasets and non-IID scenarios. The paper also provides a comprehensive analysis of metric-learning approaches in centralized and federated settings, highlighting their effectiveness in alleviating representation collapse under heterogeneous distributions.

Conclusion: Explicitly enforcing intra-class alignment via metric learning mitigates representation misalignment caused by federated aggregation, leading to better generalization in non-IID FL. The findings support the broader benefit of metric-learning approaches in both centralized and federated environments.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) enables decentralised model training across distributed clients without requiring data centralisation. However, the generalisation performance of the global model is usually degraded by data heterogeneity across clients, particularly under limited data availability and class imbalance. To address this challenge, we propose FedQuad, a novel method that explicitly enforces minimising intra-class representations while enabling inter-class splits across clients. By jointly minimising distances between positive pairs and maximising distances between negative pairs, the proposed approach mitigates representation misalignment introduced during model aggregation. We evaluate our method on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under diverse non-IID settings and varying numbers of clients, demonstrating consistent improvements over existing baselines. Additionally, we provide a comprehensive analysis of metric learning-based approaches in both centralised and federated environments, highlighting their effectiveness in alleviating representation collapse under heterogeneous data distributions.

</details>


### [317] [SGD for Variational Inference: Tackling Unbounded Variance via Preconditioning and Dynamic Batching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07531)
*Hippolyte Labarrière,Cesare Molinari,Silvia Villa,Lorenzo Rosasco*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BBVI with unbounded gradient variance under the BG condition; proves ELBO solvability and provides finite-time and asymptotic convergence guarantees for minibatch projected SGD with dynamic batching and preconditioning in elliptic location-scale families; supports numerical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge between stochastic optimization theory and BBVI practice, where stochastic gradients have unbounded variance that violates standard SGD assumptions; establish existence of an ELBO maximizer and rigorous convergence under a realistic noise model (BG).

Method: Theoretically analyze the elliptic location-scale family; prove existence of the ELBO solution; develop convergence analysis for Minibatch PSGD with dynamic batching and preconditioning under the Blum-Gladyshev condition; derive finite-time and asymptotic guarantees; supplement with numerical experiments.

Result: Proved existence of an ELBO optimum; established convergence guarantees for Minibatch PSGD with dynamic batching and preconditioning under BG noise; demonstrated practical efficacy via numerical experiments.

Conclusion: The work provides a rigorous bridge between BBVI practice and stochastic optimization theory under BG noise; dynamic batching and preconditioning are effective for achieving robust convergence in modern inference tasks.

Abstract: Black-Box Variational Inference (BBVI) typically relies on Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) to optimize the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO). However, the stochastic gradients in BBVI inherently exhibit unbounded variance, violating standard assumptions and instead satisfying the weaker Blum-Gladyshev (BG) condition, where variance grows quadratically with distance from the optimum. In this paper, we bridge the gap between stochastic optimization theory and the practical instances of BBVI. Focusing on the broad elliptic location-scale family of parameterized distributions, we offer two main contributions. First, we prove the existence of an ELBO solution, a foundational property usually assumed a priori in the literature. Second, we establish comprehensive convergence guarantees spanning finite-time and asymptotic regimes for Minibatch Projected SGD (PSGD) equipped with dynamic batching and preconditioning under the BG condition. Our theoretical framework demonstrates that dynamic batching combined with preconditioning systematically enables rigorous guarantees even in complex settings. We illustrate our theoretical findings with numerical results, highlighting the efficacy of our approach for modern inference tasks.

</details>


### [318] [Revisiting Transformer Layer Parameterization Through Causal Energy Minimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07588)
*Jin Xu,Camille Couturier,Victor Rühle,Saravan Rajmohan,James Hensman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unifying energy-based view (CEM) of Transformer layers shows weight-tied MHA corresponds to gradient updates on an interaction energy, while a gated MLP with shared projections corresponds to an element-wise energy, enabling a design space of parameter-efficient, energy-guided Transformer variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a principled, energy-based interpretation of Transformer parameterization to rationalize empirical choices and guide design.

Method: Formulate Transformer layers as optimization steps on conditional energy functions; derive MHA as gradient update on interaction energy; derive gated MLP as element-wise energy with shared projections; outline design space and potential preconditioners and recursive updates; evaluate on language modeling tasks.

Result: CEM-derived layers train stably at moderate hundred-million-parameter scale and can match baselines despite constrained parameterizations.

Conclusion: CEM offers a useful lens linking Transformers to energy-based models and motivates energy-guided layer designs.

Abstract: Transformer blocks typically combine multi-head attention (MHA) for token mixing with gated MLPs for token-wise feature transformation, yet many choices in their parameterization remain largely empirical. We introduce Causal Energy Minimization (CEM), a framework that recasts Transformer layers as optimization steps on conditional energy functions while explicitly accounting for layer parameterization. Extending prior energy-based interpretations of attention, CEM shows that weight-tied MHA can be derived as a gradient update on an interaction energy, and that a gated MLP with shared up/down projections can be viewed through an element-wise energy. This perspective identifies a design space for Transformer layers that includes within-layer weight sharing, diagonal-plus-low-rank interactions, lightweight preconditioners, and recursive updates. We evaluate CEM-derived layers in language-modeling experiments at the moderate hundred-million-parameter scale. Despite their constrained parameterizations, these layers train stably and can match corresponding Transformer baselines. Overall, our results suggest that CEM provides a useful lens for understanding Transformer layer parameterization, connecting Transformer architectures to energy-based models and motivating further exploration of energy-guided layer designs.

</details>


### [319] [Flatness and Gradient Alignment Are Both Necessary: Spectral-Aware Gradient-Aligned Exploration for Multi-Distribution Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07914)
*Aristotelis Ballas,Christos Diou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes SAGE, a method combining sharpness-aware perturbations and gradient alignment for multi-distribution learning; introduces an excess-risk decomposition into alignment and curvature terms; uses polar factor of gradient matrices via Newton-Schulz; shows empirical gains on domain generalization and multi-task learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sharpness-aware (SAM) and gradient-alignment methods address only a single geometric property of the loss landscape. In multi-distribution settings, both flatness and gradient alignment influence generalization, and ignoring either is structurally limiting. The paper argues for a joint consideration and provides a decomposition to justify it.

Method: Derives an excess-risk decomposition yielding two additive leading terms: an alignment term proportional to trace(Hbar^{-1} Σ_g) and a curvature term proportional to Hbar, where Hbar is the average Hessian and Σ_g the gradient covariance across distributions. Proposes SAGE: uses the polar factor of each layer's gradient matrix (computed via Newton-Schulz) for an isotropic ascent step, and injects isotropic noise at the descent step scaled by cross-distribution gradient disagreement to capture the alignment term.

Result: Empirical evaluation on five domain-generalization and two multi-task learning benchmarks shows state-of-the-art performance on DomainBed and general improvements to base MTL solvers, competitive with or surpassing current state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Jointly targeting curvature and alignment is necessary for robust performance in multi-distribution learning; SAGE operationalizes this idea and delivers empirical gains across tasks.

Abstract: Sharpness-aware and gradient-alignment methods have been shown to improve generalization, however each family of methods targets a single geometric property of the loss landscape, while ignoring the other. In this paper, we show that this omission is structurally unavoidable and that both flatness and gradient alignment should be considered in multi-distribution learning settings. Specifically, we derive an excess-risk decomposition that yields two additive leading-order terms: (i) an alignment term, controlled by the trace of $\bar{H}^{-1}Σ_g$ and (ii) a curvature term, controlled by $\bar{H}$, where $\bar{H}$ is the average Hessian and $Σ_g$ is the covariance of the gradient across distributions. Notably, $\bar{H}$ appears inverted in one and non-inverted in the other. We further show, via a counterexample, that neither quantity bounds the other in general, so no algorithm targeting only one term can guarantee low excess risk. Motivated by this decomposition, we propose SAGE (Spectral-Aware Gradient-Aligned Exploration) that targets both terms. The curvature component replaces SAM's gradient-scaled perturbation with the polar factor of each layer's gradient matrix, computed via Newton-Schulz iteration, so that the ascent step probes all directions with similar magnitude. On the other hand, the alignment component injects isotropic noise at the descent step, the magnitude of which scales with cross-distribution gradient disagreement. Experiments on five domain-generalization and two multi-task learning benchmarks show that the proposed method establishes a new state-of-the-art on DomainBed and acts as a general-purpose improvement to base MTL solvers, remaining competitive with, or even surpassing, state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [320] [On the Invariance and Generality of Neural Scaling Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07546)
*Xing Han,Ziyin Liu,Suchi Saria,Paul Pu Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural scaling laws can be transported across domains via invariants under information-preserving transformations and predictable effects of information-reducing transformations, enabling cross-domain extrapolation of scaling behavior with limited data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for generalizable scaling laws that avoid expensive, domain-specific sweeps by transferring laws from a well-resourced source domain to new domains where full sweeps are infeasible.

Method: Identify invariants: scaling laws are preserved under bijective (information-preserving) data transformations and change in predictable, information-theoretic ways under non-bijective transformations that lower information resolution ρ. Validate across language, vision, and speech; demonstrate two cross-domain applications (general-text to health-record LM scaling; time-series classification with noise) to recover data-scaling exponents.

Result: Demonstrate cross-domain transportability: a single axis (information resolution ρ) supports transporting scaling laws; in the time-series/noise experiment, recover data-scaling exponents to within ~3% error (and similar improvements for the health-record LM setting).

Conclusion: Scaling laws can be generalized across domains by exploiting information-theoretic invariants and controlled information loss, enabling reliable predictions of scaling behavior in new domains with limited resources.

Abstract: Neural scaling laws establish a predictable relationship between model performance and data or compute, offering crucial guidance for resource allocation in new domains and tasks. Yet such laws are most needed precisely where they are hardest to obtain: fitting one for a new model task pair demands expensive sweeps that typically exhaust the very compute budget the law is meant to economize. This paper poses the research question of how to develop generalizable scaling laws: laws fit once on a well-resourced source domain and reliably transported to new domains where running a full sweep is infeasible, which requires a fundamental understanding of when and why scaling properties change. We address this by identifying the right invariants: scaling laws are preserved under bijective (information-preserving) transformations of the data and modified in predictable, information-theoretically grounded ways under non-bijective transformations that lower its information resolution $ρ$: a single axis along which a law fit in one domain can be transported to another. We validate this across language, vision, and speech, and demonstrate two cross-domain applications: predicting scaling for language models trained on electronic health records from laws fit on general text, and predicting time-series classification scaling under varying levels of noise injection, recovering the data-scaling exponents to within $3\%$ error.

</details>


### [321] [Disagreement-Regularized Importance Sampling for Adversarial Label Corruption](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07551)
*Csongor Horváth,Ida-Maria Sintorn,Prashant Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DR-IS uses disagreement between independent proxy ensembles to sub-sample for importance sampling under label corruption; it yields finite-sample guarantees and robust performance against high-norm attacks, improving over EL2N and complementing AUM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address collapse of standard IS under label corruption by formalizing ε-contamination and providing a robust, provable sub-sampling strategy.

Method: Disagreement-Regularized Importance Sampling (DR-IS): sub-sampling based on loss rank-disagreement across independent proxy ensembles; finite-sample concentration bounds; separation between corrupted and boundary-clean groups; contamination control.

Result: Finite-sample bounds: empirical rank disagreement upper-bound for bulk corrupted; lower-bound for boundary-clean; rate O(sqrt(log(N/δ)/K)) with prob 1-δ; empirical robustness under targeted high-norm attacks; improved robustness over magnitude-based EL2N; complementary to AUM; provides explicit certificates and contamination bounds.

Conclusion: DR-IS advances robust sub-sampling for IS under label noise, offering provable guarantees and practical robustness, with competitive performance against attacks and synergy with training-dynamics approaches.

Abstract: Standard Importance Sampling (IS) collapses under label corruption because high-norm examples, prioritized for variance reduction, are often adversarial outliers. We formalize this misalignment using an $\varepsilon$-contamination model and propose Disagreement-Regularized Importance Sampling (DR-IS), a sub-sampling method based on loss rank-disagreement across independent proxy ensemble. We prove finite-sample concentration bounds showing that the empirical rank disagreement of bulk corrupted examples is bounded above, and that of boundary-clean examples bounded below, both at rate $O(\sqrt{\log(N/δ)/K})$ with probability $1-δ$; when the structural expectation gap $Δ'$ between the two groups is positive and the boundary-clean set is at least as large as the selected subset, these bounds certify strict separation and control the contamination rate of the selected subset. Empirically, DR-IS remains robust under targeted high-norm attacks that break magnitude-based methods such as the Error $L_2$-norm (EL2N) on benchmark datasets. DR-IS complements training-dynamics approaches like Area Under the Margin ranking (AUM), offering improved robustness in the loss-aligned regime alongside explicit finite-sample concentration certificates and a contamination bound limiting noise leakage from the statistical tail of corrupted points.

</details>


### [322] [Beyond Distribution Estimation: Simplex Anchored Structural Inference Towards Universal Semi-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07557)
*Yaxin Hou,Jun Ma,Hanyang Li,Bo Han,Jie Yu,Yuheng Jia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Universal Semi-supervised Learning (UniSSL) and SAGE, a representation-centric method that doesn't assume known unlabeled distributions. SAGE uses high-order sample relationships via a simplex-anchored graph-state equipartition and a simplex equiangular tight frame to stabilize inter-class separation; employs distribution-agnostic weighting and an auxiliary branch to filter pseudo-labels, achieving +8.52% accuracy on five benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In SSL with scarce labels and unlabeled data from unknown distributions, pseudo-labeling under distribution assumptions yields many errors and representation confusion. There's a need for distribution-agnostic, representation-level strategies.

Method: Proposes SAGE that models high-order inter-sample dependencies to form a structural consensus for representation learning. Uses a simplex equiangular tight frame as a coordinate frame to separate classes. Introduces a distribution-agnostic weighting strategy to emphasize reliable pseudo-labels and an auxiliary branch to isolate erroneous pseudo-labels.

Result: Five standard benchmarks show consistent state-of-the-art gains, averaging 8.52 percentage points in accuracy.

Conclusion: Shifts SSL from distribution estimation of unlabeled data to structure-based representation learning, demonstrating significant performance gains and robustness to unlabeled data distribution unknowns.

Abstract: Semi-supervised learning faces significant challenges in realistic scenarios where labeled data is scarce and unlabeled data follows unknown, arbitrary distributions. We formalize this critical yet under-explored paradigm as Universal Semi-supervised Learning (UniSSL). Existing methods typically leverage unlabeled data via pseudo-labeling. However, they often rely on the idealized assumption of a uniform unlabeled data distribution or require sufficient labeled data to estimate it. In the UniSSL setting, such dependencies lead to numerous erroneous pseudo-labels, thereby triggering representation confusion. Fortunately, we observe that inter-sample relations captured by representations are more reliable than pseudo-labels. Leveraging this insight, we shift our focus to representation-level structural inference to bypass distribution estimation. Accordingly, we propose Simplex Anchored Graph-state Equipartition (SAGE), which captures high-order inter-sample dependencies to establish structural consensus for guiding representation learning. Meanwhile, to mitigate representation confusion, we employ vectors that satisfy a simplex equiangular tight frame to serve as a coordinate frame for guiding inter-class representation separation. Finally, we introduce a weighting strategy based on distribution-agnostic metrics to prioritize reliable pseudo-labels and an auxiliary branch to isolate potentially erroneous pseudo-labels. Evaluations on five standard benchmarks show that SAGE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, with an average accuracy gain of $\textbf{8.52\%}$.

</details>


### [323] [Bilevel Graph Structure Learning, Revisited: Inner-Channel Origins of the Reported Gain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07577)
*Minkyoung Kim,Beakcheol Jang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Most gains in bilevel graph structure learning arise from inner-loop training dynamics rather than graph rewiring; a new diagnostic, frozen-φ, separates these effects and provides robust evaluation across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reassess commonly attributed gains to learned adjacency in bilevel GSL; quantify contributions from inner dynamics versus graph rewiring; establish standardized diagnostics and predictive framework.

Method: Introduce frozen-φ, a control that freezes the graph while preserving the inner-loop training schedule to split the bilevel gain into an inner-channel (T-step training dynamics with implicit gradient regularization) and a graph-channel (graph rewiring). Evaluate on six benchmarks including spatio-temporal flow forecasting and node classification with Bernoulli edge-level parameterization; compare with classical spectral diagnostics; propose graph distillation and a three-precondition framework.

Result: Inner channel accounts for 78–101% of the gain in spatio-temporal forecasting and 37–44% in node classification (Bernoulli parameterization); spectral diagnostics can dissociate from task gain; frozen-φ standardizes the diagnostic, with graph distillation as a method-agnostic complement; a three-precondition framework predicts the sign of the bilevel gain across six benchmarks.

Conclusion: The major contributor to performance gains is the inner-loop training dynamics; frozen-φ provides a rigorous diagnostic for bilevel GSL, enabling reliable attribution and guiding design; a framework to predict gain sign enhances interpretability and transferability across tasks.

Abstract: Bilevel graph structure learning is widely understood to improve graph neural networks by jointly optimizing model parameters and a learned graph structure, with the resulting performance gain attributed to the rewired adjacency. We find that this attribution may be overstated: training-dynamics effects in the inner loop, rather than the rewiring itself, capture a substantial share of the gain. To establish this, we introduce frozen-$φ$, a control that freezes the graph while retaining the inner-loop training schedule. This decomposes the bilevel gain into an inner channel of $T$-step training dynamics with implicit gradient regularization and a graph channel of the graph rewiring itself. On spatio-temporal flow forecasting the inner channel matches or exceeds the full bilevel pipeline, accounting for 78-101% of the gain; on node classification it accounts for 37-44% under a Bernoulli edge-level parameterization. We also verify that classical spectral diagnostics can dissociate from task gain. We propose frozen-$φ$ as a standardized diagnostic for bilevel graph structure learning, with graph distillation as a method-agnostic complement. A three-precondition framework further predicts the sign of the bilevel gain on all six benchmarks.

</details>


### [324] [An Efficient Hybrid Sparse Attention with CPU-GPU Parallelism for Long-Context Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07719)
*Feiyu Yao,Zhixiong Niu,Xiaqing Li,Yongqiang Xiong,Juan Fang,Qian Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fluxion introduces a co-design for hybrid sparse attention using CPU-resident KV caches to accelerate long-context inference, achieving near-FULL accuracy with substantial speedups over fixed sparse baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context inference often relies on CPU-resident key-value caches due to memory constraints, exposing inefficiencies in both GPU-only and CPU-GPU hybrid designs. Block-sparse attention helps but is insufficient; cross-device coordination and budget-aware scheduling are needed to exploit CPU memory and overlap computation.

Method: Proposes three core ideas: 1) output-aware KV budgeting, 2) head-specific and granularity-aware sparse configuration, 3) cross-device coordinated execution for sparse attention. Implements Fluxion with a lightweight head-property predictor, a granularity-budget selector, and a priority-based scheduler to jointly optimize KV budget, sparsity pattern, and CPU-GPU overlap.

Result: Evaluated on 2 models, 3 benchmarks, and 40 tasks. Fluxion maintains accuracy with worst-average degradation of -0.26 relative to FULL and achieves 1.5× to 3.7× speedup over the strongest fixed sparse hybrid baseline (which uses a KV budget of 0.05).

Conclusion: Co-designing budgets, sparsity granularity, and cross-device execution enables efficient hybrid sparse attention for long-context inference with minimal accuracy loss.

Abstract: Long-context inference increasingly operates over CPU-resident KV caches, either because decoding-time KV states exceed GPU memory capacity or because disaggregated prefill-decode systems place KV data in host memory. Although block-sparse attention reduces attention cost in this setting, sparsity alone is insufficient for end-to-end efficiency. GPU-only designs remain constrained by PCIe bandwidth and metadata memory overhead, while CPU-GPU hybrid designs still suffer from substantial GPU idle time and bottlenecks in CPU-side top-k selection and sparse attention computation.
  Fluxion is built on three key insights: output-aware KV budgeting, head-specific and granularity-aware sparse configuration, and cross-device coordinated execution for sparse attention over CPU-resident KV caches. Guided by these insights, Fluxion combines a lightweight head-property predictor, a granularity-budget selector, and a priority-based scheduler to jointly optimize budget allocation, sparse configuration, and CPU-GPU execution overlap. This co-design enables hybrid sparse attention to achieve both accuracy and system efficiency in long-context inference. Across 2 models, 3 benchmarks, and 40 tasks, Fluxion preserves quality well -- the worst average degradation is only -0.26 relative to FULL, while delivering 1.5$\times$-3.7$\times$ speedup over the strongest fixed sparse hybrid baseline, whose KV budget is only 0.05.

</details>


### [325] [Curated Synthetic Data Doesn't Have to Collapse: A Theoretical Study of Generative Retraining with Pluralistic Preferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07724)
*Ali Falahati,Mohammad Mohammadi Amiri,Kate Larson,Lukasz Golab*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Recursive retraining with heterogeneous reward signals mitigates mode collapse and yields a diverse, high-reward distribution that aligns with a weighted Nash bargaining solution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-reward recursive retraining tends to collapse outputs to maximize that objective; prior work suggests adding real data. The paper reframes this as an alignment problem and explores whether multi-objective curation can preserve diversity.

Method: Formalize the dynamics of recursive training under multiple reward functions (heterogeneous preferences), and provide analytical proofs that under certain conditions the model converges to a stable distribution that spreads probability across several high-reward regions; show this limiting distribution satisfies a weighted Nash bargaining solution.

Result: Under specified conditions, training converges to a diverse distribution over high-reward regions rather than collapsing to a single mode; the distribution adheres to a weighted Nash bargaining solution.

Conclusion: Value aggregation in synthetic retraining loops can be interpreted through bargaining theory; employing multiple reward signals can preserve diversity and offer a formal account for how competing preferences shape the learned policy.

Abstract: Recursive retraining of generative models poses a critical representation challenge: when synthetic outputs are curated based on a fixed reward signal, the model tends to collapse onto a narrow set of outputs that over-optimize that objective. Prior work suggests that such collapse is unavoidable without adding real data into the mix. We revisit this conclusion from an alignment perspective and show that collapse can be mitigated through curation based on multiple reward functions. We formalize the dynamics of recursive training under heterogeneous preferences and prove that, under certain conditions, the model converges to a stable distribution that allocates probability mass across competing high-reward regions. The limiting distribution preserves diversity and provably satisfies a weighted Nash bargaining solution, offering a formal interpretation of value aggregation in synthetic retraining loops.

</details>


### [326] [Optimal Recourse Summaries via Bi-Objective Decision Tree Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07598)
*Ioannis Chatzis,Jason Liartis,Athanasios Voulodimos,Giorgos Stamou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SOGAR learns Pareto-optimal recourse summaries by training a shallow axis-aligned decision tree to partition individuals into subgroups with a shared action, enabling post-hoc trade-offs between recourse effectiveness and cost and yielding globally interpretable, low-cost, and effective recourse summaries that outperform prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recourse methods provide instance-level guidance but are inadequate for global auditing and bias detection, as aggregating local actions is costly and inconsistent. A compact, interpretable summary of recourse across subgroups is needed to compare fairness and effectiveness at the population level.

Method: Formulate recourse summary learning as an optimal decision-tree learning problem and compute the Pareto front of two objectives: recourse effectiveness and recourse cost. Use shallow axis-parallel trees and sparse leaf actions to produce stable, low-cost summaries.

Result: SOGAR yields stable, low-cost, and effective recourse summaries that outperform existing approaches on effectiveness and cost metrics across subgroups, and enables post-hoc selection of the desired trade-off without retraining.

Conclusion: SOGAR provides a principled framework for globally interpretable recourse summaries, balancing efficiency and impact, and facilitating fair and scalable auditing of classifier behavior.

Abstract: Actionable Recourse provides individuals with actions they can take to change an unfavorable classifier outcome. While useful at the instance level, it is ill-suited for global auditing and bias detection, since aggregating local actions is costly and often inconsistent. Recourse Summaries address this limitation by partitioning the population and assigning one shared action per subgroup, enabling comparison across subgroups. Designing summaries involves a fundamental trade-off between recourse effectiveness and recourse cost, which existing methods do not adequately address. We introduce Summaries of Optimal and Global Actionable Recourse (SOGAR), which formulates recourse summary learning as an optimal decision tree learning problem and finds the Pareto front -- the complete set of solutions where improving one objective necessarily worsens the other. SOGAR enables post-hoc selection of the desired trade-off without retraining. Using shallow axis-parallel decision trees and sparse leaf actions, SOGAR produces stable, low-cost, and effective recourse summaries that outperform existing approaches across effectiveness and cost metrics.

</details>


### [327] [Intelligent Truck Matching in Full Truckload Shipments using Ping2Hex approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07733)
*Srinivas Kumar R,Jose Mathew,Ankit Singh Chauhan,Dinesh Rajkumar,Aravind Manoj,Mohit Goel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes ITM 2.0, an ML-based probabilistic ranking system to match trucks to shipments using GPS data when vehicle identifiers are missing; leverages Uber H3 spatial indexing for route features and LightGBM with threshold-based post-processing; shows large precision gains and doubled coverage; robust to 1 km geocoding errors and sparse pings; production deployment at Project44.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address missing or corrupted vehicle identifiers that prevent traditional matching, enabling real-time visibility and accurate ETAs in full truckload supply chains.

Method: Formulate matching as probabilistic ranking. Discretize GPS pings with Uber H3 hex grid to derive route similarity features plus temporal information; train LightGBM gradient boosting model with threshold-based post-processing; compare offline models (SVM, XGBoost, LightGBM) and perform ablations and production shadow testing.

Result: Demonstrates substantial gains over rule-based baselines: 26 percentage point precision improvement in North America, 14 points in Europe, and doubled coverage. Robust to geocoding errors up to 1 km, supports multiple candidate trucks, and works with sparse pings. Deployed in production at Project44 for full truckload shipments.

Conclusion: ITM 2.0 delivers accurate truck-to-shipment matching under incomplete identifiers, enabling pervasive visibility through ML-driven probabilistic ranking with scalable, robust deployment.

Abstract: Accurate truck-to-shipment matching using GPS data is foundational for full truckload supply chain visibility, enabling real-time tracking and accurate estimated time of arrival (ETA) predictions. However, missing or corrupted vehicle identifiers prevent traditional matching approaches, leaving shipments without visibility. This paper presents Intelligent Truck Matching (ITM) 2.0, a machine learning system that addresses this critical gap by formulating matching as a probabilistic ranking problem. Our approach leverages Uber H3 hexagonal spatial indexing to discretize GPS pings into route similarity features, combined with temporal information, then applies LightGBM gradient boosting with threshold-based post-processing. Through rigorous evaluation including offline model selection (SVM, XGBoost, LightGBM), comprehensive ablation studies, and production shadow testing, we demonstrate substantial gains over rule-based baselines. ITM 2.0 achieves 26 percentage point precision improvement in North America and 14 points in Europe, while doubling coverage. Deployed in production at Project44 handling full truckload shipments, the system demonstrates robustness to geocoding errors up to 1 km, multiple candidate trucks, and sparse pings.

</details>


### [328] [When Losses Align: Gradient-Based Composite Loss Weighting for Efficient Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07756)
*Ivan Karpukhin,Andrey Savchenko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A gradient-based bilevel optimization learns pretraining loss weights online to align the pretraining gradient with the downstream objective, reducing hyperparameter tuning cost while maintaining or improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperparameter tuning for weights of multiple loss terms in large-scale pretraining is computationally expensive. An online method that automatically aligns pretraining objectives with downstream goals can dramatically cut search cost.

Method: A bilevel optimization framework updates the weights of the composite pretraining loss by aligning its gradient with the gradient of a downstream objective. The method leverages loss structure to avoid multiple full backpropagations, reducing overhead to roughly 30% above a single training run.

Result: Empirical evaluation on event-sequence modeling and self-supervised computer vision shows that the approach matches or improves carefully tuned baselines while substantially reducing tuning cost compared to random or Bayesian search.

Conclusion: Online gradient-based weighting of multi-term pretraining losses is effective and practical, enabling efficient hyperparameter tuning without compromising performance.

Abstract: Modern deep models are often pretrained on large-scale data with missing labels using composite objectives, where the relative weights of multiple loss terms act as hyperparameters. Tuning these weights with random search or Bayesian optimization is computationally expensive, as it requires many independent training runs. To address this, we propose a gradient-based bilevel method that learns pretraining loss weights online by aligning the composite pretraining gradient with a downstream objective. By exploiting the structure of the loss, the method avoids the multiple backward passes typically required by truncated backpropagation through the full model, reducing the overhead of hyperparameter tuning to approximately 30% above a single training run. We evaluate the approach on event-sequence modeling and self-supervised computer vision, where it matches or improves upon carefully tuned baselines while substantially reducing the cost of hyperparameter tuning compared to random or Bayesian search.

</details>


### [329] [Learning Large-Scale Modular Addition with an Auxiliary Modulus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07648)
*Hanato Kikuchi,Ryosuke Masuya,Kazuhiko Kawamoto,Hiroshi Kera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces covariate-shift-free training for learning parity/modular addition by using an auxiliary modulus Kq to reduce wrap-around, enabling scalable learning with large N and q, and achieving high τ-accuracy with limited data, outperforming a sparse baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning parity functions under modular addition is input-sensitive. A prior approach scaled by increasing zeros to reduce effective summands, but this creates covariate shift between training and test distributions. A method that scales without shifting input distribution is needed.

Method: Train with an auxiliary modulus Kq during optimization, which decreases wrap-around frequency and problem difficulty while preserving identical input distribution across training and testing. The study analyzes covariate shift theoretically and empirically and benchmarks against a sparse baseline.

Result: Demonstrates strong scalability and sample efficiency. For N=64 and q=974269, 100k samples yield 97.0% τ-accuracy at τ=0.05, while the sparse method yields 9.5% with the same data and 93.9% with 1M samples.

Conclusion: Proposes a covariate-shift-free training approach for modular addition that scales to large N and q with limited data, achieving high accuracy and outperforming sparse baselines; method preserves input distribution while reducing problem difficulty.

Abstract: Learning parity functions, more general modular addition, is a challenging machine learning task due to its input sensitivity. A recent study substantially scaled modular addition learning in both the number of summands and the modulus. Its key idea is to increase zeros in training sequences, reducing the effective number of summands and thus controlling training difficulty; however, this induces covariate shift between training and test input distributions. This study theoretically and empirically analyzes this side effect and proposes a covariate-shift-free method for modular addition. Specifically, we introduce an auxiliary modulus $Kq$ during training, which reduces wrap-around frequency and problem difficulty while preserving the same input distribution across training and testing. Experiments show strong scalability and sample efficiency: even for large input length $N$, large modulus $q$, and small datasets -- where the sparse method fails to learn -- our method achieves equal or better match accuracy and relaxed $τ$-accuracy. For example, at $N=64$ and $q=974269$, our method trained on 100K samples achieves $97.0\%$ $τ$-accuracy at $τ=0.05$, while the sparse method achieves only $9.5\%$ with the same data size and $93.9\%$ even when extended to 1M samples.

</details>


### [330] [POETS: Uncertainty-Aware LLM Optimization via Compute-Efficient Policy Ensembles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07775)
*Nicolas Menet,Andreas Krause,Abbas Rahimi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: POETS introduces Policy Ensembles for Thompson Sampling, a KL-regularized ensemble framework that directly trains policy ensembles to capture epistemic uncertainty, using a shared backbone with independent LoRA adapters. It yields theoretical KL-regularized Thompson sampling regret bounds and state-of-the-art sample efficiency in domains like protein search and quantum circuit design, with robust off-policy performance and small-data benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the exploration–exploitation trade-off in sequential decision-making and black-box optimization by coupling uncertainty quantification with policy optimization, while avoiding the complexity of training a separate uncertainty-aware reward model.

Method: Train a policy ensemble with KL regularization that implicitly encodes a reward function. Instead of modeling uncertainty via a separate reward model, POETS matches implicitly encoded rewards to online bootstrapped data. The ensemble shares a pre-trained backbone and uses independent LoRA branches to maintain diversity. The approach is interpreted as KL-regularized Thompson sampling and comes with regret guarantees.

Result: Theoretical: derives cumulative regret bounds of O(sqrt(T γ_T)). Empirical: achieves state-of-the-art sample efficiency across domains such as protein search and quantum circuit design; improves RL trajectories, with robustness in off-policy settings and small-data regimes.

Conclusion: POETS provides a scalable, uncertainty-aware policy optimization framework that unifies uncertainty quantification with policy learning via KL-regularized policy ensembles. It offers strong theoretical guarantees and broad empirical efficacy, while reducing computational burden through backbone sharing and LoRA-based diversification.

Abstract: Balancing exploration and exploitation is a core challenge in sequential decision-making and black-box optimization. We introduce POETS ($\textbf{Po}$licy $\textbf{E}$nsembles for $\textbf{T}$hompson $\textbf{S}$ampling), a novel framework that bridges uncertainty quantification and policy optimization. Our approach is grounded in the insight that policies trained with Kullback-Leibler (KL) regularization implicitly encode an underlying reward function. Building on this, POETS bypasses the complex, nested process of training an uncertainty-aware reward model and separately fitting a policy to this model. Instead, we directly train a policy ensemble to capture epistemic uncertainty by matching implicitly encoded reward functions to online, bootstrapped data. To overcome the prohibitive compute and memory constraints of ensembling Large Language Models (LLMs), POETS utilizes an efficient architecture: the ensemble shares a pre-trained backbone while maintaining diversity through independent Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) branches. Theoretically, we prove that POETS implicitly conducts KL-regularized Thompson sampling and thus inherits strong cumulative regret bounds of ${\mathcal O}(\sqrt{T γ_T})$. Empirically, we demonstrate that POETS achieves state-of-the-art sample efficiency across diverse scientific discovery domains, including protein search and quantum circuit design. Furthermore, it improves the optimization trajectories of reinforcement learning, proving particularly robust in off-policy settings with experience replay or in small dataset regimes.

</details>


### [331] [Direction-Preserving Number Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07662)
*Bardia Zadeh,George A. Constantinides*

Main category: cs.LG
