TL;DR: Quantifies how well product-structured scalar alphabets (low-precision formats) cover vector directions compared to spherical codes, showing standard formats are suboptimal and optimizing alphabets yields gains; explains why NVIDIA NVFP4 with 4 bits (E2M1) performs well and provides Lean proofs and open-source code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand and improve directional representation accuracy in low-precision ML, by analyzing the inherent suboptimality of common scalar formats and exploring optimized alphabets for direction coverage.

Method: Develop a geometric framework for directional coverage of product-structured codes; analytically bound the suboptimality gap to spherical codes in low and high dimensions; prove suboptimality of standard formats (two's complement, fixed-point, floating-point); numerically optimize scalar alphabets across block dimensions; compare with standard formats; analyze NVFP4 (E2M1) performance; provide Lean formal proofs and open-source code.

Result: Derived quantitative suboptimality gaps between product codes and spherical codes; identified that standard formats are suboptimal; optimized alphabets yield improved directional coverage; observed that for four bits, NVIDIA’s E2M1 alphabet closely matches the optimized alphabet, explaining its empirical strength; supplied open-source Lean proofs and experimental code along with the optimized alphabets.

Conclusion: There is potential to design new scalar number formats that improve directional representation in low-precision ML; directional coverage depends on block size, and optimized alphabets can approach or surpass standard formats in practice; the work provides theoretical, empirical, and formal proof resources to support such development.

Abstract: Low-precision number formats are widely used in modern machine learning systems due to their efficiency. Accurate direction representation is key to the accuracy of vector operations. This work precisely explores the extent to which the direction of a vector can be represented by selecting its scalar elements from a common finite alphabet of a given size. This is standard practice in machine learning, where low-precision significands may be narrow-width floating-point or integer values. A geometric framework is introduced for analyzing the directional coverage of such product-structured codes.
  This work analytically quantifies the suboptimality gap between such product-structured codes and spherical codes for the vector as a whole, in both low and asymptotically high dimensions. Furthermore, within the product code class, it is proven that the standard formats of two's complement, fixed-point, and floating-point are suboptimal, again with quantified gap, pointing to the potential to develop new scalar number formats.
  Such scalar alphabets are numerically optimized across multiple block dimensions for directional coverage, including the dimension used in NVIDIA's NVFP4 format. Experimental results are presented comparing the performance of standard formats and the optimized alphabet. We find that for four bits, NVIDIA's choice of E2M1 closely approximates the optimized alphabet, providing a geometric explanation for its strong performance in low-precision machine learning workloads and an analytical understanding of the link between that superiority and block size.
  We provide open-source formal proofs in Lean for the theorems in this work, along with the experimental code and the optimized alphabets obtained.

</details>


### [332] [Structured Coupling for Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07676)
*Xavier Sumba,Carles Balsells-Rodas,Yingzhen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCFM couples structured latent-variable learning with flow matching to yield an unconditional, simulation-free flow model that learns interpretable structure without sacrificing sample quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard flow matching scales well but lacks interpretable latent structure; latent-variable models capture structure but often degrade generative quality. A unified approach is needed to learn structured latent representations while preserving high-quality generation.

Method: Introduce structured latent variables and exogenous noise into the source, jointly learning a structured prior via latent-variable modeling and a continuous transport map via flow matching. Use a shared time-dependent recognition network for both variational inference and intermediate-time flow velocity estimation, yielding an unconditional, simulation-free flow model where the latent-variable model can assist flow sampling.

Result: Empirically, SCFM enables unsupervised latent representation learning for clustering, disentanglement, and downstream tasks, while remaining competitive with standard flow matching in sample quality, demonstrating that meaningful structure can be learned without sacrificing generative fidelity.

Conclusion: SCFM provides a principled framework to jointly learn structured latent representations and transport maps, bridging flow matching and latent-variable modeling to produce interpretable structure without compromising generative performance.

Abstract: Standard flow matching scales well but typically relies on an unstructured source distribution, limiting its ability to learn interpretable latent structure. Latent-variable models, by contrast, capture structure but often sacrifice generative quality. We bridge this gap by proposing Structured Coupling for Flow Matching (SCFM), a cooperative framework that augments flow matching with structured latent representation learning. By introducing structured latent variables and exogenous noise into the source, SCFM jointly learns a structured prior (via latent variable modeling) and a continuous transport map (via flow matching). It uses a shared time-dependent recognition network for both latent variable model variational inference and intermediate-time flow velocity estimation. This yields a structurally informed yet unconditional, simulation-free flow model, where the latent variable model can also assist flow sampling. Empirically, SCFM facilitates unsupervised latent representation learning for clustering, disentanglement and downstream tasks, while remaining competitive with flow matching in sample quality, showing that meaningful structure can be learned without sacrificing generative fidelity.

</details>


### [333] [Neural Operators as Efficient Function Interpolators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07792)
*Vasilis Niarchos,Angelos Sirbu,Sokratis Trifinopoulos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reframes neural operators via a base-space and composition to enable efficient, accurate finite-dimensional interpolation; competitive with MLPs and KANs; TFNO applied to nuclear masses achieves strong RMSE with good efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance data efficiency and scalability of neural operators in finite-dimensional settings by introducing an auxiliary base-space, allowing finite-dimensional functions to be viewed as operators through composition; aims for accuracy with fewer parameters and faster training.

Method: Introduce a base-space concept; view finite-dimensional functions as operators acting by composition on base-space functions; benchmark on analytic function families of increasing complexity/dimensionality; compare neural operators to MLPs and Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks; apply a Tensorized Fourier Neural Operator (TFNO) to learn residual corrections for nuclear mass models; use an ensemble to improve robustness and RMSE.

Result: Neural operators match or exceed the accuracy of standard MLPs and KANs with substantially fewer parameters and shorter training times; TFNO ensemble achieves a held-out RMSE of 198.2 keV on nuclear masses, placing it among leading neural approaches while maintaining parameter efficiency.

Conclusion: The base-space reframing makes neural operators a scalable, parameter-efficient framework for finite-dimensional function interpolation across analytic benchmarks and structured scientific data.

Abstract: Neural operators (NOs) are designed to learn maps between infinite-dimensional function spaces. We propose a novel reframing of their use. By introducing an auxiliary base-space, any finite-dimensional function can be viewed as an operator acting by composition on functions of the base-space. Through a range of benchmarks on analytic functions of increasing complexity and dimensionality, we demonstrate that NOs can match or outperform standard multilayer perceptrons and Kolmogorov--Arnold Networks in accuracy while requiring significantly fewer parameters and training time. As a real-world application, we apply a two-dimensional Tensorized Fourier Neural Operator (TFNO) to the nuclear chart, learning a correction to state-of-the-art nuclear mass models as a partially observed residual field. A TFNO ensemble reaches a held-out root-mean-square error of 198.2 keV, placing it among the best recent neural-network approaches while retaining high parameter efficiency and short training times. More broadly, these results introduce NOs as a scalable framework for finite-dimensional function interpolation, from analytic benchmarks to structured scientific data.

</details>


### [334] [Toward Privileged Foundation Models:LUPI for Accelerated and Improved Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07799)
*Xueying Ding,Leman Akoglu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PIQL introduces Privileged Information to speed up training and improve generalization for tabular foundation models by incorporating aggregate statistics and data-generating-program encodings; it enables train-time PI transfer to inference, supported by theoretical convergence analysis and empirical gains in convergence, final loss, and generalization, reducing data and compute needs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training large foundation models is computationally intensive and slow to converge. Leveraging privileged information (PI) aims to reduce the approximation gap and data requirements, accelerating learning and improving generalization in tabular settings.

Method: Propose two forms of PI: (i) aggregate dataset-level statistics to reduce context dependence, (ii) encodings of the underlying data-generating program. Develop an architecture that reconstructs train-time-only PI from observed context at inference, enabling PI transfer. Provide theoretical analysis of conditions under which PI reduces population-level approximation error and accelerates convergence in finite-data regimes.

Result: Theoretical analysis delineates conditions under which PI reduces the population-level gap and speeds convergence. Empirically, PIQL shows faster convergence, lower final loss, and improved generalization for tabular foundation models, with reduced data and compute requirements.

Conclusion: PI-guided pretraining is a principled and practical paradigm for enhancing efficiency and performance of foundation models.

Abstract: Training foundation models is computationally intensive and often slow to converge.We introduce PIQL,Privileged Information for Quick and Quality Learning, the first framework to systematically integrate privileged information (PI) to simultaneously accelerate learning and improve generalization in tabular foundation models (TFMs). We construct two complementary forms of PI: (i) aggregate dataset-level statistics that reduce the burden on in-context learning, and (ii) encodings of the underlying data-generating program, providing knowledge beyond observable data. We further design an architecture that effectively transfers the train-time-only PI by learning to reconstruct it from observed context at inference. We provide a theoretical analysis characterizing conditions under which PI reduces the population-level approximation gap and accelerates convergence in finite-data regimes. Empirical evidence shows that PIQL enables TFMs to achieve faster convergence, lower final loss, and better generalization, in effect, reducing data and compute requirements. Our work establishes PI-guided pretraining as a principled and practical paradigm for improving the efficiency and performance of foundation models.

</details>


### [335] [Prune-OPD: Efficient and Reliable On-Policy Distillation for Long-Horizon Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07804)
*Zhicheng Yang,Zhijiang Guo,Yifan Song,Minrui Xu,Yongxin Wang,Yiwei Wang,Xiaodan Liang,Jing Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Prune-OPD dynamically re-allocates training compute by detecting prefix drift between student and teacher; it down-weights unreliable dense rewards and truncates rollouts when drift is detected, improving efficiency without sacrificing performance on long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) suffers when the student’s prefix diverges from the teacher, making dense rewards less exploitable and wasting compute on drifted trajectories; a mechanism to align training budget with supervision quality is needed.

Method: Monitor local compatibility between student and teacher predictions (e.g., top-k overlap) to detect drift in real time; upon severe drift, monotonically down-weight subsequent rewards and trigger dynamic rollout truncation; reallocate compute to reliable teacher supervision; if compatibility is high, preserve or expand long-context supervision by extending the training window.

Result: Across diverse teacher-student pairs, Prune-OPD reduces training time by 37.6%–68.0% when dense rewards become unreliable, while preserving or improving performance on AMC, AIME, and HMMT; when compatibility remains high, it automatically preserves long-context supervision by expanding the training window.

Conclusion: Prune-OPD improves OPD not by blindly shortening rollouts but by reallocating computation toward locally exploitable teacher rewards, achieving efficiency gains with little or no loss in performance and sometimes gains on challenging benchmarks.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) leverages dense teacher rewards to enhance reasoning models. However, scaling OPD to long-horizon tasks exposes a critical flaw: as the student's generated prefix inevitably diverges from the teacher's thought process, the teacher's dense reward loses local exploitability. Continuing to generate and evaluate tokens on these ``drifted'' trajectories not only degrades reward quality but also incurs massive computational waste. To address this, we introduce \textbf{Prune-OPD}, a framework that dynamically aligns training budgets with supervision quality. By continuously monitoring the local compatibility between student and teacher predictions (e.g., via top-$k$ overlap), Prune-OPD detects prefix-drift events in real time. Upon detecting severe drift, it monotonically down-weights subsequent unreliable rewards and triggers dynamic rollout truncation. This allows the training process to halt futile generation and reallocate compute strictly to reliable teacher supervision. Across diverse teacher-student combinations, Prune-OPD consistently aligns computation with supervision reliability. When prefix drift makes dense teacher rewards unreliable, it reduces training time by 37.6\%--68.0\% while preserving, and often improving, performance on challenging benchmarks (AMC, AIME, HMMT). When student-teacher compatibility remains high, it automatically preserves long-context supervision by expanding the training window. These results suggest that Prune-OPD improves OPD not by blindly shortening rollouts, but by reallocating computation toward locally exploitable teacher rewards.

</details>


### [336] [Fortifying Time Series: DTW-Certified Robust Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07690)
*Shijie Liu,Tansu Alpcan,Christopher Leckie,Sarah Erfani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First certifiable robustness for time-series anomaly detection under DTW via randomized smoothing, bridging DTW distances to Lp-norm robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robustness to adversarial manipulation is critical in time-series anomaly detection. Lp-based certificates are ill-suited for time-series due to temporal distortions; DTW captures temporal alignment more naturally, but there were no DTW-based certified guarantees.

Method: Introduce a DTW-certified robust defense by adapting randomized smoothing to DTW. Establish a lower-bound transformation that connects Lp-norm perturbations to DTW distance, enabling certifiable robustness under DTW. Validate theoretically and empirically across datasets/models, including DTW-based adversarial attacks.

Result: Significant improvements in robustness under DTW-based attacks, e.g., up to 18.7% absolute F1-score gain over traditional certified models, demonstrated across multiple datasets and models.

Conclusion: DTW-certified robustness for time-series anomaly detection is feasible and effective by bridging DTW to Lp-norm robustness via a lower-bound transformation; opens avenues for more practical, DTW-aligned certifications and invites further tightening of bounds and broader evaluation.

Abstract: Time-series anomaly detection is critical for ensuring safety in high-stakes applications, where robustness is a fundamental requirement rather than a mere performance metric. Addressing the vulnerability of these systems to adversarial manipulation is therefore essential. Existing defenses are largely heuristic or provide certified robustness only under $\ell_p$-norm constraints, which are incompatible with time-series data. In particular, $\ell_p$-norm fails to capture the intrinsic temporal structure in time series, causing small temporal distortions to significantly alter the $\ell_p$-norm measures. Instead, the similarity metric \emph{Dynamic Time Warping} (DTW) is more suitable and widely adopted in the time-series domain, as DTW accounts for temporal alignment and remains robust to temporal variations. To date, however, there has been no certifiable robustness result in this metric that provides guarantees. In this work, we introduce the first \emph{DTW-certified robust defense} in time-series anomaly detection by adapting the randomized smoothing paradigm. We develop this certificate by bridging the $\ell_p$-norm to DTW distance through a lower-bound transformation. Extensive experiments across various datasets and models validate the effectiveness and practicality of our theoretical approach. Results demonstrate significantly improved performance, e.g., up to 18.7\% in F1-score under DTW-based adversarial attacks compared to traditional certified models.

</details>


### [337] [Toward Better Geometric Representations for Molecule Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07693)
*Shaoheng Yan,Zian Li,Cai Zhou,Qiaojing Huang,Kai Liu,Muhan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LENSEs improves representation-conditioned molecule generation by aligning and enriching pre-trained encoder representations through a multi-head representation extractor, a semantic perceptual loss, and a node-level alignment loss, yielding higher validity and stability and smoother representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pretrained molecular encoders (e.g., UniMol) produce non-smooth, underutilized representations for generation. The work aims to exploit the representation space more fully to improve generation quality and semantics.

Method: Introduce three mechanisms: (1) a representation head that jointly extracts multi-level representations during generation; (2) a molecule perceptual loss operating in the representation space to guide the generator semantically; (3) a node-level REPA loss aligning the generator's hidden states with encoder representations to reduce the semantic gap between pretraining and generation.

Result: On GEOM-DRUG, LENSEs achieves 97.28% validity and 98.51% molecule stability, surpassing baselines. Additional analyses show 4.6x reduction in the Lipschitz constant and improved QM9 probing, indicating smoother, more informative representations and suggesting the approach as a potential pretraining paradigm for molecular encoders.

Conclusion: Generative training with explicit representation alignment objectives can leverage pretrained representation spaces more effectively, producing higher-quality molecular generation and offering a pathway to use alignment-based objectives as pretraining for molecular encoders.

Abstract: Geometric representation-conditioned molecule generation provides an effective paradigm that decouples molecule representation modeling from structure generation. By decoupling molecule generation into two stages-first generating a meaningful molecule representation, and then generating a 3D molecule conditioned on this representation-the efficiency and quality of the generation process can be significantly enhanced. However, its effectiveness is fundamentally limited by the quality of the representation space: pretrained molecular encoders, such as UniMol, produce representations that are non-smooth and not fully exploited during the generative training process. In this work, we propose LENSEs, a framework that better exploits the potential of molecule representations in representation-conditioned generation methods. In particular, LENSEs introduces three complementary mechanisms: (1) a representation head, simultaneously trained during generative tasks, that extracts multi-level representations from the pretrained encoder; (2) a molecule perceptual loss that optimizes the generator in a semantic-informative representation space; and (3) a node-level representation alignment (REPA) loss that explicitly aligns the generator's hidden states with encoder representations, reducing the semantic gap between pretraining and generation. We demonstrate the effectiveness of these improvements through extensive molecule generation tasks. Specifically, on the challenging molecule generation dataset GEOM-DRUG, LENSEs achieves 97.28% validity and 98.51% molecule stability, surpassing existing advanced methods. Further analyses through Lipschitz constant reduction (4.6x) and QM9 probing tasks also demonstrate the smoother, more informative refined representations, establishing generative training with alignment objectives as a potential pretraining paradigm for molecular encoders.

</details>


### [338] [Bayesian Fine-tuning in Projected Subspaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07706)
*Viktar Dubovik,Patryk Marszałek,Jacek Tabor,Tomasz Kuśmierczyk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a parameter-efficient Bayesian fine-tuning framework for LoRA that achieves uncertainty quantification in a very low-dimensional parameter space, maintaining efficiency while improving calibration and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LoRA lacks uncertainty quantification, leading to overconfident predictions; Bayesian LoRA improves calibration but greatly increases trainable parameters and can hinder training stability, undermining efficiency gains.

Method: Introduce a novel framework for parameter-efficient Bayesian fine-tuning that projects weight-space uncertainty into a low-dimensional subspace. The method leverages the observation that weight covariances are low-rank, enabling effective uncertainty modeling with few parameters.

Result: Empirical results show strong performance with improved calibration and generalization and preserved computational efficiency. Uncertainty can be effectively modeled in a low-dimensional space, and weight covariances exhibit low ranks.

Conclusion: Uncertainty quantification for LoRA-like adapters can be achieved efficiently by low-dimensional Bayesian projections, delivering calibration and generalization benefits without sacrificing efficiency.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) enables parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large models by decomposing weight updates into low-rank matrices, significantly reducing storage and computational overhead. While effective, standard LoRA lacks mechanisms for uncertainty quantification, leading to overconfident and poorly calibrated models. Bayesian variants of LoRA address this limitation, but at the cost of a significantly increased number of trainable parameters, partially offsetting the original efficiency gains. Additionally, these models are harder to train and may suffer from unstable convergence. In this work, we propose a novel framework for parameter-efficient Bayesian fine-tuning, demonstrating that effective uncertainty quantification can be achieved in very low-dimensional parameter spaces. The proposed method achieves strong performance with improved calibration and generalization while maintaining computational efficiency. Our empirical findings show that, with the appropriate projection of the weight space uncertainty can be effectively modeled in a low-dimensional space, and weight covariances exhibit low ranks.

</details>


### [339] [Approximation-Free Differentiable Oblique Decision Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07837)
*Subrat Prasad Panda,Blaise Genest,Arvind Easwaran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DTSemNet provides an invertible neural-network representation of hard oblique decision trees enabling end-to-end gradient-based training without approximation, with an annealed Top-k gradient for regression; it achieves state-of-the-art performance in classification and regression and extends to reinforcement learning as DT policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Oblique decision trees offer interpretability and strong performance on tabular data, but training accurate oblique DTs is hard due to nonconvex optimization and overfitting, especially for regression. Current differentiable DTs rely on soft boundaries or STE approximations, which introduce inaccuracies.

Method: Introduce DTSemNet: a semantically equivalent, invertible representation of hard oblique DTs as neural networks allowing end-to-end gradient descent without approximation. For regression, analyze STE limitations and adopt an annealed Top-k gradient to provide accurate signals. Compare against state-of-the-art differentiable DTs and demonstrate applicability as programmatic policies in reinforcement learning.

Result: DTSemNet-trained oblique DTs outperform state-of-the-art differentiable DTs on classification and regression benchmarks. The framework also enables using DTSemNet as DT policies in RL environments.

Conclusion: A gradient-based, non-approximate training paradigm for hard oblique DTs is feasible via an invertible neural-network representation. The annealed Top-k gradient improves regression optimization. DTSemNet broadens the applicability of DTs to RL and maintains interpretability.

Abstract: Decision Trees (DTs) are widely used in safety-critical domains such as medical diagnosis, valued for their interpretability and effectiveness on tabular data. However, training accurate oblique DTs is challenging due to complex optimization landscapes and overfitting risks, particularly in regression. Recent advances have introduced differentiable formulations that enable gradient-based training and joint optimization of decision boundaries and leaf regressors. Yet, existing approaches typically rely on approximations, either through probabilistic softening of boundaries (soft DTs) or quantized gradients such as the Straight-Through Estimator (STE). To overcome these limitations, we propose DTSemNet, a novel, semantically equivalent, and invertible representation of hard oblique DTs as neural networks. DTSemNet enables end-to-end training with standard gradient descent, eliminating the need for approximations in both classification and regression. While classification aligns naturally with this formulation, regression remains challenging due to the joint optimization of internal nodes and leaf regressors. To address this, we analyze the limitations of STE and introduce an annealed Top-k method that provides accurate gradient signals without approximation. Extensive experiments on classification and regression benchmarks show that DTSemNet-trained oblique DTs outperform state-of-the-art differentiable DTs. Furthermore, we demonstrate that DTSemNet can serve as programmatic DT policies in reinforcement learning environments, thereby broadening their applicability.

</details>


### [340] [\mathsf{VISTA}: Decentralized Machine Learning in Adversary Dominated Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07841)
*Hanzaleh Akbari Nodehi,Parsa Moradi,Soheil Mohajer,Mohammad Ali Maddah-Ali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive incentive-aware aggregation (VISTA) for adversary-dominated decentralized SGD; tunes acceptance threshold from optimization history to balance speed and accuracy, preserving SGD-like convergence without honest-majority assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decentralized machine learning often relies on untrusted worker nodes. Robust aggregations assume an honest majority but fail with adversaries controlling most workers. Modeling adversaries as rational, incentive-driven agents allows long-horizon decisions and flexible defenses.

Method: Propose VISTA, an adaptive algorithm that adjusts the acceptance/rejection threshold based on the optimization history. In iterative learning, permissive acceptance accelerates early progress but admits more corruption, while strict rules improve accuracy but cause rejections. The method formalizes incentive-aware adaptation and analyzes convergence under such settings.

Result: Numerical experiments show VISTA improves convergence relative to static thresholds. A rigorous convergence analysis demonstrates that, with proper incentive-aware adaptation, adversary-dominated decentralized learning retains the asymptotic convergence behavior of SGD without requiring an honest majority.

Conclusion: Incentive-aware adaptive thresholds enable robust decentralized learning under adversarial majority, achieving SGD-like convergence and providing a shift from purely robust aggregation to incentive-driven defenses.

Abstract: Decentralized machine learning often relies on outsourcing computations, such as gradient evaluations, to untrusted worker nodes. Existing robust aggregation methods can mitigate malicious behavior under honest-majority assumptions, but may fail when adversaries control a majority of the workers. We study this adversary-dominated setting through an incentive-oriented framework in which reports are accepted and rewarded only when they are mutually consistent up to a threshold. This turns the adversary from a pure saboteur into a rational agent that trades off increasing estimation error against the risk of rejection and loss of reward.
  We consider iterative optimization under this model. Unlike one-shot computation, iterative learning requires long-horizon decisions: permissive acceptance rules enable faster early progress but admit more adversarial corruption, while strict rules improve estimation accuracy but cause frequent rejections. We propose \mathsf{VISTA}, an adaptive algorithm that tunes the acceptance threshold using the optimization history. Numerical results show that \mathsf{VISTA} improves convergence over static thresholds. We also provide a rigorous convergence analysis showing that, with suitable incentive-aware adaptation, adversary-dominated decentralized learning can retain the asymptotic convergence behavior of standard SGD without relying on an honest majority.

</details>


### [341] [On the Tradeoffs of On-Device Generative Models in Federated Predictive Maintenance Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07860)
*Usevalad Milasheuski,Piero Baraldi,Enrico Zio,Stefano Savazzi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated generative models (VAE, GAN, DDPM) are analyzed for unsupervised time-series anomaly detection in federated predictive maintenance, introducing a taxonomy of partial component sharing as model personalization; findings show GANs benefit from full federation for stability, while DDPMs can outperform full federation under bandwidth constraints with decoder sharing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preserve data ownership in FL for IoT-based predictive maintenance and enable effective unsupervised anomaly detection using generative models; address communication overhead and personalization via partial sharing.

Method: Empirical analysis of VAEs, GANs, and DDPMs under full and partial federation settings, with subsets of model components shared; propose a taxonomy for federated generative models; evaluate on real-world time-series data focusing on utility, stability, scalability, and bandwidth.

Result: GAN-based configurations: full federation improves training stability versus independent local training but remains less robust than VAE- and DDPM-based alternatives. DDPMs: partial federation, especially decoder sharing, can outperform full federation in bandwidth-constrained, non-IID settings.

Conclusion: Partial component sharing is a principled mechanism for model personalization in federated generative models for PdM; different models exhibit distinct trade-offs in utility, stability, and scalability under heterogeneous and bandwidth-limited FL conditions.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for preserving client data ownership and control over distributed Internet of Things (IoT) environments. While discriminative models dominate most FL use cases, recent advances in generative models -- such as Variational Autoencoders (VAE), Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN), and Diffusion Models (DM) -- offer new opportunities for unsupervised anomaly detection in time series analysis, with relevant applications in predictive maintenance (PdM) in critical industrial infrastructures. In this work, we present a comprehensive analysis of VAEs, GANs, and DMs in the context of federated PdM. We analyze their performance and communication overhead under both full and partial federation setups, where only subsets of model components are shared. Building on this analysis, the paper proposes a novel taxonomy for federated generative models that formalizes partial component sharing as a principled mechanism for model personalization. Our experiments over a real-world time series dataset reveal distinct trade-offs in model utility, stability, and scalability, especially in heterogeneous and bandwidth-constrained FL settings. For the evaluated GAN-based configurations, full federation improves training stability relative to independent local training, although the model remains less robust than the VAE- and DDPM-based alternatives. For DMs, however, partial federation -- especially decoder sharing -- can outperform full federation in bandwidth-constrained, non-IID settings.

</details>


### [342] [KL for a KL: On-Policy Distillation with Control Variate Baseline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07865)
*Minjae Oh,Sangjun Song,Gyubin Choi,Yunho Choi,Yohan Jo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: vOPD introduces a control variate baseline (value function) for On-Policy Distillation, reducing gradient variance via a closed-form per-token negative reverse KL between teacher and student; supports full-vocabulary or top-k approximations; achieves stable and efficient distillation with competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OPD suffers from high gradient variance due to a single-sample Monte Carlo estimator, causing instability; a lightweight, principled variance-reduction method is needed.

Method: Formulate OPD as policy-gradient RL and apply a control variate baseline in the form of a value function derived from the forward pass. The value function equals the per-token negative reverse KL between student and teacher, computable without extra critics. This baseline is subtracted as a detached term to preserve an unbiased gradient. Costs are reduced by offering a top-k approximation of the baseline.

Result: vOPD consistently outperforms vanilla OPD on mathematical and scientific reasoning benchmarks and matches the performance of the expensive full-vocabulary baseline, while reducing computational overhead.

Conclusion: A principled RL variance-reduction approach stabilizes On-Policy Distillation with efficient baseline estimates; top-k baselines offer additional cost savings without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: On-Policy Distillation (OPD) has emerged as a dominant post-training paradigm for large language models, especially for reasoning domains. However, OPD remains unstable in practice due to the high gradient variance of its single-sample Monte Carlo estimator, and recipes for stable training are still immature. We propose vOPD (On-Policy Distillation with a control variate baseline), which casts OPD as policy-gradient RL and stabilizes it by introducing a control variate baseline-canonically a value function -- from the RL literature. We show that the OPD value function admits a closed form as the per-token negative reverse KL divergence between the student and the teacher, available directly from the already-computed forward pass with no additional critic or inference. Existing stabilization methods either compute the full token-level reverse KL over the entire vocabulary, adding significant overhead, or restrict it to a top-k support, biasing the objective. vOPD instead preserves the lightweight single-sample estimator, subtracting the value function as a detached baseline to keep the gradient unbiased while reducing variance. Furthermore, we show that a top-k approximation of the baseline further lowers cost without compromising performance. Across mathematical and scientific reasoning benchmarks, vOPD consistently outperforms vanilla OPD and matches the most expensive full-vocabulary baseline, offering an efficient stabilization of On-Policy Distillation through principled RL variance reduction.

</details>


### [343] [Robust and Reliable AI for Predictive Quality in Semiconductor Materials Manufacturing with MLOps and Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07752)
*Min Gao,Julia Maria Perathoner,Anton Ludwig Bonin,Steven Eulig,Gianni Klesse*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A five-batch fixed retraining cadence without hyperparameter optimization provides robust performance across drift scenarios and reduces computational cost, with conformal prediction delivering valid uncertainty intervals for proactive QC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Maintain model accuracy in manufacturing ML under evolving process conditions, equipment degradation, and material variability; address practical needs for efficient retraining and uncertainty quantification.

Method: Five-year real manufacturing data; evaluate retraining frequencies and hyperparameter optimization strategies; use control limit normalized residuals as metric; test abrupt and gradual drift; implement conformal prediction for prediction intervals; compare strategies in terms of accuracy and compute.

Result: Fixed cadence of every five batches without HP retuning achieves superior performance across drift conditions; reduces computational overhead vs HP-optimized strategies; conformal prediction yields strong statistical guarantees for intervals; intervals help proactive QC.

Conclusion: Provide guidelines for robust MLOps in manufacturing with balance between compute and accuracy, and reliable uncertainty quantification; adopt fixed low-frequency retraining with optional non retuning; use conformal prediction in decision-making.

Abstract: Semiconductor materials manufacturing presents unique challenges for machine learning deployment due to evolving process conditions, equipment degradation, and raw material variability that can cause model performance deterioration over time. This study benchmarks machine learning operations (MLOps) retraining strategies using five years of real manufacturing data to identify optimal retraining approaches for quality prediction. We evaluate various retraining frequencies and hyperparameter optimization strategies using control limit normalized residuals as key performance metric. Results demonstrate that a fixed retraining cadence every five production batches without hyperparameter retuning achieves superior performance across all drift conditions while significantly reducing computational overhead compared to strategies incorporating hyperparameter optimization. This approach effectively maintains model accuracy during both abrupt process changes and gradual equipment degradation patterns. To address the critical need for uncertainty quantification in manufacturing decision-making, we implement conformal prediction to generate prediction confidence intervals with strong statistical guarantees. This enables proactive quality control by identifying when prediction intervals fall within acceptable control limits, transforming traditional reactive quality management into a predictive framework. The findings provide practical guidelines for implementing robust MLOps strategies in manufacturing environments where computational efficiency and reliable uncertainty quantification are paramount for operational success.

</details>


### [344] [Rethinking State Tracking in Recurrent Models Through Error Control Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07755)
*Jiwan Chung,Heechan Choi,Seon Joo Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Affine recurrent networks cannot maintain robust state tracking; error accumulation along state-separating directions leads to a finite horizon of reliable tracking governed by a readability threshold of the decoder. Expressivity alone is insufficient; error control dynamics determine practical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond purely expressive capacity of recurrent architectures and study how error dynamics constrain state-tracking ability, particularly for affine trackers like State-Space Models and Linear Attention.

Method: Theoretical analysis proving limitations of affine recurrent networks in correcting errors along state-separating subspaces while preserving representations; derivation of a readability threshold based on between-class separation and within-class spread; empirical validation on group state-tracking tasks showing the crossing of this threshold predicts horizon of downstream accuracy loss across trained models.

Result: Affine trackers exhibit finite-horizon state tracking governed by accumulated, task-relevant error; robust tracking fails when within-class spread overtakes initial between-class separation, as predicted by the readability threshold; the horizon of accuracy loss correlates with the threshold crossing across models.

Conclusion: Robust state tracking hinges on error control mechanisms, not solely on architectural expressivity; to achieve enduring tracking, models must manage the geometry of errors over time, prompting attention to training objectives and architecture that constrain or correct drift along state-distinguishing directions.

Abstract: The theory of state tracking in recurrent architectures has predominantly focused on expressive capacity: whether a fixed architecture can theoretically realize a set of symbolic transition rules. We argue that equally important is error control, the dynamics governing hidden-state drift along the directions that distinguish symbolic states. We prove that affine recurrent networks, a class of models encompassing State-Space Models and Linear Attention, cannot correct errors along state-separating subspaces once they preserve state representations. Consequently, practical affine trackers do not learn robust state tracking; rather, they learn finite horizon solutions governed by accumulated state-relevant error. We characterize the mechanics of this failure, showing that tracking remains readable only while the accumulating within-class spread remains small relative to the initial between-class separation. We demonstrate empirically on group state-tracking tasks that this breakdown is predictable: tracking collapses when the distinguishability ratio crosses the readability threshold of the trained decoder. Across trained models, the point of this crossing predicts the horizon at which downstream accuracy fails. These results establish that robust state tracking is determined not only by an architecture's theoretical expressivity but crucially by its error control.

</details>


### [345] [Trajectory as the Teacher: Few-Step Discrete Flow Matching via Energy-Navigated Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07924)
*Amin Karimi Monsefi,Dominic Culver,Nikhil Bhendawade,Manuel R. Ciosici,Yizhe Zhang,Irina Belousova*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TS-DFM replaces blind trajectory sampling in discrete flow matching with guided navigation using an energy compass, achieving faster, lower perplexity student models than the traditional multi-step teacher.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The causal bottleneck in discrete flow model distillation is the trajectory quality, not student capacity. Early mistakes in a training trajectory propagate and degrade the student when mirroring the teacher.

Method: Introduce a lightweight energy compass that evaluates candidate continuations at each trajectory midpoint and selects the most coherent path during training (guided navigation). Training-only shaping; inference cost unchanged; evaluated on a ~170M-parameter LM.

Result: An 8-step shaped student achieves 32% lower perplexity than a 1,024-step teacher and runs 128x faster; gains are consistent across source distributions and three evaluators; TS-DFM achieves the best perplexity among discrete-generation baselines, even when others use 6x more data or 5x larger models.

Conclusion: Guided trajectory navigation dissolves the trajectory bottleneck in discrete flow matching, enabling efficient, high-quality discrete-generation models without increasing inference cost.

Abstract: Discrete flow matching generates text by iteratively transforming noise tokens into coherent language, but may require hundreds of forward passes. Distillation uses the multi-step trajectory to train a student to reproduce the process in a few steps. When the student underperforms, the usual explanation is insufficient capacity. We argue the opposite: the trajectory is the bottleneck, not the student. Each training trajectory is built through a chain of blind stochastic jumps with no evaluation of sequence quality; a single bad decision at an early midpoint propagates through subsequent steps, yet the student must imitate the result. Trajectory-Shaped Discrete Flow Matching (TS-DFM) replaces these blind jumps with guided navigation: a lightweight energy compass evaluates candidate continuations at each midpoint, selecting the most coherent. All shaping is training-only; inference cost is unchanged. On 170M-parameter language modeling, the shaped student at 8 steps achieves 32% lower perplexity than the 1,024-step teacher while being 128x faster, with gains consistent across source distributions and three evaluators of increasing scale. TS-DFM achieves the best perplexity of any discrete-generation baseline we compare against, including methods trained on 6x more data or using 5x larger models.

</details>


### [346] [INO-SGD: Addressing Utility Imbalance under Individualized Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07930)
*Xiao Tian,Jue Fan,Rachael Hwee Ling Sim,Bryan Kian Hsiang Low*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: INO-SGD: a batch-weighting SGD method to address utility imbalance in individualized DP (IDP) while satisfying IDP, by down-weighting data from less-private owners to boost more-private data; empirically feasible.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing emphasis on personalized privacy (IDP) requires protecting individual privacy preferences in ML. Existing IDP methods cause utility imbalance where data from stricter privacy individuals are underrepresented, harming performance on similar data. A method that satisfies IDP and reduces this imbalance is needed.

Method: Introduce INO-SGD, a per-batch weighting strategy that down-weights data from less-private owners within each SGD batch to increase the relative influence of more-private data across iterations, designed to satisfy IDP constraints.

Result: Demonstrates empirical feasibility: improved or balanced performance on more-private data across training iterations, while maintaining IDP satisfaction.

Conclusion: INO-SGD provides a practical, IDP-compliant route to mitigate utility imbalance in personalized DP, with potential for broader evaluation and theoretical guarantees to be explored.

Abstract: Differential privacy (DP) is widely employed in machine learning to protect confidential or sensitive training data from being revealed. As data owners gain greater control over their data due to personal data ownership, they are more likely to set their own privacy requirements, necessitating individualized DP (IDP) to fulfil such requests. In particular, owners of data from more sensitive subsets, such as positive cases of stigmatized diseases, likely set stronger privacy requirements, as leakage of such data could incur more serious societal impact. However, existing IDP algorithms induce a critical utility imbalance problem: Data from owners with stronger privacy requirements may be severely underrepresented in the trained model, resulting in poorer performance on similar data from subsequent users during deployment. In this paper, we analyze this problem and propose the INO-SGD algorithm, which strategically down-weights data within each batch to improve performance on the more private data across all iterations. Notably, our algorithm is specially designed to satisfy IDP, while existing techniques addressing utility imbalance neither satisfy IDP nor can be easily adapted to do so. Lastly, we demonstrate the empirical feasibility of our approach.

</details>


### [347] [Efficient Verification of Neural Control Barrier Functions with Smooth Nonlinear Activations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07757)
*Jun Zhang,Haibo Zhang,Chun Liu,Xiaofan Wang,Liang Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LightCROWN tightens Jacobian bounds in CROWN-based verification for neural control barrier functions by exploiting activation function analyticity (e.g., tanh), improving verification success, speed, and scalability; demonstrated on inverted pendulum, Dubins car, and planar quadrotor; code released at github.com/Autonomous-Systems-and-Control-Lab/verify-neural-CBF.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CROWN-based methods use conservative linear relaxations for Jacobian bounds when verifying neural control barrier functions, which limits scalability for nonlinear activations like tanh.

Method: Introduce LightCROWN by leveraging the analytical properties of activation functions to compute tighter Jacobian bounds within the CROWN framework, leading to less conservative over-approximations.

Result: LightCROWN yields improved verification success rates (up to 100% in reported cases), along with faster verification times and better scalability on nonlinear control systems such as the inverted pendulum, Dubins car, and planar quadrotor.

Conclusion: The approach provides a generalizable improvement for CROWN-based verification of neural CBFs, enabling more efficient verification of complex NCBFs; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Formal verification of neural control barrier functions (NCBFs) remains challenging, especially for neural networks with nonlinear activations like \(\tanh\). Existing CROWN-based methods rely on conservative linear relaxations for Jacobian bounds, limiting scalability. We propose LightCROWN, which computes tighter Jacobian bounds by exploiting the analytical properties of activation functions. Experiments on nonlinear control systems including the inverted pendulum, Dubins car, and planar quadrotor demonstrate that LightCROWN improves verification success rates up to 100\%, while enhancing speed and scalability. Our approach provides a generalizable improvement for CROWN-based frameworks, enabling more efficient verification of complex NCBFs. The code can be found at github.com/Autonomous-Systems-and-Control-Lab/verify-neural-CBF.

</details>


### [348] [Pre-trained Tabular Foundation Models as Versatile Summary Networks for Neural Posterior Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07765)
*Elliot Pickens,Chiraag Gohel,Sidharth Satya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A training-free, modular SBI framework (PFN-NPE) uses a pretrained TabPFN encoder as a fixed summary network for simulator outputs, paired with a downstream inference head (default normalizing flows). It achieves competitive posterior estimation across settings, preserves marginal/location information, but may miss joint posterior structure; results advocate for TabPFN as a versatile SBI summary network with modular inference heads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage pretrained TabPFN, trained on synthetic tabular data, to produce informative summaries for simulation-based Bayesian inference without additional training, enabling a modular pipeline where the inference head can be problem-specific.

Method: Compute TabPFN-derived summaries from simulator outputs using a fixed encoder, then feed these summaries into a downstream inference head (typically a normalizing flow) to approximate the posterior. Evaluate against standard SBI methods and use diagnostic probes to assess what posterior information is captured by the summaries.

Result: PFN-NPE with TabPFN summaries matches or surpasses established posterior-approximation methods in many settings. Diagnostic probes show the summaries preserve posterior location and marginal information, though they may underrepresent joint posterior structure. The approach demonstrates robustness across diverse SBI scenarios while remaining modular in the inference component.

Conclusion: TabPFN can serve as an effective, training-free summary network for SBI across a range of tasks, enabling a modular inference pipeline, though attention should be given to potential limitations in capturing joint posterior dependencies.

Abstract: In this work, we study TabPFN as a training-free, modular summary network for simulation-based Bayesian inference (SBI). Tabular foundation models such as TabPFN are pretrained on broad families of synthetic tabular data-generating processes and adapt at test time through in-context learning, making them natural candidates for SBI, where posterior estimation often depends on learning informative summaries of simulated observations. We propose PFN-NPE: a general recipe that uses a pretrained TabPFN encoder as a fixed summary network for simulator outputs, then pairs the resulting summaries with a downstream inference head chosen for the problem. With normalizing flows as the default inference head, PFN-NPE matches established posterior approximation methods and sometimes outperforms them. More importantly, diagnostic probes show that the TabPFN-derived summaries often preserve useful posterior location and marginal information. These analyses also reveal a limitation in that TabPFN-derived summaries may struggle to represent the joint posterior structure even when the marginals are well recovered. Still, our experiments show that TabPFN can serve as an effective summary network across a diverse set of SBI settings, with the inference network left modular and task-dependent.

</details>


### [349] [Training-Induced Escape from Token Clustering in a Mean-Field Formulation of Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07772)
*Noboru Isobe,Daisuke Inoue,Masaaki Imaizumi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Training reshapes mean-field Transformer clustering: a phase where tokens cluster under attention, then diverge in final layers; analyzed via entropy-regularized energy, suggesting a training-aware mean-field theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend mean-field analyses of Transformer dynamics from fixed parameters to training dynamics, clarifying how optimization reshapes token distributions and inference behavior.

Method: Analyze a noisy mean-field Transformer where only a parameter-linear FFN is trained under L2 regularization. Derive an entropy-regularized interaction energy encoding attention-induced clustering and study a training-induced phase transition affecting final-layer dynamics.

Result: Identification of a training-induced phase: after initial attention-driven clustering, token distributions depart from the clustered regime in the final layers. Mathematical analysis elucidates the mechanism via entropy-regularized energy.

Conclusion: Proposes a training-aware mean-field theory of Transformer dynamics that treats training and inference together, highlighting how optimization reshapes clustering and final-layer behavior.

Abstract: Transformers perform inference by iteratively transforming token representations across layers. This layerwise computation has been studied empirically, and recent mean-field theories of Transformer dynamics explain how attention can drive token distributions toward clustering. However, existing mean-field analyses largely treat model parameters as prescribed, leaving open how training reshapes this clustering picture. We study this question in a noisy mean-field Transformer in which only a parameter-linear FFN is trained under $L^2$ regularization. We find and analyze a training-induced phase in the dynamics: after initially following attention-driven clustering, the token distribution can leave the clustered regime near the final layers. Our mathematical analysis is based on an entropy-regularized interaction energy that captures the clustering bias of attention. More broadly, our results point toward a training-aware mean-field theory of Transformer dynamics, in which training and inference dynamics are treated together.

</details>


### [350] [It Just Takes Two: Scaling Amortized Inference to Large Sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07972)
*Antoine Wehenkel,Michael Kagan,Lukas Heinrich,Chris Pollard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decouples representation learning from posterior modeling in neural posterior estimation using a mean-pool Deep Set encoder trained on small sets, enabling a posterior head to operate on aggregated embeddings for large deployment sets with training cost largely independent of set size N.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational and memory bottlenecks when conditioning on variable-sized observation sets in amortized posterior estimation, while preserving accuracy across diverse data regimes.

Method: Train a mean-pool Deep Set encoder on sets of up to two elements to learn robust set representations. Use pre-aggregated embeddings (mean-pooled) and finetune the inference/posterior head on these embeddings, making training cost largely independent of deployment set size N.

Result: Across objectives (scalar, image, multi-view 3D, molecular, high-dimensional generation) and deployment sizes N in the thousands, the approach matches or outperforms standard baselines while requiring substantially less compute.

Conclusion: Decoupling representation learning from posterior modeling enables scalable neural posterior estimation to large set sizes; the method is theoretically grounded by the Deep Sets framework and generalizes to diverse domains, but practical considerations include the cost of per-element encoding at inference and potential limits of small-set training to capture complex interactions in very large sets.

Abstract: Neural posterior estimation has emerged as a powerful tool for amortized inference, with growing adoption across scientific and applied domains. In many of these applications, the conditioning variable is a set of observations whose elements depend not only on the target but also on unknown factors shared across the set. Optimal inference therefore requires treating the set jointly, which in turn requires training the estimator at the deployment set size -- a regime where memory and compute quickly become prohibitive. We introduce a simple, theoretically grounded strategy that decouples representation learning from posterior modeling. Our method trains a mean-pool Deep Set on sets of size at most two, producing an encoder that generalizes to arbitrary set sizes. The inference head is then finetuned on pre-aggregated embeddings, making training cost essentially independent of the deployment set size N. Across scalar, image, multi-view 3D, molecular, and high-dimensional conditional generation benchmarks with N in the thousands, our approach matches or outperforms standard baselines at a fraction of the compute.

</details>


### [351] [Where's the Plan? Locating Latent Planning in Language Models with Lightweight Mechanistic Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07984)
*Nicole Ma,Nick Rui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Future-rhyme information in LM representations is linearly decodable and scales with model size, but causal influence is model-dependent: Gemma-3-27B shows a layer-30 handoff where the causal driver shifts from the rhyme word to the line boundary, while other models maintain rhyme-word dependence with negligible boundary causality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how structural constraints on future tokens form during forward passes in language models and whether they causally drive generation, using a clean test task (rhyming couplets) across multiple models and scales.

Method: Use rhyming-couplet completion as a probe task. Apply two lightweight techniques—linear probing and activation patching—across Qwen3, Gemma-3, and Llama-3 at >10 scales. Localize causal drivers with two-stage path patching to identify head groups.

Result: Future-rhyme information is linearly decodable at the line boundary in all models and scales, with signal strengthening with scale. Only Gemma-3-27B shows a clear causal handoff, migrating the causal driver from the rhyme word to the line boundary around layer 30. Other models rely on the rhyme word throughout generation, showing near-zero boundary causality despite strong decodable signal. Two-stage patching localizes the Gemma-3-27B handoff to five attention heads, recovering ~90% of rhyme-routing capacity at the newline.

Conclusion: There is heterogeneous planning-site formation across models: encoding of forward-looking constraints is widespread, but causal reliance is model-specific and can shift across layers. The approach successfully identifies where and how rhyme-based planning influences generation, revealing distinct mechanisms (word-level vs. boundary-level causality) and pinpointing the heads involved.

Abstract: We study planning site formation in language models -- where internal representations of structurally-constrained future tokens form during the forward pass, and whether they causally drive generation. Using rhyming-couplet completion as a clean test of forward-looking constraint, we apply two lightweight methods (linear probing and activation patching) across Qwen3, Gemma-3, and Llama-3 at more than ten scales. Probing shows that future-rhyme information is linearly decodable at the line boundary, with signal that strengthens with scale in all three families. Activation patching reveals that only Gemma-3-27B causally relies on this encoding, exhibiting a handoff in which the causal driver migrates from the rhyme word to the line boundary around layer 30. Every other model we test conditions on the rhyme word throughout generation, with near-zero causal effect at the line boundary despite strong probe signal. We localize the Gemma-3-27B handoff to five attention heads through two-stage path patching that recover ~90% of the rhyme-routing capacity at the newline.

</details>


### [352] [Graph-Structured Hyperdimensional Computing for Data-Efficient and Explainable Process-Structure-Property Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07999)
*Jingzhan Ge,Ajeeth Vellore,Ajinkya Palwe,Ahsan Khan,David Gorsich,Matthew P. Castanier,SeungYeon Kang,Farhad Imani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PSP-HDC: a graph-structured hyperdimensional computing framework for PSP prediction in multiphoton photoreduction, enabling intrinsic explanations and robust performance under data sparsity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data for multiphoton photoreduction PSP are sparse, heterogeneous, and interaction-dominated, rendering traditional feature-vector models underdetermined and baselines unreliable; availability of calibrated submodels is limited during early development. A principled PSP-aware method with interpretable explanations is needed.

Method: Encode a directed process-structure-property (PSP) graph as an internal prior using a trainable scalar-to-hypervector encoder to produce parameter-specific embeddings on a fixed hyperdimensional basis. Representations are built by graph-aligned binding and bundling along PSP dependencies, with predictions retrieved via associative memory against class prototypes. The same prototype memories enable both decision and attribution, yielding explanations at parameter, group, and within-group levels. Memory alignment and separation quantify prototype formation during training.

Result: Applied to sheet-resistance regime prediction for a 3D lithography platform: accuracy 0.910 ± 0.077 over 1000 random splits; 0.896 under process-fold generalization, outperforming strong baselines.

Conclusion: PSP-HDC provides accurate PSP predictions in sparse, heterogeneous data regimes while delivering intrinsic, multi-level explanations and improved generalization for multiphoton photoreduction–driven 3D microstructure fabrication.

Abstract: Multiphoton photoreduction enables high-fidelity fabrication of complex 3D microstructures, yet reliable process-structure-property (PSP) prediction remains difficult because the available data are sparse, heterogeneous, and interaction-dominated. In this regime, conventional feature-vector models are statistically underdetermined, making them prone to spurious correlations, poor regime transfer, and unstable post hoc explanations, whereas mechanistic pipelines depend on calibrated submodels that are rarely available during early process development. We present PSP-HDC, a graph-structured hyperdimensional computing framework that encodes a directed PSP graph as an internal prior for representation, inference, and explanation. A trainable scalar-to-hypervector encoder learns parameter-specific embeddings on a fixed hyperdimensional basis to accommodate heterogeneous scales and noise. Sample representations are then composed through graph-aligned binding and bundling along directed PSP dependencies, and prediction is performed by associative-memory retrieval against class prototypes. Because the same prototype memories support both decision making and attribution, PSP-HDC provides intrinsic explanations at the parameter, group, and within-group levels, while memory alignment and separation quantify prototype formation during training. On sheet-resistance regime prediction for the 3D platform, PSP-HDC achieves an accuracy of 0.910 +/- 0.077 over 1000 random splits and 0.896 under process-fold generalization, outperforming strong baselines.

</details>


### [353] [Position: Mechanistic Interpretability Must Disclose Identification Assumptions for Causal Claims](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08012)
*Zezheng Lin,Fengming Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Audit finds lack of explicit identification assumptions in mechanistic interpretability papers; causal claims often rest on validation metrics without stated assumptions; proposes a disclosure norm to separate validation from identification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Increase transparency and reliability of causal claims in mechanistic interpretability by making identification assumptions explicit and testable.

Method: Audited 10 papers across four methodological strands for presence of dedicated identification sections; assessed use of validation metrics (faithfulness, completeness, monosemanticity, alignment, ablations) as causal support without explicit assumptions; conducted a second audit with two coders on n=30 to verify reproducibility; examined Dim B/D counts for coding-rule sensitivity.

Result: No dedicated identification sections found; validation metrics are used as causal support without listing identifying assumptions; main finding direction reproduced by second coder; Dim B/D counts vary with coding rules; ambiguity remains about what constitutes identification.

Conclusion: Recommend a disclosure norm: declare when a claim is causal, specify the identification strategy, enumerate assumptions (at least one stressed), and explain how conclusions would shift if assumptions fail; emphasize that validation metrics are not identification.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability papers increasingly use causal vocabulary: circuits, mediators, causal abstraction, monosemanticity. Such claims require explicit identification assumptions. A purposive audit of 10 papers across four methodological strands finds no dedicated identification-assumptions section and a recurring pattern: validation metrics such as faithfulness, completeness, monosemanticity, alignment, or ablation effects are reported as causal support without stating the assumptions that make them identifying. A two-human-coder audit on $n=30$ reproduces the direction of the main finding: dedicated identification sections are absent, and validation-metric substitution is common, though exact Dim B/D counts are coding-rule sensitive. The paper proposes a disclosure norm: state whether the claim is causal, name the identification strategy, enumerate assumptions, stress at least one, and explain how conclusions shift if assumptions fail. Validation is not identification.

</details>


### [354] [Beyond Pairs: Your Language Model is Secretly Optimizing a Preference Graph](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08037)
*Ning Liu,Chuanneng Sun,Kristina Klinkner,Shervin Malmasi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphDPO generalizes Direct Preference Optimization by modeling rollout-induced preferences as directed acyclic graphs, preserving transitivity via a graph-based Plackett–Luce objective, with equivalence-class layers and zero intra-layer loss; maintains linear per-prompt complexity and supports optional ground-truth anchoring. Empirically, it improves reasoning and program-synthesis tasks over standard DPO/paired approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploit richer, multi-rollout preference structure that standard pairwise DPO discards, to leverage transitivity and reduce unstable learning from conflicting gradients.

Method: Construct a directed acyclic preference graph from rollout rankings; encode dominance relations as edges; optimize a graph-structured Plackett–Luce objective that aggregates supervision over graph neighborhoods; introduce equivalence-class layers for responses with identical preferences (intra-layer edges contribute zero loss); maintain linear complexity via efficient log-sum-exp computation; optionally insert verified solutions as dominant nodes (ground-truth anchoring) with an annealed schedule to gradually relax oracle supervision.

Result: Empirical evaluation on reasoning and program synthesis tasks shows superior performance compared to pairwise/listwise DPO, indicating graph-structured preference modeling is scalable and robust.

Conclusion: GraphDPO offers a principled generalization of DPO that enforces transitivity and leverages full preference structure while retaining efficiency; standard DPO is recovered as a special case; supports anchoring for stability and can improve downstream LM alignment tasks.

Abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) aligns language models using pairwise preference comparisons, offering a simple and effective alternative to Reinforcement Learning (RL) from human feedback. However, in many practical settings, training data consists of multiple rollouts per prompt, inducing rich preference structure that pairwise DPO fails to exploit. Collapsing such data into independent pairs discards transitivity, introduces redundant or conflicting supervision, and can lead to unstable optimization. We propose Graph Direct Preference Optimization (GraphDPO), a principled generalization of DPO that operates over directed acyclic preference graphs induced by rollout rankings. GraphDPO encodes dominance relations as edges and optimizes a graph-structured Plackett--Luce-inspired objective that aggregates supervision over graph neighborhoods, enforcing transitivity while recovering standard DPO as a special case. To handle discrete or sparse signals, we introduce an equivalence-class construction where responses with identical preferences form graph layers, and intra-layer edges contribute zero loss, preventing spurious gradients. Despite leveraging full graph structure, GraphDPO maintains linear per-prompt complexity via efficient log-sum-exp aggregation. We further incorporate optional ground-truth anchoring by inserting verified solutions as dominant nodes and applying an annealed schedule that stabilizes early training while gradually relaxing oracle supervision. Experiments on reasoning and program synthesis tasks demonstrate superior performance, suggesting that graph-structured preference modeling is a scalable and robust alternative to pairwise and listwise alignment objectives.

</details>


### [355] [Flexible Routing via Uncertainty Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07805)
*Charlotte Peale,Siddartha Devic,Parikshit Gopalan,Udi Wieder,Aravind Gollakota*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces an uncertainty-aware router for model routing that decomposes total uncertainty into irreducible and reducible components via higher-order predictors, enabling low-cost prediction, selective oracle routing, or abstention; offers regret guarantees and is adaptable to loss/cost settings without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To balance performance and cost in ML systems by efficiently routing queries between cheap models and expensive oracles, while reducing unnecessary oracle calls and supporting abstention.

Method: Decompose total uncertainty into irreducible and reducible components using higher-order predictors. Use this decomposition to decide: (i) predict with the weak model when uncertainty is low, (ii) route to the oracle when reducible uncertainty is high, (iii) abstain when irreducible uncertainty is high. The approach is parameterized to adapt to different loss functions and cost budgets without retraining and provides theoretical regret guarantees.

Result: Theoretical guarantees bounding regret relative to optimal task-specific routers. Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets shows benefits in regimes where reducible and irreducible uncertainty are not too correlated.

Conclusion: A flexible, uncertainty-aware router that unifies routing and abstention, offering cost-aware decision-making with provable performance guarantees, and practical effectiveness when reducible and irreducible uncertainties are moderately separable.

Abstract: A key strategy for balancing performance and cost in modern machine learning systems is to dynamically route queries to either a low-cost model or a more expensive oracle (such as a large pretrained model or human expert), an approach known as model routing. In this work we present a new uncertainty-aware router that (1) avoids unnecessary oracle calls on inherently ambiguous queries, and (2) adapts dynamically to different loss functions and cost parameters through simple hyperparameter changes, without retraining. Our method, applicable to any classification setting where multiple independent annotations per input are available, is based on decomposing total uncertainty into irreducible and reducible components using higher-order predictors [Ahdritz et al., 2025]. This enables a unified approach to both routing and abstention: predict with the weak model when uncertainty is low, route to the oracle when reducible uncertainty is high, and abstain when irreducible uncertainty is high. Our router comes with strong theoretical guarantees bounding regret relative to optimal task-specific routers. We conduct experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets that demonstrate the benefits of our approach in suitable regimes -- in particular, whenever reducible and irreducible uncertainty are not too correlated.

</details>


### [356] [The Minimax Rate of Second-Order Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07808)
*Kamil Ciosek,Banafsheh Rafiee,Sina Ghiassian,Nicolò Felicioni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Minimax rate for estimating second-order calibration error in binary classification is established; the sech kernel yields analytic calibration functions enabling a 1/√n rate (up to log factors) via polynomial regression, with matching lower bound; finite-sample guarantees for second-order Platt scaling; bucket-free calibration definition and relation to prior bucketed work; experiments validate the rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibrate epistemic-uncertainty estimates of higher-order predictors to match the conditional variance on level sets; provide finite-sample, minimax-optimal guarantees and practical recalibration procedures.

Method: Introduce sech perturbation kernel to enforce smoothness and analyticity of calibration functions; apply polynomial regression to estimate the second-order calibration error; derive upper and lower bounds showing 𝑂~(1/√n) rates; establish bucket-free definition and connect to bucketed formulations; derive finite-sample guarantee for second-order Platt scaling.

Result: Achieves a minimax-optimal 𝑂~(1/√n) rate for estimating the second-order calibration error; provides a matching Ω(1/√n) lower bound (up to log factors); yields finite-sample guarantees for second-order Platt scaling; offers a bucket-free definition of second-order calibration and relates it to Ahdritz et al. (2025); experiments corroborate the rate and recalibrated uncertainty quality.

Conclusion: The combination of analytic calibration functions via the sech kernel and polynomial regression yields minimax-optimal estimation of second-order calibration error, with practical implications for post-hoc recalibration (second-order Platt scaling) and a clarified, bucket-free formulation that aligns with prior bucketed approaches.

Abstract: We characterize the minimax rate of estimating the second-order calibration error for binary classification, which quantifies whether a higher-order predictor's epistemic-uncertainty estimate matches the conditional variance of the label probability on its level sets. Our key observation is that the sech perturbation kernel, previously used only to enforce smoothness of calibration functions, in fact makes them analytic in a strip of half-width $hπ/2$. Polynomial regression then estimates the calibration error at rate $\tilde{O}(1/\sqrt{n})$, with explicit constants, a qualitative improvement over the $O(n^{-1/4})$ rate achievable by bucketing or kernel smoothing. A matching $Ω(1/\sqrt{n})$ lower bound establishes minimax optimality up to logarithmic factors.
