As a corollary, we give the first finite-sample guarantee for second-order Platt scaling, yielding a post-hoc procedure that recalibrates both the mean prediction and the epistemic-variance estimate of any higher-order predictor. Along the way, we provide a bucket-free definition of second-order calibration and relate it quantitatively to the bucketed formulation of Ahdritz et al. [2025]. Our experiments confirm the predicted rate and the quality of the recalibrated uncertainties.

</details>


### [357] [OrScale: Orthogonalised Optimization with Layer-Wise Trust-Ratio Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07815)
*Yuxuan Lou,Yang You*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OrScale extends Muon with a layer-wise trust ratio based on the Frobenius norm of the actual parameter-space update, enabling per-layer update magnitude control; introduces OrScale-LM for language models; provides theoretical guarantees and empirical gains over Muon/AdamW, including CIFAR-10 and large-scale pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Muon's updates are orthogonalized but largely scaled by a global learning rate, limiting per-layer adaptation and stability; need a layer-adaptive mechanism that respects the true update direction in parameter space.

Method: Define the denominator as the Frobenius norm of the actual parameter-space update direction; apply to general matrix layers (OrScale) and combine Moonlight shape scaling with per-layer calibration for language models (OrScale-LM); analyze failures of Muon-LAMB hybrids due to denominator shape degeneracy, clip saturation, and weight-decay decoupling; show coupling weight decay fixes issues; provide O(1/sqrt(T)) convergence under nuclear-norm criterion; show layer-adaptive descent gains and initialization-preserving calibration.

Result: Empirically, OrScale ranks first on CIFAR-10 with DavidNet (93.70% -> 94.05% validation top-1); OrScale-LM improves FineWeb-Edu pretraining across three of four scales from 125M to 1.1B parameters and outperforms AdamW at every scale; theoretical results include nonconvex convergence guarantee and measurable gains.

Conclusion: OrScale provides stable, layer-adaptive updates that preserve muP-style learning-rate transfer, resolves Muon-LAMB issues, and offers both theoretical guarantees and practical performance gains on vision and language-model tasks.

Abstract: Muon improves neural-network training by orthogonalizing matrix-valued updates, but it leaves each layer's update magnitude controlled mostly by a global learning rate. We introduce OrScale, a trust-ratio extension of Muon built on a simple rule: the denominator of a layer-wise ratio should measure the Frobenius norm of the actual parameter-space direction that will be applied. This yields OrScale for general matrix layers and OrScale-LM for language models, where Moonlight shape scaling is combined with one-time per-layer calibration so every trust ratio starts at one. We analyze why three natural Muon-LAMB hybrids fail through shape-degenerate denominators, raw-momentum clip saturation, and decoupled weight-decay runaway, and show that the real-update-direction denominator with coupled weight decay avoids these failures. Theoretically, OrScale admits an O(1/sqrt(T)) nonconvex convergence guarantee in a nuclear-norm criterion, a strict layer-adaptive descent gain under measurable layer heterogeneity, and calibration properties that preserve muP-style learning-rate transfer at initialization. Empirically, OrScale ranks first on CIFAR-10/DavidNet across three seeds, improving Muon from 93.70% to 94.05% validation top-1, and OrScale-LM improves FineWeb-Edu pre-training versus Muon+Moonlight at three of four scales from 125M to 1.1B parameters while outperforming AdamW at every scale.

</details>


### [358] [Scaling Categorical Flow Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07820)
*Oscar Davis,Anastasiia Filippova,Pierre Ablin,Victor Turrisi,Amitis Shidani,Marco Cuturi,Louis Béthune*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Scales discrete diffusion for language modeling to a 1.7B base flow trained on 2.1T tokens, enabling 4-step CFM generation with high entropy and competitive LM scores; introduces a semi-discrete likelihood bound and training guidelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable accelerated sampling and flow-based advantages for discrete LM data at scale; evaluate scalability, scoring capability, and practical training considerations for CFMs.

Method: Train a 1.7B base flow on 2.1T tokens and self-distill it into a CFM; evaluate few-step generation (as few as 4 steps) with maintained token entropy; derive a semi-discrete likelihood bound for CFMs; use CFMs to score LM benchmarks; analyze training challenges and provide guidance on loss weighting and time scheduling.

Result: CFMs generate diverse, high-quality text in 4 steps with near-data-level token entropy; LM benchmarks achieve results in the same range as discrete diffusion methods; a likelihood bound for semi-discrete CFMs is established; practical insights on training at scale are provided.

Conclusion: CFMs scale to large LM settings, offering efficient few-step generation and competitive evaluation capability, though scaling introduces training challenges that require careful loss weighting and time scheduling. Further work is needed to refine training at scale.

Abstract: Continuous diffusion and flow matching models could represent a powerful alternative to autoregressive approaches for language modelling (LM), as they unlock a host of advantages currently reserved for continuous modalities, including accelerated sampling and tilting. Recently, several works have demonstrated the possibility of generating discrete data continuously by a simple flow matching process between a Gaussian and the one-hot encoded data distribution. They have further shown the feasibility of accelerated sampling via Categorical Flow Maps (CFMs), resulting in competitive sample quality in the few-step regime. However, this method had only been evaluated at relatively modest scales ($<1$B), leaving the question of its scalability completely open. In this article, we train a $1.7$B-parameter base flow model on $2.1$T tokens and self-distill it into a CFM that generates diverse, high-quality text in as few as $4$ inference steps while maintaining near-data-level token entropy. Furthermore, we introduce a likelihood bound for CFMs in the semi-discrete setting, and show that they can be used to score the model on standard LM benchmarks, achieving results in the same range as discrete diffusion methods. Finally, we uncover some of the challenges that arise from training these models at scale, and we provide prescriptive insights on loss weighting and time scheduling.

</details>


### [359] [RelAgent: LLM Agents as Data Scientists for Relational Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07840)
*Xingyue Huang,Louis Tichelman,Jinwoo Kim,Krzysztof Olejniczak,İsmail İlkan Ceylan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An LLM-driven two-phase system (search and inference) called RelAgent synthesizes SQL-based features and a classical model to produce fast, deterministic, and interpretable relational predictions deployable on standard databases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Relational learning spans graph-based, tabular, and sequence-based approaches, each with strengths and weaknesses. There is a need for interpretable, scalable pipelines that leverage LLMs for feature-program synthesis while ensuring fast, deterministic inference in familiar database environments.

Method: Two-phase architecture. In the search phase, an LLM agent interacts with database, validation, and evaluation workspace tools to construct SQL feature programs and select a predictive model. In the inference phase, the produced program is executed without further LLM calls. The final predictor combines SQL-derived features with a classical model, yielding human-readable features and a query-defined feature map for predictions.

Result: The predictor is fast, deterministic, and intrinsically interpretable, as features are defined by readable SQL queries and predictions depend only on the resulting feature map, enabling scalable deployment using standard database systems.

Conclusion: RelAgent demonstrates a viable path for interpretable, deployable relational learning by coupling LLM-driven feature-program synthesis with traditional modeling, highlighting the practicality of SQL-based feature maps for scalable DB-backed deployment.

Abstract: Relational learning is a challenging problem that has motivated a wide range of approaches, including graph-based models (e.g., graph neural networks, graph transformers), tabular methods (e.g., tabular foundation models), and sequence-based approaches (e.g., large language models), each with its own advantages and limitations. We propose RelAgent, an LLM-based autonomous data scientist for relational learning, which operates in two phases. In the search phase, an LLM agent uses database, validation, and evaluation workspace tools to construct SQL feature programs and select a predictive model. In the inference phase, the resulting program is executed without further LLM calls. The final predictor consists of SQL queries and a classical model, enabling fast, deterministic, and intrinsically interpretable predictions: features are human-readable queries, and predictions depend only on the resulting query-defined feature map, enabling scalable deployment using standard database systems.

</details>


### [360] [Distributional simplicity bias and effective convexity in Energy Based Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07844)
*Aurélien Decelle,Alfonso de Jesús Navas Gómez,Beatriz Seoane*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gradient-flow dynamics of energy-based learning with binary variables yield two fixed point classes: data-consistent points that reproduce the target distribution and spurious points that satisfy stationarity without matching it. Under gradient dynamics, lower-order interactions are learned first, creating a hierarchical progression and explaining the distributional simplicity bias and the lack of non-data-consistent low-order fixed points in practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand training dynamics of non-convex energy-based models by analyzing the effective model, offering a mechanistic account for observed biases and stability properties.

Method: Dynamical analysis of the gradient flow over positive distributions on binary variables, interpreting the energy as a generalized Ising model with higher-order interactions or its Fourier expansion; classification of fixed points and stability; demonstration of a hierarchical learning order.

Result: Identification of two fixed-point types (data-consistent and spurious); characterization of local stability: data-consistent points have stable or neutral perturbations, with neutral directions leaving the effective model invariant; gradient dynamics implement a learning hierarchy where lower-order interactions are learned before higher-order ones.

Conclusion: Provides a mechanistic explanation for the distributional simplicity bias and clarifies why certain non-data-consistent fixed points at low orders are not observed; offers insights into the dynamics and progression of energy-based learning.

Abstract: Energy-based learning is a powerful framework for generative modelling, but its training is inherently non-convex, leading potentially to sensitivity to initialisation, poor local optima, and unstable gradient dynamics. We present a dynamical analysis of energy-based learning through the lens of the effective model, which can be interpreted as either a generalised Ising model with higher-order interactions or the Fourier expansion of the energy. Under sufficient expressivity, we show that the gradient flow induced by learning strictly positive distributions over binary variables admits two types of fixed points: data-consistent points, which exactly reproduce the target distribution, and spurious points, which satisfy stationarity without matching the target distribution. Around data-consistent points, we show that perturbations are either stable or neutral, with neutral directions leaving the effective model invariant. Finally, we show that gradient dynamics induce a hierarchy in which lower-order interactions are learned before higher-order ones. This provides a mechanistic explanation for the distributional simplicity bias and clarifies why fixed points that are not data-consistent at low orders are not observed in practice.

</details>


### [361] [Actor-Critic Algorithm for Dynamic Expectile and CVaR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07857)
*Yudong Luo,Erick Delage*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Surrogate policy gradient without transition perturbation for dynamic risk optimization under softmax; model-free learning for dynamic risk measures (expectile, CVaR) via elicitability; model-free off-policy actor-critic inspired by Expected SARSA/Expected Policy Gradient; empirically risk-averse policies with strong performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic risk-averse RL faces two main challenges: updating policies often requires perturbing transitions, which is impractical, and value learning may depend on model-based components. There is a need for model-free methods that effectively handle dynamic risk measures.

Method: (1) Surrogate policy gradient under softmax without transition perturbation. (2) Model-free value-learning methods for dynamic risk measures (dynamic expectile and CVaR) leveraging elicitability. (3) A model-free off-policy actor-critic algorithm inspired by Expected SARSA and Expected Policy Gradient.

Result: Empirical results in domains with verifiable risk-averse behavior show the algorithm learns risk-averse policies and consistently outperforms existing methods.

Conclusion: Advances risk-averse RL via a perturbation-free surrogate gradient, model-free dynamic risk learning through elicitability, and a competitive off-policy actor-critic framework.

Abstract: Optimizing dynamic risk with stochastic policies is challenging in both policy updates and value learning. The former typically requires transition perturbation, while the latter may rely on model-based approaches. To address these challenges, we propose a surrogate policy gradient without transition perturbation under softmax policy parameterization. We further develop model-free value learning methods for dynamic expectile and conditional value-at-risk by leveraging elicitability. Finally, inspired by Expected SARSA and Expected Policy Gradient, a model-free off-policy actor-critic algorithm is constructed. Empirical results in domains with verifiable risk-averse behavior show that our algorithm can learn risk-averse policy and consistently outperforms other existing methods.

</details>


### [362] [ADKO: Agentic Decentralized Knowledge Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07863)
*Lucas Nerone Rillo,Zhanhong Jiang,Nastaran Saadati,Aditya Balu,Baskar Ganapathysubramanian,Chinmay Hegde,Soumik Sarkar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADKO enables privacy-preserving, cooperative black-box optimization among autonomous agents using private GP surrogates and knowledge tokens, with a theory-backed decomposition of regret and practical token-pruning, achieving sublinear regret under fidelity constraints and outperforming baselines in NAS and scientific discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve sample-efficient, privacy-preserving, and scalable multi-agent optimization for heterogeneous objectives and limited communication, where sharing raw data or models is undesirable or infeasible.

Method: Each agent maintains a private Gaussian Process surrogate trained on local data. Agents exchange compact knowledge tokens containing directional signals, advantage scores, and optional LM-derived insights; tokens are lossy, designed to preserve privacy. The framework integrates GP-UCB, parallel Bayesian optimization, decentralized learning, and LM-guided discovery. The authors analyze dual information loss: token compression via mutual-information fidelity and LM approximation error (bias + stochastic noise). They also propose fidelity-aware token pruning under a memory budget.

Result: Theoretical results show cumulative regret decomposes into GP error, LM bias, LM noise, and compression loss, with necessary and sufficient conditions for sublinear regret. Empirical validation on neural architecture search and scientific discovery demonstrates improved performance over strong baselines and confirms the theory.

Conclusion: ADKO provides a unified, information-theoretically grounded approach to private, decentralized BO with LM-guided insights, offering principled token-pruning and sublinear-regret guarantees under stated fidelity constraints, and is validated on realistic tasks.

Abstract: We present Agentic Decentralized Knowledge Optimization (ADKO), a framework for collaborative black-box optimization across autonomous agents that achieves sample efficiency, privacy preservation, heterogeneous-objective handling, and communication efficiency. Each agent maintains a private Gaussian Process (GP) surrogate trained on local data and communicates only through knowledge tokens-compact, lossy summaries containing directional signals, advantage scores, and optional language-model (LM) insights-without sharing raw data or model parameters. ADKO unifies GP-Upper Confidence Bound (GP-UCB), parallel Bayesian optimization, decentralized learning, and LM-guided discovery. We provide the first formal analysis of dual information loss: token compression, quantified via mutual-information-based fidelity, and LM approximation error, decomposed into bias and stochastic noise. Our main result shows cumulative regret decomposes into GP error, LM bias, LM noise, and compression loss, with necessary and sufficient conditions for sublinear regret. We also propose fidelity-aware token pruning to preserve high-information tokens under memory budget. Experiments on neural architecture search and scientific discovery validate the theory and show consistent improvements over strong baselines.

</details>


### [363] [Black-box model classification under the discriminative factorization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07878)
*Hayden Helm,Merrick Ohata,Carey Priebe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces discriminative factorization to assess query-set quality for auditing black-box generative models. The probability of chance-level classification decays exponentially with the query budget. On three auditing tasks, factorization parameters predict the empirical decay rate, and query sets selected via the estimated discriminative field reproduce the ordering of oracle query sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address sensitivity of low-dimensional model representations to the choice of query set in black-box model auditing and to identify a principled criterion for high- vs low-quality query sets.

Method: Develop a discriminative factorization framework; derive that chance-level classification probability decays exponentially with the number of queries; apply to three auditing tasks to estimate factorization parameters; compare against oracle query sets.

Result: Estimated factorization parameters successfully predict the empirical decay rate of classification performance; discriminative-field-guided query sets reproduce the oracle ordering of query sets.

Conclusion: Discriminative factorization provides a principled mechanism to gauge and optimize query-set quality for black-box model-level auditing, enabling better design of query strategies.

Abstract: Access to modern generative systems is often restricted to querying an API (the ``black-box" setting) and many properties of the system are unknown to the user at inference time. While recent work has shown that low-dimensional representations of models based on the relationship between their embedded responses to a set of queries are useful for inferring model-level properties, the quality of these representations is highly sensitive to the query set. We introduce the \emph{discriminative factorization} to distinguish between high- and low-quality query sets in the context of black-box model-level classification. Under this framework, the probability of chance-level classification decays exponentially in the query budget. On three auditing tasks, estimated factorization parameters predict the empirical performance decay rate. We conclude by showing that query sets selected using the estimated discriminative field reproduce the empirical ordering of oracle query sets.

</details>


### [364] [Adaptive Regularization for Sparsity Control in Bregman-Based Optimizers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07892)
*Ahmad Aloradi,Tim Roith,Emanuël A. P. Habets,Daniel Tenbrinck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An adaptive regularization scheme for sparse training with Bregman-based optimizers reduces hyperparameter sensitivity and reliably reaches user-specified sparsity (75–99%) while learning speaker-verification models, yielding faster early convergence and competitive or better final accuracy, plus improved out-of-distribution robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: L1/Bregman sparsity depends non-trivially on the regularization parameter λ; parameter sensitivity is high, particularly for LinBreg and AdaBreg, requiring expensive sweeps to hit a target sparsity.

Method: Introduce an adaptive λ update rule guided by the difference between current sparsity and target sparsity; analyze the algorithm and validate on ECAPA-TDNN and ResNet34 with VoxCeleb and CNCeleb.

Result: The approach consistently attains sparsity targets in the 75–99% range; it converges faster than an oracle-tuned non-adaptive baseline in early training and achieves equal-error-rate (EER) on par with or better than the baseline; retains improvements in out-of-distribution robustness compared to dense baselines.

Conclusion: Adaptive regularization retains the advantages of non-adaptive Bregman sparsity methods while reducing hyperparameter tuning burden, delivering reliable sparsity, faster convergence, and robust performance in speaker-recognition tasks; the method appears to generalize beyond the tested setups.

Abstract: Sparse training reduces the memory and computational costs of deep neural networks. However, sparse optimization methods, e.g., those adding an $\ell_1$ penalty, often control sparsity only indirectly through a regularization parameter $λ$, whose mapping to the final sparsity rate is non-trivial. In our experiments, we found this parameter sensitivity to be particularly pronounced for Bregman-based optimizers. Specifically, the two variants LinBreg and AdaBreg reach the same sparsity at $λ$ values that differ by up to two orders of magnitude, requiring expensive trial-and-error sweeps to achieve a user-specified sparsity. To address this, we propose an adaptive regularization scheme that updates $λ$ based on the difference between the model's current sparsity and the target sparsity. We analyze the resulting algorithm and evaluate it on automatic speaker verification with ECAPA-TDNN and ResNet34 on VoxCeleb and CNCeleb. The proposed method reliably achieves sparsity targets ranging between 75% and 99%. It also converges faster than the oracle-tuned non-adaptive baseline during early training and matches or surpasses its final performance in equal error rate. We further show that the adaptive scheme inherits key properties from its non-adaptive counterpart, including improved out-of-distribution robustness over the dense baselines.

</details>


### [365] [Curvature Beyond Positivity: Greedy Guarantees for Arbitrary Submodular Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07902)
*Yixin Chen,Alan Kuhnle*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends curvature to all submodular functions, enabling a greedy with pruning to achieve curvature-controlled guarantees even for negative/non-monotone objectives; supports a multilinear-extension variant for general constraints; validated by experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many practical submodular objectives include costs causing negativity and non-monotonicity. Existing guarantees require non-negativity and monotonicity; a unified curvature-based theory is needed to extend classical results.

Method: Generalizes curvature to all submodular functions; analyzes a pruning-enabled greedy algorithm to derive curvature-dependent multiplicative guarantees; extends via a multilinear extension to handle general combinatorial constraints; includes empirical curvature sweep.

Result: For non-monotone functions with curvature c_g in [1,2.2), the bound surpasses the best known uniform 0.401 ratio for non-negative functions and recovers the classic monotone non-negative guarantee (1−e^{−c_g})/c_g.

Conclusion: Introduces a unified curvature-based framework that covers monotone/non-monotone and non-negative/negative submodular objectives, yielding practical algorithms with provable guarantees and broad applicability, supported by experiments.

Abstract: Submodular functions -- functions exhibiting diminishing returns -- are central to machine learning. When the objective is monotone and non-negative, the greedy algorithm achieves a tight $63\%$ approximation. But many practical objectives incorporate costs that make them negative on some inputs, and all existing multiplicative guarantees require non-negativity. Prior work handles negativity through additive bounds for the special class of decomposable functions and non-monotonicity through partial-monotonicity parameters, but these address each difficulty in isolation and neither extends the classical structural theory. We extend \emph{curvature} -- a parameter measuring how far a function deviates from linearity -- to all submodular functions, handling both non-monotonicity and negativity through a single classical concept. A greedy algorithm with pruning achieves a curvature-controlled multiplicative ratio for \emph{any} submodular function, including those taking negative values -- the first such guarantee beyond monotonicity and non-negativity. In the non-monotone regime $1 \le c_g < 2.2$, the bound strictly beats the best known uniform ratio of $0.401$ (for non-negative $f$), and it recovers the classical $(1-e^{-c_g})/c_g$ guarantee for monotone functions. A multilinear-extension variant extends the framework to general combinatorial constraints via multilinear relaxation. Experiments on cost-penalized experimental design, coverage, feature selection, and a curvature sweep on Multi-News passage selection support the theory.

</details>


### [366] [Tree SAE: Learning Hierarchical Feature Structures in Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07922)
*Tue M. Cao,Hoang X. Nhat,Raed Alharbi,Phi Le Nguyen,My T. Thai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tree SAE combines activation and reconstruction constraints to learn hierarchical structures directly from within a feature set, outperforming traditional SAEs in identifying hierarchical pairs while keeping competitive benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Activation-coverage alone is insufficient for reliable parent–child hierarchy discovery; false positives occur when semantically unrelated concepts appear as parent-child due to activation patterns. A deeper functional link is needed to correctly infer hierarchies in sparse autoencoders.

Method: Introduce a reconstruction constraint alongside the existing activation constraint to enforce a deeper functional link between hierarchical levels. Train a Tree SAE to jointly optimize activation and reconstruction losses, enabling learning of hierarchical structures from the feature set itself.

Result: Tree SAEs significantly outperform existing SAEs at learning hierarchical pairs, while maintaining competitive performance to state-of-the-art on several key benchmarks.

Conclusion: Tree SAE enables reliable discovery and mapping of hierarchical concept structures, including the geometry of child feature subspaces, and can reveal complex hierarchies encoded within large language models, demonstrating practical utility in analyzing representation geometries.

Abstract: Learning hierarchical features in Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) is essential for capturing the structured nature of real-world data and mitigating issues like feature absorption or splitting. Existing works attempt to identify hierarchical relationships within independent feature sets by relying on activation coverage, the assumption that child feature should only activate when its parent feature activates. However, we demonstrate that this condition alone is insufficient; that is, it often produces false positives where parent and child concepts are semantically unrelated. To address this, we introduce a novel reconstruction condition that enforces a deeper functional link between hierarchical levels. By combining both activation and reconstruction constraints, we propose the Tree SAE, a model designed to learn hierarchical structures directly from within the feature set. Our results demonstrate that Tree SAEs significantly surpass the existing SAEs at learning hierarchical pairs while maintaining competitive performance to the state-of-the-art on several key benchmarks. Finally, we demonstrate the practical utility of our Tree SAE in mapping the geometry of child feature subspaces and uncovering the complex hierarchical concept structures encoded within large language models.

</details>


### [367] [Prototype Guided Post-pretraining for Single-Cell Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07938)
*Sachini Weerasekara,Natasha Darras,Sagar Kamarthi,Colles Price,Jacqueline Isaacs*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CellRefine is a post-pretraining approach for single-cell foundation models that uses marker-gene sets as structural priors to refine cell embeddings between pretraining and fine-tuning, addressing long-tailed cell-type distributions and covariate shifts; it yields up to 15% improvement on downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-cell representation learning faces severe class imbalance and covariate shifts; current pretrained models struggle to generalize during fine-tuning. There is a need for an intermediate stage that leverages domain priors to tighten the latent cell manifold.

Method: Introduce CellRefine, a post-pretraining framework that operates between pretraining and fine-tuning. It employs a multi-faceted objective incorporating marker-gene sets as structural priors to guide post-pretraining and refine the latent embedding space of cells.

Result: Empirical evaluations across multiple computational biology tasks show consistent downstream gains, with improvements up to 15%.

Conclusion: Post-pretraining with structural priors via CellRefine can enhance generalization and downstream performance of single-cell foundation models by refining the latent cell embeddings.

Abstract: Single-cell representation learning (SCRL) from gene expression data offers a way to uncover the complex regulatory logic underlying cellular function. Inspired by large language models in natural language modeling, several single-cell pretrained models have recently been proposed that treat genes as tokens and cells as sentences. However, these models are fundamentally limited by the long-tailed nature of cell-type distributions and struggle to generalize under covariate shifts in gene expression data. While fine-tuning is often used to mitigate these issues, we observe that performance remains bounded. To address this challenge, we introduce CellRefine, a post-pretraining method that operates between the pretraining and fine-tuning stages of a single-cell foundation model. CellRefine uses a multi-faceted objective that incorporates marker-gene sets as structural priors to guide post-pretraining and refine the latent embedding manifold of cells. Across multiple computational biology tasks, empirical results show that CellRefine consistently improves downstream performance, yielding gains up to 15%.

</details>


### [368] [Slowly Annealed Langevin Dynamics: Theory and Applications to Training-Free Guided Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07950)
*Atsushi Nitanda,Dake Bu,Yueming Lyu,Tanya Veeravalli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SALD introduces a slowly annealed Langevin dynamics sampler to track a path of moving target distributions with non-asymptotic convergence guarantees via a KL differential inequality. It advances to VA-SALD, integrating pretrained model marginals to enable training-free guided generation with convergence guarantees and bias correction for diffusion-based models; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of tracking time-varying target distributions and enabling training-free guidance in pretrained score-based models, while providing theoretical guarantees and understanding of the effects of intermediate inequalities and guidance bias.

Method: SALD slows down the annealing trajectory to enhance contraction and simplify the path of intermediate targets; VA-SALD augments this with explicit marginal-model information to correct guidance-induced deviations, analyzed through KL differential inequalities.

Result: Non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for SALD and VA-SALD; clearer roles of intermediate functional inequalities and guidance bias; a principled framework for training-free guided generation in diffusion-based models; public code release.

Conclusion: The slow annealing framework yields robust tracking and guided generation guarantees, combining contraction properties with bias correction to enable practical, training-free diffusion model guidance.

Abstract: We study Slowly Annealed Langevin Dynamics (SALD), a sampler for tracking a path of moving target distributions and approximating the terminal target through time slowdown. We establish non-asymptotic convergence guarantees via a KL differential inequality, showing that slowdown improves tracking through contraction of intermediate targets and the complexity of the path. Motivated by training-free guided generation with pretrained score-based generative models, we further introduce Velocity-Aware SALD (VA-SALD), which explicitly incorporates the underlying marginal distributions of the pretrained model and uses slowdown to correct the additional deviation induced by guidance. This yields a principled framework for training-free guided generation for diffusion-based and related generative model families, together with convergence guarantees that clarify the roles of intermediate functional inequalities and guidance bias. Code is available at https://github.com/anitan0925/sald.

</details>


### [369] [Convergent Stochastic Training of Attention and Understanding LoRA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07959)
*Zhengkai Sun,Dibyakanti Kumar,Alejandro F Frangi,Anirbit Mukherjee,Mingfei Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unifies a theoretical framework showing trainability of transformers with LoRA via Poincaré inequalities and Langevin-like dynamics, claiming data- and architecture-size-independent trainability for attention layers and LoRA on shallow nets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why stochastic training (SGD-like) reliably minimizes losses for attention/LoRA parameterizations without strong data or model-size assumptions.

Method: Prove that the empirical regression losses for an attention layer and LoRA-parameterized shallow nets induce Poincaré inequalities for the associated Gibbs measures; use this to deduce convergence properties of an SDE that mimics SGD under mild regularization.

Result: Establishes trainability in a unified framework for attention and LoRA cases, with results that do not rely on data distribution or network size assumptions.

Conclusion: Provides a foundational, data- and size-agnostic theoretical justification for trainability of attention-based models with LoRA; invites further extension to deeper architectures and data-dependent analyses.

Abstract: Transformers have revolutionized machine learning and deploying attention layers in the model is increasingly standard across a myriad of applications. Further, for large models, it is common to implement Low Rank Adaptation (LoRA), whereby a factorized parameterization of them is trained, to achieve a surprisingly beneficial accuracy-size trade-off. In this work, via a unified framework we rigorously establish trainability of such models under stochastic methods. We prove that for any mild regularization, the empirical regression loss on a attention layer and LoRA on a shallow neural net, both induce Poincaré inequality for the corresponding Gibbs' measure. Then it follows via invoking recent results that a certain SDE, which mimics the SGD, minimizes the corresponding losses. In both the cases, our first-of-its-kind results of trainability on attention and nets, do not rely on any assumptions on the data or the size of the architecture.

</details>


### [370] [Graph Representation Learning Augmented Model Manipulation on Federated Fine-Tuning of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07961)
*Hanlin Cai,Kai Li,Houtianfu Wang,Haofan Dong,Yichen Li,Falko Dressler,Ozgur B. Akan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AugMP is a novel attack on federated fine-tuning (FFT) of LLMs that uses graph-based learning to model benign update correlations and an augmented Lagrangian optimization to craft stealthy malicious updates. It degrades global and local model performance while preserving benign-like characteristics to evade defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address vulnerabilities in FFT where adversaries can submit manipulated updates that corrupt aggregation and degrade the global LLM, highlighting the need for stealthy, efficient manipulation techniques.

Method: Build a graph representation learning framework to capture feature correlations among benign updates; develop an iterative manipulation algorithm using an augmented Lagrangian dual formulation to optimize malicious updates that achieve adversarial objectives while preserving benign-like parameters.

Result: AugMP achieves strongest manipulation among baselines, reducing global LLM accuracy by up to 26% and local agents' average accuracy by up to 22%; maintains statistical and geometric consistency with benign updates, evading conventional distance- and similarity-based defenses.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a potent, stealthy manipulation threat in FFT-based LLMs, underscoring the need for robust aggregation defenses and detection methods that can identify distribution-preserving adversarial updates.

Abstract: Federated fine-tuning (FFT) has emerged as a privacy-preserving paradigm for collaboratively adapting large language models (LLMs). Built upon federated learning, FFT enables distributed agents to jointly refine a shared pretrained LLM by aggregating local LLM updates without sharing local raw data. However, FFT-based LLMs remain vulnerable to model manipulation threats, in which adversarial participants upload manipulated LLM updates that corrupt the aggregation process and degrade the performance of the global LLM. In this paper, we propose an Augmented Model maniPulation (AugMP) strategy against FFT-based LLMs. Specifically, we design a novel graph representation learning framework that captures feature correlations among benign LLM updates to guide the generation of malicious updates. To enhance manipulation effectiveness and stealthiness, we develop an iterative manipulation algorithm based on an augmented Lagrangian dual formulation. Through this formulation, malicious updates are optimized to embed adversarial objectives while preserving benign-like parameter characteristics. Experimental results across multiple LLM backbones demonstrate that the AugMP strategy achieves the strongest manipulation performance among all competing baselines, reducing the global LLM accuracy by up to 26% and degrading the average accuracy of local LLM agents by up to 22%. Meanwhile, AugMP maintains high statistical and geometric consistency with benign updates, enabling it to evade conventional distance- and similarity-based defense methods.

</details>


### [371] [FLAM: Evaluating Model Performance with Aggregatable Measures in Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07962)
*Fabian Stricker,Jose A. Peregrina,David Bermbach,Christian Zirpins*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FLAM: a federated learning performance evaluation method using aggregatable measures that matches centralized evaluation without a global test set.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In FL, data are distributed so global metrics cannot be computed directly; standard aggregation based on local samples can diverge from centralized results; existing definitions focus on accuracy and fail to generalize to other metrics, causing inconsistencies that misguide deployment.

Method: Introduce FLAM (Aggregatable Performance Measures). The method defines aggregatable metrics that align with centralized evaluation; does not require a global test dataset; provides theoretical justification and construction for broad metrics beyond accuracy.

Result: The approach yields the same results as centralized evaluation for various metrics while preserving privacy; enables consistent performance assessment across participants.

Conclusion: FLAM resolves misalignment between local- and centralized evaluation in FL, generalizes beyond accuracy, and supports reliable deployment decisions without global test data.

Abstract: Performance evaluation is essential for assessing the quality of machine learning (ML) models and guiding deployment decisions. In federated learning (FL), assessing the performance is challenging because data are distributed across participants. Consequently, the coordinator must rely on locally computed evaluation metrics and aggregate them to assess the global model. A key challenge is that common aggregation strategies, such as weighted averaging based on the local samples per participant, do not always produce the same results as centralized evaluation. Existing definitions of performance evaluation are largely tailored to accuracy and do not generalize to other metrics, leading to inconsistencies between participant-based and centralized evaluation. However, such discrepancies are inconsistent with the FL objective and lead to a wrong calculation of the metric.
  To address this issue, we examine the underlying reasons for these discrepancies and propose FLAM, a performance evaluation method based on aggregatable measures that yields the same results as centralized evaluation without the need for a global test dataset.

</details>


### [372] [Aggregation in conformal e-classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07963)
*Vladimir Vovk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-conformal e-predictors are studied experimentally, with proposed simpler and more flexible modifications to the aggregation approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To balance predictive performance and computational efficiency in conformal prediction by leveraging the ease of aggregating e-predictors; investigate cross-conformal e-prediction and introduce simpler, more flexible variants.

Method: Experimental evaluation of cross-conformal e-prediction and its proposed modifications; analysis of aggregation behavior, validity, and practical flexibility.

Result: Not specified in the abstract.

Conclusion: Not specified in the abstract.

Abstract: Aggregating conformal predictors is a standard way of balancing their predictive and computational efficiency while retaining their validity, at least approximately. An important advantage of conformal e-predictors is that they are easier to aggregate without sacrificing their validity. This paper studies experimentally cross-conformal e-prediction, which is an existing method of aggregating conformal e-predictors, and its modifications that are conceptually simpler and more flexible.

</details>


### [373] [When Diffusion Model Can Ignore Dimension: An Entropy-Based Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07969)
*Ahmad Aghapour,Erhan Bayraktar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Discretization error for diffusion samplers with Gaussian mixtures is governed by latent entropy rather than ambient dimension; step complexity scales linearly with latent entropy and logarithmically with data second moment; extends to discrete targets; supports efficiency in high dimensions under compact latent representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain why diffusion samplers remain efficient in high dimensions despite theoretical bounds often scaling with ambient dimension; provide an information-theoretic perspective tied to latent structure.

Method: Information-theoretic analysis of discretization error for diffusion samplers. Derives bounds for Gaussian mixtures showing the error is controlled by the Shannon entropy of the latent mixture component. Converts to a step complexity bound linear in latent entropy; extends arguments to discrete targets.

Result: Discretization error is bounded by the latent entropy; leading step complexity scales linearly with latent entropy and depends only logarithmically on the second moment of the data. The framework extends to discrete target distributions where entropy governs complexity.

Conclusion: Diffusion sampling can remain efficient in high dimensions if the data distribution admits a compact latent representation; the paper provides a unified information-theoretic explanation for observed practical efficiency and suggests focusing on latent structure in sampler design.

Abstract: Diffusion models perform remarkably well on high-dimensional data such as images, often using only a modest number of reverse-time steps. Despite this practical success, existing convergence theory does not fully explain why such samplers remain efficient in high dimensions. Many prior KL guarantees bound the discretization error in terms of the ambient dimension, while other improved results replace this dependence using intrinsic-dimensional or geometric structure assumptions. In this work, we develop an alternative information-theoretic perspective on diffusion sampler convergence. We prove that, for Gaussian mixture targets, the discretization error is controlled by the Shannon entropy of the latent mixture component rather than by the ambient dimension. Consequently, the leading step complexity scales linearly with latent entropy and depends only logarithmically on the second moment of the data. Our analysis also extends to discrete target distributions, where the relevant complexity is the entropy of the target rather than the dimension of the embedding space. These results suggest that diffusion sampling can remain efficient in high-dimensional spaces when the data distribution admits a compact latent representation, as is widely believed to be the case for natural images.

</details>


### [374] [Self-Play Enhancement via Advantage-Weighted Refinement in Online Federated LLM Fine-Tuning with Real-Time Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07977)
*Seohyun Lee,Wenzhi Fang,Dong-Jun Han,Seyyedali Hosseinalipour,Christopher G. Brinton*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPEAR is an online federated fine-tuning method for LLMs that uses feedback-guided self-play to train with standard MLE on correct completions and confidence-weighted unlikelihood on tail tokens of incorrect completions, avoiding ground-truth contexts and expensive data generation. It is resource-efficient and suitable for edge-device FL, achieving superior performance over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of offline, ground-truth-dependent, and non-federated feedback-based learning. The work targets integrating external user feedback across large networks via federated learning, while maintaining efficiency on resource-constrained edge devices.

Method: SPEAR builds a feedback-guided self-play loop per prompt to generate naturally contrastive pairs. Training uses (i) standard maximum likelihood for correct completions and (ii) confidence-weighted unlikelihood on tail tokens of incorrect completions. It requires only partial, non-answer feedback, eliminating the need for expensive group generations or ground-truth contexts. The method supports online training in a federated setup with resource efficiency; code is publicly available.

Result: Validated on multiple benchmark datasets with superior performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines; demonstrates online federated fine-tuning efficiency and effectiveness; implementation available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: SPEAR offers an effective, online, federated approach for refining LLMs through feedback-guided self-play, achieving improved task performance while reducing data-generation and communication burdens on edge devices.

Abstract: Recent works have advanced feedback-based learning systems, whereby a foundation model is able to intake incoming feedback (e.g., a user) to self-improve, creating a self-loop system of training. However, existing works are limited in needing to consider an offline setup to allow for such feedback-based methods, and are further limited in the need of requiring privileged ground-truth contexts for training. Moreover, there is limited consideration of federated learning (FL), which is particularly well-suited for incorporating external feedback across large networks of end users, for example, but requires methods to be efficient for training on resource-constrained edge devices. Therefore, we introduce SPEAR (Self-Play Enhancement via Advantage-Weighted Refinement), an efficient online learning algorithm for federated LLM fine-tuning. SPEAR utilizes a feedback-guided self-play loop to construct naturally contrastive pairs per prompt which are utilized to be trained on (i) standard maximum likelihood on correct completions and (ii) confidence-weighted unlikelihood on tail tokens of incorrect completions. Without the need of expensive group generations and ground-truth contexts for training (i.e., only partial, non-answer feedback), in contrast with existing works, SPEAR can be trained both online and in a resource-efficient manner. We validate SPEAR across various benchmark datasets, demonstrating its superior performance in comparison to state-of-the-art baselines. The implementation code is publicly available at https://github.com/lee3296/SPEAR.

</details>


### [375] [Susceptibilities and Patterning: A Primer on Linear Response in Bayesian Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07980)
*Chris Elliott,Daniel Murfet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a susceptibility framework for neural networks, linking data perturbations to posterior expectations and covariances. Distinguishes two observables: per-sample losses yield the influence matrix; component-local observables yield the structural susceptibility matrix. The susceptibility matrix (up to nβ) is the Jacobian from data distributions to structural coordinates; its pseudo-inverse solves a linearized patterning problem. Grounded in statistical mechanics, with empirical estimators and links to loss-landscape geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a principled, physics-inspired way to quantify how data perturbations affect neural networks, via derivatives of posterior expectations that equal covariances (fluctuation–dissipation). Connect influence measures with structural decompositions and data-driven patterning tasks.

Method: Define the susceptibility of an observable φ to a data perturbation as the derivative of a posterior expectation; use the fluctuation–dissipation theorem to equate this to a posterior covariance. Specialize to: (i) per-sample losses yielding the influence matrix; (ii) component-local observables yielding the structural susceptibility matrix. Show that the susceptibility matrix is (up to a factor nβ) the Jacobian of the map from data distributions to structural coordinates. Propose the pseudo-inverse as a linearized solver for producing desired structural changes (patterning problem). Discuss estimation and relation to loss-landscape geometry.

Result: Formalizes susceptibility as a unifying object; identifies two concrete matrices (influence and structural susceptibility); proves (up to nβ) that the susceptibility matrix equals the Jacobian of the data-to-structure map; provides a linearized solution method for data perturbations via its pseudo-inverse; links to empirical estimators and loss-landscape geometry.

Conclusion: The framework connects data perturbations, posterior statistics, and neural-network structure, enabling interpretable descriptors (influence and structural susceptibility) and actionable data design through the pseudo-inverse of the susceptibility matrix, grounded in statistical-mechanical principles.

Abstract: These notes introduce the theory of susceptibilities as developed in [arXiv:2504.18274, arXiv:2601.12703] for interpreting neural networks. The susceptibility of an observable $φ$ to a data perturbation is defined as a derivative of a posterior expectation, which by the fluctuation--dissipation theorem equals a posterior covariance. Different choices of $φ$ yield different objects: per-sample losses give the influence matrix (the Bayesian influence function of [arXiv:2509.26544]), while component-localized observables give the structural susceptibility matrix that pairs model components with data patterns. The susceptibility matrix is (up to a factor of $nβ$) the Jacobian of the map from data distributions to structural coordinates; its pseudo-inverse provides a linearized solution to the patterning problem of [arXiv:2601.13548]: finding data perturbations that produce a desired structural change. We motivate the theory from its statistical-mechanical foundations, then give a detailed exposition of susceptibilities, their empirical estimators, and their connection to the geometry of the loss landscape.

</details>


### [376] [Bayesian Sensitivity of Causal Inference Estimators under Evidence-Based Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07993)
*Nikita Dhawan,Daniel Shen,Leonardo Cotta,Chris J. Maddison*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Bayesian Sensitivity Value (BSV) as an alternative to worst-case sensitivity analyses for causal inference, extending the s-value framework to three common causal assumptions, with Monte Carlo estimation and real-world priors; shows worst-case can be implausible, and demonstrates BSV in an observational diabetes-treatment weight-loss study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the over-pessimism and potential uninformative nature of worst-case sensitivity analyses by incorporating priors derived from real-world evidence, yielding more informative robustness assessments.

Method: Generalize the s-value framework (Gupta & Rothenhäusler, 2023) to three standard causal assumptions. Introduce Bayesian Sensitivity Value (BSV) as the expected sensitivity of an estimate to assumption violations under priors built from empirical evidence. Estimate BSV via Monte Carlo sampling and apply to observational data on diabetes treatments and weight loss.

Result: BSV provides an expectation of sensitivity under plausible priors, avoiding overemphasis on extreme, unrealistic data-generating changes that drive some worst-case analyses. The approach is demonstrated empirically in a study of diabetes treatments affecting weight loss.

Conclusion: BSV offers a more informative, Bayesian-oriented sensitivity analysis by integrating real-world priors, potentially applicable across various causal inference tasks; highlights limitations of worst-case criteria and encourages prior-informed robustness assessments.

Abstract: Causal inference, especially in observational studies, relies on untestable assumptions about the true data-generating process. Sensitivity analysis helps us determine how robust our conclusions are when we alter these underlying assumptions. Existing frameworks for sensitivity analysis are concerned with worst-case changes in assumptions. In this work, we argue that using such pessimistic criteria can often become uninformative or lead to conclusions contradicting our prior knowledge about the world. To demonstrate this claim, we generalize the recent s-value framework (Gupta & Rothenhäusler, 2023) to estimate the sensitivity of three different common assumptions in causal inference. Empirically, we find that, indeed, worst-case conclusions about sensitivity can rely on unrealistic changes in the data-generating process. To overcome this, we extend the s-value framework with a new sensitivity analysis criterion: Bayesian Sensitivity Value (BSV), which computes the expected sensitivity of an estimate to assumption violations under priors constructed from real-world evidence. We use Monte Carlo approximations to estimate this quantity and illustrate its applicability in an observational study on the effect of diabetes treatments on weight loss.

</details>


### [377] [Interpreting Reinforcement Learning Agents with Susceptibilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08007)
*Chris Elliott,Einar Urdshals,David Quarel,Daniel Murfet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Susceptibilities extend neural-network interpretability to deep RL by analyzing how perturbations to the loss affect posterior expectations of observables in regret; applied to a simple gridworld to reveal internal, stagewise parameter-space development not evident from policy alone, validated with activation-steering; suggested extension to RLHF post-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish an interpretable diagnostic for RL training dynamics by linking loss perturbations to observable expectations, capturing internal parameter-space development beyond policy performance.

Method: Generalize the notion of susceptibilities to the regret setting in deep RL, apply to a toy gridworld with nontrivial stagewise development, validate via activation-steering, and discuss how the framework could extend to RLHF post-training.

Result: Susceptibilities uncover internal features of the model's development in parameter space that are not detectable from policy dynamics alone; the gridworld exhibits nontrivial stagewise behavior, and activation-steering corroborates the interpretability signal.

Conclusion: The susceptibility framework can be extended to RLHF post-training; it provides a richer diagnostic of RL training dynamics beyond policy trajectories, potentially informing architecture and training strategy choices.

Abstract: Susceptibilities are a technique for neural network interpretability that studies the response of posterior expectation values of observables to perturbations of the loss. We generalize this construction to the setting of the regret in deep reinforcement learning and investigate the utility of susceptibilities in a simple gridworld model that nevertheless exhibits non-trivial stagewise development. We argue that susceptibilities reveal internal features of the development of the model in parameter space that one cannot detect purely by studying the development of the learned policy. We validate these results with activation-steering, and discuss the framework's extension to RLHF post-training.

</details>


### [378] [Adaptive Domain Decomposition Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Traffic State Estimation with Sparse Sensor Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08028)
*Eunhan Ka,Ludovic Leclercq,Satish V. Ukkusuri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive Domain Decomposition PINN (ADD-PINN) uses residual-guided spatial partitioning for LWR-based offline traffic-field reconstruction, achieving lower error and faster training than XPINN, with ablations and NGSIM controls showing when decomposition is advantageous.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address PINN over-smoothing of shockwaves under sparse fixed sensors in LWR traffic models, enabling accurate offline speed-field reconstruction while maintaining computational efficiency.

Method: Two-stage framework: 1) train a coarse global PINN; 2) derive spatial residuals to place subdomain boundaries and initialize child subnetworks in a decomposition-enabled mode; incorporate a data-driven shock indicator to fall back to a single-domain model when transition evidence is weak; evaluate across large offline MOTION dataset and supplementary NGSIM experiments as controls.

Result: ADD-PINN achieves the lowest relative L2 error in 18/25 configurations and 14/15 sparse-sensing cases; training ~2.4x faster than XPINN; ablation supports spatial-only decomposition; NGSIM controls show shock indicator suppresses decomposition and single-domain fallsback rank first in all runs.

Conclusion: Residual-guided spatial decomposition is an effective PINN-family design for offline traffic reconstruction with sparse sensing, enabling localized handling of transitions; the shock indicator provides a robust fallback mechanism, suggesting adaptive decomposition should be considered in similar PDE-constrained learning tasks.

Abstract: Traffic state estimation from sparse fixed sensors is challenging because physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) tend to over-smooth the shockwaves admitted by the Lighthill-Whitham-Richards (LWR) model. This study proposes Adaptive Domain Decomposition Physics-Informed Neural Networks (ADD-PINN), a two-stage residual-guided framework for LWR-based offline speed-field reconstruction. A coarse global PINN is first trained; its spatial residual profile is then used to place subdomain boundaries and initialize child subnetworks in a decomposition-enabled mode, while a data-driven shock indicator can retain a single-domain fallback when localized evidence of transition is weak. The primary offline I-24 MOTION evaluation spans five days, five sensor configurations, and ten seeds per configuration, yielding 1,500 runs in total. Against neural and physics-informed baselines, ADD-PINN attains the lowest relative L2 error in 18 of 25 configurations and in 14 of 15 sparse-sensing cases, while training 2.4 times faster than the extended PINN (XPINN) baseline. An ablation study supports spatial-only decomposition as an effective default for fixed-sensor traffic reconstruction in the evaluated settings. Supplementary Next Generation Simulation (NGSIM) experiments serve as a negative control: the shock indicator suppresses decomposition in all 50 runs, and the default single-domain fallback ranks first across all sensor configurations. These results support residual-guided spatial decomposition as an effective PINN-family design for offline reconstruction when sparse fixed sensing coincides with localized transition regions.

</details>


### [379] [Don't Get Your Kroneckers in a Twist: Gaussian Processes on High-Dimensional Incomplete Grids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08036)
*Mads Greisen Højlund,August Smart Lykke-Møller,Henry Moss,Ove Christiansen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CUTS-GPR delivers numerically exact Gaussian process regression in high dimensions with an additive kernel on an incomplete grid, achieving near-linear scaling in N and low-order polynomial scaling in D, enabling Bayesian modeling of high-dimensional potential energy surfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable numerically exact Gaussian process regression in high-dimensional settings, particularly for modeling high-dimensional potential energy surfaces (PES) in computational chemistry, where standard GPR scales poorly.

Method: Introduce an additive kernel with an incomplete grid to reveal a structured kernel matrix. Develop an extremely fast kernel matrix–vector product that scales nearly linearly (or linearly) with N and with low-order polynomial scaling in D by exploiting the grid-induced structure.

Result: Demonstrate scalability with benchmarks on billions of data points and thousands of dimensions. Full GPR with hyperparameter optimization completed in hours for N=447,265 and D=24.

Conclusion: CUTS-GPR enables Bayesian modeling of high-dimensional PES, addressing a longstanding challenge in computational chemistry.

Abstract: We introduce CUTS-GPR, a new method for performing numerically exact Gaussian process regression (GPR) in high-dimensional settings. The key component of CUTS-GPR is an extremely fast kernel matrix-vector product, which exhibits near-linear or even linear scaling with the amount of training data, $N$, and low-order polynomial scaling with dimensionality, $D$. This is obtained by combining an additive kernel with an incomplete grid and exploiting the resulting structure of the kernel matrix. We demonstrate the scalability of the matrix-vector product by running benchmarks with billions of data points and thousands of dimensions. Full GPR calculations, including hyperparameter optimization, are completed in a matter of hours for $N = 447 265$ and $D = 24$. We demonstrate that our CUTS-GPR enables Bayesian modeling of high-dimensional potential energy surfaces - a longstanding challenge in computational chemistry.

</details>


### [380] [Reinforcement Learning for Exponential Utility: Algorithms and Convergence in Discounted MDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08053)
*Gugan Thoppe,L. A. Prashanth,Ankur Naskar,Sanjay Bhat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Presents two Q-value extensions for exponential-utility RL in discounted MDPs, proves contraction properties and fixed points, and develops two convergent model-free algorithms (two-timescale Q-learning and a one-timescale sublinear operator) with detailed convergence analyses, establishing a theoretical foundation for value-based RL under exponential utilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of principled value-based reinforcement learning algorithms for exponential-utility optimization in discounted Markov decision processes under fixed risk aversion.

Method: Builds on the Bellman-type equation for exponential utility (Porteus 1975), derives two Q-value style extensions, proves contraction in the L∞ and sup-log/Thompson metrics, characterizes fixed points, and shows that the induced greedy stationary policy is optimal among stationary policies. Proposes two model-free algorithms: (i) a two-timescale Q-learning–style method with almost-sure convergence and finite-time rates via timescale separation; (ii) a one-timescale method governed by a sublinear power-law operator, with convergence proven using local Lipschitzness, monotonicity, homogeneity, and Dini derivatives and a scalar finite-time analysis.

Result: Structural contraction properties, identified fixed points, and optimality of the greedy stationary policy for the exponential-utility objective; two convergent model-free algorithms with provable convergence (almost-sure for the two-timescale method) and a sublinear-operator-based method with specialized convergence analysis.

Conclusion: Provides a foundational framework for value-based reinforcement learning under exponential-utility objectives, enabling principled, convergent, model-free RL algorithms that account for risk aversion.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for exponential-utility optimization in discounted Markov decision processes (MDPs) lacks principled value-based algorithms. We address this gap in the fixed risk-aversion setting. Building on the Bellman-type equation for exponential utility studied in \cite{porteus1975optimality}, we derive two Q-value-style extensions and show that the associated operators are contractions in the $L_\infty$ and sup-log/Thompson metrics, respectively. We characterize their fixed points and prove that the induced greedy stationary policy is optimal for the exponential-utility objective among stationary policies. These structural results lead to two model-free algorithms: a two-timescale Q-learning--style algorithm, for which we establish almost-sure convergence and provide finite-time convergence rates via timescale separation, and a one-timescale algorithm governed by a sublinear power-law operator. Since the latter does not admit a global contraction in standard metrics, we prove its convergence using delicate arguments based on local Lipschitzness, monotonicity, homogeneity, and Dini derivatives, and provide a scalar finite-time analysis that highlights the challenges in obtaining convergence rates in the vector case. Our work provides a foundation for value-based RL under exponential-utility objectives.

</details>


### [381] [GRAPHLCP: Structure-Aware Localized Conformal Prediction on Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08074)
*Peyman Baghershahi,Fangxin Wang,Debmalya Mandal,Sourav Medya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRAPHLCP: a proximity-based localized conformal prediction framework for graphs that explicitly uses graph topology via a feature-aware densification step and a Personalized PageRank kernel to model structural proximity, enabling topology-aware anchor sampling and calibration weighting with finite-sample marginal guarantees and improved test conditional coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph data exhibit complex, non-iid dependencies that are not well captured by embedding-based locality. Standard conformal prediction relies on embedding proximity and can yield imprecise or overly conservative prediction sets on graphs. There is a need for localization that directly incorporates graph structure and inter-node dependencies while maintaining finite-sample coverage guarantees.

Method: 1) Feature-aware densification to alleviate locality bias in sparse graphs; 2) compute a Personalized PageRank-based kernel to capture structural proximity; 3) topology-dependent anchor sampling and calibration weighting that reflect local and long-range dependencies; 4) apply local conformal prediction with these topology-aware components to obtain calibrated prediction sets.

Result: Empirical evaluation on multiple regression and classification datasets shows GRAPHLCP preserves marginal coverage with finite samples and achieves favorable test conditional coverage under various conditioning scenarios, outperforming baseline CP methods that rely on embedding-space proximity.

Conclusion: GRAPHLCP successfully integrates graph topology into localized conformal prediction, providing finite-sample guarantees and improved conditional calibration for graph-structured tasks while remaining computationally viable through densification and PPR-based proximity modeling.

Abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) provides a distribution-free approach to uncertainty quantification with finite-sample guarantees. However, applying CP to graph neural networks (GNNs) remains challenging as the combinatorial nature of graphs often leads to insufficiently certain predictions and indiscriminative embeddings. Existing methods primarily rely on embedding-space proximity for localization, which can be unreliable for graphs and yield inefficient prediction sets. We propose GRAPHLCP, a proximity-based localized CP framework that explicitly incorporates graph topology and inter-node dependencies into localization and weighting. Our approach introduces a feature-aware densification step to mitigate locality bias in sparse graphs, followed by a Personalized PageRank-based kernel computation to model structural proximity. This enables topology-dependent anchor sampling and calibration weighting that captures both local and long-range dependencies. Extensive experiments on several regression and classification datasets demonstrate that GRAPHLCP guarantees marginal coverage with finite samples while efficiently attaining favorable test conditional coverage across various conditioning scenarios.

</details>


### [382] [Zero-Shot Imagined Speech Decoding via Imagined-to-Listened MEG Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08075)
*Maryam Maghsoudi,Shihab Shamma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A three-stage pipeline decodes imagined speech by leveraging paired listened MEG data, mapping imagined to listened responses, then using a contrastive decoder trained on listened data to infer imagined words; held-out subject decoding is above chance and improves with more training data, indicating scalability for BCI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imagined speech decoding is challenging due to scarce, poorly aligned datasets. The authors exploit richer, well-labeled listened-speech MEG data and trained musicians to improve temporal alignment across conditions, enabling cross-condition mapping between imagined and listened neural responses.

Method: Six linear and neural models map imagined MEG to listened MEG. A contrastive word decoder is trained on listened MEG responses using four embedding strategies (semantic, acoustic, phonetic, plus one more). Inference for imagined trials proceeds by mapping imagined MEG to listening space and decoding with the listened decoder. Evaluations use a null baseline on unseen subjects and rank-based analysis.

Result: Imagined words are decodable significantly above chance in held-out subjects. Performance improves with training data size, suggesting scalability to realistic BCI scenarios.

Conclusion: The approach establishes a cross-condition decoding framework linking imagined to listened neural representations, with a scalable pipeline that could enable practical imagined-speech BCIs.

Abstract: Decoding imagined speech from non-invasive brain recordings is challenging because imagined datasets are scarce and difficult to align temporally across subjects and sessions In this work, we propose a new approach to the decoding of imagined speech that leverages the richer and more reliably labeled recordings during listening to speech. We collected paired listened and imagined MEG recordings to rhythmic melodic and spoken stimuli from trained musicians. Using trained musicians helped improve temporal alignment across conditions. We then developed a three-stage decoding pipeline that revealed consistent and meaningful relationships between neural activity evoked by imagining and listening to the same stimuli. First, we trained six linear and neural models to map imagined MEG responses to listened responses. We evaluated these models against a null baseline from unseen subjects to validate that the predicted-listening responses preserve stimulus-specific information. In the second stage, we trained a contrastive word decoder exclusively on the listened MEG responses, and evaluated it using four embedding strategies including semantic, acoustic, and phonetic representations. In the third stage, we process the imagined MEG responses from held-out subjects through the mapping pipeline to compute the corresponding listening responses that are then decoded by the listened decoder. Using rank-based analysis, we show that the imagined words are decodable significantly above chance. We shall report here the results of a proof-of-concept implementation to decode imagined speech, where all evaluations are performed on held-out subjects. We also demonstrate that performance improves with training data size, suggesting that this approach is scalable and can directly be made applicable to realistic brain-computer interface scenarios.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [383] [Modular Lie Algebraic PDE Control of Multibody Flexible Manipulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06709)
*Sadeq Yaqubi,Jouni Mattila*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A modular, PDE-based control framework for flexible serial robots using se(3) screw theory, achieving global exponential convergence with adaptive parameters and inter-link cancellation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable stable, scalable control of flexible multibody robotic systems in 3D by accounting for elastic deformations within a unified screw-theoretic model.

Method: Screw-theoretic Lie algebra model (se(3)); replace strain PDE to eliminate distributed elastic acceleration; twist trajectories from deflection-compensating inverse kinematics; nominal per-link controllers with Lyapunov guarantees; adaptive parameter estimates; composite Lyapunov functions V = sum(nu_i) and V^a = sum(nu_i^a) yielding inter-link power cancelation via Newton's third law and frame invariance; Euler-Bernoulli energy bound for deformation; modular stability proof; simulations.

Result: Exponential convergence of twist and parameter errors (global); exact inter-link interaction cancellation; bounded elastic deformation; numerical simulations validate physical consistency.

Conclusion: Provides modular, scalable stability certificates for chains of arbitrary length, enabling real-time control of flexible manipulators with provable stability.

Abstract: This paper addresses PDE-based control for flexible multibody robotic systems, presenting a subsystem-based framework for serial manipulators with arbitrary links in 3D space. The approach uses a screw-theoretic Lie-algebraic model where motion, deformation, and forces are expressed as body-fixed twists and wrenches in se(3). By substituting a strain-based deformation PDE into the dynamics, distributed elastic acceleration is eliminated, yielding a model governed by twist acceleration and the deformation field. Subsystem twist trajectories are generated from task-space endpoints via deflection-compensating inverse kinematics, providing real-time correction for tip deformation. A nominal controller for each link ensures exponential decay of twist errors via a Lyapunov function nu_i. An adaptive modification replaces physical parameters with online estimates, establishing exponential convergence of both twist and parameter errors. Summing over all links, composite Lyapunov functions V = sum(nu_i) and V^a = sum(nu_i^a) yield time derivatives where inter-link interaction power terms telescope to zero. This cancellation is ensured by Newton's third law and the frame invariance of the power pairing on se(3) x se*(3), establishing global exponential convergence of tracking errors. Bounded elastic deformation is guaranteed by an Euler-Bernoulli energy argument. The screw-theoretic structure renders interaction cancellation exact, making the stability certificate modular and scalable to chains of arbitrary length. Numerical simulations demonstrate the scheme's physical consistency.

</details>


### [384] [An Aerial Manipulator for Perception-Driven Flower Targeting Toward Contactless Pollination in Vertical Farming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06759)
*Chenzhe Jin,Zhuohang Wu,Yifan Cai,Xiangqi Li,Jan Ming Kevin Tan,Narsimlu Kemsaram,Valerio Modugno*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Aerial manipulator with perception-guided flower detection and precise end-effector positioning for contactless pollination in vertical farming; integrates onboard RGB-D perception, MPPI-based UAV control on PX4, and a lightweight 2-DoF manipulator; validated in MuJoCo and real UAV lab with centimeter-level accuracy; establishes groundwork for future pollen manipulation modules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Declining natural pollinators threaten crop production in indoor vertical farming; need non-contact, accurate flower targeting and positioning to enable pollination without damaging delicate flowers.
