Method: Perception-driven detection/localization using onboard RGB-D; MPPI-based UAV control on PX4; lightweight 2-DoF end-effector; evaluation in MuJoCo simulation and UAV lab flower-targeting testbed.

Result: Achieves stable UAV flight, reliable flower localization, and centimeter-level end-effector positioning accuracy; consistent trajectory convergence and target alignment in simulation; stable positioning and alignment under aerial constraints in real-world lab tests.

Conclusion: The platform serves as a robust carrier and positioning framework for future contactless pollination; provides a practical foundation for integrating advanced contactless end effectors (e.g., acoustic pollen manipulation) in future work.

Abstract: The decline of natural pollinators has created a major challenge for crop production in controlled indoor agriculture, particularly in vertical farming environments where natural insect pollination is absent. This motivates the development of robotic systems capable of performing precise flower targeting tasks while minimizing physical interference with delicate floral structures. This paper presents an aerial manipulator platform for perception driven flower detection, localization, and approach in vertical farming environments. The proposed system integrates onboard RGBD based perception, model predictive path integral (MPPI) based unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) control on a PX4 platform, and a lightweight 2DoF manipulator for precise end effector positioning. The platform is evaluated in both MuJoCo simulation and UAV lab experiments using a flower targeting testbed. The experimental results demonstrate stable UAV flight, reliable flower localization, and centimeter level end effector positioning accuracy. In simulation, the proposed controller achieves consistent trajectory convergence and accurate target alignment. In the real world UAV lab environment, the integrated perception control manipulation framework enables stable flower targeted positioning and end effector alignment under constrained aerial operation. These results validate the proposed aerial manipulator as a robust robotic carrier and positioning framework for future contactless pollination systems. While the current study focuses on perception guided targeting and positioning, the developed platform provides a practical foundation for integrating advanced contactless end effectors, including acoustic based pollen manipulation modules, in future work.

</details>


### [385] [Bi3: A Biplatform, Bicultural, Biperson Dataset for Social Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06863)
*Andrew Stratton,Phani Teja Singamaneni,Pranav Goyal,Rachid Alami,Christoforos Mavrogiannis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Bi3 is a new social robot navigation dataset collected in a constrained lab with 74 participants across USA and France, featuring five navigation algorithms, two robot platforms, and multimodal data (10.5 hours of ground-truth motion, RGB video, and user impressions). It serves as a benchmark for modeling human motion and robot control in dense, constrained environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for diverse, crowded-environment datasets to advance human-robot interaction (HRI) research, particularly for social navigation where close human-robot encounters occur. Bi3 aims to fill gaps in experimental realism, platform variety, participant diversity, and multimodal capture to enable robust modeling and evaluation.

Method: Original experimental design in a constrained lab creating close encounters between humans and a robot. Data collection includes 10.5 hours of ground-truth motion data, RGB video, and subjective impressions. Five navigation algorithms and two robot platforms. Participant pool of 74 people from two sites in the USA and France. Multimodal data streams allow analysis via metrics like interaction density and human velocity.

Result: Bi3 provides a diverse, modeling-complex dataset that can benchmark social navigation in crowded spaces. The dataset supports training of human motion prediction models and robot navigation policies, and highlights diverse interaction patterns across algorithms, platforms, and participants.

Conclusion: Bi3 contributes to understanding productive human-robot co-navigation in constrained environments and serves as a resource for advancing navigation policies and motion modeling in densely populated settings.

Abstract: We contribute Bi3, a dataset of social robot navigation among groups of people in a constrained lab space. Compared to prior data collection efforts for social robot navigation, our dataset is unique in that it features: an original experiment design giving rise to close navigation encounters between two humans and a robot; five different navigation algorithms; two different robot platforms; a diverse participant pool of 74 people recruited from two sites in the USA and France; multimodal data streams including 10.5 hours of human and robot ground-truth motion tracks, RGB video, and user impressions over robot performance. Our analysis of the collected dataset through metrics like interaction density and human velocity suggests that Bi3 represents a benchmark of unique diversity and modeling complexity. Bi3 contributes towards understanding how humans and robots can productively mesh their activities in constrained environments, and can be a resource for training models of human motion prediction and robot control policies for navigation in densely crowded spaces.

</details>


### [386] [Traffic Scenario Orchestration from Language via Constraint Satisfaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06966)
*Frieda Rong,Chris Zhang,Kelvin Wong,Raquel Urtasun*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Language-in, simulation-out approach using foundation-model reasoning to convert natural-language scenario descriptions into constraint-based representations and solve for actor behaviors in closed-loop AV testing; outperforms baselines, especially for ego-reactive scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need scalable, controllable, precise AV scenario testing; manual scripting is precise but not scalable; statistical models struggle with precise controllability for out-of-distribution cases.

Method: Translate natural-language descriptions into a constraint-based scenario representation via foundation-model reasoning; use off-the-shelf solvers to generate actor behaviors that satisfy testing constraints; test in closed-loop to ensure ego-reactivity; evaluate on a diverse benchmark.

Result: Significant improvement in orchestration success rate over baselines; closed-loop ego-reactive scenarios are particularly improved and important.

Conclusion: The framework enables precise, scalable testing scenario generation for AVs, with emphasis on ego-reactive specifications; demonstrates the viability of combining LLM reasoning with constraint solvers in simulation-based testing.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) require extensive testing in simulation, but test case generation for driving scenarios is laborious. The desired scenarios are often out-of-distribution and have precise requirements on interactions with the AV policy under test. Manually programming scenarios allows for precise controllability but is difficult to scale. On the other hand, statistical models can leverage compute and data, but struggle with precise controllability when out-of-distribution. We cast scenario orchestration as a constraint-solving problem and present a language-in, simulation-out scenario orchestrator for closed-loop testing AVs. Our approach leverages foundation model reasoning to translate general, natural language descriptions into a set of constraints as a scenario representation. This then allows us to leverage off the shelf solvers to solve for actor behaviors which meet precise testing intentions in closed-loop. Under a benchmark of carefully crafted and diverse scenario descriptions, our approach greatly outperforms our baselines in orchestration success rate. We further show that our closed-loop approach is especially important for scenarios which require ego-reactive specifications.

</details>


### [387] [AirBender: Adaptive Transportation of Bendable Objects Using Dual UAVs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07003)
*Jiawei Xu,Longsen Gao,Rafael Fierro,David Saldaña*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An adaptive, model-free cooperative controller for two quadrotors carrying a bendable object, with Lyapunov-based asymptotic stability, validated on hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manipulating deformable objects in midair is difficult due to uncertain elasticity and limited actuation; existing controllers rely on explicit models, which are often unavailable or inaccurate. A robust framework is needed for stable, high-performance trajectory tracking during collaborative manipulation by aerial robots without exact elasticity models.

Method: Develop an adaptive controller that estimates and compensates for unknown deformable properties in real time, enabling two aerial vehicles to jointly follow a trajectory while transporting a bendable object. The approach uses Lyapunov analysis to prove asymptotic stability and does not require an explicit elasticity model. Hardware experiments validate the method under various scenarios.

Result: Demonstrates successful, stable trajectory tracking in hardware experiments when transporting bendable objects with two quadrotors, indicating robustness to unknown deformable properties and practical feasibility of the approach.

Conclusion: The proposed adaptive controller enables stable, model-free cooperative manipulation of bendable objects by aerial robots, validating its effectiveness for midair transport tasks and suggesting its applicability to more complex deformable payloads.

Abstract: The interaction of robots with bendable objects in midair presents significant challenges in control, often resulting in performance degradation and potential crashes, especially for aerial robots due to their limited actuation capabilities and constant need to remain airborne. This paper presents an adaptive controller that enables two aerial vehicles to collaboratively follow a trajectory while transporting a bendable object without relying on explicit elasticity models. Our method allows on-the-fly adaptation to the object's unknown deformable properties, ensuring stability and performance in trajectory-tracking tasks. We use Lyapunov analysis to demonstrate that our adaptive controller is asymptotically stable. Our method is evaluated through hardware experiments in various scenarios, demonstrating the capabilities of using multirotor aerial vehicles to handle bendable objects.

</details>


### [388] [Intention assimilation control for accurate tracking with variable impedance in teleoperation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07037)
*Atsushi Takagi,Yanan Li,Hiroaki Gomi,Etienne Burdet*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: IAC estimates the leader's target position instead of its current position to guide the follower, enabling accurate tracking with adjustable impedance and without high stiffness; validated on two 7-DOF robots, outperforming TIC in several tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Solve the trade-off between tracking accuracy and safety in teleoperation by decoupling tracking from stiffness and enabling user-intent-driven impedance adaptation.

Method: Develop an intention assimilation control (IAC) scheme that predicts the leader's target position and transmits it to the follower. The follower's impedance can be modulated on-the-fly to reflect user intention or task requirements. Validated on two 7-DOF manipulators with four experiments (free tracking, balloon, peg insertion, table polishing with force feedback).

Result: IAC achieves higher tracking accuracy, improved task completion rate, and reduced completion time compared to tele-impedance control (TIC) across the experiments.

Conclusion: IAC provides accurate follower positioning while allowing real-time impedance modulation, enhancing both unilateral and bilateral teleoperation without resorting to high stiffness.

Abstract: Robot systems for teleoperation commonly use a spring-like force pulling the follower robot towards the leader's position to track their movements. With this control strategy, the tracking accuracy deteriorates when the follower' stiffness is low, but high stiffness poses a danger to objects or people in the follower robot's environment. To address this trade-off between tracking accuracy and safety, we propose an alternative intention assimilation control (IAC) strategy where the robot's tracking accuracy can be ensured without high stiffness. Different from traditional approaches, which transmit the leader's current position to the follower, this new controller estimates the leader's target position and transmits it to the follower. With this strategy, the follower impedance can be changed on-the-fly to continuously reflect the user's desired impedance or modulated automatically to fulfill the task requirements. Our controller was validated on two 7 degree-of-freedom manipulators, yielding high tracking accuracy with varying impedance. Four experiments were conducted to compare {teleoperation} with IAC to tele-impedance control (TIC) during free tracking, interaction with a balloon, during peg insertion, and table polishing with force feedback. The results show that IAC increases tracking accuracy, improves task completion rate and reduces completion time. IAC enables the robot to accurately replicate the user's movement while giving them freedom to modulate the impedance according to their intention, providing an unprecedented level of control of the follower's position and its impedance during unilateral and bilateral teleoperation.

</details>


### [389] [Dr-BA: Separable Optimization for Direct Radar Bundle Adjustment & Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07041)
*Daniil Lisus,Cedric Le Gentil,Timothy D. Barfoot*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Dr-BA is a radar bundle adjustment pipeline operating on 2D spinning radar intensity images that jointly optimizes dense maps and poses in a separable formulation; it enables direct radar-only localization and achieves state-of-the-art performance with public code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: All-weather robustness of radar makes it a critical modality for autonomous systems; existing methods rely on sparse point clouds and separate estimation; a dense, end-to-end BA on radar would improve accuracy and cross-session localization.

Method: Formulates BA as a separable optimization decoupling pose estimation and mapping, using full radar returns from multiple scans to jointly estimate dense maps and poses; extends to direct radar-only localization within a pre-built map;

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art radar BA and cross-session localization on over 200 km of data across five routes; code released.

Conclusion: Dr-BA provides an efficient, general solution for radar BA and DRL with competitive accuracy and all-weather applicability; facilitates robust navigation in GPS-denied environments; code available.

Abstract: This paper introduces Dr-BA, a first-of-its-kind radar bundle adjustment (BA) framework that operates directly on 2D spinning radar intensity images. Unlike camera or lidar sensors, radar is largely unaffected by precipitation, making it a critical modality for autonomous systems that require all-weather robustness. Existing state estimation approaches using spinning radar typically extract sparse point clouds from range-azimuth-intensity measurements and apply point cloud alignment techniques to estimate vehicle motion, scene structure, or to localize within an existing map. In contrast, Dr-BA uses the full radar returns from multiple scans to jointly estimate dense maps and sensor poses. By formulating the problem as a separable optimization, we derive an efficient and general solution that decouples pose estimation from mapping. In addition to solving the BA problem, this formulation naturally extends to direct radar-only localization (DRL) within a previously built map. Dr-BA achieves state-of-the-art radar-based BA and cross-session localization performance, demonstrated on more than 200 km of on-road data across five distinct routes. Our implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/utiasASRL/dr_ba.

</details>


### [390] [PISTO: Proximal Inference for Stochastic Trajectory Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07215)
*Hongzhe Yu,Zinuo Chang,Yongxin Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PISTO: a proximal, VI-inspired stochastic trajectory optimization that stabilizes STOMP updates via KL-regularized Gaussian proposals and computes mean updates via importance-weighted MC; shows improved success and efficiency on robotic arm and MuJoCo tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve stability and performance of stochastic trajectory optimization in non-differentiable/discontinuous cost settings; reveal and exploit a variational inference structure in STOMP.

Method: Demonstrate that STOMP minimizes KL divergence to a Boltzmann trajectory distribution; introduce PISTO with KL regularization between successive Gaussian proposals (proximal objective with trust region); derive closed-form mean updates as expectations under a surrogate distribution; estimate these expectations with importance-weighted Monte Carlo sampling; maintain a derivative-free algorithm.

Result: PISTO achieves ~89% success on robot arm motion planning, outperforming CHOMP (63%) and STOMP (68%), with shorter, smoother paths and ~2x speed relative to other stochastic methods; also outperforms CEM and MPPI baselines on contact-rich MuJoCo tasks in reward.

Conclusion: A proximal VI formulation yields stable, derivative-free trajectory optimization that preserves STOMP’s non-differentiable cost handling while improving convergence, speed, and task performance across manipulation and locomotion tasks.

Abstract: Stochastic trajectory optimization methods like STOMP enable planning with non-differentiable costs, offering substantial flexibility over gradient-based approaches. We show that STOMP implicitly minimizes the KL divergence from a Boltzmann trajectory distribution, revealing an elegant Variational Inference (VI) structure underlying its updates. Building on this insight, we propose the \textit{Proximal Inference for Stochastic Trajectory Optimization} (PISTO) algorithm that stabilizes the updates by augmenting the objective with a KL regularization between successive Gaussian proposals. This proximal formulation admits a trust-region interpretation and yields closed-form mean updates computable as expectations under a surrogate distribution. We estimate these expectations via importance-weighted Monte Carlo sampling, producing a simple, derivative-free algorithm that inherits STOMP's ability to handle non-differentiable and discontinuous costs without modification. On robot arm motion planning benchmarks, PISTO achieves an 89\% success rate -- outperforming CHOMP (63\%) and STOMP (68\%) -- while producing shorter, smoother paths at twice the speed of competing stochastic methods. We further validate PISTO on contact-rich MuJoCo locomotion and manipulation tasks, where it consistently outperforms both CEM and MPPI baselines in reward.

</details>


### [391] [Palm-sized Omnidirectional Vision-Based UAV Exploration with Sparse Topological Map Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07275)
*Zirui Wang,Xinjia Luo,Haotian Sun,Jun Ma,Jian Guo,Boyu Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Lightweight exploration for micro-UAVs using omnidirectional vision and a sparse topological map to avoid dense occupancy maps; frontiers modeled as topological nodes; global planning on a sparse graph; validated on a palm-sized UAV with low resource use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SWaP constraints on micro-UAVs make dense occupancy grids, LiDAR, and high-resolution point clouds impractical. There is a need for real-time, memory- and compute-efficient exploration with reliable frontier detection under limited depth accuracy.

Method: Use a multi-fisheye camera setup for omnidirectional FoV and depth estimation. Represent frontier regions as topological nodes rather than explicit boundaries. Maintain a sparse topological map consisting of key nodes and descriptors. Perform global path planning directly on the sparse graph, avoiding occupancy grids and global point clouds.

Result: Demonstrates efficient exploration with very low computational consumption in both simulation and real-world tests on a palm-sized UAV (11 cm wheelbase, 400 g).

Conclusion: A sparse topological map with omnidirectional vision enables memory- and computation-efficient exploration for SWaP-constrained micro-UAVs. Frontier handling via topological nodes reduces depth estimation demands, while planning on a sparse graph maintains navigational capability. Promising for resource-limited aerial robotics, with potential trade-offs in boundary precision and reliance on robust node descriptors.

Abstract: Classic exploration methods often rely on dense occupancy maps or high-resolution point clouds for frontier detection and path planning, resulting in substantial memory consumption and computational overhead. Moreover, micro UAVs under size, weight, and power (SWaP) constraints are not practical to be equipped with sensors like LiDAR to obtain accurate environmental geometric measurements. This paper presents a lightweight autonomous exploration system that leverages omnidirectional vision and sparse topological map guidance. Specifically, we utilize a multi-fisheye camera setup to achieve omnidirectional Field of View (FoV) and perform depth estimation. To address the limited depth estimation accuracy, frontiers are represented as potential unexplored regions characterized by topological nodes instead of explicit boundaries, enabling efficient identification of frontier regions without maintaining occupancy grids or global point clouds. Unlike classic dense representations, our approach abstracts the environment using a sparse topological map composed of key nodes and their descriptors, reducing memory consumption and computational demands. Global path planning is performed directly on the sparse graph. The proposed method is validated in both simulation and on a palm-sized vision-based UAV with an 11 cm wheelbase and a 400 g weight in real-world experiments, demonstrating that our method can achieve efficient exploration with extremely low computational consumption.

</details>


### [392] [Variable Aerodynamic Damping via Co-Contraction: A Dynamic Isomorphism with Variable Stiffness Actuators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07292)
*Antonio Franchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A redundant dual-rotor actuator uses aerodynamic co-contraction to adjust passive aero-mechanical damping while keeping net thrust constant, via a Variable Aerodynamic Damping Actuator (VADA). It shows damping increases along constant-force fibers under mild aerodynamic hardening, validated by Blade Element Theory, and is dynamically isomorphic to stiffness modulation in variable-stiffness actuation. The fiber-density principle also improves active aerodynamic promptness and clarifies impedance control through common-mode/differential-mode actuation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for tunable passive damping and impedance control in redundant multirotors while preserving commanded net force; explore muscle-like co-activation and speed–inflow coupling to enhance responsiveness and safety.

Method: Derive from Blade Element Theory to obtain a minimal, inflow-affine thrust model; define incremental damping as the local thrust–velocity sensitivity at trim; prove monotonic increase of this damping along constant-force fibers under a mild aerodynamic hardening condition; formalize as Variable Aerodynamic Damping Actuator (VADA); establish dynamic isomorphism to variable-stiffness actuation (VSA); provide impedance-form representation to separate common-mode and differential-mode actuations.

Result: Incremental damping increases monotonically along constant-force fibers under aerodynamic hardening; validation via first-principles BEM-based thrust model; VADA enables tunable passive damping with constant net thrust; speed–inflow coupling identified as the mechanism; impedance analysis clarifies mode roles and equilibrium velocity.

Conclusion: VADA offers a principled mechanism to tailor passive impedance in redundant rotors, leveraging aerodynamic co-contraction and fiber-density concepts, and aligns with VSA in tendon-like co-activation while improving active aerodynamic response and clarifying control of equilibrium dynamics.

Abstract: We prove that aerodynamic co-contraction in a redundant dual-rotor actuator can tune a passive, trim-defined aero-mechanical damping while keeping the commanded net force constant. In particular, we define an incremental damping coefficient as the local sensitivity of net thrust to air-relative velocity at a trim and prove that it increases monotonically along constant-force fibers under a mild aerodynamic hardening condition. We then validate the required damping and hardening properties from a first-principles Blade Element Theory derivation, which yields a minimal thrust model affine in inflow and explicitly reveals the speed--inflow coupling driving the effect. The resulting mechanism is formalized as a Variable Aerodynamic Damping Actuator (VADA) and shown to be dynamically isomorphic to stiffness modulation in antagonistic variable-stiffness actuation (VSA), similar to the co-contraction of tendons by muscle co-activation. The same fiber-density principle also enhances the active aerodynamic promptness measure of redundant multirotors. Finally, an impedance-form representation clarifies the roles of common-mode and differential-mode actuation in the control of passive impedance and the equilibrium velocity of the VADA system.

</details>


### [393] [BioProVLA-Agent: An Affordable, Protocol-Driven, Vision-Enhanced VLA-Enabled Embodied Multi-Agent System with Closed-Loop-Capable Reasoning for Biological Laboratory Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07306)
*Zhaohui Du,Zhe Wang,Hongmei Fei,Xiwen Cao,Ting Xiao,Qi Wang,Huanbo Jin,Jiaming Gu,Quan Lu,Zhe Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BioProVLA-Agent is a protocol-driven, vision-enhanced embodied multi-agent system for biological manipulation, combining protocol parsing, visual state verification, and embodied execution via Vision-Language-Action models; AugSmolVLA enhances robustness to visual perturbations. Evaluated on hierarchical tasks, it outperforms baselines in stability, especially with transparent objects and degraded visuals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce repetitive manual wet-lab work, improve reproducibility, and enable reliable embodied execution in unstructured wet-lab environments through protocol-driven planning, perception-verification, and embodied control.

Method: Three-agent architecture: (1) Tailored LLM Protocol Agent converts protocols into verifiable subtasks; (2) VLM-RAG Verification Agent uses observations, robot states, retrieved knowledge, and success/failure examples to judge readiness and completion; (3) VLA Embodied Agent executes verified subtasks with a lightweight policy. AugSmolVLA provides online augmentation for transparent labware, reflections, illumination shifts, and overexposure. Evaluation across 15 atomic tasks, 6 composite workflows, 3 bimanual tasks.

Result: AugSmolVLA achieves higher execution stability than ACT, X-VLA, and the original SmolVLA in normal and high-exposure settings, especially for precise placement, transparent-object manipulation, composite workflows, and visually degraded scenes.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a practical path toward accessible, protocol-centered, and verification-capable embodied AI for biological manipulation, with potential for broader adoption in wet-lab automation.

Abstract: Biological laboratory automation can reduce repetitive manual work and improve reproducibility, but reliable embodied execution in wet-lab environments remains challenging. Protocols are often unstructured, labware is frequently transparent or reflective, and multi-step procedures require state-aware execution beyond one-shot instruction following. Existing robotic systems often rely on costly hardware, fixed workflows, dedicated instruments, or robotics-oriented interfaces. Here, we introduce BioProVLA-Agent, an affordable, protocol-driven, vision-enhanced embodied multi-agent system enabled by Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for biological manipulation. The system uses protocols as the task interface and integrates protocol parsing, visual state verification, and embodied execution in a closed-loop workflow. A Tailored LLM Protocol Agent converts protocols into verifiable subtasks; a VLM-RAG Verification Agent assesses readiness and completion using observations, robot states, retrieved knowledge, and success/failure examples; and a VLA Embodied Agent executes verified subtasks through a lightweight policy. To improve robustness under wet-lab visual perturbations, we develop AugSmolVLA, an online augmentation strategy targeting transparent labware, reflections, illumination shifts, and overexposure. We evaluate the system on a hierarchical benchmark covering 15 atomic tasks, 6 composite workflows, and 3 bimanual tasks, including tube loading, sorting, waste disposal, cap twisting, and liquid pouring. Across normal and high-exposure settings, AugSmolVLA improves execution stability over ACT, X-VLA, and the original SmolVLA, especially for precise placement, transparent-object manipulation, composite workflows, and visually degraded scenes. These results suggest a practical route toward accessible, protocol-centered, and verification-capable embodied AI for biological manipulation.

</details>


### [394] [AT-VLA: Adaptive Tactile Injection for Enhanced Feedback Reaction in Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07308)
*Xiaoqi Li,Muhe Cai,Jiadong Xu,Juan Zhu,Hongwei Fan,Yan Shen,Guangrui Ren,Hao Dong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AT-VLA introduces Adaptive Tactile Injection and a Tactile Reaction Dual-Stream to enhance Vision-Language-Action robots by selectively injecting tactile signals and decoupling sensing streams for fast real-time control, achieving 0.04 s closed-loop responses and validated on contact-rich tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLAs struggle with contact-rich manipulation; while tactile signals help, naively finetuning with a new modality risks degrading pretrained capabilities. Additionally, slow inference hinders real-time tactile feedback usage.

Method: Adaptive Tactile Injection dynamically decides when and where to inject tactile signals, minimizing interference with pretrained representations. Tactile Reaction Dual-Stream separates processing into a slow visual-language stream for perceptual reasoning and a fast tactile control stream for high-frequency interaction, enabling real-time closed-loop control (~0.04 s).

Result: Real-world experiments demonstrate effectiveness in contact-rich manipulation; claims of fast (0.04 s) tactile responses and real-time closed-loop control are supported by the reported experiments.

Conclusion: AT-VLA provides a principled approach to integrating tactile feedback into pretrained VLAs without degrading their capabilities, while delivering fast tactile-driven control and improved manipulation performance; validated on real-world tasks; a project page is provided.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have significantly advanced the capabilities of robotic agents in executing diverse tasks; however, they still face challenges in contact-rich manipulation scenarios that require precise physical interactions. To address this limitation, recent studies have attempted to incorporate tactile signals during downstream tasks, enabling pretrained VLAs to interpret tactile feedback. Nevertheless, introducing new modalities during finetuning, which are rarely present in the pretrain stage, may disrupt the pretrained capabilities of VLAs. In addition, the inherently slow inference speed of VLAs hampers real-time responsiveness and limits the effective utilization of tactile feedback for action adjustment. To overcome these challenges, we propose Adaptive Tactile Vision-Language-Action (AT-VLA), which introduces a novel Adaptive Tactile Injection mechanism. This mechanism dynamically determines the appropriate timing and locations for tactile injection, incorporating only when it significantly contributes to action generation, thereby minimizing interference with pretrained representations. Furthermore, to enable rapid and accurate tactile responses, we propose a Tactile Reaction Dual-Stream mechanism, which decouples sensory processing into a slow visual-language stream for low-frequency perceptual reasoning and a fast tactile control stream for high-frequency physical interaction understanding, achieving real-time close-loop responses within 0.04 s. Real-world experiments thoroughly validate the effectiveness of AT-VLA in contact-rich manipulation tasks. The project page is available at: https://sites.google.com/view/at-vla.

</details>


### [395] [CSR: Infinite-Horizon Real-Time Policies with Massive Cached State Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07325)
*Robin Karlsson,Go Suzui*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CSR + ASR enable real-time, high-frequency operation of massive LLMs for robotics by maintaining stable prefixes, incremental extension, and asynchronous state reconciliation to minimize TTFT latencies; empirical gains include 26x latency reduction and SOTA recall with sustained latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Make massive LLMs usable as continuous cognitive engines for robotics by reducing time-to-first-token (TTFT) latency while preserving global context.

Method: Define and prove necessary conditions for latency-optimal task structure: prefix stability, incremental extensibility, asynchronous state reconciliation. Implement CSR for KV-cache reuse and ASR for parallel memory eviction. Empirically validate on robotic hardware and embodied AI benchmarks.

Result: 26-fold latency reduction for 120K token contexts with 235B model on real robot (14.67s -> 0.56s). Achieves SOTA recall (0.836 vs 0.459) with RAG-like latency on embodied AI benchmark. ASR yields bounded, spike-free TTFT over 10 eviction cycles. Enables >2 Hz continuous policies.

Conclusion: CSR + ASR provide a practical framework to deploy massive LLMs as real-time embodied agents with stable latency and high recall; approach balances context handling, cache reuse, and asynchronous memory management to sustain long-horizon operation.

Abstract: Deploying massive large language models (LLMs) as continuous cognitive engines for robotics is bottlenecked by the time-to-first-token (TTFT) latency required to process extensive state histories. Existing solutions like RAG or sliding windows compromise global context or incur prohibitive re-computation costs. We formalize the optimal task structure for minimizing latency and theoretically prove that prefix stability, incremental extensibility, and asynchronous state reconciliation are necessary conditions for real-time performance. Building on these proofs, we introduce the Cached State Representation (CSR) framework as the practical instantiation of these properties, ensuring optimal KV-cache reuse. To sustain these properties over infinite horizons, we further propose an Asynchronous State Reconciliation (ASR) algorithm that offloads state memory eviction to a parallel computational resource to eliminate latency spikes. On a physical robot wirelessly connected to an on-premise GPU server, CSR achieves a 26-fold latency reduction (14.67s to 0.56s) for 120K token contexts with a 235B parameter model compared to a standard baseline. On an embodied AI benchmark, we achieve SOTA recall (0.836 vs. 0.459) while maintaining RAG-level latency. ASR is validated to sustain bounded, spike-free TTFT over 10 eviction cycles in continuous real-world operation. Together, CSR and ASR enable massive LLMs to function as continuously operating, high-frequency (> 2 Hz) embodied policies.

</details>


### [396] [Weather-Robust Scene Semantics with Vision-Aligned 4D Radar](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07367)
*Kali Hamilton,Christoffer Heckman*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A lightweight radar-to-VLM pipeline aligns a radar encoder to frozen SigLIP vision embeddings and decodes structured scene captions with ~7M trainable parameters, achieving weather-robust perception and addressing a token-norm mismatch via projector-output LayerNorm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cameras and LiDAR deteriorate in rain, fog, and snow, while radar remains robust. Leverage a frozen vision-language model (VLM) to produce reliable, structured captions with minimal trainable parameters.

Method: Align a radar encoder to frozen SigLIP vision embeddings and decode captions through a frozen VLM (~7M trainable params). Identify token-norm mismatch as a dominant failure when bridging radar to VLM and fix with projector-output LayerNorm. Evaluate on K-RADAR across fog/light snow/heavy snow sequences. Analyze encoder complexity, caption format, and pooling to inform design.

Result: Radar configurations outperform a camera baseline that suffers >90% hallucination under adverse weather. Token-norm mismatch is mitigated by applying LayerNorm at the projector output. Comprehensive analysis reveals tradeoffs in encoder complexity, caption format, and pooling strategies that guide future radar-VLM pipeline design.

Conclusion: A compact, frozen-VLM–driven radar perception pipeline can deliver robust, structured scene captions under adverse weather, with explicit guidance on normalization and design tradeoffs for future work.

Abstract: Cameras and LiDAR degrade in rain, fog, and snow, while millimeter-wave radar remains largely unaffected. We align a radar encoder to frozen SigLIP vision
  embeddings and decode structured scene captions through a frozen vision-language model (VLM) with approximately 7M trainable parameters. On K-RADAR with
  held-out fog, light snow, and heavy snow sequences, all radar configurations outperform a camera baseline that collapses to over 90% hallucination. We
  identify a token-norm mismatch as the dominant failure mode when bridging radar to a frozen VLM and show that projector-output LayerNorm resolves it.
  Analysis of encoder complexity, caption format, and pooling strategy reveals tradeoffs that inform future radar-VLM pipeline design.

</details>


### [397] [MORPH-U: Multi-Objective Resilient Motion Planning for V2X-Enabled Autonomous Driving in High-Uncertainty Environments via Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07370)
*Shih-Yu Lai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MORPH-U: a CARLA-based closed-loop stack fusing LiDAR/radar/camera with V2X into a Local Dynamic Map; uses Hybrid-A* replanning triggered by validated hazards or map changes; employs a Pareto-frontier multi-objective to balance tracking, safety, responsiveness, and smoothness; includes a Byzantine-inspired acceptance gate to resist false DENM injections; experiments show improved safety, tunable trade-offs, and resilience to attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: V2X communication offers hazard warnings beyond LOS but introduces uncertainty (delay, drop, forgery) and map changes require real-time replanning; need robust planning/control under uncertain event-driven updates.

Method: Integrates multi-sensor data (LiDAR/radar/camera) with V2X (CAM/DENM) into a Local Dynamic Map; triggers Hybrid-A* replanning when hazards or map changes affect planned route; formulates a multi-objective optimization over tracking error, minimum TTC safety margin, responsiveness, and smoothness; uses Pareto-frontier to select operating points; introduces a lightweight Byzantine-inspired acceptance gate combining a quorum rule with an on-board sensor veto to prevent replanning due to faulty V2X triggers.

Result: Experiments in dynamic CARLA scenarios show that V2X-augmented LDM improves downstream safety; Pareto tuning yields controllable accuracy-comfort trade-offs; the gate prevents replanning under saturated false-DENM injection (p_attack = 1.0).

Conclusion: MORPH-U demonstrates robust, V2X-enabled planning under uncertain event-driven updates, offering adjustable trade-offs via Pareto optimization and resilience against false DENM through a Byzantine-inspired gate; supports safe and responsive autonomous driving in realistic simulators.

Abstract: V2X can warn an autonomous vehicle about hazards beyond line-of-sight, but it also brings uncertainty: messages may be delayed, dropped, or even forged. Meanwhile, map knowledge may change during a trip, forcing the vehicle to replan under tight real-time budgets. This paper studies how to make motion planning and low-level control robust to such uncertain, event-driven updates. We present MORPH-U, a CARLA-based closed-loop stack that fuses LiDAR/radar/camera with V2X (CAM/DENM) into a Local Dynamic Map (LDM) and triggers Hybrid-A* replanning when validated hazards or map changes affect the planned route. We expose the planning/control trade-offs via a multi-objective formulation over tracking error, safety margin (minimum TTC), responsiveness, and smoothness, and select operating points using Pareto-frontier analysis. To avoid unsafe replanning from faulty V2X triggers, MORPH-U adds a lightweight Byzantine-inspired acceptance gate that combines a quorum rule with an on-board sensor veto. Experiments in dynamic CARLA scenarios show that V2X-augmented LDM improves downstream safety, Pareto tuning provides controllable accuracy-comfort trade-offs, and the gate prevents replanning under saturated false-DENM injection ($p_{\text{attack}}=1.0$).

</details>


### [398] [Escaping the Diversity Trap in Robotic Manipulation via Anchor-Centric Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07381)
*Yanzhe Chen,Kevin Yuchen Ma,Qi Lv,Yiqi Lin,Zechen Bai,Chen Gao,Mike Zheng Shou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A theory-guided data-budget adaptation for Vision-Language-Action models through a Coverage–Density Trade-off; introduces Anchor-Centric Adaptation (ACA) to stabilize policy at core anchors and expand coverage at high-risk boundaries, improving real-robot performance under fixed data budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adapting VLA models to real hardware under limited demonstrations is impeded by an “embodiment gap” and a diversity trap where more diverse (single-shot) data can increase estimation noise. The work formalizes how to allocate a fixed data budget between density (estimation) and coverage (extrapolation) for reliable policy transfer.

Method: Decompose policy error into estimation (density) and extrapolation (coverage) terms; derive an interior optimal allocation of conditions under a fixed budget. Propose ACA: a two-stage approach—(1) stabilize a policy skeleton via repeated demonstrations at core anchors; (2) selectively expand coverage to high-risk boundaries using teacher-forced error mining and constrained residual updates.

Result: Real-robot experiments show that ACA improves task reliability and success rates compared to standard diverse sampling strategies given the same data budget.

Conclusion: The Coverage–Density Trade-off framework plus ACA provides a practical, data-efficient pathway for adapting VLA models to real hardware, with demonstrated gains in reliability and success under limited demonstrations; potential applicability to broader embodied AI adaptation tasks.

Abstract: While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer broad general capabilities, deploying them on specific hardware requires real-world adaptation to bridge the embodiment gap. Since robot demonstrations are costly, this adaptation must often occur under a strict data budget. In this work, we identify a critical diversity trap: the standard heuristic of "maximizing coverage" by collecting diverse, single-shot demonstrations can be self-defeating due to non-vanishing estimation noise. We formalize this phenomenon as a Coverage--Density Trade-off. By decomposing the policy error into estimation (density) and extrapolation (coverage) terms, we characterize an interior optimal allocation of unique conditions for a fixed budget. Guided by this analysis, we propose Anchor-Centric Adaptation (ACA), a two-stage framework that first stabilizes a policy skeleton through repeated demonstrations at core anchors, then selectively expands coverage to high-risk boundaries via teacher-forced error mining and constrained residual updates. Real-robot experiments validate our trade-off framework and demonstrate that ACA significantly improves task reliability and success rates over standard diverse sampling strategies under the same budget.

</details>


### [399] [PathPainter: Transferring the Generalization Ability of Image Generation Models to Embodied Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07496)
*Yijin Wang,Yuru Tian,Xijie Huang,Weiqi Gai,Mo Zhu,Xin Zhou,Yuze Wu,Fei Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A BEV-based navigation framework uses a language-conditioned image generation model to infer user intent, produce traversability masks, and guide ground/near-ground robots via cross-view localization that aligns odometry with BEV maps; validated on UAV for a 160 m outdoor task, illustrating transfer of foundation-model world understanding to embodied navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploit BEV as a global priors source to improve navigation reliability and long-range planning, while addressing odometry drift and the need to interpret human intent through natural language; demonstrate that foundation-model capabilities can transfer to embodied robotics.

Method: Leverage BEV as global priors; use an image-generation model to parse natural-language instructions into a target destination and generate traversability masks. During execution, perform cross-view localization to align robot odometry with the BEV map and reduce drift. Validate via extensive benchmarks and a UAV test with only a local motion planner achieving a 160 m navigation task.

Result: Extensive benchmarks validate the approach; the UAV completes a 160 m outdoor long-range navigation task using the proposed system, demonstrating effective use of BEV priors and cross-view localization. The work illustrates that foundation-model-based world understanding can generalize to embodied navigation.

Conclusion: BEV priors combined with cross-view localization enable robust, long-range navigation across platforms and show how foundation models can be leveraged for embodied navigation, highlighting generalization and potential cross-domain applicability.

Abstract: Bird's-eye-view (BEV) images have been widely demonstrated to provide valuable prior information for navigation. Given the global information provided by such views, two key challenges remain: how to fully exploit this information and how to reliably use it during execution. In this paper, we propose a navigation system that uses BEV images as global priors and is designed for ground and near-ground robotic platforms. The system employs an image generation model to interpret human intent from natural language, identify the target destination, and generate traversability masks. During execution, we introduce cross-view localization to align the robot's odometry with the BEV map and mitigate long-term drift in conventional odometry. We conduct extensive benchmark experiments to evaluate the proposed method and further validate it on a UAV platform. Using only a conventional local motion planner, the UAV successfully completes a 160-meter outdoor long-range navigation task. This work demonstrates how the world-understanding capabilities of foundation models can be transferred to embodied navigation, enabling robots to benefit from the strong generalization ability of existing image generation models.

</details>


### [400] [Is the Future Compatible? Diagnosing Dynamic Consistency in World Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07514)
*Bo-Kai Ruan,Teng-Fang Hsiao,Ling Lo,Hong-Han Shuai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Action-state consistency is a key reliability axis for World Action Models (WAMs): aligning predicted actions with induced state transitions improves rollout quality and enables value-free planning via a consensus test-time strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: WAMs generate imagined futures whose visual plausibility does not guarantee dynamic coherence with the action sequence; a diagnostic axis is needed to assess reliability beyond visuals.

Method: Systematic study across joint-prediction and inverse-dynamics models; quantify action-state consistency; analyze success/failure of rollouts on tasks including RoboCasa and RoboTwin 2.0; identify background collapse; propose a test-time, value-free consensus ranking among candidate rollouts for selection.

Result: Action-state consistency systematically differentiates successful vs failed rollouts and tracks similar value-estimate trends; background collapse reveals deceptively consistent but low-dynamics trajectories; a consensus-based test-time ranking improves success rates without extra training or reward modeling.

Conclusion: Action-state consistency serves as both a diagnostic tool and a practical signal for value-free planning in WAMs, enriching reliability assessment and rollout selection.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) enable decision-making through imagined rollouts by predicting future observations and actions. However, the reliability of these imagined futures remains under-examined: is a generated future merely visually plausible, or is it dynamically compatible with the action sequence it claims to model? In this work, we identify action-state consistency, the alignment between predicted actions and induced state transitions, as a missing reliability axis for WAMs. Through a systematic study across representative joint-prediction and inverse-dynamics models, we find that action-state consistency systematically separates successful and failed rollouts across many tasks and follows similar success-failure trends as learned value estimates. These results suggest that consistency captures decision-relevant structure beyond visual realism. We further identify background collapse as an important boundary condition, where low-dynamics failed trajectories can become deceptively consistent because static futures are easier to predict. Building on these findings, we introduce a value-free consensus strategy for test-time selection, which ranks candidate rollouts by agreement among predicted futures. This strategy improves success rates on RoboCasa and RoboTwin 2.0 without additional training or reward modeling. Taken together, our findings establish action-state consistency as both a diagnostic tool for evaluating WAM reliability and a practical signal for value-free planning.

</details>


### [401] [Search-based Robustness Testing of Laptop Refurbishing Robotic Software](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07530)
*Erblin Isaku,Hassan Sartaj,Shaukat Ali,Malaika Din Hashmi,Francois Picard*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PROBE is a search-based robustness testing approach using multi-objective optimization to find minimal, localized perturbations that trigger failures in object-detection models used by laptop-refurbishing robots; it outperforms random search (3–7x) with smaller perturbations, and the perturbations transfer across models; metamorphic relations offer additional stability insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure robustness of object-detection models embedded in robotic refurbishment workflows, reducing risk of misdetections that could cause damage during laptop disassembly and improving alignment with the European Circular Economy Action Plan.

Method: A multi-objective search-based testing pipeline (NSGA-II) that searches for localized perturbations maximizing failure induction (localization accuracy loss and confidence drop) while minimizing perturbation magnitude. The approach enables diverse failure discovery and employs metamorphic relations to gain insights into robustness even in non-failing cases.

Result: PROBE is 3× to 7× more effective than random search in generating failure-inducing perturbations, requires smaller perturbations, and the discovered perturbations transfer across different object-detection models. Metamorphic relations provide additional robustness insights for non-failing cases.

Conclusion: PROBE provides an effective, transferable robustness testing framework for object-detection models in laptop refurbishing robotics, enabling more reliable operation and safer handling of devices; metamorphic-relations-based analysis complements traditional failure-focused evaluation.

Abstract: The Danish Technological Institute (DTI) focuses on transferring advanced technologies (including robots) to the industry and the public sector. One key application is laptop refurbishment using specialized robots, aimed at promoting reuse, reducing electronic waste, and supporting the European Circular Economy Action Plan. The software of such robots often includes features that use object detection models to detect objects for various purposes, such as identifying screws for laptop disassembly or detecting stickers to remove them. Ensuring the robustness of such models to small input variations remains a critical challenge, and addressing it is important to avoid potential damage to laptops during refurbishment. In this paper, we propose PROBE, a search-based robustness testing approach that leverages multi-objective optimization to identify minimal, localized perturbations that expose failures in object detection models used in the software of laptop refurbishing robots. PROBE employs NSGA-II to systematically explore the perturbation space, optimizing for failure induction considering both localization and confidence, and perturbation magnitude, while enabling the discovery of diverse failure cases. Results show that PROBE is 3$\times$ to 7$\times$ more effective than random search in generating failure-inducing perturbations, while requiring smaller perturbation magnitudes, and that the generated perturbations transfer across models. We further show that metamorphic relations provide additional insights into model robustness, enabling the assessment of stability even in non-failing cases.

</details>


### [402] [How to utilize failure demo data?: Effective data selection for imitation learning using distribution differences in attention mechanism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07560)
*Kana Miyamoto,Kanata Suzuki,Tetsuya Ogata*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a latent-representation based imitation learning method that leverages failure demonstrations by embedding success-vs-failure discrepancies into an attention mechanism, with an inference-time latent mode selection from the initial state and a post-training metric to select informative failure samples; results show improved success rates with failure data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation learning often uses only successful demonstrations, yet failure data is available but underutilized. Existing approaches require extra processing or autonomous rollouts. The work aims to stably incorporate failure data into training to improve robustness and data efficiency.

Method: Train latent representations of success–failure discrepancies and integrate them into an attention mechanism. At inference, select a latent mode from the initial observation to guide actions. Introduce a post-training metric that measures attention discrepancy between failure samples and successful demos to filter/weight failure data for training.

Result: In simulations, incorporating failure data improves task success rates. The proposed attention-discrepancy metric identifies failure samples that are beneficial when combined with successful demonstrations.

Conclusion: The approach facilitates more efficient use of collected demonstrations in robotic data collection pipelines by effectively leveraging failure data through latent-discrepancy representations and attention-based selection.

Abstract: Imitation learning for robotic tasks has relied primarily on policies trained only on successful demonstrations, although failures are unavoidable during human data collection. Many existing approaches for exploiting failure data require additional data processing or iterative policy updates through autonomous rollouts, making it difficult to directly and stably utilize failure data accumulated during data collection. In this work, we propose a method that learns latent representations of success-failure discrepancies and incorporates them into the attention mechanism. During inference, an appropriate latent mode is selected from the initial observation to improve action stability. Furthermore, we introduce a post-training metric that quantifies the attention discrepancy between each failure sample and successful demonstrations to select failure data. Simulation results show that the proposed method improves task success rates when trained with failure data and that the proposed metric identifies failure samples that are beneficial for learning when combined with successful demonstrations. These results suggest that the proposed method can support more efficient use of collected demonstrations in robotic data collection pipelines.

</details>


### [403] [MemCompiler: Compile, Don't Inject -- State-Conditioned Memory for Embodied Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07594)
*Xin Ding,Xinrui Wang,Yifan Yang,Hao Wu,Shiqi Jiang,Qianxi Zhang,Liang Mi,Hanxin Zhu,Kun Li,Yunxin Liu,Zhibo Chen,Ting Cao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MemCompiler replaces static ahead-of-time memory injection with a state-conditioned memory compiler that dynamically selects and compiles only relevant memory, improving effectiveness and reducing latency across several benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static memory injection (AMMI) misaligns with the agent's evolving state and can harm lightweight executors, motivating a dynamic, state-aware approach to memory usage.

Method: Train a Memory Compiler that ingests a structured Brief State of the agent, and outputs executable guidance by selecting and compiling memory into two channels: a text channel and a latent Soft-Mem channel preserving non-text perceptual information.

Result: Across Alf World, EmbodiedBench, and ScienceWorld, MemCompiler yields up to +129% improvement over no-memory on open-source backbones; approaches frontier closed-source systems; reduces per-step latency by 60%.

Conclusion: State-conditioned memory compilation improves both effectiveness and efficiency, making memory use more aligned with the agent's current state and workloads.

Abstract: Existing memory systems for embodied agents typically inject retrieved memory as static context at episode start, a paradigm we term Ahead-of-time Monolithic Memory Injection (AMMI). However, this static design quickly becomes misaligned with the agent's evolving state and may degrade lightweight executors below the no-memory baseline. To address this, we propose MemCompiler, which reframes memory utilization as State-Conditioned Memory Compilation. A learned Memory Compiler reads a structured Brief State capturing the agent's current execution state and dynamically selects and compiles only relevant memory into executable guidance. This guidance is delivered through a text channel and a latent Soft-Mem channel that preserves perceptual information not expressible in text. Across Alf World, EmbodiedBench, and ScienceWorld, MemCompiler consistently improves over no-memory across open-source backbones (up to +129%), matches or approaches frontier closed-source systems, and reduces per-step latency by 60%, demonstrating that state-aware memory compilation improves both effectiveness and efficiency.

</details>


### [404] [BrickCraft: Visuomotor Skill Composition with Situated Manual Guidance for Long-Horizon Interlocking Brick Assembly](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07605)
*Jichuan Yu,Bowei Li,Zhenran Tang,Guanxing Lu,Chuxiong Hu,Ruixuan Liu,Changliu Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BrickCraft is a compositional framework for long-horizon interlocking brick assembly that uses a relative anchoring approach to a reference brick, enabling reusable primitive skills, spatially grounded visuomotor control via situated manuals, and a compositional execution pipeline for generalization to unseen structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge high-level assembly planning with fine-grained manipulation for interlocking brick tasks, addressing long-horizon reasoning, spatial grounding, and generalization under limited demonstrations.

Method: Define a relative task formulation anchored to a reference brick; decompose into a finite set of reusable primitive skills; use situated manuals to project assembly intent onto real-time observations; employ a compositional execution pipeline that chains spatially grounded skills.

Result: Experimental validations show BrickCraft learns proficient assembly from a limited set of demonstrations and generalizes compositionally to unseen structures.

Conclusion: BrickCraft offers a scalable, generalizable framework for long-horizon interlocking brick assembly by integrating relative formulation, situated manuals, and compositional execution.

Abstract: Autonomous robotic assembly of interlocking bricks demands seamless integration of long-horizon task reasoning, spatial grounding, and fine-grained manipulation. This paper presents BrickCraft, a compositional framework designed for long-horizon and generalizable interlocking brick assembly. BrickCraft models the assembly process using a relative formulation, where each step is anchored to a reference brick within the partial structure, thereby decomposing complex tasks into a finite set of reusable primitive skills. BrickCraft bridges the gap between high-level assembly plans and physical execution through situated manuals, which provide explicit spatial guidance for learned visuomotor skills by projecting the assembly intent onto real-time robot observations. Finally, BrickCraft employs a compositional execution pipeline that chains these spatially grounded skills to accomplish long-horizon assembly tasks. Extensive experimental validations demonstrate that BrickCraft acquires proficient assembly skills from a limited set of demonstrations and exhibits strong compositional generalization to unseen structures. The project website is available at https://intelligent-control-lab.github.io/BrickCraft.

</details>


### [405] [PhySPRING: Structure-Preserving Reduction of Physics-Informed Twins via GNN](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07687)
*Yixiong Jing,Xingyuan Chen,Guangming Wang,Olaf Wysocki,Haibing Wu,Brian Sheil*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PhySPRING learns a hierarchical, differentiable GNN-based coarsening of spring-mass digital twins to reduce topology complexity while preserving dynamics, yielding faster rollouts with maintained fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address mismatch between high-resolution visual reconstructions and physically tractable models, reducing redundant topology and computation in physics-based digital twins for robotics.

Method: A fully differentiable GNN that jointly learns a hierarchy of coarsened graph topologies and their spring-mass parameters. At each reduction level, nodes with similar dynamic responses are merged; each reduced layer remains an explicit spring-mass system. Training uses observations from the PhysTwin benchmark; evaluation in Real2Sim pipeline.

Result: Improved dense reconstruction and dynamics prediction over PhysTwin; reduced models retain stability and fidelity; up to 2.30x speed-up; comparable success rates in robotics tasks and improved action-sampling effectiveness in policy evaluation.

Conclusion: PhySPRING enables efficient, structure-preserving topology reduction for spring-mass digital twins, achieving faster simulations without sacrificing fidelity or robotic utility.

Abstract: Physics-based digital twins aim to predict the dynamics of real-world objects under interaction, enabling real-to-sim-to-real applications in robotics. Current approaches reconstruct such twins as explicit physical models (such as spring-mass systems) to predict the dynamics, but the resulting models often inherit the resolution of the visual reconstruction rather than being reduced to the physical complexity required to reproduce task-relevant dynamics. This mismatch introduces redundant topology, making repeated forward-dynamics rollouts unnecessarily expensive. To address this challenge, we present PhySPRING, an fully differentiable GNN-based method to reduce complexity in spring--mass digital twins. PhySPRING jointly learns a hierarchy of coarsened graph topologies and their mechanical parameters from observations. At each reduction level, PhySPRING merges nodes with similar learned dynamic responses to optimize the topology, while maintaining every reduced layer as an explicit spring--mass system. On the PhysTwin benchmark, PhySPRING improves dense reconstruction and prediction accuracy over PhysTwin, while reduced models retain stable physical and visual fidelity with up to a 2.30 times speed-up. We further demonstrate the effectiveness of PhySPRING in a Real2Sim robot policy-evaluation pipeline, where the reduced models are substituted zero-shot into ACT and $π_0$ evaluations, maintaining comparable manipulation success rates across downsampling levels while improving action-sampling effectiveness. Together, PhySPRING enables efficient and structure-preserving spring--mass reduction without sacrificing fidelity or robotic utility.

</details>


### [406] [Offline-Online Hierarchical 3D Global Relocalization With Synthetic LiDAR Sensing and Descriptor-Space Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07741)
*Jiahua Ren,Kai Shen,Muhua Zhang,Lei Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An offline–online hierarchical framework decouples the pose search space for scalable 3D relocalization, achieving fast average relocalization (≈3 s) and high accuracy (≈8 cm) with order-of-magnitude efficiency gains over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In large-scale 3D environments, the pose search space is vast and computing a global relocalization is expensive, causing long online relocalization times. A decoupled offline representation can accelerate retrieval and registration.

Method: An offline phase generates candidate poses and their geometric descriptor indices by simulating LiDAR scans in a grid-map. In the online phase, a coarse pose is retrieved globally, followed by refined 6-DoF registration of the point cloud to obtain precise pose estimates.

Result: Real-world experiments show an average relocalization time of 3 seconds and an average localization accuracy of 8 cm in 3D environments, with an order-of-magnitude improvement in computational efficiency compared to existing global relocalization methods while maintaining comparable accuracy.

Conclusion: The offline–online hierarchical decoupling effectively reduces the search space and computational burden for large-scale 3D relocalization while preserving localization accuracy, making it suitable for real-time mobile-robot applications.

Abstract: 3D global relocalization is one of the key capabilities for mobile robots in practical applications. However, in large scale spaces, existing methods often suffer from prolonged online relocalization time due to factors such as the massive pose search space and high computational overhead. To address these issues, this paper proposes an offline-online hierarchical framework that decouples the search space. In the offline phase, candidate positions and their corresponding geometric descriptor indices are generated in the map by simulating LiDAR scans within the grid map. In the online phase, a coarse pose estimate is first obtained via global retrieval, followed by point cloud registration to output precise 6-DoF pose estimates. Real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves an average relocalization time of 3 s and an average localization accuracy of 8 cm in 3D environments. Compared with existing global relocalization methods, the proposed method achieves an order-of-magnitude improvement in computational efficiency while delivering comparable relocalization accuracy.

</details>


### [407] [Sensitivity-Based Robust NMPC for Close-Proximity Offshore Wind Turbine Inspection with a Tilted Multirotor](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07771)
*Giuseppe Silano,Martin Saska*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Sensitivity-based robust NMPC with online constraint tightening for a tilted multirotor to guarantee tower clearance under model mismatch and gusts, preserving nominal NMPC structure and validated by Monte Carlo on a boundary-critical helix inspection path; clearance violations eliminated at the cost of modestly higher solve times.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nominal NMPC can fail safety constraints (clearance around tall cylindrical structures) when mass, inertia, drag, thrust effectiveness, or wind differs from nominal assumptions. A robust yet computationally lightweight approach is needed that leverages existing NMPC frameworks for close-proximity offshore inspection.

Method: Augment nominal NMPC with online constraint tightening based on first-order parametric state sensitivities to create a structured-uncertainty margin, plus a stage-dependent additive gust margin. The receding-horizon optimization remains unchanged; only sensitivity propagation and margin evaluation are added.

Result: Monte-Carlo test over 500 uncertainty realizations on a boundary-critical helical inspection trajectory shows elimination of clearance violations observed under nominal NMPC, with a moderate increase in solve time.

Conclusion: The proposed sensitivity-based robust NMPC provides safety-guaranteeing tightening without altering the optimization framework, achieving robust clearance in the presence of model mismatch and gusts at a modest computational cost.

Abstract: Close-proximity offshore wind turbine inspection requires strict clearance control around large cylindrical structures under wind and model mismatch. Nominal Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (NMPC) may violate safety constraints when mass, inertia, thrust effectiveness, drag, or wind conditions differ from nominal assumptions. We propose a sensitivity-based robust NMPC for a tilted multirotor that robustifies the tower-clearance constraint via online constraint tightening. First-order parametric state sensitivities provide a structured-uncertainty margin, while bounded gusts are handled by a stage-dependent additive margin. The formulation augments the nominal NMPC with sensitivity propagation and margin evaluation only, leaving the receding-horizon optimization structure unchanged. Monte-Carlo evaluation over 500 uncertainty realizations on a boundary-critical helical inspection trajectory shows that the proposed controller eliminates the clearance violations observed under nominal NMPC at the cost of a moderate increase in solve time.

</details>


### [408] [NoiseGate: Learning Per-Latent Timestep Schedules as Information Gating in World Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07794)
*Wen Huang,Haoran Sun,Yongjian Guo,Yunxuan Ma,Haoran Li,Jing Long,Zhouying Mo,Zhong Guan,Yucheng Guo,Shuai Di,Junwu Xiong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: NoiseGate introduces per-latent time gating in joint video-action models, enabling a learnable schedule over latent frames to modulate their reliability for action generation, improving performance on RoboTwin tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prevailing diffusion-forcing WAMs use a single global timestep for all latent frames, imposing an unjustified prior that all frames are equally reliable for action generation. Per-latent scheduling can gate information flow and improve conditioning for action decisions.

Method: Independent per-latent timestep sampling during backbone training; a lightweight Gating Policy Network emits per-latent time increments during denoising; task-reward optimization trains the schedule policy without hand-crafted priors; built on a joint video–action Mixture-of-Transformers backbone.

Result: NoiseGate delivers consistent performance gains on diverse RoboTwin random-scene manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: A per-latent information-gating policy enables selective use of latent frames in WAMs, leveraging independent time schedules to enhance action-conditioned video generation and control.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) are an emerging family of policies that tie robot action generation to future-observation modeling. In this work, we focus on the joint video--action modeling paradigm, where actions and imagined future observations are co-generated along a shared denoising or flow trajectory, so that perception, prediction, and control are coupled within one generative process. Existing WAMs typically realize this paradigm with a Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT), where video and action tokens interact through shared self-attention. This architecture can in principle assign a separate timestep $t_f$ to each predicted latent frame, yet current systems collapse this degree of freedom onto a single shared scalar $t$. Under the noise-as-masking view of Diffusion Forcing, this shared schedule imposes the unjustified prior that every predicted latent is equally reliable for action generation. We instead view the per-latent schedule as a \emph{learnable information-gating policy}: by changing a latent frame's noise level, the policy modulates the reliability of its Key/Value contribution to the action tokens. We propose \textbf{NoiseGate}, which combines independent per-latent timestep sampling during backbone training, a lightweight Gating Policy Network that emits per-latent time increments during denoising, and task-reward optimization that trains the schedule policy without hand-crafted shape priors. Built on a joint video--action MoT backbone, NoiseGate delivers consistent gains on diverse RoboTwin random-scene manipulation tasks.

</details>


### [409] [Many-to-Many Multi-Agent Pickup and Delivery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07835)
*Ethan Schneider,Jingkai Chen,Tianyi Gu,Kunlei Lian,Seth Hutchinson,Sonia Chernova*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A Many-to-Many MAPD algorithm (M2M) and its SKU-aware variant (M2M-wSKU) improve throughput in warehouse tasking by solving a NP-hard four-dimensional assignment; they outperform prior methods, with up to 22,000 more tasks completed in 8-hour simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real warehouses involve many-to-many MAPD with multiple pick and place locations per SKU, creating a 4D assignment problem that is NP-hard; scalable, efficient, and SKU-aware planning is needed to meet throughput and safety requirements.

Method: Introduce M2M, a Many-to-Many MAPD algorithm with two variants: M2M, which minimizes estimated task durations, and M2M-wSKU, which additionally incorporates SKU distribution into the objective; formulate and address a many-to-many 4D assignment problem within a warehouse setting.

Result: Simulation over 8-hour warehouse operations shows M2M variants consistently match or outperform the prior state of the art, with M2M completing up to 22,000 more tasks on average across varying environments and inventory densities.

Conclusion: The M2M family effectively handles many-to-many MAPD by incorporating SKU-aware objectives, achieving substantial throughput gains and demonstrating the practicality of SKU-informed planning for automated warehouses.

Abstract: Multi-robot systems in automated warehouses must manage continuous streams of pickup-and-delivery tasks while ensuring efficiency and safety. Prior work on Multi-Agent Pickup-and-Delivery (MAPD) has largely focused on the one-to-one variant, where each task has a fixed pickup and delivery location. In contrast, real warehouses often present many-to-many MAPD scenarios, where items, tracked by stock keeping unit (SKU) identifiers, can be retrieved from or stored at multiple locations, resulting in an NP-hard four-dimensional assignment problem. To solve the many-to-many MAPD problem, we contribute our algorithm: Many-to-Many Multi-Agent Pickup and Delivery (M2M). We experiment with two variants of our algorithm: one that minimizes estimated task durations (M2M), and one which incorporates SKU distribution into the objective function (M2M-wSKU). Simulation results over 8-hour warehouse operations show that our method consistently matches or outperforms prior state of the art, with M2M completing up to 22,000 more tasks on average across different environments and warehouse inventory densities.

</details>


### [410] [Melding LLM and temporal logic for reliable human-swarm collaboration in complex scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07877)
*Junfeng Chen,Yuxiao Zhu,An Zhuo,Xintong Zhang,Shuo Zhang,Guanghui Wen,Xiwang Dong,Meng Guo,Zhongkui Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Neuro-symbolic long-horizon planning for human-swarm teams: formal task planning with LLM-grounded reasoning, uncertainty-aware scheduling, and sparse operator interaction for dynamic multi-robot missions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address cognitive burden and invalid task sequencing in LLM-based planning under dynamic, long-horizon missions; improve reliability and scalability of human-swarm collaboration.

Method: Formalize goals using temporal logic; represent admissible orders via task automata; condition LLMs on formal constraints and live perception to generate executable subtasks; use uncertainty-aware scheduler to allocate tasks across heterogeneous swarm; implement event-triggered operator interactions; validate on heterogeneous fleet.

Result: Demonstrates executable subtask sequences satisfying mission rules; scheduler maximizes parallelism and remains resilient to disruptions; robust to hardware actuation and communication uncertainties; similar results across fleet deployments.

Conclusion: Supports a formal, scalable paradigm for reliable, low-overhead human-swarm collaboration in dynamic environments.

Abstract: Robot swarms promise scalable assistance in complex and hazardous environments. Task planning lies at the core of human-swarm collaboration, translating the operator's intent into coordinated swarm actions and helping determine when validation or intervention is required during execution. In long-horizon missions under dynamic scenarios, however, reliable task planning becomes difficult to maintain: emerging events and changing conditions demand continual adaptation, and sustained operator oversight imposes substantial cognitive burden.
