To address this, we treat rationality as context- and annotation-dependent. We design an approach to dynamically adjust the rationality parameter beta during reward learning using an LLM-as-judge to assess the likely presence of cognitive biases. This approach effectively downweights comparisons that are likely to reflect biased or unreliable judgments. Empirically, we show that this approach learns a more rational downstream model, even when finetuning on datasets with strongly biased preferences.

</details>


### [436] [Self-Programmed Execution for Language-Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06898)
*Luke J. O'Connor*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Self-Programmed Execution (SPE) and Spell to remove fixed orchestration in LM agents; formalizes agentic machines; demonstrates frontier models can operate under SPE/Spell to perform agentic tasks; raises questions on self-orchestration learning and provides code for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overwrite fixed, externally-imposed turn-based orchestration with a model-controlled, self-editing program (the orchestrator) to study agentic behavior of language models.

Method: Formalize SPE via agentic machines where an SPE state can load any state of an embedded machine; develop Spell, a Lisp-based language enabling self-editing and safe re-evaluation of effectful expressions; conduct experiments with frontier models not trained for SPE/Spell to perform agentic tasks.

Result: Frontier models can operate under SPE/Spell and accomplish challenging agentic tasks, demonstrating LM agents can function without a fixed orchestration policy.

Conclusion: LMs can act as agents without fixed orchestration; invites exploration of what self-orchestration strategies models could learn when trained for self-programmed execution; code is available for reproducibility.

Abstract: At the heart of existing language model agents is a fixed orchestrator program responsible for the state transition between consecutive turns. This paper introduces self-programmed execution (SPE), an agent architecture in which the model completion is itself the orchestrator program, and the harness evaluates this program but does not impose its own orchestration policy. I formalize this idea using agentic machines: an SPE state is one from which a model completion can load any state of an embedded copy of the machine, meaning that it is subject to no fixed turn-to-turn orchestration policy. Realizing SPE in practice is nontrivial because the same data is both model context and executable program. I therefore introduce Spell, a Lisp-based language in which programs can edit and re-evaluate themselves, and effectful expressions like model invocations are structured such that re-evaluating an edited program does not replay its side effects. Experiments with existing models, not trained for SPE or Spell, show that frontier models can operate in this regime and accomplish challenging agentic tasks. These results demonstrate how an LM can act as an agent without any fixed orchestration policy, and they raise the question of what self-orchestration strategies might be learned by a model trained for self-programmed execution. Code is available at https://github.com/lukejoconnor/spell .

</details>


### [437] [Multi-Objective Constraint Inference using Inverse reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06951)
*Syed Ihtesham Hussain Shah,Floris den Hengst,Aneta Lisowska,Annette ten Teije*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MOCI learns shared constraints and individual preferences from heterogeneous expert trajectories, enabling robust constraint inference and preference learning when experts pursue different objectives; it outperforms baselines with good predictive performance and competitive efficiency on grid-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing constraint inference methods assume homogeneous demonstrations and struggle to capture individual preferences, limiting applicability to real-world settings with diverse objectives. There is a need to jointly model shared constraints and personal preferences under heterogeneity.

Method: Propose Multi-Objective Constraint Inference (MOCI), a framework that jointly infers common constraints and per-expert preferences from heterogeneous trajectories, capable of handling conflicting objectives and maintaining computational efficiency (exact algorithm details not provided in the abstract).

Result: Empirical evaluation shows MOCI significantly surpasses baselines in predictive performance while preserving competitive computational efficiency on a standard grid-world benchmark.

Conclusion: MOCI is accurate, flexible, and computationally practical for real-world constraint inference and preference learning under heterogeneity.

Abstract: Constraint inference is widely considered essential to align reinforcement learning agents with safety boundaries and operational guidelines by observing expert demonstrations. However, existing approaches typically assume homogeneous demonstrations (i.e., generated by a single expert or multiple experts with identical objectives). They also have limited ability to capture individual preferences and often suffer from computational inefficiencies. In this paper, we introduce Multi-Objective Constraint Inference (MOCI), a novel framework designed to jointly extract shared constraints and individual preferences from heterogeneous expert trajectories, where multiple experts pursue different objectives. MOCI effectively models and learns from diverse, and potentially conflicting, behaviors. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that MOCI significantly outperforms existing baselines, achieving improved predictive performance, and maintaining competitive computational efficiency on a standard grid-world benchmark. These results establish MOCI as an accurate, flexible, and computationally practical approach for real-world constraint inference and preference learning tasks.

</details>


### [438] [Learning and Reusing Policy Decompositions for Hierarchical Generalized Planning with LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06957)
*Shirin Sohrabi,Haritha Ananthakrishnan,Harsha Kokel,Kavitha Srinivas,Michael Katz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Hierarchical Component Learning for Generalized Policies (HCL-GP), a dynamic policy-learning framework for LLM-based agents that learns parameterized, reusable components from successful executions, stores them in a component library, and composes policies via semantic retrieval; demonstrates strong generalization and efficiency on AppWorld benchmarks and open-source models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve generalization and reuse of LLM-based policies by integrating classical planning concepts (generalized planning and hierarchical task decomposition) with a reusable component library and semantic retrieval, addressing (i) automated component discovery, (ii) cross-task generalization of components, and (iii) efficient retrieval.

Method: Propose HCL-GP that (1) learns parameterized policies that generalize across task instances, (2) automatically decomposes successful executions to extract reusable components, (3) organizes components into a library and enables compositional policy generation, and (4) uses semantic search for efficient retrieval. Evaluated on AppWorld benchmark with comparisons to static synthesis and baselines, including open-source models.

Result: On AppWorld: 98.2% accuracy on normal tasks and 97.8% on challenge tasks with unseen applications, a 15.8-point improvement over static synthesis. For open-source models, dynamic reuse yields 62.5% success versus near-zero without reuse.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that combining classical planning concepts with LLM-based agents—via a reusable component library and compositional policy generation—can substantially improve accuracy and efficiency, enabling better generalization to unseen tasks.

Abstract: We present a dynamic policy-learning approach that combines generalized planning and hierarchical task decomposition for LLM-based agents. Our method, Hierarchical Component Learning for Generalized Policies (HCL-GP ), learns parameterized policies that generalize across task instances and automatically extracts reusable components from successful executions, organizing them into a component library for compositional policy generation. We address three challenges: (1) learning components through automated decomposition, (2) generalizing components to maximize reuse, and (3) efficient retrieval via semantic search. Evaluated on the AppWorld benchmark, our approach achieves 98.2% accuracy on normal tasks and 97.8% on challenge tasks with unseen applications, improving 15.8 points over static synthesis on challenging scenarios. For open-source models, dynamic reuse enables 62.5% success versus near-zero without reuse. This demonstrates that classical planning concepts can be effectively integrated with LLM agents for improved accuracy and efficiency.

</details>


### [439] [Optimal Experiments for Partial Causal Effect Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06993)
*Tobias Maringgele,Jalal Etesami*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Defines and addresses the max-potency problem: cost-constrained selection of experiments to tighten bounds on a causal query, proves NP-hardness, and offers graph-based pruning criteria (path-interception and ID-based identifiability) to dramatically reduce candidate experiments; demonstrates on ER and bnlearn graphs and an NHANES observational study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficiently allocate limited experimental budget to tighten bounds of causal queries, by prioritizing experiments that guarantee the largest worst-case reduction in bound width (epistemic potency) demonstrably before data are observed.

Method: 1) Formalize potency as worst-case bound reduction; 2) prove NP-hardness via 0-1 knapsack; 3) extend a polynomial-programming framework (Duarte et al., 2023) to evaluate potency in discrete settings; 4) introduce two pruning criteria: a path-interception rule exploiting district structure for linear-time zero-potency certification; an identifiability check using the ID algorithm; 5) empirically evaluate pruning on ER random graphs and 11 bnlearn networks (50-88% prune); 6) show combinatorial inertness of ID-pruned experiments in the subset search; 7) end-to-end NHANES demonstration estimating physical activity effect on diabetes.

Result: A general and scalable approach to pre-select experiments by computing epistemic potency and pruning candidate experiments using graph-based checks, leading to substantial reductions in search space (50-88% pruning on benchmarks) and enabling tractable end-to-end experiment design on real data.

Conclusion: Cost-constrained experimental design for tightening causal-bounds is computationally hard, but practical pruning criteria grounded in graph structure and identifiability dramatically reduce search effort, with demonstrated applicability to real observational data.

Abstract: Causal queries are often only partially identifiable from observational data, and experiments that could tighten the resulting bounds are typically costly. We study the problem of selecting, prior to observing experimental outcomes, a cost-constrained subset of experiments that maximally tightens bounds on a target query. We formalize this as the max-potency problem, where epistemic potency measures the worst-case reduction in bound width guaranteed by an experiment, and show that this problem is NP-hard via a reduction from 0-1 knapsack. Building on the polynomial-programming framework of Duarte et al. (2023), we give a general procedure for evaluating epistemic potency in discrete settings. To control the super-exponential search space, we introduce two graphical pruning criteria that depend only on the causal graph and the query: a novel path-interception rule that exploits district structure to certify zero potency in linear time, and an identifiability check based on the ID algorithm. On Erdos-Renyi random graphs and 11 bnlearn benchmark networks, the two criteria together prune 50-88% of candidate experiments on average without solving a single polynomial program. For the general subset search, we show that ID-pruned experiments are combinatorially inert, yielding a super-exponential reduction in the number of subsets evaluated. We close with an end-to-end demonstration on observational NHANES data, selecting optimal experiments for estimating the effect of physical activity on diabetes.

</details>


### [440] [Adaptive auditing of AI systems with anytime-valid guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07002)
*Siyu Zhou,Patrick Vossler,Venkatesh Sivaraman,Yifan Mai,Jean Feng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a hypothesis-testing framework for adaptive audits of generative AI failures using Safe Anytime-Valid Inference; frames dual nulls and shows asymptotic inverse relation; achieves anytime-valid error control with small samples (~20 observations).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional inference breaks under adaptive annotation strategies common in AI safety audits. There is a need for rigorous statistical guarantees when sampling decisions are made on the fly with limited data.

Method: Model the problem with two competing nulls (model null: no failure below threshold; auditor null: their sampling can uncover a failure). Use Safe Anytime-Valid Inference (SAVI) and testing-by-betting to construct e-processes, establishing asymptotic duality between the nulls.

Result: The proposed procedures maintain anytime-valid Type I error control, outperform fixed-sample methods, and can yield rigorous conclusions with as few as about 20 observations.

Conclusion: If the auditor is sufficiently powerful, the two nulls become asymptotically inverses, and passing a stringent audit effectively certifies global robustness of the AI system.

Abstract: A major bottleneck in characterizing the failure modes of generative AI systems is the cost and time of annotation and evaluation. Consequently, adaptive testing paradigms have gained popularity, where one opportunistically decides which cases and how many to annotate based on past results. While this framework is highly practical, its extreme flexibility makes it difficult to draw statistically rigorous conclusions, as it violates classical assumptions: the number of observations is typically limited (often 10 to 50 cases) and decisions regarding sampling and stopping are made in the midst of data collection rather than based a pre-specified rule. To characterize what statistical inferences can be drawn from highly adaptive audits, we introduce a hypothesis testing framework from two 'dueling' perspectives: (i) the model's null that asserts there is no failure mode with performance below a target threshold versus (ii) the auditor's null that asserts they have a sampling strategy that will uncover a failure mode. Leveraging Safe Anytime-Valid Inference (SAVI), we formalize the auditor as conducting 'testing by betting', which translates into simultaneous e-processes for testing the dueling null hypotheses. Furthermore, if the auditor is sufficiently powerful, we prove that these two hypotheses are asymptotically inverses of each other, in that passage of a stringent audit does in fact certify the AI system as being globally robust. Empirically, we demonstrate that our proposed testing procedures maintain anytime-valid type-I error control, outperform pre-specified testing methods, and can reach statistically rigorous conclusions sometimes with as few as 20 observations.

</details>


### [441] [Behavior Cue Reasoning: Monitorable Reasoning Improves Efficiency and Safety through Oversight](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07021)
*Christopher Z. Cui,Taylor W. Killian,Prithviraj Ammanabrolu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Behavior Cue Reasoning (BCR): train LLMs to emit special token sequences before target behaviors to enable monitoring/control of reasoning; enables a monitor to prune wasted reasoning, recover safe actions, and improve overseen reasoning with no performance cost across domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address misaligned and hard-to-monitor reasoning in LLMs where harmful or unsafe behaviors surface only after reasoning completion; need tractable, scalable oversight.

Method: Introduce Behavior Cues—special token sequences emitted immediately before specific implicit/explicit behaviors; use them as signals and control levers. Fine-tune a weaker external monitor with reinforcement learning for reasoning oversight, using a compressed cue-only signal; evaluate in environments with resource or safety constraints using a rule-based monitor to test recovery of safe actions. Validate across two model families and three domains.

Result: Behavior Cues enable the monitor to prune up to 50% of otherwise wasted reasoning tokens; in a near-optimal monitoring setup, recover safe actions from 80% of unsafe traces, increasing success rate from 46% to 96%; overall improved monitorability and controllability without performance loss across the tested domains/models.

Conclusion: Demonstrates scalable oversight by training the model itself to reason in a way that is more tractable for oversight, with broad applicability across domains and model families; code to be released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs) poses a challenge for oversight as many misaligned behaviors do not surface until reasoning concludes. To address this, we introduce Behavior Cue Reasoning for making LLM reasoning more controllable and monitorable. Behavior Cues are special token sequences that a model is trained to emit immediately before specific implicit and explicit behaviors, acting as dual purpose signal and control levers. When fine-tuning a weaker external monitor with Reinforcement Learning for reasoning oversight, a compressed view of only information surfaced by Behavior Cues is sufficient signal for the monitor to prune up to 50% of otherwise wasted reasoning tokens in complex math problem solving. When leveraged by an almost optimal rule-based monitor in an environment where excessive constraint violations results in failure, \ours allows for the recovery of safe actions from 80% of reasoning traces that would otherwise end with the proposal of an unsafe action, more than doubling the success rate from 46% to 96%. Through evaluation across two model families and three domains, we show that \bcreasoning improves reasoning monitorability and controllability with no cost to performance. More broadly, our work progresses scalable oversight by demonstrating how the monitored model itself can be trained to reason more tractably to oversight.
  Code to be released at https://github.com/christopherzc/text-games

</details>


### [442] [The Context Gathering Decision Process: A POMDP Framework for Agentic Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07042)
*Chinmaya Kausik,Adith Swaminathan,Nathan Kallus*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Formalizes the LLM agent search problem as a Context Gathering Decision Process (CGDP), a specialized POMDP, and views LLM behavior as approximate Thompson Sampling. Proposes two plug-and-play interventions—persistent, predicate-based belief state and a programmatic exhaustion gate—for memory management and search control. Empirically shows up to 11.4% gains in multi-hop reasoning and up to 39% token savings across three QA domains, arguing for modular, non-interfering agentic search improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents in large, information-rich environments suffer from memory corruption and context window limitations. Without explicit infrastructure, agents generate lossy states, leading to redundant work and premature stopping. A formal CGDP framework aims to enable principled, modular improvements to agentic search by refining belief states rather than expanding raw search.

Method: Formulates the problem as a Context Gathering Decision Process (CGDP), a specialized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process. Models LLM behavior as approximate Thompson Sampling within CGDP. Introduces a predicate-based decomposition to separate implicit search into explicit modular operations. Develops two plug-and-play interventions: (1) a persistent, predicate-based belief state that bounds context while preserving multi-hop reasoning; (2) a programmatic exhaustion gate that halts unproductive search without causing premature stopping. 

Result: Empirical evaluation across four methods and three question-answering domains demonstrates that substituting the LLM’s implicit state with a CGDP-driven belief state improves multi-hop reasoning by up to 11.4%. The programmatic exhaustion gate reduces token usage by up to 39% without degrading performance, indicating improved efficiency and robustness of iterative LLM agents. 

Conclusion: Framing the LLM agent loop as CGDP enables modular, non-interfering enhancements to agentic search. This perspective guides the design of interventions that manage memory and search effort without sacrificing reasoning capabilities, suggesting a principled path for improving real-world LLM-powered agents.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are deployed in complex environments -- such as massive codebases, enterprise databases, and conversational histories -- where the relevant state far exceeds their context windows. To navigate these spaces, an agent must iteratively explore the environment to find relevant information. However, without explicit infrastructure, an agent's working memory can degrade into lossy representations of the search state, resulting in redundant work (e.g. repetitive looping) and premature stopping. In this work, we formalize this challenge as the Context Gathering Decision Process (CGDP), a specialized Partially Observable Markov Decision Process, where an agent's objective is to adaptively refine its belief state to isolate the necessary information for a task. We model an LLM's behavior as approximate Thompson Sampling within this CGDP, and introduce a predicate-based method that decomposes an LLM's implicit search into explicit and modular operations. We then derive two plug-and-play interventions for iterative LLM agents: a persistent, predicate-based belief state that bounds context while preserving multi-hop reasoning, and a programmatic exhaustion gate that halts unproductive search without premature stopping. Across four methods and three question-answering domains, we empirically validate that replacing an LLM's implicit state with our CGDP-motivated belief state improves multi-hop reasoning by up to $11.4\%$; while the modular programmatic exhaustion detection saves up to $39\%$ of tokens without any degradation in agent performance. Ultimately, we argue that framing the LLM agent loop as a CGDP can guide the design of modular, non-interfering improvements to agentic search harnesses.

</details>


### [443] [2.5-D Decomposition for LLM-Based Spatial Construction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07066)
*Paul Whitten,Li-Jen Chen,Sharath Baddam*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A neuro-symbolic 2.5-D decomposition pipeline for 3D construction tasks dramatically reduces coordinate errors in LLMs, achieving near-ceiling accuracy, outperforming baselines, and transferring to edge hardware; results generalize to broader tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs exhibit systematic 3D coordinate errors when directly guiding block placements; gravity and other deterministic constraints fix degrees of freedom, presenting an opportunity to separate planning from execution to improve reliability in autonomous construction.

Method: Two-stage approach: the LLM plans in the 2D horizontal plane while a deterministic executor computes vertical placements from column occupancy; this removes the gravity-constrained degrees of freedom from the LLM's output space; evaluated on the Build What I Mean benchmark; ablation confirms the dominance of 2.5-D decomposition; extended to edge hardware and additional tasks (IGLU).

Result: Achieves 94.6% mean structural accuracy across 12 runs on Build What I Mean (near the 97.6% ceiling due to architect-agent errors); outperforms GPT-4o (90.3%) and best competing system (76.3%). Ablation shows 2.5-D decomposition accounts for 50.7 percentage points. Edge hardware (Nemotron-3 120B on NVIDIA Jetson Thor AGX) reaches 94.5% without prompt changes. Transfer to 500 IGLU tasks confirms generalization beyond the primary benchmark.

Conclusion: Removing deterministic dimensions from the LLM output space improves reliability for autonomous construction/assembly tasks constrained by gravity or similar fixed degrees of freedom; the approach generalizes to other tasks and is deployable on edge hardware.

Abstract: Autonomous systems that build structures from natural-language instructions need reliable spatial reasoning, yet large language models (LLMs) make systematic coordinate errors when generating three-dimensional block placements. We present a neuro-symbolic pipeline based on \emph{2.5-D decomposition}: the LLM plans in the two-dimensional horizontal plane while a deterministic executor computes all vertical placement from column occupancy, eliminating an entire class of errors. On the Build What I Mean benchmark (160 rounds), GPT-4o-mini with this pipeline achieves 94.6\% mean structural accuracy across 12 independent runs, within 3.0 percentage points of the 97.6\% ceiling imposed by architect-agent errors that no builder-side improvement can address. This outperforms both GPT-4o at 90.3\% and the best competing system at 76.3\%. A controlled ablation confirms that 2.5-D decomposition is the dominant contributor, accounting for 50.7 percentage points of accuracy. The pipeline transfers directly to edge hardware: Nemotron-3 120B running locally on an NVIDIA Jetson Thor AGX matches the cloud result at 94.5\% with no prompt modifications. The underlying principle, removing deterministic dimensions from the LLM's output space, applies to any autonomous construction or assembly task where gravity or other physical constraints fix one or more degrees of freedom. A transfer experiment on 500 IGLU collaborative building tasks confirm the effect generalizes beyond the primary benchmark.

</details>


### [444] [TeamBench: Evaluating Agent Coordination under Enforced Role Separation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07073)
*Yubin Kim,Chanwoo Park,Taehan Kim,Eugene Park,Samuel Schmidgall,Salman Rahman,Chunjong Park,Cynthia Breazeal,Xin Liu,Hamid Palangi,Hae Won Park,Daniel McDuff*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TeamBench is a OS-enforced role-separation benchmark (851 task templates, 931 seeded instances) to diagnose agent coordination beyond pass rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether high team pass rates reflect genuine coordination or role substitution when roles are enforced via access controls rather than prompts.

Method: Developed TeamBench with separated Planner, Executor, and Verifier roles; enforced access restrictions on specification, workspace editing, and final certification; compared prompt-only versus sandbox-enforced teams; analyzed verifier behavior and performed ablations; included a 40-session human study.

Result: Prompt-only and sandbox-enforced teams show statistically indistinguishable pass rates; prompt-only runs lead to 3.6× more cases where the verifier edits the executor’s code; verifiers approve 49% of submissions that fail the deterministic grader; removing the verifier improves mean partial score; team value is conditional (beneficial when single agents struggle, harmful when they perform well); human study reveals interaction patterns not captured by pass rate; humans alone perform tasks directly; human–agent pairings tend to approvals; human teams spend more effort coordinating missing information across roles.

Conclusion: OS-enforced role separation reveals coordination patterns not seen in pass-rate metrics; relying on prompts alone can mask coordination gaps; TeamBench provides a framework for diagnosing and informing the design of multi-agent coordination under strict role separation.

Abstract: Agent systems often decompose a task across multiple roles, but these roles are typically specified by prompts rather than enforced by access controls. Without enforcement, a team pass rate can mask whether agents actually coordinated or whether one role effectively did another role's work. We present TeamBench, a benchmark with 851 task templates and 931 seeded instances for evaluating agent coordination under operating system-enforced role separation. TeamBench separates specification access, workspace editing, and final certification across Planner, Executor, and Verifier roles, so that no role can read the full requirements, modify the workspace, and certify the final answer. Prompt-only and sandbox-enforced teams reach statistically indistinguishable pass rates, but prompt-only runs produce 3.6 times more cases where the verifier attempts to edit the executor's code. Verifiers approve 49% of submissions that fail the deterministic grader, and removing the verifier improves mean partial score in the ablation. Team value is also conditional. Teams benefit when single agents struggle, but hurt when single agents already perform well. A 40-session human study under the same role separation shows that our benchmark exposes interaction patterns that pass rate misses. Solo participants work through the task directly, human participants paired with agents often collapse into quick approval, and human teams spend more effort coordinating missing information across roles.

</details>


### [445] [Online Allocation with Unknown Shared Supply](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07080)
*Tzeh Yuan Neoh,Davin Choo,Mengchu Yue,Milind Tambe*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduction of OSSA, a stateful online allocation problem; proposes GPA policy achieving 4/3-approximation to offline optimum; proves tightness; adds a learning-augmented extension; empirical evaluation shows superior performance when supply is scarce.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world prepositioning under fixed-charge costs and lost-sales penalties without backlog; need online policies with competitive guarantees.

Method: Deterministic threshold-proportional policy GPA; analytic proofs of 4/3-approximation to optimal offline solution with additive term independent of total supply; matching lower bounds; learning-augmented extension incorporating forecasts; experiments on synthetic and real data.

Result: GPA achieves 4/3-approximation, tight; additive error unavoidable; learning-augmented extension robust to bad forecasts; empirical performance superior to baselines when global supply is scarce.

Conclusion: GPA is provably near-optimal under OSSA; learning-augmented version practically valuable; OSSA captures key tradeoffs in prepositioning under uncertainty; results bridge online policies and forecast-informed methods.

Abstract: Many real-world resource allocation systems, such as humanitarian logistics and vaccine distribution, must preposition limited supply across multiple locations before demand is realized while stockouts incur irreversible service losses. To study this, we introduce the Online Shared Supply Allocation (OSSA) problem, a stateful online model in which a central hub allocates a finite, unknown supply to multiple sites facing sequential demand under fixed-charge transportation costs and lost-sales penalties. Unlike classical make-to-stock or make-to-order inventory models, OSSA precludes backlogging and replenishment only hedges against future demand. To tackle OSSA, we propose a deterministic threshold-proportional policy GPA and prove that it achieves a $4/3$-approximation to the offline optimum up to an additive term independent of the total supply. We complement this with matching lower bounds showing that the $4/3$ ratio is tight and that the additive-error dependence is unavoidable, even for randomized algorithms that know the total supply upfront. Finally, we develop a learning-augmented extension to GPA that principally incorporates imperfect forecasts (e.g., from human experts or ML models) commonly available in practice, enabling us to exploit high-quality advice while being robust against arbitrary bad ones. Synthetic and real-world experiments show that GPA outperforms natural baselines with global supply is scarce.

</details>


### [446] [ARMOR: An Agentic Framework for Reaction Feasibility Prediction via Adaptive Utility-aware Multi-tool Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07103)
*Ye Liu,Botao Yu,Xinyi Ling,Daniel Adu-Ampratwum,Xia Ning*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ARMOR is an agentic framework that orchestrates multiple AI tools for reaction feasibility prediction, using tool-specific utilities, hierarchical prioritization, and memory-augmented conflict resolution to outperform baselines, especially when tools disagree.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Individual tools vary in performance across reactions; combining tools intelligently is necessary to achieve robust feasibility predictions.

Method: Organize tools into a hierarchy; model tool-specific utilities and strengths; adaptively select and defer tools; resolve conflicts via memory-augmented reasoning; produce final prediction per reaction.

Result: On a public dataset, ARMOR consistently outperforms single-tool and simple aggregation baselines; gains are pronounced for reactions with conflicting tool predictions; code provided.

Conclusion: ARMOR demonstrates that leveraging complementary tool strengths with structured organization and memory-based conflict resolution yields superior reaction feasibility predictions and potential applicability to other multi-tool tasks.

Abstract: Reaction feasibility prediction, as a fundamental problem in computational chemistry, has benefited from diverse tools enabled by recent advances in artificial intelligence, particularly large language models. However, the performance of individual tools varies substantially across reactions, making it difficult for any single tool to consistently perform well across all cases. This raises a critical challenge: how to effectively leverage multiple tools to obtain more accurate feasibility predictions. To address this, we propose ARMOR, an agentic framework that explicitly models tool-specific utilities, adaptively prioritizes tools, and further resolves the potential tool conflicts to produce the final prediction for each reaction. Unlike existing approaches that rely on simple aggregation or heuristic assignment over various tools, ARMOR organizes tools into a hierarchy that prioritizes top-performing tools and defers others when needed, characterizes their strengths through tool-specific patterns, and resolves conflicts via memoryaugmented reasoning. Extensive experiments on a public dataset demonstrate that ARMOR consistently outperforms strong baselines, including single-tool methods as well as various tool aggregation and tool selection approaches. Further analysis shows that the improvements are particularly significant on reactions with conflicting tool predictions, highlighting the effectiveness of ARMOR in leveraging the complementary strengths of multiple tools. The code is available via https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ARMOR-E13F.

</details>


### [447] [Switchcraft: AI Model Router for Agentic Tool Calling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07112)
*Sharad Agarwal,Pooria Namyar,Alec Wolman,Rahul Ambavat,Ankur Gupta,Qizheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Switchcraft is a tool-use–oriented model router that inline-selects the lowest-cost model meeting correctness, yielding substantial cost savings. It achieves strong accuracy on tool-use tasks while dramatically reducing inference costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the high inference costs of agentic AI systems that call external tools by designing a router specialized for tool-use rather than generic chat completion.

Method: Inline routing with a correctness constraint; evaluation framework on five function-calling benchmarks; DistilBERT-based classifier; deployed under a latency budget; comparison across models to minimize cost.

Result: 82.9% accuracy, matching or surpassing the best individual model; 84% reduction in inference cost; savings of over $3,600 per million queries; larger models do not consistently outperform smaller ones on tool-use tasks; nominally cheaper models may incur higher total cost due to token-heavy reasoning.

Conclusion: Demonstrates cost-aware deployment of agentic AI without sacrificing correctness; tool-use-optimized routing can significantly reduce costs for external-tool invocation.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems that invoke external tools are powerful but costly, leading developers to default to large models and overspend inference budgets. Model routing can mitigate this, but existing routers are designed for chat completion rather than tool use. We present Switchcraft, the first (to the best of our knowledge) model router optimized for agentic tool calling. Switchcraft operates inline, selecting the lowest-cost model subject to correctness. We construct an evaluation framework on five function-calling benchmarks and train a DistilBERT-based classifier, deployed under a latency budget. Switchcraft achieves 82.9% accuracy -- matching or exceeding the best individual model -- while reducing inference cost by 84%, saving over $3,600 per million queries. We find that larger models do not consistently outperform smaller ones on tool-use tasks, and that nominally cheaper models can incur higher total cost due to token-intensive reasoning. Our work enables cost-aware agentic AI deployment without sacrificing correctness.

</details>


### [448] [AdaTKG: Adaptive Memory for Temporal Knowledge Graph Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07121)
*Seunghan Lee,Jun Seo,Jaehoon Lee,Sungdong Yoo,Minjae Kim,Tae Yoon Lim,Dongwan Kang,Hwanil Choi,SoonYoung Lee,Wonbin Ahn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AdaTKG introduces adaptive per-entity memory for temporal knowledge graphs, updating memory with each observed interaction via a shared-scalar exponential moving average. This enables online refinement and zero-shot handling, yielding gains over static baselines; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static entity representations in temporal knowledge graphs do not reflect interaction histories; there is a need for dynamic, interaction-aware representations that evolve as entities participate in facts.

Method: Maintain a per-entity memory updated online with every observed fact using a learnable exponential moving average governed by a single shared scalar. No per-entity parameters are learned; memory accumulates over time, allowing unseen entities to be handled.

Result: Extensive experiments show consistent gains over temporal knowledge graph baselines, validating the effectiveness of adaptive memory. Code is publicly available.

Conclusion: Adaptive, interaction-aware memory improves predictive performance in TKGs by refining entity representations with each participation, enabling online learning and better generalization.

Abstract: Temporal knowledge graphs (TKGs) represent time-stamped relational facts and support a wide range of reasoning tasks over evolving events. However, existing methods produce entity representations that are static at the entity level, in that each representation is a function of learned parameters only and retains no trace of the interactions in which the entity has participated. In this paper, we depart from this static view and propose that each entity be modeled as an adaptive process whose representation is refined every time the entity participates in a fact. To this end, we propose AdaTKG, which maintains a per-entity memory that is updated with every observed interaction, with the memory accumulating online and predictions improving as more interactions arrive. Specifically, we instantiate the memory update as a learnable exponential moving average governed by a single shared scalar instead of using learnable parameters for each entity, enabling AdaTKG to handle entities unseen during training. Extensive experiments confirm consistent gains over TKG baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of adaptive memory. Code is publicly available at: https://github.com/seunghan96/AdaTKG.

</details>


### [449] [Can You Break RLVER? Probing Adversarial Robustness of RL-Trained Empathetic Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07138)
*Deeraj S K,Sadhana Devarajan,Krishna Mehra,Sudhakar Mishra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AEB and ECS assess empirical robustness of RL-based empathetic LMs under adversarial users. RLVER-Think shows stronger empathetic responses than baselines, but ECS (state-tracking proxy) does not improve, indicating a dissociation between responsive empathy and observable state-tracking in this setting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL-based emotion-reward frameworks assume cooperative users; real interactions include gaslighting and manipulation. There is a need for benchmarks that stress-test empathetic agents under adversarial dynamics and metrics that separate emotional responsiveness from state-tracking capabilities.

Method: Introduce Adversarial Empathy Benchmark (AEB) with six adversarial trajectory types and discriminative rewards that penalize formulaic replies. Propose Emotional Consistency Score (ECS) to disentangle tracking of user emotions from the agent's attempt to influence them. Evaluate across eight scenario-matched conditions using two RLVER variants (Think and No-Think) and two base models (Qwen-1.5B, Qwen-7B), with 480 adversarial dialogues.

Result: RLVER-PPO-Think substantially outperforms the same-scale untuned baseline (0.963 vs. 0.761, p<0.001, r=0.688) with zero dialogue collapses and 47% higher hidden-intention detection. However, ECS remains nearly flat and shows no significant difference between RLVER-PPO-Think and Base-7B-Think (p=0.650), suggesting training improves emotional responsiveness without measurable gains in observable state tracking.

Conclusion: The ECS–Final Score gap indicates a behavioral/legibility dissociation within this simulator family rather than evidence of deeper internal understanding or clinical readiness. ECS may not capture state-tracking improvements, underscoring the need for complementary metrics to assess genuine understanding under adversarial dynamics.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable emotion rewards RLVER has produced language models with strong empathetic performance, evaluated on benchmarks that assume cooperative, honest users. Yet real emotional interactions systematically violate this assumption: users gaslight, escalate, and pressure AI systems for unconditional validation, dynamics that cooperative benchmarks cannot surface. We construct the Adversarial Empathy Benchmark AEB and introduce the Emotional Consistency Score ECS to evaluate empathetic robustness under adversarial conditions. AEB comprises six psychologically grounded adversarial trajectory types with discriminative reward structures that penalize formulaic responses; ECS formally disentangles a model's capacity to track user emotional states from its capacity to improve them. In a controlled experiment across eight scenario-matched conditions (think and no-think conditions on 2 RLVER models, and 2 base models (Qwen 1.5B and 7B) with 480 adversarial dialogues), RLVER-PPO-Think substantially outperforms the same-scale untuned baseline (0.963 vs. 0.761, \(p<0.001, r=0.688\)), with zero dialogue collapses and 47\% higher hidden-intention detection. However, ECS remains nearly flat and is not significantly different for RLVER-PPO-Think versus Base-7B-Think (\(p=0.650\)): RL training improves emotional responsiveness without measurable gains in observable state tracking. We interpret the ECS--FS (Final Score) gap as a behavioral/legibility dissociation inside this simulator family, not as evidence about internal understanding or clinical readiness.

</details>


### [450] [SREGym: A Live Benchmark for AI SRE Agents with High-Fidelity Failure Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07161)
*Jackson Clark,Yiming Su,Saad Mohammad Rafid Pial,Yifang Tian,Lily Gniedziejko,Hans-Arno Jacobsen,Yinfang Chen,Tianyin Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SREGym is a high-fidelity, extensible benchmark for agentic SRE with 90 realistic fault scenarios; it reveals substantial performance variance across failure types and is open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional SRE benchmarks are too simplistic and hard to extend due to bespoke designs; a realistic, modular, fault-injection benchmark is needed to properly evaluate AI SRE agents in production-like conditions.

Method: Built on live cloud-native stacks with fault injectors across layers; simulates ambient noise and diverse failure modes (including metastable and correlated failures); a modular framework coordinating fault/noise injectors; currently offers 90 SRE problems.

Result: Evaluation across frontier agents shows capabilities vary greatly by failure type, with up to ~40% differences in end-to-end results; SREGym demonstrates the need for diverse, high-fidelity benchmarks and is actively maintained as an open-source project.

Conclusion: SREGym provides a high-fidelity, extensible platform to robustly evaluate agentic SREs in realistic conditions, enabling broader adoption by researchers and practitioners.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly used to diagnose and mitigate failures in production systems, known as agentic Site Reliability Engineering (SRE). Current SRE benchmarks are limited to oversimplistic SRE tasks and are unfortunately hard to extend due to bespoke designs. We present SREGym, a high-fidelity benchmark for SRE agents. SREGym exposes a live system environment built atop real-world cloud-native system stacks, where high-fidelity failure scenarios are simulated through fault injectors. SREGym models the complexity of production environments by simulating (1) a wide range of faults at different layers, (2) various ambient noises, and (3) diverse failure modes such as metastable failures and correlated failures. SREGym is architected as a modular, extensible framework that orchestrates fault and noise injectors across stacks. SREGym currently includes 90 realistic, challenging SRE problems. We use SREGym to evaluate frontier agents and show that their capabilities varies significantly in addressing different kinds of failures, with up to 40% differences in end-to-end results. SREGym is actively maintained as an open-source project and has been used by researchers and practitioners.

</details>


### [451] [Repeated Deceptive Path Planning against Learnable Observer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07174)
*Shiyue Cao,Pei Xu,Likun Yang,Lei Cui,Shizhao Yu,Shiyu Zhang,Yongjian Ren,Xiaotang Chen,Kaiqi Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RDPP introduces DeMP, a two-level meta-planning framework to counter learnable observers in deceptive path planning, combining episode-level adaptation and meta-level learning to reduce observer adaptation lag, achieving superior deception with competitive path cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DPP assumes static observers; real adversaries can learn from history and adapt, creating a gap in deception performance.

Method: Two-level optimization: episode-level adaptation adjusts policies in response to updated observer predictions; meta-level updates learn across episodes how observers update their models, accelerating future adaptation; integrated within DeMP.

Result: Experiments demonstrate DeMP substantially outperforms existing RDPP methods while maintaining competitive path cost.

Conclusion: Modeling repeated interactions with learnable adversaries is key for sustained deception; DeMP provides a general framework and insights for deception/privacy in multi-agent systems.

Abstract: We study the problem of deceptive path planning (DPP), where an agent aims to conceal its true destination from external observers. While existing work assumes static, non-learning observers, real-world adversaries-such as in critical goods transportation or military operations-can adapt by learning from historical trajectories. To address this gap, we introduce Repeated Deceptive Path Planning (RDPP), a new formulation that explicitly models learnable observers. We show that existing DPP methods fail under this setting, as they cannot adapt to evolving adversarial predictions. While incorporating observer previous predictions into updates enables some adaptation, such incremental updates cause accumulative lag that degrades deception. To this end, we propose Deceptive Meta Planning (DeMP), a two-level optimization framework that combines episode-level adaptation, which enables short-term policy adjustment to counter updated observer, and meta-level updates, which leverage cross-episode feedback to capture how observers update their models and accelerate adaptation in future episodes. In this way, DeMP mitigates the accumulation of adaptation lag, enabling sustained deception against a learning observer. Experiments across environments demonstrate that DeMP significantly outperforms existing approaches in RDPP while maintaining competitive path cost. Our results highlight the importance of modeling repeated interactions with learnable adversaries, providing new insights into deception and privacy in multi-agent systems.

</details>


### [452] [Three-in-One World Model: Energy-Based Consistency, Prediction, and Counterfactual Inference for Marketing Intervention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07199)
*Junichiro Niimi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Three-in-One world-model using a Deep Boltzmann Machine to learn a latent belief from demographics, time, and lagged history, enabling energy-based evaluation, outcome prediction, and counterfactual inference with adapters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify predictive and causal inference under a single latent representation of latent consumer heterogeneity and internal states, addressing fragmentation across prediction, language, and causal models in marketing.

Method: Train a frozen belief representation in a DBM from demographics, time, and lagged actions/outcomes; attach lightweight adapters for three tasks (energy-based consistency via free energy, outcome prediction, counterfactual inference by fixed belief with varying actions); evaluate in a controlled simulation where latent traits are known; apply free-energy clamps to penalize implausible counterfactuals.

Result: Adapters match strong MLP baseline on visit- and purchase-AUC; better recovery of heterogeneous treatment effects than S-, T-, X-, DR- learners and Causal Forest; largest advantage under a confounded price-promotion scenario; free-energy clamps penalize counterfactuals inconsistent with prior exposure, with penalties modulated by latent base preference; overall evidence that DBM beliefs disentangle latent traits robust to counterfactual queries.

Conclusion: DBM-based latent beliefs provide an integrated world-model substrate for marketing interventions, enabling reliable predictive and counterfactual reasoning that preserves latent trait structure across tasks.

Abstract: Marketing decisions reflect the interaction of latent consumer heterogeneity, time-varying internal states, and explicit interventions, a structure that current prediction- and language-oriented models do not capture in a unified manner. We propose a Three-in-One world-model architecture in which a Deep Boltzmann Machine (DBM) learns a frozen belief representation from demographics, time, and lagged actions and outcomes, with lightweight task-specific adapters attached on top. The same belief supports three tasks within a single framework: (i) energy-based consistency evaluation through the DBM's free energy, (ii) outcome prediction through adapters, and (iii) counterfactual inference by holding the belief fixed and varying only the action input given to the adapter. Using a controlled simulation in which the latent price sensitivity, promotion responsiveness, and base preference of each consumer are known, we show that the adapters match a strong MLP baseline on visit- and purchase-AUC while recovering heterogeneous treatment effects substantially better than S-, T-, X-, and DR-learner meta-learners and a Causal Forest baseline built on the same raw features, with the largest gap on a confounded price-promotion intervention. Complementing this, free-energy clamps systematically penalize counterfactual purchase trajectories that lack prior promotional exposure, and the penalty itself depends on the latent base preference in the expected direction. These results indicate that DBM beliefs disentangle latent traits in a form that survives counterfactual queries, providing an integrated world-model substrate for marketing intervention.

</details>


### [453] [Towards Autonomous Business Intelligence via Data-to-Insight Discovery Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07202)
*Dongming Wu,Junwen Li,Ming Lu,Gang Wang,Ting Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AIDA is an end-to-end autonomous insight discovery agent designed for complex business environments, using a proprietary DSL and Pareto-guided reasoning to outperform workflow-based agents in a large metric/dimension space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transform fragmented enterprise data into actionable insights for LLMs constrained by complex schemas, limited dynamic SQL generation, and the need for multi-dimensional analysis.

Method: An end-to-end autonomous framework (AIDA) with a scalable instant retail environment (200+ metrics, 100+ dimensions), a proprietary DSL bridging semantic reasoning with SQL execution, and a reinforcement learning system that casts business analysis as Pareto-principle-guided cumulative reasoning.

Result: AIDA significantly outperforms workflow-based agents, achieving superior environmental perception and deeper, multi-perspective analysis; demonstrating viability for industrial-scale BI systems.

Conclusion: Autonomous intelligence can transform industrial-scale business intelligence; AIDA demonstrates viability and provides a path toward autonomous BI in complex enterprise settings.

Abstract: Transforming fragmented enterprise data into actionable insights remains a significant challenge for LLMs, constrained by complex database schemas, limitations in dynamic SQL generation, and the need for deep multi-dimensional analysis.In this paper, we propose AIDA(Autonomous Insight Discovery Agent), the first end-to-end framework designed for autonomous exploration in complex business environments. We establish a highly flexible instant retail environment encompassing 200+ metrics and 100+ dimensions, and integrates a proprietary Domain-Specific Language (DSL) that bridges semantic reasoning with precise SQL execution. Our reinforcement learning system subsequently formulates business analysis as a Pareto Principle-guided cumulative reasoning process. Experimental results demonstrate that AIDA significantly outperforms workflow-based agents, and extensive evaluations further reveal that AIDA achieves superior environmental perception and more in-depth analysis from diverse perspectives. Our work ultimately establishes the transformative potential of autonomous intelligence for industrial-scale business intelligence systems.

</details>


### [454] [HMACE: Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Collaborative Evolution for Combinatorial Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07214)
*Yuping Yan,Jirui Han,Fei Ming,Yuanshuai Li,Yaochu Jin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HMACE is a heterogeneous multi-agent collaborative evolution framework that decomposes heuristic search for NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems into four role-specialized agents (Proposer, Generator, Evaluator, Reflector) with memory-informed updates, achieving superior quality-efficiency trade-offs and state-of-the-art gaps with low token usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of monolithic LLM-driven heuristic design (rigid templates, memory-limited exploration, premature convergence) by introducing autonomous, memory-guided, collaborative search architecture.

Method: Four autonomous agents with defined roles: Proposer for strategy exploration, Generator for executable heuristic synthesis, Evaluator for empirical assessment, Reflector for updating an archive-based memory. The system uses behavior-aware retrieval, lightweight candidate filtering, and fitness-grounded archive updates to steer search toward diverse, promising heuristics while avoiding redundant evaluations.

Result: HMACE achieves favorable quality-efficiency trade-offs versus SOTA single-agent and multi-agent baselines across COPs (TSP, Online BPP, MKP, PFSP). In matched LLM-driven reference comparisons, HMACE attains the lowest average gaps on TSP (0.464%) and Online BPP (0.223%), with token usage of 0.13M and 0.42M respectively, substantially lower than baselines.

Conclusion: Decomposing heuristic design into a coordinated, memory-aware multi-agent workflow enables more diverse and efficient heuristic search for COPs, delivering strong performance and substantial efficiency gains, with potential applicability to a broad range of problems.

Abstract: Large Language Models have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for automated heuristic design for NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems. Despite this progress, existing LLM-based methods typically rely on monolithic workflows constrained by rigid templates, thereby restricting memory-guided exploration and triggering premature convergence to local optima. To design an autonomous and collaborative architecture, we introduce HMACE, a Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Collaborative Evolution framework that reconceptualizes heuristic search as an organizational design problem. HMACE decomposes each evolutionary generation into an autonomous, role-specialized loop with four coordinated agents: a Proposer for strategy exploration, a Generator for executable heuristic synthesis, an Evaluator for empirical assessment, and a Reflector for archive-backed memory update. By coupling behavior-aware retrieval, lightweight candidate filtering, and fitness-grounded archive updates, HMACE guides the search toward diverse and promising heuristic behaviors while avoiding redundant evaluations. Extensive evaluations on representative COPs, including TSP, Online BPP, MKP, and PFSP, show that HMACE achieves a favorable quality-efficiency trade-off compared to state-of-the-art single-agent and multi-agent baselines. In the matched LLM-driven reference comparison, HMACE achieves the lowest average gaps on TSP and Online BPP (0.464\% and 0.223\%, respectively), while requiring only 0.13M and 0.42M tokens for the two tasks, substantially fewer than the compared baselines.

</details>


### [455] [MEMOREPAIR: Barrier-First Cascade Repair in Agentic Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07242)
*Yang Zhao,Chengxiao Dai,Mengying Kou,Yue Xiu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MemoRepair provides a barrier-first cascade-repair contract for agentic memory to fix cascade invalidations; it reduces exposure to stale memory via a max-weight predecessor-closure formulation solved by a single s-t min-cut, with strong empirical gains on ToolBench and MemoryArena.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To prevent derived artifacts from invalidated sources (due to tool/API changes) from continuing to influence future actions, causing security/consistency issues in agentic memory.

Method: Formalize cascade update as cascade repair problem; introduce MemoRepair barrier-first contract: withdraw invalid descendants; construct successors from retained support and repaired predecessors under current interface; republish only validated predecessor-closed successors; reduce to scalar repair-selection; publication problem becomes maximum-weight predecessor closure, solvable exactly by an s-t min-cut.

Result: With complete influence provenance, invalidated-memory exposure drops from 69.8-94.3% (no cascade repair) to 0%; compared to exhaustive Repair all, MemoRepair recovers 91.1-94.3% of validated successors and lowers repair operator cost from 1.0 to 0.57-0.76.

Conclusion: MemoRepair offers an exact, efficient solution to cascade repair in agentic memory via a min-cut formulation, yielding substantial exposure reductions and favorable repair-cost tradeoffs in evaluated domains.

Abstract: Agentic memory evolves across tasks into durable derived artifacts: summaries, cached outputs, embeddings, learned skills, and executable tool procedures. When a source artifact is deleted, corrected, or invalidated by tool or API migration, descendants derived from that source can remain visible and steer future actions with stale support. We formalize this failure mode as the cascade update problem, where repair targets the visible derived state of the memory store. We present MemoRepair, a barrier-first cascade-repair contract for agentic memory. A repair event induces a controlled transition from invalidated descendant state to validated successor state: affected descendants are withdrawn before repair, successors are constructed from retained support and staged repaired predecessors under the current interface, and republication is restricted to validated predecessor-closed successors. This contract induces a scalarized repair-selection problem for a fixed repair-cost tradeoff. We show that the induced publication problem reduces to maximum-weight predecessor closure and can be solved exactly by a single s-t min-cut. Experiments on ToolBench and MemoryArena show that, with complete influence provenance, MemoRepair reduces invalidated-memory exposure from 69.8-94.3% under systems without cascade repair to 0%. Compared with exhaustive Repair all, it recovers 91.1-94.3% of validated successors while reducing normalized repair-operator cost from 1.00 to 0.57-0.76.

</details>


### [456] [EnvSimBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating and Improving LLM-Based Environment Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07247)
*Yi Liu,TingFeng Hui,Wei Zhang,Li Sun,Ningxin Su,Jian Wang,Sen Su*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EnvSimBench provides a formal EnvSim Ability benchmark and a constraint-driven pipeline for reliable LLM-based environment simulation; it uncovers a universal state-change cliff in SOTA LLMs and demonstrates measurable gains in synthesis yield and cost reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable scalable, diverse agent training by replacing manual environments with LLM-simulated counterparts, while addressing LLMs' instability in updating multi-state environments and providing a rigorous evaluation framework.

Method: Formalize EnvSim Ability; construct EnvSimBench with 400 samples across 167 diverse environments with verifiable labels and three-axis difficulty stratification; systematically evaluate SOTA LLMs to reveal a state-change cliff; design a constraint-driven simulation pipeline to reduce hallucinations and improve synthesis yield by 6.8% and cut costs by >90%.

Result: State-of-the-art LLMs exhibit a universal state-change cliff: near-perfect accuracy when environment state is invariant but catastrophic failures when multiple state updates are required; the proposed constraint-driven pipeline reduces hallucinations and increases synthesis yield by 6.8% and reduces costs by over 90%.

Conclusion: EnvSimBench provides a diagnostic framework and practical optimization path for reliable LLM-based environment simulation, laying a foundation for scalable agent training; code and data are available at the linked repository.

Abstract: Scalable AI agents training relies on interactive environments that faithfully simulate the consequences of agent actions. Manually crafted environments are expensive to build, brittle to extend, and fundamentally limited in diversity. A promising direction is to replace manually crafted environments with LLM-simulated counterparts. However, this paradigm hinges on an unexamined core assumption: LLMs can accurately simulate environmental feedback. In practice, LLM-simulated environments suffer from hallucinations, logical inconsistencies, and silent state drift failures that corrupt agent reward signals and compound the construction costs that the paradigm was designed to eliminate. To address this gap, we propose EnvSimBench with four contributions: 1) We provide the first formal definition and operationalization of Environment Simulation Ability (EnvSim Ability) as a quantifiable research objective. 2) We construct EnvSimBench, a rigorous benchmark covering 400 samples across 167 diverse environments, equipped with verifiable labels and fine-grained difficulty stratification along three axes. 3) Systematic evaluations reveal that all state-of-the-art language models suffer from a universal state change cliff: they achieve near-perfect accuracy on tasks when the environment state remains invariant, yet fail catastrophically when multiple states need simultaneous updates. This finding exposes EnvSim Ability as a critical yet largely unaddressed capability gap. 4) We design a constraint-driven simulation pipeline that substantially reduces hallucination, boosts environment synthesis yield by 6.8%, and cuts costs by over 90%. Overall, EnvSimBench serves as both a diagnostic framework and a practical optimization path for reliable LLM-based environment simulation, establishing a foundation for scalable agent training. Code and data are available at https://github.com/cookieApril/EnvSimBench

</details>


### [457] [Can Agents Price a Reaction? Evaluating LLMs on Chemical Cost Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07251)
*Yuyang Wu,Yue Huang,Shuaike Shen,Xujian Wang,Shuhao Zhang,Qiyao Xue,Weichen Liu,Runtian Gao,Jian Ma,Xiangliang Zhang,Olexandr Isayev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ChemCost: a ground-truth benchmark for evaluating chemical procurement cost estimation by grounding chemical identities to pricing data, retrieving quotes, selecting purchasable packs, normalizing quantities, and computing costs; includes 1,427 evaluable reactions, 2,261 chemicals, and 230,775 supplier quotes, with noise-injected variants for robustness. Strong LLMs with tool access achieve only 50.6% accuracy within 25% relative error; stage-level failures highlight parsing, evidence integration, pack selection, and non-convergent tool use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of judge-free, ground-truth evaluation for scientific tool use in chemistry; move beyond curated demonstrations and LLM-as-judge scoring by providing end-to-end, ground-truth cost estimation grounded to real pricing data.

Method: Create ChemCost benchmark with frozen pricing snapshot across thousands of chemicals and supplier quotes; define end-to-end task: ground identities, retrieve quotes, form valid purchasable packs, normalize quantities, compute reaction cost; support scalar scoring and stage-level diagnosis; add controlled noise-injected views (aliases, quantities, missing fields, input formatting); evaluate frontier/open-weight/chemistry-specialized LLM agents with tool access.

Result: Benchmark yields ground-truth, judge-free evaluation across grounding, retrieval, procurement, and arithmetic; strongest agents reach 50.6% accuracy within 25% relative error on clean inputs, with substantial degradation under realistic noise; stage-level analysis identifies brittle parsing, poor evidence integration, invalid pack selection, and non-convergent tool use as main failure modes.

Conclusion: Grounding and tool-use robustness remain major bottlenecks in chemistry procurement tasks; ChemCost enables principled, diagnostic evaluation and underscores the need for improved parsing, source integration, and robust planning under noise to advance tool-using agents in scientific domains.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have become increasingly capable as tool-using agents, with benchmarks spanning diverse general agentic tasks. Yet rigorous evaluation of scientific tool use remains limited. In chemistry, recent agents can plan syntheses and invoke domain-specific tools, but evaluations often rely on curated demonstrations, expert assessment, or LLM-as-judge scoring rather than exact, judge-free ground truth. We address this gap with chemical procurement cost estimation, a practical task in which an agent must ground chemical identities, retrieve supplier quotes, select valid purchasable packs, normalize quantities, and compute cost from a reaction description. We introduce ChemCost, a benchmark of 1,427 evaluable reactions grounded to a frozen pricing snapshot covering 2,261 chemicals and 230,775 supplier quotes, supporting scalar scoring and stage-level diagnosis of grounding, retrieval, procurement, and arithmetic failures. To evaluate robustness, we further construct controlled noise-injected views that perturb chemical aliases, quantity expressions, missing fields, and input formatting. Experiments with frontier, open-weight, and chemistry-specialized LLM agents show that tool access is necessary but insufficient for solving the task. The strongest agents reach only 50.6% accuracy within 25% relative error on clean inputs and degrade substantially with realistic noise. Stage-level analysis further shows that failures arise from brittle parsing, ineffective evidence integration, invalid pack selection, and non-convergent tool use.

</details>


### [458] [Finite-Time Analysis of MCTS in Continuous POMDP Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07703)
*Da Kong,Vadim Indelman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Finite-time, probabilistic guarantees for Monte Carlo Tree Search in POMDPs with discrete and continuous observations; introduces Voro-POMCPOW using Voronoi partitions to achieve finite branching and partitioning guarantees; provides polynomial concentration bounds and high-probability estimates; shows competitive empirical performance and relevance to continuous MDPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rigorous finite-time guarantees for MCTS in POMDPs remain open due to nonstationarity and dependencies from heuristic action selection. Aims to close this gap for both discrete and continuous observation spaces and to extend to continuous MDPs.

Method: 1) Extend polynomial exploration bonus to UCB in POMDPs for discrete observations, deriving polynomial concentration bounds for root-value estimation. 2) Propose an abstract partitioning framework and derive a finite-time bound on partitioning loss for continuous observations. 3) Introduce Voro-POMCPOW, a finite-time guarantee variant of POMCPOW that adaptively partitions observations via Voronoi cells, preserving observation generator and maintaining finite branching. 4) Empirically validate performance and theoretical guarantees; argue applicability to continuous MDPs.

Result: Discrete POMDPs: polynomial concentration bounds for empirical root-value estimates under an extended UCB. Continuous POMDPs: high-probability bounds on value estimates with an abstract partitioning framework; Voro-POMCPOW achieves finite-time guarantees and competitive performance while preserving the original observation generator.

Conclusion: Provides a rigorous finite-time analysis framework for MCTS in POMDPs across discrete and continuous observation spaces, filling a gap in the theory; the Voronoi-based partitioning approach offers a practical mechanism to obtain finite branching and theoretical guarantees, with potential applicability to continuous MDPs.

Abstract: This paper presents a finite-time analysis for Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) in Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs), with probabilistic concentration bounds in both discrete and continuous observation spaces. While MCTS-style solvers such as POMCP achieve empirical success in many applications, rigorous finite-time guarantees remain an open problem due to the nonstationarity and the interdependencies induced by heuristic action selection (e.g., UCB). In the discrete setting, we address these challenges by extending the polynomial exploration bonus to UCB in POMDP setting, yielding polynomial concentration bounds for the empirical value estimation at the root node. For continuous observation spaces, we introduce an abstract partitioning framework and propose a finite-time bound on partitioning loss. Under mild conditions, we prove highprobability bound on value estimates in POMDPs with continuous observation space. Specifically, we propose Voro-POMCPOW, a variant of POMCPOW with f inite-time guarantees that adaptively partitions the continuous observation space using Voronoi cells. This approach maintains a finite branching factor while preserving the original observation generator. Empirical validation demonstrates that the proposed Voro-POMCPOW shows competitive performance while providing theoretical guarantees. Although our analysis focuses on continuous POMDPs, the techniques developed herein are also applicable to continuous MDPs, closing another gap on the MDP side.

</details>


### [459] [Structured Role-Aware Policy Optimization for Multimodal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07274)
*Bingqing Jiang,Difan Zou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SRPO introduces role-aware token-level credit assignment for multimodal RLVR, separating perception and reasoning tokens and guiding updates via self-distilled on-policy contrasts, improving evidence-grounded reasoning while preserving GRPO rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal reasoning with sequence-level rewards obscures token-level contributions; distinguishing perception (visual evidence) and reasoning (answer derivation) tokens can yield more reliable, evidence-grounded conclusions.

Method: Structured Role-aware Policy Optimization (SRPO) refines the GRPO advantage into token-level advantages without altering the reward function. Perception tokens are emphasized by their visual dependency under original vs corrupted visuals; reasoning tokens are emphasized by their consistency with the generated perception. A shared trajectory-level baseline unifies these signals, yielding positive token weights that scale updates.

Result: Experiments on diverse multimodal reasoning benchmarks show SRPO improves evidence-grounded reasoning, demonstrating the benefit of role-aware credit beyond uniform sequence-level credit.

Conclusion: Role-aware token-level credit assignment is effective for reliable multimodal reasoning; SRPO achieves this without external reward models or separate teachers and maintains GRPO optimization direction.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR), especially with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), has shown strong potential for improving the reasoning capabilities of large vision-language models (LVLMs). However, in multimodal reasoning, final-answer rewards are typically assigned at the sequence level and do not distinguish the functional roles of different tokens, making it difficult to determine whether a correct answer is supported by task-relevant visual evidence. In this paper, we revisit multimodal RLVR from the perspective of role-aware token-level credit assignment, where structured responses are decomposed into perception tokens for extracting visual evidence and reasoning tokens for deriving answers from that evidence. Based on this perspective, we propose Structured Role-aware Policy Optimization (SRPO), which refines the sequence-level GRPO advantage into role-aware token-level advantages without changing the reward function. Specifically, SRPO assigns role-specific credit by using self-distilled on-policy contrasts: perception tokens are emphasized according to their visual dependency under original versus corrupted visual inputs, while reasoning tokens are emphasized according to their consistency with the generated perception. These role-specific signals are further unified through a shared trajectory-level baseline, yielding positive token weights that adjust relative update magnitudes while preserving the original GRPO reward and optimization direction, without requiring external reward models or separate teachers. Experiments across diverse multimodal reasoning benchmarks show that SRPO improves evidence-grounded reasoning, highlighting the importance of moving beyond uniform sequence-level credit toward role-aware optimization for reliable multimodal reasoning.

</details>


### [460] [Signal Reshaping for GRPO in Weak-Feedback Agentic Code Repair](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07276)
*Jia Li,Yuxin Su,Ting Peng,Hailiang Huang,Yuetang Deng,Michael R. Lyu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Signal-reshaped GRPO for code-agent RL under weak feedback; outcome, process, and within-group signals enable effective trajectory ranking and credit, yielding substantial end-to-end gains over base GRPO and over binary rewards; privileged distillation is insufficient for long tool-use trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Weak feedback in code agents means rollout signals do not capture semantic success; need to reweight and structure signals to recover semantic ranking and proper credit assignment.

Method: Minimal signal-reshaping construction that preserves group-normalized GRPO, including: compile-and-semantic layered rewards to reshape trajectory ranking; step-level process scores outside group normalization to adjust within-trajectory updates; failure-cause-aware rollout governance to preserve within-group comparability. Evaluation on end-to-end code-agent tasks with comparisons to binary rewards and token-level distillation.

Result: End-to-end accuracy increases from 0.385 (zero-shot) to 0.535 with full reshaping; removing compile-only middle tier with binary rewards reduces performance; adding process-score weighting raises accuracy from 0.48 to 0.53 and reduces average evaluation steps from 23.50 to 17.02; privileged-prompt distillation improves local distribution but fails to replace outcome semantics, process credit, or within-group comparability.

Conclusion: Appropriate signal reshaping is essential for GRPO under weak feedback in code-agent RL; outcome semantics, process credit, and within-group comparability jointly drive gains; token-level distillation cannot substitute for these signals in long, tool-use trajectories.

Abstract: Code-agent RL often receives weak feedback: rollout-time signals are reliable and executable, but capture only necessary or surface conditions for task success rather than the target semantic predicate. Using agentic compile-fix as the setting, we study signal reshaping for standard GRPO under such feedback. Our central claim is that GRPO's within-group comparison is meaningful only after three kinds of signals are reshaped: outcome rewards recover semantic ranking, process signals localize intra-trajectory credit, and rollouts from the same prompt remain execution-comparable.
