Motivation: Evaluate whether agents can infer, execute, and revise novel tool-use procedures under explicit long-range dependencies beyond familiar, short-range interactions. Address the gap between local tool use and deep contextual reasoning.

Method: Introduce an escape-room-style benchmark where tasks are defined by a DAG over tools and items. Agents must invoke real external functions, track hidden state incrementally, propagate intermediate results, and submit a deterministically verifiable final answer. Includes 270 instances across five difficulty tiers and supports fully automated evaluation. Tested with 16 LLM agents and human participants.

Result: Humans achieve 98.3% success at difficulty-5 and 80.0% at difficulty-25; the best model drops from 90.0% to 60.0% over the same range. Trajectory analysis points to failures in long-range state tracking, clue adherence, and intermediate-result propagation. The paper positions AgentEscapeBench as a diagnostic testbed for evaluating current capabilities and guiding future training for robust general-purpose reasoning and adaptation.

Conclusion: AgentEscapeBench provides a rigorous, automated framework to measure and diagnose long-range, tool-grounded reasoning in agents, with implications for training toward more robust, adaptable problem-solving.

Abstract: As LLM-based agents increasingly rely on external tools, it is important to evaluate their ability to sustain tool-grounded reasoning beyond familiar workflows and short-range interactions. We introduce AgentEscapeBench, an escape-room-style benchmark that tests whether agents can infer, execute, and revise novel tool-use procedures under explicit long-range dependency constraints. Each task defines a directed acyclic dependency graph over tools and items, requiring agents to invoke real external functions, track hidden state revealed incrementally, propagate intermediate results, and submit a deterministically verifiable final answer. AgentEscapeBench includes 270 instances across five difficulty tiers and supports fully automated evaluation. Experiments with sixteen LLM agents and human participants show that performance drops sharply as dependency depth increases: humans decline from 98.3% success at difficulty-5 to 80.0% at difficulty-25, while the best model drops from 90.0% to 60.0%. Trajectory analysis attributes model failures mainly to breakdowns in long-range state tracking, clue adherence, and intermediate-result propagation. These findings suggest that current agents can often handle local tool use but still struggle with deep contextual dependencies. We hope AgentEscapeBench can serve as a diagnostic testbed for measuring current agent capabilities and informing future training efforts toward more robust general-purpose reasoning, action, and adaptation.

</details>


### [489] [TraceFix: Repairing Agent Coordination Protocols with TLA+ Counterexamples](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07935)
*Shuren Xia,Qiwei Li,Taqiya Ehsan,Jorge Ortiz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TraceFix presents a verification-first pipeline for LLM multi-agent coordination that synthesizes a protocol topology IR, generates PlusCal logic, and iteratively repairs using TLC counterexamples until full verification; verified protocols are executed under a runtime monitor, yielding strong DL/LL reduction and robust performance under reduced model capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure reliable, deadlock- and livelock-free coordination among LLM-driven agents by integrating formal verification (TLA+/TLC) into the agent design loop, and to measure performance and scalability across tasks, including fault scenarios and reduced model capability.

Method: 1) Synthesize a protocol topology IR from a task description. 2) Generate PlusCal coordination logic from the IR. 3) Iteratively repair the protocol using counterexamples produced by TLC until verification succeeds. 4) Compile verified process bodies into per-agent system prompts. 5) Execute under a runtime monitor that rejects out-of-topology operations. 6) Evaluate on 48 tasks across 16 scenario families, plus runtime comparisons and ablations.

Result: All 48 tasks reach full TLC verification; 62.5% pass on the first attempt; no task requires more than four repair iterations. State spaces span six orders of magnitude yet verification completes in under 60 seconds per task. In 3,456 runtime runs, topology-monitored execution yields the highest average task completion (89.4%) and full completion rate (81.5%), with verified protocols degrading runtimes at about half the rate of prompt-only and chat-only baselines when model capability is reduced. A paired ablation shows DL/LL reduction from 31.1% to 14.1%, most pronounced under fault injection.

Conclusion: Integrating formal verification into the LLM multi-agent coordination loop yields reliable, scalable coordination, with significant reductions in deadlock/livelock and improved performance under weaker models. The approach demonstrates practical verification-time feasibility even for large state spaces and provides tangible runtime and reliability benefits.

Abstract: We present TraceFix, a verification-first pipeline for Large Language Model (LLM) multi-agent coordination. An agent synthesizes a protocol topology as a structured intermediate representation (IR) from a task description, generates PlusCal coordination logic, and iteratively repairs the protocol using counterexamples from the TLA+ model checker (TLC) until verification succeeds. Verified process bodies are compiled into per-agent system prompts and executed under a runtime monitor that rejects out-of-topology coordination operations. On 48 tasks spanning 16 scenario families, all tasks reach full TLC verification; 62.5% pass on the first attempt and none requires more than four repair iterations. State spaces span six orders of magnitude yet verification completes in under 60 s for every task. A 3,456-run runtime comparison shows that topology-monitored execution achieves the highest task completion (89.4% average, 81.5% full) and that runtimes using the verified protocol degrade at roughly half the rate of prompt-only and chat-only baselines when model capability is reduced. A paired ablation under a fixed runtime shows that TLC-verified protocols cut deadlock/livelock (DL/LL) from 31.1% to 14.1%, with the largest separation under fault injection.

</details>


### [490] [The Limits of AI-Driven Allocation: Optimal Screening under Aleatoric Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07979)
*Santiago Cortes-Gomez,Mateo Dulce Rubio,Carlos Patino,Bryan Wilder*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A two-stage allocation framework integrating screening with algorithmic targeting under a fixed budget; the optimal policy screens at the margin of algorithmic allocation and then directly targets the highest-risk units; screening gains rise with aleatoric uncertainty; illustrated with income-based social protection and Colombian demining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address irreducible (aleatoric) uncertainty in individual vulnerability that causes misallocation under pure predictive targeting; determine how to optimally combine screening costs with predictive targeting to improve resource allocation under budget constraints.

Method: Theoretical two-stage model: a screening stage observes true outcomes for a subset of units before a final allocation stage; derive the optimal screening rule and allocation rule, characterize when screening complements or substitutes algorithmic targeting; provide empirical illustrations via two applications.

Result: Optimal policy: screen units at the margin of algorithmic allocation and directly target the highest-risk units; screening yields efficiency gains that grow with population-level aleatoric uncertainty; quantifies when screening is beneficial.

Conclusion: Strategic integration of screening with algorithmic targeting can improve allocation efficiency under budget constraints; results inform policy design for social protection and humanitarian contexts, including when and how much screening to employ.

Abstract: The rise of machine learning has shifted targeted resource allocation in policy and humanitarian settings toward algorithmic targeting based on predicted risk scores. This approach is typically cheaper and faster than traditional screening procedures that directly observe the latent vulnerability status through physical verification. Yet, even access to the true conditional vulnerability probability cannot eliminate misallocation: aleatoric uncertainty over individual vulnerability status is irreducible, and probabilistic targeting inevitably misallocates some resources. In this work we study how screening and algorithmic targeting should be optimally combined in a two-stage allocation framework where a screening stage observes true outcomes for a subset of units before a final allocation stage assigns the resource under a fixed coverage budget. We show that the optimal strategy screens units at the margin of algorithmic allocation, while directly targeting the highest-risk units. Furthermore, we empirically characterize when screening and algorithmic targeting act as complements or substitutes: efficiency gains from screening grow as the aleatoric uncertainty in the population increases. We illustrate our framework with applications in income-based social protection programs and humanitarian demining in Colombia, where the tension between screening costs and allocation efficiency is operationally consequential.

</details>


### [491] [Abductive Reasoning with Probabilistic Commonsense](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08011)
*Joseph Cotnareanu,Chiara Roverato,Han Zhou,Didier Chetelat,Yingxue Zhang,Mark Coates*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PACS introduces a probabilistic abductive framework to model varying commonsense beliefs in reasoning. It uses an LLM and a formal solver to sample proofs reflecting individual beliefs and aggregates results to predict majority truth judgments, outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Commonsense beliefs vary across individuals, and formal solvers lack world knowledge. Prior approaches assume universal commonsense facts, limiting reasoning. A probabilistic, abductive framework accounts for variation and aims to predict what most people would judge as true or false.

Method: Propose Probabilistic Abductive CommonSense (PACS), which integrates an LLM with a formal solver to generate and sample proofs representing distinct commonsense beliefs, then aggregates conclusions across samples to reach a final judgment.

Result: Empirically, PACS outperforms chain-of-thought reasoning, prior neurosymbolic methods, and search-based approaches across multiple benchmarks.

Conclusion: Modeling diverse commonsense beliefs via probabilistic abductive sampling improves performance and provides a viable approach for aligning logic-based reasoning with human judgments.

Abstract: Recent efforts to improve the reasoning abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) have focused on integrating formal logic solvers within neurosymbolic frameworks. A key challenge is that formal solvers lack commonsense world knowledge, preventing them from making reasoning steps that humans find obvious. Prior methods address this by using LLMs to supply missing commonsense assumptions, but these approaches implicitly assume universal agreement on such commonsense facts. In reality, commonsense beliefs vary across individuals. We propose a probabilistic framework for abductive commonsense reasoning that explicitly models this variation, aiming to determine whether most people would judge a statement as true or false. We introduce Probabilistic Abductive CommonSense (PACS), a novel algorithm that uses an LLM and a formal solver to sample proofs as observations of individuals' distinct commonsense beliefs, and aggregates conclusions across these samples. Empirically, PACS outperforms chain-of-thought reasoning, prior neurosymbolic methods, and search-based approaches across multiple benchmarks.

</details>


### [492] [Learning CLI Agents with Structured Action Credit under Selective Observation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08013)
*Haoyang Su,Ying Wen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes two learning enhancements for RL-based CLI agents: σ-Reveal for selective token context and A^3 for turn-level credit assignment, plus a ShellOps benchmark suite to study CLI information extraction and editing tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CLI agents operate in high-dimensional, structured action spaces with sparse, long-horizon rewards. Current RL methods underutilize native CLI structures and face two bottlenecks: identifying task-relevant evidence from partial observations and assigning credit across long action sequences.

Method: σ-Reveal: an inference-time mechanism that selects token-budgeted context for a CLI to improve observation efficiency. A^3 (Action Advantage Assignment): a native agentic RL method that derives turn-level advantages from episode feedback, AST-based action sub-chain residuals, and tree-level trajectory margins to improve credit assignment without increasing algorithmic complexity. ShellOps: a verifiable dataset suite for CLI tasks in repository-like environments to evaluate these ideas.

Result: Demonstrates that exploiting CLI action structure with σ-Reveal and A^3 improves learning efficiency and task performance on shell-driven information extraction and file-editing tasks; provides ShellOps as a benchmark for reproducibility and further study of CLI RL.

Conclusion: Leveraging structured CLI actions and appropriate credit-assignment mechanisms can significantly enhance RL for real-world CLI tasks, and the ShellOps suite offers a concrete platform for evaluating such approaches.

Abstract: Command line interface (CLI) agents are emerging as a practical paradigm for agent-computer interaction over evolving filesystems, executable command line programs, and online execution feedback. Recent work has used reinforcement learning (RL) to learn these interaction abilities from verifiable task feedback, yet few methods exploit the native structured attributes of CLI actions as learning signals. Beyond this underused action structure, CLI learning also couples two bottlenecks for coding agents. First, the agent must identify task-relevant evidence in a large codebase from partial observations. Second, sparse terminal rewards must be assigned to the actions that shape a long multi-turn trajectory. We study these bottlenecks through shell-driven information extraction and file editing tasks. For selective observation, we introduce $σ$-Reveal, an inference-time mechanism that selects token-budgeted context for the same CLI. For credit assignment, we propose Action Advantage Assignment ($\mathrm{A}^3$), a native agentic RL method that preserves the algorithmic complexity of standard agentic RL. $\mathrm{A}^3$ constructs turn-level advantages from episode-level relative feedback, abstract syntax tree (AST) based action sub-chain residuals, and tree-level trajectory margins. To further evaluate this problem setting, we construct ShellOps, a verifiable dataset suite covering CLI tasks in repository environments.

</details>


### [493] [Reason to Play: Behavioral and Brain Alignment Between Frontier LRMs and Human Game Learners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08019)
*Botos Csaba,Sreejan Kumar,Austin Tudor David Andrews,Laurence Hunt,Chris Summerfield,Joshua B. Tenenbaum,Rui Ponte Costa,Marcelo G. Mattar,Momchil Tomov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Frontier large reasoning models (LRMs) closely mirror human learning and brain activity in complex, novel games, outperforming RL and Bayesian agents in both behavior and neural prediction, driven by in-context representations rather than downstream planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether modern AI systems can emulate human-like learning, planning, and brain dynamics in complex, naturalistic tasks by comparing frontier LRMs to RL and Bayesian baselines using fMRI data.

Method: Participants learn novel video games requiring rule discovery, hypothesis revision, and multi-step planning while undergoing concurrent fMRI. The study benchmarks models on three criteria: gameplay performance, alignment with human learning behavior, and prediction of brain activity. Comparisons include frontier LRMs, model-free and model-based deep RL agents, and a Bayesian theory-based agent, with permutation controls and targeted manipulations to test reliance on in-context representations.

Result: LRMs best reproduce human discovery-phase behavior and predict brain activity about 10x more accurately than RL-based approaches across cortical and subcortical regions; results are robust to permutation controls. Targeted manipulations indicate brain alignment stems from in-context game-state representations rather than downstream planning or reasoning.

Conclusion: LRMs provide a compelling computational account of human learning and decision making in complex, naturalistic environments, potentially surpassing traditional RL and Bayesian approaches in aligning with human behavior and neural dynamics.

Abstract: Humans rapidly learn abstract knowledge when encountering novel environments and flexibly deploy this knowledge to guide efficient and intelligent action. Can modern AI systems learn and plan in a similar way? We study this question using a dataset of complex human gameplay with concurrent fMRI recordings, in which participants learn novel video games that require rule discovery, hypothesis revision, and multi-step planning. We jointly evaluate models by their ability to play the games, match human learning behavior, and predict brain activity during the same task, comparing a suite of frontier Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) against model-free and model-based deep reinforcement learning agents and a Bayesian theory-based agent. We find that frontier LRMs most closely match human behavioral patterns during game discovery and predict brain activity an order of magnitude better than both reinforcement learning alternatives across cortical and subcortical regions, with effects robust to permutation controls. Through targeted manipulations, we further show that brain alignment reflects the model's in-context representation of the game state rather than its downstream planning or reasoning. Our results establish LRMs as compelling computational accounts of human learning and decision making in complex, naturalistic environments. Project page with interactive replays: https://botcs.github.io/reason-to-play/

</details>


### [494] [VecCISC: Improving Confidence-Informed Self-Consistency with Reasoning Trace Clustering and Candidate Answer Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08070)
*James Petullo,Sonny George,Dylan Cashman,Nianwen Xue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VecCISC reduces the cost of weighted majority voting for LLM reasoning by filtering reasoning traces with semantic similarity to remove duplicates, degenerate, or hallucinated traces before critic evaluation, achieving substantial token savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The overhead of critic-based confidence scoring in CISC imposes high computational and monetary cost; there is a need to prune candidate traces without sacrificing accuracy.

Method: Introduce VecCISC, an adaptive framework that measures semantic similarity to prune semantically equivalent or invalid reasoning traces, thereby reducing the number of traces requiring critic scoring; evaluated across five challenging datasets spanning multiple domains.

Result: On five benchmarks (math, chemistry, biology, commonsense, humanities), VecCISC achieves a 47% reduction in total token usage while maintaining or surpassing CISC accuracy.

Conclusion: VecCISC offers a practical, cost-effective approach to inference-time reasoning with LLMs by reducing unnecessary critic evaluations while preserving performance; demonstrates broad applicability across domains.

Abstract: A standard technique for scaling inference-time reasoning is Self-Consistency, whereby multiple candidate answers are sampled from an LLM and the most common answer is selected. More recently, it has been shown that weighted majority voting (e.g. Confidence-Informed Self Consistency (CISC)), which assigns a confidence value to each candidate answer and chooses the answer with the largest accumulated score, tends to be more accurate on a wide range of popular benchmarks. In practice, weighted majority voting necessitates calling a critic LLM on each candidate's reasoning trace to produce the answer's confidence score. This secondary series of LLM calls greatly increases the overhead and cost of weighted majority voting, despite its potential performance benefits. To reduce this expense, we propose VecCISC, a lightweight, adaptive framework that uses a measure of semantic similarity to filter reasoning traces that are semantically equivalent to others, degenerate, or hallucinated, thus decreasing the number of candidate answers that must be evaluated by the critic. To ensure adequate experimental thoroughness, we evaluate VecCISC on five challenging, widely-adopted datasets spanning the domains of mathematics, chemistry, biology, commonsense reasoning, and the humanities. Our results demonstrate that VecCISC reduces the total token usage by 47%, while maintaining or exceeding the accuracy of CISC.

</details>


### [495] [XDecomposer: Learning Prior-Free Set Decomposition for Multiphase X-ray Diffraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.05866)
*Hanyu Gao,Bin Cao,Yunyue Su,Tong-Yi Zhang,Qiang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A prior-free, set-based framework for simultaneous decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns, inferring phase components, proportions, and structures without priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multiphase PXRD analysis is a bottleneck due to entangled phases; existing methods rely on single-phase inputs or priors, limiting applicability to real-world mixtures.

Method: Formulates multiphase XRD analysis as a set prediction problem. Introduces a phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism and diffraction-consistent reconstruction within a unified, prior-free architecture to infer an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their proportions, and corresponding structural representations without candidate lists or prior phase numbers.

Result: Extensive experiments on simulated and experimental datasets show substantial improvements in reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, with strong generalization to unseen mixtures.

Conclusion: XDecomposer enables data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduces dependence on prior-guided, iterative phase matching; code is openly available.

Abstract: Multiphase powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis remains a fundamental bottleneck in structure identification, as real-world synthesis often produces complex mixtures whose constituent phases (components) cannot be reliably disentangled. While recent advances in representation-based crystal retrieval and generation suggest the possibility of inferring structures directly from PXRD, existing approaches largely assume single-phase inputs and break down in multiphase settings. Here, we present XDecomposer, a prior-free framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns without requiring candidate phase lists, structural templates, or prior knowledge of phase number. We formulate multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem, where the model infers an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations within a unified architecture. A phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism, together with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction, enables accurate source separation while preserving crystallographic fidelity. Extensive experiments on both simulated and experimental datasets show that XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, while maintaining strong generalization to unseen mixtures. These results provide a practical route toward data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduce long-standing dependence on prior-guided iteratively phase matching. The code is openly available at https://github.com/Licht0812/XDecomposer

</details>


### [496] [MASPO: Joint Prompt Optimization for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.06623)
*Zhexuan Wang,Xuebo Liu,Li Wang,Zifei Shan,Yutong Wang,Zhenxi Song,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MASPO automatically refines prompts across multi-agent LLM systems via joint evaluation and evolutionary beam search, improving global success without ground-truth labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Local-agent prompt optimization often misaligns with system-wide goals; a joint, system-wide optimization that bridges local interactions and global outcomes is needed.

Method: Introduce MASPO with a joint evaluation mechanism that scores prompts by downstream success of successor agents, not local validity, and a data-driven evolutionary beam search to explore high-dimensional prompt spaces.

Result: MASPO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art prompt optimization methods across 6 tasks, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 2.9.

Conclusion: MASPO effectively bridges local interactions and global outcomes in LLM-based MAS, offering automatic, iterative prompt refinement; code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based Multi-agent systems (MAS) have shown promise in tackling complex collaborative tasks, where agents are typically orchestrated via role-specific prompts. While the quality of these prompts is pivotal, jointly optimizing them across interacting agents remains a non-trivial challenge, primarily due to the misalignment between local agent objectives and holistic system goals. To address this, we introduce MASPO, a novel framework designed to automatically and iteratively refine prompts across the entire system. A core innovation of MASPO is its joint evaluation mechanism, which assesses prompts not merely by their local validity, but by their capacity to facilitate downstream success for successor agents. This effectively bridges the gap between local interactions and global outcomes without relying on ground-truth labels. Furthermore, MASPO employs a data-driven evolutionary beam search to efficiently navigate the high-dimensional prompt space. Extensive empirical evaluations across 6 diverse tasks demonstrate that MASPO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art prompt optimization methods, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 2.9. We release our code at https://github.com/wangzx1219/MASPO.

</details>
